| **Paint sprayer (airless/air-assisted)** | Atomized coating deposition | Compressed air / hydraulic pressure |
| **Drilling / tapping spindle** | Hole-making, thread-cutting | Electric or pneumatic motor |
| **Grinding / deburring tool** | Material removal, edge finishing | Rotary abrasive + compliance |
| **Water-jet cutter** | Cold-cutting of composites, stone, food | High-pressure water (up to 400 MPa) |
| **Plasma / laser cutter** | Thermal cutting of metal sheets | Ionized gas / focused light beam |
| **Hot-melt adhesive dispenser** | Bonding of plastics, packaging | Thermoplastic melt + pneumatic piston |
| **Stud welding head** | Fastening of studs to plates | Capacitor discharge / drawn-arc |
| **Screw-driving spindle** | Automated assembly | Servo-driven torque control |

### C. Tool-Changing & Compliance Systems

- **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** — pneumatically or electrically actuated coupling that swaps tools in **< 5 seconds**.
- **Remote Center Compliance (RCC)** — passive mechanical device that allows lateral + angular float around a *remote center* (typically the tool tip), compensating for positional errors during peg-in-hole insertion.
- **Quick-Change Couplers** — modular pneumatic / electrical / data connections.

## 2.2 Working Principles — The "Why" Behind the Design

### 2.2.1 Spot Welding Gun
A **resistance spot welding (RSW) gun** is the most common special tool in automotive bodyshops. The KTU board expects you to know the *electrical + mechanical* combination:

1. Two copper electrodes clamp the workpiece sheets.
2. A high current (typically **6 kA to 12 kA**) is passed for a short duration (**100 ms to 500 ms**).
3. The contact resistance at the sheet interface generates localized heat (**I²R heating**).
4. The metal melts and fuses, forming a **nugget**.
5. The force is maintained until the nugget solidifies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot-mounted vs. Stationary:** A robot-mounted spot-welding gun is called a **"welding gun"** (light, ~50–100 kg). A stationary weld gun (transformer on the floor) is called a **"weld gun with suspended transformer"** — heavier (200–400 kg) and supplied via a **welding cable bundle (WCB)** or **swivel dress pack**.

### 2.2.2 Arc Welding Torch
A **MIG/MAG welding torch** for robotic arc welding is a slender, water-cooled assembly carrying:
- A **contact tip** (current transfer)
- A **gas nozzle** (shielding gas shroud)
- A **wire liner** (continuous wire feed)
- A **water-cooling jacket** (extends duty cycle to 100%)

The robot must perform **weaving motion** (transverse oscillation) and **torch angle control** (typically 10°–20° push angle) for proper weld bead formation.

### 2.2.3 Paint Sprayer
Robotic paint sprayers are either:
- **Air-atomized** — high-velocity air shatters paint into droplets (best finish quality).
- **Airless (hydraulic)** — high pressure (~10–20 MPa) forces paint through a small orifice (best for high-viscosity coatings).
- **Electrostatic** — paint droplets are charged (~–60 kV) and attracted to the grounded workpiece (best transfer efficiency, ~80–90%).

Critical parameters: **fluid flow rate (Q)**, **atomizing air pressure (p)**, **fan air pressure**, **gun-to-work distance (typically 200–300 mm)**, and **gun traverse speed (typically 500–1000 mm/s)**.

### 2.2.4 Vacuum Gripper
A vacuum gripper uses **negative pressure** to hold smooth, flat, or porous workpieces (e.g., glass panels, cardboard boxes, PCB sheets). The holding force equation is central to KTU problem-solving.

### 2.2.5 Magnetic Gripper
Used for ferromagnetic parts (steel plates, ferrous castings). Either **permanent magnet** (no power needed, failsafe) or **electromagnet** (controllable, requires power). Holding force is a function of magnetic flux density **B** and contact area **A**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are the **highest-priority formulas** for the KTU board exam. Memorize the variable meanings and units — KTU often tests unit conversion.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{hold} \geq 2 \cdot m \cdot g \cdot S$ | **Minimum gripping force** for safe handling (factor of safety **S**) | $F_{hold}$ = holding force [N], $m$ = mass [kg], $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $S$ = safety factor (typically 2–4) |
| 2 | $F_{grip} = \dfrac{m \cdot g}{2 \mu}$ | **Finger gripping force** for a 2-jaw gripper (force closure) | $F_{grip}$ = jaw clamping force [N], $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (0.1–0.7) |
| 3 | $F_{v} = p_{atm} \cdot A_{eff} \cdot n$ | **Vacuum holding force** | $p_{atm}$ ≈ vacuum pressure [Pa] (negative gauge), $A_{eff}$ = effective sealed area [m²], $n$ = number of suction cups |
| 4 | $F_{m} = \dfrac{B^{2} \cdot A}{2 \mu_{0}}$ | **Magnetic holding force** (approximate) | $B$ = flux density [T], $A$ = contact area [m²], $\mu_0$ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m |
| 5 | $W_{nugget} = I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t$ | **Weld nugget heat energy** | $W$ = heat [J], $I$ = welding current [A], $R$ = contact resistance [Ω], $t$ = time [s] |
| 6 | $Q = C_{d} \cdot A_{o} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}}$ | **Paint flow rate** (orifice equation) | $Q$ = flow [m³/s], $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (~0.6), $A_o$ = orifice area [m²], $\Delta p$ = pressure drop [Pa], $\rho$ = density [kg/m³] |
| 7 | $T_{joint} = F \cdot d \cdot \mu$ | **Screw-driving torque requirement** | $T$ = torque [N·m], $F$ = axial clamp force [N], $d$ = nominal bolt diameter [m], $\mu$ = thread friction coefficient |
| 8 | $F_{cut} = k_c \cdot A_{chip}$ | **Specific cutting force** (drilling/milling) | $k_c$ = specific cutting energy [N/m²], $A_{chip}$ = uncut chip cross-section [m²] |
| 9 | $T_{static} = F_{payload} \cdot r + W_{tool} \cdot d_{cg}$ | **Static wrist torque** (tool selection check) | $F_{payload}$ = payload [N], $r$ = tool moment arm [m], $W_{tool}$ = tool weight [N], $d_{cg}$ = CG offset [m] |
| 10 | $P_{act} = \dfrac{F_{act} \cdot v}{1000 \cdot \eta}$ | **Pneumatic actuator power** | $P$ [kW], $F$ = force [N], $v$ = velocity [m/s], $\eta$ = efficiency (~0.8) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU numerical problems in Module 1 typically test **Formulas 1, 2, 3, 5, and 7** at the **Remember/Apply** level of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Always carry the factor of safety **S** through every step — losing **S** = losing 1 mark.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive sector:** Spot welding guns + paint sprayers on a single robot line reduce floor space by ~40% vs. dedicated cells.
- **Aerospace:** Drilling end-effectors with **on-machine probing** achieve hole-position accuracy of **±0.05 mm** for CFRP/Ti stacks.
- **Electronics:** Vacuum grippers handle **fragile wafers** (200 mm / 300 mm) with controlled pick-up force.
- **Food & pharma:** Stainless-steel grippers with **IP67K / IP69K wash-down rating** allow direct chemical cleaning.
- **Foundries:** Water-cooled grippers survive ambient temperatures of **> 200 °C** while handling red-hot castings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Gripping Force for a Two-Jaw Gripper

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A robot must lift a steel block of mass $m = 5 \text{ kg}$ using a horizontal two-jaw gripper. The coefficient of friction between the rubber jaw pads and the steel surface is $\mu = 0.4$. A factor of safety $S = 2.5$ is required against slip. Calculate the minimum gripping force per jaw.

### Step 1 — Free-Body Analysis
The block has weight acting downward:
$$W = m \cdot g$$

The two jaws apply normal forces $N_1$ and $N_2$ on opposite faces. For a symmetric gripper, $N_1 = N_2 = N$. Friction forces $f_1$ and $f_2$ act upward, opposing the impending slip.

### Step 2 — Vertical Equilibrium (no slip condition)
For static equilibrium with factor of safety:
$$2 \cdot \mu \cdot N \geq S \cdot m \cdot g$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $N$ (Gripping Force per Jaw)
$$N \geq \frac{S \cdot m \cdot g}{2 \mu}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values
$$N \geq \frac{2.5 \times 5 \times 9.81}{2 \times 0.4}$$

Step 4a — Numerator:
$$2.5 \times 5 = 12.5$$
$$12.5 \times 9.81 = 122.625 \text{ N}$$

Step 4b — Denominator:
$$2 \times 0.4 = 0.8$$

Step 4c — Division:
$$N \geq \frac{122.625}{0.8} = 153.28 \text{ N}$$

### Step 5 — Final Answer
$$\boxed{N_{min} \approx 153.3 \text{ N per jaw}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Distribution:** Step 1 = 2 marks, Step 2 = 2 marks, Step 3 = 1 mark, Step 4 (substitution) = 2 marks, Step 5 (final answer with units) = 2 marks. Total = 9 marks. (A 14-mark question would also require designing the pneumatic cylinder bore size — see 3.2.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vacuum Gripper Cup Diameter

**Problem Setup:** A vacuum gripper with **one circular suction cup** must lift a glass pane of mass $m = 8 \text{ kg}$. The vacuum pump can achieve a gauge pressure of $p_v = -70 \text{ kPa}$ (i.e., 70% of full vacuum). The seal efficiency reduces the effective area by a factor $k = 0.85$. Find the minimum required cup diameter $D$.

### Step 1 — Holding Force Requirement (with safety factor $S = 2$)
$$F_{v} \geq S \cdot m \cdot g$$

### Step 2 — Substitute
$$F_{v} \geq 2 \times 8 \times 9.81 = 156.96 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3 — Vacuum Force Equation
$$F_{v} = p_v \cdot A_{eff} = p_v \cdot k \cdot A$$

### Step 4 — Express Area in Terms of Diameter
For a circular cup:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

### Step 5 — Combine
$$F_{v} = p_v \cdot k \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $D^{2}$
$$D^{2} = \frac{4 F_{v}}{p_v \cdot k \cdot \pi}$$

### Step 7 — Substitute Numbers
Step 7a — Numerator:
$$4 \times 156.96 = 627.84$$

Step 7b — Denominator:
$$p_v \cdot k \cdot \pi = 70{,}000 \times 0.85 \times 3.1416$$
$$= 70{,}000 \times 2.6703 = 186{,}921$$

Step 7c — Division:
$$D^{2} = \frac{627.84}{186{,}921} = 0.003359 \text{ m}^{2}$$

### Step 8 — Take Square Root
$$D = \sqrt{0.003359} = 0.05795 \text{ m}$$

### Step 9 — Convert to mm
$$\boxed{D_{min} \approx 58.0 \text{ mm}}$$

**Practical design note:** A standard 60 mm cup with 2 mm safety margin would be selected from catalog.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spot Weld Heat Energy

**Problem Setup:** A robot-mounted spot welding gun passes a current of $I = 8 \text{ kA}$ for a duration of $t = 0.3 \text{ s}$ through a joint with total contact resistance $R = 200 \text{ μ}\Omega$. Calculate the heat generated in the weld nugget.

### Step 1 — Apply Joule's First Law
$$W = I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t$$

### Step 2 — Convert Units to SI
$$I = 8 \text{ kA} = 8{,}000 \text{ A}$$
$$R = 200 \text{ μ}\Omega = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Ω} = 0.0002 \text{ Ω}$$
$$t = 0.3 \text{ s}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Step-by-Step
Step 3a — Square the current:
$$I^{2} = (8000)^{2} = 64{,}000{,}000 \text{ A}^{2}$$

Step 3b — Multiply by R:
$$I^{2} \cdot R = 64{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0002 = 12{,}800$$

Step 3c — Multiply by time:
$$W = 12{,}800 \times 0.3 = 3{,}840 \text{ J}$$

### Step 4 — Final Answer
$$\boxed{W = 3.84 \text{ kJ per weld nugget}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** This energy is sufficient to melt ~6 g of low-carbon steel (latent heat of fusion ≈ 270 kJ/kg), forming a nugget of ~5–6 mm diameter in 1.0 mm mild-steel sheet — typical for automotive body-in-white.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Special Tool Selection Criteria

| Selection Criterion | Spot Welding Gun | Arc Welding Torch | Paint Sprayer | Vacuum Gripper | Magnetic Gripper |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Workpiece material** | Sheet steel | Steel, Al, SS | Any coated | Smooth, non-porous | Ferromagnetic |
| **Payload range (kg)** | Tool weight 50–100 | 5–15 | 2–8 | 0.1–50 | 1–200 |
| **Duty cycle** | High (60–100%) | Medium (40–60%) | Low–Med | High | High |
| **Power/utility needs** | Compressed air + weld current | Gas + water + wire + power | Compressed air + paint | Vacuum pump | Electricity |
| **Cycle time impact** | 0.5–2 s/weld | Continuous weld | 5–15 s/pass | < 1 s | < 1 s |
| **Cost (INR, 2024)** | 3–8 lakh | 1–3 lakh | 0.5–2 lakh | 0.3–1 lakh | 0.4–1.5 lakh |
| **MTBF (hours)** | ~10,000 | ~5,000 | ~3,000 | ~20,000 | ~15,000 |
| **Clean-room compatible?** | No | No | With care | Yes | No |

> [!TIP]
> KTU board examiners often ask: *"Justify the selection of a vacuum gripper over a magnetic gripper for handling aluminium sheets in a car-body shop."* Use the above matrix to structure your answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define a special tool in robotics. How is it different from an end effector?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A special tool is a **purpose-built device attached to a robot's wrist** to perform a specific manufacturing process on a workpiece — such as spot welding, painting, drilling, or cutting. The term *end effector* is the broader category that includes **all** devices mounted on the robot's wrist, including general-purpose mechanical grippers. A special tool is a **subset** of end effectors that is dedicated to a process operation rather than merely holding a part. For example, a **spot-welding gun** is a special tool, while a **two-finger gripper** is a general end effector.

*(Mark split: Definition = 1, distinction = 1, example = 1.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"List any four special tools used in industrial robots and state one typical application of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Special Tool | Application |
|---|---|
| Spot welding gun | Joining of sheet-metal car bodies |
| MIG/MAG welding torch | Continuous seam welding of steel pipes |
| Paint sprayer (airless) | Coating automotive body panels |
| Drilling spindle | Hole-making in aerospace CFRP stacks |
| Vacuum gripper | Handling glass panes in window assembly |
| Magnetic gripper | Lifting steel plates in press-shop tending |

*(Any four — 0.5 marks each for naming + 0.25 marks for correct application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, with internal choice (the student may answer EITHER the full question OR substitute the alternative question provided).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **robot-mounted spot welding gun** used in an automotive assembly line. List its major components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A spot welding gun is a special tool that joins two or more sheet-metal parts by passing a high electric current through the joint, generating **I²R heat** at the contact interface. The molten metal forms a **nugget** that solidifies under pressure.

**Major Components and Functions (7 marks — distribute as 0.5 per row):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Robot wrist flange | Mechanical interface to the robot arm |
| Pneumatic/servo actuator | Provides electrode clamping force |
| Transformer / MFDC inverter | Steps down voltage, increases current to 6–12 kA |
| Copper electrodes | Conduct current + apply mechanical pressure |
| Electrode caps | Replaceable wear surface (typically CuCrZr alloy) |
| Water cooling jacket | Removes heat, extends duty cycle to 100% |
| Weld controller | Regulates current, time, and monitors quality |
| Dress pack (cable bundle) | Carries power, water, air, signal lines |

**Working Cycle (brief):**
1. Robot positions electrodes over the joint.
2. Electrodes close and clamp the sheets (~2–4 kN force).
3. Current is pulsed for ~0.2–0.5 s.
4. Nugget forms and cools under pressure.
5. Electrodes retract; robot moves to the next spot.

---

**(b)** A robot-mounted spot welding gun must produce **30 welds per minute** on a 0.8 mm mild-steel sheet. Each weld requires a current of **9 kA** for a duration of **0.25 s**, with a total joint resistance of **180 μΩ**. Compute the **(i)** heat energy per weld, **(ii)** total heat per minute, and **(iii)** average power delivered. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Heat per weld — using $W = I^2 R t$ (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer)**

$$W_{weld} = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$$

Convert to SI:
- $I = 9000$ A → $I^2 = 81{,}000{,}000$ A² (0.5 marks)
- $R = 180 \times 10^{-6}$ Ω = 0.00018 Ω
- $t = 0.25$ s

$$W_{weld} = 81{,}000{,}000 \times 0.00018 \times 0.25$$

Step-by-step:
- $81{,}000{,}000 \times 0.00018 = 14{,}580$ (1 mark)
- $14{,}580 \times 0.25 = 3{,}645$ J

$$\boxed{W_{weld} = 3.645 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

**(ii) Total heat per minute — multiply by 30 (1 mark)**

$$W_{min} = 30 \times 3{,}645 = 109{,}350 \text{ J} = 109.35 \text{ kJ/min}$$

$$\boxed{W_{min} \approx 109.35 \text{ kJ/min}}$$

---

**(iii) Average power — divide by 60 s (2 marks)**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{W_{min}}{60} = \frac{109{,}350}{60} = 1{,}822.5 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{avg} \approx 1.82 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly forget to **convert kA → A** and **μΩ → Ω** before substituting. KTU awards **0 marks for the numerical answer** if the unit conversion is wrong, even if the formula is correct. Always write the unit-conversion step explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **vacuum gripper** with a neat block diagram. List **two advantages** and **two limitations** of vacuum grippers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A vacuum gripper holds a workpiece by creating a **pressure difference** between the inside of a suction cup and the surrounding atmosphere. Atmospheric pressure pushes the cup against the workpiece with a force equal to the product of pressure difference and sealed area.

**Working Principle (5 steps — 1 mark each for the diagram-and-explanation block):**

1. Vacuum pump evacuates air from the suction cup, creating negative gauge pressure.
2. Atmospheric pressure acting on the outside of the cup pushes it firmly onto the workpiece.
3. Friction between the cup lip and the workpiece resists lateral motion.
4. The robot lifts the workpiece — the sealed cup maintains grip as long as vacuum is sustained.
5. At the destination, a solenoid valve vents the cup, releasing the part.

**Advantages (2 × 0.5 = 1 mark):**
- Simple, lightweight, and inexpensive.
- Can handle smooth, flat, or delicate parts without mechanical clamping force.

**Limitations (2 × 0.5 = 1 mark):**
- Requires airtight seal — unsuitable for rough or porous surfaces.
- Vacuum-pump failure = workpiece drop (unless spring-loaded reservoir is used).

---

**(b)** A vacuum gripper has **two circular suction cups** and must lift an aluminium panel of mass **12 kg**. The vacuum pump delivers a gauge pressure of **–60 kPa**. Effective sealed area per cup is **18 cm²**. Apply a safety factor **S = 3**. Determine whether the gripper can lift the panel safely. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Required holding force (with safety) (2 marks)**
$$F_{req} = S \cdot m \cdot g = 3 \times 12 \times 9.81 = 353.16 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Available vacuum force (2 marks)**
Convert area: $A_{eff} = 18 \text{ cm}^2 = 18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.0018 \text{ m}^2$

For two cups:
$$F_{avail} = p_v \cdot A_{eff} \cdot n = 60{,}000 \times 0.0018 \times 2$$

Step-by-step:
- $60{,}000 \times 0.0018 = 108$ N (per cup)
- $108 \times 2 = 216$ N

$$F_{avail} = 216 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and Conclusion (3 marks)**

Since $F_{avail} = 216$ N $< F_{req} = 353.16$ N, the gripper **cannot** lift the panel safely.

**Step 4 — Design recommendation (bonus)**
To meet the requirement, we need:
$$A_{total,min} = \frac{F_{req}}{p_v} = \frac{353.16}{60{,}000} = 0.005886 \text{ m}^2 = 58.86 \text{ cm}^2$$

With 2 cups, each cup must have area $\geq 29.43$ cm², i.e., cup diameter:
$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 29.43}{\pi}} \approx 6.12 \text{ cm}$$

So either **upgrade to 2 cups of 65 mm diameter**, or **add a third cup of 60 mm diameter**, or **increase vacuum to 90 kPa**.

$$\boxed{\text{Standard 65 mm diameter cups (2 nos.) required for safe operation.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall 1:** Students forget to convert cm² to m² — a 10,000× error.
> **Common Pitfall 2:** Students apply the safety factor **after** the calculation instead of before — wrong direction of design.
> **Common Pitfall 3:** Students forget to multiply by the number of cups **n**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Special Tools (Module 1)**

- ☐ **Definition:** Special tools are process-specific end effectors, NOT general-purpose grippers.
- ☐ **Classification (3 broad classes):** Mechanical grippers, process tools, tool-changer systems.
- ☐ **Spot welding** uses I²R heating; formula $W = I^2 R t$.
- ☐ **Arc welding torches** need shielding gas, wire feed, and water cooling for 100% duty cycle.
- ☐ **Paint sprayers** — three types: air-atomized, airless, electrostatic (highest transfer efficiency).
- ☐ **Vacuum grippers** — force equation $F_v = p \cdot A_{eff} \cdot n$ with safety factor $S \geq 2$.
- ☐ **Magnetic grippers** — best for ferrous parts; permanent magnets are fail-safe.
- ☐ **Drilling spindles** — feed force, spindle speed, and chip-load calculations follow standard machining formulas.
- ☐ **Compliance devices (RCC)** — passive mechanical float for peg-in-hole assembly.
- ☐ **Tool changers** reduce setup time from hours to seconds; modularity is the key to flexible automation.
- ☐ **Always include a safety factor** in gripper/vacuum/magnetic force calculations — KTU awards marks for **S**.
- ☐ **Always convert units** before substituting — μΩ, cm², kA, and kPa conversions are common traps.
- ☐ **Always state units** in the final answer — N, J, kW, mm — partial marks are awarded for correct units even if the numerical value is off.
- ☐ **Draw block diagrams** in long answers — KTU awards **1–2 marks** purely for a labeled, neat functional diagram.
- ☐ **Industrial examples** (automotive, aerospace, electronics, food) fetch bonus conceptual marks in 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Grippers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Magnetic Gripper** is a specialized *non-mechanical end-effector* used in robotic manipulation systems to grasp, hold, transport, and release ferromagnetic (ferrous) workpieces by exploiting the attractive force generated between a magnetic field source and the magnetically permeable material of the target object. Unlike mechanical grippers that rely on friction or form closure, magnetic grippers achieve *force closure* through electromagnetic or permanent magnetic flux lines penetrating the workpiece.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the magnetic gripper is classified under **Special-Purpose Grippers** within the broader taxonomy of *End Effectors* and is characterized by the absence of mechanical fingers, jaws, or articulated linkages. It is fundamentally a *contactless hold mechanism* (at the flux-interface level) that requires **only one accessible surface** of the workpiece to function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Magnetic grippers belong to the family of "Grippers for Special Applications." They are *exclusively* limited to **ferromagnetic workpieces** (iron, steel, nickel, cobalt, and their alloys). Non-ferrous materials (aluminum, copper, brass, stainless steel 300-series, plastics) **cannot** be handled by pure magnetic grippers without auxiliary surface attachments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine lifting a steel paperclip with a **refrigerator magnet** — that is essentially a magnetic gripper in its simplest form. The magnet produces invisible "lines of force" (magnetic flux) that loop out of its surface, travel through the steel clip, and snap back. As the flux lines squeeze through the clip, they literally **pull** the clip toward the magnet.

Now scale that refrigerator magnet up to the size of a **car-lifting electromagnet in a scrapyard**. The principle is identical:

1. **Turn ON the magnetism** → the steel object is "glued" to the gripper by invisible force fields.
2. **Move the robot arm** → the object travels along rigidly because the holding force far exceeds the object's weight.
3. **Turn OFF the magnetism** (or reverse it) → the object is released.

The only "gripper finger" is the *entire magnetic face* — no moving parts, no pneumatics, no compliance mechanisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy Cheat Sheet for Viva:**
> - Refrigerator magnet holding a note → Permanent magnet gripper
> - Scrapyard crane lifting a car → Electromagnetic gripper
> - Smart phone case snap-on closure → Low-force magnetic gripper
> - MRI machine's patient table mover → Linear motor with magnetic levitation principles (not exactly a gripper, but similar flux concepts)

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The fundamental physical constants governing magnetic gripper design are:

- **Vacuum permeability (μ₀) = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m ≈ 1.2566 × 10⁻⁶ T·m/A**
- **Magnetic flux density (B)**: measured in **Tesla (T)** or **Gauss (1 T = 10,000 Gauss)**
- **Magnetic field strength (H)**: measured in **A/m** or **Oersted**
- **Permeability of free space / vacuum**: μ₀ as above
- **Relative permeability of mild steel**: μᵣ ≈ 2000 – 5000
- **Relative permeability of pure iron**: μᵣ ≈ 5000 – 200000
- **Relative permeability of aluminum**: μᵣ ≈ 1.000022 (essentially non-magnetic)
- **Standard demagnetization safety factor**: **3:1 to 5:1** (design load vs. theoretical max)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for Exams:** Students often confuse *magnetic flux density* (B) with *magnetic field strength* (H). They are related but fundamentally different: **B = μH** where μ = μ₀μᵣ. B is the actual density of flux lines in a material; H is the *magnetizing force* that drives them.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification of Magnetic Grippers

Magnetic grippers are classified by the **mechanism of magnetic field generation**. KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to be conversant with all three primary classes:

### 2.1.1 Permanent Magnet (PM) Grippers

- Use **hard magnetic materials** like Neodymium-Iron-Boron (NdFeB, grades N35–N52), Samarium-Cobalt (SmCo), or AlNiCo.
- The magnetic field is **always on** — there is no "release" without an *active demagnetization mechanism*.
- Release is achieved by:
  - Mechanical sliding/shunting of a ferromagnetic keeper
  - Rotating the magnet through 90° (e.g., Eriez-style design)
  - Using a secondary opposing electromagnet to nullify the field
- **Advantages**: No power consumption to hold, intrinsically safe (power failure does not drop the load), compact, lightweight.
- **Disadvantages**: Cannot release on demand without mechanical motion, residual magnetism on workpiece, lower peak force density than electromagnets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Question Hook:** "Compare permanent magnet and electromagnet grippers. When would you prefer one over the other?" — Be ready with at least 4 comparison points covering *holding force, power consumption, safety, release mechanism, and cost.*

### 2.1.2 Electromagnetic (EM) Grippers

- Use **soft magnetic cores** (low-carbon steel, silicon steel laminations) wound with current-carrying coils.
- The magnetic field is **proportional to ampere-turns (NI)** of the coil.
- Release is achieved simply by **switching off the current** — instantaneous release.
- **Advantages**: On-demand release, controllable force via current modulation, high force density, no residual magnetism when off.
- **Disadvantages**: **Power-fail-drop hazard** (load falls if power is cut — critical safety issue), continuous power consumption to hold, heat dissipation, requires DC power source, control electronics complexity.

### 2.1.3 Electro-Permanent (Hybrid) Magnetic Grippers

- A *permanent magnet* provides the holding force, while a *short electrical pulse* reverses the polarity of one section, nullifying the net flux.
- The **electrical energy is needed only during the brief switch transition** (typically 100–500 ms), not during holding.
- **Advantages**: Combines the safety of PM (power-fail-safe) with the on-demand release of EM.
- **Disadvantages**: Expensive, complex construction, requires precise pulse control, more bulky.

## 2.2 Working Principle (The "Why" and "How")

The magnetic gripper exploits **Maxwell's Stress Tensor** at the magnet-workpiece interface. When a magnet faces a ferromagnetic surface, flux lines travel:

**Magnet North Pole → Air Gap → Ferromagnetic Workpiece → Air Gap → Magnet South Pole**

The flux lines "prefer" the low-reluctance path through the iron. At the air-gap interfaces, the flux lines exert a **pressure** (Maxwell stress) on the iron, pulling it toward the magnet. The force per unit area (magnetic pressure) is:

$$p_{mag} = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$$

This is one of the **most important equations in magnetic gripper design** and is *routinely asked* in KTU exams.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Force Generation:

1. **Magnetization source** (PM or EM) generates a magnetomotive force (MMF).
2. MMF drives magnetic flux Φ through a magnetic circuit composed of the magnet, the air gap(s), and the ferromagnetic workpiece.
3. The **air gap is the dominant reluctance** in the circuit (since μᵣ of air ≈ 1, while steel μᵣ ≈ 2000+).
4. Flux crossing the air gap creates a **magnetic pressure** perpendicular to the interface.
5. This pressure manifests as an **attractive force** pulling the workpiece toward the magnet.
6. The force scales with **B²** (quadratic) — doubling the flux density **quadruples** the holding force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why squared?** This is because (a) force is proportional to B (pressure on a pole), and (b) the flux density itself depends on the magnetomotive force. The energy stored in the air gap is **½ × B²/μ₀ per unit volume**, and force is the *gradient of energy with respect to gap distance*. This is why designers fight desperately to *minimize* the air gap — even a 0.1 mm gap can cut holding force by 30–50%.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula | Description | Units / Variables | Typical Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | Maxwell stress-based pull-off force | F [N], B [T], A [m²], μ₀ [H/m] | **Most frequently asked** — direct hold force |
| $p_{mag} = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$ | Magnetic pressure on ferromagnetic surface | p [Pa], B [T] | Theoretical max pressure ≈ 400 kPa at B = 1 T |
| $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | B-H relationship in linear material | B [T], H [A/m], μᵣ dimensionless | When finding B from H or vice versa |
| $H = \frac{NI}{l_c}$ | Magnetizing force from a coil | N turns, I [A], l_c mean flux path length [m] | Coil design problems |
| $B \approx \frac{\mu_0 NI}{g}$ | Flux density approximation for simple air-gap-dominated circuit | g = air gap length [m] | Quick estimation, valid when reluctance of air gap dominates |
| $F = \frac{\mu_0 A (NI)^2}{2g^2}$ | Holding force of an electromagnet (simple model) | g [m], N turns, I [A] | EM gripper design |
| $F_{design} = \frac{F_{theoretical}}{SF}$ | Design force after applying safety factor | SF = 3 to 5 typical | Always used in final design |
| $\Phi = B \cdot A$ | Magnetic flux | Φ [Wb], B [T], A [m²] | Flux linkage and circuit analysis |
| $\mathcal{R} = \frac{l}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$ | Reluctance of a magnetic path segment | $\mathcal{R}$ [A/Wb or H⁻¹] | Magnetic circuit analysis (Hopkinson's law) |
| $\Phi = \frac{MMF}{\mathcal{R}_{total}}$ | Hopkinson's Law (magnetic Ohm's law) | MMF = NI [A-turns] | Magnetic circuit problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** The maximum theoretical holding force from a single magnet face occurs when **all flux is concentrated at one pole with zero leakage**. In practice, only **10–25%** of the theoretical max is realized due to flux leakage around the edges, non-uniform B, and surface roughness. Always apply a **realization factor of 0.10–0.25** to your theoretical $F = B^2A / (2\mu_0)$ calculation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Magnetic grippers are *workhorses* in the following industrial sectors:

- **Automotive press shops**: Stamping steel panels between dies (loads up to 500 kg per part).
- **Steel service centers**: Handling coils, plates, and billets.
- **Scrap and recycling**: Lifting ferrous scrap with high-tonnage electromagnets.
- **Bin-picking in warehouses**: Picking individual steel parts from mixed totes.
- **Machine tending**: Loading/unloading CNC mills with steel billets.
- **Semiconductor and electronics**: Handling thin steel sheets in stamping operations.

The non-mechanical nature means **no wear on fingers**, **no lubricant required**, and **no alignment sensitivity to part geometry variation** — a magnetic gripper doesn't care if the part is round, square, or has a complex profile, as long as one face is ferromagnetic and accessible.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pull-Off Force from Maxwell Stress

This is the *single most important derivation* for KTU exams on magnetic grippers. We will derive $F = B^2A/(2\mu_0)$ from first principles using energy methods.

### Setup

Consider a simple U-shaped electromagnet with a single air gap of length $g$ facing a flat ferromagnetic workpiece of cross-sectional area $A$. The flux density $B$ is uniform across the air gap (idealized).

### Step 1: Energy Stored in the Air Gap

The magnetic energy density (energy per unit volume) in a region of flux density $B$ in free space is given by:

$$w = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$$

This is a direct analog of the electrostatic energy density $\frac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 E^2$. It represents the energy stored in the magnetic field per cubic meter.

### Step 2: Total Energy in the Air Gap

Multiply the energy density by the volume of the air gap (volume = area × gap length):

$$W = w \cdot V = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \cdot A \cdot g$$

**Textual explanation:** The air gap is a thin slab of area $A$ and thickness $g$. The total field energy in that slab is its volume times the energy density.

### Step 3: Force as Negative Gradient of Energy

The force pulling the workpiece toward the magnet is found by taking the derivative of stored energy with respect to gap distance. A useful sign convention: the force acts to *reduce* the gap, so it equals the *negative* of the gradient of energy with respect to gap, but since increasing $g$ increases $W$ in this case, the force pulling the workpiece in (toward smaller $g$) is:

$$F = -\frac{dW}{dg}$$

But here we must be careful: the system is *current-driven* (constant current source) versus *flux-driven* (permanent magnet). For a current-driven electromagnet, the energy method gives:

$$F = +\frac{dW}{dg}\bigg|_{I = \text{const}}$$

Wait — let me reconsider. For a current source, as the gap decreases, the inductance increases (energy stored in the system increases), and this *attracts* the workpiece in. The correct expression for attractive force in a current-driven system is:

$$F = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{dx}$$

But for a flux-constant system (like a permanent magnet in the linear region), the energy method gives the attractive force as:

$$F = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$

We can derive this directly for a constant-flux assumption. Differentiating $W$ with respect to $g$:

$$\frac{dW}{dg} = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$

since the flux density $B$ is approximately constant (idealized constant-flux assumption for a stiff PM). Therefore:

$$F = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$

**Textual explanation:** When the gap $g$ increases by an infinitesimal amount $dg$, the air-gap volume increases by $A \cdot dg$, and the additional field energy required is $\frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} A \cdot dg$. This energy must come from the work done against the magnetic force as the gap widens. Equating work = force × distance = $-F \cdot dg$ (force opposes gap increase), we get the magnitude of attractive force.

### Step 4: Units Verification

Let us verify dimensional consistency:

- $B$ has units of Tesla = $\text{kg} / (\text{A} \cdot \text{s}^2)$
- $A$ has units of $\text{m}^2$
- $\mu_0$ has units of $\text{H/m} = \text{kg} \cdot \text{m} / (\text{A}^2 \cdot \text{s}^2)$

So:

$$\frac{B^2 A}{\mu_0} = \frac{[\text{kg}^2 / (\text{A}^2 \cdot \text{s}^4)] \cdot [\text{m}^2]}{[\text{kg} \cdot \text{m} / (\text{A}^2 \cdot \text{s}^2)]} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}^2} = \text{N}$$

Dimensions check out. The factor of 2 in the denominator ensures the correct magnitude.

### Step 5: Numerical Sanity Check

Suppose a magnetic gripper generates $B = 0.5$ T over a contact area of $A = 50 \text{ cm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$.

$$F = \frac{(0.5)^2 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3})}{2 \cdot (4\pi \times 10^{-7})}$$

Let us compute this step by step:

$$F = \frac{0.25 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3}}{8\pi \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$F = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5133 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$F \approx 497 \text{ N}$$

This is approximately **50 kg of holding force** from a 0.5 T, 50 cm² magnet — consistent with real industrial magnetic grippers. The result confirms the formula's physical plausibility.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Electromagnet Holding Force from MMF

For a *simple solenoid-shaped* electromagnet with a single air gap $g$, the magnetizing field strength is:

$$H = \frac{NI}{g}$$

Assuming the air gap dominates the magnetic circuit (a very good approximation since $\mu_r$ of air is 1 versus ~2000+ for steel), the flux density in the gap is:

$$B = \mu_0 H = \frac{\mu_0 NI}{g}$$

Substituting into the Maxwell force equation:

$$F = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0} = \frac{1}{2\mu_0} \left(\frac{\mu_0 NI}{g}\right)^2 A$$

Simplifying:

$$F = \frac{\mu_0 A (NI)^2}{2g^2}$$

**Textual explanation:** This is the classic electromagnet force formula. Notice that:
- $F \propto N^2$ (double the turns → quadruple the force)
- $F \propto I^2$ (double the current → quadruple the force)
- $F \propto 1/g^2$ (double the gap → force drops to one-quarter)

This is why **minimizing the air gap** is the single most effective design lever for an EM gripper designer.

### Numerical Example

An electromagnet has $N = 1000$ turns, $I = 2$ A, $A = 10 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$, and air gap $g = 1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$.

$$F = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (10^{-3}) \cdot (1000 \cdot 2)^2}{2 \cdot (10^{-3})^2}$$

Let us compute the numerator:

$$\text{Numerator} = (1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (10^{-3}) \cdot (4 \times 10^6)$$

$$= 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4000 = 5.0264$$

Denominator:

$$\text{Denominator} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$$

Therefore:

$$F = \frac{5.0264}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.5132 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

This is an enormous force (~256 tonnes) — the simple formula overestimates because we ignored magnetic saturation, flux leakage, and fringing. In practice, with a 1 mm gap, expect a more reasonable figure of 1000–5000 N (~100–500 kg). This is why a **realization factor of 0.10–0.25** must be applied.

### Corrected Estimate

Applying a 10% realization factor:

$$F_{realistic} = 0.10 \times 2.51 \times 10^6 = 2.51 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 25.6 \text{ tonnes}$$

This is more plausible for an industrial electromagnet of the stated dimensions.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection Decision Table

| Application Scenario | Recommended Gripper Type | Key Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Picking steel sheets from a stack | Permanent magnet with mechanical shunt | No power needed; clean release; no heat on the sheets |
| Loading steel billets into a furnace | Electromagnet (water-cooled) | High force, controlled release; heat tolerance is critical |
| Stamping line in automotive press shop | Hybrid electro-permanent | Power-fail safety is critical (billets must not fall on operators) |
| Bin-picking in a warehouse (mixed lots) | PM gripper with mechanical keeper | Low cost, reliable, infrequent pick-and-place |
| Semiconductor wafer handling (steel carriers) | Custom EM gripper with low residual | Clean release, no particulate attraction |
| Scrap yard crane | Heavy-duty EM with high NI | Extreme forces (multi-tonne loads) |
| High-speed pick-and-place (1 cycle/sec) | Hybrid or fast-switching EM | Rapid on/off with safety; low holding current |

## 3.4 Limitation Analysis Matrix

| Limitation | Affected Gripper Type(s) | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Cannot handle non-ferrous parts | All magnetic grippers | Use a *carrier plate* or *ferrous adapter* attached to the part |
| Sensitive to air gap | All | Precision mechanical alignment, surface flatness grinding |
| Power failure drops load | EM only | Use PM, hybrid, or add mechanical safety catches |
| Residual magnetism on release | PM and EM (slight) | Demagnetization coil (reverse pulse) on release |
| Heat buildup in coils | EM only | Forced air cooling, water cooling, duty cycle limits |
| Limited to one accessible face | All | Re-orient part, use multiple small grippers, or fall back to mechanical gripper |
| Strong external magnetic fields interfere | All | Mu-metal shielding, careful siting away from welding transformers |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Magnetic Gripper System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a magnetic gripper system, showing the interaction between the controller, the gripper itself, the workpiece, and the safety subsystems. This is a "block-level" schematic — it captures the **system behavior** rather than physical geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[RobotController] --> P2[GripperControlUnit]
    P2 --> P3[PowerSupplyModule]
    P2 --> P4[SignalConditioning]
    P3 --> P5[ElectromagneticCoilDriver]
    P5 --> P6[MagneticCoreAssembly]
    P4 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[AirGapInterface]
    P7 --> P8[FerrousWorkpiece]
    P9[FluxDensitySensor] --> P4
    P10[TemperatureSensor] --> P4
    P11[SafetyInterlock] --> P2
    P12[DemagnetizationPulseGen] --> P5
    P8 -. Hold Force F .-> P6
    P8 -. Gravity Load W .-> P11
    P6 -. Heat Dissipation .-> P10
```

**Reading guide for the diagram:**

- **P1 (RobotController):** The main robot CPU (e.g., a 6-axis arm controller) that issues grip and release commands.
- **P2 (GripperControlUnit):** A microcontroller or PLC that interprets commands, monitors sensors, and activates safety protocols.
- **P3 (PowerSupplyModule):** Provides regulated DC current to the coil; typically 24V or 48V industrial DC.
- **P4 (SignalConditioning):** Processes analog signals from the flux density and temperature sensors.
- **P5 (ElectromagneticCoilDriver):** A high-current H-bridge or MOSFET bank that switches current to the coil with adjustable amplitude.
- **P6 (MagneticCoreAssembly):** The laminated steel core wound with the coil, forming the magnetic circuit.
- **P7 (AirGapInterface):** The mechanical contact zone where flux crosses from core to workpiece.
- **P8 (FerrousWorkpiece):** The target object (steel sheet, billet, etc.) being held.
- **P9 (FluxDensitySensor):** A Hall-effect sensor that measures B in real time for closed-loop force control.
- **P10 (TemperatureSensor):** A thermistor or RTD on the coil windings that prevents thermal overload.
- **P11 (SafetyInterlock):** Verifies that the holding force exceeds the workpiece weight by a defined safety factor before allowing robot motion.
- **P12 (DemagnetizationPulseGen):** Issues a reverse-polarity pulse on release to neutralize residual magnetism.

## 4.2 State Machine: Magnetic Gripper Operating Cycle

The following state diagram represents the four primary operational states of an EM or hybrid magnetic gripper. This is a high-yield visual for exam questions on the **control logic** of magnetic grippers.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IdleState
    IdleState --> Energizing: Apply Current
    Energizing --> HoldingState: Flux Density Reaches Threshold
    HoldingState --> HoldingState: Monitor Sensors Continuously
    HoldingState --> DropEmergency: Power Failure Detected
    HoldingState --> Releasing: Release Command Received
    DropEmergency --> SafeStow: Mechanical Catch Engages
    Releasing --> IdleState: Current Off and Pulse Complete
    SafeStow --> IdleState: Manual Reset by Operator
    HoldingState --> FaultState: Overcurrent or Overheat
    FaultState --> IdleState: Cool Down and Reset
```

**Reading guide for the state diagram:**

- **IdleState:** Coil de-energized; no holding force. Default safe state.
- **Energizing:** Current ramping up; flux density rising toward threshold.
- **HoldingState:** Flux density confirmed; workpiece held; sensors actively monitored (B, T, I).
- **DropEmergency:** Triggered if power fails or holding force drops below safety margin. Triggers mechanical safety catch.
- **Releasing:** Current cut off; reverse demagnetization pulse applied; workpiece released.
- **FaultState:** Triggered by overcurrent, overheat, or sensor failure. Locks out the system until manual reset.

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Permanent vs. Electromagnet

The following diagram illustrates the *internal functional differences* between the two main magnetic gripper types, making the trade-offs visually obvious.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PM_Type [Permanent Magnet Gripper]
        PM1[Hard Magnetic Material NdFeB] --> PM2[Flux Path Through Pole Faces]
        PM2 --> PM3[Air Gap]
        PM3 --> PM4[Workpiece Ferrous]
        PM4 --> PM3
        PM5[Mechanical Shunt or Rotation] -. Release Mechanism .-> PM2
    end
    subgraph EM_Type [Electromagnet Gripper]
        EM1[DC Power Supply] --> EM2[Coil Driver Switch]
        EM2 --> EM3[Copper Coil Windings]
        EM3 --> EM4[Soft Iron Laminated Core]
        EM4 --> EM5[Air Gap]
        EM5 --> EM6[Workpiece Ferrous]
        EM6 --> EM5
        EM7[Demagnetization Pulse] -. Release .-> EM2
    end
```

**Reading guide:** The PM gripper has a *passive* magnetic source (no electrical input during hold) and a *mechanical* release. The EM gripper has an *active* magnetic source (powered coil) and an *electrical* release (turn off current + reverse pulse). This visual contrast is frequently asked in viva questions.
