**(ii) Force from strain.** For a cantilever, the surface strain at the fixed end is

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{6 F L}{E \, b \, h^2}
$$

Solving for $F$:

$$
F = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, E \, b \, h^2}{6 L}
$$

Substituting:

$$
F = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-4})(2.1 \times 10^{11})(0.015)(0.003)^2}{6 (0.080)}
$$

Compute the numerator step by step:

$$
2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.1 \times 10^{11} = 5.25 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
5.25 \times 10^{7} \times 0.015 = 7.875 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
7.875 \times 10^{5} \times (0.003)^2 = 7.875 \times 10^{5} \times 9 \times 10^{-6} = 7.0875
$$

Denominator:

$$
6 \times 0.080 = 0.48
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,F = \dfrac{7.0875}{0.48} \approx 14.77 \text{ N}\,}
$$

*(2 marks part (i), 3 marks formula, 2 marks numerical — total 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Tactile & Piezoelectric Sensors**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **piezoelectric force sensor**. State two advantages and two limitations. Derive the relation between applied force and output charge. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Block Diagram (described in words for 2 marks):*
Force → Quartz/PZT crystal → Charge generation (Q) → Charge amplifier → Voltage output → ADC → Robot controller.

*Working:* When a force $F$ is applied to a piezoelectric crystal, the crystal lattice distorts, displacing positive and negative charge centres. This produces equal and opposite charges $Q$ on the two faces of the crystal (direct piezoelectric effect). The charge is collected by electrodes, converted to a voltage by a charge amplifier with feedback capacitor $C_f$, and then digitized.

$$
V_{out} = -\dfrac{Q}{C_f} = -\dfrac{d \cdot F}{C_f}
$$

*Advantages:*
1. Extremely wide bandwidth (1 Hz to 100 kHz) — ideal for impact and vibration.
2. Very high stiffness — minimal mechanical deflection, so it does not disturb the contact.
3. Self-generating — no excitation voltage required.

*Limitations:*
1. Cannot measure static (DC) force — charge leaks away.
2. Sensitive to temperature variations and electromagnetic interference.
3. Requires a high-impedance charge amplifier.

*Derivation:* The piezoelectric constitutive relation gives

$$
Q = d \cdot F
$$

where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge constant** (C/N). For a crystal of capacitance $C_p$, the open-circuit voltage is

$$
V = \dfrac{Q}{C_p} = \dfrac{d \cdot F}{C_p}
$$

*(2 marks diagram, 2 marks advantages/limitations, 3 marks derivation — total 7 marks.)*

---

**(b)** Describe the construction of a **tactile sensor array**. How is the **center of pressure (CoP)** computed from the array readings? An $8 \times 8$ tactile array with $2$ mm cell pitch records the following pressures (kPa) in a $4 \times 4$ patch (other cells zero):

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 5 & 2 \\
0 & 6 & 10 & 3 \\
1 & 8 & 7 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 2 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute the total contact force (assume each cell area = $4 \text{ mm}^2$) and the CoP coordinates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Construction (2 marks):* A tactile sensor array is a 2D grid of $M \times N$ miniature pressure-sensing elements fabricated using MEMS, FSR, or capacitive-pad technology. Each cell has its own signal-conditioning and ADC channel. The array is bonded to a compliant elastomer skin so that contact pressure is transmitted uniformly to each cell.

*Center of Pressure (1 mark):*

$$
x_{CoP} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} x_j \, P_{ij}}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} P_{ij}}, \quad
y_{CoP} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} y_i \, P_{ij}}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} P_{ij}}
$$

*Numerical Computation (4 marks):*

**Step 1 — Sum all pressures:**

$$
\Sigma P = 0+0+5+2+0+6+10+3+1+8+7+1+0+0+2+0 = 45 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Total contact force** (cell area $A = 4 \text{ mm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$):

$$
F = \Sigma P \cdot A = (45 \times 10^3)(4 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.18 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — CoP computation.** Using cell-centre coordinates: $x_j = (j - 0.5) \cdot 2$ mm, $y_i = (i - 0.5) \cdot 2$ mm (with $i, j = 1..4$).

Row sums and column sums (for numerator):
- $P_{13}=5, P_{14}=2, P_{23}=10, P_{24}=3, P_{33}=7, P_{34}=1, P_{43}=2$
- $P_{22}=6, P_{23}=10, P_{32}=8, P_{33}=7$

Compute $\Sigma j \cdot P_{ij}$ (column weighted sum):

$$
1(0+0+5+2) + 2(0+6+10+3) + 3(1+8+7+1) + 4(0+0+2+0)
$$

$$
= 1(7) + 2(19) + 3(17) + 4(2) = 7 + 38 + 51 + 8 = 104
$$

Compute $\Sigma i \cdot P_{ij}$ (row weighted sum):

$$
1(0+0+5+2) + 2(0+6+10+3) + 3(1+8+7+1) + 4(0+0+2+0)
$$

$$
= 1(7) + 2(19) + 3(17) + 4(2) = 104
$$

(Symmetric data, as expected.)

**Step 4 — CoP coordinates:**

$$
x_{CoP} = 2 \text{ mm} \cdot \dfrac{104}{45} \approx 4.62 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
y_{CoP} = 2 \text{ mm} \cdot \dfrac{104}{45} \approx 4.62 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F = 0.18 \text{ N}, \quad (x_{CoP}, y_{CoP}) \approx (4.62, 4.62) \text{ mm}\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error** (–2 marks): In a full-bridge derivation, students often write $V_o = +V_s G \varepsilon$ instead of $-V_s G \varepsilon$ (or vice-versa). The sign just indicates the wiring; the **magnitude** is what matters. Examiners deduct only if the sign affects a downstream calculation.
> 2. **Wrong Wheatstone Pairing** (–3 marks): Placing all four gauges in the same orientation (all tension or all compression) gives **zero output**. Always verify the pairing gives a non-zero result before submitting.
> 3. **Mixing Piezoelectric Equations** (–2 marks): Students confuse $Q = dF$ (charge) with $V = dF/C$ (voltage) and forget the role of $C_f$ in a charge amplifier. State which one you are computing.
> 4. **Unit Conversion** (–1 mark): $1 \text{ mm}^2 = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$, $1 \mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$. Always convert before substituting.
> 5. **CoP Formula Mistake** (–2 marks): CoP is the **pressure-weighted** average, NOT the unweighted average. Use the $\Sigma jP$ form, not $\Sigma j / N$.
> 6. **Forgetting full-bridge factor 4×** (–2 marks): Many students derive a quarter-bridge formula and use it for a full-bridge sensor. Always check the configuration in the question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Force & Contact-Type External Sensors**
>
> ✅ **Force Sensor** = vector quantity (magnitude + direction), measured at a single point, e.g., wrist F/T sensor.
> ✅ **Tactile Sensor** = 2D pressure distribution array (matrix of cells).
> ✅ **Contact (Binary) Sensor** = ON/OFF detection of physical touch.
> ✅ **Strain Gauge** uses $\Delta R/R = G \varepsilon$; metallic $G \approx 2$, semiconductor $G = 100$–$200$.
> ✅ **Wheatstone Bridge** formulas: Quarter-bridge $V_o = V_s G \varepsilon / 4$; Full-bridge $V_o = V_s G \varepsilon$ (4× more sensitive, auto temperature-compensated).
> ✅ **Cantilever flexure**: $\varepsilon = 6FL/(Ebh^2)$ at the fixed-end surface — memorize this KTU favourite.
> ✅ **Piezoelectric**: $Q = dF$, $V = Q/C_p$ — dynamic only, wide bandwidth, no DC measurement.
> ✅ **Capacitive**: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ — gap changes with force, high sensitivity, low drift.
> ✅ **FSR**: $R(F) = R_0 F^{-k}$, $k \approx 0.5$–$1.0$ — non-linear, used in low-cost grippers.
> ✅ **Tactile Total Force**: $F = \sum_{i,j} P_{ij} A_{cell}$ (sum of all cell forces).
> ✅ **Center of Pressure**: $x_{CoP} = \sum x_j P_{ij} / \sum P_{ij}$ (pressure-weighted average).
> ✅ **6-axis F/T sensor** output: $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{C} \mathbf{v}$ (calibration matrix maps bridge voltages to forces/moments).
> ✅ **Cross-talk** between axes must be **< 5 %** for a quality wrist sensor.
> ✅ **Force-controlled assembly** = outer loop: $F_d \to e = F_d - F_{measured} \to$ compliance controller $\to$ joint torques.
> ✅ **Piezoelectric advantages**: high bandwidth, high stiffness, self-generating. **Limitations**: no DC, needs charge amplifier, temperature sensitive.
> ✅ **Strain gauge advantages**: static + dynamic, mature technology, cheap. **Limitations**: low-level signal, prone to thermal drift, requires precision bridge.
> ✅ **Limit switch** is the simplest, cheapest, most reliable binary contact sensor — used as end-of-travel stop and safety interlock.
> ✅ **Whisker sensor** detects contact and rough direction; used in small mobile robots (insect-inspired navigation).

**Mnemonic for Contact Sensor Types — "FBS":** **F**orce, **B**inary, **S**lip — these are the three behavioural categories every contact-type sensor falls into.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Contact Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Contact Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – PECDT633 (Basics of Robotics and Automation)**, a **Non-Contact Type Sensor (Transducer)** is defined as a measurement device that detects the presence, position, displacement, velocity, or physical state of a target object **without any physical/mechanical touching** between the sensor and the target. Energy transfer (electromagnetic field, acoustic wave, light beam, or magnetic flux) replaces mechanical contact, eliminating wear, friction, and mechanical lag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag (PECDT633 / Module 2):** *“Non-contact type – proximity, range, position, velocity sensors; optical, inductive, capacitive, magnetic, ultrasonic, and vision-based transducers used in robotic feedback loops.”*

The **six canonical non-contact sensing principles** prescribed in the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Inductive** (eddy-current, variable reluctance)
2. **Capacitive** (change of dielectric / plate gap)
3. **Magnetic / Hall-Effect** (flux density sensing)
4. **Optical / Photoelectric** (light beam interruption / reflection)
5. **Ultrasonic / Acoustic** (time-of-flight echo)
6. **Vision / Image-based** (CCD / CMOS pixel array)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Bat and the TV Remote"**
> - A **bat** flies through a dark cave without touching walls — it sends ultrasonic chirps and listens for the echo (**Ultrasonic Sensor**).
> - You change channels on a TV by pointing a remote without touching the TV — it sends an invisible **infrared light beam** (**Optical / IR Sensor**).
> - Your phone screen turns off when you lift it to your ear — a **capacitive/proximity sensor** detects the electrical field of your skin without contact.
> 
> **All three are non-contact sensors** — they "feel" the world through *fields and waves*, not through fingers.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants are **mandatorily required** for KTU numerical answers:

- Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Speed of sound in air (20 °C): **v = 343 m/s**
- Permittivity of free space: **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- Permeability of free space: **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
- Planck’s constant: **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**

## 1.4 Why Non-Contact Sensors Dominate Modern Robotics

| Reason | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|
| **Zero mechanical wear** | Lifespan of 10⁸ – 10⁹ switching cycles (vs. 10⁶ for contact switches) |
| **High switching frequency** | Up to **5 kHz** for inductive, **1 MHz** for optical |
| **Hygienic** | Suitable for food, pharma, biomedical robotics |
| **Detects fragile / soft targets** | No deformation of workpiece |
| **Long operational life** | No fatigue failure of moving contacts |
| **High repeatability** | Typical **±0.01 mm** resolution for inductive, **±0.1 mm** for ultrasonic |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 Inductive Proximity Sensor (Eddy-Current Type)
- **Step 1:** A high-frequency AC current (typically 100 kHz – 1 MHz) is driven through a coil, generating an alternating magnetic field at the sensor face.
- **Step 2:** When a **ferrous / conductive target** enters this field (within rated sensing range *Sₙ*, usually 0.8 – 60 mm), **eddy currents** are induced on the target surface (Faraday’s Law).
- **Step 3:** These eddy currents create a counter-magnetic field (**Lenz’s Law**), which **damps the oscillator amplitude**.
- **Step 4:** A trigger circuit detects the amplitude drop and switches the output transistor (NPN/PNP).
- **Why it works:** Only metals disturb the field — plastic, wood, and water are ignored.

### 2.1.2 Capacitive Proximity Sensor
- **Step 1:** Two electrodes form a capacitor at the sensing face; an oscillator charges it at a reference frequency.
- **Step 2:** Approach of *any* object (metal, plastic, liquid, powder) changes the **dielectric constant** or the effective plate geometry.
- **Step 3:** The change in capacitance shifts the oscillator frequency; a discriminator trips the output.

### 2.1.3 Optical / Photoelectric Sensor (Three Modes)
| Mode | Principle | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Through-beam** | Separate emitter & receiver; object breaks the beam | Long range (up to 50 m) |
| **Retro-reflective** | Beam reflects from a corner-cube reflector | Mid range (≤ 10 m) |
| **Diffuse reflective** | Beam reflects from target itself | Short range (≤ 1 m) |

### 2.1.4 Ultrasonic Sensor
- **Step 1:** A piezo crystal is excited at **f = 40 kHz** (most common industrial band).
- **Step 2:** A burst of sound waves is emitted; the timer starts.
- **Step 3:** Echo is received; timer stops. **Distance d = (v · t) / 2**, where *v ≈ 343 m/s* in air.

### 2.1.5 Hall-Effect Sensor
- **Step 1:** A constant current *I* flows through a semiconductor slab.
- **Step 2:** A perpendicular magnetic flux density *B* deflects charge carriers to one side, producing a transverse **Hall voltage** V_H.
- **Step 3:** V_H is proportional to *B*, allowing non-contact field/position measurement.

### 2.1.6 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)
- A **mutual-induction** device: primary excited by AC; two secondaries in series-opposition. Core displacement *x* produces a differential output proportional to *x*.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-YIELD for KTU ESE & Internal evaluations.**

| # | Sensor | Governing Equation | Variables / Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Inductive Coil** | $\displaystyle L = \frac{N^{2}\,\mu\,A}{l}$ | L (H), N (turns), μ (H/m), A (m²), l (m) | High-frequency AC; μ = μ₀μᵣ |
| 2 | **Capacitive** | $\displaystyle C = \frac{\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A}{d}$ | C (F), A (m²), d (m), εᵣ (dielectric constant) | εᵣ > 1 always |
| 3 | **Hall Effect** | $\displaystyle V_{H} = \frac{I\,B}{q\,n_{s}\,t}$ | V_H (V), I (A), B (T), q (C), n_s (m⁻³), t (m) | Valid for thin semiconductor |
| 4 | **Photoelectric** | $\displaystyle E = h\,f$ | E (J), h (J·s), f (Hz) | Above threshold for electron emission |
| 5 | **Ultrasonic ToF** | $\displaystyle d = \frac{v \, t}{2}$ | d (m), v (m/s), t (s) | v depends on medium & temperature |
| 6 | **LVDT Output** | $\displaystyle V_{out} = K\,x$ | V_out (V), K (V/m), x (m) | Linear in central ±5 mm range |
| 7 | **Operating Range Rule** | $\displaystyle S_{r} \le 0.81\,S_{n}$ | S_r (real range), S_n (nominal) | KTU standard mounting condition |
| 8 | **Inductive Frequency** | $\displaystyle X_{L} = 2\pi f L$ | X_L (Ω) | Sensor coil impedance |

> **Note on table syntax:** All vertical absolute-value bars have been replaced with `\vert` / `\mid` to preserve Markdown table integrity (per KTU engine rule).

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Robotics

- **Automotive welding lines:** Inductive sensors detect fixture position (IP67, oil-resistant).
- **Pick-and-place SCARA robots:** Photoelectric through-beam sensors count components on a conveyor.
- **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle):** Ultrasonic ToF sensors avoid obstacle collision up to 4 m.
- **Surgical robotics:** Capacitive sensors provide haptic force feedback without sterility breach.
- **Servo motor commutation:** Hall-effect sensors provide rotor position feedback in BLDC drives.
- **CNC tool changers:** LVDT verifies tool insertion depth in the spindle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Rule (PECDT633):** Any derivation question carries a **minimum 7 marks**. Skipping steps results in 50% mark loss. Show **every algebraic transition**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inductance of an Inductive Proximity Coil

**Statement:** A proximity sensor coil has *N* = 500 turns, mean magnetic path length *l* = 0.05 m, cross-sectional area *A* = 1.2 × 10⁻⁵ m², and is wound on a ferrite core of relative permeability μᵣ = 1200. Compute the inductance *L* and its impedance at f = 200 kHz.

### Step 1 — Write the governing equation
The inductance of a coil wound on a high-permeability ferromagnetic core is given by:

$$L = \frac{N^{2}\,\mu_{0}\,\mu_{r}\,A}{l}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute all numerical values
We substitute N = 500, μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m, μᵣ = 1200, A = 1.2 × 10⁻⁵ m², l = 0.05 m.

$$L = \frac{(500)^{2}\;\cdot\;(4\pi \times 10^{-7})\;\cdot\;(1200)\;\cdot\;(1.2 \times 10^{-5})}{0.05}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator
Compute (500)² = 2.5 × 10⁵. Multiply the constants:

$$\text{Numerator} = (2.5 \times 10^{5})\;\cdot\;(4\pi \times 10^{-7})\;\cdot\;(1200)\;\cdot\;(1.2 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.5 \times 10^{5} \;\times\; 4\pi \;\times\; 10^{-7} \;\times\; 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \;\times\; 1200$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.5 \times 4\pi \times 1.2 \times 1200 \;\times\; 10^{5-7-5}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.5 \times 4\pi \times 1.2 \times 1200 \;\times\; 10^{-7}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 14400\pi \times 10^{-7} \;\approx\; 4.524 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{(H·numerator terms)}$$

### Step 4 — Divide by l = 0.05 m

$$L = \frac{14400\pi \times 10^{-7}}{0.05} = 288000\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H}$$

$$L \approx 9.05 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{H} = 90.5\ \text{mH}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the inductive reactance at f = 200 kHz

$$X_{L} = 2\pi f L = 2\pi \times (2 \times 10^{5}) \times (9.05 \times 10^{-2})$$

$$X_{L} = 4\pi \times 10^{5} \times 9.05 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$X_{L} \approx 1.137 \times 10^{5}\ \Omega \approx 113.7\ \text{k}\Omega$$

### Step 6 — Conclusion / Engineering Interpretation
This high impedance means the coil draws very little current at rest. When a metal target approaches, eddy-current loss reduces the effective *L* by 5–15%, dropping the oscillator amplitude and switching the output stage.

> **Final Answer:** **L ≈ 90.5 mH**, **X_L ≈ 113.7 kΩ** at 200 kHz.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Capacitive Sensor — Liquid Level Detection

**Statement:** A cylindrical capacitive level sensor has inner radius *a* = 5 mm, outer radius *b* = 6 mm, length *L* = 0.4 m, and is half-immersed in oil of εᵣ = 2.4. Compute the capacitance when empty and when half-full.

### Step 1 — Governing equation for a cylindrical capacitor
For a coaxial cylindrical capacitor with vacuum / air as dielectric:

$$C = \frac{2\pi\,\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,L}{\ln(b/a)}$$

### Step 2 — Empty condition (air, εᵣ ≈ 1)

$$C_{\text{air}} = \frac{2\pi \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 1 \times 0.4}{\ln(6/5)}$$

$$\ln(1.2) \approx 0.1823$$

$$C_{\text{air}} = \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.4}{0.1823} = \frac{2.224 \times 10^{-11}}{0.1823}$$

$$C_{\text{air}} \approx 1.22 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F} = 122\ \text{pF}$$

### Step 3 — Half-full condition (series combination of two capacitors)
The empty half has εᵣ₁ = 1; the immersed half has εᵣ₂ = 2.4. The two half-lengths act as **capacitors in parallel** (same V, half length each):

$$C_{\text{air,half}} = \frac{C_{\text{air}}}{2} = 61\ \text{pF}$$

$$C_{\text{oil,half}} = \frac{2\pi \times \varepsilon_{0} \times 2.4 \times 0.2}{0.1823} = \frac{2\pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.4 \times 0.2}{0.1823}$$

$$C_{\text{oil,half}} \approx 1.464 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F} = 146.4\ \text{pF}$$

### Step 4 — Total half-full capacitance (parallel)

$$C_{\text{half-full}} = C_{\text{air,half}} + C_{\text{oil,half}} = 61 + 146.4 = 207.4\ \text{pF}$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
The change ΔC = 207.4 − 122 = **85.4 pF** is detected by the oscillator circuit, giving a non-contact, hygienic liquid-level reading.

> **Final Answer:** **C_empty ≈ 122 pF**, **C_half-full ≈ 207 pF**, **ΔC ≈ 85 pF**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ultrasonic Distance Measurement

**Statement:** A robot-mounted ultrasonic sensor emits a 40 kHz burst, receives the echo 23 ms later. Compute distance to obstacle. Take v = 343 m/s.

### Step 1 — Time-of-flight equation

$$d = \frac{v\,t}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$d = \frac{343 \times 23 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{7.889}{2}$$

$$d \approx 3.94\ \text{m}$$

### Step 3 — Sanity check
Ultrasonic sensors are typically rated 0.1 m to 4 m; 3.94 m is just within range. The robot’s collision-avoidance controller will trigger a stop.

> **Final Answer:** **d ≈ 3.94 m**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Sensor

| Target / Application | Best Non-Contact Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Metal object, 0–8 mm range | **Inductive** | Immune to dust, oil, water |
| Plastic pellet, liquid level | **Capacitive** | Detects non-metals |
| Magnet on a rotating shaft | **Hall Effect** | Low cost, digital output |
| Conveyor object counting, 0–5 m | **Photoelectric (through-beam)** | Long range, fast response |
| Transparent bottle detection | **Capacitive or Retro-reflective (polarized)** | Light passes through glass |
| AGV obstacle at 3 m | **Ultrasonic** | Penetrates dust, fog |
| Bin picking of random parts | **Vision (2D/3D camera)** | Highest information density |
| Haptic feedback in surgical tool | **Capacitive / Eddy-current** | Sub-micron resolution |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use Mermaid v10+ syntax. Node IDs are alphanumeric, labels are plain text, no reserved keywords, and no markdown formatting inside quoted labels (per KTU engine rules).

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Non-Contact Sensor Feedback Loop in a Robot Joint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Target Object] -->|Disturbs Field| B[Non-Contact Sensor]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    C --> D[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    D --> E[Microcontroller / Robot Controller]
    E --> F[Decision Logic Compare with Setpoint]
    F --> G[Actuator Driver PWM Signal]
    G --> H[Robot Joint Servo Motor]
    H -->|Mechanical Motion| A
    E --> I[Display HMI / Data Log]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Inductive Proximity Sensor Internals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSOR_MODULE
        OSC[High Frequency Oscillator 100 kHz to 1 MHz]
        COIL[Sensing Coil Ferrite Core]
        DET[Detector Stage Demodulator]
        TRIG[Trigger Comparator Schmitt]
        OUT[Output Stage NPN or PNP Transistor]
    end
    OSC --> COIL
    COIL -->|Eddy Current Loss| TGT[Conductive Target]
    TGT -.->|Damps Amplitude| COIL
    COIL --> DET
    DET --> TRIG
    TRIG --> OUT
    OUT --> PLC[Robot PLC Input]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Ultrasonic ToF Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Microcontroller] -->|Trigger Pulse| TX[40 kHz Piezo Transmitter]
    TX -->|Sound Wave| OBJ[Obstacle]
    OBJ -->|Reflected Echo| RX[40 kHz Piezo Receiver]
    RX --> AMP[Bandpass Amplifier 40 kHz]
    AMP --> COMP[Comparator Threshold Detect]
    COMP --> ECHO[Echo Pin Time Stamp]
    P --> TMR[Start Timer]
    TMR -->|Stop on Echo| P
    P --> DISP[Distance d = vt divided by 2]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting a Non-Contact Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Target is metallic?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Range needed greater than 30 mm?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is target liquid or powder?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> IND_LR[Inductive Long Range]
    Q2 -- No --> IND_SR[Inductive Standard]
    Q3 -- Yes --> CAP[Capacitive Sensor]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is target transparent?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> CAP2[Capacitive or Polarized Retro]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Range greater than 1 m?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> ULT[Ultrasonic or Through-Beam Photo]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Profile shape required?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> VIS[Vision Camera 2D 3D]
    Q6 -- No --> DIFF[Diffuse Photoelectric]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Schematic — Output Characteristics of the Six Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inductive] -->|Digital NPN PNP| OUT1[Switch Output]
    B[Capacitive] -->|Digital or Analog 0 to 10 V| OUT2[Switch or 4 to 20 mA]
    C[Hall Effect] -->|Digital Latch Switch| OUT3[Open Collector]
    D[Photoelectric] -->|Digital or Analog| OUT4[Relay or Transistor]
    E[Ultrasonic] -->|Analog 0 to 10 V or Serial| OUT5[Continuous Distance]
    F[Vision] -->|Image Matrix Pixels| OUT6[Ethernet GigE Vision]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** pattern: 3-mark short answers in Part A, and 14-mark long answers with internal choice in Part B (Module level, two sub-parts of 7 marks each).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three advantages of non-contact sensors over contact-type sensors in a robotic work cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**
1. **No mechanical wear** → operational life of 10⁸ to 10⁹ cycles versus 10⁶ for limit switches.
2. **Higher switching frequency** → up to 5 kHz enables high-speed pick-and-place robotics.
3. **Detect fragile / soft / deformable targets** without surface damage and can sense non-metallic targets (capacitive/ultrasonic types).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is the principle of operation of an inductive proximity sensor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An inductive proximity sensor works on the **eddy-current principle**. A high-frequency AC current (≈ 100 kHz – 1 MHz) flows through a ferrite-cored coil, producing an alternating magnetic field at the sensing face. When a **ferrous or conductive target** enters this field, **eddy currents** are induced on the target (Faraday’s Law). These eddy currents generate a counter-flux (Lenz’s Law) that **damps the oscillator amplitude**. A trigger circuit detects this amplitude drop and switches the output transistor, providing a non-contact ON/OFF signal. **(3 marks — statement of principle + reference to Faraday/Lenz + output)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 14 Marks]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **ultrasonic non-contact distance sensor**. Derive the distance equation. State one industrial application.

**(b) [7 marks]** A capacitive proximity sensor has plate area *A* = 2 × 10⁻³ m², plate separation *d* = 0.5 mm, and dielectric constant εᵣ = 4.5. Compute (i) the capacitance *C*, (ii) the capacitance if a 0.2 mm thick plastic sheet (εᵣ = 3.1) is inserted between the plates.

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** (Use the Mermaid block from Section 4.3 above; in the answer script, draw a labelled block diagram: Microcontroller → Trigger → 40 kHz Piezo TX → Air medium → Target → Echo → Piezo RX → Amplifier → Comparator → Echo pin).

**Working (3 marks):**
1. The microcontroller sends a 10 µs trigger pulse to the transmitter, exciting a piezo crystal at its **resonant frequency of 40 kHz**.
2. The crystal emits a burst of 8 – 16 sound waves into the air.
3. Simultaneously, an internal timer starts counting.
4. The sound wave reflects from the target; the echo is captured by the receiver piezo.
5. The receiver output is bandpass-amplified and fed to a comparator that produces a clean digital echo pulse.
6. The timer stops on the rising edge of the echo pulse; the elapsed time *t* is recorded.

**Distance Equation (1.5 marks):**
The wave travels to the target and back, so the one-way distance is:

$$d = \frac{v\,t}{2}$$

with *v* = speed of sound in air = **343 m/s** at 20 °C.

**Application (0.5 mark):** Used in **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** for obstacle avoidance up to 4 m range.

#### Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** A = 2 × 10⁻³ m², d = 0.5 mm = 5 × 10⁻⁴ m, εᵣ = 4.5, ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m.

**(i) Capacitance without plastic sheet (3.5 marks):**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 4.5 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$C = \frac{8.854 \times 4.5 \times 2}{5} \times 10^{-12-3+4} = \frac{79.686}{5} \times 10^{-11}$$

$$C \approx 1.594 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F} = 159.4\ \text{pF}$$

**(ii) With 0.2 mm plastic sheet inserted (3.5 marks):**
The system now behaves as **two capacitors in series**: air gap = 0.5 − 0.2 = 0.3 mm, plastic = 0.2 mm.

$$C_{1}\,(\text{air}) = \frac{\varepsilon_{0} \times 1 \times A}{0.3 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 59.0\ \text{pF}$$

$$C_{2}\,(\text{plastic}) = \frac{\varepsilon_{0} \times 3.1 \times A}{0.2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.1 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 274.5\ \text{pF}$$

**Series combination:**

$$\frac{1}{C_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{C_{1}} + \frac{1}{C_{2}} = \frac{1}{59.0} + \frac{1}{274.5} = 0.01695 + 0.00364 = 0.02059$$

$$C_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{0.02059} \approx 48.6\ \text{pF}$$

> **Final Answers:** **C (no plastic) ≈ 159.4 pF**, **C (with plastic) ≈ 48.6 pF**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / 14 Marks]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of an LVDT**. Derive the expression for its output voltage. Mention the significance of phase-sensitive detection.

**(b) [7 marks]** An inductive proximity sensor has a nominal range S_n = 8 mm. Using the **KTU mounting rule**, compute (i) the safe operating range S_r, and (ii) the maximum safe distance for reliable target detection if a correction factor of 0.85 is applied for a stainless-steel target.

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) [7 marks]

**Construction (2 marks):** An LVDT consists of **one primary winding (P)** centrally placed and **two identical secondary windings (S₁ and S₂)** wound on either side of the primary, all on a common cylindrical former. A movable soft-iron core slides along the axis of the former, coupled mechanically to the displacement under measurement.

**Working (2.5 marks):**
- The primary is excited by an AC reference voltage V_p (typically 1 – 5 V at 50 Hz – 20 kHz).
- The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (S₁ and S₂ outputs subtracted).
- When the core is at the **null (central) position**, the EMFs induced in S₁ and S₂ are equal and opposite → net output = 0.
- When the core moves toward S₁ by displacement *x*, the flux linkage with S₁ increases and with S₂ decreases → differential output proportional to *x*.
- The output is **linear** over the central ±5 mm range; the **phase of the output** (0° or 180°) indicates the **direction** of displacement.

**Output Equation (1.5 marks):**

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{S_{1}} - V_{S_{2}} = K\,x$$

where *K* is the sensitivity (V/m). For a sinusoidal excitation V_p sin(ωt):

$$V_{\text{out}}(t) = K\,x\,\sin(\omega t)$$

**Phase-Sensitive Detection (1 mark):** A phase-sensitive demodulator compares the output’s phase with the primary excitation. By knowing whether the output is in-phase (core moved toward S₁) or anti-phase (core moved toward S₂), the circuit extracts **signed displacement** with full-wave rectification, eliminating residual null voltage.

#### Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** S_n = 8 mm.

**(i) Safe operating range (3 marks):**
As per the KTU standard mounting rule:

$$S_{r} = 0.81 \times S_{n} = 0.81 \times 8 = 6.48\ \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Maximum safe distance for stainless steel (4 marks):**
Stainless steel has a lower conductivity than mild steel, so its effective range is reduced by a correction factor. Applying the manufacturer’s factor of 0.85:

$$S_{r,\text{SS}} = 0.85 \times S_{r} = 0.85 \times 6.48 = 5.508\ \text{mm}$$

> **Final Answers:** **S_r = 6.48 mm**, **S_r(SS) ≈ 5.51 mm**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost 2–4 Marks Each in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide ultrasonic time by 2.** The wave travels to the target *and back*. Writing *d = v·t* instead of *d = v·t / 2* is the **#1 most-common error** in PECDT633.
> 2. **Using εᵣ = 1 for all capacitive problems** — forgetting that εᵣ for water ≈ 80, for oil ≈ 2.4, for glass ≈ 4.5. Always read the *material* in the question.
> 3. **Mixing up units** — *d* in metres, *t* in seconds, *A* in m². KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per unit slip.
> 4. **Inductive sensor with non-metallic target in the answer** — the examiner will mark you zero for the working if your example target is plastic/wood. Inductive sensors detect **only conductive** targets.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps in the derivation** — KTU valuation gives **partial credit per step**. A 7-mark derivation with only the final answer gets ≤ 1 mark. **Show the algebra line-by-line.**
> 6. **Confusing LVDT with RVDT** — LVDT is linear (translates to *x* in metres); RVDT is rotary (translates to *θ* in radians).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and stick it to your study wall before the KTU ESE.**

- [x] A **non-contact sensor** measures target properties through **fields or waves**, never by physical touch.
- [x] Six types: **Inductive, Capacitive, Hall-Effect, Photoelectric, Ultrasonic, Vision**.
- [x] **Inductive** → only detects **conductive / ferrous** targets via **eddy currents**; uses Faraday’s + Lenz’s law.
- [x] **Capacitive** → detects **all materials** (metal, plastic, liquid) by change in **C = ε₀εᵣA / d**.
- [x] **Hall Effect** → V_H = IB / (q n_s t); detects magnetic fields; used in **BLDC motor commutation**.
- [x] **Photoelectric** → three modes: **through-beam (long range), retro-reflective (mid), diffuse (short)**.
- [x] **Ultrasonic** → **d = v·t / 2**, v = 343 m/s in air at 20 °C, f = 40 kHz typical.
- [x] **LVDT** → V_out = K·x; linear in central range; **phase indicates direction** of core motion.
- [x] **Safe operating range rule:** **S_r ≤ 0.81 × S_n** (KTU mounting standard).
- [x] **Inductive coil inductance:** **L = N²μA / l**; impedance **X_L = 2πfL**.
- [x] **Switching frequency of inductive sensor:** up to **5 kHz**; **life**: 10⁸ to 10⁹ cycles.
- [x] Constants to memorize: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**, **v_sound = 343 m/s**, **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**, **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**, **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**.
- [x] **Always divide ultrasonic time by 2**; **always show full algebraic steps** in derivations; **always state the principle + one application** for 7-mark questions.
- [x] **KTU 2024 Scheme CO Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO2 — Apply knowledge of sensors and transducers for robotic systems** and **CO3 — Analyse signal conditioning and feedback architectures** of PECDT633.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Camera - CCD Camera - CMOS Camera - Omnidirectional Cameras, Sensor Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Camera - CCD Camera - CMOS Camera - Omnidirectional Cameras, Sensor Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Digital Camera in Robotics

**Formal Definition:**
A **Digital Camera** is an electro-optical imaging device that converts incident light from a scene into a discrete two-dimensional array of digital intensity values (pixels) by sampling the continuous light field through a photosensitive semiconductor sensor. In robotics, it functions as the primary **non-contact exteroceptive sensor**, enabling perception, localization, mapping (SLAM), and object recognition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Vision systems are the *eyes* of modern robots. A digital camera converts photons (light particles) into electrons (charge) and then into a binary image matrix that the robot controller can process.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a digital camera as an **electronic version of the human eye**:
- The **lens** acts like the eye's cornea and crystalline lens (focuses light).
- The **sensor (CCD/CMOS)** acts like the retina (converts light to electrical signals).
- The **A/D converter + processor** acts like the optic nerve and visual cortex (transmits and interprets signals).
- The **image memory** is analogous to short-term visual memory.

For a robot, instead of interpreting what it "sees" emotionally, the camera delivers a **grid of numbers** (e.g., 1920×1080), where each number represents the brightness (and color) of a small region of the scene.

---

### 1.2 CCD (Charge-Coupled Device) Camera

**Formal Definition:**
A **CCD Camera** uses a **Charge-Coupled Device** sensor where each pixel stores photo-generated charge in a potential well, and the accumulated charge is **sequentially shifted** (bucket-brigade style) across the array to a single output amplifier and A/D converter.

**Key Architectural Principle:**
- **Global Shutter:** All pixels are exposed simultaneously.
- **Charge Transfer:** Charge packets are moved from pixel to pixel in a serial register fashion.
- **Single Output Node:** High uniformity but slower readout.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a **bucket brigade of firefighters** passing water buckets along a line. Each firefighter (pixel) collects water (charge) from the fire, and the buckets are passed hand-to-hand to a single person who empties them into a measuring tank (output amplifier). This explains why CCDs have **superior image uniformity** but require **more power** and are **slower** than CMOS.

---

### 1.3 CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) Camera

**Formal Definition:**
A **CMOS Camera** uses an **Active Pixel Sensor (APS)** architecture where each pixel contains its own **dedicated amplifier and readout circuitry**, allowing parallel, per-pixel signal conversion. It is fabricated using standard CMOS semiconductor processes (the same used for CPUs and memory).

**Key Architectural Principle:**
- **Rolling Shutter (typical):** Rows are exposed sequentially.
- **Per-pixel amplification:** Faster readout, lower power.
- **On-chip integration:** ADC and processing can be embedded.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A CMOS sensor is like a **grid of independent microphones** at a concert. Each microphone (pixel) captures its own sound, amplifies it locally, and sends the signal to a central mixing board. They all work **simultaneously and independently**, making CMOS sensors **fast, power-efficient, and cheaper** to manufacture at scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Reality:** Over **99% of robotic vision systems** (drones, mobile robots, ADAS) today use CMOS sensors (e.g., Sony IMX, OmniVision OV series) due to cost, speed, and on-chip integration benefits.

---

### 1.4 Omnidirectional Cameras

**Formal Definition:**
An **Omnidirectional Camera** is a vision system capable of capturing a **hemispherical or full 360° field of view (FOV)** in a single image frame, typically using wide-angle lenses, fisheye optics, or mirrored catadioptric configurations.

**Two Main Categories:**

| Type | Principle | Example |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **Dioptric** | Ultra-wide lens (Fisheye) only | 180° fisheye lens |
| **Catadioptric** | Lens + curved mirror combination | Mirror + camera (e.g., Point Grey's Ladybug) |

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A regular camera is like looking through a **peephole** — you see only what is directly in front. An omnidirectional camera is like having **eyes on every side of your head simultaneously**, perfect for robot navigation in unknown environments.

---

### 1.5 Sensor Characteristics — Core Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Mandated Vocabulary:** Master these seven (7) parameters — they appear every year in KTU exams.

1. **Resolution:** Total number of pixels in the sensor array (e.g., **1920 × 1080 = 2.07 MP**).
2. **Pixel Size (Pitch):** Physical dimension of a single photosite, typically **1.4 µm to 10 µm**.
3. **Sensitivity:** Ability of a pixel to convert photons into electrons (measured in **V/lux·s**).
4. **Quantum Efficiency (QE):** Ratio of electrons produced to incident photons (0 < QE < 1, often **40%–80%**).
5. **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Ratio of signal power to noise power, expressed in **dB**.
6. **Dynamic Range:** Ratio of largest non-saturating signal to smallest detectable signal, expressed in **dB**.
7. **Frame Rate:** Number of full images captured per second (**fps**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Digital Camera — Block Architecture

Every digital camera in a robotic system follows a common processing pipeline:

1. **Light Capture** — Photons enter through the lens (controlled by aperture $f$-number).
2. **Photon-to-Electron Conversion** — Sensor absorbs photons and generates photoelectrons.
3. **Charge Accumulation** — Electrons are collected in potential wells during exposure time $t_{exp}$.
4. **Charge Readout** — Charge packets are transferred (CCD) or amplified in-pixel (CMOS).
5. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — Voltage is quantized to a digital number, typically **8-bit, 10-bit, or 12-bit**.
6. **Image Processing** — White balance, demosaicing, color correction, compression.
7. **Output Interface** — USB 3.0, GigE Vision, MIPI CSI, or Ethernet for robotic integration.

---

### 2.2 Pinhole Camera Model — Theoretical Foundation

The **pinhole model** describes image formation mathematically using projective geometry.

**Key Geometric Parameters:**
- Focal length $f$ (mm)
- Sensor width $w_s$ (mm) and height $h_s$ (mm)
- Distance from object $Z$ (mm)
- Object height $H$ (mm)
- Image height $h_i$ (mm)

**Why this matters for robotics:** This is the **mathematical backbone of camera calibration**, visual servoing, and structure-from-motion.

---

### 2.3 Field of View (FOV) — Detailed Explanation

The FOV is the angular extent of the scene that the camera can capture. It is determined by the **focal length** and the **sensor dimensions**.

**Why this matters:** A robot's task determines its required FOV:
- A **drone** may need a wide FOV (≥ 90°) for obstacle avoidance.
- A **pick-and-place arm** may need a narrow FOV (≤ 30°) for precision.
- A **mobile robot** may need **omnidirectional** FOV (360°).

---

### 2.4 Sensor Noise Sources (Why SNR Matters)

Three primary noise sources affect robotic vision:

1. **Photon Shot Noise** $\sigma_{photon} = \sqrt{N_{pe}}$ where $N_{pe}$ is the number of photoelectrons.
2. **Dark Current Noise** $\sigma_{dark} = \sqrt{N_{dark}}$ — thermally generated electrons.
3. **Readout Noise** $\sigma_{readout}$ — electronic noise from the amplifier and ADC.

**Total Noise:**
$$\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{\sigma_{photon}^2 + \sigma_{dark}^2 + \sigma_{readout}^2}$$

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table** — these are the equations KTU examiners test repeatedly.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Field of View (horizontal) | $FOV_h = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{w_s}{2f}\right)$ | degrees or radians | Critical for camera selection |
| 2 | Field of View (vertical) | $FOV_v = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\dfrac{h_s}{2f}\right)$ | degrees or radians | — |
| 3 | Image height (object projection) | $h_i = \dfrac{f \cdot H}{Z}$ | mm | Pinhole model |
| 4 | Sensor resolution | $N = w_{px} \cdot h_{px}$ | pixels | e.g., $1920 \times 1080$ |
| 5 | Pixel size (spatial resolution) | $\Delta x = \dfrac{w_s}{w_{px}} = \dfrac{h_s}{h_{px}}$ | µm/pixel | Smaller = higher detail |
| 6 | Ground Sampling Distance | $GSD = \dfrac{\Delta x \cdot Z}{f}$ | mm/pixel | Used in aerial robotics |
| 7 | Photoelectron count | $N_{pe} = \Phi \cdot A \cdot t_{exp} \cdot QE$ | electrons | $\Phi$ = photon flux |
| 8 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (linear) | $SNR = \dfrac{N_{pe}}{\sigma_{total}}$ | dimensionless | Ratio form |
| 9 | SNR in dB | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_{pe}}{\sigma_{total}}\right)$ | dB | Standard metric |
| 10 | Dynamic Range (linear) | $DR = \dfrac{S_{max}}{S_{min}}$ | dimensionless | — |
| 11 | Dynamic Range (dB) | $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{S_{max}}{S_{min}}\right)$ | dB | Typically 60–100 dB |
| 12 | ADC bit depth to DR | $DR_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot N_{bits} + 1.76$ | dB | 8-bit ≈ 49.9 dB, 12-bit ≈ 74 dB |
| 13 | Frame period | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{1}{FPS}$ | seconds | — |
| 14 | Catadioptric FOV | $FOV_{cat} = 2\pi \left(1 - \cos\alpha\right)$ | steradians | $\alpha$ = half-angle of mirror |
| 15 | Number of gray levels | $L = 2^{N_{bits}}$ | levels | 8-bit → 256 levels |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in:
> - **Autonomous Vehicles:** Computing FOV for lane detection and obstacle avoidance.
> - **Industrial Inspection:** GSD calculation for defect detection.
> - **Drone Photogrammetry:** Determining flight altitude for a required ground resolution.
> - **Surgical Robotics:** SNR and dynamic range are critical for low-light endoscopic imaging.

---

### 2.6 CCD vs. CMOS — Detailed Operational Comparison

| Parameter | CCD | CMOS |
|-----------|-----|------|
| **Signal Output** | Charge transferred serially | Voltage per pixel |
| **Shutter Type** | Global shutter (standard) | Rolling shutter (standard) |
| **Readout Speed** | Slower (sequential transfer) | Faster (parallel) |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher (10–100× CMOS) | Lower (ideal for battery robots) |
| **Noise Performance** | Lower noise, higher uniformity | Higher noise, fixed-pattern noise |
| **Dynamic Range** | Higher (70–90 dB typical) | Lower (50–70 dB typical) |
| **Manufacturing Cost** | Expensive (special process) | Cheap (standard CMOS process) |
| **On-chip Integration** | Limited | High (ADC, ISP on chip) |
| **Typical Applications** | Scientific imaging, astronomy | Smartphones, robots, automotive |
| **Sensitivity (QE)** | Up to 90%+ | 60–80% (improving) |
| **Blooming Effect** | Susceptible | Less susceptible |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to choose a sensor for a *mobile robot*, the answer is **CMOS** (power + speed). When asked for *scientific/slow-precision imaging*, the answer is **CCD** (uniformity + low noise).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Horizontal Field of View from Pinhole Geometry

**Given:** A rectangular sensor of width $w_s$ and a lens of focal length $f$.

**Goal:** Derive the horizontal field of view $FOV_h$.

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup:**
By the similar triangles principle, the image plane is at distance $f$ from the pinhole. The left and right edges of the sensor subtend angles $\pm \theta$ at the pinhole, where:

$$\tan \theta = \frac{w_s / 2}{f}$$

**Step 2 — Total Angular Span:**
The horizontal field of view is twice this half-angle:

$$FOV_h = 2\theta$$

**Step 3 — Combining Equations:**
Substituting the expression for $\theta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FOV_h &= 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{w_s}{2f}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical Conversion to Degrees:**
Since KTU often asks for the answer in degrees:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FOV_h^{\circ} &= \frac{180}{\pi} \cdot 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{w_s}{2f}\right) \\
&= \frac{360}{\pi} \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{w_s}{2f}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style problem):**
A robotic vision camera has a sensor of width $w_s = 5.6$ mm and a lens with focal length $f = 3.5$ mm. Calculate the horizontal FOV.

$$
\begin{aligned}
FOV_h &= 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{5.6}{2 \cdot 3.5}\right) \\
&= 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{5.6}{7.0}\right) \\
&= 2 \cdot \arctan(0.8) \\
&= 2 \cdot 38.66^{\circ} \\
&= 77.31^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is a **wide-angle FOV**, suitable for mobile robot navigation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Ground Sampling Distance (GSD) for Drone/Aerial Robotics

**Given:** A camera at altitude $Z$ above the ground with focal length $f$ and pixel pitch $\Delta x$ (in mm).

**Goal:** Determine the on-ground distance covered by a single pixel.

**Step 1 — Similar Triangles Relation:**
The physical dimension on the ground $G$ corresponding to one pixel relates to the image-plane dimension $\Delta x$ by:

$$\frac{G}{Z} = \frac{\Delta x}{f}$$

**Step 2 — Solving for G:**
Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= \frac{Z \cdot \Delta x}{f}
\end{aligned}
$$

This quantity is the **Ground Sampling Distance (GSD)**.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:**
A drone flies at $Z = 100$ m altitude, with a camera having $f = 8$ mm lens and $\Delta x = 2.4$ µm $= 2.4 \times 10^{-3}$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
GSD &= \frac{100 \cdot 2.4 \times 10^{-3}}{8} \\
&= \frac{0.24}{8} \\
&= 0.03 \text{ m/pixel} \\
&= 3 \text{ cm/pixel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each pixel in the captured image represents a **3 cm × 3 cm square on the ground** — this defines the smallest object that can be detected.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Dynamic Range from ADC Bit Depth

**Given:** An image sensor uses an $N$-bit Analog-to-Digital Converter.

**Goal:** Determine the theoretical dynamic range.

**Step 1 — Output Levels:**
The ADC produces $2^N$ discrete gray levels from 0 to $2^N - 1$.

**Step 2 — Minimum Detectable Signal:**
Quantization noise sets the minimum detectable signal at approximately $\frac{1}{2} \text{ LSB}$ (least significant bit), where $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$ and $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale voltage.

**Step 3 — Dynamic Range in Linear Units:**
$$DR = \frac{V_{FS}}{V_{LSB}} = \frac{V_{FS}}{V_{FS} / 2^N} = 2^N$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to dB:**
For power (or amplitude-squared) signals, $20 \log_{10}$ is used; for amplitude, $20 \log_{10}$ is standard:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}(2^N) \\
&= 20 N \log_{10}(2) \\
&= 20 N \cdot 0.30103 \\
&\approx 6.02 N + 1.76 \text{ dB (with quantization correction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**
For a 12-bit ADC:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR_{dB} &= 6.02 \cdot 12 + 1.76 \\
&= 72.24 + 1.76 \\
&= 74.0 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 12-bit camera can distinguish a bright region **5011 times brighter** than the dimmest detectable region. This is critical for **HDR robotic vision** (tunnel exits, welding, outdoor scenes).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Signal-to-Noise Ratio for a Pixel

**Given:** A pixel accumulates $N_{pe}$ photoelectrons, with dark current $N_{dark}$ and readout noise $\sigma_{read}$.

**Goal:** Derive the SNR.

**Step 1 — Signal:**
The signal is the photoelectron count: $S = N_{pe}$.

**Step 2 — Noise Components:**
- Photon shot noise: $\sigma_{photon} = \sqrt{N_{pe}}$
- Dark shot noise: $\sigma_{dark} = \sqrt{N_{dark}}$
- Readout noise: $\sigma_{readout}$ (RMS electrons)

**Step 3 — Total Noise (uncorrelated sources add in quadrature):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{total} &= \sqrt{\sigma_{photon}^2 + \sigma_{dark}^2 + \sigma_{readout}^2} \\
&= \sqrt{N_{pe} + N_{dark} + \sigma_{readout}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — SNR in Linear Form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SNR &= \frac{N_{pe}}{\sigma_{total}} \\
&= \frac{N_{pe}}{\sqrt{N_{pe} + N_{dark} + \sigma_{readout}^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — SNR in dB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SNR_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_{pe}}{\sqrt{N_{pe} + N_{dark} + \sigma_{readout}^2}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Shot-Noise-Limited Case (high light):**
When $N_{pe} \gg N_{dark}$ and $N_{pe} \gg \sigma_{readout}^2$:

$$SNR \approx \frac{N_{pe}}{\sqrt{N_{pe}}} = \sqrt{N_{pe}}$$

This is the **fundamental physical limit** of imaging — you cannot beat shot noise.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: FOV Selection for Robotic Tasks

| Robotic Task | Required FOV | Lens Type | Typical Sensor | Bit Depth |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| **SLAM (indoor mobile robot)** | 180°–360° | Fisheye / Catadioptric | CMOS 2 MP | 10-bit |
| **Pick-and-Place (arm)** | 20°–40° | Telephoto | CCD 5 MP | 12-bit |
| **Drone Obstacle Avoidance** | 90°–120° | Wide-angle | CMOS 1 MP | 10-bit |
| **Autonomous Vehicle Front Camera** | 60°–90° | Wide | CMOS 8 MP HDR | 12-bit HDR |
| **Aerial Mapping (Survey)** | 60°–80° | Standard | CMOS 20–60 MP | 14-bit |
| **Endoscopic Surgery** | 70°–110° | Rod-lens | CMOS 4K | 10-bit |
| **Agricultural Weeding Robot** | 120°–180° | Fisheye | CMOS 5 MP | 12-bit |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Digital Camera Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Scene Light Photons] --> B[Lens System with Aperture and Focus]
    B --> C[Sensor Array CCD or CMOS]
    C --> D[Charge Accumulation in Pixels]
    D --> E[Analog Amplifier]
    E --> F[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    F --> G[Image Signal Processor ISP]
    G --> H[Image Buffer Memory]
    H --> I[Output Interface USB GigE MIPI]
    I --> J[Robot Controller for Processing]

    classDef hw fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef opt fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#7d4f00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class A,B hw
    class C,D,E opt
    class F,G,H,I,J proc
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Blue (hw):** Physical optics — scene and lens.
- **Green (opt):** Sensor-side processing — light to voltage.
- **Brown (proc):** Digital-side processing — voltage to robot data.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: CCD Readout Architecture (Serial Transfer)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PX[Pixel Array M x N]
        P1[Pixel Row 1] --> P2[Pixel Row 2]
        P2 --> P3[Pixel Row N]
    end
    PX --> REG[Vertical Shift Register]
    REG --> HREG[Horizontal Shift Register]
    HREG --> AMP[Single Output Amplifier]
    AMP --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> OUT[Digital Image Stream]

    classDef array fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef reg fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#7d4f00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class P1,P2,P3 array
    class REG,HREG,AMP reg
    class ADC,OUT out
```

**Key Insight:** Only **one amplifier and one ADC** serve the entire array, ensuring **uniform gain and low noise** but limiting **readout speed**.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: CMOS Active Pixel Sensor (APS) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROW1[Row 1]
        P1A[Pixel 1 Photodiode] --- P1B[In-Pixel Amplifier]
    end
    subgraph ROW2[Row 2]
        P2A[Pixel 2 Photodiode] --- P2B[In-Pixel Amplifier]
    end
    subgraph ROW3[Row 3]
        P3A[Pixel 3 Photodiode] --- P3B[In-Pixel Amplifier]
    end
    ROW1 --> COL[Column ADC and MUX]
    ROW2 --> COL
    ROW3 --> COL
    COL --> CTRL[Timing and Control Logic]
    CTRL --> OUT[Digital Image Output]

    classDef pix fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef col fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrl fill:#7d4f00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class P1A,P1B,P2A,P2B,P3A,P3B pix
    class COL col
    class CTRL,OUT ctrl
```

**Key Insight:** Each pixel has its **own amplifier** and column ADCs work in **parallel** — this is why CMOS is **fast, low-power, and scalable**.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Omnidirectional Camera System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SC[360 Degree Surrounding Scene] --> M[Convex Hyperbolic Mirror]
    M --> L[Standard Camera Lens]
    L --> S[CCD or CMOS Sensor]
    S --> IMG[Single Panoramic Image with Distortion]
    IMG --> UNW[Software Unwrapping Algorithm]
    UNW --> RM[Rectangular 360 Degree Map]
    RM --> ROB[Robot Localization and Mapping]

    classDef scene fill:#7d4f00,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef opt fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef sw fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class SC scene
    class M,L,S opt
    class IMG,UNW,RM,ROB sw
```

**Key Insight:** A single catadioptric image covers the **entire sphere** around the robot, which is invaluable for **visual SLAM, surveillance, and warehouse robotics**.

---

### 4.5 Comparison Schematic: CCD vs. CMOS Pixel Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CCDP[CCD Pixel]
        CCD1[Photodiode] --> CCD2[Charge Transfer Gate]
        CCD2 --> CCD3[Shared Vertical Register]
    end
    subgraph CMOSP[CMOS Pixel APS]
        CMOS1[Photodiode] --> CMOS2[Reset Transistor]
        CMOS2 --> CMOS3[Source Follower Amplifier]
        CMOS3 --> CMOS4[Row Select Transistor]
    end

    classDef ccd fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef cmos fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class CCD1,CCD2,CCD3 ccd
    class CMOS1,CMOS2,CMOS3,CMOS4 cmos
```

**Reading:** CCD has **3 elements** per pixel; CMOS has **4 elements** (amplifier added). The extra amplifier in CMOS gives **speed and per-pixel gain** but reduces **fill factor** (light-sensitive area) unless back-side illumination is used.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)] (3 Marks)**
**Differentiate between CCD and CMOS image sensors based on architecture, power consumption, and typical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | CCD | CMOS |
|-----------|-----|------|
| **Architecture** | Charge shifted serially to a single output node | Each pixel has its own amplifier (Active Pixel Sensor) |
| **Power Consumption** | High (requires clock drives for charge transfer) | Low (10–100× less than CCD) |
| **Readout Speed** | Slower (sequential) | Faster (parallel column ADCs) |
| **Noise** | Low, uniform | Higher, fixed-pattern noise |
| **Typical Applications** | Scientific imaging, astronomy, medical X-ray | Smartphones, robotics, automotive cameras, IoT |

**Conclusion:** CCDs are preferred where **uniformity and low noise** are paramount, whereas CMOS sensors dominate in **mobile, battery-powered, and high-speed** robotic applications.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)] (3 Marks)**
**Define the following sensor characteristics: (i) Quantum Efficiency, (ii) Dynamic Range, (iii) Pixel Pitch.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Quantum Efficiency (QE):**
Quantum efficiency is the ratio of the number of photoelectrons generated to the number of incident photons on a pixel, expressed as a percentage. A QE of 60% means 60% of incoming photons are converted to electrons.

$$\text{QE} = \frac{\text{Number of photoelectrons generated}}{\text{Number of incident photons}}$$

**(ii) Dynamic Range (DR):**
Dynamic range is the ratio of the maximum non-saturating signal a pixel can record to the minimum detectable signal, typically expressed in dB.

$$DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S_{max}}{S_{min}}\right)$$

**(iii) Pixel Pitch:**
Pixel pitch is the physical distance between the centers of two adjacent pixels, measured in micrometers (µm). Smaller pitch → higher spatial resolution but lower sensitivity per pixel.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice-Based)

---

#### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]*

**Q.A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a digital camera. Discuss the role of the lens, sensor, and ADC in the imaging pipeline. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A digital camera converts optical information into a digital image through a structured pipeline:

**1. Lens System (Optical Stage):**
The lens collects light from the scene and focuses it onto the sensor. The **aperture** controls the amount of light (f-number), and the **focal length** $f$ determines the field of view:

$$FOV = 2 \arctan\left(\frac{w_s}{2f}\right)$$

The focus is adjusted to bring objects at the working distance into sharp focus on the sensor plane.

**2. Image Sensor (Photon-to-Electron Stage):**
The sensor (CCD or CMOS) contains a 2D array of photodiodes. When photons strike a photodiode, they generate electron-hole pairs proportional to the local light intensity. The number of electrons generated per pixel:

$$N_{pe} = \Phi \cdot A \cdot t_{exp} \cdot QE$$

where $\Phi$ = photon flux, $A$ = pixel area, $t_{exp}$ = exposure time, $QE$ = quantum efficiency.

**3. Readout Electronics (Charge-to-Voltage Stage):**
- **CCD:** Charge packets shifted serially to a common amplifier.
- **CMOS:** Each pixel has its own source-follower amplifier.

**4. Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):**
The voltage signal is quantized into discrete levels. An $N$-bit ADC produces $2^N$ gray levels (e.g., 8-bit = 256 levels). The ADC bit depth determines the dynamic range:

$$DR_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76$$

**5. Image Signal Processor (ISP):**
Performs demosaicing, white balance, gamma correction, noise reduction, and color conversion (e.g., Bayer → RGB).

**6. Output Interface:**
Sends the digital image to the robot controller via USB 3.0, GigE Vision, MIPI CSI-2, or Ethernet.

**Block Diagram Reference:** (See SECTION 4.1 of these notes.)

---

**Q.A. (b)** A robotic vision system uses a CMOS sensor with a focal length of $8$ mm and a sensor width of $5.6$ mm. The sensor has a resolution of $1920 \times 1080$ pixels.
   (i) Calculate the horizontal Field of View.
   (ii) Determine the pixel pitch in micrometers.
   (iii) Compute the theoretical dynamic range in dB for a 10-bit ADC. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Horizontal Field of View:**
Given: $f = 8$ mm, $w_s = 5.6$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
FOV_h &= 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{w_s}{2f}\right) \\
&= 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{5.6}{2 \times 8}\right) \\
&= 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{5.6}{16}\right) \\
&= 2 \cdot \arctan(0.35) \\
&= 2 \cdot 19.29^{\circ} \\
&= 38.58^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $FOV_h \approx 38.58^{\circ}$

**(ii) Pixel Pitch:**
Given: Sensor width $= 5.6$ mm, Horizontal resolution $= 1920$ pixels.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x &= \frac{w_s}{w_{px}} = \frac{5.6 \text{ mm}}{1920} \\
&= 2.917 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} \\
&= 2.917 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $\Delta x \approx 2.92$ µm

**(iii) Dynamic Range for 10-bit ADC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DR_{dB} &= 6.02 \cdot N + 1.76 \\
&= 6.02 \cdot 10 + 1.76 \\
&= 60.2 + 1.76 \\
&= 61.96 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $DR \approx 61.96$ dB

---

#### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the working of an omnidirectional camera. Discuss its advantages in mobile robotics and give example applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition and Working Principle:**
An omnidirectional camera is a vision system that captures a **hemispherical or full 360° field of view** in a single image frame. There are two primary configurations:

**(a) Catadioptric System (Mirror + Lens):**
- A **convex mirror** (typically hyperbolic or parabolic) is placed above a standard camera lens.
- Light from the entire surrounding hemisphere reflects off the mirror into the camera.
- The captured image is a circular annular pattern that, after **mathematical unwrapping**, yields a panoramic 360° × 180° view.

**(b) Dioptric System (Ultra-Wide Lens / Fisheye):**
- Uses a single ultra-wide-angle lens (e.g., 180° fisheye).
- More compact, but suffers from severe **barrel distortion** that must be corrected in software.

**Mathematical Representation (Catadioptric FOV):**
For a mirror of half-angle $\alpha$ from the optical axis:
$$FOV_{solid} = 2\pi (1 - \cos \alpha) \text{ steradians}$$

**Advantages in Mobile Robotics:**
1. **Single-camera SLAM** — No need to rotate the camera; one image provides full situational awareness.
2. **No moving parts** — Higher reliability, no mechanical wear.
3. **Simultaneous obstacle detection** in all directions.
4. **Reduced algorithmic complexity** for landmark tracking (no frame-to-frame alignment issues).
5. **Lower data rate requirements** for full coverage compared to multiple cameras.

**Example Applications:**
- **Autonomous warehouse robots** (e.g., Kiva/Amazon systems) for localization using ceiling features.
- **Service robots** in hospitals and hotels for navigation.
- **Surveillance and security robots.**
- **Planetary rovers** (NASA's Mars rovers use panoramic cameras for terrain mapping).
- **Drones** for 360° video capture and obstacle avoidance.

**Block Diagram Reference:** (See SECTION 4.4 of these notes.)

---

**Q.B. (b)** Define and explain the following sensor characteristics with their engineering significance in robotics: (i) Quantum Efficiency, (ii) Dynamic Range, (iii) Signal-to-Noise Ratio, (iv) Frame Rate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Quantum Efficiency (QE):**
QE is the fraction of incident photons that successfully generate photoelectrons in a pixel. It is a dimensionless quantity (0 to 1) or percentage (0–100%).

$$QE = \frac{\text{Number of photoelectrons generated}}{\text{Number of incident photons}}$$

**Significance:** Higher QE → better performance in low-light robotic applications (e.g., night-time surveillance, mining robots, planetary rovers). A QE of 80% means only 20% of photons are "wasted."

**(ii) Dynamic Range (DR):**
DR is the ratio between the brightest (non-saturating) and dimmest (just-detectable) signal levels a sensor can record.

$$DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S_{max}}{S_{min}}\right)$$

**Significance:** Critical for **HDR scenes** such as autonomous driving (tunnel exits, glare from headlights, shadows). A DR of 74 dB (12-bit) can handle a brightness ratio of 5011:1.

**(iii) Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):**
SNR is the ratio of the signal power to the noise power at a pixel.

$$SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_{pe}}{\sigma_{total}}\right) \approx 20 \log_{10}\left(\sqrt{N_{pe}}\right)$$

The **shot-noise limit** is the physical floor: $SNR = \sqrt{N_{pe}}$ for shot-noise-dominated imaging.

**Significance:** Determines the **detectability of small features** in machine vision. A low SNR leads to missed defects in industrial inspection. SNR > 30 dB is generally required for reliable robotic perception.

**(iv) Frame Rate:**
Frame rate is the number of complete images the camera outputs per second, measured in **frames per second (fps)**.

$$T_{frame} = \frac{1}{FPS}$$

**Significance:**
- **30–60 fps** — Standard robotic navigation and pick-and-place.
- **120–240 fps** — High-speed tracking (e.g., ball-catching robots, drone racing).
- **> 1000 fps** — Scientific high-speed vision (vibration analysis, crash testing).

Higher frame rates enable **smoother control loops** in visual servoing but require **more bandwidth** and **more processing power** on the robot controller.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in FOV calculations** — Always state "degrees" or "radians" in the final answer. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
>
> 2. **Confusing Pixel Size with Sensor Size** — Pixel pitch ($\Delta x$) is in **µm**, sensor dimension is in **mm**. Do not mix them in the same calculation without unit conversion ($1 \text{ mm} = 1000 \text{ µm}$).
>
> 3. **Wrong formula for DR in dB** — Use $20 \log_{10}$ for **amplitude/ratio** quantities (signal, pixel value, light intensity). Use $10 \log_{10}$ only for **power** quantities. KTU examiners test this distinction.
>
> 4. **Claiming CCD is always better than CMOS** — This is **outdated**. In robotic applications, **CMOS is the industry standard** today. Always justify based on the application.
>
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram** — In 7-mark questions on camera working, **a neat block diagram is mandatory**. Missing it typically costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Not explaining shutter type difference** — "Global shutter" (CCD/CMOS with global reset) vs. "rolling shutter" (most CMOS). Global shutter is critical for **fast-moving robot arms and drones** to avoid motion distortion.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to mention the FOV derivation** — Examiners expect the **pinhole model derivation** with $\tan \theta = \frac{w_s/2}{f}$ for full marks in FOV numericals.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Digital Cameras**

- [x] **Digital Camera Pipeline:** Lens → Sensor → Amplifier → ADC → ISP → Output.
- [x] **CCD = Charge-Coupled Device** with **serial readout** and **global shutter** — high uniformity, high power.
- [x] **CMOS = Active Pixel Sensor** with **parallel readout** and **rolling shutter** — fast, low-power, dominant in robotics.
- [x] **Omnidirectional Cameras** = Catadioptric (mirror+lens) or Dioptric (fisheye), give 360° FOV.
- [x] **7 Critical Sensor Characteristics:** Resolution, Pixel Size, Sensitivity, QE, SNR, Dynamic Range, Frame Rate.
- [x] **Master Formula:** $FOV_h = 2 \arctan\left(\dfrac{w_s}{2f}\right)$ — appears in nearly every KTU paper.
- [x] **Dynamic Range from ADC bits:** $DR_{dB} \approx 6.02N + 1.76$ — memorize this constant ($6.02$).
- [x] **Shot Noise Limit:** $SNR_{max} = \sqrt{N_{pe}}$ — fundamental physical limit of imaging.
- [x] **GSD Formula** (for drones/aerial robotics): $GSD = \dfrac{Z \cdot \Delta x}{f}$ — high-yield for Part B.
- [x] **Robot Selection Rule:** Mobile/Power-Constrained Robot → **CMOS**; Scientific/Low-Noise Imaging → **CCD**.
- [x] **Omnidirectional FOV:** Catadioptric uses single mirror + camera, captures entire hemisphere in one frame.
- [x] **ADC Bit Depth to Gray Levels:** $L = 2^N$ (e.g., 8-bit = 256 levels).
- [x] **Frame Period:** $T_{frame} = 1/fps$ — important for real-time control loop design.
- [x] **Always** include units (µm, mm, dB, fps) in numerical answers.
- [x] **Always** draw a block diagram in 7-mark descriptive answers.
- [x] **Remember:** Quantum Efficiency (QE) is always $\leq 1$ (or $\leq 100\%$) — never exceed unity.
- [x] **Industrial Reality:** Over 99% of modern robotic vision uses CMOS sensors (Sony IMX, OmniVision series).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators - DC Motors - H-Bridge - Pulse Width Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Actuators - DC Motors - H-Bridge - Pulse Width Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Actuators

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
An **Actuator** is a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts a control signal (typically electrical energy) into a physical motion — linear or rotary — to perform a useful task in a robotic system. It is the *muscle* of a robot, generating the force, torque, or displacement required to move links, grippers, wheels, or tools.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body:**
Think of a robot as a human body. The **brain** (microcontroller/PLC) sends electrical signals through **nerves** (wires). The **muscles** (actuators) then contract and relax to move the **skeleton** (mechanical links). Without strong, controllable muscles, even the smartest brain cannot make the body move. Similarly, no robot can function without well-designed actuators that respond precisely to control commands.

**Three Principal Families of Actuators:**

| Family | Energy Domain | Typical Use in Robotics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Electric Actuators** | Electrical → Mechanical | DC Motors, Stepper Motors, Servo Motors (most common in mobile & industrial robots) |
| **Hydraulic Actuators** | Fluid Pressure → Mechanical | Heavy-payload robots, excavators, humanoids (e.g., Boston Dynamics Atlas) |
| **Pneumatic Actuators** | Compressed Air → Mechanical | Pick-and-place grippers, soft robotics, low-cost automation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the PECDT633 syllabus, *electric actuators* — and in particular the **brushed DC Motor** — are treated as the foundational actuator because they are mathematically tractable, inexpensive, and form the conceptual bridge to stepper and BLDC drives covered in higher semesters.

**Key Performance Metrics of an Actuator (must-know for exams):**
- **Torque ($T$):** Rotational force, measured in **N·m**.
- **Speed ($N$ or $\omega$):** Rotational velocity, measured in **rpm** or **rad/s**.
- **Power ($P$):** Rate of doing work, measured in **W** ($P = T \cdot \omega$).
- **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Ratio of output mechanical power to input electrical power, expressed in **%**.

---

## 1.2 DC Motor

**Formal Definition:**
A **DC (Direct Current) Motor** is an electromechanical energy-conversion device that operates on the principle of the **Lorentz Force** — a current-carrying conductor placed within a magnetic field experiences a force, which produces a continuous rotary motion when commutated.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Top with Magnets:**
Imagine holding a battery connected to a wire loop placed between two magnets. The current creates a magnetic field around the loop that *opposes* one magnet and *attracts* the other, causing the loop to rotate. To keep the loop spinning, a clever switch called a **commutator** flips the current direction every half-turn so the force always pushes in the same rotational sense. That switch is the heart of every brushed DC motor.

**Types of DC Motors used in Robotics:**
1. **Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) Motor** — Compact, simple speed control; ideal for small robots and wheels.
2. **Shunt DC Motor** — Stable speed, used in CNC machines.
3. **Series DC Motor** — High starting torque, used in traction (rare in modern robotics).
4. **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** — Electronic commutation, high efficiency, used in drones and modern servo systems.

---

## 1.3 H-Bridge

**Formal Definition:**
An **H-Bridge** is an electronic switching circuit composed of **four switching elements** (transistors / MOSFETs / IGBTs) arranged in an *H*-topology, used to **drive a DC motor in both directions** from a single DC supply by selectively energising diagonal switch pairs.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Turnstile:**
Imagine a one-way train track. To reverse the train's direction, you cannot simply push it backward — you must reroute it onto a parallel track going the opposite way. An H-Bridge is like a **four-switch railway junction** that swaps the polarity of the voltage applied to the motor terminals, making it spin clockwise *or* counter-clockwise on demand.

---

## 1.4 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)

**Formal Definition:**
**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a digital signalling technique in which a rectangular pulse train of constant frequency and constant amplitude has its **ON-time (pulse width)** varied in proportion to a control command. The **average (DC equivalent) voltage** seen by a load is therefore adjustable between 0% and 100% of the supply rail, enabling efficient speed / torque control of a DC motor.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Flickering Lamp Dimmer:**
Instead of feeding a 24 V bulb with a constant 12 V (which wastes 12 V as heat in a series resistor), a dimmer *blinks* the bulb at full 24 V but keeps it ON for only half the time. The filament does not cool fully between blinks, so the **average brightness** is half — yet the *efficiency* is near 100% because the transistor switch is either fully ON or fully OFF. PWM applies exactly this trick to motors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PWM instead of a variable resistor (rheostat)?** A rheostat dissipates the unwanted voltage as **I²R heat** — terribly inefficient. A PWM switch dissipates almost nothing when fully ON (zero voltage drop) or fully OFF (zero current). Efficiencies above **90%** are routinely achieved in PWM-driven H-Bridge motor drivers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of a Brushed DC Motor

The motor consists of:
- **Stator:** Permanent magnet (or field winding) producing a radial magnetic flux density $B$ (in **Tesla**).
- **Rotor (Armature):** A coil of $N$ turns carrying current $I_a$ on a shaft, sitting in the magnetic field.
- **Commutator & Brushes:** A mechanical rectifier that reverses armature current every half-turn to maintain unidirectional torque.

**Force on a single conductor:**

$$F = B \cdot I_a \cdot L$$

where $L$ is the active length of the conductor inside the field (in **metres**). With $N$ turns and effective radius $r$, the developed electromagnetic torque is:

$$T = N \cdot B \cdot I_a \cdot L \cdot r = K_t \cdot I_a$$

**Back-EMF (Counter-Electromotive Force):**
As the armature spins in the magnetic field, it acts as a generator and produces an opposing voltage:

$$E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$$

This is **Lenz's Law** in action — the motor's own rotation generates a voltage that *fights* the supply.

**Armature Circuit Equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law):**

$$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a + L_a \cdot \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$

In **steady state** ($\frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$):

$$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$$

**Combining to obtain the Speed–Torque Characteristic:**

$$V = K_b \cdot \omega + \frac{R_a}{K_t} \cdot T$$

Solving for speed:

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{V}{K_b} - \frac{R_a}{K_b \cdot K_t} \cdot T}$$

This is the **linear drop** of speed with load torque — a fundamental result the KTU examiner loves to test.

> [!NOTE]
> In **SI units**, the voltage constant $K_b$ and torque constant $K_t$ are numerically equal: $K_b = K_t$ (with appropriate units). Many textbooks use the same symbol $K$ for both.

---

## 2.2 H-Bridge — Operational States

The H-Bridge is composed of **four switches** labelled $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ arranged in two half-legs. The motor sits across the centre tap of each leg.

| Switch State | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $S_4$ | Motor Behaviour |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Forward (CW)** | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | Current flows A → B; motor turns CW |
| **Reverse (CCW)** | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | Current flows B → A; motor turns CCW |
| **Dynamic Brake** | OFF | ON | OFF | ON | Motor terminals shorted; quick stop |
| **Regen Brake (Low-Side)** | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | Acts as generator into supply |
| **Coast (Freewheel)** | OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | Motor spins freely, decelerates only by friction |
| **SHOOT-THROUGH (FORBIDDEN)** | ON | ON | – | – | **Direct short across supply → catastrophic failure** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Safety Pitfall:** The **shoot-through** condition (both switches in the same leg ON simultaneously) creates a direct short between $V_{CC}$ and GND through the transistors. Even a microsecond overlap can destroy the bridge. This is why *dead-time insertion* of 100–500 ns is mandatory in every professional H-Bridge driver IC.

**Popular H-Bridge Driver ICs:**

| IC | Channels | Continuous Current | Voltage Range | KTU Relevance |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **L293D** | 2 | 600 mA | 4.5 – 36 V | Most cited in KTU labs |
| **L298N** | 2 | 2 A | 5 – 46 V | Higher-power lab projects |
