| **DRV8871** | 1 | 3.6 A | 6.5 – 45 V | Modern low-Rdson variant |
| **BTS7960** | 1 (half) | 43 A | 5.5 – 27 V | High-torque robotic joints |

---

## 2.3 Pulse Width Modulation — Mathematical Treatment

A PWM waveform is fully described by three parameters:

1. **Period $T$** (seconds) — time of one complete ON–OFF cycle.
2. **Frequency $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$** (Hz) — typically **1 kHz to 25 kHz** for DC motors.
3. **Duty Cycle $D$** (dimensionless, expressed as %) — fraction of the period during which the signal is HIGH.

$$\boxed{D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T} = T_{ON} \cdot f}$$

**Average (DC-equivalent) voltage delivered to the load:**

$$\boxed{V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{CC}}$$

**R.M.S. voltage (for thermal/ripple analysis):**

$$V_{rms} = V_{CC} \cdot \sqrt{D}$$

**Effective motor speed under PWM:**

$$\omega_{PWM} \approx D \cdot \omega_{max}$$

where $\omega_{max}$ is the no-load speed at full supply $V_{CC}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why high PWM frequency?** Above ~20 kHz, the switching noise lies *above* the human audible range, eliminating the high-pitched whine. The motor's mechanical inertia (rotor mass) integrates the pulses so the shaft sees a smooth average torque.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque from armature current | $T$ in N·m, $I_a$ in A |
| 2 | $E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF induced by rotation | $E_b$ in V, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 3 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature voltage (steady state) | All in SI |
| 4 | $\omega = \dfrac{V}{K_b} - \dfrac{R_a}{K_b K_t} T$ | Speed–torque line | $\omega$ in rad/s, $T$ in N·m |
| 5 | $K_b = K_t$ (SI units) | Equivalence of constants | V·s/rad $\equiv$ N·m/A |
| 6 | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical output power | W |
| 7 | $P_{elec} = V \cdot I_a$ | Electrical input power | W |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{P_{elec}} \times 100$ | Efficiency | % |
| 9 | $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T}$ | PWM duty cycle | dimensionless or % |
| 10 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{CC}$ | Equivalent DC voltage from PWM | V |
| 11 | $V_{rms} = V_{CC} \sqrt{D}$ | R.M.S. of PWM signal | V |
| 12 | $\omega_{PWM} \approx D \cdot \omega_{max}$ | Approx. motor speed under PWM | rad/s |
| 13 | $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{60 \, \omega}{2\pi}$ | Conversion rad/s → rpm | rpm |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Industry Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **DC Motor + H-Bridge + PWM** | **Drone propellers** (BLDC + ESC), **EV traction** (Tesla inverter), **CNC spindle drives**, **robotic wheel chairs**, **3-D printer extruders**, **Mars-rover wheel actuators** (Curiosity uses brushed DC gear-motors with PWM). |
| Speed control by PWM | Allows the *same* motor to operate at *any* speed without rewinding or using rheostats — a foundational concept in **variable-frequency drives (VFDs)**. |
| H-Bridge topology | The very same topology, scaled up to high-voltage IGBTs, forms the building block of **three-phase inverters** used in industrial drives and solar micro-inverters. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Speed–Torque Equation of a DC Motor

This derivation is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question** and is reproduced here in exhaustive, line-by-line form.

**Step 1 — Write the force on a current-carrying conductor.**

A single straight conductor of active length $L$ (in metres) carrying current $I_a$ (in amperes) in a magnetic flux density $B$ (in tesla) experiences a force:

$$F = B \cdot I_a \cdot L$$

This is the **Lorentz force** $F = I \, L \times B$, written for the perpendicular-field case.

**Step 2 — Convert linear force to torque.**

If the conductors are wound on a cylindrical armature of effective radius $r$ and there are $N$ active conductors contributing to torque:

$$T = N \cdot B \cdot I_a \cdot L \cdot r$$

Group the geometric terms into a single constant $K_t$ (torque constant):

$$K_t = N \cdot B \cdot L \cdot r$$

Therefore:

$$T = K_t \cdot I_a \qquad \text{...(Equation A)}$$

**Step 3 — Express the back-EMF.**

As the armature rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s) inside the same flux $B$, the *relative motion* of conductors through the field induces an EMF by **Faraday's Law**:

$$E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$$

where $K_b$ is the back-EMF constant. In SI units, $K_b = K_t$.

**Step 4 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the armature loop.**

In steady state, the supply voltage $V$ is dropped across the armature resistance $R_a$ and the back-EMF $E_b$:

$$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a \qquad \text{...(Equation B)}$$

**Step 5 — Solve Equation (B) for $I_a$.**

$$I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{V - K_b \, \omega}{R_a}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $I_a$ into Equation (A) to obtain $T$.**

$$T = K_t \cdot \frac{V - K_b \, \omega}{R_a}$$

$$T = \frac{K_t \, V}{R_a} - \frac{K_t \, K_b}{R_a} \, \omega$$

**Step 7 — Solve this final equation for $\omega$ as a function of $T$.**

$$\frac{K_t \, K_b}{R_a} \, \omega = \frac{K_t \, V}{R_a} - T$$

$$\omega = \frac{V}{K_b} - \frac{R_a}{K_t \, K_b} \, T$$

This is the canonical **linear speed–torque characteristic**:

$$\boxed{\omega = \omega_0 - m \cdot T}$$

where:
- $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_b}$ is the **no-load (ideal) speed**,
- $m = \dfrac{R_a}{K_t K_b}$ is the **slope of the speed-torque line** (negative, so speed falls as load rises).

**Step 8 — Physical interpretation of the two intercepts.**

- At **no load** ($T = 0$): $\omega = \omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_b}$. The motor spins fastest.
- At **stall** ($\omega = 0$): $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_t \, V}{R_a}$. The maximum torque the motor can deliver — occurs when back-EMF is zero and the *entire* supply drops across $R_a$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Speed Calculation Under PWM

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
A permanent-magnet DC motor has the following name-plate data:
$V = 24$ V, $R_a = 1.2 \, \Omega$, $K_b = 0.05$ V·s/rad, no-load speed $N_0 = 4500$ rpm.
A **PWM signal of frequency 25 kHz with duty cycle $D = 60\%$** is applied through an H-Bridge.
**Find:**
(a) The no-load back-EMF.
(b) The expected average motor speed at $D = 60\%$.
(c) The r.m.s. voltage seen by the motor.
(d) The ON-time of each PWM pulse.

**Solution:**

**(a) No-load back-EMF:**

Convert speed: $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2 \pi N_0}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 4500}{60} = 150 \pi \;\text{rad/s} \approx 471.24 \;\text{rad/s}$

$$E_{b0} = K_b \cdot \omega_0 = 0.05 \times 471.24 = 23.56 \;\text{V}$$

This is close to the supply (24 V), as expected — at no-load, the only voltage drop is the small $I_a R_a$ across the brush resistance, so nearly all of $V$ appears as back-EMF.

**(b) Average motor speed under PWM:**

The effective DC voltage is:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{CC} = 0.60 \times 24 = 14.4 \;\text{V}$$

Assuming linear scaling (valid for moderate load, ignoring $I_a R_a$ drop):

$$\omega_{PWM} \approx D \cdot \omega_0 = 0.60 \times 471.24 = 282.74 \;\text{rad/s}$$

Convert to rpm:

$$N_{PWM} = \frac{60 \times 282.74}{2 \pi} \approx 2700 \;\text{rpm}$$

**(c) R.M.S. voltage:**

$$V_{rms} = V_{CC} \cdot \sqrt{D} = 24 \times \sqrt{0.60} = 24 \times 0.7746 = 18.59 \;\text{V}$$

**(d) ON-time of each pulse:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{25\,000} = 40 \; \mu s$$

$$T_{ON} = D \cdot T = 0.60 \times 40 \; \mu s = 24 \; \mu s$$

$$\boxed{T_{ON} = 24 \; \mu s, \quad T_{OFF} = 16 \; \mu s}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why Use PWM with H-Bridge, Not a Rheostat?

| Criterion | Rheostat (Linear Control) | PWM + H-Bridge (Switched Control) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Efficiency** | Low (often 30–50%) — wasted as $I^2 R$ heat | High (>90%) — transistor is either fully ON or fully OFF |
| **Heat dissipation** | Large heatsink required | Minimal heatsink, often none for small motors |
| **Speed range** | Limited by resistor tolerance | Full 0–100% smooth, software-controlled |
| **Bidirectional control** | Requires extra DPDT switch | Inherent — switch pair selection gives direction |
| **Braking** | Not free; external brake required | Free — dynamic / regenerative modes in firmware |
| **Cost at low power** | Cheap | Slightly higher (needs MOSFET/IC) |
| **EMI / Acoustic noise** | Silent | Ultrasonic PWM (>20 kHz) is inaudible |

---

## 3.4 Logical Step-wise Control Flow in a Robotic Joint (Block Logic)

**Step 1:** The microcontroller (e.g., Arduino, STM32) receives a *desired position / speed* from the trajectory planner.

**Step 2:** A **PID controller** computes the required *actuator effort* (a number from 0 to 255 for an 8-bit PWM, or 0 to 65535 for 16-bit).

**Step 3:** The PWM peripheral of the MCU generates a pulse train with duty cycle $D = \dfrac{\text{effort}}{2^n - 1}$.

**Step 4:** The PWM signal is routed to the **enable (EN) pin** of an H-Bridge IC such as L293D.

**Step 5:** Two **direction pins** (IN1, IN2) of the H-Bridge are set HIGH/LOW to select CW or CCW rotation.

**Step 6:** The H-Bridge delivers a *chopped* current of average $D \cdot V_{CC}$ to the motor, producing a torque proportional to $D$.

**Step 7:** A **quadrature encoder** on the motor shaft feeds back actual speed/position, closing the loop.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — DC Motor Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Supply Voltage V] --> ra[Armature Resistance Ra]
    ra --> la[Armature Inductance La]
    la --> eb[Back EMF Source Eb]
    eb --> gnd1[Return to GND]
    field[Field Flux Phi] -.-> eb
    eb -.-> torque[Mechanical Torque T]
    torque --> shaft[Rotating Shaft]
    shaft -.-> emf[Induces Eb proportional to omega]
    emf -.-> eb
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The horizontal chain `V → Ra → La → Eb` is the electrical equivalent circuit of the armature.
- The dotted lines show that **field flux** and **shaft speed $\omega$** *induce* the back-EMF and develop torque — the two-way electromechanical coupling that makes a DC motor a *transducer*, not a passive resistor.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — H-Bridge Four Operating States

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BAT [DC Supply Vcc]
        p[Positive Rail]
        n[Ground Rail]
    end

    subgraph LEG1 [Left Leg]
        s1[Switch S1 High Side]
        s2[Switch S2 Low Side]
    end

    subgraph LEG2 [Right Leg]
        s3[Switch S3 High Side]
        s4[Switch S4 Low Side]
    end

    p --> s1
    s1 --> m1[Motor Terminal A]
    m1 --- M[Motor Armature]
    M --- m2[Motor Terminal B]
    m2 --> s4
    s4 --> n
    p --> s3
    s3 --> m2
    m1 --> s2
    s2 --> n
```

**Operating Mode Mapping (read against the above):**

| Mode | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | Current Path | Motor Direction |
| :--- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :--- | :--- |
| Forward | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | $V_{CC} \to S_1 \to A \to B \to S_4 \to GND$ | Clockwise |
| Reverse | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | $V_{CC} \to S_3 \to B \to A \to S_2 \to GND$ | Counter-clockwise |
| Brake (Low) | OFF | ON | OFF | ON | A→B shorted through S2 and S4 | Decelerates |
| Coast | OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | Motor open-circuited | Freewheel |

> [!NOTE]
> The two half-legs (LEG1 and LEG2) are mirror images. **Never close S1 and S2 simultaneously**, nor **S3 and S4** — that is the dreaded *shoot-through* that welds transistors and releases the magic smoke.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete PWM-Driven H-Bridge Speed Controller

```mermaid
graph LR
    cmd[Desired Speed from PID] --> pwm[PWM Generator Module]
    t1[Triangle Wave Oscillator] --> pwm
    pwm -->|PWM Pulse Train| dir[Direction Logic IN1 IN2]
    micro[Microcontroller GPIO] --> dir
    dir --> hb[H-Bridge Driver L293D]
    psu[24V DC Power Supply] --> hb
    hb -->|Switched Current| motor[DC Motor + Encoder]
    motor -->|Shaft Rotation| load[Robot Joint or Wheel]
    enc[Quadrature Encoder] -->|Feedback Q1 Q2| micro
    micro --> cmd
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (cmd → micro → dir → hb → motor → enc → micro) is the *closed-loop control*.
- The **inner path** (t1 → pwm) shows the *carrier waveform generation* (typically a hardware timer inside the MCU).
- The **PSU → hb** branch supplies the *raw* DC voltage that the H-Bridge chops at high frequency.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Timing Diagram — PWM Signal Across One Period

```mermaid
graph LR
    t0[Time t0]:::nodeStyle --> t1[Time t1]
    t1 --> t2[Time t2]
    t2 --> t3[Time t3]
    t3 --> t4[Time t4 = t0 + T]

    t0 -.->|Ton starts| high1[HIGH Level Vcc]
    t1 -.->|Ton ends| low1[LOW Level 0V]
    t2 -.->|Ton starts again| high2[HIGH Level Vcc]
    t3 -.->|Ton ends| low2[LOW Level 0V]
    t4 -.->|Period T complete| cycle[Repeat]

    classDef nodeStyle fill:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px;
```

**Analytical reading:**
- $T_{ON} = t_1 - t_0 = t_3 - t_2$
- $T = t_4 - t_0$
- $D = T_{ON}/T$
- $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{CC}$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an Actuator. List any four types of actuators used in robotics. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **actuator** is a device that converts an input control signal — typically electrical energy — into a physical motion (linear or rotary) to perform mechanical work in a robotic system. It is the *muscle* of a robot.

The four principal types of actuators used in robotics are:

1. **Electric actuators** (e.g., DC motor, stepper motor, servo motor) — most common in modern mobile and industrial robots.
2. **Hydraulic actuators** — used where very high force/torque is required (e.g., heavy-payload manipulators, humanoid robots).
3. **Pneumatic actuators** — used in low-cost pick-and-place and soft-robotics applications.
4. **Piezoelectric / Shape-Memory-Alloy actuators** — used in micro-robotics and precision positioning.

> *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing and briefly identifying use — 3 marks total)*

---

### Question 2: What is meant by H-Bridge? Why is it necessary in robotic motor control? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **H-Bridge** is a four-switch electronic circuit, typically built using four transistors (BJTs or MOSFETs) arranged in an "H" topology, used to drive a DC motor in **both forward and reverse directions** from a single DC supply.

It is necessary because:
1. A DC motor's direction of rotation depends on the **polarity** of the voltage applied to its terminals. The H-Bridge allows a microcontroller to *electronically* reverse this polarity.
2. It enables **PWM-based speed control** by rapidly switching the supply, achieving high efficiency.
3. It supports **braking modes** (dynamic and regenerative) that are essential for accurate robotic joint positioning.

> *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for necessity — 3 marks total)*

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, divided as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A: Comprehensive Question on DC Motor Theory + H-Bridge** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the speed–torque equation of a DC motor starting from the force on a current-carrying conductor.** Draw the equivalent circuit. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Force on a current-carrying conductor (1 mark):**

A single straight conductor of active length $L$ (in metres) carrying current $I_a$ (in amperes) perpendicular to a magnetic flux density $B$ (in tesla) experiences a Lorentz force:

$$F = B \cdot I_a \cdot L$$

**2. Torque developed (1 mark):**

If there are $N$ active conductors on the armature at effective radius $r$, the developed electromagnetic torque is:

$$T = N \cdot B \cdot I_a \cdot L \cdot r = K_t \cdot I_a$$

where $K_t = N B L r$ is the **torque constant** (N·m/A).

**3. Back-EMF (1 mark):**

As the armature rotates at $\omega$ rad/s in the same field, an EMF $E_b$ is induced (Faraday's law):

$$E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$$

where $K_b$ is the back-EMF constant (V·s/rad). In SI units, $K_b = K_t$.

**4. Armature circuit equation (1 mark):**

In steady state, Kirchhoff's Voltage Law gives:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**5. Solve for current and substitute (2 marks):**

$$I_a = \frac{V - K_b \omega}{R_a}$$

$$T = K_t \cdot \frac{V - K_b \omega}{R_a} = \frac{K_t V}{R_a} - \frac{K_t K_b}{R_a} \omega$$

**6. Final speed–torque form (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{V}{K_b} - \frac{R_a}{K_t K_b} \, T}$$

This is a straight line of slope $-\dfrac{R_a}{K_t K_b}$ passing through no-load speed $\omega_0 = V/K_b$ and stall torque $T_{stall} = K_t V / R_a$.

**Equivalent Circuit (any drawing showing V → Ra → (Eb) loop) — full credit for neat labelled diagram.**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With a neat sketch, explain the working of an H-Bridge circuit. What is shoot-through and how is it prevented? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. H-Bridge Circuit Diagram (2 marks):**

An H-Bridge consists of four switches $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ — usually power MOSFETs — arranged as two half-legs. The DC motor is connected between the mid-points of the two half-legs, and the supply $V_{CC}$ is applied across the top of the legs with ground at the bottom. The circuit forms the letter "H" with the motor as the crossbar.

**2. Forward operation (1.5 marks):**

When $S_1$ and $S_4$ are turned ON, current flows from $V_{CC} \to S_1 \to$ terminal A of the motor $\to$ terminal B $\to S_4 \to$ ground. The motor rotates in the **clockwise** direction.

**3. Reverse operation (1.5 marks):**

When $S_3$ and $S_2$ are turned ON (and $S_1$, $S_4$ are OFF), current flows from $V_{CC} \to S_3 \to$ terminal B $\to$ terminal A $\to S_2 \to$ ground. The motor rotates in the **counter-clockwise** direction.

**4. Brake and Coast (1 mark):**

- **Brake:** turning ON $S_2$ and $S_4$ (or $S_1$ and $S_3$) shorts the motor terminals; the back-EMF dissipates as braking torque.
- **Coast:** all switches OFF — motor freewheels.

**5. Shoot-through (1 mark):**

If both $S_1$ *and* $S_2$ (or $S_3$ and $S_4$) are turned ON simultaneously, a *direct short* is created between $V_{CC}$ and ground through the bridge. This causes a large uncontrolled current that instantly destroys the transistors. It is prevented by inserting a **dead-time** (typically 100–500 ns) in the gate-driving logic so that one switch is fully OFF before its complement turns ON. Most modern H-Bridge ICs (e.g., L293D, DRV8871) have built-in dead-time generators.

---

### **Question B: Comprehensive Question on PWM + Numerical DC Motor Problem** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain Pulse Width Modulation technique for speed control of a DC motor. Draw the relevant waveforms. Derive the relation between average voltage and duty cycle. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept of PWM (1.5 marks):**

Pulse Width Modulation is a technique in which a digital signal of constant frequency but variable ON-time is applied to a switching device (e.g., the enable pin of an H-Bridge). By varying the ON-time, the *average* voltage delivered to the motor is controlled, thereby controlling its speed.

**2. Definition of duty cycle (1 mark):**

$$D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T}$$

**3. Derivation of average voltage (1.5 marks):**

The instantaneous output is $V_{CC}$ during $T_{ON}$ and 0 during $T_{OFF}$. The average over one period $T$ is:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_0^{T_{ON}} V_{CC} \, dt + \int_{T_{ON}}^{T} 0 \, dt \right]$$

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \cdot V_{CC} \cdot T_{ON} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T} \cdot V_{CC}$$

$$\boxed{V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{CC}}$$

**4. Waveforms (2 marks):**

- *Triangular / sawtooth carrier wave* of frequency $f_c$ (1 to 25 kHz).
- *DC reference* signal equal to $D \cdot V_{CC}$.
- The comparator output is HIGH when reference > carrier, LOW otherwise — yielding the PWM pulse train.
- The *motor terminal voltage* is a rectangular wave swinging between $V_{CC}$ and 0 with duty cycle $D$; the *motor current* is a much smoother exponential, integrated by the armature's mechanical and electrical inertia.

**5. Why high frequency? (1 mark):**

If $f_c > 20$ kHz, the resulting acoustic noise lies above the human audible range. Additionally, the high chopping rate makes the motor current virtually ripple-free, reducing torque pulsations.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 $\Omega$. At no load, the armature draws 5 A and the speed is 1500 rpm. When loaded, the armature current rises to 50 A. Calculate: (i) the back-EMF constant $K_b$, (ii) the torque developed at full load, and (iii) the full-load speed. (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $V = 220$ V
- $R_a = 0.5 \; \Omega$
- $I_{a0} = 5$ A (no load)
- $N_0 = 1500$ rpm
- $I_{a,FL} = 50$ A (full load)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Back-EMF at no load (1.5 marks):**

$$E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 220 - (5)(0.5) = 220 - 2.5 = 217.5 \;\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Convert no-load speed to rad/s (1 mark):**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{2 \pi N_0}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 50 \pi = 157.08 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Back-EMF constant $K_b$ (1 mark):**

$$K_b = \frac{E_{b0}}{\omega_0} = \frac{217.5}{157.08} = 1.385 \;\text{V·s/rad}$$

**Step 4 — Torque constant (0.5 marks):**

In SI units, $K_t = K_b = 1.385 \;\text{N·m/A}$.

**Step 5 — Full-load back-EMF (1 mark):**

$$E_{b,FL} = V - I_{a,FL} R_a = 220 - (50)(0.5) = 220 - 25 = 195 \;\text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Full-load speed (1.5 marks):**

Since $E_b = K_b \omega$, the full-load speed is:

$$\omega_{FL} = \frac{E_{b,FL}}{K_b} = \frac{195}{1.385} = 140.83 \;\text{rad/s}$$

$$N_{FL} = \frac{60 \times \omega_{FL}}{2 \pi} = \frac{60 \times 140.83}{2 \pi} \approx 1345 \;\text{rpm}$$

**Step 7 — Full-load developed torque (0.5 mark):**

$$T_{FL} = K_t \cdot I_{a,FL} = 1.385 \times 50 = 69.23 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $K_b = 1.385$ V·s/rad
- $T_{FL} = 69.23$ N·m
- $N_{FL} \approx 1345$ rpm (a speed drop of $1500 - 1345 = 155$ rpm under load)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Losers:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing $K_b$ in V/rpm instead of V·s/rad — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm → rad/s** before using $\omega$ — **lose 1 mark**.
> 3. **Wrong back-EMF direction in Lenz's law** (using $V + I_a R_a$ instead of $V - I_a R_a$).
> 4. **PWM duty cycle confusion:** Mixing up $T_{ON}/T$ with $T_{ON}/T_{OFF}$ — they are *not* the same.
> 5. **H-Bridge shoot-through:** Drawing the bridge with both diagonal switches ON *and* the same-leg pair ON simultaneously — this is physically impossible and a sign of conceptual confusion.
> 6. **Stating $K_b = K_t$ without the qualifier "in SI units"** — partial credit only.
> 7. **In numericals, using $V_{CC}$ instead of $V_{avg}$** when computing speed under PWM control.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **Actuator** = energy converter (electrical → mechanical); the *muscle* of a robot.
- ⚡ Three families: **Electric, Hydraulic, Pneumatic**. Electric DC motors dominate modern robotics.
- ⚡ **DC motor working principle:** Lorentz force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field, with **commutation** to maintain unidirectional rotation.
- ⚡ **Two fundamental constants:** $K_t$ (N·m/A) and $K_b$ (V·s/rad); **numerically equal in SI units**.
- ⚡ **Back-EMF** $E_b = K_b \omega$ *opposes* the supply — Lenz's law in action.
- ⚡ **Armature equation** (steady state): $V = E_b + I_a R_a$.
- ⚡ **Speed-torque line:** $\omega = \dfrac{V}{K_b} - \dfrac{R_a}{K_t K_b} T$ — *linear and falling*.
- ⚡ **Stall torque** $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_t V}{R_a}$; **No-load speed** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_b}$.
- ⚡ **H-Bridge** = 4 switches (MOSFETs/BJTs) in an H-topology; diagonal pairs energised for forward/reverse rotation.
- ⚡ **Shoot-through** = catastrophic short circuit; prevented by **dead-time** insertion (~100–500 ns).
- ⚡ **PWM** = switching the supply at constant frequency with variable duty cycle $D = T_{ON}/T$.
- ⚡ **Average PWM voltage** $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{CC}$; **R.M.S.** $V_{rms} = V_{CC} \sqrt{D}$.
- ⚡ PWM frequency should be **> 20 kHz** to be inaudible and produce smooth torque.
- ⚡ **H-Bridge + PWM** together = bidirectional, efficient, software-controllable motor drive.
- ⚡ **Speed conversion:** $\omega \, (\text{rad/s}) = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$; reverse: $N = \dfrac{60 \omega}{2 \pi}$.
- ⚡ **Mechanical power:** $P = T \omega$; **Electrical power:** $P = V I_a$; **Efficiency** $\eta = P_{mech}/P_{elec}$.
- ⚡ Popular driver ICs: **L293D, L298N, DRV8871, BTS7960** — know the current and voltage limits.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stepper Motors - Servos
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stepper Motors - Servos

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Stepper Motor — Formal Definition

A **Stepper Motor** is a brushless, synchronous electromagnetic device that converts a series of digital electrical pulses into discrete mechanical angular displacements (steps). Each input pulse advances the rotor by a precisely fixed angular increment, called the **step angle**, without requiring any feedback sensor for position control (open-loop operation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** A stepper motor is the only electrical machine whose position can be controlled accurately in an *open loop*. This property makes it indispensable in low-cost robotic joints, CNC machines, and 3D printers.

### 1.2 Servo Motor — Formal Definition

A **Servo Motor** is a rotary or linear actuator that uses a closed-loop feedback control system (typically position, velocity, or torque feedback) to precisely control its angular/linear position, velocity, and acceleration. It consists of a motor coupled with a position sensor (encoder/potentiometer) and a controller that continuously minimizes the error between the commanded and actual values.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Stepper Motor Analogy — "The Staircase Walker":**
Imagine climbing a staircase where every step is exactly the same height. A stepper motor is like a person who can ONLY move by taking complete, equal-sized steps — never a half-step, never a jump. A controller simply says "take 1 step," "take 50 steps," or "take 200 steps backward," and the motor obeys exactly. The staircase itself has "teeth" (magnetic poles) that lock the person (rotor) firmly in place between steps.

**Servo Motor Analogy — "The Self-Correcting Driver":**
Imagine a person driving a car toward a GPS-destination. They constantly look at the map (feedback sensor), compare the current position to the destination (error calculation), and adjust the steering wheel (control signal) in real time. Even if a side-wind pushes the car off-course, the driver corrects it. A servo motor behaves identically — it is a *self-correcting* actuator.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard step angles in commercial stepper motors:** **1.8°** (200 steps/rev — most common), **0.9°** (400 steps/rev), **7.5°** (48 steps/rev).
- **Supply voltages for hobby servos:** typically **4.8 V – 6.0 V DC**.
- **Servo PWM control signal frequency:** **50 Hz** (period **20 ms**); pulse width **1 ms – 2 ms** maps to **0° – 180°**.
- **Holding torque** of a stepper motor: typically **0.1 N·m to 50 N·m** depending on frame size (NEMA 17, NEMA 23, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Answers:** Stepper = open-loop digital positioning. Servo = closed-loop continuous positioning. Examiners specifically look for this contrast in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stepper Motor — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Energize a Phase:**
When a DC pulse is applied to a stator phase winding, current flows and produces a magnetic field. The rotor (with salient poles or permanent magnets) aligns itself with the energized stator pole to minimize the magnetic reluctance of the flux path.

**Step 2 — Rotor Locks:**
Once aligned, the rotor remains locked at that angular position as long as the phase is energized. This holding force is the **holding torque** (or detent torque).

**Step 3 — Switch to Next Phase:**
The controller de-energizes the current phase and energizes the next phase in sequence. The rotor rotates by exactly **one step angle (β)** to align with the new magnetic field.

**Step 4 — Accumulate Steps:**
By counting the number of pulses sent, the controller knows the exact angular displacement of the rotor. No encoder is required → **open-loop control**.

**Step 5 — Reverse Direction:**
Reversing the phase-energization sequence (clockwise ↔ anticlockwise) reverses rotor motion.

#### 2.1.1 Types of Stepper Motors

| Type | Rotor Construction | Steps per Rev | Special Feature |
|------|-------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | Soft-iron toothed rotor (no magnet) | Up to 1000 | Step angle depends on stator/rotor tooth count |
| **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Axially magnetized permanent-magnet rotor | 48–100 | Higher holding torque, coarse step |
| **Hybrid (HB)** | Toothed rotor with axially magnetized permanent magnet | 200, 400 | Combines VR resolution + PM torque → most used |

#### 2.1.2 Full-Step vs Half-Step Excitation

- **Full-step (1-phase ON or 2-phase ON):** Each pulse → 1 full step. With 2-phase ON, torque is ~40% higher.
- **Half-step:** Alternates 1-phase ON and 2-phase ON → halves the effective step angle and gives smoother motion.
- **Microstepping:** Each full step subdivided into 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, or 256 microsteps using sinusoidal current ramping in the two phases.

### 2.2 Servo Motor — Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Command Input:**
A reference signal (desired position θ_ref) is given to the servo controller (e.g., PWM pulse for hobby servos, ±10 V analog for industrial servos).

**Step 2 — Feedback Measurement:**
A position sensor (optical encoder, resolver, or potentiometer) continuously measures the actual rotor position θ_actual.

**Step 3 — Error Generation:**
The controller computes the error: 
$$e(t) = \theta_{ref}(t) - \theta_{actual}(t)$$

**Step 4 — Control Action (PID):**
A PID (Proportional–Integral–Derivative) controller generates a corrective voltage/current command to the motor's power amplifier.

**Step 5 — Motor Actuation & Loop Closure:**
The motor moves; the new θ_actual is sensed; the loop closes continuously at high bandwidth (typically **1–10 kHz**).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r / N_p}$ | Step angle (simplified for hybrid stepper) | $\beta$ in degrees |
| 2 | $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ | Step angle in general (m = phases, $N_r$ = rotor teeth) | degrees |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{360°}{\beta}$ | Number of steps per revolution | dimensionless integer |
| 4 | $f_{step} = \dfrac{\omega}{6\beta}$ | Required pulse rate to achieve angular velocity $\omega$ | pulses/sec (Hz) |
| 5 | $T_h \propto N_r \cdot B \cdot A$ | Holding torque proportional to rotor teeth, flux density, area | N·m |
| 6 | $\tau_e = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Electrical time constant of phase winding | seconds |
| 7 | $G(s) = \dfrac{\theta(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{s(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_b K_m}$ | Open-loop transfer function of DC servo motor | — |
| 8 | $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b}$ | No-load speed of DC servo | rad/s |
| 9 | $T_m = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Developed motor torque | N·m |
| 10 | $K_b = K_t$ (in SI units) | Back-EMF constant equals torque constant | V·s/rad = N·m/A |
| 11 | $s_{t} = \dfrac{T \cdot t_r}{2}$ | Slew rate relation for stepper acceleration | rad/s² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for Servo:** $K_b = K_t$ holds strictly in SI units. In CGS or English units, conversion factors must be applied. Examiners often test this equality.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stepper Motors:** 3D printers (X/Y/Z axis), CNC routers, textile machines, camera autofocus lenses, disk drives, pick-and-place machines.
- **Servo Motors:** Robotic arms (joint torque control), drone gimbal stabilizers, CNC feed drives, antenna positioning, autonomous vehicles (steering), humanoid robots.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Step Angle for a Hybrid Stepper Motor

**Statement:** A 2-phase hybrid stepper motor has 8 stator pole pairs (i.e., 8 teeth on each of the two stator stacks) and the rotor carries 50 teeth. Determine the step angle and the number of steps per revolution.

**Given:**
- Number of phases: $m = 2$
- Rotor teeth: $N_r = 50$
- Stator teeth per phase per stack: 8

**Step 1 — General Step Angle Formula:**

For a multi-stack or hybrid stepper, the step angle is governed by the smallest mechanical increment that causes a realignment of rotor and stator teeth across all phases simultaneously.

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$$

where:
- $m$ = number of stator phases
- $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values:**

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{2 \times 50}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Numerator-Denominator Product:**

$$2 \times 50 = 100$$

**Step 4 — Final Calculation:**

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{100} = 3.6°$$

**Step 5 — Steps per Revolution:**

$$N = \frac{360°}{\beta} = \frac{360°}{3.6°} = 100 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Conclusion:** The motor advances **3.6° per pulse** and completes one full revolution in **100 pulses**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Pulse Frequency for a Desired Rotor Speed

**Statement:** For the motor above ($\beta = 3.6°$), determine the pulse rate required to achieve a shaft speed of **240 RPM**.

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to Angular Velocity:**

$$\omega = 240 \text{ RPM} = 240 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$\omega = 240 \times 0.10472 = 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Number of Pulses per Second:**

Each step = $\beta$ degrees = $\dfrac{\beta \pi}{180}$ radians.

$$f_{step} = \frac{\omega}{\beta_{rad}} = \frac{\omega}{\beta \cdot \pi / 180} = \frac{180 \cdot \omega}{\pi \cdot \beta}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$f_{step} = \frac{180 \times 25.133}{\pi \times 3.6} = \frac{4523.94}{11.31}$$

**Step 5 — Compute:**

$$f_{step} \approx 400 \text{ pulses/second} \; (\text{Hz})$$

**Cross-check:** 240 RPM = 4 rev/s. With 100 steps/rev, we need $4 \times 100 = 400$ pulses/s ✓.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the DC Servo Motor Transfer Function

**Statement:** Derive the transfer function $\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)}$ for a separately excited DC servo motor.

**Step 1 — Write the Armature Voltage Equation:**

$$V_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

**Step 2 — Back-EMF Equation:**

$$e_b(t) = K_b \cdot \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical Torque Equation:**

$$T_m(t) = J \frac{d^2\theta(t)}{dt^2} + B \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt} + T_L(t)$$

**Step 4 — Motor Torque–Current Relation:**

$$T_m(t) = K_t \cdot i_a(t)$$

**Step 5 — Take the Laplace Transform of All Equations (neglecting $T_L$ for simplicity):**

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_b s \theta(s)$$

$$K_t I_a(s) = (J s^2 + B s) \theta(s)$$

**Step 6 — Solve the Mechanical Equation for $I_a(s)$:**

$$I_a(s) = \frac{(J s^2 + B s) \theta(s)}{K_t}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute into the Armature Equation:**

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s^2 + B s) \theta(s)}{K_t} + K_b s \theta(s)$$

**Step 8 — Factor Out $\theta(s)$:**

$$V_a(s) = \theta(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s^2 + B s)}{K_t} + K_b s \right]$$

**Step 9 — Common Denominator $K_t$:**

$$V_a(s) = \frac{\theta(s)}{K_t} \left[ (R_a + L_a s)(J s^2 + B s) + K_b K_t s \right]$$

**Step 10 — Final Transfer Function:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ (R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t \right]}}$$

This is the standard third-order transfer function used in KTU servo control derivations.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Stepper vs Servo

| Parameter | Stepper Motor | DC/AC Servo Motor |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Control mode | Open-loop | Closed-loop |
| Position sensor | Not required | Encoder/Resolver mandatory |
| Holding torque at standstill | Excellent (full rated) | Depends on control loop |
| Speed range | Low–Moderate (≤ 3000 RPM typical) | High (≥ 5000 RPM) |
| Torque at high speed | Drops sharply | Constant up to rated speed |
| Step accuracy | ±5% (no cumulative error per step) | Determined by encoder resolution |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (sensor + drive) |
| Heat dissipation | High at standstill | Low (only proportional to load) |
| Resonance issues | Pronounced at certain speeds | Negligible |
| Typical robotic use | Pick-and-place, simple joints | Precision arms, CNC, humanoid joints |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Stepper Motor Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrl[Controller / Microcontroller] -->|Digital Pulses| drv[Bipolar / Unipolar Driver IC]
    drv -->|Switched DC| phaseA[Phase A Winding]
    drv -->|Switched DC| phaseB[Phase B Winding]
    phaseA --> rot[Stepper Motor Rotor]
    phaseB --> rot
    rot -->|Mechanical Shaft| load[Robot Joint / Linear Stage]
    ps[DC Power Supply 5V to 24V] --> drv

    classDef ctrln fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef drv fill:#b45309,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff

    classDef load fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,color:#ffffff
    classDef ps fill:#4c1d95,stroke:#1e1b4b,color:#ffffff

    class drv drv
    class phaseA,phaseB phase

    class ps ps
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Servo Motor Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cmd[Reference Input theta_ref] --> sum[Junction Summing Point]
    sens[Encoder / Resolver Feedback] --> sum
    sum -->|Error e t| pid[PID Controller]
    pid --> amp[Power Amplifier PWM Driver]
    amp -->|Va| motor[Servo Motor DC or AC]
    motor -->|Shaft Motion| out[Robot Joint Output]
    out --> load[Mechanical Load]
    out --> sens

    classDef in fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef fb fill:#065f46,stroke:#022c22,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrl fill:#b45309,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff

    classDef mech fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,color:#ffffff

    class sum,pid ctrl
    class amp pwr
    class motor mech
    class load,out,sens fb
```

### 4.3 Internal Architecture — Hybrid Stepper Motor Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Front Stator Stack Phase A 8 Teeth] --> B[Rotor 50 Teeth Axially Magnetized Permanent Magnet]
    B --> C[Rear Stator Stack Phase B 8 Teeth Offset by Quarter Tooth Pitch]
    A --> D[Bronze Bearings and Shaft]
    C --> D

    classDef st fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef rt fill:#b45309,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff

    class B rt
    class D sh
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow — Servo Motor Error-Correction Loop

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R as Reference theta_ref
    participant S as Summing Junction
    participant P as PID Controller
    participant A as Power Amplifier
    participant M as Servo Motor
    participant E as Encoder
    R->>S: Send desired position
    E->>S: Send measured theta_actual
    S->>P: Compute error e t
    P->>A: Generate voltage Va
    A->>M: Drive motor
    M->>E: Shaft rotates
    E-->>S: Update feedback
    Note over M,E: Loop runs continuously at 1 to 10 kHz
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*List any three advantages of stepper motors over servo motors in robotic applications.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **No feedback sensor required** — operates in open loop, reducing wiring, cost, and complexity of the robotic controller.
2. **Excellent holding torque at standstill** — the rotor locks firmly when the phase is energized, useful for static holding of a robot gripper or joint without additional braking.
3. **Direct digital compatibility** — each digital pulse corresponds to a precise angular step, allowing direct interfacing with microcontrollers/PLCs without complex DAC stages.

*(Any 3 of: open-loop, no encoder, low cost, brushless, high holding torque, digital pulse interface)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a servo motor. What is the role of an encoder in its operation?*

**Model Answer:**

A **servo motor** is a rotary/linear actuator that uses a closed-loop feedback mechanism to precisely control position, velocity, or torque according to a reference command.

The **encoder** is a position sensor mounted on the motor shaft that converts mechanical angular displacement into electrical pulses. These pulses are decoded by the controller to determine the rotor's actual position θ_actual in real time. The controller continuously compares θ_actual with the commanded θ_ref, computes the error, and adjusts the drive signal — making the encoder the **feedback element** that closes the loop and enables precise control.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Internal choice is provided. Students may answer **either Question A or Question B**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Stepper Motor [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the open-loop control system of a permanent-magnet stepper motor used to drive a single-axis robot joint. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A permanent-magnet (PM) stepper motor has an axially magnetized rotor with N and S poles. Its stator carries two phase windings (A and A') wound on salient poles. Open-loop control means **no position feedback** is used — the controller simply issues digital pulses and *assumes* the motor has moved correctly.

**Block diagram description (refer Section 4.1):**

1. **Controller / Microcontroller (e.g., Arduino, PLC):** Generates a sequence of TTL-level pulses and a direction bit. The number of pulses = desired steps; pulse frequency = desired speed.
2. **Driver IC (e.g., ULN2003 for unipolar, A4988 / DRV8825 for bipolar):** Amplifies the low-current logic pulses to high-current levels (0.5 A to 3 A) required by the stator windings. It contains H-bridges for bipolar motors and Darlington arrays for unipolar motors.
3. **Stepper Motor:** Each pulse energizes a phase in the sequence A → B → A' → B' → A (for full-step). The rotor aligns to the new field, advancing by one step angle $\beta$.
4. **Power Supply:** Provides the DC voltage (5 V to 24 V typical) and current capacity.
5. **Robot Joint / Linear Stage:** Coupled to the shaft through a gear train or lead screw for force amplification and motion conversion (rotary → linear).

**Operating sequence:** For a 1.8° PM stepper (200 steps/rev) commanding 90° rotation → 50 pulses at, say, 200 Hz → completes motion in 0.25 seconds.

**Advantages of open loop:** cheap, simple, no sensor calibration.
**Disadvantage:** If the load torque exceeds holding torque, the motor stalls silently and the controller is unaware → loss of synchronization. KTU examiners often test this concept.

---

**(b)** A hybrid stepper motor has 2 phases and 50 rotor teeth. It is required to drive a conveyor belt in a pick-and-place robot at 120 RPM through a 4:1 reduction gear. Calculate: (i) the step angle, (ii) the number of steps per revolution of the motor shaft, and (iii) the pulse rate needed. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $m = 2$ phases
- $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth
- Conveyor (output) speed: $N_{out} = 120$ RPM
- Gear reduction: 4:1 (motor turns 4× faster than conveyor)

**Step (i) — Step Angle:**

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r} = \frac{360°}{2 \times 50} = \frac{360°}{100} = 3.6°$$

**Step (ii) — Steps per Revolution of Motor:**

$$N = \frac{360°}{\beta} = \frac{360°}{3.6°} = 100 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Step (iii) — Motor Shaft Speed and Pulse Rate:**

With a 4:1 reduction, motor shaft speed:
$$N_{motor} = 4 \times 120 = 480 \text{ RPM}$$

Angular velocity:
$$\omega = 480 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 50.27 \text{ rad/s}$$

Pulse rate (steps per second):
$$f_{step} = \frac{N_{motor}}{60} \times N = \frac{480}{60} \times 100 = 800 \text{ Hz}$$

**Cross-check:** $800 \text{ pulses/s} \times 3.6°/\text{pulse} = 2880°/\text{s} = 480 \text{ RPM}$ ✓

**Final Answers:**
- Step angle: $\beta = 3.6°$
- Steps per revolution: $100$
- Pulse rate: $800 \text{ Hz}$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Servo Motor [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a closed-loop position control system using a DC servo motor and explain the function of each block. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Refer to the closed-loop block diagram drawn in **Section 4.2** above. The functional description of each block is as follows:

1. **Reference Input (θ_ref):** The desired angular position command, given as a PWM pulse width (1–2 ms), an analog voltage (±10 V), or a digital word from a microcontroller.
2. **Summing Junction:** Algebraically subtracts the feedback signal from the reference to generate the **error signal** $e(t) = \theta_{ref}(t) - \theta_{actual}(t)$.
3. **Controller (PID / PI / P):** Processes the error and produces a control voltage. A PID controller minimizes steady-state error, improves transient response, and damps oscillations.
4. **Power Amplifier (PWM/Linear):** Boosts the low-power controller output to the voltage/current levels required by the motor armature (typically 24–96 V, 5–20 A in industrial servos).
5. **DC Servo Motor:** Produces torque $T_m = K_t I_a$ proportional to armature current, and rotates the shaft with angular velocity $\omega = d\theta/dt$.
6. **Mechanical Load (Robot Joint):** Inertia $J$ and friction $B$ of the load are coupled to the motor shaft via a gear train.
7. **Position Sensor (Encoder/Resolver):** Mounted on the motor shaft, generates N pulses per revolution (e.g., 2500 ppr → 10000 counts after quadrature decoding). The position is reconstructed by the controller.
8. **Feedback Path:** Returns the measured position to the summing junction, closing the loop. The loop runs at high frequency (1–10 kHz) to reject disturbances.

**Closed-loop significance:** Any load disturbance, parameter drift, or noise is detected by the sensor and corrected automatically — ensuring high accuracy, stiffness, and dynamic response.

---

**(b)** For a separately excited DC servo motor, the following data is given: $R_a = 2 \;\Omega$, $L_a = 0.5 \text{ H}$, $K_b = K_t = 0.1 \text{ V·s/rad}$, $J = 0.01 \text{ kg·m}^2$, $B = 0.001 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$. Derive and simplify the transfer function $\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)}$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Start from the standard transfer function derived in Section 3.3:**

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ (R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t \right]}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values:**

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{0.1}{s \left[ (2 + 0.5 s)(0.01 s + 0.001) + (0.1)(0.1) \right]}$$

**Step 3 — Expand $(2 + 0.5s)(0.01s + 0.001)$:**

$$(2)(0.01s) + (2)(0.001) + (0.5s)(0.01s) + (0.5s)(0.001)$$

$$= 0.02s + 0.002 + 0.005 s^2 + 0.0005 s$$

$$= 0.005 s^2 + 0.0205 s + 0.002$$

**Step 4 — Add $K_b K_t = 0.01$:**

$$0.005 s^2 + 0.0205 s + 0.002 + 0.01 = 0.005 s^2 + 0.0205 s + 0.012$$

**Step 5 — Form the Final Transfer Function:**

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{0.1}{s \left[ 0.005 s^2 + 0.0205 s + 0.012 \right]}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator by 200 to normalize:**

$$\frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{20}{s \left[ s^2 + 4.1 s + 2.4 \right]}$$

**Step 7 — Final simplified form:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{20}{s^3 + 4.1 s^2 + 2.4 s}}$$

This is a third-order type-1 system, which is the standard KTU answer form.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Writing $K_b = K_t$ without specifying **SI units** — examiners deduct marks when units are not mentioned.
> 2. Confusing **holding torque** (stepper) with **stall torque** (servo) — they are NOT the same concept.
> 3. Forgetting the **direction bit** in stepper pulse-train diagrams.
> 4. Drawing the closed-loop block diagram *without a feedback arrow* — instant 2-mark penalty.
> 5. Computing step angle without mentioning that $m$ (number of phases) is a multiplier.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Stepper motor** = open-loop, brushless, digital pulses → discrete angular steps.
- **Servo motor** = closed-loop, requires encoder/resolver, continuous positioning.
- **Step angle formula:** $\beta = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ — memorize for derivations.
- **Steps per revolution:** $N = \dfrac{360°}{\beta}$.
- **Three types of steppers:** VR, PM, Hybrid — Hybrid is the most common in robotics.
- **Microstepping** = subdividing each full step into 2, 4, 8, …, 256 micro-steps using sinusoidal current control for smoother motion.
- **Hobby servo** = 50 Hz PWM, 1 ms → 0°, 1.5 ms → 90°, 2 ms → 180°.
- **Industrial DC servo transfer function** is third-order type-1: $G(s) = \dfrac{K_t}{s[(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t]}$.
- **In SI units:** $K_b \text{ (V·s/rad)} = K_t \text{ (N·m/A)}$.
- **Resonance** is a problem unique to stepper motors at certain pulse frequencies; servos do not suffer from it.
- **Steady-state error** of an open-loop stepper = 0 (mechanical detent) but **stall detection** = impossible without feedback.
- **Holding torque** depends on phase current; doubling current roughly doubles torque but quadruples heat dissipation.
- **Pulse rate to achieve speed $n$ RPM:** $f_{step} = \dfrac{n}{60} \times N$.
- **Stepper is preferred** when: cost is critical, holding torque is needed at standstill, motion is low-speed, and open-loop simplicity is acceptable.
- **Servo is preferred** when: high accuracy, high speed, variable load, smooth motion, and closed-loop stiffness are required.
- **In robotic applications** (KTU focus): Stepper = end-effector, small joints, hobby robots; Servo = industrial arms, CNC, humanoid joints.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuators ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Hydraulic Actuators — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A *Hydraulic Actuator* is a mechanical device that converts the **hydraulic energy** (pressure energy) of a pressurized incompressible fluid — typically a mineral-based or synthetic oil — into useful **linear or rotary mechanical motion** (kinetic energy) to perform work in a robotic or industrial automation system. The hydraulic power transmission chain follows a closed-loop architecture: a **hydraulic pump** (prime mover) draws oil from a reservoir, pressurizes it, and delivers it through **control valves** to the actuator (cylinder or motor). The actuator's piston or vanes are displaced by the fluid pressure, and the spent fluid is returned to the reservoir via a return line. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Operating Parameter:** Industrial hydraulic systems operate within a standard pressure range of **70 bar to 350 bar** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 0.1 MPa). A common industrial norm is **≈ 210 bar (3000 psi)** for high-force robotic manipulators and CNC machine tool feed drives. ### 1.2 Pneumatic Actuators — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A *Pneumatic Actuator* is a mechanical device that converts the **pneumatic energy** (pressure energy) of a compressible gas — typically **atmospheric air** (or sometimes nitrogen) — into controlled **linear or rotary mechanical motion** for use in low-to-medium load automation tasks. Pneumatic systems rely on a **compressor** (which converts electrical/mechanical energy to pneumatic energy), an **FRL unit** (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) for air conditioning, **directional control valves**, and the actuator itself. The exhaust air is vented to the atmosphere (open-loop). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Operating Parameter:** Standard industrial pneumatic pressure is **6 bar (≈ 0.6 MPa ≈ 87 psi)**, with a typical working range of **4 bar to 8 bar**. For low-power applications, pressures as low as **2 bar** may be used; for specialized robotics, **10 bar** systems exist. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Syringe and Bicycle Pump **Intuition for Hydraulic Actuators (The Syringe Model):** Imagine a medical syringe. When you push the plunger, the **incompressible fluid** (water/oil) inside transfers the force directly to the output plunger with virtually **no loss** and **no delay**. If you connect two syringes of different cross-sections by a thin tube filled with water, pushing one plunger down by 10 mm will force the other plunger up by an amount governed purely by the area ratio, but the **force gets multiplied** inversely. This is exactly the principle of Pascal's Law in a hydraulic cylinder — pressure is constant, force scales with area. **Intuition for Pneumatic Actuators (The Bicycle Pump Model):** Now imagine a bicycle pump. The air inside is **springy and compressible**. When you push the handle, some energy goes into compressing the air, some into moving the piston, and some leaks out. The motion is **less precise** and **less stiff** than a hydraulic system, but it is **fast**, **lightweight**, and **clean** — perfect for tasks like opening/closing grippers, indexing parts on a conveyor, or actuating small robot tool-changers. > [!TIP] > **Quick Memory Trick:** *Hydro = Heavy / Strong / Slow / Precise* (think heavy excavator arm). *Pneuma (air) = Light / Fast / Soft / Clean* (think pick-and-place robot gripper). ### 1.4 Why These Actuators Matter in Robotics In a robotic system, the **actuator is the "muscle"** of the robot. For robots that must lift heavy payloads, perform high-force assembly (e.g., spot welding car bodies), or maintain precise position under load, **hydraulic actuators** are the industry standard. For robots that perform high-speed, repetitive, light-duty tasks (e.g., packaging, electronic component placement, food handling), **pneumatic actuators** dominate. Therefore, a robotics engineer *must* understand both technologies, as specified in the KTU PECDT633 Module 2 syllabus. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuators Both actuator families split along two geometric axes: 1. **Linear Actuators** (translation in a straight line) — called *cylinders* or *ram actuators*. 2. **Rotary Actuators** (rotation about a shaft) — called *motors* (or *limited-rotation* actuators for partial sweeps). | Category | Hydraulic Linear | Hydraulic Rotary | Pneumatic Linear | Pneumatic Rotary | |----------|------------------|------------------|------------------|------------------| | Common Names | Hydraulic Cylinder, Ram | Hydraulic Motor (gear/vane/piston) | Air Cylinder | Air Motor (vane/piston) | | Typical Pressure | 100–350 bar | 100–350 bar | 4–8 bar | 4–8 bar | | Force/Torque Density | Very High | Very High | Moderate | Moderate | | Speed | Slow–Medium | Medium | Fast | Very Fast | | Stiffness/Position Accuracy | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (air is spongy) | Poor | | Leak Sensitivity | High (oil) | High | Low (exhaust to air) | Low | ### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Linear Cylinders #### (A) Single-Acting Cylinder - Has **one fluid port** only. - Hydraulic/pneumatic pressure extends the piston in **one direction** only. - The return stroke is achieved by an external **spring**, **gravity**, or **load weight**. - Useful for: clamping, stamping, simple ejection. #### (B) Double-Acting Cylinder - Has **two fluid ports** (cap-end and rod-end). - Fluid pressure can drive the piston in **both directions** (extend and retract) actively. - Most widely used in robotic end-effectors and machine tool feed axes. #### (C) Telescopic Cylinder - Multi-stage (nested pistons). - Provides a **long stroke** from a **short retracted length**. - Used in dump trucks, large crane booms — not common in small robots. #### (D) Tandem Cylinder - Two pistons in series on a common rod. - Output force is roughly **doubled** for the same pressure. ### 2.3 Detailed Breakdown of Rotary Actuators - **Hydraulic Motors** are continuous-rotation devices; classified by their internal design: - **Gear Motors** (external or internal gear) — simple, robust, moderate efficiency. - **Vane Motors** (balanced or unbalanced) — quiet, good low-speed torque. - **Piston Motors** (radial or axial, e.g., bent-axis piston) — high efficiency, used in high-power robotics. - **Pneumatic Air Motors** — typically **vane type** (most common) or **piston type** (for higher torque). - **Limited-Rotation Hydraulic Actuators** (also called *hydraulic rotary actuators* or *helical actuators*) — produce a partial rotation (e.g., 90°, 180°, 270°). Used for robot wrist joints where continuous rotation isn't required. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master These Equations — They Appear in Every KTU Exam.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols & Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions | |---|----------|---------|-----------------|------------------------------| | 1 | Cylinder Output Force (extension) | $F_{ext} = P \cdot A_{p}$ | $F$: N, $P$: Pa, $A_p$: m² | Full piston area exposed | | 2 | Cylinder Output Force (retraction) | $F_{ret} = P \cdot A_{p} - P \cdot A_{r} = P \cdot (A_p - A_r)$ | $A_r$: m², rod cross-section | Both sides pressurized; usually annulus area | | 3 | Effective Piston Area (extension) | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $D$: piston diameter (m) | Full bore area | | 4 | Annulus Area (retraction) | $A_a = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$ | $d$: rod diameter (m) | Net area on retraction side | | 5 | Piston Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$ | $Q$: flow rate (m³/s), $v$: m/s | Constant for steady flow | | 6 | Piston Velocity (retraction) | $v_{ret} = \dfrac{Q}{A_a}$ | — | Smaller area → faster retraction | | 7 | Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$ | $P_{hyd}$: W | Conservation of energy (ideal) | | 8 | Hydraulic Motor Torque | $T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}$ | $\Delta P$: pressure drop (Pa), $D_m$: motor displacement (m³/rev) | For a fixed-displacement motor | | 9 | Motor Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{Q}{D_m}$ | $\omega$: rad/s | Q must match motor displacement | | 10 | Motor Shaft Power | $P_{shaft} = T \cdot \omega = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | — | Ideal (100% volumetric + mechanical) | | 11 | Pneumatic Air Consumption | $V_{air} = Q \cdot t \cdot \dfrac{P_{abs,work}}{P_{abs,atm}}$ | $V$: m³ (free air), $t$: s | Convert to free-air delivery (FAD) | | 12 | Pneumatic Force (extended) | $F = \eta \cdot P_{gauge} \cdot A_p$ | $\eta$: efficiency factor ($\approx$ 0.85–0.95) | Accounts for seal friction, back-pressure | | 13 | Cylinder Stroke Time | $t = \dfrac{V_{cyl}}{Q} = \dfrac{A_p \cdot L}{Q}$ | $L$: stroke length (m) | Useful for cycle time calculations | | 14 | Pascal's Law | $\Delta P = \rho g h$ (hydrostatic) or $P_1 = P_2$ (static) | $\rho$: kg/m³, $g$: 9.81 m/s² | Foundation of hydraulic transmission | | 15 | Bulk Modulus (fluid stiffness) | $K = -V \dfrac{dP}{dV}$ | $K$: Pa | For oil: $K \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ GPa | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Use:** Equation (1) is the *single most important* formula for sizing a hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder in a robot design. KTU 2024 Scheme questions typically test a numerical problem combining equations (1), (3), (5), and (7) — for example: *"A robotic arm uses a double-acting cylinder of bore 50 mm and rod 25 mm at 100 bar supply. Find the extension force, retraction force, and extension time for a 200 mm stroke at 10 L/min flow."* ### 2.5 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic (Why It Works) **Hydraulic Cylinder — Why it produces force:** - The pump creates a pressure $P$ in the fluid (energy added by the prime mover). - This pressure acts uniformly on every boundary of the fluid (Pascal's Law, 1653). - The piston is a movable boundary; on the cap-end, the full area $A_p$ feels the pressure. - The net force is $F = P \cdot A_p$ (Newton's 2nd Law integrated over a closed surface). - When the fluid is vented on the rod side (or vice versa for retraction), the asymmetry produces motion. **Pneumatic Cylinder — Why it differs:** - The working fluid is **air**, which is **compressible** (adiabatic bulk modulus $\approx$ 1.4 × 10⁵ Pa at atmospheric pressure — *1000× softer* than oil). - Therefore, energy is stored in the air itself; motion lags behind valve actuation. - The system exhibits **non-linear** pressure-volume behavior (following $PV^\gamma =$ constant, $\gamma = 1.4$ for air). - This causes the **"soft"** or **"springy"** feel of pneumatic systems — a key reason why pneumatics are *not* used where precise position control is required without a closed-loop servo. > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for Exam:** If a KTU question asks *why hydraulics are stiffer than pneumatics*, your answer must mention **fluid bulk modulus**: oil is ~10,000× stiffer than atmospheric air. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Force and Velocity of a Double-Acting Cylinder **Given:** - Bore diameter $D = 0.05$ m (50 mm) - Rod diameter $d = 0.025$ m (25 mm) - Supply pressure $P_s = 100$ bar $= 10 \times 10^6$ Pa - Pump flow rate $Q = 10$ L/min $= 10 \times 10^{-3} / 60$ m³/s $= 1.6667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s - Stroke length $L = 0.20$ m (200 mm) **Step 1 — Compute the piston (full bore) area.** $$ \begin{aligned} A_p &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times (0.05)^2}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} \\ &= 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ This is the cross-sectional area on the **cap-end** of the piston. **Step 2 — Compute the annulus (effective) area on the rod side.** $$ \begin{aligned} A_a &= \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times ((0.05)^2 - (0.025)^2)}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times (0.0025 - 0.000625)}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times 0.001875}{4} \\ &= 1.4726 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ This is the net area exposed to pressure on the **retraction stroke**. **Step 3 — Compute the extension force (theoretical).** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ext} &= P_s \cdot A_p \\ &= (10 \times 10^6) \times (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 19635 \text{ N} \approx 19.635 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ This is the theoretical maximum force available during extension. (In practice, multiply by mechanical efficiency $\eta \approx 0.90$ to get $\approx 17.67$ kN usable force.) **Step 4 — Compute the retraction force (theoretical).** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ret} &= P_s \cdot A_a \\ &= (10 \times 10^6) \times (1.4726 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 14726 \text{ N} \approx 14.726 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ Note that the retraction force is **lower** than extension force because the rod occupies part of the working area on the rod side. (This is a fundamental design constraint: a double-acting cylinder is *asymmetric* in force.) **Step 5 — Compute the extension velocity.** $$ \begin{aligned} v_{ext} &= \frac{Q}{A_p} \\ &= \frac{1.6667 \times 10^{-4}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= 0.0849 \text{ m/s} \approx 84.9 \text{ mm/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Compute the retraction velocity.** $$ \begin{aligned} v_{ret} &= \frac{Q}{A_a} \\ &= \frac{1.6667 \times 10^{-4}}{1.4726 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= 0.1132 \text{ m/s} \approx 113.2 \text{ mm/s} \end{aligned} $$ Observe the inverse relationship: the *smaller* the area, the *faster* the piston moves at the same flow rate. **Step 7 — Compute the time for full extension.** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{ext} &= \frac{L}{v_{ext}} \\ &= \frac{0.20}{0.0849} \\ &= 2.356 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8 — Compute the hydraulic power input.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{hyd} &= P_s \cdot Q \\ &= (10 \times 10^6) \times (1.6667 \times 10^{-4}) \\ &= 1666.7 \text{ W} \approx 1.667 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 9 — Compute the mechanical power output (extension).** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{mech} &= F_{ext} \cdot v_{ext} \\ &= 19635 \times 0.0849 \\ &= 1666.6 \text{ W} \approx 1.667 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ Notice $P_{hyd} \approx P_{mech}$ — this confirms **conservation of energy** (neglecting friction). This cross-check is a standard KTU 2024 evaluation trick. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Air Consumption of a Pneumatic Cylinder **Given:** - Pneumatic cylinder bore $D = 0.08$ m - Stroke $L = 0.30$ m - Working (gauge) pressure $P_g = 6$ bar - Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar - Absolute working pressure $P_{abs} = P_g + P_{atm} = 7.013$ bar **Step 1 — Compute the piston area.** $$ \begin{aligned} A_p &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.08)^2}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the cylinder internal volume per stroke (cap-end side).** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{cyl} &= A_p \cdot L = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.30 = 1.508 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 1.508 \text{ L} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Convert this to free-air volume (FAD equivalent).** The air supplied by the compressor is at atmospheric pressure. The air inside the cylinder is compressed to $P_{abs} = 7.013$ bar. By Boyle's Law ($P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$, isothermal approx): $$ \begin{aligned} V_{free} &= V_{cyl} \times \frac{P_{abs,work}}{P_{abs,atm}} \\ &= 1.508 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{7.013}{1.013} \\ &= 1.508 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.923 \\ &= 1.044 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3 = 10.44 \text{ L (free air)} \end{aligned} $$ This means **for every full extension stroke, ~10.44 L of free air** is consumed from the compressor. This is a critical figure for sizing the compressor in a pneumatic robot cell. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favorite) | Parameter | Hydraulic System | Pneumatic System | |-----------|-----------------|------------------| | Working Fluid | Oil (incompressible) | Air (compressible) | | Operating Pressure | 70–350 bar (typical 100–210 bar) | 4–8 bar (typical 6 bar) | | Force Density | Very High (≈ 30–50 kN from 50 mm bore at 200 bar) | Moderate (≈ 1.9 kN from 50 mm bore at 6 bar) | | Speed | 0.1 to 1 m/s (controlled) | 1 to 5 m/s (very fast) | | Position Accuracy | ±0.01 mm possible (servo) | ±1 mm typical (limited by air compressibility) | | Stiffness | Very high (oil bulk modulus ≈ 1.7 GPa) | Low (air bulk modulus ≈ 0.14 MPa) | | Energy Storage | Limited (small accumulator) | High (large receiver tank) | | Cost of System | High (pump, valves, filtration, leakage control) | Low (compressor, simple FRL) | | Maintenance | High (oil changes, leak repair) | Very Low (vent to atmosphere) | | Cleanliness | Can contaminate environment (oil leaks) | Clean (air exhaust is safe) | | Noise | Moderate (pump noise) | High (exhaust noise) | | Fire / Explosion Risk | Low (oil is not flammable in bulk, but spills are) | None (air is inert) | | Typical Robotic Application | Heavy-payload arm, CNC, press automation | Grippers, indexing, small pick-place, PCB assembly | | Energy Efficiency | 60–80% overall | 10–30% (compressor is very inefficient) | > [!TIP] > **Exam Strategy:** When a KTU 2024 question asks *compare* hydraulic and pneumatic actuators, structure your answer as a *table* (as above) plus a short prose justification. Examiners give full marks only if you mention **at least 6** distinct comparison points. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hydraulic System ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["Electric Motor
Prime Mover"] --> node2["Hydraulic Pump
Fixed or Variable Displacement"] node2 --> node3["Pressure Relief Valve
Safety at Max P"] node3 --> node4["Pressure Gauge
System Monitoring"] node4 --> node5["Directional Control Valve
4 by 3 DCV"] node5 --> node6["Flow Control Valve
Throttle for Speed"] node6 --> node7["Double Acting Cylinder
Bore D, Rod d"] node7 --> node8["Linear Motion Output
Stroke L"] node7 --> node9["Return Line"] node9 --> node10["Reservoir Tank
With Filter"] node10 --> node2 style node1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000 style node2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000 style node3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000000 style node4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000 style node5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000000 style node6 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000000 style node7 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000000 style node8 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000000 style node9 fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#000000 style node10 fill:#D2B48C,stroke:#000000 ``` **Explanation of the Flow:** The electric motor drives the hydraulic pump, which draws filtered oil from the reservoir and pressurizes it. The pressure relief valve protects the system from over-pressure spikes. The pressurized oil flows through a directional control valve (4/3) to control cylinder direction, and a flow control valve regulates piston speed. The double-acting cylinder converts fluid energy to linear mechanical output. Spent oil returns to the reservoir through the return line, completing the closed loop. ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pneumatic System ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Atmospheric Air
Inlet"] --> nodeB["Air Compressor
Electric Driven"] nodeB --> nodeC["Receiver Tank
Pressure Storage"] nodeC --> nodeD["FRL Unit
Filter Regulator Lubricator"] nodeD --> nodeE["Shut Off Valve
Isolation"] nodeE --> nodeF["Directional Control Valve
5 by 2 or 3 by 2 DCV"] nodeF --> nodeG["Flow Control Valve
Meter Out Speed"] nodeG --> nodeH["Double Acting Air Cylinder
Bore D, Rod d"] nodeH --> nodeI["Linear Piston Output
Stroke L"] nodeH --> nodeJ["Exhaust to Atmosphere
With Silencer"] style nodeA fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000000 style nodeB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000 style nodeC fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000000 style nodeD fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000000 style nodeE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000000 style nodeF fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000000 style nodeG fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000 style nodeH fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000000 style nodeI fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000 style nodeJ fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#000000 ``` **Explanation of the Flow:** Atmospheric air enters the compressor and is pressurized (typically to 7–8 bar). It is stored in a receiver tank to buffer demand spikes. The FRL unit removes moisture/particles (Filter), regulates pressure (Regulator), and adds oil mist for lubrication (Lubricator) — protecting downstream components. A directional control valve routes air to either side of the double-acting cylinder. A flow control valve meters the exhaust air to control retraction speed. The piston produces linear output. Exhaust air is vented to atmosphere, typically through a silencer for noise reduction. ### 4.3 Operational State Diagram — Double-Acting Cylinder ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> StateRetracted StateRetracted --> StateExtending: DCV Shifted to Position A
Cap End Pressurized StateExtending --> StateExtended: Piston Reaches End of Stroke
Limit Switch Triggered StateExtended --> StateRetracting: DCV Shifted to Position B
Rod End Pressurized StateRetracting --> StateRetracted: Piston Returns to Home
Cycle Complete StateRetracted --> [*] ``` **Explanation of the States:** The cylinder begins in the **retracted** state. When the directional control valve (DCV) is shifted to Position A, the cap-end is pressurized and the piston **extends**. When the end of the stroke is reached, a limit switch (or position sensor) signals the controller. The DCV then shifts to Position B, pressurizing the rod-end (and exhausting the cap-end), causing the piston to **retract**. Once back at the home position, the cycle is complete and ready to repeat. ### 4.4 Component Hierarchy — Pneumatic Cylinder Internal Structure ```mermaid flowchart TB cylEnd1["End Cap Rear"] --> cylBarrel["Cylinder Barrel
Honned Inner Surface"] cylBarrel --> cylPiston["Piston with Seals
Single or Double Lip"] cylPiston --> cylRod["Piston Rod
Hard Chrome Plated"] cylRod --> cylEnd2["End Cap Front
With Rod Seal"] cylBarrel -.-> portA["Port A
Cap End Inlet"] cylBarrel -.-> portB["Port B
Rod End Inlet"] cylPiston -.-> cushion["Cushion Region
For End of Stroke Damping"] cylRod -.-> wiper["Wiper Seal
Prevents Contamination"] style cylEnd1 fill:#D2B48C,stroke:#000000 style cylBarrel fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000000 style cylPiston fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000 style cylRod fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000 style cylEnd2 fill:#D2B48C,stroke:#000000 style portA fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000000 style portB fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000000 style cushion fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000000 style wiper fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000000 ``` **Explanation of the Structure:** A double-acting cylinder consists of a precision-honed **barrel**, a **piston** carrying sealing rings (to prevent internal leakage between the two chambers), a **rod** that transmits force to the load, and two **end caps** that seal the assembly. The **ports** at both ends allow air entry and exhaust. **Cushion regions** at each end of the stroke trap a small amount of fluid that must escape through a metering orifice, providing smooth deceleration at the end of stroke and preventing mechanical impact damage. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 3 Marks]** > > **Q1.** Distinguish between a single-acting and a double-acting cylinder. Give one robotic application of each. **Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):** A *single-acting cylinder* has **only one fluid port** and produces motion in one direction only; the return stroke is achieved by an external spring, gravity, or load weight. *Application:* a pneumatic gripper that opens by spring action and closes under air pressure. A *double-acting cylinder* has **two fluid ports** (cap-end and rod-end) and can produce powered motion in **both directions** (extend and retract) by alternating which port is pressurized. *Application:* the linear actuator driving a robot arm's shoulder joint, where bidirectional motion is required under load. > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 3 Marks]** > > **Q2.** Why are hydraulic actuators preferred over pneumatic actuators in heavy-payload industrial robots? **Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):** Hydraulic actuators are preferred for heavy-payload robots because: 1. They operate at much **higher pressures** (100–350 bar) compared to pneumatic systems (4–8 bar), producing **much higher forces** from cylinders of equivalent size. 2. Hydraulic oil is **virtually incompressible** (bulk modulus ≈ 1.7 GPa), giving the system **high stiffness and excellent position accuracy** under load. 3. The closed-loop nature (oil returned to reservoir) allows **precise servo control** for trajectory following, which is essential for a multi-axis robot arm carrying a heavy payload. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]** > > **Question A (14 Marks):** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a complete **hydraulic actuation system** used in a robotic manipulator. Clearly identify the function of each component. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of **80 mm** and a rod diameter of **45 mm**. It is supplied with oil at a pressure of **160 bar** and the pump flow rate is **25 L/min**. > - (i) Calculate the **extension force** and **retraction force** available. **(3 marks)** > - (ii) Calculate the **extension velocity** and the **time** required for a **stroke of 300 mm**. **(4 marks)** #### Model Solution for Question A: **Part (a) — 7 marks:** A complete hydraulic actuation system for a robotic manipulator consists of the following components, shown in the block diagram (see Section 4.1): 1. **Prime Mover (Electric Motor):** Provides the input mechanical energy to drive the system (1 mark). 2. **Hydraulic Pump:** Converts mechanical energy from the motor into hydraulic (pressure) energy. It draws oil from the reservoir and delivers it at high pressure (1 mark). 3. **Reservoir Tank:** Stores the hydraulic oil, allows cooling and de-aeration, and contains a suction filter (1 mark). 4. **Pressure Relief Valve:** A safety valve set to open at maximum system pressure, protecting components from over-pressure damage (0.5 mark). 5. **Directional Control Valve (4/3 or 4/2):** Routes pressurized oil to either the cap-end or rod-end of the cylinder, controlling the direction of piston motion (1 mark). 6. **Flow Control Valve:** Regulates the flow rate of oil entering or leaving the cylinder, thereby controlling piston speed (0.5 mark). 7. **Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder:** The final actuator that converts fluid pressure energy into linear mechanical motion, which drives a robot link (1 mark). 8. **Return Line and Piping:** Returns the spent oil from the cylinder to the reservoir, completing the closed loop (1 mark). **Part (b)(i) — 3 marks:** **Given:** $D = 0.080$ m, $d = 0.045$ m, $P_s = 160 \text{ bar} = 160 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 16 \times 10^6$ Pa. Compute the piston (cap-end) area: $$ \begin{aligned} A_p &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ Compute the annulus (rod-end effective) area: $$ \begin{aligned} A_a &= \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times ((0.080)^2 - (0.045)^2)}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times (0.0064 - 0.002025)}{4} = 3.4393 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ Extension force: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ext} &= P_s \times A_p = (16 \times 10^6) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) = 80424.8 \text{ N} \approx 80.42 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ Retraction force: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ret} &= P_s \times A_a = (16 \times 10^6) \times (3.4393 \times 10^{-3}) = 55028.7 \text{ N} \approx 55.03 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b)(ii) — 4 marks:** Convert flow rate: $Q = 25$ L/min $= 25 \times 10^{-3} / 60 = 4.1667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. Extension velocity: $$ \begin{aligned} v_{ext} &= \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{4.1667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0829 \text{ m/s} \approx 82.9 \text{ mm/s} \end{aligned} $$ Time for stroke $L = 0.300$ m: $$ \begin{aligned} t_{ext} &= \frac{L}{v_{ext}} = \frac{0.300}{0.0829} = 3.618 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ (For completeness, retraction velocity would be $Q / A_a = 0.1211$ m/s and retraction time = 2.477 s.) --- > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]** > > **Question B (14 Marks):** > **(a)** Explain the function and importance of the **FRL unit** in a pneumatic system. List the typical operating pressure range for industrial pneumatics. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A pneumatic cylinder has a bore of **100 mm**, a stroke of **250 mm**, and operates at a gauge pressure of **6 bar**. Calculate: > - (i) The **theoretical extension force**. **(3 marks)** > - (ii) The **free air consumption per stroke** in litres, assuming atmospheric pressure is 1.013 bar. **(4 marks)** #### Model Solution for Question B: **Part (a) — 7 marks:** The **FRL unit** (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) is a critical conditioning block installed immediately downstream of the receiver tank in every industrial pneumatic system.
