It performs three sequential functions (1.5 marks each for naming + function): 1. **Filter:** Removes solid contaminants (dust, rust, pipe scale) and condensed water/moisture from the compressed air. Typical filtration is 5–40 microns. Protects downstream valves and the cylinder from wear and corrosion. (1.5 marks) 2. **Regulator:** Maintains a constant downstream working pressure regardless of variations in upstream receiver pressure or downstream demand. Allows the operator to set the required working pressure (typically 4–8 bar) via an adjustment knob. Essential for repeatable and predictable cylinder force. (1.5 marks) 3. **Lubricator:** Adds a controlled mist of oil to the air stream to lubricate internal moving parts of downstream pneumatic components (valve spools, cylinder seals, motor vanes). This extends component life dramatically. (1.5 marks) **Typical Operating Pressure Range:** Industrial pneumatic systems operate between **4 bar and 8 bar** (gauge), with **6 bar (≈ 0.6 MPa)** being the most common standard worldwide. (1 mark for correct range stated) **Additional context (1 mark):** The FRL unit must be installed as close to the load (actuator) as possible, and in the order Filter → Regulator → Lubricator, to ensure the regulator's diaphragm is protected from contamination and the lubricator's oil is not blown back into the regulator. **Part (b)(i) — 3 marks:** Convert: $D = 0.100$ m, $P_g = 6$ bar $= 6 \times 10^5$ Pa. $$ \begin{aligned} A_p &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.100)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ext} &= P_g \times A_p = (6 \times 10^5) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) = 4712.4 \text{ N} \approx 4.71 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b)(ii) — 4 marks:** Piston area = $7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m² (from above). Cylinder internal volume: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{cyl} &= A_p \times L = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.250 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 1.9635 \text{ L} \end{aligned} $$ Absolute working pressure: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{abs,work} &= P_g + P_{atm} = 6 + 1.013 = 7.013 \text{ bar} \end{aligned} $$ Free air consumption (using Boyle's Law): $$ \begin{aligned} V_{free} &= V_{cyl} \times \frac{P_{abs,work}}{P_{atm}} = 1.9635 \times \frac{7.013}{1.013} \\ &= 1.9635 \times 6.923 = 13.59 \text{ L (free air per extension stroke)} \end{aligned} $$ (For a complete double-acting cycle, multiply by 2: $\approx 27.2$ L per full cycle.) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency (Most Frequent Deduction):** Students often mix bar, psi, MPa, and Pa within a single calculation. Convert **all pressures to Pascals (Pa)** at the start. 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa exactly. KTU examiners deduct up to 1 mark for unit inconsistency. > > 2. **Forgetting to Use Annulus Area for Retraction:** Many students wrongly compute retraction force as $F_{ret} = P \cdot A_p$ (same as extension). Remember: on retraction, the rod occupies part of the working area, so use $A_a = \frac{\pi(D^2 - d^2)}{4}$. > > 3. **Neglecting Absolute vs Gauge Pressure in Pneumatics:** The free-air consumption formula **requires absolute pressures** (gauge + atmospheric). Forgetting to add 1.013 bar to the gauge pressure gives a 15–20% error. KTU examiners specifically test this trap. > > 4. **Confusing Cylinder Extension/Retraction Velocity:** Use $A_p$ (full bore) for extension and $A_a$ (annulus) for retraction. Velocity is **inversely** proportional to area at the same flow rate. > > 5. **Omitting the FRL Sequence in Pneumatic Diagrams:** A common 2-mark loss in 7-mark questions. The correct order is **Filter → Regulator → Lubricator**, never reversed. > > 6. **Confusing Bulk Modulus Values:** A question may ask "Why are pneumatics 'softer' than hydraulics?" You **must** cite the bulk modulus: oil $\approx$ 1.7 GPa, air $\approx$ 0.14 MPa at atmospheric pressure — a ratio of roughly **10,000:1**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Hydraulic actuators** use *incompressible* oil at **100–350 bar**; *high force, high stiffness, slow to medium speed, closed loop*. - [x] **Pneumatic actuators** use *compressible* air at **4–8 bar**; *moderate force, low stiffness, high speed, open loop to atmosphere*. - [x] **Single-acting cylinder** = one port, spring/gravity return. **Double-acting cylinder** = two ports, powered in both directions. - [x] **Master formula:** $F = P \times A$ — applies to *every* fluid-power cylinder. - [x] **Piston (cap-end) area:** $A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$. - [x] **Annulus (rod-end) area:** $A_a = \frac{\pi(D^2 - d^2)}{4}$. - [x] **Velocity:** $v = \frac{Q}{A}$ (smaller area → faster piston). - [x] **Power:** $P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = F \cdot v$ (energy conservation cross-check). - [x] **Motor torque:** $T = \frac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi}$; **motor speed:** $\omega = \frac{Q}{D_m}$. - [x] **Pneumatic free-air consumption:** $V_{free} = V_{cyl} \times \frac{P_{abs,work}}{P_{abs,atm}}$ (always use absolute pressure). - [x] **FRL unit:** Filter → Regulator → Lubricator (in that order). Installed close to the load. - [x] **Directional control valves:** 4/3 DCV is standard for a double-acting cylinder (4 ports, 3 positions). - [x] **Pascal's Law:** pressure in a confined incompressible fluid acts equally in all directions — the foundation of hydraulic transmission. - [x] **Bulk modulus:** oil ≈ 1.7 GPa, air ≈ 0.14 MPa → hydraulics are ~10,000× stiffer than pneumatics. - [x] **Hydraulic robot applications:** heavy-payload arms, CNC feed drives, forging robots, underwater manipulators. - [x] **Pneumatic robot applications:** grippers, tool changers, pick-and-place, indexing tables, PCB assembly, food handling. - [x] **Always cite units** in final answers: force in N or kN, pressure in Pa or bar, flow in m³/s or L/min, area in m² or mm², velocity in m/s. - [x] **Energy cross-check:** $P \cdot Q$ should equal $F \cdot v$ within rounding error — use this to verify numerical answers.

topics.title: Control - On-Off Control - PID Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control - On-Off Control - PID Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Control Systems in Robotics — The Foundational Premise

In robotics, **Control** refers to the disciplined use of sensors, actuators, and computational algorithms to force a robot's physical state (position, velocity, force, temperature) to track a desired reference trajectory. Every joint, motor, and end-effector in a robotic manipulator is governed by a control loop that continuously measures the **error** between a *commanded* (set-point) value and the *measured* (actual) value, and issues corrective actuation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Control in robotics is the mechanism by which the energy supplied to the actuators is regulated to achieve a prescribed motion or force, ensuring stability, accuracy, and desired dynamic response.

### 1.2 On-Off Control (Two-Position Control)

**On-Off Control** — also called *Bang-Bang Control* or *Two-Position Control* — is the simplest and most economical form of feedback control. The controller output can take only **two discrete states**: fully ON or fully OFF. There is no intermediate (proportional) action.

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{\max} & \text{if } e(t) > 0 \\ U_{\min} & \text{if } e(t) < 0 \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Home Geyser:** Imagine an ordinary bathroom geyser. It has no knob for "half-power." The heating coil is either **ON** (when water temperature falls below the set value) or **OFF** (when the set temperature is reached). The same principle governs the relay-driven contactor in a robotic welding station's gas valve. You either flow shielding gas or you do not — there is no "partially open" command.

### 1.3 PID Control (Proportional-Integral-Derivative Control)

**PID Control** is the workhorse of industrial automation, estimated to be embedded in over **90%** of all practical control loops worldwide. It generates a continuous, smoothly varying control signal that is a *weighted sum* of three corrective actions computed from the error signal $e(t)$:

- **P (Proportional):** Reacts to the *present* error magnitude.
- **I (Integral):** Reacts to the *accumulated past* error (eliminates steady-state offset).
- **D (Derivative):** Reacts to the *rate of change* of error (anticipates future error).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Driving a Car on a Highway:** You are cruising at 100 km/h (set point). If you are 20 km/h below the set point, you press the accelerator *proportionally harder* (P). If a long incline has been pulling your speed down for 30 seconds, you keep pressing harder to *undo* the accumulated deficit (I). If you suddenly see a downhill slope ahead, you ease off the accelerator *in advance* because you anticipate the speed surge (D). A skilled human driver is, in effect, a biological PID controller.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used to Judge a Control Loop

| Metric | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Ideal Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rise Time | $t_r$ | Time to reach 90% of set-point | Minimum |
| Settling Time | $t_s$ | Time to remain within $\pm 2\%$ band | Minimum |
| Peak Overshoot | $M_p$ | Maximum excursion past set-point (%) | Zero (or $< 5\%$) |
| Steady-State Error | $e_{ss}$ | Residual error as $t \to \infty$ | Zero |
| Integral of Absolute Error | IAE | $\int \vert e \vert\, dt$ | Minimum |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 evaluations, the examiner almost always asks the student to *identify the deficiency* (e.g., "What is the steady-state error for a pure P controller?") rather than to *memorize numerical performance thresholds*. Conceptual clarity is weighted higher than rote numbers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 On-Off Control — Operational Mechanics

The On-Off controller is a **non-linear**, **discontinuous** device. Because its output is a step function, the controlled variable oscillates continuously around the set-point. To prevent *chattering* (rapid, destructive relay cycling near the set-point), a **dead-band** or **hysteresis gap** is introduced.

**Step 1 — Error Generation:** The comparator subtracts the measured variable $y(t)$ from the set-point $r(t)$:
$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$

**Step 2 — Decision Logic with Hysteresis:**
$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{\max} & \text{if } e(t) \geq +\delta \\ U_{\min} & \text{if } e(t) \leq -\delta \\ u(t^-) & \text{if } \vert e(t) \vert < \delta \end{cases}
$$
where $\delta$ is the **hysteresis width** (dead-band).

**Step 3 — Actuator Response:** The plant receives either maximum or minimum energy; no proportional staging occurs.

**Why use it?** Cost is minimal (a single relay, comparator, and threshold detector), and the loop is intrinsically stable for sluggish thermal or fluid systems. **Why avoid it?** Wear on mechanical relays, limit cycling, and inability to damp oscillations in fast electro-mechanical systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** On-Off control is universally deployed in domestic refrigerators, air-conditioners, iron-boxes, soldering stations, and small CNC coolant pumps. In robotics, it appears in end-stop detection, gripper open/close actuation, and emergency stop circuits.

### 2.2 PID Control — Operational Mechanics

The **generalized continuous-time PID control law** is:
$$
u(t) = K_p \, e(t) \;+\; K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau \;+\; K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

where:
- $K_p$ — Proportional gain (dimensionless or unit-specific)
- $K_i$ — Integral gain (units of $1/\text{time}$)
- $K_d$ — Derivative gain (units of time)

An equivalent *parallel* form using time-constants is:
$$
u(t) = K_p \!\left[ e(t) \;+\; \frac{1}{T_i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau \;+\; T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Proportional Action (P):** $u_P = K_p \cdot e(t)$
- Increases *responsiveness* and reduces rise time.
- **Cannot** eliminate steady-state error in a Type-0 plant.
- High $K_p$ ⇒ low rise time but **high overshoot and possible instability**.

**Step 2 — Integral Action (I):** $u_I = K_i \int e(t)\, dt$
- Accumulates past errors; drives the *steady-state* error to zero.
- Increases the system type (Type-0 → Type-1, etc.).
- High $K_i$ ⇒ eliminates offset but **slows response and can cause oscillations**.

**Step 3 — Derivative Action (D):** $u_D = K_d \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$
- Acts as a *predictor*; damps oscillations by opposing rapid error growth.
- Sensitive to **measurement noise** (differentiating noise amplifies it).
- High $K_d$ ⇒ reduces overshoot and improves stability margins.

### 2.3 The Laplace-Domain Transfer Function

Taking the Laplace transform of the PID law (assuming zero initial conditions):
$$
U(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \right) E(s)
$$

Therefore, the **PID controller transfer function** is:
$$
C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}
$$

For robotic joint position control, the plant is often approximated as a second-order system:
$$
G(s) = \frac{\omega_n^2}{s(s + 2\zeta \omega_n)}
$$

The **closed-loop transfer function** with unity feedback is:
$$
T(s) = \frac{C(s) G(s)}{1 + C(s) G(s)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The poles of $1 + C(s) G(s) = 0$ (the **characteristic equation**) dictate the stability of the closed-loop system. In KTU 2024 problems, students are routinely asked to apply the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** to these characteristic polynomials.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Error signal | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Same as plant output |
| 2 | On-Off law | $u(t) = U_{\max}$ if $e > \delta$ else $U_{\min}$ | Discontinuous |
| 3 | Hysteresis width | $\delta$ | Defines dead-band |
| 4 | PID law (time-domain) | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Continuous |
| 5 | PID law (time-constant form) | $u(t) = K_p \left[ e + \dfrac{1}{T_i}\int e\,dt + T_d \dot{e} \right]$ | $T_i, T_d$ in seconds |
| 6 | Laplace form of PID | $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | Transfer function |
| 7 | Closed-loop TF (unity FB) | $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$ | Stability from denominator |
| 8 | Steady-state error (final value) | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s)$ | For Type-1 with I-action $= 0$ |
| 9 | Characteristic equation | $1 + C(s) G(s) = 0$ | Routh array applied here |
| 10 | Tuning: Ziegler-Nichols (P-only) | $K_u$ = ultimate gain, $T_u$ = ultimate period | $K_p = 0.5 K_u$, $T_i = 0.8 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$ for PID |
| 11 | Tuning: Z-N formulas (PI) | $K_p = 0.45 K_u$, $T_i = 0.8 T_u$ | Empirical |
| 12 | Tuning: Z-N formulas (PID) | $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$ | Empirical |
| 13 | Integrator windup safeguard | Conditional integration or back-calculation | Clamp $u(t)$ at actuator limits |
| 14 | Derivative filter (practical) | $C_D(s) = \dfrac{K_d s}{1 + \tau_f s}$ | $\tau_f \approx 0.01$–$0.1$ s |
| 15 | Steady-state error of P-only | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p K_{dc}} \cdot 100\%$ | $K_{dc}$ = DC gain of plant |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotic manipulator joints:** PID governs torque applied to each servo motor.
- **Mobile robots:** PID controls heading angle (steering) and forward velocity.
- **Drones & UAVs:** Cascade PID loops for pitch, roll, yaw, and altitude.
- **CNC machines:** PID regulates spindle speed and axis position.
- **Process industries:** PID controls flow, level, pressure, and temperature (FLPT loops).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transfer Function for a PID-Controlled Robotic Joint

**Given:**
- Plant: second-order robotic joint with transfer function
$$
G(s) = \frac{1}{s(Js + B)}
$$
where $J$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²), $B$ = viscous friction coefficient (N·m·s/rad).
- Controller: $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$
- Unity negative feedback: $H(s) = 1$

**Step 1 —** Write the open-loop transfer function $L(s) = C(s) G(s)$:
$$
L(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) \cdot \frac{1}{s(Js + B)}
$$

**Step 2 —** Combine the PID terms over a common denominator $s$:
$$
K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \;=\; \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}
$$

**Step 3 —** Therefore:
$$
L(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s^2 (Js + B)}
$$

**Step 4 —** The closed-loop transfer function is:
$$
T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s^2 (Js + B) + K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}
$$

**Step 5 —** Expand the denominator:
$$
\text{Denom}(s) = J s^3 + B s^2 + K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i = J s^3 + (B + K_d) s^2 + K_p s + K_i
$$

**Step 6 —** Final compact form:
$$
T(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{J s^3 + (B + K_d) s^2 + K_p s + K_i}
$$

**Interpretation:** The denominator (the characteristic polynomial) is a **cubic**. For asymptotic stability, all its roots must lie in the open left-half plane. Applying Routh's criterion yields the *necessary and sufficient* gain conditions.

---

### 3.2 Routh-Hurwitz Stability Analysis of the PID-Controlled Joint

Consider the characteristic polynomial derived above:
$$
P(s) = J s^3 + (B + K_d) s^2 + K_p s + K_i
$$

Let $J = 1$, $B = 2$ for simplicity:
$$
P(s) = s^3 + (2 + K_d) s^2 + K_p s + K_i
$$

**Step 1 —** Construct the Routh array:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $K_p$ |
| $s^2$ | $2 + K_d$ | $K_i$ |
| $s^1$ | $A$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K_i$ | — |

where
$$
A = \frac{(2 + K_d) \cdot K_p - 1 \cdot K_i}{2 + K_d} = \frac{(2 + K_d) K_p - K_i}{2 + K_d}
$$

**Step 2 —** Apply the stability condition (first column strictly positive):
- $2 + K_d > 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K_d > -2$ (always true for positive damping)
- $A > 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (2 + K_d) K_p > K_i$
- $K_i > 0$

**Step 3 —** Combining, the **necessary and sufficient stability conditions** are:
$$
\boxed{K_d > -2, \quad K_i > 0, \quad K_i < (2 + K_d) K_p}
$$

These are the *exact* inequalities a KTU examiner expects a student to derive in a 7-mark "design for stability" sub-question.

---

### 3.3 Step-Response of a Pure P Controller on a First-Order Plant

**Given:** Plant $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{1 + \tau s}$, controller $C(s) = K_p$, reference $R(s) = \dfrac{A}{s}$.

**Step 1 —** Closed-loop transfer function:
$$
T(s) = \frac{K K_p}{1 + \tau s + K K_p}
$$

**Step 2 —** Time-domain step response (for input of magnitude $A$):
$$
y(t) = \frac{A \, K K_p}{1 + K K_p} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau_{cl}} \right), \quad \tau_{cl} = \frac{\tau}{1 + K K_p}
$$

**Step 3 —** Steady-state value:
$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} y(t) = \frac{A \, K K_p}{1 + K K_p}
$$

**Step 4 —** Steady-state error:
$$
e_{ss} = A - \frac{A \, K K_p}{1 + K K_p} = \frac{A}{1 + K K_p}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This proves the **fundamental P-only limitation:** $e_{ss} \ne 0$ for a finite $K_p$. The error can be made *small* by increasing $K_p$ (high-gain) but **never** driven to exactly zero. This is the single most-tested derivation in the KTU 2024 robotics paper.

---

### 3.4 Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate-Cycle Tuning Derivation (P-only)

**Step 1 —** Set $K_i = 0$ and $K_d = 0$; only $K_p$ is active.

**Step 2 —** Increase $K_p$ slowly until the closed-loop exhibits **sustained sinusoidal oscillations** of constant amplitude. Record this critical gain as $K_u$ and the oscillation period as $T_u$.

**Step 3 —** Use the Z-N empirical table to recover $K_p$, $T_i$, $T_d$:

| Controller | $K_p$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | $0.5 K_u$ | — | — |
| PI | $0.45 K_u$ | $0.8 T_u$ | — |
| PID | $0.6 K_u$ | $0.5 T_u$ | $0.125 T_u$ |

**Step 4 —** Convert $T_i$ and $T_d$ back to $K_i$ and $K_d$:
$$
K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i}, \quad K_d = K_p \cdot T_d
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Z-N tuning yields a **quarter-amplitude-damping** response (overshoot $\approx 25\%$). It is a good *starting point* but always requires manual fine-tuning for production deployment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 On-Off Control Block Diagram (with Hysteresis)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refnode[Set Point r of t] --> compNode{Comparator}
    fbnode[Measured y of t] --> compNode
    compNode --> errNode[Error e of t]
    errNode --> hystNode[Hysteresis Element delta]
    hystNode --> actNode[Actuator Umax or Umin]
    actNode --> plantNode[Plant Robotic Joint]
    plantNode --> sensorNode[Position Sensor]
    sensorNode --> fbnode
```

**Interpretation:** The *Hysteresis Element* is the key non-linearity. The sensor feeds the measured joint angle back to the comparator, and the actuator receives a binary command. Rapid relay cycling near the set-point is suppressed because the comparator only flips state when $e(t)$ exceeds $\pm \delta$.

### 4.2 PID Control Closed-Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    setR[Reference r of t] --> sumNode{Summing Junction}
    fbY[Feedback y of t] --> sumNode
    sumNode --> errSig[Error e of t]
    errSig --> pBlock[Proportional Kp]
    errSig --> iBlock[Integral Ki over s]
    errSig --> dBlock[Derivative Kd times s]
    pBlock --> sumCtl{Summing Point}
    iBlock --> sumCtl
    dBlock --> sumCtl
    sumCtl --> ctrlOut[Control u of t]
    ctrlOut --> plantG[Plant G of s]
    plantG --> outY[Output y of t]
    outY --> sensorH[Sensor H of s]
    sensorH --> fbY
```

**Interpretation:** The error signal is split into **three parallel branches** (P, I, D). Each branch applies a *different mathematical operator* to the same error, and the three branch outputs are summed to form the actuator command $u(t)$. This is the **parallel (or non-interacting) form** of the PID controller.

### 4.3 Functional Signal-Flow of PID Action on a Robotic Joint

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cmdNode[Desired Joint Angle theta_d] --> errCalc[Error = theta_d minus theta_a]
    measNode[Actual Angle theta_a from Encoder] --> errCalc
    errCalc --> pTerm[P Term: Kp times e]
    errCalc --> iTerm[I Term: Ki times integral of e dt]
    errCalc --> dTerm[D Term: Kd times d e by dt]
    pTerm --> torqueSum[Total Torque tau]
    iTerm --> torqueSum
    dTerm --> torqueSum
    torqueSum --> motorDrv[Servo Motor Drive]
    motorDrv --> jointDyn[Robot Joint Dynamics]
    jointDyn --> measNode
```

**Interpretation:** This is a *physical* (not mathematical) view. The encoder reports $\theta_a$, the error is computed in software or analog hardware, the three corrective torques are summed, and the motor receives the net drive. Feedback is implicit through the joint dynamics block.

### 4.4 Anti-Windup Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    eIn[Error e] --> intNode[Integrator]
    intNode --> sumAw{Summing}
    fbAw[Anti Windup Feedback] --> sumAw
    sumAw --> uOut[Controller Output]
    uOut --> satNode[Saturation Limit umin to umax]
    satNode --> plantIn[Plant Input]
    plantIn --> yOut[Plant Output]
    satNode --> fbAw
```

**Interpretation:** The saturated actuator output is *fed back* to the integrator's input. When saturation is reached, the integrator is *discharged* (its accumulation is reversed) so that the I-term does not "wind up" to huge values during actuator saturation. This is essential in robotic joints with bounded motor torque.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PECDT633]**

**Q1.** Distinguish between **On-Off control** and **PID control** in the context of a robotic manipulator joint. Mention **two** advantages of PID over On-Off control.
*(Module Outcome: CO2, Bloom Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | On-Off Control | PID Control |
|---|---|---|
| Output nature | Two discrete states (ON/OFF) | Continuous, smoothly varying |
| Steady-state error | Persists as limit cycle | Zero (with integral action) |
| Oscillations | Sustained limit cycling around set-point | Damped (with proper tuning) |
| Wear & tear | High (relay chattering) | Low (analog or PWM) |
| Tuning | Not applicable | Z-N, Cohen-Coon, software auto-tuning |

**Two advantages of PID:** (i) Elimination of steady-state error via integral action. (ii) Reduced overshoot and faster settling via derivative action.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]**

**Q2.** What is **hysteresis** in an On-Off controller? Why is it necessary, and how is it implemented in a practical robotic gas-valve actuator?
*(Module Outcome: CO2, Bloom Level: Remember + Apply)*

**Model Answer:**
Hysteresis is a deliberately introduced *dead-band* ($\pm \delta$) around the set-point within which the controller output remains in its previous state. It is necessary to **prevent relay chattering** (rapid ON/OFF cycling) when the error oscillates around zero. In a robotic gas-valve actuator, hysteresis is implemented by adding a Schmitt-trigger comparator stage between the error amplifier and the solenoid relay coil. The width $\delta$ is chosen as $\approx 2\%$–$5\%$ of the set-point to balance protection against premature switching against excessive steady-state cycling.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Attempt this OR Question B**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled block diagram, explain the operation of a **PID controller** for position control of a robotic joint. Derive the expression for the control signal $u(t)$ in the time domain and in the Laplace domain.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robotic joint has the plant transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s + 5)}$. A PID controller with $K_p = 8$, $K_i = 4$, $K_d = 0.5$ is used. Determine the **closed-loop transfer function** and apply the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** to comment on stability.

**Model Solution to (a):**

The block diagram (refer Section 4.2) shows the error $e(t)$ split into three parallel paths:
- Proportional path: $u_P = K_p e(t)$
- Integral path: $u_I = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$
- Derivative path: $u_D = K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$

The total control signal is their sum:
$$
u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):
$$
U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d s\, E(s)
$$

Factoring $E(s)$:
$$
\boxed{C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}}
$$

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 —** Open-loop transfer function:
$$
L(s) = C(s) G(s) = \left(0.5 s + 8 + \frac{4}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{10}{s(s + 5)} = \frac{(0.5 s^2 + 8 s + 4)}{s} \cdot \frac{10}{s(s+5)}
$$

**Step 2 —** Simplify:
$$
L(s) = \frac{10(0.5 s^2 + 8 s + 4)}{s^2(s+5)} = \frac{5 s^2 + 80 s + 40}{s^2(s+5)}
$$

**Step 3 —** Closed-loop denominator (characteristic polynomial):
$$
1 + L(s) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s^2(s+5) + 5 s^2 + 80 s + 40 = 0
$$

**Step 4 —** Expand:
$$
s^3 + 5 s^2 + 5 s^2 + 80 s + 40 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s^3 + 10 s^2 + 80 s + 40 = 0
$$

**Step 5 —** Routh array:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $80$ |
| $s^2$ | $10$ | $40$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{10 \cdot 80 - 1 \cdot 40}{10} = 76$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $40$ | — |

**Step 6 —** First column: $\{1,\; 10,\; 76,\; 40\}$ — **all positive, no sign changes**.

**Conclusion:** The closed-loop system is **asymptotically stable**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **On-Off control** mechanism with a hysteresis band. Sketch the *output vs. error* characteristic and the *time response* showing limit cycling. Mention two industrial applications in robotics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A thermal process in a soldering station has $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{30 s + 1}$. A pure P-controller with $K_p = 10$ is used with a set-point of $200^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate the **steady-state temperature** and the **steady-state error**. What additional gain would reduce the error to $0.5^\circ\text{C}$?

**Model Solution to (a):**

The On-Off controller output is given by:
$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{\max} = 100\% & \text{if } e(t) \geq +\delta \\ U_{\min} = 0\% & \text{if } e(t) \leq -\delta \\ \text{Hold} & \text{if } \vert e(t) \vert < \delta \end{cases}
$$

The **output vs. error characteristic** is a *two-step function with a vertical jump at $e = +\delta$ and another at $e = -\delta$*. Between the two thresholds lies the hysteresis dead-band.

In the **time-response plot**, the controlled variable (e.g., soldering tip temperature) rises towards the set-point under full heating ($U_{\max}$), overshoots slightly, and crosses $+\delta$, at which point the heater switches OFF. The temperature decays, crosses $-\delta$, and the heater switches ON again. This produces a *continuous limit cycle* (typically $\pm 2$–$5^\circ\text{C}$ for a $200^\circ\text{C}$ set-point).

**Two robotic applications:**
1. Soldering iron tip temperature regulation in PCB assembly robots.
2. Coolant pump ON/OFF control in CNC machining cells.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 —** Closed-loop TF with P-only controller:
$$
T(s) = \frac{K_p G(s)}{1 + K_p G(s)} = \frac{20}{30 s + 1 + 20} = \frac{20}{30 s + 21}
$$

**Step 2 —** Steady-state output for $R(s) = \dfrac{200}{s}$:
$$
y_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot T(s) \cdot R(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{20}{30 s + 21} \cdot \frac{200}{s} = \frac{20 \times 200}{21} = 190.48^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 —** Steady-state error:
$$
e_{ss} = 200 - 190.48 = 9.52^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 —** To reduce $e_{ss}$ to $0.5^\circ\text{C}$, the new gain $K_p^{\text{new}}$ must satisfy:
$$
\frac{2 K_p^{\text{new}}}{1 + 2 K_p^{\text{new}}} \cdot 200 = 199.5 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{2 K_p^{\text{new}}}{1 + 2 K_p^{\text{new}}} = 0.9975
$$

Solving: $2 K_p^{\text{new}} = 0.9975(1 + 2 K_p^{\text{new}})$
$\Rightarrow 2 K_p^{\text{new}} = 0.9975 + 1.995 K_p^{\text{new}}$
$\Rightarrow 0.005 K_p^{\text{new}} = 0.9975$
$\Rightarrow K_p^{\text{new}} = 199.5$

**Conclusion:** The proportional gain must be increased from $10$ to approximately $199.5$ — a **20× increase** — to achieve $e_{ss} = 0.5^\circ\text{C}$. This demonstrates the fundamental P-only limitation; in practice, **integral action** is added to achieve zero steady-state error without such aggressive gain.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $e_{ss} = 0$ with stability.** A stable system can still have a large steady-state error; the two are independent concepts.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign convention.** KTU uses $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ (negative feedback). Reversing the sign flips the controller action from "direct" to "reverse" and destabilizes most plants.
> 3. **Ignoring actuator saturation.** When computing $u(t)$, students often forget to clamp the value at $U_{\min}$ and $U_{\max}$ and proceed with unrealizable signals.
> 4. **Routh array arithmetic errors.** A single miscalculation in the $s^1$ row propagates to a wrong stability conclusion — the most common mark-loss in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 5. **Skipping the integration of D-action with the P-action.** Derivative gain $K_d$ is frequently forgotten when writing the closed-loop characteristic polynomial.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **On-Off Control** is binary, non-linear, and inherently oscillatory; used in low-cost thermal and pneumatic systems.
- **Hysteresis** $\delta$ prevents relay chattering but introduces a residual cycling band of width $2\delta$.
- **PID Control** is a continuous linear combination of $P$, $I$, and $D$ actions: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$.
- **Proportional (P):** Reacts to the *present* error; reduces rise time; cannot eliminate steady-state error.
- **Integral (I):** Reacts to the *past* error; drives steady-state error to zero; slows response.
- **Derivative (D):** Reacts to the *rate* of change of error; damps overshoot; sensitive to noise.
- **Laplace-domain PID form:** $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$.
- **Closed-loop TF:** $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$.
- **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** applied to the characteristic polynomial $1 + C(s)G(s) = 0$ is the standard stability test.
- **Ziegler-Nichols tuning** (ultimate-cycle method) is a *starting-point* empirical procedure; for PID, $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.
- **Integrator windup** must be prevented via conditional integration or back-calculation when the actuator saturates.
- **Derivative filter** $\dfrac{K_d s}{1 + \tau_f s}$ is mandatory in real hardware to avoid amplifying encoder/sensor noise.
- **Steady-state error of pure P-controller:** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p K_{dc}} \cdot 100\%$ — never zero for a finite $K_p$.
- **Robotic applications of PID:** joint position, mobile-robot steering, drone attitude, CNC spindle, conveyor speed.
- **Robotic applications of On-Off:** gripper actuation, end-stop detection, emergency stop, gas/coolant valves.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Control and Position Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity Control and Position Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Robot Control?

In robotics, **Control** refers to the algorithmic and hardware mechanism by which a robot's actuators (motors at the joints) are driven so that the **end-effector** (gripper/tool) follows a desired trajectory (position, velocity, and acceleration profile) accurately, stably, and robustly.

Within the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Module 2*, two foundational control paradigms are emphasized:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Control** governs *how fast* and *in which direction* the robot's joints or end-effector moves at any given instant.
> **Position Control** governs *where* the joints or end-effector must be located at a given time, ensuring geometric accuracy of motion.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Terminology)

### 🔹 Velocity Control
**Velocity Control** is a closed-loop control strategy in which the controlled output variable is the **rate of change of position** (linear or angular velocity) of the robot's joints (joint space) or end-effector (Cartesian/task space). The controller computes the joint velocities required to track a desired Cartesian velocity of the end-effector, typically through the **inverse Jacobian**.

### 🔹 Position Control
**Position Control** is a closed-loop control strategy in which the controlled output variable is the **spatial location (and orientation)** of the robot's joints or end-effector. The controller minimizes the **position error** between a desired pose and the actual measured pose, commanding joint torques/velocities accordingly.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Driving a Car (Position Control):**
> Imagine you are driving a car and a passenger tells you, *"Stop exactly in front of the blue gate."* You continuously observe *how far* you are from the blue gate (error) and steer, accelerate, or brake to *close* that gap. The car ends up at the **right place** regardless of speed. This is **position control** — accuracy of *where* matters most.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Cruise Control on a Highway (Velocity Control):**
> Now imagine cruise control set to **80 km/h**. The system does not care if you are at km-post 42 or km-post 47; it only ensures your **speed** matches 80 km/h by adjusting throttle. This is **velocity control** — accuracy of *how fast* matters most.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — A Drone in a Wind Gust:**
> A drone tries to hover at point P. The wind keeps pushing it. The drone's controller computes (a) how far it has drifted from P → **position error**, and (b) how fast it is drifting → **velocity error**. Both errors are fed back. Industrial robots use the same dual-loop idea!

## 1.4 Why Both Controls Are Essential

| Control Type | Primary Concern | Typical Application |
|--------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Position Control** | Geometric accuracy of final pose | Pick-and-place, welding, assembly, painting |
| **Velocity Control** | Smoothness of motion & trajectory tracking | Continuous-path tasks like cutting, polishing, spray painting, glue dispensing |

A modern industrial robot is rarely purely one or the other — it is a **hybrid position-velocity (and sometimes force) controller**.

## 1.5 Control Spaces: Joint Space vs Cartesian (Task) Space

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Joint Space** — describes the robot using the angles of its revolute joints $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n$ or the displacements of prismatic joints.
> **Cartesian (Task) Space** — describes the robot by the position $(x, y, z)$ and orientation (roll, pitch, yaw) of its end-effector in the world frame.

A control law can be implemented in *either* space, but the **Jacobian matrix** is the mathematical bridge between them.

## 1.6 Standard Industrial Performance Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Accuracy:** repeatability of reaching a commanded pose (typically **±0.02 mm** for high-precision industrial robots).
- **Resolution:** smallest incremental motion the controller can command (often **0.001 mm / 0.001°**).
- **Bandwidth:** frequency range over which the controller tracks a reference (industrial robots: **10–100 Hz**).
- **Settling Time:** time to reach and stay within a tolerance band (e.g., **±2%** of final value).
- **Steady-State Error:** residual error at the end of a motion (driven to ~0 by integral action).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Jacobian: The Heart of Velocity Control

The **Jacobian matrix** $J(\theta)$ is an $m \times n$ matrix (where $m$ is the dimension of the task space and $n$ is the number of joints) that **linearly maps joint-space velocities to Cartesian (task-space) velocities** of the end-effector.

### 2.1.1 Fundamental Velocity Relationship

The relationship between joint velocities and end-effector linear/angular velocity is:

$$
\dot{x} = J(\theta)\,\dot{\theta}
$$

Where:
- $\dot{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the **end-effector twist** (linear + angular velocity)
- $J(\theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the **Jacobian matrix** evaluated at configuration $\theta$
- $\dot{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **joint velocity vector**

### 2.1.2 Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC) — Whitney (1969)

To make the end-effector move at a desired Cartesian velocity $\dot{x}_d$, we *invert* the mapping:

$$
\dot{\theta} = J(\theta)^{-1}\,\dot{x}_d
$$

For **non-square** (redundant) or **singular** Jacobians, the **Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse** $J^{+}$ is used:

$$
\dot{\theta} = J(\theta)^{+}\,\dot{x}_d
$$

Where $J^{+} = J^{T}(J J^{T})^{-1}$.

## 2.2 Position Control Architectures

### 2.2.1 Joint-Space Position Control (Decentralized / Independent Joint Control)

Each joint $i$ has its **own independent servo loop**. The control law for joint $i$ is most commonly a **PID controller**:

$$
u_i(t) = K_p\,e_i(t) + K_i \int_0^t e_i(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d\,\dot{e}_i(t)
$$

The position error for joint $i$ is:

$$
e_i(t) = \theta_{d,i}(t) - \theta_{i}(t)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PID gains $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ are tuned to satisfy: **stability**, **zero steady-state error**, and **acceptable transient response** (rise time, overshoot, settling time).

### 2.2.2 Cartesian-Space Position Control

The position error is computed **in task space**:

$$
e_{cart}(t) = x_d(t) - x(t)
$$

Then mapped to **joint-space** corrections via the **inverse Jacobian** and the **inverse kinematics**:

$$
\theta_d(t) = \text{InverseKinematics}\big(x_d(t)\big)
$$

## 2.3 The Computed Torque Control Law (Model-Based Position Control)

For high-performance position control, a model-based approach using the **manipulator dynamics equation** is used. The dynamic model of an $n$-joint robot is:

$$
M(\theta)\,\ddot{\theta} + C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\,\dot{\theta} + G(\theta) + F(\dot{\theta}) = \tau
$$

Where:
- $M(\theta)$ is the **$n \times n$ mass/inertia matrix** (symmetric, positive-definite)
- $C(\theta,\dot{\theta})$ is the **Coriolis & centrifugal matrix**
- $G(\theta)$ is the **gravitational torque vector**
- $F(\dot{\theta})$ is the **friction vector**
- $\tau$ is the **joint torque vector** applied by the actuators

The **Computed Torque Control** law is:

$$
\tau = M(\theta)\big[\ddot{\theta}_d + K_p\,e + K_d\,\dot{e}\big] + C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\,\dot{\theta} + G(\theta)
$$

Where the position and velocity errors are:

$$
e = \theta_d - \theta, \qquad \dot{e} = \dot{\theta}_d - \dot{\theta}
$$

This **linearizes** and **decouples** the nonlinear robot dynamics into $n$ independent second-order systems, each governed by the error dynamics:

$$
\ddot{e} + K_d\,\dot{e} + K_p\,e = 0
$$

By choosing $K_p$ and $K_d$ (typically diagonal matrices), the engineer places the **closed-loop poles** to achieve the desired response.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\dot{x} = J(\theta)\,\dot{\theta}$ | Forward velocity kinematics (Jacobian map) | $\dot{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, $\dot{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| 2 | $\dot{\theta} = J^{-1}(\theta)\,\dot{x}$ | Resolved rate control (square, non-singular $J$) | Valid only if $J$ is invertible |
| 3 | $\dot{\theta} = J^{+}(\theta)\,\dot{x}$ | Pseudoinverse control (redundant / singular) | $J^{+} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $e(t) = x_d(t) - x(t)$ | Task-space position error | Vector, units of m or rad |
| 5 | $e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta(t)$ | Joint-space position error | Vector, units of rad or m |
| 6 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | PID control law | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ are gain matrices |
| 7 | $\tau = M\theta\ddot{\theta} + C\theta\theta\dot{\theta} + G\theta$ | Manipulator dynamic equation | $M$: inertia, $C$: Coriolis, $G$: gravity |
| 8 | $\tau = M\theta\ddot{\theta_d} + K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + C\theta\theta\dot{\theta} + G\theta$ | Computed torque control law | Linearizes the closed-loop |
| 9 | $\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$ | Closed-loop error dynamics | $K_p, K_d > 0$ for stability |
| 10 | $\omega^2_n = K_p$, $\quad 2\zeta\omega_n = K_d$ | Natural frequency & damping relations | For 2nd-order tracking error |
| 11 | $\dot{\theta}_{max} = \sqrt{\lambda_{min}(JJ^{T})^{-1}} \cdot \dot{x}_{max}$ | Velocity limit at singular neighborhoods | $\lambda_{min}$ is smallest eigenvalue |
| 12 | $\det J = 0$ | Singularity condition | Robot loses a DOF |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, **never confuse $\dot{x}$ (Cartesian velocity) with $\dot{\theta}$ (joint velocity)**. Examiners specifically check whether the candidate understands the *direction* of the Jacobian mapping.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC Machining & 3D Printing:** Position control with sub-micron accuracy for tool-path tracking.
- **Welding Robots (e.g., automotive assembly lines):** Continuous-path velocity control with high path-following accuracy.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Combined position + velocity + force control for safe tissue interaction.
- **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Wheel-velocity control for path tracking; position control for docking.
- **Drone Swarms:** Velocity control for collision-free formation flying; position control for hovering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Velocity Kinematics from Position Kinematics

Let the end-effector pose be given by the forward kinematics function:

$$
x = f(\theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}
$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to time (using the **chain rule**):

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{\partial f(\theta)}{\partial \theta}\,\frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

By definition, the **Jacobian matrix** is:

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{\partial f(\theta)}{\partial \theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{x} = J(\theta)\,\dot{\theta}
$$

**Interpretation:** The Jacobian *locally linearizes* the nonlinear kinematic map $f(\cdot)$ at the current configuration $\theta$. For an infinitesimal joint displacement $d\theta$, the resulting end-effector displacement is $dx = J\,d\theta$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Resolved Motion Rate Control (Inverse Velocity)

**Goal:** Given a desired end-effector velocity $\dot{x}_d$, find the joint velocities $\dot{\theta}$ that achieve it.

Starting from the velocity equation:

$$
\dot{x}_d = J(\theta)\,\dot{\theta}
$$

Pre-multiplying both sides by $J^{-1}$ (assuming $J$ is square and non-singular):

$$
J^{-1}(\theta)\,\dot{x}_d = J^{-1}(\theta)\,J(\theta)\,\dot{\theta}
$$

$$
J^{-1}(\theta)\,\dot{x}_d = I\,\dot{\theta}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{\theta} = J^{-1}(\theta)\,\dot{x}_d}
$$

For a **6-DOF robot with 6 joints** ($m = n = 6$), the Jacobian is $6 \times 6$ and invertible **away from singularities**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Closed-Loop Error Dynamics for Computed Torque Control

**Step 1:** Write the manipulator dynamics:

$$
M(\theta)\,\ddot{\theta} + C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\,\dot{\theta} + G(\theta) = \tau
$$

**Step 2:** Define the position and velocity errors:

$$
e = \theta_d - \theta
$$

$$
\dot{e} = \dot{\theta}_d - \dot{\theta}
$$

Differentiating $\dot{e}$ once more:

$$
\ddot{e} = \ddot{\theta}_d - \ddot{\theta}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the control law $\tau = M(\theta)\big[\ddot{\theta}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}\big] + C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\,\dot{\theta} + G(\theta)$ into the dynamics:

$$
M(\theta)\,\ddot{\theta} + C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\,\dot{\theta} + G(\theta) = M(\theta)\big[\ddot{\theta}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}\big] + C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\,\dot{\theta} + G(\theta)
$$

**Step 4:** Cancel the common terms $C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\,\dot{\theta}$ and $G(\theta)$ on both sides:

$$
M(\theta)\,\ddot{\theta} = M(\theta)\big[\ddot{\theta}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}\big]
$$

**Step 5:** Pre-multiply both sides by $M(\theta)^{-1}$ (which exists because $M$ is positive-definite):

$$
\ddot{\theta} = \ddot{\theta}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange using $\ddot{e} = \ddot{\theta}_d - \ddot{\theta}$:

$$
\ddot{\theta}_d - \ddot{e} = \ddot{\theta}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}
$$

$$
-\ddot{e} = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}
$$

$$
\boxed{\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0}
$$

This is a **decoupled linear second-order system** in $e$. Each joint $i$ behaves like an independent critically-damped or under-damped oscillator.

**Step 7 (KTU-favorite follow-up):** For critical damping, set:

$$
K_d = 2\sqrt{K_p}
$$

Then the system has two equal real poles at $s = -\sqrt{K_p}$ and the response is critically damped with no overshoot — the standard "no-oscillation tracking" requirement for assembly robots.

## 3.4 Derivation: Pseudoinverse for Redundant Manipulators

For an $n$-DOF robot operating in an $m$-DOF task space with $n > m$ (redundant), $J$ is a **tall** $m \times n$ matrix, so $J^{-1}$ does not exist.

We seek $\dot{\theta}$ that minimizes $\|\dot{x} - J\dot{\theta}\|^2$. Solving the least-squares problem:

$$
\min_{\dot{\theta}} \|J\dot{\theta} - \dot{x}_d\|^2
$$

Setting the gradient with respect to $\dot{\theta}$ to zero:

$$
J^{T}(J\dot{\theta} - \dot{x}_d) = 0
$$

$$
J^{T}J\,\dot{\theta} = J^{T}\dot{x}_d
$$

$$
(J^{T}J)^{-1}J^{T} = J^{+}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{\theta} = J^{+}\,\dot{x}_d = (J^{T}J)^{-1}J^{T}\,\dot{x}_d}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** The pseudoinverse gives the **minimum-norm** joint velocity that best tracks $\dot{x}_d$ in the least-squares sense. This is crucial for robots like the 7-DOF KUKA LWR or the human-arm-like 7-DOF Franka Emika Panda.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> A 2-DOF planar robot has end-effector position $x = (L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12},\; L_1 s_1 + L_2 s_{12})$ where $c_1 = \cos\theta_1$, $s_1 = \sin\theta_1$, $c_{12} = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, $s_{12} = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$.
>
> Given: $L_1 = L_2 = 1$ m, $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$, $\dot{\theta}_1 = 0.5$ rad/s, $\dot{\theta}_2 = 0.3$ rad/s.
>
> **Find the end-effector linear velocity $\dot{x} = (\dot{x}_1, \dot{x}_2)^T$.**

**Step 1:** Construct the Jacobian by partial differentiation of $x$ with respect to $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$:

$$
J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial x_1}{\partial \theta_1} & \frac{\partial x_1}{\partial \theta_2} \\[4pt] \frac{\partial x_2}{\partial \theta_1} & \frac{\partial x_2}{\partial \theta_2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing each partial derivative:

$$
\frac{\partial x_1}{\partial \theta_1} = -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x_1}{\partial \theta_2} = -L_2 s_{12}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x_2}{\partial \theta_1} = L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x_2}{\partial \theta_2} = L_2 c_{12}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values. With $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$:

$$
\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 75°
$$

$$
s_1 = 0.5, \quad c_1 = 0.866, \quad s_{12} = 0.966, \quad c_{12} = 0.259
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x_1}{\partial \theta_1} = -1(0.5) - 1(0.966) = -1.466 \text{ m/rad}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x_1}{\partial \theta_2} = -1(0.966) = -0.966 \text{ m/rad}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x_2}{\partial \theta_1} = 1(0.866) + 1(0.259) = 1.125 \text{ m/rad}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x_2}{\partial \theta_2} = 1(0.259) = 0.259 \text{ m/rad}
$$

**Step 3:** Form the Jacobian:

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -1.466 & -0.966 \\ 1.125 & 0.259 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m/rad}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\dot{x} = J\,\dot{\theta}$ with $\dot{\theta} = (0.5, \; 0.3)^T$ rad/s:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = (-1.466)(0.5) + (-0.966)(0.3) = -0.733 - 0.290 = -1.023 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = (1.125)(0.5) + (0.259)(0.3) = 0.5625 + 0.0777 = 0.640 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.023 \\ 0.640 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m/s}}
$$

**Step 5 (Bonus — velocity magnitude):**

$$
\|\dot{x}\| = \sqrt{(-1.023)^2 + (0.640)^2} = \sqrt{1.0465 + 0.4096} = \sqrt{1.4561} \approx 1.207 \text{ m/s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU valuation, always **state units explicitly** in the final answer. A numerical answer *without* units is typically marked down by 0.5 to 1 mark.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Joint-Space Position Control | Cartesian-Space Position Control | Velocity Control (RMRC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reference input** | $\theta_d(t)$ | $x_d(t)$ | $\dot{x}_d(t)$ |
| **Error space** | Joint angles | End-effector pose | End-effector velocity |
| **Inverse kinematics** | Not required | Required at every step | Required only for initialization |
| **Computational cost** | Low | High (IK + Jacobian) | Moderate (Jacobian inverse only) |
| **Singularity handling** | Trivial (joint limits) | Complex (IK fails) | Critical (Jacobian becomes singular) |
| **Use case** | Point-to-point moves | Path tracking in Cartesian | Continuous velocity tracking |
| **Steady-state error** | Driven to 0 by integral action | Driven to 0 by integral action | Velocity follows reference exactly |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC) Loop

This diagram shows how a desired Cartesian velocity is converted to joint velocities through the inverse Jacobian, then commanded to the robot, with the resulting Cartesian velocity fed back via the forward Jacobian.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired End-Effector Velocity xDot_d] --> B[Inverse Jacobian J inverse at current theta]
    B --> C[Joint Velocity Command thetaDot]
    C --> D[Robot Manipulator Dynamics M C G]
    D --> E[Actual Joint Motion theta thetaDot]
    E --> F[Forward Kinematics f of theta]
    F --> G[Actual End-Effector Pose x]
    G --> H[Differentiation dx dt]
    H --> I[Actual End-Effector Velocity xDot]
    I --> J[Compare with xDot_d]
    A --> J
    J --> K[Velocity Error eDot = xDot_d minus xDot]
    K --> L[Controller Gain Matrix K]
    L --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **loop is closed through the Jacobian itself** because the inverse Jacobian $J^{-1}(\theta)$ is *configuration-dependent* and must be updated at every time step using the current joint angles.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Computed Torque (Model-Based Position) Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Desired Joint Trajectory theta_d thetaDot_d thetaDDot_d] --> B[Compute Position Error e = theta_d minus theta]
    B --> C[Compute Velocity Error eDot = thetaDot_d minus thetaDot]
    C --> D[Feedforward term thetaDDot_d]
    D --> E[PD Stabilizing term Kp e plus Kd eDot]
    E --> F[Multiply by M of theta]
    F --> G[Add Coriolis term C theta thetaDot thetaDot]
    G --> H[Add Gravity term G of theta]
    H --> I[Total Torque Command tau]
    I --> J[Robot Actuators and Joints]
    J --> K[Joint Encoders]
    K --> L[Measure theta and thetaDot]
    L --> B
    L --> C
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: PID Position Control (Independent Joint)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired Joint Angle theta_d] --> B[Subtract Actual Joint Angle theta]
    B --> C[Position Error e]
    C --> D[Proportional Gain Kp multiply e]
    C --> E[Integral Gain Ki integrate e]
    C --> F[Derivative Gain Kd multiply eDot]
    D --> G[Summing Junction]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Control Signal u]
    H --> I[Joint Motor Driver]
    I --> J[Joint Actuator]
    J --> K[Encoder Feedback]
    K --> B
```

## 4.4 Control Hierarchy in an Industrial Robot (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Task Level Planner] --> B[Trajectory Planner]
    B --> C[Inverse Kinematics Module]
    C --> D[Joint Space Reference theta_d]
    D --> E[Joint Servo Controller]
    E --> F[Motor Drive Signals]
    F --> G[Physical Robot Manipulator]
    G --> H[Sensors Encoders ForceTorque]
    H --> I[State Estimator]
    I --> E
    G --> J[Forward Kinematics]
    J --> K[Actual End Effector Pose]
    K --> L[Compare with Desired Pose]
    L --> C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The trajectory planner converts the **task-level goal** (e.g., "pick cup from table") into a **time-indexed sequence** of joint positions $\theta_d(t)$, which is then executed by the **joint servo controller** (typically a PID or computed-torque law).

## 4.5 Comparison: Position vs Velocity Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POS[POSITION CONTROL]
        A1[Reference Position x_d or theta_d] --> A2[Error Computation]
        A3[Measured Position x or theta] --> A2
        A2 --> A4[PID or Computed Torque]
        A4 --> A5[Robot]
        A5 --> A3
    end
    subgraph VEL[VELOCITY CONTROL]
        B1[Reference Velocity xDot_d] --> B2[Inverse Jacobian J inverse]
        B2 --> B3[Joint Velocity thetaDot]
        B3 --> B4[Robot]
        B4 --> B5[Forward Kinematics]
        B5 --> B6[Differentiation]
        B6 --> B7[Measured Velocity xDot]
        B7 --> B8[Compare with xDot_d]
        B8 --> B2
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between Joint-Space Position Control and Cartesian-Space Position Control in a robotic manipulator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Joint-Space Position Control | Cartesian-Space Position Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference** | Joint angles $\theta_d(t)$ | End-effector pose $x_d(t)$ |
| **Error** | $e = \theta_d - \theta$ | $e = x_d - x$ |
| **Inverse Kinematics** | Not needed at runtime | Required at every control cycle |
| **Singularity** | Joint limits only | Both kinematic and algorithmic singularities |
| **Use** | Point-to-point moves | Continuous path tracking |

> **Mark split:** Definition of each (1 m) + 2 valid differences (1 m) + Conclusion/Use-case (1 m) = **3 marks**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC)? State the governing equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Resolved Motion Rate Control is a velocity-level control technique, introduced by D. E. Whitney (1969), in which the joint velocities required to achieve a desired end-effector linear/angular velocity are computed by inverting the Jacobian matrix at the current configuration.

**Governing Equation (1.5 marks):**

$$
\dot{\theta} = J^{-1}(\theta)\,\dot{x}_d
$$

For redundant or singular cases, the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse is used: $\dot{\theta} = J^{+}(\theta)\,\dot{x}_d$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop error dynamics equation for a robot manipulator under **Computed Torque Control**. Explain clearly how this control law decouples the nonlinear robot dynamics into $n$ independent linear second-order systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-DOF planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $L_2 = 0.4$ m, and is currently at $\theta_1 = 60°$, $\theta_2 = 30°$. The end-effector must move with a desired Cartesian velocity $\dot{x}_d = (0.2, \; 0.1)^T$ m/s. Compute the required joint velocities $\dot{\theta}_1$ and $\dot{\theta}_2$ using Resolved Motion Rate Control. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the dynamic model of the $n$-DOF manipulator:

$$
M(\theta)\,\ddot{\theta} + C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\,\dot{\theta} + G(\theta) = \tau
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the position and velocity errors:

$$
e = \theta_d - \theta, \quad \dot{e} = \dot{\theta}_d - \dot{\theta}, \quad \ddot{e} = \ddot{\theta}_d - \ddot{\theta}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** State the computed torque control law:

$$
\tau = M(\theta)\big[\ddot{\theta}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}\big] + C(\theta,\dot{\theta})\,\dot{\theta} + G(\theta)
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute the control law into the dynamic equation and cancel the $C\,\dot{\theta}$ and $G(\theta)$ terms on both sides. After pre-multiplying by $M^{-1}(\theta)$:

$$
\ddot{\theta} = \ddot{\theta}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Rearrange to obtain the final closed-loop error dynamics:

$$
\boxed{\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0}
$$

**Decoupling Explanation:** Because $M(\theta)$ is cancelled by its own inverse and $C, G$ are exactly cancelled, the error dynamics of joint $i$ becomes:

$$
\ddot{e}_i + K_{d,i}\,\dot{e}_i + K_{p,i}\,e_i = 0
$$

This is an **independent linear second-order ODE** for each joint, with no cross-coupling. By choosing $K_{p,i}$ and $K_{d,i}$ as diagonal matrices, the engineer can tune each joint's response (natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_{p,i}}$, damping ratio $\zeta = K_{d,i}/(2\sqrt{K_{p,i}})$) independently. **Hence, the nonlinear, coupled $n$-DOF robot is "linearized and decoupled" into $n$ independent single-DOF systems.**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Write the forward kinematics for the 2-DOF planar robot:

$$
x_1 = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
x_2 = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the Jacobian by partial differentiation:

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute $\theta_1 = 60°$, $\theta_2 = 30°$ so $\theta_1+\theta_2 = 90°$, with $s_1 = 0.866$, $c_1 = 0.5$, $s_{12} = 1.0$, $c_{12} = 0$:

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5(0.866) - 0.4(1.0) & -0.4(1.0) \\ 0.5(0.5) + 0.4(0) & 0.4(0) \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -0.833 & -0.4 \\ 0.25 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m/rad}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $\det J$:

$$
\det J = (-0.833)(0) - (-0.4)(0.25) = 0.1
$$

Since $\det J = 0.1 \neq 0$, the Jacobian is invertible.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute the inverse Jacobian:

$$
J^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.1}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0.4 \\ -0.25 & -0.833 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ -2.5 & -8.33 \end{bmatrix} \text{ rad/m}
$$

**Step 6 (additional credit / KTU bonus):** Apply RMRC:

$$
\dot{\theta} = J^{-1}\dot{x}_d = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 4 \\ -2.5 & -8.33 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\dot{\theta}_1 = (0)(0.2) + (4)(0.1) = 0.4 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\dot{\theta}_2 = (-2.5)(0.2) + (-8.33)(0.1) = -0.5 - 0.833 = -1.333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ -1.333 \end{bmatrix} \text{ rad/s}}
$$

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Jacobian matrix** and its role in velocity control of robotic manipulators. Derive the fundamental relationship between joint velocities and end-effector velocities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain **PID-based independent joint position control** of a robotic manipulator. Write the PID control law and state the role of each term ($K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$) with a brief note on stability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** *Definition:* The Jacobian $J(\theta)$ is an $m \times n$ matrix that maps joint-space velocities to task-space (end-effector) velocities. It is the matrix of first-order partial derivatives of the forward kinematics function $f(\theta)$ with respect to the joint variables.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** *Derivation:* Let $x = f(\theta)$ where $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}^{m}$. Differentiating using the chain rule:

$$
\dot{x} = \frac{\partial f(\theta)}{\partial \theta}\,\dot{\theta} = J(\theta)\,\dot{\theta}
$$

For a 2-DOF planar arm, the Jacobian is:

$$
J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** *Role in Velocity Control:*

- The Jacobian is the **linearizing map** between joint-rate commands and Cartesian end-effector rates.
- In **Resolved Motion Rate Control**, the *inverse* Jacobian is used: $\dot{\theta} = J^{-1}\dot{x}_d$.
- In **singular configurations** ($\det J = 0$), the inverse is undefined, leading to **infinite joint velocities** — a practical limitation of velocity control.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** *Block diagram:*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired theta_d] --> B[Summing Junction +]
    B --> C[Error e = theta_d minus theta]
    C --> D[P Controller Kp]
    C --> E[I Controller Ki integral]
    C --> F[D Controller Kd derivative]
    D --> G[Sum]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Actuator]
    H --> I[Joint]
    I --> J[Encoder]
    J --> B
```

**Step 2 (2 marks):** *PID Control Law:*

$$
u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d\,\dot{e}(t)
$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** *Role of each term:*

- **Proportional ($K_p$):** Produces a control effort proportional to the *current* error. Increases speed of response and reduces (but cannot eliminate) steady-state error. Too high $K_p$ causes overshoot/oscillation.
- **Integral ($K_i$):** Integrates error over time. **Eliminates steady-state error** caused by gravity, friction, or load. Too high $K_i$ causes integrator windup and instability.
- **Derivative ($K_d$):** Acts on the *rate of change* of error. **Provides damping**, reduces overshoot, and improves transient response. Sensitive to measurement noise (often low-pass filtered in practice).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** *Stability note:* For a 2nd-order linearized joint, the closed-loop characteristic equation is $s^2 + (K_d/J_{eff})s + K_p/J_{eff} = 0$ (where $J_{eff}$ is the effective inertia). Stability requires **$K_p > 0$ and $K_d > 0$**. For critical damping, $K_d = 2\sqrt{K_p J_{eff}}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing Units:** Numerical answers without units (rad/s, m/s) lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing the direction of the Jacobian map:** Writing $\dot{\theta} = J\dot{x}$ instead of $\dot{x} = J\dot{\theta}$ is a *fatal error* worth 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to update the Jacobian:** In RMRC, students often compute $J^{-1}$ only once. Examiners expect recognition that $J^{-1}(\theta)$ is *configuration-dependent* and must be recomputed at every control cycle.
> 4. **Ignoring singularities:** When $\det J = 0$, the inverse is undefined. Examiners award marks for mentioning the **pseudoinverse** $J^{+}$ or **singularity-robust inverse** $J^{*T} = J^T(JJ^T + \lambda I)^{-1}$.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption $M(\theta) > 0$:** In computed torque derivations, students must state that $M$ is positive-definite (and hence invertible) — without this, the derivation is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Velocity & Position Control**

- ✅ **Jacobian definition:** $J(\theta) = \partial f / \partial \theta$ — the linearizing map from joint rates to task-space rates.
- ✅ **Core velocity equation:** $\dot{x} = J(\theta)\dot{\theta}$ (one-way: joint → Cartesian).
- ✅ **Resolved Rate Control:** $\dot{\theta} = J^{-1}\dot{x}_d$ (square, non-singular case).
- ✅ **Pseudoinverse form:** $\dot{\theta} = J^{+}\dot{x}_d = (J^T J)^{-1} J^T \dot{x}_d$ (redundant / singular case).
- ✅ **Singularity condition:** $\det J = 0$ → robot loses a degree of freedom.
- ✅ **Position error:** $e = \theta_d - \theta$ (joint space) or $e = x_d - x$ (Cartesian space).
- ✅ **PID control law:** $u = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$.
- ✅ **Roles:** $K_p$ → speed, $K_i$ → zero steady-state error, $K_d$ → damping.
- ✅ **Manipulator dynamics:** $M\ddot{\theta} + C\dot{\theta} + G = \tau$.
- ✅ **Computed torque law:** $\tau = M[\ddot{\theta}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}] + C\dot{\theta} + G$.
- ✅ **Decoupled error dynamics:** $\ddot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$ (linear, decoupled, second-order per joint).
- ✅ **Critical damping condition:** $K_d = 2\sqrt{K_p}$ (no overshoot).
- ✅ **Stability requirement:** $K_p > 0$ and $K_d > 0$ for the closed-loop system to be stable.
- ✅ **Industrial metrics to remember:** Repeatability ±0.02 mm, bandwidth 10–100 Hz.
- ✅ **Real-world linkage:** Position control → pick-and-place; velocity control → continuous-path tasks (welding, painting).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation of a Pure Translation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Representation of a Pure Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In rigid-body kinematics, a **Pure Translation** is defined as a rigid-body motion in which **every point on the body moves along a parallel path** and **all points have identical linear velocities** at any given instant, while the **orientation of the body remains constant** (i.e., the angular velocity is identically zero).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A pure translation is a rigid-body motion in which the orientation of the body does not change with respect to the reference (world) frame. The body undergoes a positional shift, but all axes of the body-coordinate frame remain parallel to those of the reference frame at every instant.*

Mathematically, a body undergoing pure translation from frame $\{B\}$ to a translated frame $\{B'\}$ satisfies:

$$
\hat{X}_{B'} \parallel \hat{X}_{B}, \quad \hat{Y}_{B'} \parallel \hat{Y}_{B}, \quad \hat{Z}_{B'} \parallel \hat{Z}_{B}
$$

while the origin of $\{B'\}$ is displaced by a vector $\mathbf{d} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ relative to the origin of $\{B\}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sliding Book" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:**
> Imagine placing a **closed hardcover book flat on a smooth glass table** and pushing it straight across the table. The book moves — its position changes — but the cover continues to face the ceiling, the spine continues to point the same way, and the front edge remains parallel to the table edge. **No part of the book tilts, spins, or wobbles.** This is *pure translation*.
>
> Compare this with **spinning a coin on the table** (that is *pure rotation*) or **tossing the book in the air while it tumbles** (that is *general motion* = translation + rotation).

In contrast, if you pick up the book, rotate it 90°, and place it back down, the orientation has changed — this is **not** a pure translation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of pure translation in 3D space:** $\mathbf{3}$ (along $\hat{X}$, $\hat{Y}$, and $\hat{Z}$).
- **Independent parameters required to describe it:** $d_x, d_y, d_z$ (translational components, in **meters (m)** or **millimeters (mm)** as per KTU convention).
- **Angular velocity vector:** $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \mathbf{0}_{3 \times 1}$ (zero vector).
- **Linear velocity:** Identical for **every point** of the rigid body.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Pure Translation — Step-by-Step

The representation of a pure translation in robotics is built on **three conceptual layers**:

### Layer 1 — Position Vector Representation (Simplest Form)

A pure translation is most fundamentally represented by a **3 × 1 translation vector**:

$$
\mathbf{d} = \begin{bmatrix} d_x \\ d_y \\ d_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

This vector points from the **origin of the original frame** $\{B\}$ to the **origin of the translated frame** $\{B'\}$, expressed in the **reference frame** coordinates.

- **Pros:** Compact, intuitive.
- **Cons:** Cannot directly transform homogeneous points; cannot be composed easily with rotations.

### Layer 2 — Homogeneous Transformation Matrix Representation (KTU Standard)

The KTU 2024 syllabus (and the industry-standard Craig/Spong/Khalil convention) requires the use of the **4 × 4 homogeneous transformation matrix** to represent a pure translation:

$$
T_{\text{Trans}}(d_x, d_y, d_z) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & d_x \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & d_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & d_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Key observations:
- The **upper-left 3 × 3 block** is the **Identity matrix $I_3$** — this is the *signature* of pure translation (no rotation).
- The **upper-right 3 × 1 block** contains the translation components.
- The **bottom row** is always $[0 \; 0 \; 0 \; 1]$ in any proper homogeneous transform.

### Layer 3 — Differential Translation (For Velocity-Level Analysis)

In manipulator Jacobian and differential kinematics, we use the **differential translation operator**:

$$
dT = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & \delta_x \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \delta_y \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \delta_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

such that an **infinitesimal** pure translation is $T + dT$, where the body moves by an **infinitesimal** offset $(\delta_x, \delta_y, \delta_z)$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Representation

| Representation | Why We Need It | Where KTU Uses It |
|---|---|---|
| 3 × 1 Vector $\mathbf{d}$ | Quick positional bookkeeping | Direct kinematics, trajectory planning |
| 4 × 4 Matrix $T$ | Unified rotation+translation frame change | Forward kinematics, frame composition |
| 4 × 4 Differential $dT$ | Linearized velocity-level motion | Jacobian derivation, resolved-rate control |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these before entering the exam hall.** Every formula below is a **guaranteed KTU question stem**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Translation vector | $\mathbf{d} = [d_x, d_y, d_z]^T$ | m or mm | 3 × 1 column |
| 2 | Homogeneous Trans matrix | $T = \begin{bmatrix} I_3 & \mathbf{d} \\ 0\;0\;0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | dimensionless | 4 × 4 |
| 3 | Point mapping (homogeneous) | $P' = T \cdot P$ | m | $P = [x,y,z,1]^T$ |
| 4 | Point mapping (component) | $x' = x + d_x$, $y' = y + d_y$, $z' = z + d_z$ | m | Pure additive shift |
| 5 | Inverse transform | $T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} I_3 & -\mathbf{d} \\ 0\;0\;0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | dimensionless | For pure translation, $R^{-1} = I_3$ |
| 6 | Composition of two pure translations | $T_1 \cdot T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} I_3 & \mathbf{d}_1 + \mathbf{d}_2 \\ 0\;0\;0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | dimensionless | Translations **add as vectors** |
| 7 | Differential translation | $dT = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 & \delta_x \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \delta_y \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \delta_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | m | Infinitesimal |
| 8 | Linear velocity of any point $i$ on body | $\mathbf{v}_i = \dot{\mathbf{d}}$, $\forall i$ | m/s | **Identical for all points** |
| 9 | Angular velocity of body | $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \mathbf{0}$ | rad/s | The defining condition |
| 10 | DOF of pure translation | $\text{DOF} = 3$ | — | One per Cartesian axis |

> **CRITICAL TABLE-NOTATION RULE:** In the above, all column vectors use square brackets with comma separation (e.g., $\mathbf{d} = [d_x, d_y, d_z]^T$) to avoid markdown parser conflicts with the table's pipe `$\vert$` delimiter.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC Machine Tools:** The cutting tool's *translation* along the workpiece is programmed using exactly this $T_{\text{Trans}}$ matrix structure.
- **Pick-and-Place Robots (SCARA, Cartesian gantries):** These robots perform almost **exclusively pure translation** in their end-effector motion.
- **3D Printers:** Build-plate translation is a pure-translation problem.
- **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) on factory floors:** Their path is a sequence of pure translations connected by in-place rotations.
