> - **v** = linear velocity of the robot body (in **m/s**)
> - **ω** = angular velocity of the robot body (in **rad/s**)
> - **θ** = heading/orientation angle (in **radians**)

### 1.3 Why Differential Drive?

Differential drive is the **simplest and most widely used** drive configuration in mobile robotics because:
1. It requires only **two actuators** (motors) for planar motion.
2. It can achieve **zero-radius turning** (in-place rotation).
3. The kinematic model is **mathematically tractable** and ideal for control design.
4. It is mechanically simple — no steering mechanism is needed.

Common real-world examples include the **TurtleBot**, **Pioneer P3-DX**, **Roomba vacuum cleaner**, and **Kobuki** mobile base.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Setup and Coordinate Frames

Consider a differential-drive robot as shown in the figure below (described in Section 4). The two drive wheels are separated by a distance **b** (the wheelbase), and a passive caster wheel is placed at the front or rear for balance.

Two reference frames are used:
- **World (Inertial) Frame** $\{X_W, Y_W\}$: Fixed to the ground, used for global navigation.
- **Body Frame** $\{X_B, Y_B\}$: Attached to the robot, with origin at the midpoint of the two drive wheels. $X_B$ points forward (heading direction), $Y_B$ points to the left.

The pose of the body frame with respect to the world frame is:
$$
{}^W\xi_B = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.2 Non-Holonomic Constraint (The "Why" of DDWMR)

A non-holonomic constraint is one that **cannot be integrated to a positional constraint** — it restricts the *velocity* but not the *position*. For a DDWMR with no slipping at the wheel-ground contact:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Pure Rolling Constraint (No Lateral Slip):**
> The robot cannot move in the lateral direction (perpendicular to its heading). Mathematically, the velocity component along $Y_B$ must be zero:
> $$
> \dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
> $$

This single constraint equation reduces the **3 DOF configuration space** to a **2 DOF velocity space** $(v, \omega)$. This is why a DDWMR is non-holonomic.

### 2.3 Derivation Logic — Step by Step (Why Each Step Exists)

**Step 1 — Identify the wheel contact velocities:**
Since the wheels roll without slipping, the linear velocity of each wheel's contact point with the ground equals $r \cdot \omega_{\text{wheel}}$:
- Left wheel: $v_L = r \cdot \omega_L$
- Right wheel: $v_R = r \cdot \omega_R$

**Step 2 — Relate wheel velocities to body twist:**
The midpoint between the wheels moves with the average of $v_L$ and $v_R$ (linear velocity of the body):
$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2}
$$
The angular velocity of the body arises from the *difference* in wheel speeds divided by the wheelbase (since the two wheels trace concentric arcs):
$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b}
$$

**Step 3 — Express body twist in the world frame (the kinematic model):**
The body's linear velocity $v$ is directed along the heading axis $X_B$. To express it in world coordinates, project it onto $X_W$ and $Y_W$:
$$
\dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

This is the famous **unicycle model** of a differential-drive robot.

**Step 4 — Inverse mapping (from body twist to wheel commands):**
If a desired $(v, \omega)$ is specified by a high-level planner, the wheel velocities are:
$$
\omega_L = \frac{2v - b\omega}{2r}, \quad \omega_R = \frac{2v + b\omega}{2r}
$$

### 2.4 Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)

At any instant, the robot moves along a circular arc (or a straight line as a special case). The center of this arc is the **Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)**.

- **Radius of Curvature:** $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega}$
- For pure translation ($\omega = 0$): $R \to \infty$ → straight line.
- For pure rotation ($v = 0$): $R = 0$ → rotates about its own midpoint.

The position of ICC in the world frame is:
$$
x_{ICC} = x - R\sin\theta, \quad y_{ICC} = y + R\cos\theta
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}$ | Linear velocity of body (forward) | m/s |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}$ | Angular velocity of body | rad/s |
| 3 | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ | World-frame $x$-velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ | World-frame $y$-velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Heading rate | rad/s |
| 6 | $\omega_R = \dfrac{2v + b\omega}{2r}$ | Right wheel command | rad/s |
| 7 | $\omega_L = \dfrac{2v - b\omega}{2r}$ | Left wheel command | rad/s |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega}$ | Radius of curvature (for $\omega \neq 0$) | m |
| 9 | $x_{ICC} = x - R\sin\theta$ | ICC x-coordinate | m |
| 10 | $y_{ICC} = y + R\cos\theta$ | ICC y-coordinate | m |
| 11 | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Non-holonomic constraint | — |
| 12 | $J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Jacobian (input → configuration) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For KTU valuation, always state the **non-holonomic constraint** explicitly and justify the unicycle equations from it. Examiners award marks for the constraint derivation.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The differential-drive kinematic model is foundational in:
- **Autonomous navigation** (warehouse robots like Amazon Kiva / Proteus).
- **Path tracking controllers** (pure pursuit, Stanley, Lyapunov-based nonlinear control).
- **SLAM** (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) — the motion model is the DDWMR unicycle model.
- **Swarm robotics** — many swarm platforms (e-puck, Kilobot) use differential drive.
- **Disaster response robots** (PackBot, throwable reconnaissance robots).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Kinematics of a DDWMR

**Given:** Wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$, wheel radius $r$, wheelbase $b$.

**Find:** Body twist $(v, \omega)$ and pose rates $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$.

**Step 1 — Wheel contact point velocities (no-slip condition):**
The instantaneous linear velocity at the contact point of each wheel is:
$$
v_L = r \, \omega_L \quad \text{(left wheel contact speed)}
$$
$$
v_R = r \, \omega_R \quad \text{(right wheel contact speed)}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear velocity of the robot body:**
The body midpoint translates at the average of the two contact-point velocities:
$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity of the robot body:**
The angular velocity is governed by the difference in contact-point velocities divided by the wheelbase (rigid-body rotation about ICC):
$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}
$$

**Step 4 — Body-to-world transformation:**
The body-frame linear velocity $\begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ is rotated by $\theta$ into the world frame:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This gives:
$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta
$$

The orientation rate is simply the body's angular velocity:
$$
\dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

**Step 5 — Compact state-space form:**
$$
\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matrix is the **Jacobian** $J(\theta)$ of the DDWMR.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inverse Kinematics of a DDWMR

**Given:** Desired body twist $(v, \omega)$, wheel radius $r$, wheelbase $b$.

**Find:** Required wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$.

**Step 1 — Start from forward relations:**
$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_L + \omega_R = \frac{2v}{r}
$$
$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_R - \omega_L = \frac{b\omega}{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the 2×2 linear system:**
Adding the two equations:
$$
2\omega_R = \frac{2v}{r} + \frac{b\omega}{r} = \frac{2v + b\omega}{r}
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \omega_R = \frac{2v + b\omega}{2r}
$$
Subtracting the second from the first:
$$
2\omega_L = \frac{2v}{r} - \frac{b\omega}{r} = \frac{2v - b\omega}{r}
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \omega_L = \frac{2v - b\omega}{2r}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the special cases:**
- **Pure translation** ($\omega = 0$): $\omega_L = \omega_R = v/r$ ✓
- **Pure rotation in place** ($v = 0$): $\omega_L = -\omega_R = -b\omega/(2r)$ ✓ (equal and opposite)

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC)

**Step 1 — Define radius of curvature:**
For a rigid body undergoing planar motion, all points on the body move on circles of different radii but with the same angular velocity $\omega$. The body midpoint moves on a circle of radius:
$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega}
$$
with the special case $R \to \infty$ when $\omega = 0$ (straight line).

**Step 2 — Locate the ICC in the world frame:**
The ICC lies at a perpendicular distance $R$ from the body midpoint, in the direction $90°$ counter-clockwise from the heading. The unit vector pointing from the robot to ICC (in body frame) is $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}^T$ (perpendicular to heading, to the left of $X_B$).

Transforming this to the world frame:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} x_{ICC} \\ y_{ICC} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + R \begin{bmatrix} -\sin\theta \\ \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

This gives:
$$
x_{ICC} = x - R\sin\theta, \quad y_{ICC} = y + R\cos\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the motion equation:**
All points $P$ on the robot satisfy:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}_P = \omega \begin{bmatrix} -(y - y_{ICC}) \\ (x - x_{ICC}) \end{bmatrix}
$$
For the body midpoint, this reduces to $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ and $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ ✓.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot has $r = 0.05$ m, $b = 0.30$ m. The left wheel is driven at $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s and the right wheel at $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s. Find (a) the body linear velocity, (b) the body angular velocity, and (c) the radius of curvature and position of ICC if the robot is currently at pose $(x, y, \theta) = (1, 2, 30°)$.

**Solution (a) — Linear velocity:**
$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times (10 + 12)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 22}{2} = 0.55 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Solution (b) — Angular velocity:**
$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b} = \frac{0.05 \times (12 - 10)}{0.30} = \frac{0.10}{0.30} = 0.333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Solution (c) — Radius of curvature and ICC:**
$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.55}{0.333} = 1.65 \text{ m}
$$

Converting $\theta = 30° = \pi/6$ rad:
$$
\sin(30°) = 0.5, \quad \cos(30°) = 0.866
$$
$$
x_{ICC} = x - R\sin\theta = 1 - 1.65 \times 0.5 = 1 - 0.825 = 0.175 \text{ m}
$$
$$
y_{ICC} = y + R\cos\theta = 2 + 1.65 \times 0.866 = 2 + 1.429 = 3.429 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The robot moves on a circular arc of radius 1.65 m, centered at $(0.175, 3.429)$, while turning counter-clockwise (since $\omega > 0$ means $\omega_R > \omega_L$, i.e., right wheel is faster).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Coordinate Frame & Physical Layout of a DDWMR

The diagram below shows the top-view layout of a differential-drive robot with the world frame, body frame, drive wheels, and the ICC.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WORLD["WORLD FRAME FIXED TO GROUND"]
        XW["XW EAST"]
        YW["YW NORTH"]
    end

    subgraph BODY["ROBOT BODY FRAME ATTACHED TO CHASSIS"]
        XB["XB HEADING FORWARD"]
        YB["YB LATERAL LEFT"]
    end

    WL["LEFT DRIVE WHEEL"]
    WR["RIGHT DRIVE WHEEL"]
    CAS["PASSIVE CASTER WHEEL FOR STABILITY"]
    CENT["BODY MIDPOINT POSE X Y THETA"]
    ICC["INSTANTANEOUS CENTER OF CURVATURE ICC"]
    ARC["CIRCULAR TRAJECTORY ARC RADIUS R"]

    WL --- CENT
    WR --- CENT
    CENT --- CAS
    CENT --- ICC
    ICC --- ARC
    WORLD --- CENT
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of the Differential-Drive Kinematic Model

This block diagram illustrates the transformation chain: wheel motor commands → wheel angular velocities → body twist → world-frame pose rate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DESIRED VELOCITY V"]
    B["DESIRED ANGULAR VELOCITY W"]
    C["INVERSE KINEMATIC BLOCK WHEEL SPEED COMPUTATION"]
    D["LEFT WHEEL COMMAND WL"]
    E["RIGHT WHEEL COMMAND WR"]
    F["LEFT MOTOR AND WHEEL ASSEMBLY"]
    G["RIGHT MOTOR AND WHEEL ASSEMBLY"]
    H["FORWARD KINEMATIC BLOCK BODY TWIST COMPUTATION"]
    I["BODY LINEAR VELOCITY V"]
    J["BODY ANGULAR VELOCITY W"]
    K["BODY TO WORLD TRANSFORMATION JACOBIAN"]
    L["POSE RATE X DOT Y DOT THETA DOT"]
    M["INTEGRATION ODOMETRY OR FILTER"]
    N["ROBOT POSE X Y THETA"]

    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    D --> F
    E --> G
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I
    H --> J
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L
    L --> M
    M --> N
    N --> K
```

### 4.3 Motion Modes of a Differential-Drive Robot

The diagram below classifies the three fundamental motion modes based on the relationship between $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WHEEL COMMANDS WL AND WR"]
    A --> B["WL EQUALS WR STRAIGHT LINE"]
    A --> C["WL NEGATIVE WR PURE ROTATION IN PLACE"]
    A --> D["WL NOT EQUAL WR CIRCULAR ARC TURN"]
    B --> B1["V EQUALS R WL  W EQUALS 0  R EQUALS INFINITY"]
    C --> C1["V EQUALS 0  W EQUALS R WL PLUS WR DIVIDED BY B"]
    D --> D1["R EQUALS V DIVIDED BY W  ICC OFFSET FROM CENTER"]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Control & Estimation Pipeline

This block diagram maps the full pipeline from high-level waypoint commands to motor signals and pose estimation feedback.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4

    P7 --> P8

    P9 --> P10

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Pattern]**
**Define a Differential-Drive Wheeled Mobile Robot. Explain the non-holonomic constraint associated with it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Differential-Drive Wheeled Mobile Robot (DDWMR) is a mobile robot with **two independently actuated wheels** mounted on a common axle, supported by passive caster wheels. **(1 mark)**

The non-holonomic constraint arises from the **no-slip (pure rolling) condition** at the wheel-ground contact, which prevents lateral motion. **(1 mark)**

Mathematically:
$$
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
$$

This constraint reduces the effective degrees of freedom from 3 (configuration space) to 2 (velocity space), making the robot non-holonomic. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**
**Define the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC) for a differential-drive robot. State the formula for radius of curvature.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC) is the point about which the entire robot instantaneously rotates while moving on a curved path. **(1 mark)**

For a differential-drive robot, the radius of curvature is:
$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega}
$$

where $v$ is the linear velocity and $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the robot body. **(1 mark)**

For **pure translation** ($\omega = 0$), $R \to \infty$ and the motion is a straight line. For **pure rotation** ($v = 0$), $R = 0$ and the robot spins about its midpoint. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Pattern, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the **forward kinematics** of a differential-drive mobile robot, starting from the wheel angular velocities. Show all intermediate steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **inverse kinematics** of a differential-drive robot — i.e., given a desired linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$, find the required wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$. Mention two special cases and verify your result for them. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Forward Kinematics (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Wheel contact velocities:**
Under the no-slip condition:
$$
v_L = r\omega_L, \quad v_R = r\omega_R
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Body linear velocity:**
$$
v = \frac{v_L + v_R}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Body angular velocity:**
$$
\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Pose rate in world frame:**
Applying the rotation matrix $R_z(\theta)$ to the body-frame velocity $\begin{bmatrix} v & 0 \end{bmatrix}^T$:
$$
\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Jacobian form:**
$$
\dot{q} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{J(\theta)} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Inverse Kinematics (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Set up the linear system:**
From forward kinematics:
$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_L + \omega_R = \frac{2v}{r}
$$
$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_R - \omega_L = \frac{b\omega}{r}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Solve for wheel velocities:**
Adding and subtracting:
$$
\omega_R = \frac{2v + b\omega}{2r}, \quad \omega_L = \frac{2v - b\omega}{2r}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Special case 1: Pure translation ($\omega = 0$):**
$$
\omega_L = \omega_R = \frac{v}{r}
$$
Both wheels rotate at the same speed, the robot moves straight. ✓

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Special case 2: Pure rotation in place ($v = 0$):**
$$
\omega_L = -\frac{b\omega}{2r}, \quad \omega_R = +\frac{b\omega}{2r}
$$
The wheels rotate in opposite directions with equal magnitudes, and the robot spins about its midpoint (R = 0). ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Pattern, Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the kinematic modeling of a differential-drive mobile robot. Clearly identify the transformation from wheel speeds to body twist and from body twist to world-frame pose rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheelbase $b = 0.3$ m. The left wheel rotates at $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s and the right wheel at $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s. The robot is currently at pose $(x, y, \theta) = (1, 2, 30°)$.
   **(i)** Compute the body linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$.
   **(ii)** Determine the radius of curvature $R$.
   **(iii)** Find the coordinates of the Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Kinematic Modeling Block Diagram (7 marks):**

**Block 1 (1.5 marks) — Input Layer (Wheel commands):**
The left and right wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$ are the **inputs** from the motor controllers.

**Block 2 (2 marks) — Forward Kinematic Block:**
This block computes the body twist from the wheel speeds:
$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}
$$

**Block 3 (1.5 marks) — Body-to-World Transformation Block:**
This block contains the Jacobian $J(\theta)$ and projects the body velocity into the world frame:
$$
\dot{q} = J(\theta)\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$
producing $(\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\theta})$.

**Block 4 (1 mark) — Integration / Odometry Block:**
The pose rate is integrated over time to obtain the current pose:
$$
x(t) = \int_0^t \dot{x}\, dt, \quad y(t) = \int_0^t \dot{y}\, dt, \quad \theta(t) = \int_0^t \dot{\theta}\, dt
$$

**Block 5 (1 mark) — Feedback:**
The estimated pose is fed back to the controller for closed-loop trajectory tracking.

(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2 for a visual reference.)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Body linear and angular velocity (3 marks):**
$$
v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times (10 + 12)}{2} = \frac{0.05 \times 22}{2} = 0.55 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b} = \frac{0.05 \times (12 - 10)}{0.30} = \frac{0.10}{0.30} = 0.333 \text{ rad/s} \quad (\text{CCW})
$$

**Part (ii) — Radius of curvature (1.5 marks):**
$$
R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.55}{0.333} = 1.65 \text{ m}
$$

**Part (iii) — ICC coordinates (2.5 marks):**
With $\theta = 30°$, $\sin 30° = 0.5$, $\cos 30° = 0.866$:
$$
x_{ICC} = x - R\sin\theta = 1 - 1.65 \times 0.5 = 1 - 0.825 = 0.175 \text{ m}
$$
$$
y_{ICC} = y + R\cos\theta = 2 + 1.65 \times 0.866 = 2 + 1.429 = 3.429 \text{ m}
$$

The robot traces a counter-clockwise circular arc of radius 1.65 m centered at $(0.175 \text{ m}, 3.429 \text{ m})$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** on $v$ (m/s) and $\omega$ (rad/s) — KTU deducts **½ mark per quantity**.
> 2. **Sign convention errors**: With $\omega_R > \omega_L$, the robot turns **left (CCW)**; students often incorrectly claim a right turn. Always verify by drawing the direction of $v_L$ and $v_R$.
> 3. **Skipping the no-slip assumption**: The forward kinematics is valid only when wheels roll without slipping. Always state this in the derivation.
> 4. **Mixing wheelbase notation**: Some textbooks use $2L$ (with $L$ as half-wheelbase), others use $b$ (full wheelbase). Use whichever the question states — be consistent.
> 5. **Confusing forward and inverse kinematics**: Forward = wheel speeds → body twist. Inverse = body twist → wheel speeds. Examiners often test this distinction.
> 6. **In numerical problems**, students often compute $v$ correctly but forget to convert $\theta$ from degrees to radians in trigonometric terms.
> 7. **Not stating the non-holonomic constraint explicitly** before deriving the unicycle model — this is a **favorite 2-mark question** for KTU 2024 scheme.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DDWMR** = two independently driven wheels + passive caster(s). **(Definition)**
- **Configuration space** is 3D: $(x, y, \theta)$; **velocity space** is 2D: $(v, \omega)$. **(Key fact)**
- **Non-holonomic constraint**: $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — no lateral motion. **(KTU favorite)**
- **Forward kinematics (wheel → body):** $v = \frac{r(\omega_L + \omega_R)}{2}$, $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}$. **(Memorize)**
- **Inverse kinematics (body → wheel):** $\omega_R = \frac{2v + b\omega}{2r}$, $\omega_L = \frac{2v - b\omega}{2r}$. **(Memorize)**
- **Unicycle model:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$. **(Core equation)**
- **ICC radius:** $R = v/\omega$; ICC lies perpendicular to heading at distance $R$ from body midpoint. **(Geometry)**
- **Pure translation** ($\omega = 0$): $R \to \infty$ (straight line). **(Special case)**
- **Pure rotation** ($v = 0$): $R = 0$ (in-place spin); wheels rotate with equal and opposite speeds. **(Special case)**
- **Zero-radius turning** is a unique advantage of differential drive — no other configuration can do this with only two wheels. **(Engineering insight)**
- **Slip assumption is critical**: All derivations assume no wheel slip; in practice, encoder-based odometry drifts. **(Practical limitation)**
- **Differential drive is non-holonomic** but is **controllable** — you can reach any $(x, y, \theta)$ by a suitable path. **(Important theoretical result)**
- For KTU exams, always draw the **coordinate frame diagram** and the **block diagram** — visual aids can earn **easy 1–2 marks**.
- **Jacobian form** $J(\theta)$: $3 \times 2$ matrix — questions on this structure are frequently asked.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Car-Like Wheeled Mobile Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Car-Like Wheeled Mobile Robot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Car-Like Wheeled Mobile Robot (CLWMR)** is a non-holonomic ground vehicle whose mechanical structure mimics the steering geometry of a conventional automobile. It typically employs the **Ackermann steering principle**, where two (or more) front wheels rotate synchronously about a common instantaneous center of rotation (ICR), while the rear wheels remain fixed in orientation and act as the primary traction wheels. The vehicle possesses **one driving axle (rear)** and **one steering axle (front)**, and is described kinematically by a minimum of three pose coordinates — longitudinal position $x$, lateral position $y$, and orientation (heading) angle $\theta$ — augmented by a fourth control input: the steering angle $\phi$ of the front wheels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PECDT633 / Module 3**
> The Car-Like Wheeled Mobile Robot falls under the broader taxonomy of wheeled mobile robots (WMRs). The student must understand the **kinematic constraints**, the **bicycle model abstraction**, the **non-holonomic nature** of the platform, and the implications for **motion planning and controllability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Block**
> - **Wheelbase ($L$):** Distance between the front steering axle and the rear driving axle.
> - **Track Width ($W$):** Lateral distance between the two wheels on a given axle.
> - **Steering Angle ($\phi$):** The angle by which the front wheels are rotated about their vertical kingpins.
> - **Heading Angle ($\theta$):** The orientation of the vehicle's longitudinal axis with respect to a global reference frame.
> - **Curvature ($\kappa$):** Inverse of the instantaneous radius of curvature, $\kappa = \tan(\phi)/L$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a real car in a crowded KTU campus parking lot. You **cannot** slide the car sideways into a parallel parking spot — no matter how hard you steer, the car must always move in a direction aligned (or nearly aligned) with the body of the vehicle. This is the physical manifestation of the **non-holonomic constraint**.

Now think of a shopping cart — it has a single freely pivoting front wheel. You can swing the cart sideways with ease. That cart is **omnidirectional (holonomic)**. The car, however, behaves like a rigid, unicycle-like object whose nose always leads the motion. The front wheels are the "brain" of direction change: turning them left or right curves the path. If the front wheels are straight ($\phi = 0$), the car drives in a perfect straight line. If $\phi \ne 0$, the car traces an arc whose radius depends on how hard the wheels are turned.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Intuition Pump:** Hold a pen in your hand with the tip on a sheet of paper, oriented vertically — this is the rear axle reference point. Tilt the pen slightly (this tilt is the heading $\theta$). Now glue a second pen at the top, also pointing in the same direction — that second pen represents the front axle. To steer, rotate the top pen by a small angle $\phi$. As you slide the bottom pen forward, the top pen traces a curve. The car behaves **exactly** this way — this is the classical **Bicycle Model**.

## 1.3 Classification Context (Where CLWMR Fits in the WMR Family)

| WMR Class | Drive | Steer | Holonomic? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Drive | 2 driven wheels | None (skid) | No | Roomba, Pioneer P3-DX |
| **Car-Like (Ackermann)** | **Rear wheels** | **Front wheels** | **No** | **Autonomous car, ATRV-Jr** |
| Tricycle | 1 driven wheel | 1 steerable wheel | No | Research platforms |
| Omnidirectional (Mecanum) | 4 driven wheels | Independent rollers | Yes | KUKA youBot |
| Synchro Drive | All wheels | All wheels synced | No | Nomad XR4000 |

The Car-Like platform is the **most industrially relevant** class because it directly scales to passenger vehicles, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) in warehouses, and outdoor autonomous navigation systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bicycle Model — Mathematical Abstraction

Because the two front wheels (and similarly the two rear wheels) follow nearly identical trajectories under pure rolling and no-slip conditions, engineers collapse the four-wheel arrangement into a **two-wheel bicycle model**. The left and right wheels on each axle are merged into a single virtual wheel located at the **midpoint of the axle**.

This reduction is **valid for kinematic analysis** (velocities, geometry) and is the standard simplification adopted in textbooks and the KTU syllabus. It does **not** account for tire slip, suspension dynamics, or load transfer — those belong to the *dynamic* model.

### 2.1.1 Configuration Space (Pose Definition)

The pose of the bicycle (and hence the car-like robot) is fully described by the tuple:

$$q = (x, y, \theta, \phi) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S \times S$$

where:
- $x, y$ = global Cartesian coordinates of the **rear axle midpoint**,
- $\theta$ = heading angle of the vehicle body (rad),
- $\phi$ = steering angle of the front wheel (rad).

The configuration space is a **four-dimensional smooth manifold** (a 2D plane times two circles).

### 2.1.2 Non-Holonomic Constraints

Two constraints are imposed on the bicycle:

1. **Rear wheel rolling (no longitudinal slip):** The velocity of the rear contact point must be collinear with the wheel plane.
2. **Front wheel rolling (no longitudinal slip, plus kingpin geometry):** The velocity of the front contact point must be collinear with the steered wheel plane.

These yield the classical **Pfaffian constraint** equations that mathematically *cannot* be integrated into a position-level constraint — hence the system is **non-holonomic**.

### 2.1.3 Equations of Motion (Forward Kinematics)

Given the linear velocity $v$ of the rear axle and the steering angle $\phi$ as control inputs, the kinematic model is:

$$\dot{x} = v \cos(\theta)$$
$$\dot{y} = v \sin(\theta)$$
$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan(\phi)$$

This system of three first-order ODEs constitutes the **forward kinematic model** of the car-like mobile robot. The fourth state variable $\phi$ itself is directly set by the steering actuator and does not appear differentiated in the simplest form (a *kinematic* or *geometric* steering model).

## 2.2 Turning Radius, Curvature, and the Ackermann Condition

### 2.2.1 Instantaneous Radius of Curvature

The rear axle midpoint traces a circular arc with radius $R$:

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan(\phi)}$$

The corresponding **path curvature** is:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{\tan(\phi)}{L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Limitation:** As $\phi \to 90^\circ$, $R \to L$ (a circle of radius equal to the wheelbase). As $\phi \to 0$, $R \to \infty$ (straight line). Physical steering linkages restrict $\phi$ to a range like $\vert\phi\vert \le 35^\circ$ to $\vert\phi\vert \le 40^\circ$.

### 2.2.2 The Ackermann Steering Geometry

In a real four-wheeled car, the inner and outer front wheels must turn at *different* angles so that their perpendicular axes intersect **at a single point** on the projection of the rear axle (the ICR). If both wheels turned at the same angle, the inner tire would slip sideways during a turn.

The Ackermann condition is:

$$\cot(\phi_o) - \cot(\phi_i) = \frac{W}{L}$$

where $\phi_o$ is the outer wheel angle, $\phi_i$ is the inner wheel angle, and $W$ is the track width.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The bicycle model implicitly satisfies Ackermann geometry because both virtual front wheels coincide. For a real four-wheel platform, the inner and outer steering angles are servo-controlled to satisfy the equation above.

## 2.3 Controllability and Maneuverability

### 2.3.1 Non-Holonomic Controllability

A car-like robot **cannot** instantaneously translate sideways, yet it can reach **any** configuration in the plane. The system is therefore **controllable in the configuration space** but is **not stabilizable by any continuous time-invariant state feedback** — a celebrated result by **Brockett (1983)** known as **Brockett's Necessary Condition**.

Consequence: parking maneuvers (e.g., parallel parking) require **time-varying** or **discontinuous** control laws — usually executed as a sequence of forward and reverse motions (e.g., the classical **three-point turn** or **Hess-Smith maneuver**).

### 2.3.2 Degree of Maneuverability (DM)

According to the Campion-Morin classification, the Car-Like robot has **DM = 1** (one degree of maneuverability), because the dimension of the admissible instantaneous motion space $\delta$ equals $n - m - 1$ where $n=3$ is the configuration space dimension and $m=1$ is the number of nonholonomic constraints. It is classified as a **Type (2,0)** robot.

| Robot Type | $n$ (dim of CS) | $m$ (Pfaffian) | $\delta = n - m - 1$ | Maneuverability Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unicycle | 3 | 1 | 1 | Type (2,0) |
| **Car-Like** | **3** | **1** | **1** | **Type (2,0)** |
| Differentially Steered (3 wheels) | 3 | 1 | 1 | Type (2,0) |
| Tricycle | 3 | 1 | 1 | Type (2,0) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Car-Like, Unicycle, and Tricycle robots **all share the same maneuverability class** at the kinematic level. The difference is the **shape of admissible velocity set** in the input space — Car-Like has $v$ and $\phi$ as inputs, while Differential Drive has $v_L$ and $v_R$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — every KTU ESE problem on CLWMR will use a subset of these formulas.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rear velocity-x | $\dot{x} = v \cos(\theta)$ | m/s | Longitudinal kinematic eq. |
| 2 | Rear velocity-y | $\dot{y} = v \sin(\theta)$ | m/s | Lateral kinematic eq. |
| 3 | Heading rate | $\dot{\theta} = (v/L) \tan(\phi)$ | rad/s | Yaw dynamics |
| 4 | Radius of curvature | $R = L / \tan(\phi)$ | m | Valid for $\phi \ne 0$ |
| 5 | Path curvature | $\kappa = \tan(\phi) / L$ | 1/m | $\kappa = 1/R$ |
| 6 | Steering rate bound | $\vert\dot{\phi}\vert \le \dot{\phi}_{\max}$ | rad/s | Actuator constraint |
| 7 | Velocity bound | $\vert v \vert \le v_{\max}$ | m/s | Practical drive limit |
| 8 | Ackermann condition | $\cot(\phi_o) - \cot(\phi_i) = W/L$ | dimensionless | 4-wheel model only |
| 9 | Path arc length | $s = R \cdot \Delta\psi$ | m | For a circular arc turn |
| 10 | Yaw during arc | $\Delta\psi = (s/L)\tan(\phi)$ | rad | Integrated from $\dot{\theta}$ |
| 11 | Bicycle model state | $q = (x, y, \theta, \phi)$ | tuple | 4-D configuration |
| 12 | Brockett obstruction | No continuous static feedback stabilizer exists | — | Theorem |

> **Units Convention Used Throughout:** Linear distances in **meters (m)**, angles in **radians (rad)** unless explicitly stated in degrees, time in **seconds (s)**, velocities in **m/s**, and angular velocities in **rad/s**. For exam answers, students should clearly state units to earn full marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility and Engineering Applications

The car-like kinematic model underpins the design and control of:

- **Autonomous Passenger Vehicles** (Waymo, Tesla FSD, Mobileye) — path planning and lane-change maneuvers.
- **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** in smart factories and warehouses (e.g., Amazon Kiva robots navigate using a differential drive, but the heavy-duty versions of forklifts use Ackermann geometry).
- **Planetary Rovers** (NASA's Mars Exploration Rovers, Curiosity, Perseverance) — though they use rocker-bogie suspensions, the steering commands reduce to a car-like kinematic model for slip-compensated path tracking.
- **Agricultural Autonomous Tractors** — straight-line and headland-turn planning.
- **Underwater and Indoor Service Robots** with bicycle-like steering — e.g., museum guide robots.

The mathematical tractability of the bicycle model makes it the **canonical testbed** in academic research for non-holonomic motion planning algorithms such as **Dubins curves**, **Reeds-Shepp curves**, and **RRT\* (Rapidly-exploring Random Trees)**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bicycle Model Kinematic Equations (Forward Kinematics)

### 3.1.1 Setup and Reference Frame

We place a global inertial frame $\{X, Y\}$ on the floor, and a body-fixed frame $\{x_r, y_r\}$ at the midpoint of the rear axle of the bicycle. The position of the rear contact point in the global frame is $(x, y)$. The body frame's $x_r$-axis is aligned with the vehicle's longitudinal direction, making an angle $\theta$ with the global $X$-axis. The front contact point is located at $(x_f, y_f)$ in the global frame and at $(L, 0)$ in the body frame.

The orientation of the steered front wheel (its rolling direction) makes an angle $\phi$ relative to the body $x_r$-axis.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Write the Front Contact Point Position in the Global Frame

Apply the planar rotation matrix $R(\theta)$ to the body-frame coordinates of the front contact point $(L, 0)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_f &= x + L\cos(\theta) \\
y_f &= y + L\sin(\theta)
\end{aligned}$$

This step transforms the front contact point's location from the body frame to the global frame using a pure rotation of $\theta$ about the $Z$-axis (out of the page), with no translation issue because the rear axle origin is the pivot.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Impose the Front-Wheel Rolling Constraint

The velocity of the front contact point $\left(\dot{x_f}, \dot{y_f}\right)$ must be **collinear with the wheel plane** of the steered wheel. The wheel plane has a direction vector $(\cos(\theta + \phi), \sin(\theta + \phi))$ in the global frame (the body axis is at $\theta$, and the wheel is steered by an additional $\phi$).

The orthogonality condition between the velocity and the wheel's lateral axis (perpendicular to the rolling direction) is:

$$\dot{x_f} \sin(\theta + \phi) - \dot{y_f} \cos(\theta + \phi) = 0$$

This states that the lateral component of the front wheel's velocity, in the wheel's own frame, is zero (no slip).

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Differentiate the Front Point Coordinates

Take the time derivative of the position expressions from Step 1:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x_f} &= \dot{x} - L\sin(\theta)\,\dot{\theta} \\
\dot{y_f} &= \dot{y} + L\cos(\theta)\,\dot{\theta}
\end{aligned}$$

Note the negative sign on $\dot{x_f}$'s $\dot{\theta}$ term and the positive on $\dot{y_f}$'s $\dot{\theta}$ term — these arise from differentiating $\cos(\theta)$ and $\sin(\theta)$ respectively.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Substitute into the Front Constraint

Substitute the expressions for $\dot{x_f}$ and $\dot{y_f}$ into the orthogonality equation from Step 2:

$$\begin{aligned}
\big[\dot{x} - L\sin(\theta)\dot{\theta}\big]\sin(\theta + \phi) - \big[\dot{y} + L\cos(\theta)\dot{\theta}\big]\cos(\theta + \phi) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Expand Using Trigonometric Identities

Apply the angle-sum identities:
- $\sin(\theta + \phi) = \sin\theta\cos\phi + \cos\theta\sin\phi$
- $\cos(\theta + \phi) = \cos\theta\cos\phi - \sin\theta\sin\phi$

Distribute and group:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(\sin\theta\cos\phi + \cos\theta\sin\phi) &- L\dot{\theta}\sin\theta(\sin\theta\cos\phi + \cos\theta\sin\phi) \\
- \dot{y}(\cos\theta\cos\phi - \sin\theta\sin\phi) &- L\dot{\theta}\cos\theta(\cos\theta\cos\phi - \sin\theta\sin\phi) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Simplify the $\dot{\theta}$ Coefficient

Collect the terms multiplying $L\dot{\theta}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
L\dot{\theta}\Big[ -\sin\theta(\sin\theta\cos\phi + \cos\theta\sin\phi) - \cos\theta(\cos\theta\cos\phi - \sin\theta\sin\phi) \Big]
\end{aligned}$$

Expand the brackets:

$$\begin{aligned}
= L\dot{\theta}\Big[ -\sin^2\theta\cos\phi - \sin\theta\cos\theta\sin\phi - \cos^2\theta\cos\phi + \cos\theta\sin\theta\sin\phi \Big]
\end{aligned}$$

The $\sin\theta\cos\theta\sin\phi$ terms **cancel** (one negative, one positive), and $-\sin^2\theta - \cos^2\theta = -1$:

$$= L\dot{\theta}\Big[ -\cos\phi(\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta) \Big] = -L\dot{\theta}\cos\phi$$

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Simplify the $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ Coefficients

Group the $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ coefficients:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(\sin\theta\cos\phi + \cos\theta\sin\phi) - \dot{y}(\cos\theta\cos\phi - \sin\theta\sin\phi) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.9 Step 8 — Combine and Apply Rear-Wheel Constraints

Now add the **rear-wheel rolling constraint** (velocity of rear contact point is along the body axis):

$$\dot{x}\sin(\theta) - \dot{y}\cos(\theta) = 0$$

This **forces** the velocity vector to be aligned with $\theta$, which immediately gives:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} = v\cos(\theta) \quad\quad \dot{y} = v\sin(\theta)
\end{aligned}$$

where $v = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}$ is the scalar speed of the rear axle.

### 3.1.10 Step 9 — Solve for $\dot{\theta}$

Substituting $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ and $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ into the simplified constraint from Step 7:

$$v\cos\theta(\sin\theta\cos\phi + \cos\theta\sin\phi) - v\sin\theta(\cos\theta\cos\phi - \sin\theta\sin\phi) = -L\dot{\theta}\cos\phi$$

Expand the left-hand side:

$$\begin{aligned}
v\cos\theta\sin\theta\cos\phi &+ v\cos^2\theta\sin\phi - v\sin\theta\cos\theta\cos\phi + v\sin^2\theta\sin\phi = -L\dot{\theta}\cos\phi
\end{aligned}$$

The first and third terms **cancel**, leaving:

$$v\sin\phi(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) = -L\dot{\theta}\cos\phi$$

Since $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$:

$$v\sin\phi = -L\dot{\theta}\cos\phi$$

Rearranging for $\dot{\theta}$:

$$\dot{\theta} = -\frac{v}{L}\frac{\sin\phi}{\cos\phi} = -\frac{v}{L}\tan\phi$$

The negative sign is a convention artifact (depends on the sign chosen for $\phi$ rotation). Taking the standard convention with $\phi$ defined positive for a left turn and $\theta$ increasing counter-clockwise, the sign convention becomes positive:

$$\boxed{\;\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan(\phi)\;}$$

### 3.1.11 Final Result — The Bicycle Model

The complete forward kinematic model is therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v\cos(\theta) \\
\dot{y} &= v\sin(\theta) \\
\dot{\theta} &= \frac{v}{L}\tan(\phi)
\end{aligned}$$

with the control inputs $u = (v, \phi) \in \mathbb{R} \times (-\phi_{\max}, \phi_{\max})$ and $\dot{\phi} \le \dot{\phi}_{\max}$ for a dynamic steering system.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Radius of Curvature from the Kinematic Model

### 3.2.1 Geometric Setup

Suppose the vehicle moves with constant $v$ and constant $\phi \ne 0$. The trajectory is a circular arc. Let the center of that circle (the ICR) be at $(x_c, y_c)$. The rear axle moves on a circle of radius $R$ centered at $(x_c, y_c)$, and the heading $\theta(t)$ rotates uniformly.

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Express Yaw Rate

From the third kinematic equation, with $v$ and $\phi$ constant:

$$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v\tan\phi}{L} \equiv \omega \quad \text{(constant)}$$

So $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \omega t$.

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Solve the Position Equations

Integrate the $x$ and $y$ equations:

$$\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= x_0 + \int_0^t v\cos(\theta_0 + \omega\tau)\,d\tau = x_0 + \frac{v}{\omega}\Big[\sin(\theta_0 + \omega t) - \sin(\theta_0)\Big] \\
y(t) &= y_0 + \int_0^t v\sin(\theta_0 + \omega\tau)\,d\tau = y_0 - \frac{v}{\omega}\Big[\cos(\theta_0 + \omega t) - \cos(\theta_0)\Big]
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Identify the ICR

A circle centered at $(x_c, y_c)$ with radius $R$ traced by the rear axle is:

$$(x - x_c)^2 + (y - y_c)^2 = R^2$$

The ICR is found by setting the velocity perpendicular to the radius vector. Equivalently, for a parametric circle, the position can be written as:

$$\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= x_c + R\sin(\theta_0 + \omega t) \\
y(t) &= y_c - R\cos(\theta_0 + \omega t)
\end{aligned}$$

Matching coefficients with the integrated solution:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{v}{\omega} &= R \\
\Rightarrow R &= \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{v}{v\tan\phi/L} = \frac{L}{\tan\phi}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.5 Result

$$\boxed{\;R = \frac{L}{\tan(\phi)}\;}$$

This confirms the geometry: a larger steering angle $\phi$ produces a **smaller** turning radius (tighter turn), and a longer wheelbase $L$ produces a **larger** turning radius (wider turn).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Time-Parameterized Motion Under Piecewise-Constant Inputs

The following table consolidates the analytical closed-form expressions for a CLWMR when inputs are **piecewise constant**. This is the standard "motion primitive" used in sampling-based motion planners.

| Primitive | Control Inputs | $\theta(t)$ | $x(t)$ | $y(t)$ | Arc Length $s$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Straight** | $v = v_0$, $\phi = 0$ | $\theta_0$ | $x_0 + v_0 t\cos\theta_0$ | $y_0 + v_0 t\sin\theta_0$ | $v_0 t$ |
| **Circular Arc (L)** | $v = v_0$, $\phi = \phi_0 \ne 0$ | $\theta_0 + \omega t$ | $x_0 + (v_0/\omega)[\sin(\theta_0+\omega t) - \sin\theta_0]$ | $y_0 - (v_0/\omega)[\cos(\theta_0+\omega t) - \cos\theta_0]$ | $R \cdot \omega t = v_0 t$ |
| **Stop (point turn disallowed)** | $v = 0$ | unchanged | unchanged | unchanged | $0$ |

where $\omega = v_0\tan(\phi_0)/L$ and $R = L/\tan(\phi_0)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** A car-like robot **cannot** perform a point turn (zero-radius turn) because $R \to L$ as $\phi \to 90^\circ$, never $R = 0$. This is in stark contrast to a differential-drive robot, which can rotate in place.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example — 7-Mark Template**

**Problem:** A car-like mobile robot has wheelbase $L = 2.5$ m and is currently at pose $(x, y, \theta) = (0, 0, 0^\circ)$ in the global frame. The robot is commanded to move with linear velocity $v = 1.2$ m/s and steering angle $\phi = 15^\circ$ for a duration of $T = 4$ seconds. Find:
1. The new pose $(x, y, \theta)$ at $t = T$.
2. The radius of curvature of the path.
3. The arc length traveled.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute angular velocity $\omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{v\tan(\phi)}{L} = \frac{1.2 \times \tan(15^\circ)}{2.5} = \frac{1.2 \times 0.2679}{2.5} = \frac{0.3215}{2.5} = 0.1286 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** New heading at $t = 4$ s:

$$\theta(T) = \theta_0 + \omega T = 0 + 0.1286 \times 4 = 0.5144 \text{ rad} = 29.48^\circ$$

**Step 3:** New position:

$$x(T) = \frac{v}{\omega}\big[\sin(\theta_0 + \omega T) - \sin(\theta_0)\big] = \frac{1.2}{0.1286}\big[\sin(0.5144) - 0\big] = 9.332 \times 0.4918 = 4.590 \text{ m}$$

$$y(T) = -\frac{v}{\omega}\big[\cos(\theta_0 + \omega T) - \cos(\theta_0)\big] = -9.332 \times [\cos(0.5144) - 1] = -9.332 \times [-0.1290] = 1.204 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Radius of curvature:

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan(\phi)} = \frac{2.5}{0.2679} = 9.332 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Arc length:

$$s = vT = 1.2 \times 4 = 4.8 \text{ m} \quad \text{(or equivalently } s = R\omega T = 9.332 \times 0.1286 \times 4 = 4.8 \text{ m)}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{(x, y, \theta) = (4.59 \text{ m}, \; 1.20 \text{ m}, \; 29.48^\circ), \quad R = 9.33 \text{ m}, \quad s = 4.8 \text{ m}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Marking Note:** Always carry **3–4 significant figures** in intermediate steps and quote the final answer with proper units. The KTU examiner's key will specifically check the $\omega$ and $R$ intermediate values, not just the final pose.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Car-Like Robot Architecture & Kinematic Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputV["Velocity Command v"] --> kinCore{"Kinematic Model<br/>Bicycle Eqs"}
    inputPhi["Steering Command phi"] --> kinCore
    inputV --> curv["Curvature kappa = tan phi over L"]
    inputPhi --> curv
    kinCore --> stateX["State Update x dot, y dot, theta dot"]
    stateX --> poseOut["Pose Estimate x, y, theta"]
    curv --> ackCheck{"Ackermann Condition<br/>cot phi_o minus cot phi_i = W over L"}
    ackCheck --> steerServo["Steering Servo<br/>Actuator Command"]
    steerServo --> frontWheels["Front Wheel Angles phi_i, phi_o"]
    frontWheels --> kinCore
    poseOut --> pathPlan["Path Planner<br/>Reeds-Shepp / Dubins"]
    pathPlan --> inputV
    pathPlan --> inputPhi
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The kinematic model forms the **central core** of the control loop. Path-planning algorithms (e.g., Reeds-Shepp) output velocity and steering setpoints, which are integrated by the kinematic equations to produce the robot's pose. The Ackermann condition enforces correct inner/outer wheel angle ratios when the controller is mapped to a four-wheeled physical platform.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Configuration Space State Transition Graph

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StraightLine
    StraightLine --> ArcTurn: phi nonzero
    ArcTurn --> StraightLine: phi returns to 0
    ArcTurn --> ReverseArc: v changes sign and phi nonzero
    ReverseArc --> StraightLine
    StraightLine --> ParkManeuver: low-speed switching sequence
    ParkManeuver --> GoalPose: end of three-point turn
    GoalPose --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This finite-state machine is the **practical** implementation of CLWMR motion primitives in mobile robot navigation stacks (e.g., ROS `move_base` with `teb_local_planner`).

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Fallback Schematic for Physical Drawing)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensors["Sensing Layer"]
        IMU["IMU<br/>Yaw rate feedback"]
        EncR["Rear Wheel Encoder<br/>Odometry"]
        EncS["Steering Encoder<br/>phi feedback"]
        GPS["GNSS or LiDAR Localization<br/>Global pose estimate"]
    end
    subgraph Ctrl["Control Layer"]
        Tracker["Trajectory Tracker<br/>e.g. Pure Pursuit, Stanley"]
        Planner["Local Planner<br/>MPC or TEB"]
        LowCtrl["Low-level Driver<br/>PWM to motors"]
    end
    subgraph Plant["Vehicle Plant"]
        DriveMotor["Drive Motor<br/>Rear axle"]
        SteerMotor["Steering Motor<br/>Front axle"]
        Bicycle["Bicycle Kinematic Model<br/>Equations 1, 2, 3"]
    end
    IMU --> Tracker
    EncR --> Tracker
    EncS --> Tracker
    GPS --> Planner
    Planner --> Tracker
    Tracker --> LowCtrl
    LowCtrl --> DriveMotor
    LowCtrl --> SteerMotor
    DriveMotor --> Bicycle
    SteerMotor --> Bicycle
    Bicycle --> EncR
    Bicycle --> EncS
    Bicycle --> IMU
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The **tracker** is typically a Pure Pursuit or Stanley controller for the bicycle model. The **planner** uses a model predictive control (MPC) formulation that explicitly enforces $\vert\phi\vert \le \phi_{\max}$ and $\vert v \vert \le v_{\max}$. The kinematic model lies in the *Plant* block and serves both as the prediction model and as the ground-truth simulator.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign errors on $\dot{\theta}$:** The biggest single source of lost marks. Always verify the sign convention for $\phi$ (positive for left turn → positive $\dot{\theta}$ for counter-clockwise) and state it explicitly.
> 2. **Using $\phi$ in degrees in the formulas:** The trigonometric functions in the model equations *require* radians (or equivalently, keep $\phi$ in degrees but use a precomputed $\tan$ in degrees — state this explicitly). Mixing up is a 1–2 mark penalty.
> 3. **Forgetting the non-holonomic constraint:** Students often write $\dot{y} = v\sin(\theta + \phi)$ — this is wrong; the $\phi$ appears only in the $\dot{\theta}$ equation.
> 4. **Conflating Ackermann with bicycle:** The Ackermann condition is for **four-wheeled** real cars; the bicycle model implicitly satisfies it by construction. Do not write the Ackermann equation in a bicycle-model question.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** Always mention *rolling without slipping*, *rigid body*, and *flat ground* before deriving.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Car-Like Wheeled Mobile Robot. What is meant by the Ackermann steering geometry? [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Model Answer:**

A Car-Like Wheeled Mobile Robot (CLWMR) is a non-holonomic ground vehicle that mimics the steering configuration of a passenger automobile — with a **fixed rear driving axle** and a **steered front axle**. The state is described by $(x, y, \theta, \phi)$, where $\phi$ is the steering angle. The **Ackermann steering geometry** refers to the mechanical arrangement in which the inner and outer front wheels are turned at *different* angles such that the perpendiculars to all wheel planes intersect at a **single instantaneous center of rotation (ICR)** located on the extension of the rear axle. The condition $\cot(\phi_o) - \cot(\phi_i) = W/L$ guarantees zero tire slip during a turn. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a differential drive mobile robot and a car-like mobile robot in terms of maneuverability. [3 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Differential Drive | Car-Like |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum turning radius | **Zero** (can rotate in place) | **$L/\tan(\phi_{\max})$** (cannot spin in place) |
| Configuration space | $(x, y, \theta)$ | $(x, y, \theta, \phi)$ |
| Control inputs | $(v_L, v_R)$ — left/right wheel speeds | $(v, \phi)$ — linear vel + steering |
| Point turn | Yes | No |
| Indoor suitability | Excellent (tight spaces) | Limited (open areas only) |

Both are **non-holonomic** and belong to the **Type (2,0)** maneuverability class, but the differential drive has a strictly larger admissible velocity set. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Derive the kinematic equations of a car-like mobile robot using the bicycle model. State clearly all the assumptions made. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The vehicle moves on a flat, horizontal plane.
2. Wheels roll without slipping (no longitudinal or lateral tire slip).
3. The bicycle model reduces the four-wheel vehicle to two virtual wheels (one at each axle midpoint).
4. Steering angle $\phi$ is the angle of the front virtual wheel measured from the vehicle's longitudinal axis.
5. The body is rigid.

**Derivation Sketch:**

Let the rear wheel midpoint be at $(x, y)$ in the global frame with body heading $\theta$. The front wheel is at $(L, 0)$ in the body frame, i.e., $(x + L\cos\theta, y + L\sin\theta)$ in the global frame.

*Rear wheel non-slip constraint:* The rear wheel velocity is collinear with the body axis, giving:
$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \;\; \dot{y} = v\sin\theta$$

*Front wheel non-slip constraint:* The front wheel velocity is collinear with the steered wheel plane (at angle $\theta + \phi$):
$$\big(\dot{x} - L\sin\theta\,\dot\theta\big)\sin(\theta + \phi) - \big(\dot{y} + L\cos\theta\,\dot\theta\big)\cos(\theta + \phi) = 0$$

Substituting $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ and simplifying using $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$:

$$-L\dot\theta\cos\phi = -v\sin\phi \;\Rightarrow\; \dot\theta = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi$$

**Final equations:**
$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \qquad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \qquad \dot\theta = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi$$

**(7 marks — 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 1 for stating the system, 1 for the final box)**

---

#### (b) A car-like robot with wheelbase $L = 2$ m starts at the origin with initial heading $\theta_0 = 30^\circ$. It is driven at constant $v = 1$ m/s and constant steering angle $\phi = 20^\circ$ for $T = 5$ s. Compute the final pose $(x, y, \theta)$ and the arc length traveled. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute angular velocity:**
$$\omega = \frac{v\tan\phi}{L} = \frac{1 \times \tan 20^\circ}{2} = \frac{0.3640}{2} = 0.1820 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Final heading:**
$$\theta_f = \theta_0 + \omega T = 30^\circ + 0.1820 \times 5 = 30^\circ + 0.9100 \text{ rad}$$
$$0.9100 \text{ rad} = 52.14^\circ \;\Rightarrow\; \theta_f = 30^\circ + 52.14^\circ = 82.14^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Final position:**
$$x_f = \frac{v}{\omega}\big[\sin(\theta_0 + \omega T) - \sin\theta_0\big] = \frac{1}{0.1820}\big[\sin(82.14^\circ) - \sin(30^\circ)\big]$$
$$= 5.4945 \times [0.9908 - 0.5000] = 5.4945 \times 0.4908 = 2.697 \text{ m}$$

$$y_f = -\frac{v}{\omega}\big[\cos(\theta_0 + \omega T) - \cos\theta_0\big] = -5.4945 \times [\cos(82.14^\circ) - \cos(30^\circ)]$$
$$= -5.4945 \times [0.1351 - 0.8660] = -5.4945 \times (-0.7309) = 4.016 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Arc length:**
$$s = vT = 1 \times 5 = 5 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{(x_f, y_f, \theta_f) = (2.70 \text{ m}, \; 4.02 \text{ m}, \; 82.14^\circ), \quad s = 5 \text{ m}}$$

**(7 marks — 1 for ω, 1 for θ_f, 2 each for x_f and y_f, 1 for s)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

#### (a) With a neat sketch, explain the concept of an Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) for a car-like mobile robot. Derive the relationship between the steering angle and the radius of curvature. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Q]`

**Model Solution:**

The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is the point about which the entire vehicle is instantaneously rotating at a given moment. For a car-like robot executing a steady turn with non-zero steering angle, all wheel velocity vectors are perpendicular to lines drawn from the contact patch to the ICR.

**Geometry of ICR:**

Let the wheelbase be $L$ and the steering angle of the (virtual) front wheel be $\phi$. The perpendicular to the rear wheel's rolling direction is the line through the rear contact point at angle $\theta + 90^\circ$ in the global frame. The perpendicular to the front wheel's rolling direction is the line through the front contact point at angle $\theta + \phi + 90^\circ$. The intersection of these two perpendiculars is the ICR, located at a distance $R$ from the rear contact point along the rear axle's perpendicular.

**Derivation:**

In the triangle formed by the rear contact point, the ICR, and the front contact point:
- The base (between the contact points) has length $L$.
- The angle at the front contact point (between the body axis and the front wheel's perpendicular) is $\phi$.
- The side opposite to this angle is $R$.

By trigonometry of a right triangle:
$$\tan(\phi) = \frac{L}{R} \;\Rightarrow\; R = \frac{L}{\tan(\phi)}$$

Equivalently, the curvature is $\kappa = 1/R = \tan(\phi)/L$.

**Special cases:**
- $\phi = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; R \to \infty$ (straight line motion).
- $\phi = 90^\circ$ (theoretical) $\;\Rightarrow\; R = L$ (tightest possible arc, body pivots about a point at the front axle's perpendicular).

**(7 marks — 2 for ICR explanation with diagram, 3 for derivation, 2 for special cases)**

---

#### (b) A car-like robot must move from pose $q_i = (0, 0, 0^\circ)$ to goal pose $q_g = (5, 0, 0^\circ)$. It executes the following sequence: drive forward at $\phi = 0$ for 2 s with $v = 1$ m/s, then turn left at $\phi = 30^\circ$ with $v = 1$ m/s for 3 s, then drive straight again at $\phi = 0$ for 2 s. Compute the final pose after this maneuver. Take $L = 2$ m. [7 Marks]
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Q]`

**Model Solution:**

**Phase 1 (0 to 2 s):** $\phi = 0$, $v = 1$ m/s, $\theta_0 = 0^\circ$.

- $\omega = 0$ (straight line).
- $x = 0 + 1\times 2 \times \cos 0 = 2$ m.
- $y = 0 + 1\times 2 \times \sin 0 = 0$ m.
- $\theta = 0^\circ$.

State after Phase 1: $(2, 0, 0^\circ)$.

**Phase 2 (2 to 5 s):** $\phi = 30^\circ$, $v = 1$ m/s, $\theta_0 = 0^\circ$, $\Delta t = 3$ s.

- $\omega = (1 \times \tan 30^\circ) / 2 = 0.5774 / 2 = 0.2887$ rad/s.
- $\Delta\theta = 0.2887 \times 3 = 0.8660$ rad $= 49.62^\circ$.
- $\theta_0 + \omega T = 0 + 0.8660 = 0.8660$ rad.

Position update:
- $\Delta x = (1/0.2887)\big[\sin(0.8660) - \sin(0)\big] = 3.4641 \times [0.7625 - 0] = 2.641$ m.
- $\Delta y = -(1/0.2887)\big[\cos(0.8660) - \cos(0)\big] = -3.4641 \times [0.6470 - 1] = -3.4641 \times (-0.3530) = 1.223$ m.

State after Phase 2: $(2 + 2.641, \; 0 + 1.223, \; 49.62^\circ) = (4.641, 1.223, 49.62^\circ)$.

**Phase 3 (5 to 7 s):** $\phi = 0$, $v = 1$ m/s, $\theta_0 = 49.62^\circ$, $\Delta t = 2$ s.

- $\Delta x = 1 \times 2 \times \cos(49.62^\circ) = 2 \times 0.6470 = 1.294$ m.
- $\Delta y = 1 \times 2 \times \sin(49.62^\circ) = 2 \times 0.7625 = 1.525$ m.
- $\theta$ unchanged: $49.62^\circ$.

**Final Pose:**
$$\boxed{(x_f, y_f, \theta_f) = (4.641 + 1.294, \; 1.223 + 1.525, \; 49.62^\circ) = (5.935, \; 2.748, \; 49.62^\circ)}$$

The robot **overshoots** the target (which was at $x=5$) and ends with a heading of $49.62^\circ$ instead of $0^\circ$. A practical controller would now issue a corrective reverse-arc maneuver to align with the goal — this is precisely the structure of a **Reeds-Shepp path**.

**(7 marks — 2 marks per phase + 1 for final composition + 1 for engineering interpretation)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this section on your study wall — high-density, exam-aligned, no fluff.**

- ✅ The CLWMR is a **Type (2,0)** non-holonomic robot with configuration $q = (x, y, \theta, \phi)$.
- ✅ The **Bicycle Model** equations are: $\dot x = v\cos\theta$, $\dot y = v\sin\theta$, $\dot\theta = (v/L)\tan\phi$.
- ✅ The **radius of curvature** is $R = L/\tan\phi$ — bounded below by $L/\tan(\phi_{\max})$.
- ✅ The **Ackermann condition** applies only to a four-wheeled platform: $\cot\phi_o - \cot\phi_i = W/L$.
- ✅ A CLWMR **cannot** execute a point turn — it always needs forward or reverse motion plus steering to reorient.
- ✅ **Brockett's Theorem** forbids any smooth time-invariant feedback stabilizer → parking requires switching or time-varying control.
- ✅ The CLWMR is **controllable** — any pose in $\mathbb{R}^2 \times S$ is reachable from any other.
- ✅ **Closed-form motion primitives** for constant inputs: straight line (line integral) and circular arc (trigonometric integrals).
- ✅ Real-world applications: autonomous cars, AGVs, planetary rovers, agricultural machinery, indoor service robots.
- ✅ **Sign convention for $\phi$:** positive $\phi$ → positive $\dot\theta$ (counter-clockwise yaw) under the standard right-hand rule.
- ✅ **Units:** SI throughout — meters, seconds, radians, rad/s, m/s.
- ✅ When solving KTU problems, always state **assumptions** (rolling without slip, rigid body, flat ground) before deriving — this is a 1-mark "freebie" that examiners routinely award.
- ✅ **Angle input discipline:** Use **radians** in equations; convert to degrees **only** for the final answer quotation.

> **END OF NOTES — Car-Like Wheeled Mobile Robot | Module 3 | PECDT633**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Model of a Differential Drive and a Steered Mobile Robot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Kinematic Model of a Differential Drive and a Steered Mobile Robot ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Kinematic Model of a Mobile Robot** is the mathematical abstraction that describes the relationship between the robot's actuator inputs (wheel angular velocities / steering angles) and the resulting motion of its chassis in a 2D Cartesian workspace, **without** considering the forces or torques that produce that motion. Specifically: - A **Differential Drive Mobile Robot (DDMR)** is a wheeled mobile robot whose motion is achieved by independently varying the angular velocities of two coaxial drive wheels. - A **Steered (Car-like / Ackermann) Mobile Robot** is a wheeled mobile robot that achieves directional changes by orienting a single front steering wheel through a steering angle, while a single drive input provides translational motion. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Shopping Cart vs. The Car** > - **Differential Drive** behaves like a **motorized shopping cart** with two big drive wheels at the back. To turn left, you simply slow down (or reverse) the left wheel while speeding up the right wheel. There is no steering wheel — the robot "skids" to turn, like a tank. > - **Steered Mobile Robot** behaves like a **child's ride-on toy car**. It has a fixed rear axle and a pivoting front wheel. To turn, you must rotate the steering wheel (change the steering angle). The car cannot move sideways; it must always align its wheels with the direction of travel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is "Kinematic" Different from "Dynamic"?** > Kinematics answers the *geometric* question: *"If my wheels spin at this rate, where will my robot be 1 second later?"* > Dynamics answers the *force* question: *"What motor torque is required to achieve that wheel spin rate?"* > The KTU Module 3 syllabus focuses exclusively on **kinematics**. ### 1.3 Standard Notation and Physical Constants | Symbol | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---| | $(x, y)$ | Position of robot reference point in world frame | meters (m) | | $\theta$ | Heading (orientation) angle of the robot | radians (rad) | | $v$ | Linear (translational) velocity of the robot | m/s | | $\omega$ | Angular (rotational) velocity of the robot | rad/s | | $r$ | Radius of the drive wheel | m | | $L$ | Wheelbase (distance between wheels) | m | | $\phi$ | Steering angle (car-like only) | rad | | $\omega_R, \omega_L$ | Angular velocities of Right and Left wheels | rad/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Configuration Space (Posture):** The complete state of a mobile robot in a 2D plane is described by the **posture vector** $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^T$. This 3-tuple defines a point in a 3-dimensional **Configuration Space** $\mathcal{C}$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Wheeled Mobile Robots (WMR) KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to identify WMR types. WMRs are classified by the number and type of wheels: 1. **Differential Drive (2 fixed wheels + 1 castor)**: Two independently driven wheels on a common axis. The most common in research and indoor robots (e.g., *TurtleBot*, *Roomba*). 2. **Steered / Ackermann / Car-like (1 steer + 1 drive)**: Mimics an automobile. 3. **Synchronous Drive (3 or 4 wheels steered in unison)**: All wheels rotate and steer together (e.g., *B21r* robot). 4. **Omnidirectional (Mecanum / Swedish wheels)**: Can move in any direction without reorienting. ### 2.2 Nonholonomic Constraint — The "Why" Behind the Math > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Concept: Nonholonomic Constraint** > A **nonholonomic constraint** is a velocity-level restriction that cannot be integrated into a position-level constraint. For both differential drive and steered robots, the defining constraint is: > $$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$ > This means the robot **cannot move sideways** — its velocity vector must always align with its heading $\theta$. This is *why* the robot needs 2 actuators (drive + steer, or 2 drives) but has 3 configuration variables $(x, y, \theta)$. ### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Robot Type | Forward Kinematic Equations | Inverse Kinematic Equations | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Differential Drive** | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$
$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$
$\dot{\theta} = \omega$ | $v = \dfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$
$\omega = \dfrac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | | 2 | **Differential Drive (Wheel Vel.)** | — | $\omega_R = \dfrac{2v + L\omega}{2r}$
$\omega_L = \dfrac{2v - L\omega}{2r}$ | | 3 | **Steered (Car-like)** | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$
$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$
$\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{v}{L}\tan\phi$ | $v = \pm\sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}$
$\phi = \arctan\left(\dfrac{L\dot{\theta}}{v}\right)$ | | 4 | **Nonholonomic Constraint** | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | — | | 5 | **Posture Update (Euler)** | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\Delta t\cos\theta$
$y_{k+1} = y_k + v\Delta t\sin\theta$
$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega\Delta t$ | — | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These kinematic models are the foundation of every mobile robot's **localization, path planning (RRT, A*), and motion control (PID)** algorithm. The forward model predicts where the robot *should* be; comparing this with sensor (odometry) readings gives the localization error. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differential Drive Forward Kinematics **Setup:** Consider a DDMR with two drive wheels of radius $r$ separated by distance $L$. Let $I$ be the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR), located at distance $R$ from the wheel axis midpoint. **Step 1 — Wheel Velocities in terms of Wheel Angular Velocities:** Each wheel rolls without slipping. The linear velocity of the right wheel contact point is $v_R = r\omega_R$ and of the left is $v_L = r\omega_L$. **Step 2 — Geometry of Pure Rotation about ICR:** Since the robot rotates about ICR with angular velocity $\omega$: $$v_R = (R + L/2)\omega$$ $$v_L = (R - L/2)\omega$$ **Step 3 — Solve the Linear System for $v$ and $\omega$:** Adding the two equations: $$v_R + v_L = 2R\omega$$ The **linear velocity of the robot's center** is $v = R\omega$, therefore: $$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$ Subtracting the two equations: $$v_R - v_L = L\omega$$ Therefore the **angular velocity of the robot** is: $$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$ **Step 4 — Express in World Frame (Posture Kinematics):** The robot's chassis is oriented at angle $\theta$ in the world frame. The chassis velocity components are: $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$$ $$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$$ $$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$ **Final Forward Kinematic Model:** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)\cos\theta \\ \dot{y} &= \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)\sin\theta \\ \dot{\theta} &= \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) \end{aligned}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential Drive Inverse Kinematics The inverse problem: *Given a desired $v$ and $\omega$, find the required $\omega_R$ and $\omega_L$.* From the relations derived in Step 3 of §3.1: $$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} \implies \omega_R - \omega_L = \frac{L\omega}{r}$$ $$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} \implies \omega_R + \omega_L = \frac{2v}{r}$$ Solving this 2×2 linear system by addition and subtraction: $$\begin{aligned} 2\omega_R &= \frac{2v}{r} + \frac{L\omega}{r} \\ \omega_R &= \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r} \\[6pt] 2\omega_L &= \frac{2v}{r} - \frac{L\omega}{r} \\ \omega_L &= \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r} \end{aligned}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steered (Car-like) Robot Kinematics **Setup:** The robot has wheelbase $L$ and front-wheel steering angle $\phi$. **Step 1 — Velocity of the Rear (Drive) Wheel:** Let $v$ be the longitudinal velocity of the rear-wheel center (reference point). The velocity vector is aligned with the chassis heading $\theta$: $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta \quad ; \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta$$ **Step 2 — Relate $\dot{\theta}$ to $v$ and $\phi$:** The front wheel is at distance $L$ ahead. The steering angle $\phi$ is the angle between the front wheel and the chassis axis. The instantaneous angular velocity of the chassis is the same as the rotation rate of the front wheel's direction: $$\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi$$ > [!NOTE] > **Geometric Justification:** The perpendicular distance from the ICR to the chassis axis is $L/\tan\phi$. The angular velocity is then $v \cdot \tan\phi / L$. **Step 3 — Nonholonomic Constraint Verification:** Differentiate the velocity constraint to verify: $$\dot{y}\cos\theta - \dot{x}\sin\theta = v\sin\theta\cos\theta - v\cos\theta\sin\theta = 0$$ This confirms the nonholonomic constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. **Step 4 — Inverse Kinematics:** From the forward equations, isolate the control inputs $v$ and $\phi$: $$v^2 = \dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2 \implies v = \pm\sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}$$ $$\tan\phi = \frac{L\dot{\theta}}{v} \implies \phi = \arctan\left(\frac{L\dot{\theta}}{v}\right)$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Differential Drive | Steered (Car-like) | |---|---|---| | Number of Actuators | 2 (independent wheels) | 2 (1 drive + 1 steer) | | Degrees of Freedom (DOF) | 3 (in 2D plane) | 3 (in 2D plane) | | Maneuverability | 0 (cannot move sideways) | 0 (cannot move sideways) | | Steerability | 2 (can achieve any $(v, \omega)$) | 1 (steering angle bounded) | | Turning Radius | 0 (can rotate in place) | $\geq L/\tan\phi_{max}$ | | Cost & Simplicity | Simple, cheap | More complex (Ackermann geometry) | | Indoor/Outdoor | Indoor primarily | Outdoor primarily | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Differential Drive — Reference Geometry ```mermaid graph TD A[World Frame X-Y] --> B[Robot Posture x, y, theta] B --> C{Wheel Axis Midpoint} C --> D[Left Wheel vL = r times wL] C --> E[Right Wheel vR = r times wR] D --> F[ICR Instantaneous Center] E --> F F --> G[Robot rotates with angular velocity omega] B --> H[Linear velocity v = r/2 times wR + wL] H --> I[Velocities in world frame xdot, ydot, thetadot] ``` ### 4.2 Differential Drive — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR INPUT1[Desired Linear Velocity v] --> MIX[Motor Controller] INPUT2[Desired Angular Velocity omega] --> MIX MIX --> INVK[Inverse Kinematics Module] INVK --> WRCALC[Right Wheel omegaR = 2v + L omega / 2r] INVK --> WLCALC[Left Wheel omegaL = 2v minus L omega / 2r] WRCALC --> RMOTOR[Right DC Motor] WLCALC --> LMOTOR[Left DC Motor] RMOTOR --> CHASSIS[Robot Chassis] LMOTOR --> CHASSIS CHASSIS --> FWDFK[Forward Kinematics] FWDFK --> POSE[Updated Posture x, y, theta] POSE --> ENCODER[Wheel Encoders Feedback] ENCODER --> MIX ``` ### 4.3 Steered (Car-like) Robot — Kinematic Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Desired Path Trajectory] --> B[Path Planner Module] B --> C[Set v and phi inputs] C --> D[Steering Actuator Controls phi] C --> E[Drive Motor Controls v] D --> F[Front Wheel Pivots by phi] E --> G[Rear Wheel Rotates at v] F --> H[Chassis motion with thetadot = v/L tan phi] G --> H H --> I[World Frame Posture xdot, ydot] H --> J[Heading thetadot] I --> K[Constraint Check nonholonomic] J --> K K --> L[Valid Mobile Robot Motion] ``` ### 4.4 Comparative Architecture Flow ```mermaid graph TB subgraph DIFFERENTIAL [Differential Drive] D1[Two independent drives] --> D2[Direct velocity mixing] D2 --> D3[Omega R and Omega L wheel commands] end subgraph STEERED [Steered Car-like] S1[Single drive v] --> S2[Single steer phi] S2 --> S3[Ackermann geometry constraint] S1 --> S3 end D3 --> COMMON[Nonholonomic Constraint] S3 --> COMMON COMMON --> RESULT[Restricted to 2D manifold in C-space] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Define a Differential Drive Mobile Robot. Write the nonholonomic constraint equation for a wheeled mobile robot moving in a 2D plane.* **Model Answer:** A Differential Drive Mobile Robot is a two-wheeled robot in which motion (both translation and rotation) is achieved by independently controlling the angular velocities of two coaxial drive wheels. The nonholonomic constraint is: $$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$ This constraint states that the robot's velocity vector must be aligned with its heading, hence the robot cannot translate laterally. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]** *State the forward kinematic equations of a car-like (steered) mobile robot. What is the minimum turning radius?* **Model Answer:** The forward kinematic equations are: $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta \quad ; \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta \quad ; \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi$$ The minimum turning radius is $R_{min} = L/\tan(\phi_{max})$, where $\phi_{max}$ is the maximum permissible steering angle. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — 14 Marks]** (a) **Derive the forward kinematic model of a differential drive mobile robot.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Let $r$ be the wheel radius, $L$ the distance between wheels, and the robot's center move with linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$. **Step 2:** The linear velocities at the right and left wheel contact points are: $$v_R = r\omega_R \quad ; \quad v_L = r\omega_L$$ **Step 3:** Both wheels trace a circular path about the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR) located at distance $R$ from the wheel axis midpoint: $$v_R = (R + L/2)\omega \quad ; \quad v_L = (R - L/2)\omega$$ **Step 4:** Adding and subtracting the two equations: $$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$ $$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$ **Step 5:** Resolving into world frame with heading $\theta$: $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$ Final model: $$\dot{x} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)\cos\theta$$ $$\dot{y} = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)\sin\theta$$ $$\dot{\theta} = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$ **(7 marks awarded for full derivation with diagram reference)** --- (b) **A differential drive robot has $r = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.3$ m. It is at position $(1, 1)$ with $\theta = 30°$. If $\omega_R = 10$ rad/s and $\omega_L = 6$ rad/s, compute $(v, \omega)$ and the new pose after $\Delta t = 0.1$ s.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Linear velocity:** $$v = \frac{0.05}{2}(10 + 6) = 0.025 \times 16 = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Angular velocity:** $$\omega = \frac{0.05}{0.3}(10 - 6) = 0.1667 \times 4 = 0.6667 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 3 — Pose update using Euler integration (with $\theta = \pi/6$):** $$x_{new} = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.1 \times \cos(30°) = 1 + 0.04 \times 0.866 = 1.0346 \text{ m}$$ $$y_{new} = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.1 \times \sin(30°) = 1 + 0.04 \times 0.5 = 1.02 \text{ m}$$ $$\theta_{new} = \frac{\pi}{6} + 0.6667 \times 0.1 = 0.5236 + 0.0667 = 0.5903 \text{ rad} \approx 33.82°$$ **Final Pose:** $(1.0346, \ 1.02, \ 0.5903 \text{ rad})$ **(7 marks — 2 for $v$, 2 for $\omega$, 3 for pose update with correct units)** --- **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 — 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]** (a) **Derive the forward and inverse kinematic equations of a steered (car-like) mobile robot. Show that it satisfies the nonholonomic constraint.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Forward Kinematics:** Let $v$ be the longitudinal velocity of the rear (drive) wheel and $\phi$ the steering angle of the front wheel. With wheelbase $L$ and heading $\theta$: $$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta \quad ; \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta \quad ; \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi$$ **Inverse Kinematics:** Solving for control inputs: $$v = \pm\sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2} \quad ; \quad \phi = \arctan\left(\frac{L\dot{\theta}}{v}\right)$$ **Nonholonomic Verification:** $$\dot{y}\cos\theta - \dot{x}\sin\theta = v\sin\theta\cos\theta - v\cos\theta\sin\theta = 0 \ \checkmark$$ This proves the constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ is satisfied, confirming that the robot cannot translate perpendicular to its heading. **(7 marks)** --- (b) **A car-like robot with $L = 2.5$ m is to be driven at $v = 5$ m/s along a circular path of radius $R = 50$ m.
