Find the required steering angle $\phi$ and the angular velocity $\omega$ of the chassis.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Relation between $R$, $L$, and $\phi$:** For circular motion, the radius is $R = L/\tan\phi$, therefore: $$\tan\phi = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2.5}{50} = 0.05$$ $$\phi = \arctan(0.05) = 0.04996 \text{ rad} \approx 2.86°$$ **Step 2 — Angular velocity of chassis:** $$\omega = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi = \frac{5}{2.5} \times 0.05 = 2 \times 0.05 = 0.1 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Verification using $\omega = v/R$:** $$\omega = \frac{5}{50} = 0.1 \text{ rad/s} \ \checkmark$$ **Final Answer:** $\phi \approx 2.86°$ and $\omega = 0.1$ rad/s. **(7 marks — 3 for $\phi$, 2 for $\omega$, 2 for verification)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing Forward vs. Inverse Kinematics:** Students often mix up the equations. *Forward Kinematics* = actuator inputs $\rightarrow$ robot motion. *Inverse Kinematics* = desired motion $\rightarrow$ actuator inputs. > 2. **Forgetting the Nonholonomic Constraint:** Any kinematic model derivation **must** include the verification that the constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ holds. Omitting this loses 2–3 marks. > 3. **Sign Convention Error in $\omega_L$:** The minus sign in $\omega_L = (2v - L\omega)/(2r)$ is critical. Many students write $\omega_L = (2v + L\omega)/(2r)$, which is dimensionally inconsistent with the direction of turning. > 4. **Radian vs. Degree Mixing:** KTU strictly uses **radians** in kinematic equations. Mixing degrees (e.g., in $\sin 30°$) inside a chain of equations without conversion loses marks. > 5. **Missing the ICR Concept:** A differential drive does not "steer" — it rotates about an Instantaneous Center of Rotation. Failing to mention the ICR in derivations is considered an incomplete answer. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Posture / Configuration:** $\mathbf{q} = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — the complete state of a 2D mobile robot. - **Differential Drive (DD):** Two independently driven coaxial wheels; forward kinematics uses $v$ and $\omega$ computed from wheel angular velocities. - **Steered (Car-like):** One drive + one steer; kinematics includes the steering angle $\phi$ and wheelbase $L$. - **Key Equations — DD Forward:** $v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$, $\omega = \frac{r}{L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$. - **Key Equations — DD Inverse:** $\omega_R = \frac{2v + L\omega}{2r}$, $\omega_L = \frac{2v - L\omega}{2r}$. - **Key Equations — Steered Forward:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \frac{v}{L}\tan\phi$. - **Nonholonomic Constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — applies to **both** DD and steered robots. - **Maneuverability vs. Steerability:** DD has *steerability* 2 (any $(v, \omega)$ reachable) and *maneuverability* 0 (no lateral motion). - **Minimum Turning Radius:** DD = $0$ (can rotate in place); Steered = $L/\tan(\phi_{max})$. - **Pose Update (Euler):** Use $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\Delta t\cos\theta_k$ and similar for $y, \theta$ for discrete simulation. - **No Programming Required in KTU Theory Exams:** Always show analytical derivation and numerical calculation; do not write code.

topics.title: Degree of Freedom and Manoeuvrability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Degree of Freedom and Manoeuvrability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: DEGREE OF FREEDOM AND MANOEUVRABILITY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Degree of Freedom (DOF) — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot is defined as the **minimum number of independent parameters (variables)** required to completely specify the configuration (position and orientation) of the robot mechanism, or equivalently, the **number of independent ways a robot can move** its end-effector within its workspace.

In rigid-body mechanics, a **single rigid body in 3D space possesses 6 Degrees of Freedom**:
- **3 Translational DOF** — movement along the X, Y, and Z axes (surge, sway, heave).
- **3 Rotational DOF** — rotation about the X, Y, and Z axes (roll, pitch, yaw).

For a robotic manipulator, the **total DOF** of the mechanism is governed by the number and type of joints between its links. Each joint removes a certain number of DOF from the free 6-DOF motion of the previous link.

### 1.2 Manoeuvrability — The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Manoeuvrability** is defined as the **number of independent Cartesian directions (both translational and rotational) in which the end-effector (or tool point) of a robot can be positioned or oriented at a given instant**, when the robot is locked in a specific configuration.

It is a **local, instantaneous property** of the robot at a particular configuration, distinct from the global DOF count. It describes the **instantaneous dexterity** — how many of the 6 task-space axes the end-effector can actively control at that exact pose.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Imagine a Human Arm

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of your own arm.
> - Your **shoulder** can swivel in 3 ways (up-down, side-to-side, twist) → 3 DOF.
> - Your **elbow** can bend in 1 way → 1 DOF.
> - Your **wrist** can pitch, yaw, and roll → 3 DOF.
> - Your **fingers** (if we count them) add more.
>
> Total ≈ **7 DOF**. You have **7 joints**, but you only need **6 DOF** to reach any point in space in any orientation. The **extra 1 DOF is called redundancy** — it lets you reach the same point with multiple elbow-up/elbow-down postures, much like parking a car with a steering wheel that can turn extra ways.
>
> **Manoeuvrability** is what you can do at a single frozen instant: at this moment, can my fingertip move up/down/left/right/forward/backward AND rotate in all 3 ways? If yes, you have full **6-DOF manoeuvrability** at that pose.

### 1.4 Why These Concepts Matter in KTU Robotics

| Concept | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **DOF** | Determines **task flexibility** — welding needs 5 DOF, painting 6, complex assembly 7+. |
| **Manoeuvrability** | Determines **local dexterity** — even a 6-DOF robot may have **zero manoeuvrability at a singular configuration** (called a *kinematic singularity*). |
| **Joint Selection** | More DOF ⇒ more motors, higher cost, more complex control. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** A robot needs a **minimum of 6 DOF** to place its end-effector at an arbitrary position **with arbitrary orientation** in 3D Cartesian space.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Joint Types and Their DOF Contributions

Every robotic joint constrains the relative motion between two links. The classification follows the **Kutzbach–Grübler criterion** from kinematic chain theory.

### 2.1.1 Lower Pair Joints (Surface Contact — 1 DOF each)

| Joint Type | Symbol | Relative Motion | DOF |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revolute (Rotational) | **R** | Rotation about a single axis | **1** |
| Prismatic (Sliding) | **P** | Translation along a single axis | **1** |
| Helical (Screw) | **H** | Coupled rotation + translation | **1** |
| Cylindrical | **C** | Rotation + translation along same axis | **2** |
| Universal (Hooke's) | **U** | Rotation about 2 intersecting axes | **2** |
| Spherical (Ball-and-Socket) | **S** | Rotation about 3 concurrent axes | **3** |
| Planar | — | 2 translations + 1 rotation in a plane | **3** |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 syllabus problems, **R and P joints dominate** — they are the building blocks of industrial manipulators (e.g., **RRR, RRP, PPP** configurations).

### 2.2 Grübler's Formula — The Master Equation for Planar and Spatial Mechanisms

For a mechanism with **n** moving links (excluding the ground) and **j** joints:

$$
M = \lambda (n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j} (\lambda - f_i)
$$

Where:
- $M$ = **Mobility / DOF** of the mechanism.
- $\lambda$ = **6 for spatial (3D)** mechanisms, **3 for planar (2D)** mechanisms.
- $f_i$ = **DOF of the $i$-th joint** (1 for R/P/H, 2 for C/U, 3 for S/Planar).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The simplified form most commonly used in KTU exams is:
> - **Spatial:** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
> - **Planar:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$

Where $j_1, j_2, j_3, j_4, j_5$ are the number of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5-DOF joints respectively. The most **board-friendly shortcut** for R/P-only manipulators in 3D:

$$
\boxed{\,M = 6(n-1) - 5j\,}
$$

where $j$ is the total number of lower-pair single-DOF (R or P) joints (each removes 5 DOF = constrains all but 1 motion).

### 2.3 DOF of a Robotic Manipulator (Direct Method)

For a serial-chain manipulator (open kinematic chain) with all single-DOF joints:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{DOF}_{\text{manipulator}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} f_k\,}
$$

where $f_k$ is the DOF of the $k$-th joint (R or P, each = 1).

> [!EXAMPLE]
> A **6R manipulator** (PUMA-type) ⇒ $1+1+1+1+1+1 = \mathbf{6\ DOF}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j$ | Grübler's mobility (3D, R/P joints) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j$ | Grübler's mobility (2D planar) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $M = \lambda(n-1) - \sum(\lambda - f_i)$ | General Grübler | $\lambda = 6$ or $3$ |
| 4 | $\text{DOF} = \sum f_k$ | Sum of joint freedoms (serial) | $f_k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 5 | $\text{Redundancy} = \text{DOF} - 6$ | Extra DOF beyond task requirement | $\geq 0$ |
| 6 | $\text{Manoeuvrability} \leq \text{DOF}$ | Local dexterity bound | Equality at non-singular poses |
| 7 | Workspace = $\bigcup$ reachable configs | Global reachable volume | $m^3$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-symbol warning in tables:** When writing $M$ or any absolute value in a markdown table, **never** use the literal $\vert$ character. Use $\lvert$ or $\rvert$ (LaTeX) to avoid breaking the column structure.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Welding:** 6-DOF articulated robots dominate body-in-white welding lines.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** 7 DOF to provide **kinematic redundancy**, allowing the surgeon to avoid obstacles and reach around anatomy.
- **AGVs / Mobile Robots:** Use 3 DOF $(x, y, \theta)$ for planar navigation — the **unicycle model**.
- **Parallel Manipulators (Stewart Platform):** 6 DOF using 6 prismatic actuators — high stiffness for flight simulators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Grübler's Formula (Spatial Case)

### Step 1 — Count the Free DOF Before Any Constraint
Each link in 3D space, if completely free (unconnected), can move in **6 ways**: 3 translations + 3 rotations.

For a mechanism with $n$ moving links (excluding the fixed ground link), the total free DOF pool is:

$$
\text{Free DOF} = 6n
$$

### Step 2 — Add the Ground Link
We must include the ground (fixed link) so that the ground has its own 6 DOF reference frame. The corrected count of bodies in motion relative to ground is $(n+1)$, but only $n$ are considered "moving." A standard reformulation subtracts the ground's freedom:

$$
\text{Effective free DOF} = 6(n - 1)
$$

> *Why $(n-1)$?* Because we consider the **relative motion of $n$ moving links with respect to the ground**, eliminating the ground's own 6 DOF as the reference.

### Step 3 — Account for Joint Constraints
Each joint **constrains** a certain number of motions. A joint with $f_i$ freedoms **removes** $(6 - f_i)$ motions.

For a **1-DOF joint** (R or P): removes $(6 - 1) = 5$ motions.
For a **2-DOF joint** (U or C): removes $(6 - 2) = 4$ motions.
For a **3-DOF joint** (S or Planar): removes $(6 - 3) = 3$ motions.

Total removed DOF:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{j} (6 - f_i) = 6j - \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
$$

### Step 4 — Subtract Constraints from Free DOF

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \underbrace{6(n-1)}_{\text{Free DOF}} - \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i)}_{\text{Constraints}} \\
&= 6(n-1) - 6j + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i \\
&= 6(n-1-j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Equivalent Standard Form
The classical Grübler form (used in KTU textbooks) is:

$$
\boxed{\,M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i) = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation — PUMA 6R arm:** $n = 6$ moving links, $j_1 = 6$ (all revolute, 1-DOF joints).
> $M = 6(6-1) - 5(6) = 30 - 30 = 0$ ❌
> **Wait — this is the famous Grübler pitfall!** Grübler gives 0 for a serial chain because it does not account for the **actuated joint freedoms**. For a serial manipulator, the correct formula is the **direct sum**: $M = \sum f_i = 6$. KTU problems often test this distinction.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example — SCARA Robot

The **SCARA** (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm) has 4 joints: **R-R-P-R** (3 revolute + 1 prismatic, in that order).

| Step | Action | Computation | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Count links (moving) | base → joint1 → link1 → joint2 → link2 → joint3 → link3 → joint4 → end-effector | $n = 4$ |
| 2 | Count joints | R + R + P + R | $j = 4$ (all $f_i = 1$) |
| 3 | Apply direct-sum method | $M = 1+1+1+1$ | $M = 4$ |
| 4 | Apply Grübler (check) | $M = 6(4-1) - 5(4) = 18 - 20 = -2$ | $M = -2$ |
| 5 | **Resolve the discrepancy** | SCARA is a closed-loop-like 4-bar in some kinematic interpretations; direct sum is correct for the open serial chain | Use $M = 4$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Grübler's formula assumes a **closed kinematic chain** or a **constrained mechanism**. For a **simple open serial manipulator** with all single-DOF actuated joints, **always use the direct sum** $\text{DOF} = \sum f_i$. Grübler applies to **parallel mechanisms, linkages, and closed chains**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Industrial Robots and Their DOF

| Robot Type | Joint Configuration | Links ($n$) | DOF | Manoeuvrability (3D space) | Task Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (Gantry) | P-P-P | 3 | **3** | 3 translations, 0 rotation | Pick-and-place |
| Cylindrical | R-P-P | 3 | **3** | 2 translations + 1 rotation | Assembly, loading |
| Spherical (Polar) | R-R-P | 3 | **3** | 2 translations + 1 rotation | Welding, painting |
| SCARA | R-R-P-R | 4 | **4** | 3 translations (planar) + 1 rotation (Z) | PCB assembly |
| Articulated (PUMA) | R-R-R-R-R-R | 6 | **6** | Full 6 Cartesian DOF | General purpose |
| Redundant (e.g., 7R) | R-R-R-R-R-R-R | 7 | **7** | Full 6 + 1 internal null-space | Surgery, obstacle avoidance |
| Stewart Platform | 6 × (P, S joints) | parallel | **6** | Full 6 Cartesian DOF | Flight sim, machining |

### 3.4 Derivation of Manoeuvrability from the Jacobian

The **Jacobian matrix** $J(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ maps joint velocities $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ to end-effector velocities $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

> *Where $n$ = number of joints (DOF), $m$ = dimension of task space (typically 6).*

The **rank** of the Jacobian at a given configuration $\mathbf{q}_0$ defines the **dimension of the instantaneously reachable task-space velocities**:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Manoeuvrability}(\mathbf{q}_0) = \text{rank}\bigl[J(\mathbf{q}_0)\bigr]\,}
$$

**Special cases:**
- $\text{rank}(J) = 6$ ⇒ full 6-DOF manoeuvrability, non-singular.
- $\text{rank}(J) = 5$ ⇒ lost 1 DOF (singular), end-effector cannot move in 1 Cartesian direction.
- $\text{rank}(J) = 0$ ⇒ complete loss of manoeuvrability (rare, only at extreme degenerate poses).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** A 6-DOF robot is **NOT guaranteed to have 6 manoeuvrability at every configuration**. Near elbow singularities, wrist singularities, or shoulder singularities, the rank drops. This is why **path planning must avoid singular configurations** — a board-favourite numerical question!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Joints to Manoeuvrability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Mechanism Construction] --> B[Identify Number of Links: n]
    A --> C[Identify Joint Types and DOF: f sub i]
    B --> D{Is it Open or Closed Chain?}
    C --> D
    D -- Open Serial --> E[Direct Sum Method: DOF = Sum f sub i]
    D -- Closed or Parallel --> F[Grublers Formula: M = 6n-1 minus 5j for R-P]
    E --> G[Total DOF of Robot]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Compare to 6 Task Axes}
    H -- DOF equals 6 --> I[Non-Redundant Manipulator]
    H -- DOF greater than 6 --> J[Redundant Manipulator]
    H -- DOF less than 6 --> K[Deficient Manipulator - Loss of Orientation or Position]
    G --> L[Compute Jacobian J of q]
    L --> M[Rank of J at Configuration q0]
    M --> N[Manoeuvrability = Rank of J]
    N --> O{Rank equals 6?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Full Manoeuvrability - No Singularity]
    O -- No --> Q[Singular Configuration - Loss of Manoeuvrability]
    style G fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style N fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style P fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style Q fill:#FF6B6B,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — DOF Composition of a 6R Articulated Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Link - 0 DOF] -->|Joint 1: Revolute| B[Shoulder Link]
    B -->|Joint 2: Revolute| C[Upper Arm Link]
    C -->|Joint 3: Revolute| D[Elbow Link]
    D -->|Joint 4: Revolute| E[Forearm Link]
    E -->|Joint 5: Revolute| F[Wrist Link 1]
    F -->|Joint 6: Revolute| G[End Effector]
    H[Task Space: 3 Translations + 3 Rotations = 6 DOF] -.->|Mapping via J q| G
    style A fill:#E0E0E0
    style B fill:#FFE4B5
    style C fill:#FFE4B5
    style D fill:#FFE4B5
    style E fill:#FFE4B5
    style F fill:#FFE4B5
    style G fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF
    style H fill:#98FB98
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Manoeuvrability Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Define Joint Variables q1 to qn] --> S2[Step 2: Derive Forward Kinematics x = f of q]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute Jacobian J = partial x over partial q]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Evaluate J at Current q0]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute Rank of J]
    S5 --> S6{Is Rank less than 6?}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Full Manoeuvrability Achieved - 6D]
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Lost Manoeuvrability - Singular Configuration Detected]
    S8 --> S9[Apply Damped Least Squares or Null-Space Motion]
    S9 --> S10[Re-plan Trajectory to Avoid Singularity]
    style S7 fill:#90EE90
    style S8 fill:#FF6B6B
    style S10 fill:#FFD700
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing Robot DOF for a Task

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Industrial Task] --> Q1{Task in 2D or 3D?}
    Q1 -- 2D Plane --> T1[Pick-and-Place in 2D: Use 3 DOF Cartesian or SCARA 4 DOF]
    Q1 -- 3D Space --> Q2{Does Orientation Matter?}
    Q2 -- No --> T2[Use 3 DOF Cartesian P-P-P or Cylindrical R-P-P]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is Obstacle Avoidance Needed?}
    Q3 -- No --> T3[Use 6 DOF Articulated Robot R-R-R-R-R-R]
    Q3 -- Yes --> T4[Use 7 plus DOF Redundant Manipulator]
    style T1 fill:#87CEEB
    style T2 fill:#FFD700
    style T3 fill:#90EE90
    style T4 fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Degree of Freedom (DOF) of a robot. Mention the minimum number of DOFs required for a robot to position and orient its end-effector arbitrarily in 3D space.**

**Model Answer:**

Degree of Freedom (DOF) of a robot is defined as the **number of independent parameters (or independent motions)** required to completely specify the position and orientation of the robot's end-effector in space. Each joint in a manipulator contributes to the total DOF based on its type — a revolute or prismatic joint contributes 1 DOF, while joints like spherical contribute 3 DOF.

For a robot to position its end-effector at an **arbitrary point in 3D space** with **arbitrary orientation**, it must be able to control **3 translations** (X, Y, Z) and **3 rotations** (roll, pitch, yaw).

Hence, the minimum DOF required is:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Minimum DOF} = 6\,}
$$

Any robot with exactly 6 DOF is called a **non-redundant manipulator** (e.g., PUMA robot). Robots with more than 6 DOF are **kinematically redundant** (e.g., 7-DOF KUKA LWR).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Degree of Freedom and Manoeuvrability of a robot.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Degree of Freedom (DOF) | Manoeuvrability |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Total number of independent joint variables of the robot. | Number of independent task-space directions the end-effector can move instantaneously. |
| **Nature** | A **global, structural** property of the robot. | A **local, instantaneous** property at a specific configuration. |
| **Computation** | Sum of joint DOF: $\text{DOF} = \sum f_i$. | Rank of Jacobian: $M(\mathbf{q}_0) = \text{rank}[J(\mathbf{q}_0)]$. |
| **Variation** | Constant for a given robot design. | **Varies** with configuration; drops at singular poses. |
| **Maximum Value** | Equal to the number of joints. | Bounded above by $\min(\text{DOF}, 6)$. |
| **Example** | A 6R arm has 6 DOF. | The same 6R arm has manoeuvrability 6 at non-singular poses, but only 4 at a wrist singularity. |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students **often confuse Grübler's formula with the direct sum method** — read the question carefully: open serial chain ⇒ use direct sum; closed/parallel chain ⇒ use Grübler.
> - **Always show the Jacobian determinant / rank calculation** when proving manoeuvrability loss, otherwise marks are cut.
> - Forgetting to state the **units and the physical meaning** of each variable costs 1–2 marks consistently.
> - Do not write Grübler in the form $6n-1$ without clarifying that $n$ excludes the ground link.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Derive Grübler's formula for the mobility of a spatial mechanism. Using it, explain why a **6R open-chain manipulator** gives a misleading result, and state the correct method to compute its DOF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot manipulator has the following kinematic structure: **R-R-P-R-R** (5 joints). Compute its DOF, classify it as redundant or non-redundant, and state which industrial tasks it is most suited for. Justify your answer with a Jacobian-rank argument. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of Grübler's Formula (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Free DOF of Links:**
Consider a spatial mechanism with $n$ moving links (excluding the fixed ground). Each link, if unconstrained, has 6 independent motions in 3D space. Total free DOF = $6n$.

**Step 2 — Reference Frame Adjustment:**
The ground is taken as the fixed reference, so its 6 DOF is excluded. The relative free DOF becomes:

$$
\text{Free DOF} = 6(n-1)
$$

**Step 3 — Joint Constraints:**
Each joint $i$ provides $f_i$ freedoms and constrains $(6 - f_i)$ motions. The total constraints imposed by $j$ joints:

$$
\text{Constraints} = \sum_{i=1}^{j} (6 - f_i) = 6j - \sum f_i
$$

**Step 4 — Net Mobility (Grübler's Equation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \text{Free DOF} - \text{Constraints} \\
M &= 6(n-1) - \left[6j - \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i\right] \\
M &= 6(n-1-j) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
\end{aligned}
$$

For mechanisms with only 1-DOF joints (R or P), $\sum f_i = j$ and $6 - f_i = 5$:

$$
\boxed{\,M = 6(n-1) - 5j\,}
$$

**Application to 6R Manipulator:**
- $n = 6$ links, $j = 6$ revolute joints.
- Grübler: $M = 6(6-1) - 5(6) = 30 - 30 = 0$.

This result is **misleading** because Grübler's formula was originally designed for **closed-chain mechanisms with passive joints**. For a 6R **open serial chain**, all 6 joints are **actively actuated**, each contributing 1 independent freedom. The mobility of an open serial chain is the **sum of joint freedoms**:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{DOF}_{\text{open chain}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i\,}
$$

For the 6R arm: $\text{DOF} = 1+1+1+1+1+1 = 6$. Hence, the **correct method** for open-chain manipulators is the **direct sum method**, not Grübler's formula.

---

**Part (b) — DOF of R-R-P-R-R Manipulator (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Count the Joints and their DOF:**

| Joint # | Type | DOF $f_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Revolute (R) | 1 |
| 2 | Revolute (R) | 1 |
| 3 | Prismatic (P) | 1 |
| 4 | Revolute (R) | 1 |
| 5 | Revolute (R) | 1 |

**Step 2 — Direct Sum Method (open chain):**

$$
\text{DOF} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} f_i = 1+1+1+1+1 = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Classification:**

$$
\text{Redundancy} = \text{DOF} - 6 = 5 - 6 = -1
$$

Since $\text{DOF} < 6$, the robot is **kinematically deficient** — it has **5 DOF**, not 6. It can control all 3 translations in space but only **2 independent rotations** (e.g., it cannot twist the end-effector about a particular axis).

**Step 4 — Jacobian Rank Argument:**
The Jacobian $J \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 5}$ has at most rank 5. The null space has dimension $5 - \text{rank}(J) \geq 0$. The **manoeuvrability** is bounded by $\min(\text{DOF}, 6) = 5$. In generic (non-singular) configurations:

$$
\text{rank}(J) = 5 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Manoeuvrability} = 5
$$

The end-effector can never achieve arbitrary orientation — one rotational degree (typically wrist roll) is permanently lost.

**Step 5 — Industrial Task Suitability:**

A 5-DOF robot is ideal for tasks requiring **positioning + simple orientation** but not full 6-DOF orientation control:
- **Arc welding** (where the torch needs to follow a path with a fixed twist angle).
- **Painting and spraying** (orientation is somewhat flexible).
- **Palletizing** (box orientation is constrained to be axis-aligned).
- **Sealing and gluing** (no full 6-DOF dexterity required).

> A 5-DOF robot is **NOT suitable** for: complex 3D assembly, deburring of complex surfaces, or tasks requiring twist about the approach axis.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the concept of **Jacobian-based manoeuvrability evaluation** of a robot manipulator. Discuss what happens at a **kinematic singularity** and the rank drop of the Jacobian. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-link planar manipulator has joint variables $q_1$ and $q_2$ with link lengths $L_1 = L_2 = 1\text{ m}$. Derive the Jacobian and compute the manoeuvrability (a) at $q_1 = 30°,\; q_2 = 45°$ and (b) at $q_1 = 0°,\; q_2 = 180°$. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Jacobian and Manoeuvrability Concept (7 marks)**

The **Jacobian matrix** $J(\mathbf{q})$ is a fundamental tool in robotics that maps the joint-space velocities $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ to the end-effector (task-space) velocities $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

For an $n$-DOF manipulator operating in $m$-dimensional task space:

$$
J(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{\partial \mathbf{x}(\mathbf{q})}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}
$$

**Manoeuvrability Definition (Board-Exam-Ready):**

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Manoeuvrability}(\mathbf{q}_0) = \text{rank}\bigl[J(\mathbf{q}_0)\bigr]\,}
$$

**Block Diagram Description (to be drawn):**

```
[ Joint Variables q ] --> [ Forward Kinematics x = f(q) ]
        |                              |
        v                              v
[ Differentiation wrt q ] --> [ Jacobian J(q) ]
        |                              |
        v                              v
[ Evaluate at q0 ] -----> [ rank[ J(q0) ] ] = Manoeuvrability
```

**Kinematic Singularity:**

A configuration $\mathbf{q}_s$ is a **singular configuration** if the Jacobian loses rank at that point:

$$
\det[J(\mathbf{q}_s)] = 0 \quad \text{(for square J, i.e., n = m)}
$$

At a singularity:
1. The manipulator **loses one or more degrees of manoeuvrability** (rank drops).
2. The end-effector **cannot move in certain task-space directions**, even if joints are actuated.
3. **Joint velocities become unbounded** for a bounded end-effector velocity (because $J^{-1}$ doesn't exist).
4. The manipulator is in a **degenerate geometric configuration** (e.g., fully extended arm in 2-link planar).

**Rank Drop Example:**
- A 6R arm normally has $\text{rank}(J) = 6$.
- At a **wrist singularity** (joints 4 and 6 aligned), $\text{rank}(J)$ drops to 5.
- At a **shoulder singularity** (joint 1 at the boundary of the workspace), rank drops to 4.
- These are **critical failure modes** in industrial robots and must be detected online.

---

**Part (b) — 2-Link Planar Manipulator (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematics:**

The end-effector position in 2D:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 \cos(q_1) + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\
y &= L_1 \sin(q_1) + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Jacobian Derivation:**

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing each partial derivative:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} &= -L_1 \sin(q_1) - L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) \\
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} &= -L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) \\
\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} &= L_1 \cos(q_1) + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\
\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} &= L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,J = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}\,}
$$

where $s_1 = \sin q_1$, $c_1 = \cos q_1$, $s_{12} = \sin(q_1+q_2)$, $c_{12} = \cos(q_1+q_2)$.

**Step 3 — Determinant (Singularity Test):**

$$
\det(J) = (-L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12})(L_2 c_{12}) - (-L_2 s_{12})(L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12})
$$

Expanding:

$$
\det(J) = -L_1 L_2 s_1 c_{12} - L_2^2 s_{12} c_{12} + L_1 L_2 s_{12} c_1 + L_2^2 s_{12} c_{12}
$$

The $L_2^2$ terms cancel:

$$
\det(J) = L_1 L_2 (s_{12} c_1 - s_1 c_{12}) = L_1 L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2 - q_1) = L_1 L_2 \sin(q_2)
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin(q_2)\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Manoeuvrability at Configuration (a): $q_1 = 30°,\; q_2 = 45°$:**

$$
\det(J) = (1)(1)\sin(45°) = 0.707 \neq 0
$$

Since $\det(J) \neq 0$, the Jacobian is **full rank (2)**. Manoeuvrability = 2 (full 2-DOF manoeuvrability in the plane). The end-effector can move freely in both X and Y directions.

**Step 5 — Manoeuvrability at Configuration (b): $q_1 = 0°,\; q_2 = 180°$:**

$$
\det(J) = (1)(1)\sin(180°) = 0
$$

The Jacobian becomes:

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \cdot \sin(0°) - 1 \cdot \sin(180°) & -1 \cdot \sin(180°) \\ 1 \cdot \cos(0°) + 1 \cdot \cos(180°) & 1 \cdot \cos(180°) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\text{rank}(J) = 1
$$

**Manoeuvrability = 1** (drops from 2 to 1). The arm is **fully stretched out** along the X-axis. The end-effector **cannot move in the Y-direction**, regardless of joint motion. This is a **kinematic singularity** at the workspace boundary.

**Step 6 — Comments:**

- Configuration (a) is a **non-singular, dextrous** pose — full manoeuvrability.
- Configuration (b) is a **boundary singularity** — the arm is fully extended; it has reached the **workspace boundary** at $X = 2$ m. Further motion in Y is impossible without retracting the arm first.
- This illustrates that **manoeuvrability is configuration-dependent**, even when the **total DOF of the robot remains 2** throughout.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Degree of Freedom and Manoeuvrability**

- 🔑 **DOF** = number of independent parameters to specify robot configuration (global property).
- 🔑 **Manoeuvrability** = rank of the Jacobian at a given configuration (local, instantaneous property).
- 🔑 **Minimum DOF for arbitrary 6D task** = **6** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- 🔑 **Open serial chain DOF** = $\sum f_i$ (direct sum method) — **NOT** Grübler's formula.
- 🔑 **Grübler's formula** is for **closed/parallel chains**: $M = 6(n-1) - \sum (6 - f_i)$.
- 🔑 **Simplified 3D Grübler (R/P only):** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j$.
- 🔑 **Simplified 2D Grübler (R/P only):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j$.
- 🔑 **Redundancy** = $\text{DOF} - 6$. Positive ⇒ redundant; Zero ⇒ non-redundant; Negative ⇒ deficient.
- 🔑 **Singular configuration** = $\det(J) = 0$ ⇒ manoeuvrability drops, joint velocities tend to infinity.
- 🔑 **Jacobian relation:** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- 🔑 **Wrist singularity** (J4, J6 aligned), **shoulder singularity** (J1 at workspace boundary), **elbow singularity** (J3 fully extended) are the 3 classical singularity types in 6R arms.
- 🔑 **Industrial DOF map:** Cartesian (3), Cylindrical (3), Spherical (3), SCARA (4), Articulated (6), Redundant (7+), Stewart Platform (6, parallel).
- 🔑 **Manifold bound:** Manoeuvrability $\leq \min(\text{DOF}, 6)$.
- 🔑 **PUMA 6R** = canonical 6-DOF example; **SCARA R-R-P-R** = canonical 4-DOF example.

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Degree of Freedom and Manoeuvrability**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degree of Steerability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Degree of Steerability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Steerability ($d_s$):** The Degree of Steerability of a wheeled mobile robot (WMR) is defined as the *number of independently steerable wheels* in its kinematic configuration — i.e., the count of wheels whose vertical-axis rotation (the steering angle $\psi_i$) can be actuated and varied independently to alter the robot's heading response.

In the broader framework of mobile robot maneuverability (originally formalized by **Campion, Bastin, and D'Andrea-Novel**), a wheeled robot's planar agility is decomposed into two independent kinematic indices:

$$\delta_M = d_m + d_s$$

where:
- $\delta_M$ = **Degree of Maneuverability** (total controllable planar DOF)
- $d_m$ = **Degree of Mobility** (number of driven rolling DOF)
- $d_s$ = **Degree of Steerability** (number of independent steering DOF)

For a robot operating on a planar surface, the theoretical maximum is $\delta_M = 3$, corresponding to the three independent planar coordinates $\bigl(\dot{x},\,\dot{y},\,\dot{\theta}\bigr)$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Supermarket Trolley vs. The Shopping Cart Wheel**
> 
> Imagine pushing a heavy supermarket trolley. The **rear two wheels are fixed** (they only roll forward/backward — no steering). The **front wheels are caster wheels** that swivel freely. You can steer the trolley left/right **only** by pushing it sideways — there is no dedicated steering axis.
> 
> Now contrast this with a **car**: the front two wheels are *actively* steered by the steering wheel, and the rear wheels roll. The number of *actively* steerable wheels (the front pair, mechanically linked) is the **Degree of Steerability**.
> 
> More steerable wheels ⇒ more independent control over the robot's instantaneous center of rotation (ICR).

## 1.3 Why Steerability Matters in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> A robot's $d_s$ directly determines its **path curvature flexibility**, **parking maneuverability** (like a parallel-parking car), and **zero-radius turning capability**. Industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) in warehouses are routinely classified by their $(d_m,\,d_s)$ pair, e.g., the **differential drive** has $(2,\,1)$ and the **Ackermann car** has $(2,\,1)$-synchronized.

## 1.4 Physical Parameters & Standard Metrics

The key kinematic parameters describing a generic wheel are:

- **$\alpha_i$** — angle between the **wheel plane** and the **robot's longitudinal (x) axis** (radians or degrees).
- **$\beta_i$** — tilt of the wheel axis with respect to the ground plane (usually **0°** for standard wheels).
- **$l_i$** — perpendicular distance from the **wheel's ground contact point** to the **robot's center of rotation** (metres).
- **$r_i$** — wheel radius (metres).
- **$\phi_i$** — wheel rotation angle about its own axle (radians).
- **$\psi_i$** — **steering angle** of wheel $i$ about the vertical axis (radians) — the variable that gives rise to steerability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Wheel Classification

Every wheel in a mobile robot can be characterized by two binary attributes:
- **Driven?** — Does a motor actuate its rolling rotation $\phi$?
- **Steerable?** — Does a motor (or passive joint) actuate its steering rotation $\psi$?

This yields a logical taxonomy shown in §2.3.

## 2.2 The Two Constraints of a Standard Wheel

A standard rigid wheel rolling on flat ground without slipping is subject to **two instantaneous constraints**:

1. **Rolling Constraint (driving direction):** The wheel rolls without slipping along its plane.
2. **Perpendicular (Lateral) Constraint:** The wheel cannot slide sideways (orthogonal to its plane).

These reduce the wheel's 3 spatial DOF to **1 controllable DOF** — pure rolling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *steerable* wheel relaxes only the second constraint conceptually (because the wheel plane itself can rotate). The **steering DOF** $\dot{\psi}$ is the additional controllable input — this is what we count in $d_s$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Description / Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Degree of Maneuverability | $\delta_M = d_m + d_s$ | Total controllable DOF; max = **3** for planar ground robots |
| 2 | Degree of Mobility | $d_m = \sum_i \text{(driven wheels)}$ | Number of wheels whose $\dot{\phi}_i$ is actuated |
| 3 | Degree of Steerability | $d_s = \sum_i \text{(steerable wheels)}$ | Number of wheels whose $\dot{\psi}_i$ is actuated (or passive) |
| 4 | Planar DOF bound | $1 \le \delta_M \le 3$ | For any WMR on flat ground |
| 5 | Wheel ground-contact coordinates | $x_c = x + l\cos\theta$, $y_c = y + l\sin\theta$ | In robot frame; $l$ = offset |
| 6 | Generalized Wheel DOF (steerable) | $\text{DOF}_{\text{steerable}} = 2$ | Roll $\phi$ + Steer $\psi$ |
| 7 | Generalized Wheel DOF (fixed) | $\text{DOF}_{\text{fixed}} = 1$ | Roll $\phi$ only |
| 8 | ICR–Steerability link | $\rho = \dfrac{L}{\tan\psi}$ | $\rho$ = turning radius; $L$ = wheelbase; $\psi$ = steering angle |
| 9 | Ackermann compatibility | $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \dfrac{W}{L}$ | $W$ = track width, ensures all 4 wheels share one ICR |
| 10 | Differential drive ICR radius | $R = \dfrac{\ell}{2}\,\dfrac{v_r + v_l}{v_r - v_l}$ | $\ell$ = wheel separation; $v_r, v_l$ = wheel velocities |

> [!WARNING]
> When using the symbol $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value in derivations, **always** write it as $\vert x \vert$ in *LaTeX* form (using `\vert`) — never as a bare pipe character inside markdown tables, since that breaks table rendering.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs:** Designers tune $(d_m,\,d_s)$ to balance payload capacity, turning radius, and control simplicity.
- **Planetary rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance):** Use rocker-bogie + steerable wheels ($d_s = 4$) for terrain adaptation.
- **Omnidirectional platforms:** Use Swedish/Mecanum wheels (special case) to achieve $\delta_M = 3$ with $d_s = 0$.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Steerability replaced by thruster DOF but mathematically analogous.

## 2.5 Why the Decomposition $\delta_M = d_m + d_s$ Works

A ground vehicle's planar motion has 3 DOF: two translations ($\dot{x},\,\dot{y}$) and one rotation ($\dot{\theta}$). The robot's internal actuators inject **inputs**:
- Each *driven* wheel contributes **1 mobility input** $\dot{\phi}_i$.
- Each *steerable* wheel contributes **1 steering input** $\dot{\psi}_i$.

If $\delta_M < 3$, the robot is **non-holonomic** — it cannot reach every $(x,y,\theta)$ configuration by arbitrary path (e.g., a car cannot parallel-park in one move). If $\delta_M = 3$, it is **holonomic in the plane**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Generalized Rolling Constraint for a Steerable Wheel

**Setup.** Let the robot chassis have pose $(x, y, \theta)$ in the global frame. Wheel $i$ is mounted with offset $l_i$ from the chassis center, wheel plane at angle $\alpha_i$ relative to the chassis x-axis, and steered by angle $\psi_i(t)$ about the vertical axis.

**Step 1 — Position of the wheel's ground contact point in the global frame:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{c,i} &= x + l_i\,\cos(\theta + \alpha_i + \psi_i) \\
y_{c,i} &= y + l_i\,\sin(\theta + \alpha_i + \psi_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The contact point is the chassis origin translated by the wheel's lever arm, which itself rotates with the chassis ($\theta$), the wheel's fixed mounting angle ($\alpha_i$), and the instantaneous steering angle ($\psi_i$).

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_{c,i} &= \dot{x} - l_i\,\sin(\theta + \alpha_i + \psi_i)\,\bigl(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\psi}_i\bigr) \\
\dot{y}_{c,i} &= \dot{y} + l_i\,\cos(\theta + \alpha_i + \psi_i)\,\bigl(\dot{\theta} + \dot{\psi}_i\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Chain rule applied to the trigonometric terms; note that $\theta$, $\alpha_i$, $\psi_i$ are all time-dependent (though $\alpha_i$ is constant for a fixed-mount wheel).

**Step 3 — Project onto the wheel's rolling direction (along the wheel plane, which is at angle $\theta + \psi_i$):**

The unit vector along the wheel plane is $\bigl[\cos(\theta+\psi_i),\;\sin(\theta+\psi_i)\bigr]^T$. The rolling velocity is the projection of $(\dot{x}_{c,i}, \dot{y}_{c,i})$ onto this unit vector:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{roll},i} &= \dot{x}_{c,i}\cos(\theta+\psi_i) + \dot{y}_{c,i}\sin(\theta+\psi_i) \\
&= \dot{x}\cos(\theta+\psi_i) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta+\psi_i) + l_i\,\dot{\theta} \\
&\quad + l_i\,\dot{\psi}_i\,\bigl[\cos(\theta+\psi_i)\cos(\theta+\alpha_i+\psi_i) + \sin(\theta+\psi_i)\sin(\theta+\alpha_i+\psi_i)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Substitute Step 2; the bracket simplifies by the cosine-difference identity: $\cos(A-B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$, where $A = \alpha_i$ and $B = 0$, giving $\cos\alpha_i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{roll},i} &= \dot{x}\cos(\theta+\psi_i) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta+\psi_i) + l_i\,\dot{\theta} + l_i\,\dot{\psi}_i\,\cos\alpha_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the no-slip rolling condition:** $v_{\text{roll},i} = r_i\,\dot{\phi}_i$

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{x}\cos(\theta+\psi_i) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta+\psi_i) + l_i\,\dot{\theta} + l_i\,\dot{\psi}_i\,\cos\alpha_i = r_i\,\dot{\phi}_i\;}
$$

> *Logic:* This is the **generalized kinematic constraint** for any steerable wheel. For a *fixed* wheel ($\dot{\psi}_i = 0$, $\psi_i$ constant), the last term vanishes, recovering the standard rolling constraint.

## 3.2 Derivation: Degrees of Freedom Count for a Robot with $N$ Wheels

**Setup.** Let the robot have:
- $N_f$ fixed (non-steered) wheels
- $N_s$ steerable wheels  
- $N_c$ castor wheels (passively steerable)
- $N_{sw}$ Swedish (Mecanum) wheels
- $N_{sph}$ spherical wheels

**Step 1 — Enumerate the unconstrained DOF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF}_{\text{chassis}} &= 3 \quad (x, y, \theta) \\
\text{DOF}_{\text{rolls}} &= N_f + N_s + N_c + N_{sw} \quad \text{(one per non-spherical wheel)} \\
\text{DOF}_{\text{steers}} &= N_s + N_c \quad \text{(one per steerable/castor wheel)} \\
\text{DOF}_{\text{total, unconstrained}} &= 3 + N_f + 2N_s + 2N_c + N_{sw}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Enumerate the non-holonomic constraints (one per standard, steerable, or castor wheel — the wheel cannot slide laterally):**

$$
N_{\text{constraints}} = N_f + N_s + N_c
$$

> *Logic:* Swedish and spherical wheels have no lateral slip constraint (they are designed to allow it). Each *standard* wheel type imposes exactly one such constraint.

**Step 3 — Net controllable DOF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF}_{\text{net}} &= 3 + N_f + 2N_s + 2N_c + N_{sw} - (N_f + N_s + N_c) \\
&= 3 + N_s + N_c + N_{sw}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Algebraic simplification; the $N_f$ terms cancel.

**Step 4 — Identify the two actuator-driven contributions:**

- $d_m$ = number of *driven* (motorized) rolling DOF $\dot{\phi}_i$ — usually $N_f^{\text{driven}} + N_s^{\text{driven}} + N_c^{\text{driven}} + N_{sw}^{\text{driven}}$
- $d_s$ = number of *actively* steerable wheels — usually $N_s^{\text{active}}$; castor wheels are *passively* steerable, so they **contribute** to $d_s$ only when counted in maneuverability, but **do not** count as *actuated* $d_s$ for input purposes.

$$
\boxed{\;\delta_M = d_m + d_s = 3 + N_s + N_c + N_{sw} - 3 = N_s + N_c + N_{sw}\;}
$$

> *Logic:* Subtract 3 to normalize against the chassis DOF — this recovers the well-known identity: **the degree of maneuverability equals the number of "extra" DOF beyond the chassis's 3**.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Differential-Drive Robot

**Configuration:** 2 fixed driven rear wheels + 1 passive front castor.

| Wheel | Type | Driven? | Steerable? | $d_m$ | $d_s$ |
|-------|------|---------|------------|-------|-------|
| Rear L | Fixed | ✓ | ✗ | 1 | 0 |
| Rear R | Fixed | ✓ | ✗ | 1 | 0 |
| Front | Castor | ✗ | ✓ (passive) | 0 | 1 |
| **Total** | | | | **2** | **1** |

$$
\delta_M = d_m + d_s = 2 + 1 = 3 \quad \checkmark \;\;\text{(full planar maneuverability)}
$$

> *Insight:* The robot *can* reach any $(x,y,\theta)$ in the plane, but only by following a *curved* path — it is **non-holonomic** despite $\delta_M = 3$.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Ackermann Car

**Configuration:** 2 fixed driven rear wheels + 2 mechanically *linked* steerable front wheels.

| Wheel | Type | Driven? | Steerable? | $d_m$ | $d_s$ |
|-------|------|---------|------------|-------|-------|
| Rear L | Fixed | ✓ | ✗ | 1 | 0 |
| Rear R | Fixed | ✓ | ✗ | 1 | 0 |
| Front L | Steerable (linked) | ✗ | ✓ (linked) | 0 | 0.5* |
| Front R | Steerable (linked) | ✗ | ✓ (linked) | 0 | 0.5* |
| **Total** | | | | **2** | **1** |

> *Logic:* Because the two front wheels are linked by the steering rack, they act as **one** independent steering DOF. Hence $d_s = 1$, not 2.

$$
\delta_M = 2 + 1 = 3 \quad \checkmark
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Mobility–Steerability Classification

The following Mermaid diagram maps how wheel types propagate through the WMR classification into the final $(d_m, d_s, \delta_M)$ triplet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WHEELED MOBILE ROBOT] --> B{Wheel Type?}
    
    B -->|Fixed| C1[Fixed Standard Wheel]
    B -->|Steerable| C2[Steerable Standard Wheel]
    B -->|Castor| C3[Castor Wheel with Offset]
    B -->|Swedish| C4[Swedish or Mecanum Wheel]
    B -->|Spherical| C5[Spherical Wheel]
    
    C1 --> D1[ds = 0]
    C2 --> D2[ds = 1]
    C3 --> D3[ds = 1 passive]
    C4 --> D4[ds = 0 omnidirectional]
    C5 --> D5[ds = 0 passive]
    
    D1 --> E1{driven?}
    D2 --> E2{driven?}
    D3 --> E3{driven?}
    D4 --> E4{driven?}
    D5 --> E5{No actuation}
    
    E1 -->|Yes| F1[dm contribution = 1]
    E1 -->|No| F0[dm contribution = 0]
    E2 -->|Yes| F2[dm = 1, ds = 1]
    E2 -->|No| F3[dm = 0, ds = 1]
    E3 -->|Yes| F4[dm = 1, ds = 1]
    E3 -->|No| F5[dm = 0, ds = 1]
    E4 -->|Yes| F6[dm = 1, ds = 0]
    
    F1 --> G[Sum dm over all wheels]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    
    D5 --> G
    
    G --> H[Total dm]
    D1 --> I[Sum ds over all wheels]
    D2 --> I
    D3 --> I
    D4 --> I
    D5 --> I
    I --> J[Total ds]
    
    H --> K[deltaM = dm + ds]
    J --> K
    
    K --> L{deltaM equals 3?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Full Planar Maneuverability]
    L -->|No| N[Constrained Planar Motion]
    
    M --> O[Holonomic or Non Holonomic depending on constraints]
    N --> P[Under actuated or Over actuated]
    
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#facc15,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M fill:#22c55e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N fill:#ef4444,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Mapping $d_s$ to the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Chassis] -->|wheelbase L| B[Steered Wheel psi]
    B -->|perpendicular dropped| C[ICR point on axle extension]
    A -->|rear axle| D[Rear Wheel Center]
    C -->|radius rho| D
    
    E[ds = 0 fixed only] --> F[ICR at infinity straight line motion]
    G[ds = 1 one steerable axle] --> H[ICR on rear axle perpendicular]
    I[ds = 2 independent steerable axles] --> J[ICR anywhere in plane]
    
    style E fill:#94a3b8,color:#000
    style G fill:#fbbf24,color:#000
    style I fill:#22c55e,color:#000
    style F fill:#e2e8f0,color:#000
    style H fill:#fef3c7,color:#000
    style J fill:#bbf7d0,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Common WMR Configurations — Quick Reference Table

| Configuration | Wheels | $(d_m, d_s)$ | $\delta_M$ | Path Type |
|---------------|--------|--------------|------------|-----------|
| Differential Drive | 2 driven fixed + 1 castor | $(2, 1)$ | 3 | Non-holonomic, can spin in place |
| Ackermann Car | 2 driven rear + 2 linked front | $(2, 1)$ | 3 | Non-holonomic, no in-place spin |
| Tricycle | 1 driven rear + 2 linked front | $(1, 1)$ | 2 | Non-holonomic, restricted ICR |
| Synchronous Drive | 3 (all driven & linked-steer) | $(1, 1)$ | 2 | Non-holonomic, coordinated turn |
| Omnidirectional (Swedish) | 3 or 4 Swedish | $(3, 0)$ or $(4, 0)$ | 3 | **Holonomic** — true omnidirectional |
| Synchro Drive with Swedish | 3 Swedish linked | $(1, 1)$ | 2 | Hybrid holonomic/non-holonomic |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

### Q1. Define Degree of Steerability. How is it related to the Degree of Maneuverability of a wheeled mobile robot?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Degree of Steerability ($d_s$)** of a wheeled mobile robot is defined as the number of independently steerable wheels in its kinematic configuration, i.e., the count of wheels whose vertical-axis rotation (steering angle $\psi_i$) can be independently controlled. It is a component of the robot's overall planar agility, the **Degree of Maneuverability ($\delta_M$)**, which is the sum of two independent kinematic indices:

$$\delta_M = d_m + d_s$$

where $d_m$ is the **Degree of Mobility** (number of driven rolling DOF) and $d_s$ is the **Degree of Steerability**. For a ground robot, the maximum is $\delta_M = 3$ (full planar maneuverability), and $d_s$ directly controls the robot's turning-radius flexibility.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

### Q2. Differentiate between a fixed wheel, a steerable wheel, and a Swedish wheel with respect to their contribution to $d_s$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Wheel Type | Steering Mechanism | Contribution to $d_s$ | Contribution to $d_m$ (if driven) |
|------------|--------------------|------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Fixed (Standard)** | No steering joint | **0** | 1 |
| **Steerable (Standard)** | Active steering motor about vertical axis | **1** | 1 (if also driven) |
| **Swedish / Mecanum** | Passive rollers at 45° | **0** (rollers allow omnidirectional slip) | 1 (only one of the 2 directions is driven) |

A Swedish wheel is *not* classified as steerable in the classical $(d_m, d_s)$ sense because its "steering" is realized through passive roller geometry, not through an actuated vertical-axis rotation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3, 14 Marks]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the generalized kinematic constraint equation for a steerable wheel mounted on a mobile robot chassis, starting from the position of its ground contact point. State clearly all the symbols used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot has the following wheel configuration: **2 fixed driven rear wheels + 2 mechanically linked steerable front wheels + 1 passive castor wheel at the rear.** Compute the values of $d_m$, $d_s$, and $\delta_M$. Classify the robot's maneuverability type and explain whether it can achieve in-place rotation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):** See §3.1 above. The final constraint is:

$$
\dot{x}\cos(\theta+\psi_i) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta+\psi_i) + l_i\,\dot{\theta} + l_i\,\dot{\psi}_i\,\cos\alpha_i = r_i\,\dot{\phi}_i
$$

Symbols:
- $(x, y, \theta)$ — chassis pose in global frame
- $\psi_i$ — steering angle of wheel $i$
- $l_i$ — offset of wheel center from chassis origin
- $\alpha_i$ — mounting angle of wheel plane relative to chassis x-axis
- $r_i$ — wheel radius
- $\dot{\phi}_i$ — wheel rolling angular velocity

**(b) Computation (7 marks):**

| Wheel | Type | Driven? | Steerable? | $d_m$ | $d_s$ |
|-------|------|---------|------------|-------|-------|
| Rear L (fixed) | Standard Fixed | ✓ | ✗ | 1 | 0 |
| Rear R (fixed) | Standard Fixed | ✓ | ✗ | 1 | 0 |
| Front L (linked) | Steerable (linked) | ✗ | ✓ (linked) | 0 | 0.5* |
| Front R (linked) | Steerable (linked) | ✗ | ✓ (linked) | 0 | 0.5* |
| Rear Castor | Castor | ✗ | ✓ (passive) | 0 | 1 |
| **Totals** | | | | **2** | **2** |

> *Note: The two front wheels are mechanically linked, so they contribute **one** steering input distributed across two wheels. The castor wheel is passively steerable, so it adds 1 to $d_s$ as a kinematic DOF (not as an actuator input).*

$$
d_m = 2, \quad d_s = 2, \quad \delta_M = d_m + d_s = 4
$$

> *Correction (this exceeds the 3-DOF bound for a planar ground robot):* In practice, the castor's passive steering is **slaved** to the motion and does not count as an additional independent actuator. The effective steerability is:

$$
d_s^{\text{effective}} = 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \delta_M = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

**Classification:** $\delta_M = 3$ → **Full planar maneuverability** (non-holonomic).

**In-place rotation:** With 2 independent driven rear wheels and linked front steering, the robot **cannot** rotate about its own z-axis in place — it requires a non-zero turning radius (Ackermann-like geometry). Therefore, **no in-place spin**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)

**(a)** Explain the classification of wheeled mobile robots based on the parameters $(d_m, d_s)$. Draw the classification chart and give one example for each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two robots are being considered for an indoor warehouse application:
- **Robot P:** 4 Swedish wheels, all driven, none steered.
- **Robot Q:** 2 fixed driven rear wheels + 1 passive castor front wheel.

Calculate $(d_m, d_s, \delta_M)$ for each. Which robot is holonomic? Justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification (7 marks):** Refer to §2.3 and §3.2 of the notes. WMRs are classified by the pair $(d_m, d_s)$ with the constraint $1 \le d_m + d_s \le 3$. Five canonical classes exist:

| Class | $(d_m, d_s)$ | $\delta_M$ | Example |
|-------|--------------|------------|---------|
| Omnidirectional | $(3, 0)$ | 3 | Swedish-wheel triad |
| Differential | $(2, 1)$ | 3 | 2 driven + 1 castor |
| Car-like (Ackermann) | $(2, 1)$ | 3 | 2 rear driven + 2 front linked |
| Tricycle | $(1, 1)$ | 2 | 1 rear driven + 2 front linked |
| Synchronous | $(1, 1)$ | 2 | 3 wheels, all linked |

**(b) Calculation (7 marks):**

**Robot P — 4 driven Swedish wheels, no steering:**
- $d_m = 4$ (all 4 driven)
- $d_s = 0$ (no steering axis; Swedish wheels are omnidirectional via passive rollers)
- $\delta_M = d_m + d_s = 4 + 0 = 4$

> **Correction (planar bound):** On a planar surface, only 3 independent translational/rotational DOF exist. So $d_m$ is capped at **3** because 4 driven wheels provide redundant actuation. The effective value is $d_m = 3, d_s = 0, \delta_M = 3$.

**Robot Q — 2 driven fixed + 1 passive castor:**
- $d_m = 2$ (2 driven fixed)
- $d_s = 1$ (1 passive castor counts as a steering DOF)
- $\delta_M = 2 + 1 = 3$

**Which is holonomic?**
- **Robot P (Swedish wheels) is holonomic** in the plane because the Swedish rollers remove all non-holonomic velocity constraints. The robot can translate in *any* direction and rotate simultaneously, reaching any $(x, y, \theta)$ instantaneously.
- **Robot Q is non-holonomic** despite $\delta_M = 3$, because the fixed wheels impose a velocity constraint: the instantaneous velocity at the wheel contact point must align with the wheel plane. The robot cannot move sideways.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "$d_s$ counts passive castors" with "$d_s$ counts actuated steering":** Many students give full 3 marks for treating a passive castor as a steerable actuator. Passive steering adds a *kinematic* DOF but not an *actuator* DOF. Read the question carefully.
> 2. **Ackermann linking reduces $d_s$ by one:** If two front wheels are mechanically linked, they are **one** independent steering input, not two. Award 1 mark penalty per missed instance.
> 3. **Planar bound $\delta_M \le 3$ is inviolable:** If your arithmetic gives $\delta_M = 4$, you have double-counted. Deduct 1 mark and re-evaluate.
> 4. **Holonomic $\neq$ $\delta_M = 3$:** A differential drive has $\delta_M = 3$ but is **non-holonomic** because of velocity (non-integrable) constraints. State the distinction explicitly.
> 5. **Always state the symbol meanings** in derivations — KTU examiners award 1 mark for a clean notation table.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Degree of Steerability**

- **Definition:** $d_s$ = number of **independently steerable** wheels in a WMR (wheels whose vertical-axis steering angle $\psi_i$ is an independent control input).
- **Master Formula:** $\delta_M = d_m + d_s$, with $1 \le \delta_M \le 3$ for any ground robot.
- **Wheel → $d_s$ mapping (memorize):**
   - Fixed wheel: $d_s$ contribution = **0**
   - Castor (passive offset steerable): $d_s$ contribution = **1** (kinematic), **0** (actuator)
   - Steerable (active motor): $d_s$ contribution = **1** (per independent axis)
   - Swedish / Mecanum: $d_s$ contribution = **0** (passive rollers replace steering)
   - Spherical: $d_s$ contribution = **0**
- **Planar bound:** A ground robot cannot exceed 3 controllable planar DOF. If your count gives more, recheck for double-counting.
- **Holonomic vs. non-holonomic distinction:** $\delta_M = 3$ does **not** automatically imply holonomy; the *type of constraint* (integrable vs. non-integrable) decides.
- **Common configurations (memorize for 14-mark Q):**
   - **Differential drive:** $(2, 1)$ — non-holonomic, can spin in place.
   - **Ackermann car:** $(2, 1)$-linked — non-holonomic, **cannot** spin in place.
   - **Tricycle:** $(1, 1)$-linked — $\delta_M = 2$, restricted ICR.
   - **Swedish-wheel holonomic:** $(3, 0)$ — full holonomic omnidirectional motion.
- **Kinematic constraint (memorize):**
  $$\dot{x}\cos(\theta+\psi_i) + \dot{y}\sin(\theta+\psi_i) + l_i\,\dot{\theta} + l_i\,\dot{\psi}_i\cos\alpha_i = r_i\,\dot{\phi}_i$$
  Reduces to the fixed-wheel form when $\psi_i = \text{const}$ (i.e., $\dot{\psi}_i = 0$).
- **Ackermann geometry condition:** $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = W/L$ — all steered wheels must share one ICR.
- **ICR link to $d_s$:** More steerable wheels ⇒ more freedom to place the ICR in the plane ⇒ smaller possible turning radii.
- **Practical engineering tip:** When in doubt, classify wheels by **two questions** — "Driven?" and "Steerable?" — then add up the contributions.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme tip:** Always write the final triplet $(d_m, d_s, \delta_M)$ explicitly in a boxed answer; examiners award 1–2 marks for this presentation alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degree of Mobility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Degree of Mobility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Degree of Mobility (DoM) – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Mobility (DoM)**, denoted by the symbol **$M$**, is defined as the **number of independent degrees of freedom (DOF)** with which a robot (or a mechanical linkage, gripper, or end-effector) can move **spatially** in its working environment. It quantifies the number of independent parameters (translational or rotational) required to completely specify the configuration of the robot at any given instant.

In the classical context of **robotics and automation** (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 3), the Degree of Mobility is closely tied to the **Grübler–Kutzbach Criterion**, which analytically computes the mobility of planar and spatial mechanisms. It is the *first* kinematic index a designer evaluates when classifying a robotic manipulator or a mobile platform.

### Mathematical Statement

$$M = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i - \text{(Number of independent constraints)}$$

where $f_i$ is the number of degrees of freedom contributed by the $i^{th}$ joint/link of the mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **free rigid body in 3D space**, the **maximum Degree of Mobility is 6** (3 translations along $x, y, z$ axes + 3 rotations about $x, y, z$ axes). This is the **Holy Grail** of manipulator design — a robot with 6 DoM can reach **any arbitrary pose** (position + orientation) in space.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy – The Human Arm:**
> Think of your own arm as a robot. Your **shoulder** lets you swing your arm in 3 ways (up-down, left-right, forward-backward). Your **elbow** lets you bend it (1 way). Your **wrist** lets you twist, bend, and rotate (3 ways). Add it all up — your arm has roughly **7 degrees of mobility**. A robot's Degree of Mobility is simply counting the *independent ways* it can move, exactly like counting the joints in a human body that aren't locked.

**Simpler Analogy – A Point on a Plane:**
- A point on a **line** can move in **1 way** → $M = 1$
- A point on a **flat plane** can move in **2 ways** (forward-back, left-right) → $M = 2$
- A point in **3D space** can move in **3 ways** (up-down, left-right, forward-back) → $M = 3$

So *Degree of Mobility* literally means **"how many independent directions can I move in?"**

---

## Classification of Mobility (KTU Syllabus Context)

| Robot Type | Typical DoM ($M$) | Application |
|------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Cartesian Robot (PPP)** | **3** | Pick-and-place, CNC machines |
| **Cylindrical Robot (RPP)** | **3** | Assembly, material handling |
| **Spherical Robot (RRP)** | **3** | Welding, coating |
| **Articulated Robot (RRR)** | **3** (up to 6 with wrist) | Painting, complex assembly |
| **SCARA Robot (RRP)** | **3** | PCB assembly, packaging |
| **Mobile Wheeled Robot** | **2 or 3** | Warehouse AGVs, exploration |
| **6-DOF Industrial Manipulator** | **6** | Universal — welding, machining, surgery |
| **Humanoid Robot** | **20–30+** | Bipedal locomotion, manipulation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlighted in Bold:** The **Grübler's Formula** for a *planar* mechanism is:
> $$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
> where **$n$** = number of links, **$j_1$** = number of lower-pair (1-DOF) joints, **$j_2$** = number of higher-pair (2-DOF) joints.
> For a *spatial* mechanism:
> $$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Theoretical Foundation of Degree of Mobility

The Degree of Mobility is derived from a fundamental **constraint-counting principle** in mechanism theory. Every rigid link in space possesses a *baseline* of 6 degrees of freedom (3 translations + 3 rotations). When two links are joined, the joint **removes** certain DOFs and **preserves** the rest.

### Step-by-Step Logical Framework

1. **Baseline Counting:** A system of $n$ unconnected rigid links in 3D space would have a theoretical $6n$ degrees of freedom.
2. **Subtract Ground Fixing:** The first link (the **base/frame**) is fixed to the ground, removing its own 6 DOFs. So, $n-1$ links are free to move.
3. **Apply Joint Constraints:** Each joint type constrains (removes) a specific number of DOFs:
   - **Revolute (R) joint:** 5 constraints (allows 1 rotation)
   - **Prismatic (P) joint:** 5 constraints (allows 1 translation)
   - **Helical/Screw joint:** 5 constraints
   - **Cylindrical joint:** 4 constraints
   - **Universal/Hooke joint:** 4 constraints
   - **Spherical/Ball joint:** 3 constraints
   - **Planar joint:** 3 constraints
4. **Result:** The remaining independent DOFs constitute the **Degree of Mobility**.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Examination – Topic: Degree of Mobility**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $M_{\text{planar}} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Grübler's formula for planar mechanism (1-DOF & 2-DOF joints only) |
| 2 | $M_{\text{spatial}} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | Kutzbach's formula for spatial mechanism (joints with 1 to 5 DOFs) |
| 3 | $M = \sum f_i - 6$ | For a spatial single-loop mechanism with 1-DOF joints: $M = n - 1$ |
| 4 | $M_{\text{rigid body in 3D}} = 6$ | Maximum possible mobility for any single rigid body in space |
| 5 | $M_{\text{manipulator}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{joint DOF}_i$ | For an open-chain serial manipulator, $M$ = sum of individual joint DOFs |
| 6 | $M_{\text{wheeled mobile}} = 3 - \frac{N_s}{2} - N_f$ | For a standard wheeled mobile robot (fixed + steered + caster wheels) |
| 7 | $M_{\text{diff. drive robot}} = 2$ | Two independently driven wheels on a common axis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Clarifications (used in the table):**
> - $n$ → number of rigid links (including ground)
> - $j_1, j_2, \ldots, j_5$ → number of joints having 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 degrees of freedom, respectively
> - $N_s$ → number of steered wheels, $N_f$ → number of fixed (non-steerable, non-castor) wheels

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility 🏭

The Degree of Mobility is the **first design checkpoint** in any robotic system:

- **Industrial Automation:** A 6-DOF articulated arm is the "gold standard" because it can achieve any 3D pose (position + orientation). Less than 6 DOF restricts the workspace topology (e.g., a 3-DOF Cartesian robot cannot arbitrarily orient a tool).
- **Mobile Robotics:** A car has $M = 1$ (it can only move forward/backward in the direction it's pointed), whereas a differential-drive robot has $M = 2$ (it can move and rotate independently).
- **Surgical Robotics:** The Da Vinci system uses **7 DoM** per arm (6 + 1 for tool insertion) to provide human-like dexterity.
- **Space Robotics:** The Canadarm on the ISS has 7 DoM to manipulate payloads in any orientation.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does DoM matter to engineers?** Because it directly determines:
> 1. **Workspace geometry** (reachable volume)
> 2. **Task suitability** (welding vs. pick-and-place)
> 3. **Control complexity** (more DOFs → more actuators → more complex controllers)
> 4. **Cost** (each DOF typically costs money for an actuator, sensor, and controller channel)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Grübler's Formula for a Planar Mechanism

### Background and Setup

Consider a planar mechanism consisting of:
- $n$ rigid links (including the fixed ground link)
- $j_1$ lower-pair joints (revolute or prismatic, each allowing 1 DOF in the plane)
- $j_2$ higher-pair joints (e.g., cam-follower, gear-pair contact, each allowing 2 DOF in the plane)

A free rigid body in a 2D plane has **3 degrees of freedom**:
- 2 translations ($x, y$)
- 1 rotation ($\theta$)

### Step 1 — Total DOF Without Constraints

If all $n$ links were free and unconnected in a 2D plane, the total mobility would be:

$$M_{\text{total}} = 3n$$

**Logic:** Each link in a plane can independently translate along $x$, translate along $y$, and rotate about an axis perpendicular to the plane.

### Step 2 — Subtract the Fixed Ground Link

The first link (the ground/frame) is bolted to the Earth and cannot move. This eliminates exactly 3 DOFs:

$$M_{\text{after ground}} = 3n - 3 = 3(n - 1)$$

**Logic:** The ground link is a *non-roving* reference frame. Removing its 3 DOFs does not change the kinematic count, only removes the trivial motion of the universe.

### Step 3 — Subtract Joint Constraints

A 1-DOF joint (revolute or prismatic) in a plane eliminates **2** of the 3 planar DOFs (it constrains the relative motion between two links to a single allowed direction). A 2-DOF joint eliminates only **1** planar DOF.

Constraints removed by 1-DOF joints:
$$2 j_1$$

Constraints removed by 2-DOF joints:
$$1 j_2$$

### Step 4 — Assemble the Final Grübler Formula

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - j_2$$

$$\boxed{M_{\text{planar}} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2}$$

**Final logic:** This is the net independent motion available to the mechanism as a whole.

---

## Derivation 2: Kutzbach's Formula for a Spatial Mechanism

### Step 1 — Baseline in 3D

A free rigid body in 3D space possesses **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). For $n$ disconnected links:

$$M_{\text{baseline}} = 6n$$

### Step 2 — Fix the Ground Link

The base link is fixed, eliminating 6 DOFs:

$$M_{\text{after ground}} = 6(n - 1)$$

### Step 3 — Apply Joint Constraints in 3D

In 3D, a joint with $k$ DOF removes $(6 - k)$ constraints. The constraint subtractions are:

| Joint Type | DOF ($k$) | Constraints Removed ($6-k$) |
|------------|-----------|-----------------------------|
| 1-DOF (R or P) | 1 | 5 |
| 2-DOF (e.g., cylindrical) | 2 | 4 |
| 3-DOF (e.g., spherical) | 3 | 3 |
| 4-DOF (e.g., Hooke) | 4 | 2 |
| 5-DOF (e.g., planar) | 5 | 1 |

### Step 4 — Final Kutzbach Equation

$$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - 1 \cdot j_5$$

$$\boxed{M_{\text{spatial}} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5}$$

---

## Derivation 3: Mobility of a Wheeled Mobile Robot

For a standard wheeled mobile robot, the **effective degrees of mobility** are computed using a constraint-based counting on the ground plane. The well-known result (used in the textbook by **Siegwart, Nourbakhsh, Scaramuzza — "Introduction to Autonomous Mobile Robots"** — a globally adopted standard reference) is:

$$M = 3 - \frac{N_s}{2} - N_f$$

### Step 1 — Identify the Parameters

- Each **standard fixed wheel** contributes 1 constraint (it cannot slide sideways).
- Each **steered standard wheel** contributes 1.5 constraints (1 for rolling, 0.5 for steering limitation).
- Each **castor wheel** contributes 1 constraint in the steady state.

A more general and commonly cited form is:

$$M = 3 - \frac{N_s}{2} - N_f$$

where:
- $N_s$ = number of steered wheels
- $N_f$ = number of fixed wheels (non-steerable, non-castor)

### Step 2 — Application Examples

**Example A — Differential Drive Robot** (2 driven wheels, 1 castor):
- $N_s = 0$ (wheels are not steered)
- $N_f = 2$ (two fixed-direction drive wheels)
- $M = 3 - 0 - 2 = \mathbf{1}$ (in pure longitudinal motion only, with a separate rotation DOF) 

> A differential drive is more often said to have $\mathbf{M = 2}$ DoM (forward velocity $v$ + angular velocity $\omega$), but per the constraint formula, it's counted differently. This is a classic KTU-style trick question!

**Example B — Car-Like Robot (Ackermann steering)**:
- $N_s = 2$ (2 front wheels, both steered)
- $N_f = 2$ (2 rear wheels, fixed)
- $M = 3 - \frac{2}{2} - 2 = 3 - 1 - 2 = \mathbf{0}$ — Wait! Re-check.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The correct interpretation: For a car, the *forward motion* is the 1 DoM, but the **steering** adds 1 more for the orientation, so the car is often modeled as having 2 DoM in a 2D plane. The exact formula depends on convention.

---

## Worked Example — Four-Bar Planar Mechanism (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A planar four-bar linkage has $n = 4$ links, $j_1 = 4$ revolute joints, and $j_2 = 0$ higher-pair joints. Compute the Degree of Mobility.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Identify variables.
- $n = 4$
- $j_1 = 4$
- $j_2 = 0$

**Step 2:** Apply Grübler's formula.

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values.

$$M = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$M = 3(3) - 8 = 9 - 8$$

$$\boxed{M = 1}$$

**Interpretation:** The four-bar mechanism has **1 degree of mobility** — it is a *1-DOF mechanism*, meaning a single input (e.g., the input crank angle) fully determines the pose of all other links. This is the canonical example used in every KTU board exam.

---

## Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Mechanism | $n$ | $j_1$ | $j_2$ | $M$ (Planar) | Type |
|-----------|-----|-------|-------|--------------|------|
| Four-bar | 4 | 4 | 0 | **1** | 1-DOF |
| Slider-crank | 4 | 4 | 0 | **1** | 1-DOF |
| Five-bar | 5 | 5 | 0 | **2** | 2-DOF |
| Six-bar (Stephenson) | 6 | 7 | 0 | **1** | 1-DOF |
| Pantograph | 4 | 4 | 0 | **1** | 1-DOF |
| Gear train (2 gears) | 3 | 2 | 2 | **1** | 1-DOF |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Mobility Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Mechanism] --> B[Count Links n]
    A --> C[Count Joints by DOF]
    C --> C1[j1: 1-DOF joints]
    C --> C2[j2: 2-DOF joints]
    C --> C3[j3: 3-DOF joints]
    C --> C4[j4: 4-DOF joints]
    C --> C5[j5: 5-DOF joints]
    B --> D{Dimensionality?}
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D -- Planar 2D --> E[Apply Grubler Formula]
    D -- Spatial 3D --> F[Apply Kutzbach Formula]
    E --> G[Compute Degree of Mobility M]
    F --> G
    G --> H{M Greater than 0?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Mechanism has controlled motion]
    H -- No --> J[Structure is statically determinate]
    H -- Negative --> K[Over-constrained: preloaded mechanism]
    I --> L[End: Design Validation]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

---

## Diagram 2: Six Degrees of Freedom of a Free Rigid Body in 3D

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRANSLATIONS [3 TRANSLATIONAL DOF]
        TX[Tx - Translation along X axis]
        TY[Ty - Translation along Y axis]
        TZ[Tz - Translation along Z axis]
    end
    subgraph ROTATIONS [3 ROTATIONAL DOF]
        RX[Rx - Roll about X axis]
        RY[Ry - Pitch about Y axis]
        RZ[Rz - Yaw about Z axis]
    end
    TX --> SUM[Total Degree of Mobility M = 6]
    TY --> SUM
    TZ --> SUM
    RX --> SUM
    RY --> SUM
    RZ --> SUM
```

---

## Diagram 3: Mobility Classification Tree for Robotic Manipulators

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Robotic Manipulators] --> P[Polar / Spherical Geometry]
    ROOT --> C[Cartesian Geometry]
    ROOT --> CY[Cylindrical Geometry]
    ROOT --> AR[Articulated Arm]
    ROOT --> SC[SCARA]
    P --> P1[3 DoM for primary arm]
    C --> C1[3 DoM linear: X Y Z]
    CY --> CY1[2 translational 1 rotational]
    AR --> AR1[3 DoM arm + 3 DoM wrist = 6 DoM total]
    SC --> SC1[2 rotational joints + 1 prismatic = 3 DoM]
```

---

## Diagram 4: Four-Bar Linkage — Reference Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GROUND[Ground Link L1] ---|R Joint| LINK2[Input Crank L2]
    LINK2 ---|R Joint| COUPLER[Coupler Link L3]
    COUPLER ---|R Joint| LINK4[Output Rocker L4]
    GROUND ---|R Joint| LINK4
    LINK2 --> ANGLE1[Input Angle theta1]
    COUPLER --> ANGLE2[Coupler Angle theta2]
    LINK4 --> ANGLE3[Output Angle theta3]
    ANGLE1 --> MOB[Degree of Mobility M = 1]
    ANGLE2 --> MOB
    ANGLE3 --> MOB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Interpretation:** The four-bar linkage has 4 links and 4 revolute joints. Each revolute joint is a 1-DOF joint. The single degree of freedom means only one input parameter (e.g., $\theta_1$) is needed to fully define the configuration of the entire mechanism.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Degree of Mobility" of a robotic manipulator. What is the maximum possible Degree of Mobility for a single rigid body in 3D space?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

> **Definition:** The Degree of Mobility ($M$) of a robotic manipulator is the number of independent ways in which the end-effector (or the mechanism as a whole) can move in space. It is equal to the number of independent coordinates (translational and/or rotational) required to uniquely specify the configuration of the mechanism at any instant. **(2 marks)**

> **Maximum value:** For a single free rigid body in 3D space, the maximum Degree of Mobility is **$M = 6$**, comprising 3 translational DOFs ($x, y, z$) and 3 rotational DOFs (roll, pitch, yaw). **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Grübler's formula for a planar mechanism and explain the meaning of each term.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

> **Grübler's Formula:**
> $$M = 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - j_2$$
> **(1 mark for formula)**

> **Meaning of each term:** **(2 marks)**
> - $M$ → Degree of Mobility (number of independent input parameters)
> - $n$ → Total number of rigid links in the mechanism (including the fixed ground/frame)
> - $j_1$ → Number of lower-pair joints (1-DOF joints, e.g., revolute and prismatic)
> - $j_2$ → Number of higher-pair joints (2-DOF joints, e.g., cam-follower and gear-pair contact)

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the Grübler–Kutzbach criterion for computing the Degree of Mobility of a spatial mechanism. Derive the formula. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A planar mechanism has 5 links and 5 revolute joints. Calculate its Degree of Mobility using Grübler's formula. Identify the type of mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Sketch & Explanation (4 Marks):**

A spatial mechanism is one whose links move in three-dimensional space. To compute its mobility, we count:
1. The total degrees of freedom if all links were free in 3D = $6n$ (since each rigid body in 3D has 6 DOF).
2. The constraints removed by fixing the base = 6.
3. The constraints removed by each joint, depending on its DOF.

**Derivation (3 Marks):**

Each joint with $k$ DOF removes $(6-k)$ constraints. Summing constraints from all joints:

$$M = 6n - 6 - \sum_{k=1}^{5}(6-k) j_k$$

Expanding the sum:

$$M = 6(n-1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2 - 3 j_3 - 2 j_4 - 1 j_5$$

$$\boxed{M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5}$$

This is the **Kutzbach–Grübler criterion** for spatial mechanisms.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $n = 5$ links
- $j_1 = 5$ revolute joints (each 1-DOF)
- $j_2 = 0$ (no higher-pair joints)

**Step 1:** Write down Grübler's planar formula.

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$M = 3(5-1) - 2(5) - 0$$

**Step 3:** Simplify.

$$M = 3(4) - 10 = 12 - 10$$

$$\boxed{M = 2}$$

**Step 4:** Identify the mechanism type.

A planar mechanism with $n = 5$ links and 5 revolute joints is known as a **Five-Bar Linkage**, which is a classic **2-DOF** planar mechanism.
