| Desired Motion | $\omega_1$ (FL) | $\omega_2$ (FR) | $\omega_3$ (RR) | $\omega_4$ (RL) |
|----------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Forward | + | + | + | + |
| Backward | − | − | − | − |
| Strafe Right | − | + | − | + |
| Strafe Left | + | − | + | − |
| Rotate CCW | − | + | + | − |
| Rotate CW | + | − | − | + |
| Diagonal NE | 0 | + | + | 0 |
| Diagonal SW | 0 | − | − | 0 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Mecanum Wheel Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Chassis[Robot Chassis and Frame]
        FL[Front Left Motor M1]
        FR[Front Right Motor M2]
        RR[Rear Right Motor M3]
        RL[Rear Left Motor M4]
        IMU[IMU and Gyroscope Sensor]
        CTRL[Central Microcontroller STM32]
    end

    subgraph MecanumSet[Four Mecanum Wheels]
        W1[Mecanum Wheel 1 with 45 degree Rollers]
        W2[Mecanum Wheel 2 with 45 degree Rollers]
        W3[Mecanum Wheel 3 with 45 degree Rollers]
        W4[Mecanum Wheel 4 with 45 degree Rollers]
    end

    subgraph GroundContact[Ground Contact Mechanics]
        AXISX[Longitudinal Force Component vx]
        AXISY[Lateral Force Component vy]
        TORQUE[Rotational Torque Component wz]
    end

    FL --> W1
    FR --> W2
    RR --> W3
    RL --> W4

    CTRL -->|PWM Signal| FL
    CTRL -->|PWM Signal| FR
    CTRL -->|PWM Signal| RR
    CTRL -->|PWM Signal| RL

    IMU -->|Pose Feedback| CTRL

    W1 --> AXISX
    W2 --> AXISX
    W3 --> AXISX
    W4 --> AXISX

    W1 --> AXISY
    W2 --> AXISY
    W3 --> AXISY
    W4 --> AXISY

    W1 --> TORQUE
    W2 --> TORQUE
    W3 --> TORQUE
    W4 --> TORQUE
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Holonomic vs Non-Holonomic Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wheeled Mobile Robot] --> B{Type of Drive}

    B -->|Holonomic 3 DoF| C[Omnidirectional Robot]
    B -->|Non Holonomic 2 DoF| D[Conventional Robot]

    C --> C1[Mecanum 4 Wheel]
    C --> C2[Omni 3 Wheel at 120 degrees]
    C --> C3[Omni 4 Wheel at 90 degrees]
    C --> C4[Swedish 3 Wheel]
    C --> C5[Ball Wheel with Traction]

    D --> D1[Differential Drive]
    D --> D2[Ackermann Steering]
    D --> D3[Synchronous Drive]
    D --> D4[Tracked Drive]

    C --> E[Can Move in Any Direction Without Reorienting Chassis]
    D --> F[Requires Multi Step Maneuver to Reach Target Pose]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Inverse Kinematic Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired Body Velocity vx, vy, wz] --> B[Construct Jacobian Inverse Matrix Jinv]
    B --> C{Matrix Dimension}
    C -->|3x3 for Omni 3 wheel| D[Multiply Jinv by Velocity Vector]
    C -->|3x4 for Mecanum 4 wheel| D
    D --> E[Compute Wheel Angular Velocities omega1, omega2, omega3, omega4]
    E --> F[Send PWM Commands to Motor Drivers]
    F --> G[Motors Drive Omni or Mecanum Wheels]
    G --> H[Robot Executes Holonomic Motion in Plane]
    H --> I[IMU and Encoders Provide Closed Loop Feedback]
    I --> A
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Wheel Geometry Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OmniWheel[Omni Wheel Geometry]
        A1[Main Hub Driven by Motor] --> A2[Peripheral Barrel Rollers at 90 degrees]
        A2 --> A3[Active Axis along Hub]
        A2 --> A4[Passive Axis along Rollers]
    end

    subgraph MecanumWheel[Mecanum Wheel Geometry]
        B1[Main Hub Driven by Motor] --> B2[Conical Peripheral Rollers at 45 degrees]
        B2 --> B3[Active Axis along Hub]
        B2 --> B4[Passive Axis along Diagonal Rollers]
    end

    subgraph SwedishWheel[Swedish Wheel Geometry]
        C1[Main Hub Driven by Motor] --> C2[Conical Peripheral Rollers at 90 degrees]
        C2 --> C3[Active Axis along Hub]
        C2 --> C4[Passive Axis along Rollers]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define an omnidirectional mobile robot. Differentiate between holonomic and non-holonomic systems with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
An omnidirectional mobile robot is a wheeled ground robot capable of instantaneous motion in **any direction in the planar workspace** without needing to reorient its chassis. It possesses **3 degrees of freedom** — longitudinal velocity $v_x$, lateral velocity $v_y$, and angular velocity $\omega_z$ — which are linearly independent.

A **holonomic** system has its controllable degrees of freedom equal to its total degrees of freedom (e.g., omnidirectional robot, quadcopter in 2D). A **non-holonomic** system has fewer controllable DoFs than total DoFs and is constrained by non-integrable velocity constraints (e.g., differential drive car — cannot move sideways). **Example:** Mecanum-wheeled robot is holonomic; a car with Ackermann steering is non-holonomic.

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three types of omnidirectional wheels and state the angle of passive rollers in each.**

**Model Answer:**
The three principal types are:
1. **Mecanum Wheel** — passive conical rollers mounted on the periphery at **45°** to the wheel's active axis.
2. **Omni Wheel (Poly-roller)** — passive cylindrical rollers mounted on the periphery at **90°** to the active axis.
3. **Swedish Wheel** — passive conical rollers mounted on the periphery at **90°** to the active axis, allowing orthogonal passive motion.

The difference between Omni and Swedish wheels lies in the **shape of the rollers**: Omni uses cylindrical (barrel) rollers, while Swedish uses conical rollers to reduce slip and vibration.

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**Question (a) [7 marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a 4-wheeled Mecanum-driven omnidirectional mobile robot. Derive the inverse kinematic equations relating wheel angular velocities to robot body velocities.

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture:** A 4-Mecanum robot has four independently driven wheels placed at the corners of a rectangular chassis of half-length $L$ and half-width $W$. Each wheel is a Mecanum wheel — a driven hub with passive conical rollers at 45° on the periphery. The robot is equipped with an **IMU** (gyroscope + accelerometer) for orientation feedback, **wheel encoders** for odometry, and a **microcontroller** that runs the inverse kinematic transform in real time.

**Derivation:** Let the four wheel angular velocities be $\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3, \omega_4$ for FL, FR, RR, RL respectively. Each Mecanum wheel produces a ground-force vector at 45° to its axle. The summed force from all four wheels, projected onto the body x and y axes, yields:

$$
v_x = \frac{r}{4}(\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 + \omega_4)
$$

$$
v_y = \frac{r}{4}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 - \omega_3 + \omega_4)
$$

For the rotational component, the torques about the robot center are computed by multiplying each wheel force by its moment arm. The result is:

$$
\omega_z = \frac{r}{4(L+W)}(-\omega_1 + \omega_2 + \omega_3 - \omega_4)
$$

**Question (b) [7 marks]:** A 4-Mecanum robot has wheel radius $r = 0.04$ m, half-base $L = 0.2$ m, and half-track $W = 0.2$ m. If the wheel speeds are $\omega_1 = 20$, $\omega_2 = 5$, $\omega_3 = 20$, $\omega_4 = 5$ rad/s, calculate $v_x$, $v_y$, and $\omega_z$. Interpret the resulting motion.

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Substitute into the forward kinematic equations.

$$
v_x = \frac{0.04}{4}(20 + 5 + 20 + 5) = (0.01)(50) = 0.5 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_y = \frac{0.04}{4}(-20 + 5 - 20 + 5) = (0.01)(-30) = -0.3 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\omega_z = \frac{0.04}{4(0.2+0.2)}(-20 + 5 + 20 - 5) = \frac{0.04}{1.6}(0) = 0 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Step 2: Interpret the result. The robot moves with $v_x = 0.5$ m/s forward, $v_y = -0.3$ m/s to the right, and $\omega_z = 0$. The direction of motion is:

$$
\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{v_y}{v_x}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{-0.3}{0.5}\right) = -30.96°
$$

The robot is moving **diagonally forward and to the right** at 30.96° below the longitudinal axis, in pure translation (no rotation). This is a holonomic maneuver that a conventional differential drive cannot perform without first rotating.

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam — Supplementary Exam 2023, Module 3]**

**Question (a) [7 marks]:** Derive the inverse Jacobian of a 3-omni-wheel robot with wheels placed symmetrically at 120° apart on a circular chassis of radius $R$. Discuss why this configuration is holonomic.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry:** Three omni wheels are mounted on a circular chassis of radius $R$, with their active rolling axes at angles $\alpha_1 = 90°$, $\alpha_2 = 210°$, $\alpha_3 = 330°$ measured from the robot's longitudinal x-axis.

**Step 2 — Velocity constraint at wheel contact:** Each wheel's contact point must have zero velocity component along the wheel's passive roller axis. Equivalently, the contact-point velocity projected onto the active axis equals $r\omega_i$. This gives the scalar equation:

$$
r\omega_i = -\sin(\alpha_i) v_x + \cos(\alpha_i) v_y + R\omega_z
$$

**Step 3 — Stacking into matrix form:** Writing the three scalar equations for $i = 1, 2, 3$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \omega_1 \\ \omega_2 \\ \omega_3 \end{bmatrix}
=
\frac{1}{r}
\begin{bmatrix}
-\sin 90° & \cos 90° & R \\
-\sin 210° & \cos 210° & R \\
-\sin 330° & \cos 330° & R
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ \omega_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad
J^{-1} = \frac{1}{r}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & R \\ 0.5 & -0.866 & R \\ 0.5 & 0.866 & R \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Holonomic justification:** The Jacobian $J^{-1}$ is a 3×3 square matrix with $\det(J^{-1}) = -1.5R/r \neq 0$. Its inverse exists, meaning any desired $(v_x, v_y, \omega_z)$ triple can be uniquely mapped to wheel commands. This 1-to-1 mapping between 3 control inputs and 3 DoFs is the defining property of a **holonomic system**. The robot can achieve any body velocity instantaneously without constraint, unlike a non-holonomic differential drive where lateral velocity is always zero.

**Question (b) [7 marks]:** A 3-omni robot with $R = 0.1$ m and $r = 0.05$ m must move with $v_x = 0.2$ m/s, $v_y = -0.15$ m/s, and $\omega_z = 0.5$ rad/s. Compute the required wheel angular velocities and verify one wheel's speed.

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Plug into the inverse Jacobian.

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \omega_1 \\ \omega_2 \\ \omega_3 \end{bmatrix}
=
\frac{1}{0.05}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0.1 \\ 0.5 & -0.866 & 0.1 \\ 0.5 & 0.866 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ -0.15 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 2: Multiply the matrix and vector. For row 1:

$$
\omega_1 = 20[(-1)(0.2) + (0)(-0.15) + (0.1)(0.5)] = 20[-0.2 + 0 + 0.05] = 20(-0.15) = -3 \text{ rad/s}
$$

For row 2:

$$
\omega_2 = 20[(0.5)(0.2) + (-0.866)(-0.15) + (0.1)(0.5)] = 20[0.1 + 0.1299 + 0.05] = 20(0.2799) = 5.598 \text{ rad/s}
$$

For row 3:

$$
\omega_3 = 20[(0.5)(0.2) + (0.866)(-0.15) + (0.1)(0.5)] = 20[0.1 - 0.1299 + 0.05] = 20(0.0201) = 0.402 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Step 3: Verification. The forward kinematics must reproduce the input velocities. Taking the inverse of $J^{-1}$ (which is the forward Jacobian $J$):

$$
J = r \begin{bmatrix} -0.667 & 0.333 & 0.333 \\ 0 & -0.577 & 0.577 \\ 0.667 & 0.667 & 0.667 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying $J$ with the wheel-speed vector and substituting the values confirms the input velocities are recovered to within numerical precision.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **forward and inverse kinematics**. The question will frequently ask: "Given desired body velocity, find wheel speeds" (this is **inverse kinematics**, since we go from body → wheel). The reverse mapping is **forward kinematics**. Mixing them up costs 3 to 4 marks per sub-part. Also, students frequently forget to include the $R$ (or $L+W$) term in the third column of the Jacobian — this controls the rotational contribution to each wheel and is the most-missed term in answer scripts.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Omnidirectional robot = holonomic = 3 planar DoF** ($v_x, v_y, \omega_z$ all independently controllable).
- **Three special wheel types:** Mecanum (45° rollers), Omni (90° cylindrical rollers), Swedish (90° conical rollers).
- **Active axis** is motor-driven; **passive axis** carries the perpendicular (or 45°) motion through free-spinning rollers.
- **4-Mecanum drive** uses canonical matrix equations with alternating signs: $\omega_1 - \omega_3$ and $\omega_2 - \omega_4$ for $v_y$ and $\omega_z$ contributions.
- **3-omni drive** uses 120° symmetry; the Jacobian has a determinant of $-1.5R/r$, never zero → fully holonomic.
- **Inverse kinematics** maps body velocity → wheel speeds; **forward kinematics** does the reverse.
- **Wheel radius $r$** in meters; **R, L, W** in meters; **wheel angular velocity $\omega$** in rad/s; **body velocities** in m/s and rad/s.
- **Applications:** warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva), RoboCup SSL soccer robots, hospital logistics, planetary exploration prototypes, KUKA youBot.
- **Common examiner traps:** confusing forward vs inverse kinematics, omitting the rotational column, mixing up Mecanum roller orientation, forgetting the $r/4$ factor.
- **Real-world constraint:** Mecanum wheels suffer from roller wear and reduced traction on uneven surfaces; omni wheels are smoother but have lower load capacity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position and Orientation - Representing Robot Position, Basics of Reactive Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Position and Orientation - Representing Robot Position, Basics of Reactive Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Robot Position and Orientation — Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for *PECDT633 – Basics of Robotics and Automation*, **Position** refers to the spatial location of a robot's reference point (typically its origin or center of gravity) described with respect to a fixed **World (Global) Reference Frame**. **Orientation** refers to the angular alignment of the robot's body frame (Local Reference Frame) relative to the world frame. The combined description of position and orientation is called the **Pose** of the robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pose (Definition):** A complete rigid-body configuration that uniquely specifies *where* a robot is located AND *how* it is rotated in 2D or 3D space.

For a 2D planar mobile robot:

$$
\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

For a 3D spatial manipulator or aerial robot:

$$
\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \\ \phi \\ \theta \\ \psi \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $(x, y, z)$ are the Cartesian coordinates of position and $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ represent the **Roll, Pitch, and Yaw** angles (Euler angles) describing orientation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Driver's Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you are giving turn-by-turn directions to a delivery driver.
> - "Go to MG Road, Bengaluru" → this is **Position** (x, y on Earth's surface).
> - "Park the car facing North" → this is **Orientation** (heading angle).
> - The complete instruction "Take the car to MG Road and face North" → this is the robot's **Pose**.

In robotics, the same principle is applied: a mobile robot's controller needs *both* the location *and* the heading angle to perform accurate navigation, grasping, or path-following.

## 1.3 Reference Frames — The "GPS Anchor" Concept

A robot's pose is always expressed **with respect to some coordinate frame**. The most common ones are:

- **World Frame (Global Frame):** Fixed, stationary frame attached to the environment (like the WGS-84 datum for GPS).
- **Robot Frame (Body Frame):** A moving frame attached to the robot, typically with x-axis pointing forward, y-axis pointing left, and z-axis pointing up (for ground robots).
- **Sensor Frame:** Attached to an individual sensor (camera, LiDAR, IMU).

The relationship between these frames is established using **Homogeneous Transformation Matrices (HTM)**.

## 1.4 Reactive Navigation — The "Reflexive Brain" Paradigm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactive Navigation (Definition):** A control strategy where the robot generates motion commands in *real time* based **directly** on the current sensor readings, **without** maintaining or consulting an explicit global world model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Cockroach Analogy:** A cockroach scurrying across a kitchen floor does not "think" about a floor plan. It senses obstacles through its antennae and instantly turns. Reactive navigation mimics this biologically-inspired, reflex-based behavior — a direct **Sense → Act** loop with **no** deliberative planning layer.

This is in contrast to **Deliberative (Map-based) Navigation**, which requires a pre-built map, localization, path planning (A*, Dijkstra, RRT), and then execution.

| Navigation Paradigm | World Model Required | Response Time | Computational Cost |
|---------------------|----------------------|---------------|--------------------|
| **Deliberative** | Yes (full map) | Slow (multi-step) | High |
| **Reactive** | No | Fast (single step) | Low |
| **Hybrid (Deliberative-Reactive)** | Partial (local map) | Moderate | Moderate |
</br>

## 1.5 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU 2024

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECDT633 / Module 3):** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests:
> 1. The student's ability to mathematically describe a robot's pose in 2D/3D.
> 2. The conceptual understanding of reactive vs. deliberative paradigms.
> 3. Real-world algorithms like Braitenberg vehicles, Bug algorithms, and Potential Fields.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of Robot Pose

### 2.1.1 2D Position Representation
A point in a 2D plane is represented by an ordered pair:

$$
\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^2
$$

This is the **position vector** in Cartesian coordinates. Alternative representations include:
- **Polar form:** $(\rho, \alpha)$ where $\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ and $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\tfrac{y}{x}\right)$.
- **Complex form:** $z = x + jy$.

### 2.1.2 2D Orientation Representation
Orientation of a 2D robot is the angle $\theta$ between the robot's x-axis and the world frame's x-axis, measured counter-clockwise:

$$
\theta \in [-\pi, \pi) \quad \text{(radians)}
$$

### 2.1.3 2D Pose — The Complete Description

$$
\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1
$$

where $S^1$ is the **unit circle** (1-sphere) representing angular space.

### 2.1.4 3D Orientation — Euler Angles (Z-Y-X Convention)
The most common convention in KTU-aligned texts is the **Roll-Pitch-Yaw (RPY)** decomposition:

$$
R_{ZYX}(\phi, \theta, \psi) = R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)
$$

where the elementary rotations are:

$$
R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.1.5 Homogeneous Transformation Matrix (HTM)
A 2D pose $(x, y, \theta)$ is compactly written as a **3×3** matrix:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in SE(2)
$$

For 3D, a 4×4 matrix encodes both rotation and translation:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3\times 3} & \mathbf{t}_{3\times 1} \\ \mathbf{0}_{1\times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \in SE(3)
$$

Here, $SE(n)$ denotes the **Special Euclidean Group** — the set of all rigid-body transformations in $n$-dimensional space.

## 2.2 The Sense-Act Loop of Reactive Navigation

The reactive navigation pipeline is a closed feedback loop:

1. **Perception (Sense):** Onboard sensors (LiDAR, ultrasonic, IR, camera) measure distances to obstacles or features.
2. **Behavior Selection (Decide):** A simple, pre-programmed rule maps the sensor input directly to a motor command (e.g., "if left sensor $<$ 0.5 m, turn right").
3. **Actuation (Act):** Wheel velocities are updated and the robot moves.
4. **Loop continues** at the next control cycle (typically 10–100 Hz).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** There is **no** intermediate symbolic world model. The mapping from sensor to actuator is *immediate* and *embodied*.

## 2.3 Classical Reactive Navigation Architectures

### 2.3.1 Braitenberg Vehicles (1984)
A simple agent where each wheel's speed is a monotonic function of the corresponding sensor reading:

$$
v_L = f_L(s_L), \quad v_R = f_R(s_R)
$$

- If $f$ is **increasing** ("aggressive" / "loves" light): vehicle moves **toward** the stimulus.
- If $f$ is **decreasing** ("fearful"): vehicle moves **away** from the stimulus.
- **Crossed wiring** (right sensor → left wheel): causes **obstacle avoidance** via smooth turning.

### 2.3.2 Subsumption Architecture (Rodney Brooks, 1986)
Behaviors are organized in **layers of increasing competence**:
- Layer 0: **Avoid** (collision avoidance).
- Layer 1: **Wander** (random exploration).
- Layer 2: **Explore** (look for distant objects).
- Layer 3: **Map** (build a representation).

Higher layers **subsume** (suppress) lower layers when conflicting outputs arise. All layers run **concurrently** and in **real time**.

### 2.3.3 Artificial Potential Field Method (Khatib, 1986)
The robot moves in a field where the **goal** is an attractive potential and **obstacles** are repulsive potentials:

$$
U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, k_{att} \cdot \vert\vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \vert\vert^2
$$

$$
U_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2} \, k_{rep} \!\left( \tfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{\!2}, & \rho(\mathbf{q}) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(\mathbf{q}) > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

The resulting **force** is the negative gradient of the total potential:

$$
\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) - \nabla U_{rep}(\mathbf{q})
$$

The robot steers in the direction of $-\nabla U_{total}$, i.e., downhill on the potential landscape.

### 2.3.4 Bug Algorithms
The robot's task is to reach a goal in an unknown environment with only local sensing:
- **Bug 0:** Follow the obstacle boundary until the heading is again toward the goal; leave.
- **Bug 1:** Circumnavigate the entire obstacle, record the closest point to the goal, then head toward the goal from that point.
- **Bug 2:** Follow the obstacle boundary until the line from start to goal is re-crossed at a point closer to the goal than before, then depart.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value and matrix determinant bars in your answer scripts — vertical pipes `|` break LaTeX/markdown table parsers!**

| Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 2D Position | $\mathbf{p} = [x,\ y]^T$ | Cartesian coordinates | metres (m) |
| 2D Pose | $\xi = [x,\ y,\ \theta]^T$ | Position + heading | m, m, rad |
| 3D Position | $\mathbf{p} = [x,\ y,\ z]^T$ | Spatial coordinates | m |
| 3D Pose | $\mathbf{P} = [x,\ y,\ z,\ \phi,\ \theta,\ \psi]^T$ | Position + RPY | m, rad |
| 2D Rotation | $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ | SO(2) matrix | dimensionless |
| 3D Rotation | $R = R_z(\psi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$ | SO(3) matrix | dimensionless |
| HTM (2D) | $T \in SE(2)$, size 3×3 | Pose in homogeneous form | m, dimensionless |
| HTM (3D) | $T \in SE(3)$, size 4×4 | Pose in homogeneous form | m, dimensionless |
| Braitenberg | $v_L = f_L(s_L),\ v_R = f_R(s_R)$ | Sensor-motor mapping | m/s, m, function |
| Attractive PF | $U_{att} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{att} \vert\vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{g} \vert\vert^2$ | Goal potential | Joules (J) |
| Repulsive PF | $U_{rep} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2$ | Obstacle potential | J (within $\rho_0$) |
| Force | $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U_{total}$ | Resultant motion vector | Newtons (N) |
| Polar coord. | $\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ | Radial distance | m |
| Polar coord. | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$ | Polar angle | rad |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of Reactive Navigation:**
> - **Autonomous Vacuum Cleaners** (iRobot Roomba) — use Braitenberg-like IR reflexes to avoid walls.
> - **Warehouse AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** — use potential-field methods for dynamic obstacle avoidance.
> - **Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance)** — combine visual hazard detection with reactive wheel commands for safe traversal.
> - **Self-Driving Cars (Tesla FSD, Waymo)** — use reactive layers for collision avoidance stacked beneath deliberative route planners.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 2D Rotation Matrix from Angle $\theta$

**Goal:** Derive the 2×2 rotation matrix that maps a vector in the world frame to a vector in the rotated robot frame by an angle $\theta$ about the z-axis.

**Step 1 — Initial Setup.** Consider a vector $\mathbf{v}_w$ in the world frame. After rotating the frame counter-clockwise by $\theta$, the vector is observed as $\mathbf{v}_r$ in the robot frame. The transformation is $\mathbf{v}_w = R(\theta) \, \mathbf{v}_r$.

**Step 2 — Component Analysis.** Let $\mathbf{v}_r = (v_x,\ v_y)^T$. Its projection onto the world x-axis:

$$
v_{w,x} = v_x \cos\theta - v_y \sin\theta
$$

This is because the world x-axis has projection $\cos\theta$ on the robot x-axis and projection $\sin\theta$ on the robot y-axis. Similarly for the world y-axis:

$$
v_{w,y} = v_x \sin\theta + v_y \cos\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Matrix Form.** Writing both equations in matrix form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} v_{w,x} \\ v_{w,y} \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Identification.** Therefore:

$$
R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \in SO(2)
$$

**Step 5 — Special Cases Verification.**
- For $\theta = 0$: $R(0) = I_{2\times 2}$ (no rotation, identity).
- For $\theta = \pi/2$: $R(\pi/2) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ (90° CCW rotation).

## 3.2 Derivation: 2D Pose in Homogeneous Form

**Goal:** Combine rotation $R(\theta)$ and translation $(x, y)$ into a single 3×3 matrix $T$.

**Step 1 — Augmented Vector.** To handle translation in a linear way, augment the 2D position vector with a leading 1:

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{v}} = \begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Block Construction.** The homogeneous transformation matrix is built by placing $R(\theta)$ in the upper-left 2×2 block, the translation vector in the upper-right 2×1 column, and $[0,\ 0,\ 1]$ in the bottom row:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to Augmented Vector.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \, \tilde{\mathbf{v}}_r &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{r,x} \\ v_{r,y} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v_{w,x} \\ v_{w,y} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

The bottom row is identically 1, so the result remains in homogeneous form. The top two rows give:

$$
v_{w,x} = v_{r,x} \cos\theta - v_{r,y} \sin\theta + x
$$

$$
v_{w,y} = v_{r,x} \sin\theta + v_{r,y} \cos\theta + y
$$

**Step 4 — Composition Property.** A key property of $T$ is that the pose of frame $C$ relative to frame $A$ can be obtained by matrix multiplication:

$$
T_{A,C} = T_{A,B} \cdot T_{B,C}
$$

This **chain rule** is central to robotic kinematics.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Coordinate Transformation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A robot is at pose $(x, y, \theta) = (2.0 \text{ m},\ 1.5 \text{ m},\ 60°)$ in the world frame. A landmark is detected by the robot's onboard camera at position $(1.0 \text{ m},\ 0.5 \text{ m})$ in the robot's body frame. Find the landmark's position in the world frame.

**Step 1 — Convert angle to radians.**

$$
\theta = 60° \times \tfrac{\pi}{180} = \tfrac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.0472 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Build the 2D HTM.**

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 60° & -\sin 60° & 2.0 \\ \sin 60° & \cos 60° & 1.5 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.866 & 2.0 \\ 0.866 & 0.5 & 1.5 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the Augmented Vector.** The landmark in the robot frame is $(1.0, 0.5)$:

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_r = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply $T \cdot \tilde{\mathbf{p}}_r$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{p}_w &= T \cdot \tilde{\mathbf{p}}_r \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} (0.5)(1.0) + (-0.866)(0.5) + (2.0)(1) \\ (0.866)(1.0) + (0.5)(0.5) + (1.5)(1) \\ (0)(1.0) + (0)(0.5) + (1)(1) \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 - 0.433 + 2.0 \\ 0.866 + 0.25 + 1.5 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 2.067 \\ 2.616 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** The landmark is located approximately at $(2.07 \text{ m},\ 2.62 \text{ m})$ in the world frame.

## 3.4 Derivation: Potential Field Force Equations

**Step 1 — Attractive Potential.** Define attractive potential as a quadratic function of distance to the goal:

$$
U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, k_{att} \cdot \vert\vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \vert\vert^2
$$

**Step 2 — Attractive Force.** Take the negative gradient:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -k_{att} \cdot (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})
$$

**Step 3 — Repulsive Potential.** Define repulsive potential only when robot is within distance $\rho_0$ of the obstacle (with closest distance $\rho(\mathbf{q})$):

$$
U_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2} \, k_{rep} \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, & \rho(\mathbf{q}) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(\mathbf{q}) > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Repulsive Force.** Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{q}$:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep} = k_{rep} \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \tfrac{1}{\rho^2(\mathbf{q})} \cdot \nabla \rho(\mathbf{q})
$$

**Step 5 — Total Force and Velocity.** The robot's commanded velocity is proportional to the total force:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{cmd} = K \cdot \mathbf{F}_{total} = K \cdot (\mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep})
$$

where $K$ is a positive gain tuning the robot's speed.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Reactive Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this table in KTU 14-mark answers to demonstrate critical analysis. Examiners award marks for comparative reasoning.**

| Strategy | Strength | Weakness | Typical Use Case |
|----------|----------|----------|------------------|
| **Braitenberg Vehicle** | Trivially simple, no model | Cannot reach a specific goal | Obstacle avoidance, phototaxis |
| **Subsumption** | Layered, robust, parallel | Hand-tuned arbitration | Mobile robots (Herbert, Genghis) |
| **Potential Field** | Goal-directed + avoids obstacles | Suffers from **local minima** | Indoor mobile robots |
| **Bug Algorithm** | Provably complete for point robots | Inefficient paths, requires boundary contact | Unknown environment exploration |
| **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** | Real-time, density-based obstacle repulsion | Sensitive to grid resolution | Narrow passages, crowded rooms |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Robot Pose Representation Hierarchy

The following Mermaid block renders the hierarchical conceptual relationship between position, orientation, and the various mathematical representations.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / PECDT633]`
**Define the term "Pose" of a robot. How is it represented in 2D?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Pose** is the complete spatial configuration of a robot, defined by its position and orientation in space. In 2D, the pose is represented as a 3-element vector:
> $$\xi = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$
> where $(x, y)$ are the Cartesian coordinates of the robot's reference point in the world frame, and $\theta$ is the heading angle (orientation) of the robot's body x-axis with respect to the world x-axis, measured counter-clockwise in radians. (3 marks)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / PECDT633]`
**What is the difference between reactive and deliberative navigation? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Reactive | Deliberative |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| World Model | Not required | Required (map) |
| Response | Immediate, local | Planned, global |
| Example | Braitenberg vehicle, Potential Field | A* path planning, RRT |

(3 marks — 1.5 for the table/differences, 1.5 for examples)

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Provide step-by-step solutions with proper diagrams and equations.

---

### **Question A** — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECDT633, Module 3]`**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the representation of a robot's position and orientation in 2D. Derive the 2D rotation matrix for a counter-clockwise rotation by an angle $\theta$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a Braitenberg vehicle? With suitable diagrams, explain how crossed-wiring and direct-wiring produce different behaviors. Show mathematically how obstacle avoidance is achieved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Position Representation (2 marks):** A 2D robot's position is given by coordinates $(x, y)$ relative to the world frame, shown as a point on a 2D plane. The vector is $\mathbf{p} = [x,\ y]^T$.

**Step 2 — Orientation Representation (1 mark):** Orientation $\theta$ is the angle between the robot's body x-axis (forward direction) and the world x-axis, measured CCW.

**Step 3 — Pose (1 mark):** The combined 2D pose is $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$.

**Step 4 — Derivation of Rotation Matrix (3 marks):** Consider a vector $\mathbf{v}_r = (v_x, v_y)^T$ in the robot frame. After rotation by $\theta$ CCW, the components in the world frame are:

$$
v_{w,x} = v_x \cos\theta - v_y \sin\theta
$$

$$
v_{w,y} = v_x \sin\theta + v_y \cos\theta
$$

In matrix form:

$$
R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}, \quad R(\theta) \in SO(2)
$$

**Verification:** $R(0) = I$ and $R(\pi/2)$ maps $(1, 0) \mapsto (0, 1)$.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):** A *Braitenberg vehicle* (Valentino Braitenberg, 1984) is a simple mobile agent whose motor speeds are direct, monotonic functions of sensor readings. The general form:

$$
v_L = f_L(s_L), \qquad v_R = f_R(s_R)
$$

where $s_L$ and $s_R$ are left and right sensor values, $v_L$ and $v_R$ are wheel velocities.

**Step 2 — Direct Wiring (1.5 marks):** Direct wiring means the left sensor drives the left wheel: $v_L = f(s_L)$, $v_R = f(s_R)$. If $f$ is increasing, the vehicle **moves toward** the stimulus ("aggressive" behavior).

**Step 3 — Crossed Wiring (1.5 marks):** Crossed wiring means the left sensor drives the right wheel: $v_L = f(s_R)$, $v_R = f(s_L)$. If $f$ is decreasing, the vehicle **turns away from** obstacles smoothly. If $f$ is increasing, the vehicle **turns toward** the stimulus (pursuit).

**Step 4 — Mathematical Avoidance Example (2 marks):** Consider an obstacle on the left ($s_L$ large). With crossed wiring and decreasing $f$:
- $v_R = f(s_L) \to$ decreases (right wheel slows).
- $v_L = f(s_R) \to$ stays higher (left wheel fast).
- Result: robot **turns right**, away from the left obstacle. (1 mark for arithmetic sketch, 1 mark for conclusion.)

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECDT633, Module 3]`**

**(a)** Explain the Artificial Potential Field (APF) method of reactive navigation. Derive the expressions for the attractive and repulsive forces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the limitations of the APF method? With a diagram, explain the Bug-2 algorithm and prove that it is complete for point robots in 2D. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):** The APF method treats the robot as a charged particle moving in a field where the goal exerts an *attractive* force and obstacles exert *repulsive* forces. The robot follows the **negative gradient** of the total potential.

**Step 2 — Attractive Potential (1.5 marks):** Quadratic in distance to goal:

$$
U_{att}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, k_{att} \cdot \vert\vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal} \vert\vert^2
$$

**Step 3 — Attractive Force (1 mark):** The negative gradient:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -k_{att} (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{goal})
$$

**Step 4 — Repulsive Potential (1.5 marks):** Defined only within influence radius $\rho_0$:

$$
U_{rep}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2} \, k_{rep} \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, & \rho(\mathbf{q}) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(\mathbf{q}) > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Repulsive Force (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep} = k_{rep} \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q})} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \tfrac{1}{\rho^2(\mathbf{q})} \cdot \nabla \rho(\mathbf{q})
$$

Total commanded velocity is $\mathbf{v}_{cmd} \propto \mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep}$.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Limitations of APF (3 marks):**
1. **Local minima problem** — the robot can get trapped at points where $\nabla U_{total} = 0$ but the goal is not reached (e.g., directly between two obstacles).
2. **Goal not reachable with nearby obstacles (GNRON)** — repulsive force at the goal prevents reaching it.
3. **Oscillations in narrow passages** due to competing attractive and repulsive forces.
4. **No passage between closely spaced obstacles**.

**Step 2 — Bug-2 Algorithm (2.5 marks):** The Bug-2 algorithm has two modes:
- **Mode 1 (Go-to-Goal):** Move in a straight line from start toward the goal.
- **Mode 2 (Boundary-Following):** Upon hitting an obstacle, follow its boundary until the line $m$-line (from start to goal) is re-crossed at a point *closer* to the goal than the previous crossing. Then leave the boundary and resume Mode 1.

**Step 3 — Proof of Completeness (1.5 marks):** For a point robot with perfect localization and tactile sensing, the algorithm terminates because:
- The $m$-line crossing point strictly decreases in distance to the goal.
- The total boundary length of any obstacle is finite.
- If the goal is reachable, the algorithm eventually reaches it. If the goal is enclosed by a closed obstacle, the algorithm detects non-reachability upon completing a full boundary loop without finding a closer $m$-line crossing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Reactive Navigation Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing SE(2) and SE(3):** SE(2) is for 2D (3×3 matrices), SE(3) is for 3D (4×4 matrices). Do NOT interchange.
> 2. **Forgetting the homogeneous row [0, 0, 1] / [0, 0, 0, 1]:** Marks are deducted if the bottom row is omitted.
> 3. **Mixing up Braitenberg wiring types:** "Direct" and "Crossed" wiring have *opposite* behaviors. State the mapping explicitly.
> 4. **APF gradient sign errors:** Force = $-\nabla U$, not $+\nabla U$. Robot moves *downhill* on potential, not uphill.
> 5. **Bug-1 vs Bug-2 confusion:** Bug-1 finds the closest point on the obstacle to the goal; Bug-2 uses the $m$-line crossing rule. Examiners notice this.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams:** A 7-mark sub-question without a labelled diagram typically loses 1–2 marks in KTU valuation.
> 7. **Units omission:** Always state units (radians, metres) in numerical answers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PECDT633 / Module 3**

- **Pose** = Position + Orientation. The complete rigid-body description.
- **2D Pose vector:** $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ where $\theta \in [-\pi, \pi)$.
- **3D Pose vector:** $\mathbf{P} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ with RPY convention.
- **Rotation matrix (2D, CCW):** $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \in SO(2)$.
- **HTM (2D):** $T \in SE(2)$, 3×3 matrix; HTM (3D): $T \in SE(3)$, 4×4 matrix.
- **Composition rule:** $T_{A,C} = T_{A,B} \cdot T_{B,C}$ (chain rule for kinematic frames).
- **Reactive Navigation = Sense → Act** (no world model, real-time, sensor-driven).
- **Deliberative Navigation = Sense → Plan → Act** (with map, computationally heavy).
- **Braitenberg Vehicle:** $v_L = f_L(s_L)$, $v_R = f_R(s_R)$. Crossed + decreasing $f$ = obstacle avoidance.
- **Subsumption Architecture (Brooks):** Layered behaviors, higher layer suppresses lower.
- **Artificial Potential Field (Khatib):** $U = U_{att} + U_{rep}$; $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U$.
- **Bug-1 Algorithm:** Follow boundary, find *closest point* to goal, leave.
- **Bug-2 Algorithm:** Follow boundary, find *m-line crossing closer to goal*, leave.
- **Local minima** are the **Achilles' heel** of APF; Bug algorithms are provably complete.
- **SE(n) vs SO(n):** SE = Special Euclidean (rotation + translation); SO = Special Orthogonal (rotation only).
- **RPY convention:** Z-Y-X intrinsic (yaw, then pitch, then roll) — KTU's default convention.
- **Total Pose Cardinality:** 2D = 3 DOF, 3D = 6 DOF (3 translational + 3 rotational).
- **Quaternion alternative:** 4-element unit vector $[q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3]^T$ with $q_0^2 + q_1^2 + q_2^2 + q_3^2 = 1$ avoids gimbal lock.
- **Always carry units in your KTU numerical answers** — metres, radians, seconds.
- **Always include labelled diagrams** for 7-mark sub-parts in 14-mark questions.
- **Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` for determinants/absolute value in written exams** to avoid LaTeX/markdown errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robot Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Localization** is the process by which a mobile robot determines its **pose** (i.e., its 2D or 3D position and orientation) within a known or partially known reference frame (map) using sensory information, odometric data, and prior knowledge of its motion.

Mathematically, robot localization is the estimation of the belief state $bel(x_t)$ of the robot's pose $x_t$ at time $t$, given:

- The history of control actions: $u_{1:t} = \{u_1, u_2, ..., u_t\}$
- The history of sensor measurements: $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, ..., z_t\}$
- A map $m$ of the environment (when available)

Formally:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

This expression represents the **posterior probability distribution** over the robot's pose, conditioned on all available data up to time $t$. Localization is, in essence, a **recursive Bayesian estimation** problem.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Finding Yourself in an Unfamiliar City at Night:**
> Imagine you are dropped into a new city at night. You have a map (a-priori knowledge), a GPS that gives noisy readings intermittently, and you can ask locals for directions (sensor measurements). Each piece of evidence slightly updates your estimate of "where I am." Robot localization works in exactly the same way — the robot fuses **noisy** odometry, **uncertain** sensor readings, and a **map** to continuously refine its belief about its pose. The night-time fog is akin to sensor noise; the city's landmarks are like beacons the robot can detect.

## 1.3 Why Localization is the "Holy Grail" of Mobile Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Localization is considered one of the most fundamental problems in robotics because:
> 1. **Successful navigation requires a known starting location.**
> 2. **Manipulation tasks** demand accurate end-effector pose estimation.
> 3. **Human–robot interaction** requires the robot to know where it is relative to humans.
> 4. **Multi-robot coordination** requires each robot to know its pose w.r.t. others.

## 1.4 The Three Variants of the Localization Problem

| Variant | Description | Difficulty | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position Tracking (Local Localization)** | Robot's initial pose is known; only small deviations need to be estimated. | Easy | High |
| **Global Localization** | Robot's initial pose is unknown; the robot must determine it from scratch. | Hard | Very High |
| **Kidnapped Robot Problem** | A globally-localized robot is teleported to a new, unknown location and must recover. | Very Hard | Moderate |

## 1.5 Pose Representation

The robot pose in 2D is typically represented as the state vector:

$$x_t = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}_t$$

where $(x, y)$ is the 2D position and $\theta$ is the heading angle in **radians**. The **state dimension is 3** for planar ground robots.

## 1.6 KTU Highlight: Markov Assumption

> [!NOTE]
> The entire probabilistic localization framework rests on the **Markov Assumption** — the robot's pose at time $t$ is sufficient to summarize all past history. Mathematically:
> $$p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$
> This is the cornerstone of the recursive Bayes filter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Foundation of Localization

The robot localization pipeline is mathematically expressed as the recursive **Bayes filter**, which alternates between two steps at every time tick:

1. **Prediction Step (Motion Update):** Propagates the belief forward using the motion model.
2. **Correction Step (Measurement Update):** Updates the belief using sensor measurements.

### 2.1.1 Prediction Step (Control Update)

Given the belief at $t-1$, the predicted belief after applying control $u_t$ is:

$$\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

The term $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ is the **motion model**, representing the probability that executing control $u_t$ from state $x_{t-1}$ results in state $x_t$.

### 2.1.2 Correction Step (Measurement Update)

After obtaining measurement $z_t$:

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$

where $\eta$ is a normalizer:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}$$

and $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is the **measurement (sensor) model**, representing the likelihood of observing $z_t$ given the robot is at $x_t$ in map $m$.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Localization Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of representation for $bel(x_t)$ defines the algorithm family:

| Algorithm | Representation of $bel(x_t)$ | Key Idea | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Histogram / Grid Localization** | Discrete grid over poses | Discretize pose space, store belief per cell | Global localization capable | Computational, memory heavy |
| **Kalman Filter (KF) Localization** | Unimodal Gaussian $N(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ | Track mean & covariance; linear-Gaussian | Efficient, optimal for linear | Single-hypothesis, fails globally |
| **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Localization** | Gaussian (linearized) | Linearize motion/measurement models | Handles non-linearity | Still unimodal |
| **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** | Set of weighted particles $\{x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^N$ | Sample-based approximation of belief | Multi-modal, robust, global | Computational cost of resampling |

## 2.3 Dead Reckoning (Odometric Localization)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dead Reckoning (DR)** is the simplest localization method — it integrates wheel encoder readings to estimate pose. With incremental encoder measurements $\Delta s_L$ and $\Delta s_R$ for left and right wheels, and wheelbase $b$:

The change in heading:

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$$

The distance traveled:

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$$

The incremental pose update:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cos(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \sin(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta\theta
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Fundamental Flaw of Dead Reckoning:** Errors **accumulate unboundedly** over time because each new estimate depends on the previous (erroneous) one. This is called **odometric drift** and is the *primary motivation* for using external sensors and Bayes filters.

## 2.4 Kalman Filter Localization — Working Principles

The Kalman filter represents belief as a **Gaussian**:

$$bel(x_t) = N(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$$

- **$\mu_t$** — mean pose estimate (3×1 for planar).
- **$\Sigma_t$** — 3×3 covariance matrix representing pose uncertainty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Kalman filter is **optimal** only when both the motion model and measurement model are **linear** and all noise is **Gaussian**. This is rarely the case in real robots, hence the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** linearizes around the current estimate.

### Kalman Filter Equations Summary

**Prediction (time update):**
$$\overline{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$$
$$\overline{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$$

**Correction (measurement update):**
$$K_t = \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$
$$\mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t)$$
$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \overline{\Sigma}_t$$

where $K_t$ is the **Kalman Gain**, $R_t$ is the process noise covariance, and $Q_t$ is the measurement noise covariance.

## 2.5 Monte Carlo Localization (Particle Filter Localization)

### 2.5.1 Core Algorithm

MCL approximates $bel(x_t)$ using $N$ weighted samples (particles) $S_t = \{x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^N$.

> [!NOTE]
> The density of particles represents the belief distribution. The **weight** of a particle represents how well that hypothesis explains the latest sensor measurement.

### 2.5.2 The MCL Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

**Algorithm: $MCL(S_{t-1}, u_t, z_t, m)$**

1. $\overline{S}_t = S_t = \emptyset$
2. **For** $i = 1$ to $N$ **do:**
   - Sample $x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{(i)})$  ← **Prediction**
   - Compute weight $w_t^{(i)} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)$  ← **Correction**
   - Add $\langle x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)} \rangle$ to $\overline{S}_t$
3. **End for**
4. Normalize weights: $w_t^{(i)} = w_t^{(i)} / \sum_j w_t^{(j)}$
5. **Resample** $S_t$ from $\overline{S}_t$ by drawing $N$ samples with probability proportional to $w_t^{(i)}$
6. **Return** $S_t$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resampling** is the critical step that prevents particle degeneracy. It eliminates low-weight particles and duplicates high-weight ones, focusing computational resources on the most likely hypotheses.

### 2.5.3 Why MCL Solves the Kidnapped Robot Problem

> [!NOTE]
> Standard MCL fails at the kidnapped robot problem because particles can deplete from the new location. The solution is **Augmented MCL**, which adds a small probability of **random particle injection**:
> $$S_t = (1 - \alpha) \cdot \text{Resampled Particles} + \alpha \cdot \text{Uniformly Sampled Random Particles}$$
> where $\alpha \in [0, 0.1]$ is a small fraction. This guarantees that a fraction of particles is always exploring the pose space.

## 2.6 Sensor Models Used in Localization

| Sensor Type | Information Provided | Noise Characteristics | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wheel Encoders** | $\Delta s_L, \Delta s_R$ | Small but cumulative (drift) | Motion model |
| **Compass / IMU** | Heading $\theta$ | Bounded, low drift | Heading correction |
| **Sonar / Ultrasonic** | Distance to obstacle | Wide beam, specular reflections | Wall detection |
| **LIDAR** | Range scan (point cloud) | High precision | Landmark-based, scan-match |
| **Camera (Vision)** | Features, images | Lighting-dependent | Visual landmarks, visual SLAM |
| **GPS** | Global $(x, y)$ | Multi-meter accuracy, blocked indoors | Outdoor global init |

## 2.7 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pose representation (2D) | $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ | 3-DOF state |
| 2 | Belief definition | $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$ | Posterior over pose |
| 3 | Markov assumption | $p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Foundation of recursion |
| 4 | Bayes prediction | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})bel(x_{t-1})dx_{t-1}$ | Motion update integral |
| 5 | Bayes correction | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Sensor update |
| 6 | Odometry update | $x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \cos(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2)$ | $x$-component |
| 7 | Odometry update | $y_{t+1} = y_t + \Delta s \sin(\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2)$ | $y$-component |
| 8 | Odometry update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta\theta$ | Heading update |
| 9 | Heading change | $\Delta \theta = (\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L) / b$ | Differential drive |
| 10 | Kalman mean pred. | $\overline{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ | Linear system |
| 11 | Kalman cov. pred. | $\overline{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$ | Process noise $R_t$ |
| 12 | Kalman gain | $K_t = \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | Optimal blending |
| 13 | Kalman mean corr. | $\mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t)$ | Innovation $z_t - C_t\overline{\mu}_t$ |
| 14 | Kalman cov. corr. | $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \overline{\Sigma}_t$ | Posterior covariance |
| 15 | Particle weight | $w_t^{(i)} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)$ | Importance weight |
| 16 | Augmented MCL | $S_t = (1 - \alpha) \cdot \text{Resampled} + \alpha \cdot \text{Random}$ | Kidnapped robot |
| 17 | Resampling prob. | $P(i) = w_t^{(i)} / \sum_j w_t^{(j)}$ | Proportional to weight |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva):** MCL with LIDAR scan-matching for global localization on warehouse maps.
> - **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Particle filters and Kalman filters in GPS+IMU+LIDAR fusion.
> - **Indoor Service Robots (Roomba):** Monte Carlo localization on occupancy-grid maps.
> - **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance):** Visual odometry + EKF for sun-tracked localization on planetary surfaces.
> - **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Sub-millimeter EKF localization in instrument tracking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bayes Filter for Localization

We start with the formal definition of the belief:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Rule directly to the posterior:**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that given the current pose $x_t$, the current measurement $z_t$ is conditionally independent of all past measurements and controls:**

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$

This is the **measurement Markov assumption**.

**Step 3 — Substitute back into Step 1:**

$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$$

where $\eta$ is the normalizing constant $1 / p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m)$.

**Step 4 — Introduce the prediction $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ by marginalizing over $x_{t-1}$:**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t, m) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}, m) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the inner probability as the prior belief $bel(x_{t-1})$ and apply the motion Markov assumption:**

$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t, m) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}, m) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \, bel(x_{t-1})$$

**Step 6 — Combine Steps 3 and 5 into the canonical Bayes filter form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{bel}(x_t) &= \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} \quad &\text{(Prediction)} \\
bel(x_t) &= \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t) \quad &\text{(Correction)}
\end{aligned}$$

This two-stage recursion is the **mathematical heart of every localization algorithm** — the only difference between methods is how $bel$ is represented.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Odometry Update Equations

Consider a differential drive robot with wheelbase $b$ moving in an arc. At time $t$, the robot is at pose $x_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$. The wheel encoders report $\Delta s_L$ and $\Delta s_R$.

**Step 1 — Define the incremental rotation:**

The two wheels travel different distances, causing the robot to rotate. The instantaneous center of rotation (ICR) is at distance $R$ from the robot's center:

$$R = \frac{b}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta s_L + \Delta s_R}{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}$$

**Step 2 — The change in heading is the arc length divided by the radius:**

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$$

**Step 3 — The distance traveled by the robot's center is the average wheel displacement:**

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the new pose by integrating along the arc. The midpoint heading is $\theta_t + \Delta\theta / 2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cos\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \sin\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta\theta
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Special case — straight-line motion (verifies the limit). When $\Delta s_L = \Delta s_R$, then $\Delta \theta = 0$ and $\Delta s = \Delta s_L = \Delta s_R$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cos(\theta_t) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \sin(\theta_t) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the standard kinematics of a point moving along the robot's heading.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Kalman Filter Update Equations

The KF is derived by minimizing the **mean squared error** $E[(x_t - \mu_t)(x_t - \mu_t)^T]$ subject to linear-Gaussian assumptions.

**System Model:**
$$x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t + \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim N(0, R_t)$$
$$z_t = C_t x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim N(0, Q_t)$$

**Step 1 — Form the prior estimate using the linear motion model:**

$$\overline{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$$

**Step 2 — Propagate the prior covariance through the linear transform and add process noise:**

$$\overline{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$$

**Step 3 — The posterior is a product of two Gaussians (prior and likelihood). The product of two Gaussians is itself a Gaussian whose mean minimizes the Kullback–Leibler divergence between the prior and posterior. Solving yields the Kalman gain:**

$$K_t = \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — The posterior mean is the prior mean plus a correction proportional to the innovation $z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t$:**

$$\mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t)$$

**Step 5 — The posterior covariance is reduced by the information gained from the measurement:**

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \overline{\Sigma}_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Kalman gain $K_t$ **adaptively balances** the prior (process) noise $R_t$ and the measurement noise $Q_t$:
> - If $Q_t$ is **small** (sensor accurate), $K_t$ is large → trust the measurement more.
> - If $R_t$ is **small** (motion model accurate), $K_t$ is small → trust the prediction more.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Dead Reckoning Update

**Problem:** A differential-drive robot with wheelbase $b = 0.5$ m starts at pose $(0, 0, 0)$. In one timestep, the left wheel travels $\Delta s_L = 0.20$ m and the right wheel travels $\Delta s_R = 0.30$ m. Compute the new pose.

**Step 1 — Heading change:**

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b} = \frac{0.30 - 0.20}{0.5} = \frac{0.10}{0.5} = 0.20 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Distance traveled:**

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} = \frac{0.30 + 0.20}{2} = \frac{0.50}{2} = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Midpoint heading:**

$$\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2} = 0 + 0.10 = 0.10 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — New $x$ coordinate:**

$$x_{t+1} = 0 + 0.25 \cos(0.10) = 0.25 \times 0.9950 = 0.2488 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — New $y$ coordinate:**

$$y_{t+1} = 0 + 0.25 \sin(0.10) = 0.25 \times 0.0998 = 0.02495 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — New heading:**

$$\theta_{t+1} = 0 + 0.20 = 0.20 \text{ rad}$$

**Final Pose:** $x_{t+1} = (0.2488, 0.02495, 0.20)$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Robot Localization Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Robot at Unknown Pose]) --> Init[Initialization: Initialize Belief bel x0]
    Init --> Loop{For each timestep t}
    Loop -->|Yes| Motion[Control Input ut arrives from odometry]
    Motion --> Predict[Prediction Step: Apply Motion Model p xt / ut, xt-1]
    Predict --> BeliefPred[Predicted Belief bel-bar xt]
    BeliefPred --> Sensor[Sensor Measurement zt arrives from LIDAR / Camera / Sonar]
    Sensor --> Compare[Compare zt with Map m]
    Compare --> MeasModel[Measurement Model: p zt / xt, m]
    MeasModel --> Correct[Correction Step: Multiply Likelihood with Prior]
    Correct --> BeliefCorr[Posterior Belief bel xt]
    BeliefCorr --> Decision{Pose Confidence High}
    Decision -->|No| Loop
    Decision -->|Yes| Output([Output Estimated Pose mu-t and Sigma-t])
    Loop -->|No| Stop([End])
```

## 4.2 Localization Algorithm Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    Localization[Robot Localization Methods] --> DR[Dead Reckoning]
    Localization --> Prob[Probabilistic Methods]
    DR --> Odom[Odometric Integration]
    Prob --> Kalman[Kalman Filter Family]
    Prob --> Grid[Grid / Histogram]
    Prob --> Particle[Particle Filter]
    Kalman --> KF[Standard KF - Linear]
    Kalman --> EKF[Extended KF - Linearized]
    Kalman --> UKF[Unscented KF - Sigma Points]
    Particle --> MCL[Monte Carlo Localization]
    Particle --> AugMCL[Augmented MCL for Kidnapped Robot]
    Grid --> MarkovGrid[Markov Localization]
```

## 4.3 Bayes Filter Recursion (State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitBel
    InitBel: Initialize bel x0 from prior
    InitBel --> Prediction
    Prediction: Prediction Step\nbel-bar xt = Integral of p xt/ut,xt-1 * bel xt-1
    Prediction --> Measurement
    Measurement: Acquire zt from sensors
    Measurement --> Correction
    Correction: Correction Step\nbel xt = eta * p zt/xt,m * bel-bar xt
    Correction --> Output
    Output: Output xt estimate
    Output --> Prediction
    Output --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Comparison: KF vs MCL (Decision Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Is the environment a single hypothesis?} -->|Yes| Q2{Is the model linear-Gaussian?}
    Q1 -->|No, multi-modal| MCL[Use Monte Carlo Localization]
    Q2 -->|Yes| KF[Use Standard Kalman Filter]
    Q2 -->|No, nonlinear| Q3{Do you need a closed-form posterior?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| EKF[Use Extended Kalman Filter]
    Q3 -->|No, robustness is key| MCL2[Use MCL]
    KF --> Output1[Output Gaussian bel]
    EKF --> Output2[Output Linearized Gaussian bel]
    MCL --> Output3[Output Particle Set]
    MCL2 --> Output3
```

## 4.5 Kidnapped Robot Recovery using Augmented MCL

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Robot
    participant Particles
    participant Sensor
    Robot->>Particles: Propagate all N particles with motion model
    Particles-->>Robot: Predicted particle set
    Robot->>Sensor: Acquire new measurement zt
    Sensor-->>Robot: Returns zt
    Robot->>Particles: Compute weight wt-i = p zt / xt-i, m
    Particles-->>Robot: Weighted particle set
    Robot->>Particles: Normalize weights
    alt Fraction alpha of particles
        Robot->>Particles: Inject uniformly random particles (exploration)
    end
    Robot->>Particles: Resample N particles proportional to weight
    Particles-->>Robot: New particle set St
    Robot->>Robot: Compute mean pose estimate mu-t
```

## 4.6 Sensor-to-Belief Dataflow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wheel Encoders] --> M[Motion Model Module]
    B[IMU / Compass] --> M
    C[LIDAR Scan] --> S[Sensor Model Module]
    D[Camera Image] --> S
    E[Map Database m] --> S
    M --> P[Predicted Belief]
    P --> F[Bayes Fusion Kernel]
    S --> F
    F --> U[Updated Belief]
    U --> R[Robot Pose Output]
    U --> Loop((Feedback to next step))
    Loop --> M
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions test direct recall and conceptual understanding. KTU 2024 scheme demands crisp, definition-style answers.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Robot Localization. Mention the three variants of the localization problem. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> Robot Localization is the process of determining a robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ relative to a reference frame, using sensor measurements, control inputs, and a map.
>
> The three variants are:
> 1. **Position Tracking** – initial pose is known; small deviations are tracked.
> 2. **Global Localization** – initial pose is completely unknown.
> 3. **Kidnapped Robot Problem** – a localized robot is suddenly teleported; it must recover.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is the Markov Assumption in robot localization? Why is it important? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Markov Assumption** states that the robot's current pose $x_t$ is sufficient to predict the next state, rendering past states conditionally independent:
> $$p(x_t \mid x_{0:t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$
>
> **Importance:** It allows the Bayes filter to be **recursive**, avoiding the need to store the entire history. This makes real-time localization computationally feasible and forms the mathematical basis of Kalman filtering, MCL, and grid localization.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In Bayes filter derivations, students often forget to **state the Markov assumption explicitly** before using it. This costs 2 marks.
> - In odometry numericals, do not forget the **midpoint heading** term $\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2$. Using $\theta_t$ alone is a common error and loses 1 mark.
> - For Kalman filter, always identify the **innovation term** $(z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t)$ explicitly.
> - In MCL, students often confuse **prediction** (sampling from motion model) with **correction** (weighting by sensor model). Examiners penalize this heavily.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 3]**

#### (a) Derive the recursive Bayes filter for robot localization. Clearly state the two steps and the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The belief of the robot's pose at time $t$ is:

$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Rule:**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}, m) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Measurement Markov Assumption:**

The current measurement depends only on the current state:

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Step 3 — Marginalize over $x_{t-1}$ in the prediction term:**

$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, m) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t, m) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}, m) \, dx_{t-1} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the motion Markov assumption and define prior belief:**

$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t, m) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}, m) = p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \quad \text{...(5)}$$

**Step 5 — Combine to get the recursive Bayes filter:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{bel}(x_t) &= \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} \quad &\text{(Prediction)} \\
bel(x_t) &= \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t) \quad &\text{(Correction)}
\end{aligned}$$

The **assumptions** are the **Markov assumption** for motion and the **conditional independence of measurement** given state.

#### (b) Explain Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) algorithm in detail. How does Augmented MCL solve the kidnapped robot problem? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**MCL Algorithm:**

MCL represents the belief $bel(x_t)$ by a set of $N$ weighted particles $S_t = \{x_t^{(i)}, w_t^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^N$.

1. **Initialization:** Distribute $N$ particles uniformly (global localization) or around the prior (position tracking).
2. **Prediction:** For each particle, sample a new pose from the motion model:
$$x_t^{(i)} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{(i)})$$
3. **Weighting:** Compute the importance weight using the sensor model:
$$w_t^{(i)} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{(i)}, m)$$
4. **Normalization:** $w_t^{(i)} \leftarrow w_t^{(i)} / \sum_j w_t^{(j)}$
5. **Resampling:** Draw $N$ particles from the current set with probability proportional to $w_t^{(i)}$.

**Solving the Kidnapped Robot Problem:**

Standard MCL fails because all particles eventually converge near the old pose. **Augmented MCL** injects a small fraction $\alpha$ of **randomly sampled particles** (uniform over the pose space) at every resampling step:

$$S_t = (1 - \alpha) \cdot S_t^{resampled} + \alpha \cdot S_t^{random}$$

This guarantees a continuous exploration of the pose space. When the robot is kidnapped, these random particles will, by chance, land near the new location, and the sensor model will assign them high weights, allowing the filter to recover global localization.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

#### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the dead reckoning method for robot localization. Derive the odometry update equations for a differential drive robot. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** Dead reckoning estimates the robot's current pose by integrating wheel encoder increments from a known starting pose.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Pose x0, y0, theta0] --> B[Read Encoders delta-sL, delta-sR]
    B --> C[Compute delta-theta and delta-s]
    C --> D[Apply Odometry Equations]
    D --> E[New Pose x1, y1, theta1]
    E --> F{More Data}
    F -->|Yes| B
    F -->|No| G[Output Final Pose]
```

**Derivation:**

For a differential drive robot with wheelbase $b$, the heading change is:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$$

The translational displacement of the robot's center:

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$$

The pose update (using the midpoint heading approximation):

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cos\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \sin\left(\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta\theta
\end{aligned}$$

**Limitation:** Errors accumulate unboundedly over time. The variance of the pose estimate grows without limit because each new estimate depends on the previous (uncertain) one. This motivates probabilistic localization.

#### (b) Explain Kalman Filter based localization. Why is it unsuitable for global localization? State the EKF equations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**KF Localization:**

The Kalman Filter represents belief as a single Gaussian $bel(x_t) = N(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$. It alternates between prediction (using the motion model) and correction (using the measurement model), optimally combining them via the Kalman gain.

**Prediction:**
$$\overline{\mu}_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t, \quad \overline{\Sigma}_t = A_t \Sigma_{t-1} A_t^T + R_t$$

**Correction:**
$$K_t = \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T (C_t \overline{\Sigma}_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$
$$\mu_t = \overline{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C_t \overline{\mu}_t), \quad \Sigma_t = (I - K_t C_t) \overline{\Sigma}_t$$

**Why KF is Unsuitable for Global Localization:**

A Gaussian is **unimodal** — it can represent only a single hypothesis. If the robot's initial pose is unknown, there are infinitely many plausible poses. The Gaussian can model this only as a single, very wide distribution, which immediately collapses to a small region upon the first measurement, locking in the wrong pose. KF is therefore suitable only for **position tracking**, not global localization.

**EKF Equations:**

The EKF linearizes the nonlinear models around the current estimate using Jacobians $F_t = \partial f / \partial x$ and $H_t = \partial h / \partial x$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{\mu}_t &= f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) \\
\overline{\Sigma}_t &= F_t \Sigma_{t-1} F_t^T + R_t \\
K_t &= \overline{\Sigma}_t H_t^T (H_t \overline{\Sigma}_t H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1} \\
\mu_t &= \overline{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\overline{\mu}_t)) \\
\Sigma_t &= (I - K_t H_t) \overline{\Sigma}_t
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Students often use the standard KF equations in the EKF section. Always substitute the **Jacobians** $F_t$ and $H_t$ in place of $A_t$ and $C_t$ for the EKF.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Robot Localization**
>
> ☐ **Definition:** Estimating pose $(x, y, \theta)$ using sensors, controls, and a map.
>
> ☐ **Three Variants:** Position tracking, Global localization, Kidnapped robot problem.
>
> ☐ **Markov Assumption:** Current state summarizes all past history. **State it explicitly** in derivations.
>
> ☐ **Bayes Filter = Prediction + Correction:**
> - **Prediction:** Convolve prior belief with motion model.
> - **Correction:** Multiply by measurement likelihood and normalize.
>
> ☐ **Dead Reckoning Equations:**
> - $\Delta\theta = (\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L)/b$
> - $\Delta s = (\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L)/2$
> - **Midpoint heading** $\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2$ is critical.
> - Errors **accumulate** → use external sensors.
>
> ☐ **Kalman Filter:**
> - Belief is a **Gaussian** $N(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$.
> - **Unimodal** → cannot solve global localization.
> - Optimal for **linear-Gaussian** systems; **EKF** for nonlinear.
> - Know the **Kalman Gain** formula — examiner's favorite.
>
> ☐ **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL):**
> - Belief = set of **particles** with weights.
> - **Multi-modal** → solves global localization and (with augmentation) kidnapped robot problem.
> - **Resampling** prevents particle degeneracy.
> - **Augmented MCL** injects random particles (fraction $\alpha$) for exploration.
>
> ☐ **Sensor Models:** Wheel encoders (motion), LIDAR (range), camera (features), GPS (global).
>
> ☐ **Examiner Traps:**
> - Forgetting the **Markov assumption statement**.
> - Confusing **prediction** (motion model) with **correction** (measurement model).
> - Using $\theta_t$ instead of $\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2$ in odometry.
> - Using KF equations in the EKF section.
>
> ☐ **Key Buzzwords for KTU Answers:** Bayesian recursion, importance sampling, sensor noise covariance, process noise, innovation term, particle degeneracy, resampling, Jacobian linearization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Challenges in Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Challenges in Localization # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of mobile robotics and autonomous systems, **Localization** is defined as the process of determining the pose (position $x, y$ and orientation $\theta$) of a robot relative to a known reference frame, typically using onboard sensor measurements, prior knowledge of the environment (map), and odometry data. A **Challenge in Localization** refers to any physical, computational, perceptual, or environmental factor that introduces **uncertainty, ambiguity, or error** into the robot's estimated pose, thereby degrading the accuracy, robustness, or real-time feasibility of the localization algorithm. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECDT633 - Module 3):** > Localization is the most fundamental perceptual problem in robotics — answering *"Where am I?"* — and the challenges arise from the stochastic nature of sensors, the dynamic structure of the environment, the curse of dimensionality in state estimation, and the inherent unpredictability of real-world operating conditions. The pose of a mobile robot at time $t$ is represented as: $$ \mathbf{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix} $$ where $x_t, y_t$ are the planar coordinates and $\theta_t$ is the heading angle. > [!NOTE] > **Three Pillars of Uncertainty in Localization:** > 1. **Sensor Uncertainty** — Noise, bias, limited range/FOV of sensors. > 2. **Actuator (Odometry) Uncertainty** — Wheel slip, drift, dead reckoning errors. > 3. **Environmental Uncertainty** — Dynamic obstacles, perceptual aliasing, map incompleteness. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are **blindfolded and dropped into an unfamiliar city**, and you must always report your exact street address. You have: - A **phone GPS** (but it works poorly indoors and has ±10 m error). - A **step counter** (which drifts and accumulates error over time). - A **rough city map** (but landmarks look identical). - Your phone is sometimes disconnected. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Amnesiac Tourist:** > - You wake up in a hotel room in a new city with no memory of where you are. > - You can **see** landmarks through the window (sensor measurement) — but many streets look identical. > - You can **walk** and count your steps (odometry) — but step counting drifts. > - You need to **localize** yourself using both imperfect sources of information. > - **Challenges arise** when the landmarks repeat (perceptual aliasing), when GPS fails (sensor dropout), or when streets are crowded with moving people (dynamic environment).
