| **Sensor model fit** | Excellent for sparse features | Excellent for dense range data |
| **Path planning** | Requires roadmap extraction | Direct A* / D* on grid |
| **Computation cost** | Higher (geometric math) | Lower (lookup) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Continuous Map Building Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Suite - LIDAR, Camera, IMU] --> B[Preprocessing - Noise Filter, Calibration]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction - SIFT, Hough, Edges]
    C --> D{Map Type Selector}
    D --> E[Point Cloud Module - ICP Registration]
    D --> F[Line Feature Module - Hough Transform]
    D --> G[Polygon Module - Region Growing]
    D --> H[Landmark Module - EKF Data Association]
    E --> I[Continuous Map Database]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Frame Transform Engine - SE2 / SE3]
    J --> K[Uncertainty Propagation Module - Jacobian, GP]
    K --> L[Map Update Buffer]
    L --> M[Global Optimizer - Pose Graph SLAM]
    M --> N[Refined Continuous Map Output]
    N --> O[Downstream Consumers - Planner, Localizer, Decision]
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- The pipeline begins with **raw sensor data** (LiDAR point clouds, camera images, IMU streams).
- **Preprocessing** removes noise and aligns sensor frames.
- **Feature Extraction** identifies primitives — SIFT keypoints, Hough lines, edge points.
- The **Map Type Selector** branches into four parallel continuous map construction paths.
- All four outputs are fused in a **Continuous Map Database**.
- The **Frame Transform Engine** propagates observations from sensor frame → robot frame → world frame using $SE(2)$ or $SE(3)$ transformations.
- The **Uncertainty Propagation Module** computes Jacobians and applies Gaussian noise models.
- The **Global Optimizer** (Pose Graph SLAM / Bundle Adjustment) minimizes reprojection error across all loop closures.
- The final output is a refined, globally consistent **Continuous Map** for the planner to consume.

## 4.2 Conceptual Map of Continuous Representation Types

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[Continuous Map Representation] --> R1[Point Cloud]
    R --> R2[Line Map]
    R --> R3[Polygon Map]
    R --> R4[Landmark Map]
    R --> R5[Elevation GP Map]
    R1 --> P1[Raw LiDAR Scans]
    R1 --> P2[ICP Aligned Clouds]
    R2 --> P3[Hough Transform Output]
    R2 --> P4[RANSAC Line Fitting]
    R3 --> P5[Bounded Regions]
    R3 --> P6[Voronoi Cells]
    R4 --> P7[Sparse Visual Features]
    R4 --> P8[Beacon Observations]
    R5 --> P9[2.5D Terrain]
    R5 --> P10[3D Volumetric GP]
```

**Explanation:** This hierarchy shows the *family tree* of continuous map types. Point clouds are the most primitive (raw data), while line, polygon, and landmark maps introduce increasing semantic abstraction. Elevation maps (Gaussian Process based) are the most statistically sophisticated, providing continuous uncertainty estimates.

## 4.3 Coordinate Frame Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[World Frame W] -->|T_WR| R[Robot Frame R]
    R -->|T_RS1| S1[Sensor 1 Frame - LIDAR]
    R -->|T_RS2| S2[Sensor 2 Frame - Camera]
    S1 -->|T_S1P| P1[Point P - Obstacle]
    S2 -->|T_S2P| P1
    W -.->|T_WP via chain| P1
```

**Explanation:** A point $P$ observed by two different sensors can be transformed to the world frame via the chain rule of $SE(3)$ matrices:
$T_{WP} = T_{WR} \cdot T_{RS_i} \cdot T_{S_iP}$. This is the **fundamental equation of sensor fusion** in continuous mapping — the same world point must be reachable consistently through all sensor paths (loop closure consistency).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to draw the frame hierarchy, always label the transformation matrices ($T_{WR}$, $T_{RS}$, etc.) explicitly. Marks are awarded for *correct notation*, not just boxes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define continuous map representation. List any two continuous map types with one real-world example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Continuous map representation is a method of encoding an environment using analytical geometric primitives (points, lines, polygons, landmarks) without quantizing the workspace into a discrete grid. It preserves the true continuous geometry of the world and offers variable resolution.

**Two continuous map types:**
1. **Point Cloud Map** — e.g., LiDAR scan of a building stored as $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ coordinates.
2. **Landmark Map** — e.g., Visual SLAM uses sparse SIFT feature landmarks for autonomous drone navigation.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between metric and topological maps. Which is generally continuous?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Metric Map** | **Topological Map** |
|---|---|
| Stores actual spatial coordinates | Stores connectivity between places |
| Continuous in nature (metric) | Discrete graph structure (nodes/edges) |
| High memory cost | Compact representation |
| Example: occupancy grid, point cloud | Example: corridor graph of a building |

**Continuous:** Metric maps are generally continuous; topological maps are inherently discrete graph structures.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B**

**(a)** Explain in detail the different types of **continuous map representations** used in robotics. Compare their memory requirements, accuracy, and suitable environments. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A mobile robot is at world pose $(X, Y, \Theta) = (2.0 \text{ m}, 1.5 \text{ m}, 30°)$. Its laser range finder (mounted at sensor offset $(0.1, 0, 0)$ m in robot frame, aligned with robot heading) returns a single beam measurement $z = (r = 5.0 \text{ m}, \phi = 15°)$. Compute the **world coordinates** of the detected obstacle. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Five types of continuous maps:**

1. **Point Cloud Map** — Set of 3D points $p_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$. Memory: $O(N)$ where $N$ is the number of points. Accuracy: $\sim$mm level. Best for: raw 3D environments, outdoor terrain.

2. **Line/Feature Map** — Line segments with parameters $(\rho_j, \theta_j)$. Memory: $O(L)$ for $L$ lines. Accuracy: cm level. Best for: structured corridors, indoor offices with rectilinear walls.

3. **Polygon Map** — Closed polygons $P_k$ with $n_k$ vertices. Memory: $O(\sum n_k)$. Accuracy: high (topologically correct). Best for: floor plans, region-based path planning.

4. **Landmark Map** — Sparse distinguishable features $l_j = (x_j, y_j, s_j)$. Memory: $O(M)$, very compact. Accuracy: high when features are distinct. Best for: GPS-denied outdoor, visual SLAM.

5. **Elevation/Gaussian Process Map** — Continuous height function $h(x, y)$. Memory: $O(N^2)$ in naive form. Accuracy: highest (smooth interpolation). Best for: rough terrain, legged robots.

**Summary table (write in exam):**

| Type | Memory | Accuracy | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Point cloud | $O(N)$ | mm | 3D scanning |
| Line | $O(L)$ | cm | Indoor corridors |
| Polygon | $O(\sum n_k)$ | cm | Floor plans |
| Landmark | $O(M)$ | cm | Visual SLAM |
| GP elevation | $O(N^2)$ | mm | Terrain mapping |

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Robot pose: $X = 2.0$ m, $Y = 1.5$ m, $\Theta = 30° = \pi/6$ rad
- Sensor offset: $(x_s, y_s) = (0.1, 0)$ m, sensor aligned with robot ($\phi_s = 0$)
- Measurement: $r = 5.0$ m, $\phi = 15° = \pi/12$ rad

**Step 1 — Compute sensor origin in world frame:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Theta_s &= \Theta + \phi_s = 30° + 0° = 30° \\
x_s^{\text{world}} &= X + x_s \cos\Theta_s - y_s \sin\Theta_s = 2.0 + 0.1\cos 30° - 0 = 2.0 + 0.1(0.866) = 2.0866 \text{ m} \\
y_s^{\text{world}} &= Y + x_s \sin\Theta_s + y_s \cos\Theta_s = 1.5 + 0.1(0.5) + 0 = 1.55 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the beam direction in world frame:**

$$
\phi_{\text{world}} = \phi + \Theta_s = 15° + 30° = 45° = \pi/4 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute obstacle coordinates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{world}} &= x_s^{\text{world}} + r\cos(\phi_{\text{world}}) = 2.0866 + 5.0 \cos 45° \\
&= 2.0866 + 5.0(0.7071) = 2.0866 + 3.5355 = \mathbf{5.6221 \text{ m}} \\
y_{\text{world}} &= y_s^{\text{world}} + r\sin(\phi_{\text{world}}) = 1.55 + 5.0 \sin 45° \\
&= 1.55 + 5.0(0.7071) = 1.55 + 3.5355 = \mathbf{5.0855 \text{ m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Obstacle location in world frame $\approx (5.62, 5.09)$ m.

---

### **❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Hough Transform-based line extraction** for continuous line maps. How is the Hough parameter space discretized? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **Jacobian $J_z$** for the sensor measurement model $(r, \phi) \rightarrow (x, y)$ and explain its role in uncertainty propagation for continuous maps. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Hough Transform for Line Extraction:**

The Hough Transform detects lines by exploiting the **duality** between points in image space and sinusoidal curves in parameter space.

**Step 1 — Parameterization:** A line is represented as:

$$
x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho
$$

where $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance from origin and $\theta$ is the angle of the normal.

**Step 2 — Edge Detection:** Apply Canny/Sobel to obtain edge pixels $(x_i, y_i)$.

**Step 3 — Voting:** For each edge pixel, iterate over $\theta \in [0, \pi)$ in small steps (e.g., $1°$), compute:

$$
\rho = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta
$$

Increment the accumulator $A(\rho, \theta)$ by 1.

**Step 4 — Peak Detection:** Local maxima in $A(\rho, \theta)$ correspond to dominant lines in the scene. Threshold (e.g., 50 votes) filters weak responses.

**Diagrammatic representation:**

```
Image Space          Hough Parameter Space
   y |                  ρ
     |   *  *            |   *  
     |  *    *           |  * *
     | *  --  *   --->   | *   *  *  (sinusoidal)
     |  *    *           |  * *
     |   *  *            |   *
     |______ x           |________ θ
```

**Discretization of Hough space:** $\Delta\rho$ is chosen based on image resolution (e.g., $1$ pixel) and $\Delta\theta$ is typically $1°$ to $2°$. Total accumulator size: $\frac{\rho_{\max}}{\Delta\rho} \times \frac{\pi}{\Delta\theta}$ cells.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Derivation of Jacobian $J_z$:**

The measurement model in sensor frame is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= r\cos\phi \\
y &= r\sin\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating $(x, y)$ with respect to $(r, \phi)$:

$$
J_z = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial r} & \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \phi} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial r} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \phi} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi & -r\sin\phi \\ \sin\phi & r\cos\phi \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step-by-step:**

- $\frac{\partial x}{\partial r} = \frac{\partial}{\partial r}(r\cos\phi) = \cos\phi$
- $\frac{\partial x}{\partial \phi} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \phi}(r\cos\phi) = -r\sin\phi$
- $\frac{\partial y}{\partial r} = \frac{\partial}{\partial r}(r\sin\phi) = \sin\phi$
- $\frac{\partial y}{\partial \phi} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \phi}(r\sin\phi) = r\cos\phi$

**Role in uncertainty propagation:**

If the measurement covariance is $\Sigma_z = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_r^2 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_\phi^2 \end{bmatrix}$, then the Cartesian uncertainty is:

$$
\Sigma_{xy} = J_z \, \Sigma_z \, J_z^\top
$$

**Key engineering insight:** Notice that the variance in the $y$-direction (lateral) contains a factor of $r^2\sigma_\phi^2$, meaning **uncertainty grows quadratically with range** for angular errors. This is why long-range LiDAR requires very precise encoders and why the localization accuracy degrades with distance — a key principle in continuous map design.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Map Representation questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sensor offset** — Students often assume the sensor is at the robot origin $(0, 0)$ when the problem states a non-zero offset. Always add the offset before applying the beam rotation. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Angle mode confusion** — Mixing degrees and radians. The formulas require *consistent units*. In the KTU paper, if the problem states "30°", use $30°$ in $\cos/\sin$ (calculator in degree mode), NOT $\pi/6$. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Hough parameter $\rho$ sign** — In the standard Hough convention, $\rho \geq 0$ and $\theta \in [0, \pi)$. Some students use $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ which is technically valid but loses the uniqueness property. **(−0.5 mark)**
>
> 4. **Forgetting Jacobian transpose** — In EKF covariance update, it is $J_z \Sigma_z J_z^\top$, NOT $J_z \Sigma_z J_z$. The transpose is on the *second* factor. **(−1.5 marks)**
>
> 5. **Confusing map types** — Writing "occupancy grid" as a continuous representation. Occupancy grids are inherently **discrete**. Continuous alternatives include Gaussian Process elevation maps or signed distance fields. **(−1 mark)**

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Map Representation: Continuous Representations**

- ✅ A **continuous map** preserves the exact geometry of the environment using analytical primitives; **no grid quantization**.
- ✅ The **five main types** are: **Point Cloud, Line (Hough), Polygon, Landmark, and Gaussian Process (elevation)** maps.
- ✅ **Metric maps** store coordinates; **topological maps** store connectivity. **Hybrid maps** combine both.
- ✅ Coordinate transformation follows the **chain rule**: $T_{WP} = T_{WR} \cdot T_{RS} \cdot T_{SP}$, using **homogeneous $SE(2)$ or $SE(3)$ matrices**.
- ✅ The **inverse sensor model** $p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$ updates cell probabilities, but in continuous maps we use the **forward sensor model** and feature matching.
- ✅ **Hough line equation**: $x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta = \rho$, with $\rho \geq 0$ and $\theta \in [0, \pi)$.
- ✅ The **Jacobian** for range-bearing → Cartesian is $J_z = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi & -r\sin\phi \\ \sin\phi & r\cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$, used in EKF-SLAM covariance propagation.
- ✅ **Quantization error** in a grid of cell size $\Delta$ is $\frac{\Delta}{2}$; continuous maps have $\approx 0$ such error.
- ✅ **MSE** = $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(m_i - \hat{m}_i)^2$ is the standard map quality metric.
- ✅ Continuous maps are **preferred** for: high-precision tasks, large-scale outdoor environments, structured indoor corridors, and rough terrain.
- ✅ **Pose Graph SLAM** globally refines continuous maps by minimizing reprojection error across loop closures.
- ✅ Memory ordering: Landmark ($O(M)$) < Line ($O(L)$) < Point Cloud ($O(N)$) < Polygon ($O(\sum n_k)$) < GP ($O(N^2)$ naive).
- ✅ **KLU pitfall to avoid:** Never call an occupancy grid "continuous" — it is fundamentally **discrete**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decomposition Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Decomposition Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decomposition Strategies** in robotics and automation refer to the systematic methodologies used to break down a complex, high-level robotic task into a hierarchy of simpler, well-defined, and executable sub-tasks, primitives, or motion commands that can be handled by lower-level controllers, actuators, and planning algorithms.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, decomposition strategies form the bridge between **task-level planning** (what needs to be done) and **motion-level execution** (how the joints and end-effectors actually move). They allow a robot controller to translate a goal such as *"assemble the gear onto the shaft"* into a sequence of measurable geometric, temporal, and logical steps.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Master Chef's Recipe**
> Imagine a customer orders *"Biriyani"*. The chef does not hand the cook a single instruction. Instead, the recipe is **decomposed**:
> - **Strategic Layer (Task):** Cook Biriyani
> - **Tactical Layer (Sub-tasks):** Prepare rice, prepare masala, fry onions, mix and steam
> - **Operational Layer (Primitives):** Chop 500g onions, heat oil to 180 °C, stir for 4 minutes
>
> Each layer is *atomic* (cannot be broken further meaningfully) and *executable* by a specific person or machine.

A robotic system works the same way. The decomposition hierarchy is:

$$
\text{High-Level Task} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Sub-tasks} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Primitives} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Joint/Actuator Commands}
$$

### Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Task** | High-level objective (e.g., "pick and place") |
| **Sub-task** | A smaller logical chunk of a task |
| **Primitive** | Atomic, executable motion or action |
| **Precondition** | Condition that must be true before a primitive executes |
| **Postcondition / Effect** | World state after the primitive executes |
| **AND-decomposition** | All sub-tasks must be performed (conjunction) |
| **OR-decomposition** | Any one of the alternative sub-tasks can be performed (disjunction) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A primitive is the *smallest indivisible unit* of action in a robotic plan. It is typically characterized by **one dominant control mode** (position, force, velocity, or compliant motion).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Decomposition is Needed

Modern robotic cells must handle tasks that are:

1. **Geometrically complex** — multiple links, multiple obstacles, 6-DOF end-effector poses.
2. **Logically composite** — actions depend on sensor feedback and previous outcomes.
3. **Computationally intractable** if treated as a single monolithic planning problem (the configuration space $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ becomes too large).

Decomposition reduces computational complexity from **exponential** in the worst case to often **polynomial** when the task has a natural hierarchical or modular structure.

### 2.2 Major Decomposition Strategies

#### (a) Hierarchical (Top-Down) Decomposition

- The task $T$ is recursively split into sub-tasks $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k$ until each leaf is a primitive.
- Implemented through **Hierarchical Task Networks (HTN)** or **Structured Task Graphs**.
- The decomposition tree satisfies:

$$
T \;\rightarrow\; \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Used extensively in **manufacturing automation** (e.g., assembly line programming) and **ROS Behavior Trees**.

#### (b) AND/OR Graph Decomposition

- Each non-primitive node $N$ is either an **AND-node** (all children must succeed) or an **OR-node** (any child may succeed).
- A solution is a subgraph containing exactly one expansion for every AND-node and at least one valid expansion for every OR-node.
- Search algorithms: **AO\***, **Recursive AND/OR search**.

**Logical representation:**

$$
\text{AND}(N) = N_1 \wedge N_2 \wedge \ldots \wedge N_k
$$

$$
\text{OR}(N) = N_1 \vee N_2 \vee \ldots \vee N_k
$$

#### (c) Functional (Module-Based) Decomposition

- The robot work-cell is divided into functional modules: *perception*, *planning*, *manipulation*, *gripping*, *inspection*.
- Each module has well-defined **Inputs**, **Outputs**, and **Interfaces**.
- This is the foundation of the **Three-Tier Robot Architecture**:
  - **Deliberative Layer** (Planner)
  - **Executive Layer** (Sequencer)
  - **Reactive Layer** (Controller)

#### (d) Spatial / Cell Decomposition

- The free workspace $\mathcal{W}_{free}$ is partitioned into a set of convex polygonal (2D) or polyhedral (3D) cells $\{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n\}$.
- Adjacency graph $G = (V, E)$ where vertices are cells and edges exist between cells sharing a boundary that the robot can cross.
- **Exact Cell Decomposition** (e.g., trapezoidal decomposition) gives complete planning guarantees.
- **Approximate Cell Decomposition** (e.g., quadtree/octree) trades completeness for memory efficiency.

**Adjacency rule for an $n$-cell decomposition:**

$$
E = \{ (C_i, C_j) \;\vert\; C_i \cap C_j \neq \emptyset,\; C_i, C_j \in \mathcal{W}_{free} \}
$$

#### (e) Temporal Decomposition

- Long-duration tasks are split along the time axis into phases: *approach*, *contact*, *operation*, *retract*.
- Each phase has its own **controller gains**, **reference trajectories**, and **force/impedance profiles**.
- Critical for **force-controlled assembly** and **welding** tasks.

#### (f) Probabilistic / Sampling-Based Decomposition

- The configuration space is not partitioned explicitly. Instead, random samples are connected into a graph.
- Examples: **PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap)**, **RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree)**.
- Planning is decomposed into *learning phase* (sampling) and *query phase* (graph search).

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AND-node size | $\;\vert \text{AND}(N) \vert = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \vert N_i \vert\;$ | Multiplicative complexity |
| 2 | OR-node size | $\;\vert \text{OR}(N) \vert = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert N_i \vert\;$ | Additive complexity |
| 3 | Cell adjacency | $E = \{(C_i,C_j) \mid C_i \cap C_j \neq \emptyset\}$ | Graph edges |
| 4 | Workspace fraction | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Vol}(\mathcal{W}_{free})}{\text{Vol}(\mathcal{W}_{total})}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | PRM connectivity | $k$-nearest neighbours with $k \in [15, 30]$ typical | Sampling parameter |
| 6 | HTN depth | $d = $ number of levels in task tree | Integer |
| 7 | Primitive cost | $J_p = w_1 t_p + w_2 E_p + w_3 \epsilon_p$ | Weighted sum, $w_i \ge 0$ |
| 8 | Decomposition ratio | $r_d = \dfrac{\#\text{primitives}}{\#\text{high-level tasks}}$ | $\ge 1$ always |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** AND/OR decomposition is the theoretical backbone of classical AI planning systems (STRIPS, PDDL). HTN decomposition is the workhorse of NASA's **DS-1 Remote Agent** and modern autonomous space robotics.

### 2.4 Selection Criterion — Which Strategy to Use?

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner's Favourite):**
> - **Geometrically dominated tasks** (path planning) → *Spatial / Cell / PRM decomposition*
> - **Logically dominated tasks** (assembly sequences) → *AND/OR / HTN decomposition*
> - **Time-critical tasks** (welding, deburring) → *Temporal decomposition*
> - **Large-scale automation cells** → *Functional + Hierarchical decomposition*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Complexity Reduction by Hierarchical Decomposition

Consider a planning problem with branching factor $b$ and depth $d$ when treated monolithically. The state space size is:

$$
S_{\text{mono}} = b^{d}
$$

Suppose the decomposition splits the depth $d$ into $L$ independent levels, each with branching factors $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_L$ such that $b_1 \cdot b_2 \cdots b_L \ll b^{d}$ at each level. We proceed step by step.

**Step 1 — Express monolithic complexity**

$$
S_{\text{mono}} = b^{d}
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose depth**

Let $d = d_1 + d_2 + \ldots + d_L$. The independent search at each level gives:

$$
S_{\text{dec}} = \sum_{i=1}^{L} b_i^{d_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply AM-GM inequality**

For fixed $b$ and $d$, the sum of powers is bounded by:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{L} b_i^{d_i} \;\le\; L \cdot b^{d/L}
$$

This follows from the concavity of $x \mapsto b^{x}$ for $b > 1$.

**Step 4 — Take the ratio**

$$
\frac{S_{\text{dec}}}{S_{\text{mono}}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{L} b_i^{d_i}}{b^{d}} \;\le\; \frac{L \cdot b^{d/L}}{b^{d}} = L \cdot b^{d(1/L - 1)}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

Since $\frac{1}{L} - 1 < 0$ for $L \ge 2$, the exponent is negative, so:

$$
\frac{S_{\text{dec}}}{S_{\text{mono}}} \;\rightarrow\; 0 \quad \text{as} \quad d \rightarrow \infty
$$

Thus, hierarchical decomposition provides an **exponential speed-up** in the worst case.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Decomposition Strategies

> The following table is the **single most important comparative matrix** for KTU ESE answers on this topic.

| Strategy | Decomposes By | Best Suited For | Completeness | Optimality | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hierarchical (HTN)** | Task structure | Assembly, missions | Yes (if methods complete) | Heuristic | Low–Medium |
| **AND/OR** | Logical alternatives | Classical planning, fault recovery | Yes (with AO*) | Optimal cost | Exponential worst case |
| **Functional / Modular** | System role | Software architecture | N/A (design) | N/A | N/A |
| **Exact Cell** | Workspace geometry | 2D/3D path planning | Yes (resolution-complete) | Yes (in cells) | $O(n \log n)$ for $n$ cells |
| **Approximate Cell (Quadtree)** | Workspace geometry | Large maps | Resolution-complete | Near-optimal | $O(n)$ memory |
| **Temporal** | Time / phase | Force control, welding | N/A | Phase-optimal | Linear in phases |
| **PRM (Sampling)** | Random samples | High-DOF manipulators | Probabilistically complete | Not optimal | $O(n \log n)$ build |
| **RRT (Sampling)** | Random samples | Dynamic / cluttered env. | Probabilistically complete | Not optimal | $O(n)$ online |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Decomposing an Assembly Task

**Task:** Insert a peg into a hole using a 6-DOF robot arm.

**Step 1 — Hierarchical decomposition**

$$
T_{\text{insert}} \;\rightarrow\; \{ T_{\text{approach}},\; T_{\text{align}},\; T_{\text{contact}},\; T_{\text{insert}},\; T_{\text{retract}} \}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify pre/postconditions**

For the **contact** primitive:

$$
\text{Pre: } \|p_{\text{peg}} - p_{\text{hole}}\|_2 < \delta, \quad \theta_{\text{peg}} = \theta_{\text{hole}} \pm \epsilon
$$

$$
\text{Post: } F_{\text{contact}} = F_{\text{threshold}} \;\wedge\; \text{peg} \in \text{contact region}
$$

**Step 3 — Map to controller modes**

| Sub-task | Control Mode | Sensor Required |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Position control | Vision / encoder |
| Align | Vision-guided position | Camera |
| Contact | Hybrid force/position | Force/torque sensor |
| Insert | Impedance control | Force/torque sensor |
| Retract | Position control | Encoder |

**Step 4 — Validate decomposition**

Check that the union of preconditions of $T_{i+1}$ is satisfied by the postcondition of $T_i$:

$$
\text{Post}(T_i) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Pre}(T_{i+1}) \quad \forall i
$$

If any link is broken, the planner must insert a *recovery* sub-task — a clear demonstration of **OR-decomposition** at that point.

### 3.4 Algorithm Outline — Building an AND/OR Decomposition

The following describes the *classical recursive expansion* used in automated planners such as **STRIPS** and **PDDL planners** (no code, just a formal procedure).

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Algorithm: } \text{Expand}(N, \mathcal{M}) \\
&1.\; \text{If } N \text{ is a primitive, return } N. \\
&2.\; \text{Choose a method } m \in \mathcal{M}(N). \\
&3.\; \text{If } m \text{ is AND, expand all children } N_1 \wedge \ldots \wedge N_k. \\
&4.\; \text{If } m \text{ is OR, generate alternatives } N_1 \vee \ldots \vee N_k. \\
&5.\; \text{Recursively call Expand on every non-primitive child.} \\
&6.\; \text{Verify preconditions of each primitive against current world model.} \\
&7.\; \text{Return the expanded tree / graph.}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Task Decomposition (HTN) Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootT["Task Assemble Product"] --> sT1["Sub-task Mount Gear"]
    rootT --> sT2["Sub-task Tighten Bolt"]
    rootT --> sT3["Sub-task Inspect"]
    sT1 --> pT1["Primitive Approach"]
    sT1 --> pT2["Primitive Align"]
    sT1 --> pT3["Primitive Insert"]
    sT2 --> pT4["Primitive Reach Wrench"]
    sT2 --> pT5["Primitive Apply Torque"]
    sT3 --> pT6["Primitive Scan with Camera"]
    sT3 --> pT7["Primitive Compare with Spec"]
```

    nodeA["AND Task: Complete Assembly"]
    nodeA --> nodeA1["Sub-task Pick Gear"]

```

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** A well-drawn decomposition diagram in your answer script can fetch **1–2 extra marks** under the *"presentation and clarity"* head, even if the textual content is average.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]
Define **decomposition strategy** in the context of robotic task planning. Mention **two** advantages.

**Model Answer:**
A decomposition strategy is a systematic method of breaking down a complex robotic task into a hierarchy of smaller, well-defined sub-tasks and atomic primitives that can be individually planned, scheduled, and executed by lower-level controllers.

*Advantages:*

1. Reduces computational complexity by isolating sub-problems that can be solved independently.
2. Enables modularity — each sub-task can be reused, tested, and verified separately, improving reliability and maintainability of the robotic cell.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two distinct advantages. Avoid generic "simpler" answers.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]
Differentiate between **AND-decomposition** and **OR-decomposition** with one robotics example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | AND-Decomposition | OR-Decomposition |
|---|---|---|
| Logical operator | Conjunction ($\wedge$) | Disjunction ($\vee$) |
| Execution | All children must succeed | Any one alternative suffices |
| Search complexity | $\prod \vert N_i \vert$ | $\sum \vert N_i \vert$ |
| Example | "Pick" **and** "Place" must both occur to complete a pick-and-place task | "Insert peg" can be done either by *vertical push* **or** *tilted slide* |

*Example explanation:* In a pick-and-place task, both the *grasp* and the *transport* must be executed in sequence — this is an AND-decomposition. In contrast, when inserting a peg, the robot may choose between a vertical force-insert OR a tilted compliant-insert — this is an OR-decomposition.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

**Q3.A.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Hierarchical Task Network (HTN)** decomposition strategy with a neat block diagram. Discuss how it differs from flat task planning.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a robotic *gear-shaft assembly* task, draw the AND/OR decomposition graph and identify:
- (i) All AND-nodes
- (ii) All OR-nodes
- (iii) The role of preconditions and postconditions at each primitive level.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HTN Decomposition (7 marks)**

HTN planning decomposes a high-level task into subtasks by recursively applying *methods*. Each method $m$ for a task $T$ is a prescription:

$$
m : T \;\rightarrow\; (T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k) \quad \text{with preconditions} \;\; \text{Pre}(m)
$$

- The planner maintains a **task network** $(T, \prec, \mathcal{V})$ where $\prec$ denotes ordering and $\mathcal{V}$ denotes variable bindings.
- Decomposition proceeds **top-down** until every task in the network is a primitive (executable by the controller).
- **Difference from flat planning:**
  - Flat planning searches a single large state-space.
  - HTN uses *domain-specific knowledge* encoded in methods, dramatically pruning the search.
  - HTN is **task-oriented**, not just **goal-oriented**.

*A simple text-based block diagram (to be drawn in exam):*

```
        High-Level Task T
              |
        +-----+-----+
        |           |
   Sub-task T1   Sub-task T2
        |           |
   +----+----+   Primitive P2
   |         |
Primitive P1 Sub-task T3
              |
         Primitive P4
```

**(b) AND/OR Graph for Gear-Shaft Assembly (7 marks)**

**Task:** Assemble gear onto a vertical shaft.

```
                [AND] Complete Assembly
                /          |            \
       [AND] Pick       [OR] Align    [AND] Press-fit
       /      \         /   |   \       /        \
   Reach   Grasp    Vision  IR   Force   Push     Verify
   (Prim)  (Prim)   (Prim)(Prim)(Prim)  (Prim)   (Prim)
```

**Identification:**

- **(i) AND-nodes:** *Complete Assembly*, *Pick*, *Press-fit* — all their children must execute in order.
- **(ii) OR-nodes:** *Align* — vision, IR, or force-based alignment may be used depending on sensor availability.
- **(iii) Pre/Postconditions:**

| Primitive | Precondition | Postcondition |
|---|---|---|
| Reach | Gripper empty | Gripper above gear |
| Grasp | Gripper above gear, gear stationary | Gear held, force $F \ge F_{\min}$ |
| Vision-align | Shaft in FOV, gear grasped | $\vert \theta_e \vert < 1^\circ$ |
| Push | Aligned, contact detected | Insertion depth $d \ge d_{\text{spec}}$ |
| Verify | Insertion done | $\Delta d_{\text{measured}} = d_{\text{spec}}$ |

The OR-node *Align* allows the system to **recover** if vision fails by switching to force-based alignment — a classic example of fault-tolerant decomposition.

---

**Q3.B.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks — *Alternative Choice*]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe **spatial / cell decomposition** as a motion-planning strategy. Derive the adjacency rule and explain with a sketch how the planner finds a path from start cell to goal cell.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare **PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap)** decomposition with **exact cell decomposition** under the heads: (i) completeness, (ii) memory requirement, (iii) suitability for dynamic environments, (iv) path optimality. Conclude with a recommendation for a 6-DOF manipulator operating in a cluttered bin-picking cell.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Spatial / Cell Decomposition (7 marks)**

**Definition:** The free workspace $\mathcal{W}_{free} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ (or $\mathbb{R}^3$) is partitioned into a finite set of convex cells $\{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n\}$ such that:

$$
\mathcal{W}_{free} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} C_i, \quad C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \text{ for } i \neq j
$$

**Trapezoidal decomposition** (2D) extends a vertical line from each obstacle vertex; the resulting vertical strips are then split at obstacle boundaries into trapezoids. Each trapezoid is a cell.

**Adjacency rule derivation:**

Two cells $C_i$ and $C_j$ are *adjacent* if their boundaries share a segment that the robot can physically cross without intersecting an obstacle:

$$
E = \{ (C_i, C_j) \;\vert\; \partial C_i \cap \partial C_j \neq \emptyset,\; \text{segment} \subset \mathcal{W}_{free} \}
$$

The planner:
1. Identifies the cells containing the start $q_s$ and goal $q_g$.
2. Builds the adjacency graph $G = (V, E)$.
3. Runs **A\*** or **Dijkstra** on $G$ to find a path $C_{s} = C_{i_0}, C_{i_1}, \ldots, C_{i_m} = C_g$.
4. Within each cell, the robot moves in a straight line (since the cell is convex).

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):**

A workspace with one rectangular obstacle is split into 5 trapezoidal cells. Edges connect adjacent cells through vertical strips. The start cell and goal cell are highlighted, and a thick path of 3 cells is shown going around the obstacle.

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks)**

| Head | PRM | Exact Cell Decomposition |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Completeness | Probabilistically complete (as $n \to \infty$) | Resolution-complete (complete in the limit of infinite resolution) |
| (ii) Memory | $O(n)$ for $n$ samples; edges typically $k$-NN with $k = 15$–$30$ | $O(n)$ cells, but $n$ grows exponentially with dimension |
| (iii) Dynamic env. | Re-planning needed; better for static, repeatable queries | Static only; any change forces full re-decomposition |
| (iv) Path optimality | Sub-optimal (depends on sampling density) | Optimal *within* the chosen cell resolution |

**Recommendation for 6-DOF bin-picking:**

For a 6-DOF manipulator in a cluttered bin-picking cell, **PRM is recommended** because:
- Exact cell decomposition in $\mathbb{R}^6$ (configuration space) suffers from the *curse of dimensionality* — cell count explodes.
- Bin-picking is a *multi-query* task (the same workspace is used thousands of times), so investing in building a roadmap once is highly economical.
- The cluttered environment has many narrow passages where PRM's sampling-based nature outperforms deterministic grid methods.
- A hybrid **RRT-Connect** variant can be added for online single-query refinement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating decomposition with sequencing.** Decomposition is *what* and *how to break* a task; sequencing is *the order*. Many students mix the two and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing "primitive" with "joint command".** A primitive is a *symbolic action* (e.g., "grasp"); a joint command is the *numeric trajectory* (e.g., $q_1(t) = 0.5 + 0.3t$). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Omitting preconditions/postconditions** in AND/OR diagrams. This is worth at least **1 mark** in any 7-mark sub-question.
> 4. **Stating that PRM is "complete".** It is *probabilistically complete*, not complete. A direct -1 mark penalty is common.
> 5. **Drawing AND/OR graphs without labelling AND vs OR nodes** with brackets. The examiner cannot award graph marks if the node types are ambiguous.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Decomposition Strategies):**

- ☐ **Decomposition = breaking a complex robotic task** into smaller, executable sub-tasks and primitives.
- ☐ **Three layers of any robotic system:** Deliberative (planner), Executive (sequencer), Reactive (controller).
- ☐ **Hierarchical / HTN decomposition** is top-down and uses *methods* with preconditions.
- ☐ **AND-nodes** require **all** children to succeed; **OR-nodes** require **at least one** alternative to succeed.
- ☐ **Cell decomposition** partitions $\mathcal{W}_{free}$ into convex cells; adjacency forms a graph.
- ☐ **PRM** = sampling-based, probabilistically complete, ideal for high-DOF static multi-query planning.
- ☐ **RRT** = sampling-based, ideal for single-query online / dynamic planning.
- ☐ **Temporal decomposition** is essential whenever the control law (position / force / impedance) changes with time.
- ☐ Every **primitive** must have explicit **preconditions** and **postconditions** for the planner to verify safety.
- ☐ A correct decomposition satisfies: $\text{Post}(T_i) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Pre}(T_{i+1})$ for every consecutive pair.
- ☐ Functional decomposition leads to **modular software** — the foundation of ROS, OROCOS, and modern robot frameworks.
- ☐ **Complexity reduction:** monolithic $b^d$ vs decomposed $\sum b_i^{d_i}$ — exponential speed-up is possible.
- ☐ KTU favourite: a comparative table between two strategies is worth **5–7 marks** almost by itself.
- ☐ Always **label AND/OR nodes explicitly** in diagrams; ambiguity costs marks.
- ☐ The "**curse of dimensionality**" disqualifies exact cell decomposition in $\mathbb{R}^{\ge 4}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current Challenges in Map Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Current Challenges in Map Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Map Representation** in mobile robotics refers to the formal mathematical encoding of an environment's spatial structure using data acquired from onboard sensors (LIDAR, cameras, ultrasonic, IMU, wheel odometry, etc.). Under the KTU 2024 Scheme outcomes for PECDT633, the *current challenges* concern the open research problems that arise when these representations are pushed beyond controlled laboratory conditions into real-world deployment.

A robot map $M$ is a data structure that satisfies the fundamental relationship:

$$M = f(\{z_t, u_t\}_{t=0}^{T})$$

where $z_t$ is the sensor observation at time $t$, $u_t$ is the control input (odometry), and $f(\cdot)$ is the mapping function. The current challenges in the field revolve around making $f(\cdot)$ **scalable**, **robust**, **semantically rich**, and **computationally tractable** under uncertainty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECDT633 Module 3):**
> Students must be able to *identify, compare, and justify* the limitations of classical map representations (occupancy grids, feature maps, topological graphs) when deployed in unstructured, dynamic, large-scale, or multi-robot scenarios. The module explicitly lists "Current Challenges" as a high-weightage topic for both Continuous Evaluation (CE) and End Semester Examinations (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blind Tourist

Imagine you are a **blind tourist** dropped into an unfamiliar city with only a cane (your sensor) and a notebook (your map). Every time you tap the cane, you write down what you sense — the distance to a wall, the texture of the ground, the sound of traffic. Your notebook is your *map representation*.

Now consider the challenges:
- Your cane **misses thin objects** (sensor noise / occlusions).
- People and cars keep **moving around you** (dynamic environments).
- The city is **enormous** (large-scale mapping).
- You may visit a place you have already been to, but you **fail to recognize it** (loop closure failure).
- You have only **limited notebook pages** (memory constraints).
- Another blind tourist may help, but **merging your notes is messy** (multi-robot map fusion).

The "current challenges in map representation" are exactly these real-world stressors that classical map frameworks were not originally designed to handle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics Used in Map Quality Evaluation (KTU Expected Terminology):**
> - **Map Accuracy** — measured in **meters (m)** or **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)** between estimated and ground-truth landmarks.
> - **Map Consistency** — dimensionless ratio (ideally → 1.0) of cycle-closure error to path length.
> - **Update Rate / Computational Cost** — measured in **Hertz (Hz)** or **Floating-Point Operations per Second (FLOPS)**.
> - **Memory Footprint** — measured in **Megabytes (MB)** or **bytes/cell** for grid maps.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Current Challenges

The open problems in robotic map representation can be grouped into **seven principal axes**. Each is described below with its underlying theoretical cause and engineering consequence.

#### 2.1.1 The Dynamic Environment Problem
Classical occupancy grid maps assume the world is *static*. A cell is either occupied or free at a given instant. In reality, pedestrians, vehicles, doors opening, and furniture being moved violate this assumption.

The probability update for a static cell is:

$$P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \left[1 + \frac{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)} \cdot \frac{1 - P(m_i)}{P(m_i)} \cdot \frac{1}{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}\right]^{-1}$$

When cells flip between occupied/free, this Bayesian recursion accumulates contradictory evidence, producing **ghost maps** (persistent false obstacles) or **erased walls** (real obstacles filtered out as noise).

#### 2.1.2 The Data Association Problem
When a new observation must be matched to an existing landmark or grid cell, errors compound. The Joint Compatibility Branch and Bound (JCBB) test is often cited:

$$D^2 = \nu_j^T \, S_j^{-1} \, \nu_j \;\leq\; \chi^2_{d,\, 0.95}$$

where $\nu_j$ is the innovation vector and $S_j$ is its covariance. As the number of landmarks $n$ grows, the number of candidate associations grows combinatorially ($O(n^k)$ for $k$ new features per scan), making real-time association intractable — this is the **computational complexity of data association**.

#### 2.1.3 The Loop Closure Problem
A loop closure occurs when the robot re-observes a previously mapped region. Theoretically, this corrects accumulated drift. Practically, **perceptual aliasing** (different places looking identical) causes *false positive* loop closures, catastrophically corrupting the map. Modern solutions use bag-of-words (e.g., DBoW2) and deep learned descriptors, but place recognition under **perceptual variability** (lighting, season, viewpoint) remains unsolved.

#### 2.1.4 The Scale & Memory Problem
A high-resolution 2D grid covering a $100\,\text{m} \times 100\,\text{m}$ area at $5\,\text{cm}$ resolution requires:

$$N_{\text{cells}} = \frac{100\,\text{m}}{0.05\,\text{m}} \times \frac{100\,\text{m}}{0.05\,\text{m}} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ cells}$$

For 3D voxel grids at $10\,\text{cm}$ resolution of a building, $N_{\text{voxels}}$ easily exceeds $10^8$ — exceeding RAM budgets of small embedded robots. The challenge is **sub-linear map storage** (e.g., octrees, hierarchical hashing).

#### 2.1.5 The Uncertainty Propagation Problem
Every sensor reading carries covariance $\Sigma_{sensor}$. When fused into the map via EKF or GraphSLAM, the posterior covariance of every map landmark grows with path length $\ell$ approximately as:

$$\sigma_{\text{pos}} \propto \sqrt{\ell} \quad \text{(diffusive drift)}$$

without loop closure. The challenge is **probabilistic consistency** — guaranteeing the map's covariance estimates actually bound the true error.

#### 2.1.6 The Multi-Robot Map Fusion Problem
When $R$ robots each build a local map $\{M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_R\}$, fusing them requires solving a global alignment (registration) problem. The transformation $T_{ij} \in SE(2)$ or $SE(3)$ between robot $i$ and robot $j$ must be estimated without prior knowledge — a chicken-and-egg problem with **unknown initial relative pose**.

#### 2.1.7 The Semantic Gap Problem
Metric maps store geometry; they do not know that a "rectangular region on a table" is a *cup*. Modern research pushes toward **semantic maps** that bind object labels to geometry. The challenge is *open-vocabulary recognition* and *temporal persistence* of semantics.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Expression | Key Variable | Typical Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bayesian Occupancy Update | $P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$ | $m_i$ — grid cell | dimensionless $[0,1]$ | Core of grid mapping (Moravec & Elfes, 1985) |
| Mahalanobis Innovation | $D^2 = \nu^T S^{-1} \nu$ | $S$ — innovation covariance | dimensionless | Data association gating |
| Chi-Square Gate | $D^2 \leq \chi^2_{d, 0.95}$ | $d$ — DOF | dimensionless | Hypothesis acceptance threshold |
| EKF Covariance Propagation | $P_{t+1} = F_t P_t F_t^T + Q_t$ | $Q$ — process noise | varies | Online SLAM update |
| GraphSLAM Information Form | $\Omega = \sum_{(i,j) \in E} H_{ij}^T \, \Sigma_{ij}^{-1} \, H_{ij}$ | $\Omega$ — information matrix | varies | Globally consistent batch SLAM |
| Diffusive Drift Bound | $\sigma_{\text{pos}} \approx k \sqrt{\ell}$ | $\ell$ — path length | $\text{m}^{1/2}$ | Predictable odometry error growth |
| Grid Cell Count | $N_{\text{cells}} = (L/r)^d$ | $r$ — resolution, $d$ — dim | cells | Memory budgeting |
| ICP Alignment Error | $E(T) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \Vert p_k - T \, q_k \Vert^2$ | $T \in SE(3)$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Multi-robot map registration |
| Octree Memory Scaling | $N_{\text{nodes}} = O(n \log(L/r_{\min}))$ | $n$ — occupied cells | nodes | Sub-linear large-scale storage |
| SLAM Computational Bound | $O(n^2)$ to $O(n)$ (with sparsity) | $n$ — landmarks | ops | Algorithm scalability assessment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Answer Scripts:** When writing any Mahalanobis or chi-square expression in an examination, always state the **degrees of freedom $d$** explicitly. Examiners award marks for this identification.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These challenges are not academic curiosities — they directly govern the deployment of:
- **Autonomous Vehicles** (Waymo, Tesla): dynamic environment handling and HD map freshness.
- **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva, Locus Robotics): multi-robot SLAM and loop closure in repetitive aisles.
- **Search & Rescue Robots** (DARPA SubT): semantic mapping in collapsed, GPS-denied environments.
- **Domestic Service Robots** (iRobot, SoftBank Pepper): long-term autonomy and dynamic household maps.
- **Drone Swarms** (Skydio, DJI): decentralized map merging under bandwidth constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Diffusive Drift Bound for Odometry Error

This derivation is a KTU-favourite because it ties sensor noise theory directly to map representation. The objective is to derive the well-known square-root growth law of position uncertainty with travel distance.

**Starting Premise:**
A 2D mobile robot uses wheel encoders with incremental displacement noise. Each incremental motion $\delta s$ has independent Gaussian noise with standard deviation $\sigma_s$ in translation and $\sigma_\theta$ in rotation.

#### Step 1 — Express Position Error Per Step

After $N$ independent steps of nominal length $\Delta$, the total path length is:

$$\ell = N \cdot \Delta$$

The variance in displacement of *one* step, due to translation noise, is:

$$\sigma_{\Delta}^2 = \sigma_s^2 \cdot \Delta^2 \quad \text{(dimensionally } \text{m}^2\text{)}$$

Wait — let us be more careful. The encoder reports distance $s$ with absolute error $\sigma_s$ per meter traveled. Therefore per-step translation variance scales linearly with step length:

$$\text{Var}(\delta x_k) = \sigma_s^2 \cdot \Delta$$

#### Step 2 — Propagate Over N Independent Steps

Because each step is independent (odometry errors do not correlate step-to-step), variances add:

$$\sigma_{\text{total}}^2 = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \text{Var}(\delta x_k) = N \cdot \sigma_s^2 \cdot \Delta$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute $N = \ell / \Delta$

$$\sigma_{\text{total}}^2 = \frac{\ell}{\Delta} \cdot \sigma_s^2 \cdot \Delta = \sigma_s^2 \cdot \ell$$

#### Step 4 — Take the Square Root

$$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_s \sqrt{\ell}$$

This is the **diffusive drift bound**. Position uncertainty grows as the square root of path length, not linearly — a critical fact for map representation, because it means an unconstrained open-loop trajectory will eventually lose all metric accuracy without external corrections (loop closures, GPS, or landmarks).

> **Engineering Interpretation:** If $\sigma_s = 0.02$ (2% encoder error) and $\ell = 1000\,\text{m}$, then $\sigma_{\text{total}} = 0.02 \times \sqrt{1000} \approx 0.63\,\text{m}$. After 1 km, the robot's *belief* of where it is may be wrong by ~63 cm — large enough to corrupt a 5 cm grid map entirely.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Matching Challenges to Map Types

| Challenge ↓ \ Map Type → | Occupancy Grid | Feature Map (EKF) | Topological Graph | Hybrid (Metric + Topological) | Semantic / Neural Map |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic Environments | **Fails** (ghosts) | **Fails** (false features) | Partial (node-level) | Better with temporal layer | **Best** (object-level) |
| Loop Closure | Manual | Manual JCBB | Natural | **Strong** | Learned (deep) |
| Memory Scalability | **Poor** ($L/r)^d$ | Excellent | **Best** | Good | Moderate |
| Data Association | N/A | **Bottleneck** $O(n^k)$ | Easy | Moderate | End-to-end learned |
| Uncertainty Quantification | Per-cell prob | Covariance $P$ | Weak | Mixed | Emerging |
| Multi-Robot Fusion | ICP-based | Feature matching | Graph matching | **Strong** | **Research frontier** |
| Semantic Reasoning | None | None | Weak (node labels) | Moderate | **Native** |
| Real-time Computability | Yes (with octree) | Yes (for small $n$) | **Yes** | Moderate | GPU-dependent |

### 3.3 Derivation: Octree Memory Scaling for Large-Scale Maps

A full 3D voxel grid of volume $L^3$ at resolution $r$ requires:

$$N_{\text{voxels,full}} = \left(\frac{L}{r}\right)^3$$

A standard octree only subdivides a node when it contains both occupied *and* free volume. For sparse environments (e.g., indoor rooms where most cells are free), let $n$ be the number of occupied voxels. Each occupied voxel requires at most:

$$\log_2\left(\frac{L}{r_{\min}}\right) \quad \text{internal nodes to reach it from the root}$$

Therefore total octree node count is:

$$N_{\text{octree}} = O\!\left(n \cdot \log_2\frac{L}{r_{\min}}\right)$$

For a $50\,\text{m}$ building at $5\,\text{cm}$ resolution with $n = 10^5$ occupied cells:

$$N_{\text{octree}} \approx 10^5 \times \log_2(1000) \approx 10^5 \times 10 = 10^6 \text{ nodes}$$

Compared to full voxel count:

$$N_{\text{voxels,full}} = (50/0.05)^3 = 10^3 \times 10^3 \times 10^3 = 10^9 \text{ voxels}$$

A **1000× memory reduction** — this is why octree-based maps (OctoMap) are the practical answer to the scale challenge.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Modern Mapping System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Sensor Suite LIDAR Camera IMU Encoder"] --> B["Front End Preprocessing Feature Extraction"]
    B --> C{"Front End Odometry Estimate x_t u_t"}
    C --> D["Local Map Builder Submap Construction"]
    D --> E{"Loop Closure Detector Place Recognition"}
    E -- "Match Found" --> F["Back End Optimizer Graph SLAM Pose Graph"]
    E -- "No Match" --> G["Map Memory Storage Octree Hash Grid"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Semantic Labeler Neural Network Fusion"]
    H --> I["Final Map Output M Metric Topologic Semantic"]
    C --> F
    F -- "Corrected Poses" --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows denote data flow; dashed arrows denote corrective feedback (loop closure updates re-feed refined pose estimates into the local map builder). This is the canonical architecture used by systems such as ORB-SLAM3, Cartographer, and RTAB-Map.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. *Do not* confuse **data association** with **loop closure detection** — they are related but distinct. Data association matches observations frame-to-frame; loop closure matches observations across a long temporal gap. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. *Always* state the **square-root growth law** $\sigma = \sigma_s \sqrt{\ell}$ with units. Marks are deducted when units are omitted.
> 3. *Never* answer "current challenges" with only a generic list — KTU 2024 expects each challenge to be tied to a **specific map representation type** that fails.
> 4. For 14-mark questions, **diagrams carry 2-3 marks**. Always include a labeled block diagram or flowchart.

---

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

#### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**
*List any three current challenges in robotic map representation and give a one-line reason why each is challenging.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Dynamic Environments:** Classical occupancy grids assume a static world; moving entities cause contradictory evidence that produces "ghost" obstacles or erases real walls.
2. **Data Association Ambiguity:** Perceptual aliasing (different places looking identical) makes it hard to decide whether a sensor reading corresponds to a previously mapped landmark or a new one.
3. **Loop Closure Verification:** Recognizing a previously visited place with sufficient confidence to correct accumulated pose drift is hard under varying lighting, viewpoint, and seasonal changes.

*(Each point carries 1 mark — the KTU board awards 1 mark for naming the challenge and 1 mark for the justification, hence 3 points suffice.)*

#### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Define the "diffusive drift" of odometry error and write its standard mathematical form.*

**Model Answer:**

Diffusive drift refers to the **statistical accumulation of position uncertainty** in an open-loop dead-reckoning trajectory, where the standard deviation of the position error grows as the square root of the path length:

$$\sigma_{\text{position}} = \sigma_s \sqrt{\ell}$$

where $\sigma_s$ is the per-meter translation noise standard deviation (m/m) and $\ell$ is the total distance traveled (m). The square-root growth arises because independent step-errors add in variance, not magnitude.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark equation, 1 mark interpretation.)*

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the *dynamic environment challenge* in map representation. How does it specifically affect occupancy grid maps? Describe any one engineering mitigation strategy.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a labeled block diagram, describe the architecture of a modern Graph-SLAM based mapping system. Identify which blocks address which of the seven current challenges.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The dynamic environment challenge arises because the foundational assumption of classical metric maps — that the world is static over the mapping duration — is violated in nearly every real-world scenario (humans walking, vehicles moving, doors opening).

**Effect on Occupancy Grids:**
In a standard Bayesian occupancy grid, each cell's probability is updated via the recursive Bayes filter. For a cell $m_i$, the log-odds update rule is:

$$\ell(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \ell(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1}) + \ell(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) - \ell(m_i)$$

If a pedestrian repeatedly walks through cell $m_i$, the cell receives *occupied* and *free* updates in alternation. Because log-odds are additive, these updates partially cancel, but the *expected value* of the cell probability stabilizes at a value that does not correspond to physical reality. The result is:
- **Ghost Obstacles:** Cells where a moving agent once stood retain an inflated occupancy probability.
- **Erased Walls:** A real, static wall may be misclassified as free if it was occluded by a moving agent for many frames.

**Engineering Mitigation — DBSCAN + Velocity Filtering:**
The OctoMap framework and its successors apply a *DBSCAN clustering* of new point-cloud returns, then estimate per-cluster radial velocity (via consecutive scan differencing). Clusters with non-zero velocity are flagged as *dynamic* and excluded from the map update. Only static clusters contribute to $\ell(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)$. This preserves the integrity of the static map while the dynamic agents are tracked separately in an *object-level* layer.

*(2 marks challenge definition, 3 marks occupancy grid math + effect, 2 marks mitigation — 7 marks total.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

A modern Graph-SLAM system decomposes the mapping problem into two coupled graphs: a **front-end** for sensor processing and data association, and a **back-end** for global optimization.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ FRONT-END                                      │
│  Sensors → Preprocessing → Odometry → Local Submap │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
               │ Submap additions
               ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ LOOP CLOSURE DETECTOR                           │
│  Bag-of-Words / Deep Descriptor Match           │
│  (Addresses Axis 3: Loop Closure)               │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
               │ Loop constraints
               ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ BACK-END POSE GRAPH OPTIMIZER                   │
│  Solves:  min Σ ‖e_ij‖²_Σij                    │
│  (Addresses Axis 5: Uncertainty Propagation)    │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
               │ Corrected poses
               ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ MAP MEMORY (Octree / TSDF)                      │
│  (Addresses Axis 4: Scale & Memory)             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Mapping of blocks to challenges:**
- Front-end odometry: Addresses **data association** via JCBB (Axis 2).
- Loop closure detector: Directly addresses **perceptual aliasing and loop closure** (Axis 3).
- Back-end optimizer: Addresses **drift and uncertainty consistency** (Axis 5).
- Octree/TSDF storage: Addresses **scale and memory** (Axis 4).
- Semantic labeler (downstream): Addresses **semantic gap** (Axis 7).
- Multi-robot inter-robot measurements (extended block): Addresses **multi-robot fusion** (Axis 6).
- DBSCAN/velocity filter front-end pre-stage: Addresses **dynamic environments** (Axis 1).

*(2 marks diagram, 3 marks block descriptions, 2 marks challenge-mapping table — 7 marks total.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alt Set]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the *diffusive drift bound* $\sigma_{\text{position}} = \sigma_s \sqrt{\ell}$ for odometric error accumulation. State the assumptions clearly and discuss one practical consequence for map representation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast metric, topological, and hybrid map representations with respect to four of the seven current challenges. Use a comparison table.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

*Assumptions:*
1. Each incremental motion step $\Delta$ of the robot has independent translation noise with standard deviation $\sigma_s$ **per unit length traveled** (units: m/m).
2. There is no external correction (no loop closures, no GPS, no landmark fixes).
3. The motion is approximately linear over the relevant scale (no large rotational effects on translation variance).

*Derivation:*

Step 1: For one step of length $\Delta$, the variance in displacement along the travel direction is:

$$\text{Var}(\delta x_1) = (\sigma_s \cdot \Delta)^2 = \sigma_s^2 \cdot \Delta^2$$

Wait — this needs re-examination. If $\sigma_s$ is the *per-meter* error standard deviation, the variance per step of length $\Delta$ is:

$$\text{Var}(\delta x_1) = \sigma_s^2 \cdot \Delta$$

*(This is a critical subtlety: variance of a per-unit-rate random walk grows linearly with the time/distance accumulated.)*

Step 2: For $N$ independent steps, by linearity of variance for independent random variables:

$$\sigma_{\text{total}}^2 = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \text{Var}(\delta x_k) = N \cdot \sigma_s^2 \cdot \Delta$$

Step 3: The total path length is $\ell = N \cdot \Delta$, so $N = \ell / \Delta$. Substituting:

$$\sigma_{\text{total}}^2 = \frac{\ell}{\Delta} \cdot \sigma_s^2 \cdot \Delta = \sigma_s^2 \cdot \ell$$

Step 4: Take the square root:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_s \cdot \sqrt{\ell}}$$

*Practical Consequence for Map Representation:*
An unconstrained open-loop trajectory cannot be relied upon for accurate metric maps. If a robot travels 1 km with $\sigma_s = 0.02$ (2% encoder noise), the position uncertainty is $0.02 \times \sqrt{1000} \approx 0.63\,\text{m}$. For a map of resolution $r = 0.05\,\text{m}$, this error is more than 12 cells wide — entirely incompatible with high-resolution metric mapping. **Therefore, every long-horizon mapping system must incorporate some form of drift correction (loop closure, GPS, or external landmarks).** This is precisely why the loop closure problem is a "current challenge" — it is the *only* way to keep maps globally consistent over long trajectories.

*(2 marks assumptions, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks consequence — 7 marks total.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Comparison Table: Map Representations vs. Current Challenges**

| Challenge | Metric (Occupancy Grid) | Topological Graph | Hybrid (Metric + Topological) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dynamic Environments** | **Poor** — ghosts and erasure of real walls because every cell is updated identically. | **Moderate** — nodes can be marked as "temporarily inaccessible" but topology is brittle to node deletion. | **Moderate-Good** — local metric submaps can be re-built when local dynamics are detected. |
| **Loop Closure** | **Weak** — global grid must be re-warped; computationally expensive. | **Strong** — node re-visit is the natural primitive; edge costs recomputed. | **Strong** — topological nodes trigger submap re-alignment (e.g., Atlas framework). |
| **Memory Scalability** | **Poor** — $(L/r)^d$ scaling; 3D voxel grids blow up. | **Excellent** — one node per place; sparse edges. | **Good** — only metric detail stored where needed. |
| **Data Association** | **N/A** — implicit per cell. | **Moderate** — node matching via appearance. | **Moderate** — leverages both layers. |

**Synthesis:**
- Metric maps excel at *short-horizon, dense-reconstruction tasks* (e.g., robot arm obstacle avoidance within a 2 m workspace) but fail on long-horizon scalability.
- Topological maps excel at *long-horizon navigation* (e.g., multi-floor buildings) but sacrifice fine-grained geometry needed for manipulation.
- Hybrid maps attempt to **inherit the strengths of both**, which is why they are the dominant paradigm in modern systems (e.g., Cartographer, Karto SLAM).

*(2 marks table headers & completeness, 3 marks challenge-by-challenge content, 2 marks synthesis — 7 marks total.)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Seven Current Challenges:** Dynamic Environments · Data Association · Loop Closure · Scale & Memory · Uncertainty Propagation · Multi-Robot Fusion · Semantic Gap.
- ✅ **Square-Root Drift Law:** $\sigma = \sigma_s \sqrt{\ell}$ — derived from independent step-noise assumption; *always* quote units.
- ✅ **Mahalanobis Gate:** $D^2 = \nu^T S^{-1} \nu \leq \chi^2_{d, 0.95}$ — the standard data association test.
- ✅ **Octree Memory:** $O(n \log(L/r_{\min}))$ versus full voxel $O((L/r)^d)$ — sparse-environment saver.
- ✅ **Bayesian Occupancy Update:** Log-odds formulation is additive; this is why dynamic objects corrupt grids.
- ✅ **Graph SLAM** solves a least-squares problem: $\min \sum_{(i,j) \in E} \| e_{ij} \|^2_{\Sigma_{ij}}$ over pose-graph edges.
- ✅ **Loop Closure** is the *only* mechanism that converts a $\sqrt{\ell}$ drift trajectory back to a bounded-error map.
- ✅ **Multi-Robot Fusion** requires inter-robot measurements or feature overlap; ICP or feature-based registration of submaps.
- ✅ **Semantic Mapping** is the current research frontier — binding neural-network-detected object classes to metric primitives.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark answers, always include: (1) a definition, (2) a labeled diagram/table, (3) mathematical formulation, (4) a practical example.
- ✅ For 3-mark answers, the formula **with units** + one-line justification is the safest structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Probabilistic Map-Based Localization (Only Kalman Method)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Probabilistic Map-Based Localization (Only Kalman Method)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Probabilistic Map-Based Localization** is the process by which a mobile robot determines its pose (position and orientation) within a known environment map, while explicitly maintaining a probability distribution over all possible poses rather than a single deterministic estimate. The robot fuses **noisy sensor measurements** with **noisy motion commands** to recursively refine its belief about its location.

The **Kalman Filter (KF)** is the most widely deployed algorithm for solving the continuous-state localization problem. It is a recursive Bayesian estimator that maintains the robot's belief as a **unimodal multivariate Gaussian distribution**, characterized entirely by a mean vector (best estimate of pose) and a covariance matrix (associated uncertainty).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Terminology (2024 Scheme):**
> The Kalman Method assumes the environment is mapped a-priori, the robot dynamics and sensor models are linear (or linearized), and all noise sources are zero-mean Gaussian. Under these conditions, the Kalman Filter is the **optimal minimum mean-square-error (MMSE) estimator**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blind Walk in Your House"

Imagine waking up in your own house with **eyes closed**, holding a noisy pedometer (step counter) in one hand and a slightly inaccurate wall-distance sensor in the other:

- **Pedometer (Motion / Odometry)**: Counts your steps and direction changes. Over time, errors accumulate — your estimate of where you are *drifts*.
- **Wall Sensor (Measurement)**: Tells you, for example, "the wall is 1.2 m to your right" — but with some ±5 cm uncertainty.
- **Map (Prior Knowledge)**: You know the floor plan of your house.

You constantly **predict** where you should be based on your steps, then **correct** that prediction whenever the wall sensor reports a value that disagrees with the map. The Kalman Filter is the mathematical machinery that does *exactly this fusion* — but optimally, with all uncertainties tracked rigorously as Gaussian distributions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Probabilistic?**
> A deterministic estimate like "I am at (x = 2.3, y = 1.8)" is dangerous — it carries no confidence. The Kalman Filter instead answers: *"I am at (2.3, 1.8) with a 95% confidence ellipse of radius 0.15 m."* This uncertainty is essential for safe navigation, sensor fusion, and decision-making.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range (Indoor Mobile Robot) |
|---|---|---|
| **State vector x** | Pose of robot (x, y, θ) | Real-valued in ℝ³ |
| **Covariance P** | Uncertainty matrix | Symmetric positive-definite 3×3 |
| **Process noise Q** | Motion uncertainty | ~0.01 to 0.1 m² |
| **Measurement noise R** | Sensor uncertainty | ~0.001 to 0.05 m² |
| **Update rate** | Filter frequency | **10 Hz to 100 Hz** |
| **Convergence time** | Time to localize | ~2 to 10 seconds |

> [!TIP]
> A robot's covariance matrix `P` shrinks (uncertainty decreases) every time a measurement agrees with the map. It grows (uncertainty increases) during pure prediction (dead-reckoning). The diagonal trace `tr(P)` is a scalar measure of total pose uncertainty.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Localization Framework

Robot localization is formally a problem of estimating the **belief** — a posterior probability distribution over the robot's state given all sensor data and control inputs up to time `t`:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where:
- $x_t$ = robot state at time $t$ (e.g., pose $[x, y, \theta]^T$)
- $z_{1:t}$ = sequence of all measurements from time $1$ to $t$
- $u_{1:t}$ = sequence of all control inputs (odometry, motor commands) from $1$ to $t$

### 2.1.1 The Bayes Filter Recursion

The general solution is given by the Bayes filter, executed in two steps:

**Step A — Prediction (Motion Update):** Apply the control input to the prior belief using the motion model $p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$:

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step B — Correction (Measurement Update):** Multiply by the likelihood of the measurement using the sensor model $p(z_t \mid x_t)$:

$$\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t)\, \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)$$

where $\eta$ is a normalization constant ensuring the posterior integrates to 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Kalman Filter is the **closed-form analytic solution** of the Bayes filter under three assumptions:
> 1. The motion model is **linear in state and control**.
> 2. The sensor model is **linear in state**.
> 3. All noise is **additive, zero-mean, uncorrelated Gaussian**.

## 2.2 Linear Gaussian System Model

A linear Gaussian system is described by:

$$x_t = A_t\, x_{t-1} + B_t\, u_t + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$

$$z_t = H_t\, x_t + \delta_t, \quad \delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

| Matrix | Name | Role |
|---|---|---|
| $A_t$ | State transition matrix | Maps previous state to current |
| $B_t$ | Control input matrix | Maps control command to state change |
| $H_t$ | Observation matrix | Maps state to expected measurement |
| $Q_t$ | Process noise covariance | Uncertainty in motion model |
| $R_t$ | Measurement noise covariance | Uncertainty in sensor model |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Kalman Filter — Two-Stage Recursion)

### Prediction (Time-Update) Stage

$$\boxed{\hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} = A_t \hat{x}_{t-1\mid t-1} + B_t u_t}$$

$$\boxed{P_{t\mid t-1} = A_t\, P_{t-1\mid t-1}\, A_t^{T} + Q_t}$$

### Correction (Measurement-Update) Stage

**Innovation (Measurement Residual):**
$$\tilde{y}_t = z_t - H_t \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}$$

**Innovation Covariance:**
$$S_t = H_t\, P_{t\mid t-1}\, H_t^{T} + R_t$$

**Optimal Kalman Gain:**
$$\boxed{K_t = P_{t\mid t-1}\, H_t^{T}\, S_t^{-1}}$$

**Updated State Estimate:**
$$\boxed{\hat{x}_{t\mid t} = \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} + K_t\, \tilde{y}_t}$$

**Updated Covariance (Joseph form for numerical stability):**
$$\boxed{P_{t\mid t} = (I - K_t H_t)\, P_{t\mid t-1}\, (I - K_t H_t)^{T} + K_t R_t K_t^{T}}$$

A computationally simpler (but less stable) form is:
$$P_{t\mid t} = (I - K_t H_t)\, P_{t\mid t-1}$$

## 2.4 Specialization to 2D Mobile Robot Localization

For a differential-drive mobile robot operating in a known 2D map, the state vector is:

$$x_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.4.1 Motion Model (Odometry)

The robot moves by translation $\Delta s$ and rotation $\Delta \theta$:

$$x_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_{t-1} + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta\theta/2) \\ y_{t-1} + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{t-1} + \Delta\theta/2) \\ \theta_{t-1} + \Delta\theta \end{bmatrix} + \varepsilon_t$$

This is **non-linear** in $\theta$. For strict linear KF, one must **linearize** at the current operating point (this is the basis of the **Extended Kalman Filter — EKF**).

### 2.4.2 Measurement Model (Range / Beacon)

If the robot observes a beacon at known map location $(x_b, y_b)$ and measures the range $r$ and bearing $\phi$:

$$z_t = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_b - x_t)^2 + (y_b - y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(y_b - y_t,\; x_b - x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix} + \delta_t$$

This is also **non-linear** → EKF linearization required.

## 2.5 Why the Kalman Filter Works for Localization

| Property | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Optimality** | Minimum-variance unbiased estimator under linear-Gaussian assumptions |
| **Recursion** | Requires only previous estimate, not full history → **O(n²) per step** |
| **Uncertainty tracking** | Covariance `P` grows during prediction, shrinks during update |
| **Multi-sensor fusion** | Naturally combines odometry + GPS + LIDAR + IMU |
| **Computational efficiency** | Solvable in real time on embedded processors (e.g., STM32, Raspberry Pi) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** The Kalman Gain $K_t$ is essentially an *adaptive blending factor*. When measurement noise $R_t$ is small (sensor very accurate), $K_t \to I$ and the filter trusts the sensor fully. When $R_t$ is large, $K_t \to 0$ and the filter ignores the sensor, relying on the prediction.
