# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kalman Filter Equations from First Principles

We derive the optimal estimator under the linear-Gaussian assumption. The goal: find the state estimate $\hat{x}_{t\mid t}$ that **minimizes the mean-square error**:

$$J(\hat{x}) = \mathbb{E}\left[ (x_t - \hat{x})^T (x_t - \hat{x}) \right]$$

### 3.1.1 Setup of the Joint Gaussian

Given the linear system, the prior estimate $\hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}$ and the measurement $z_t$ are jointly Gaussian:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} \\ z_t \end{bmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left( \begin{bmatrix} \mu \\ H\mu \end{bmatrix},\; \begin{bmatrix} P_{t\mid t-1} & P_{t\mid t-1} H^T \\ H P_{t\mid t-1} & H P_{t\mid t-1} H^T + R_t \end{bmatrix} \right)$$

### 3.1.2 Conditional Mean (The Optimal Estimate)

The conditional distribution of $\hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}$ given $z_t$ is also Gaussian. The conditional mean is:

$$\hat{x}_{t\mid t} = \mu + \text{Cov}(\hat{x},z)\, \text{Var}(z)^{-1}\, (z_t - H\mu)$$

Substituting the covariances:

$$\hat{x}_{t\mid t} = \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} + P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T (H_t P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T + R_t)^{-1} (z_t - H_t \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1})$$

Recognizing the bracket as $K_t$:

$$\hat{x}_{t\mid t} = \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} + K_t (z_t - H_t \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1})$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Posterior Covariance

The posterior covariance is computed from the conditional Gaussian identity:

$$P_{t\mid t} = P_{t\mid t-1} - P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T S_t^{-1} H_t P_{t\mid t-1}$$

Substituting $K_t = P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T S_t^{-1}$:

$$P_{t\mid t} = P_{t\mid t-1} - K_t H_t P_{t\mid t-1} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t\mid t-1}$$

The **Joseph form** is obtained by algebraic rearrangement of the definition of the estimation error:

$$e_t = x_t - \hat{x}_{t\mid t} = (I - K_t H_t)(x_t - \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}) - K_t \delta_t$$

Computing $\mathbb{E}[e_t e_t^T]$ with cross-terms vanishing (since $e_{t\mid t-1}$ and $\delta_t$ are independent):

$$\begin{aligned} P_{t\mid t} &= (I - K_t H_t) P_{t\mid t-1} (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t R_t K_t^T \end{aligned}$$

This symmetric form is **numerically robust** in floating-point implementations.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — 1D Position Tracking

**Problem:** A robot moves along a 1D line. State $x_t$ = position. Measurements of position are taken by an external camera.

**System Parameters:**
- $A = 1$ (state doesn't change without control)
- $B = 1$ (control = velocity)
- $H = 1$ (measurement is direct position)
- $Q = 0.1$ (process noise variance)
- $R = 0.5$ (measurement noise variance)

**Initial conditions:**
- $\hat{x}_0 = 0$, $P_0 = 1.0$

**Sequence of events:**

| Time $t$ | Control $u_t$ | Measurement $z_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0 | 1.2 |
| 2 | 1.0 | 2.1 |
| 3 | 1.0 | 3.3 |

### Step-by-Step Computation at t = 1

**Prediction:**

$$\hat{x}_{1\mid 0} = A \hat{x}_0 + B u_1 = (1)(0) + (1)(1.0) = 1.0$$

$$P_{1\mid 0} = A P_0 A^T + Q = (1)(1.0)(1) + 0.1 = 1.1$$

**Innovation:**

$$\tilde{y}_1 = z_1 - H \hat{x}_{1\mid 0} = 1.2 - (1)(1.0) = 0.2$$

**Innovation Covariance:**

$$S_1 = H P_{1\mid 0} H^T + R = (1)(1.1)(1) + 0.5 = 1.6$$

**Kalman Gain:**

$$K_1 = P_{1\mid 0} H^T S_1^{-1} = (1.1)(1) \cdot \frac{1}{1.6} = 0.6875$$

**Updated State:**

$$\hat{x}_{1\mid 1} = \hat{x}_{1\mid 0} + K_1 \tilde{y}_1 = 1.0 + (0.6875)(0.2) = 1.1375$$

**Updated Covariance (simplified form):**

$$P_{1\mid 1} = (I - K_1 H) P_{1\mid 0} = (1 - 0.6875)(1.1) = 0.34375$$

### Step-by-Step Computation at t = 2

**Prediction:**

$$\hat{x}_{2\mid 1} = A \hat{x}_{1\mid 1} + B u_2 = 1.1375 + 1.0 = 2.1375$$

$$P_{2\mid 1} = A P_{1\mid 1} A^T + Q = 0.34375 + 0.1 = 0.44375$$

**Innovation:**

$$\tilde{y}_2 = z_2 - H \hat{x}_{2\mid 1} = 2.1 - 2.1375 = -0.0375$$

**Innovation Covariance:**

$$S_2 = (1)(0.44375)(1) + 0.5 = 0.94375$$

**Kalman Gain:**

$$K_2 = (0.44375)(1) \cdot \frac{1}{0.94375} = 0.4702$$

**Updated State:**

$$\hat{x}_{2\mid 2} = 2.1375 + (0.4702)(-0.0375) = 2.1199$$

**Updated Covariance:**

$$P_{2\mid 2} = (1 - 0.4702)(0.44375) = 0.2351$$

### Step-by-Step Computation at t = 3

**Prediction:**

$$\hat{x}_{3\mid 2} = 2.1199 + 1.0 = 3.1199$$

$$P_{3\mid 2} = 0.2351 + 0.1 = 0.3351$$

**Innovation:**

$$\tilde{y}_3 = 3.3 - 3.1199 = 0.1801$$

**Innovation Covariance:**

$$S_3 = 0.3351 + 0.5 = 0.8351$$

**Kalman Gain:**

$$K_3 = (0.3351) \cdot \frac{1}{0.8351} = 0.4013$$

**Updated State:**

$$\hat{x}_{3\mid 3} = 3.1199 + (0.4013)(0.1801) = 3.1922$$

**Updated Covariance:**

$$P_{3\mid 3} = (1 - 0.4013)(0.3351) = 0.2006$$

### 3.2.1 Summary Table of Iteration Results

| $t$ | $\hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}$ | $P_{t\mid t-1}$ | $K_t$ | $\hat{x}_{t\mid t}$ | $P_{t\mid t}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0000 | 1.1000 | 0.6875 | 1.1375 | 0.3438 |
| 2 | 2.1375 | 0.4438 | 0.4702 | 2.1199 | 0.2351 |
| 3 | 3.1199 | 0.3351 | 0.4013 | 3.1922 | 0.2006 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The covariance $P_{t\mid t}$ is **monotonically decreasing** and asymptotically approaches a steady-state value. This represents the filter "locking on" to the true trajectory. In a real robot, the steady-state $P$ value reflects the balance between process noise $Q$ (uncertainty injected each step) and measurement noise $R$ (uncertainty removed each step).

## 3.3 Steady-State Analysis — Predicting Convergence

For a time-invariant linear system, the covariance update converges to a fixed point $P_\infty$ satisfying the **Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE)**:

$$P_\infty = A P_\infty A^T + Q - A P_\infty H^T (H P_\infty H^T + R)^{-1} H P_\infty A^T$$

In our 1D example with $A = H = 1$:

$$P_\infty = P_\infty + Q - \frac{P_\infty^2}{P_\infty + R}$$

Multiplying through by $(P_\infty + R)$:

$$P_\infty(P_\infty + R) = (P_\infty + R)(P_\infty + Q) - P_\infty^2$$

Expanding and simplifying:

$$P_\infty R = (P_\infty + R) Q$$

$$P_\infty R = P_\infty Q + R Q$$

$$P_\infty (R - Q) = R Q$$

$$\boxed{P_\infty = \frac{RQ}{R - Q}}$$

For our values $Q = 0.1$, $R = 0.5$:

$$P_\infty = \frac{(0.5)(0.1)}{0.5 - 0.1} = \frac{0.05}{0.4} = 0.125$$

And the steady-state Kalman gain:

$$K_\infty = \frac{P_\infty}{P_\infty + R} = \frac{0.125}{0.125 + 0.5} = 0.2$$

This means at steady state, **20% of the innovation** is incorporated into the state update — the rest is filtered as noise.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strengths, Limitations & Comparison

| Aspect | Linear Kalman Filter | Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) | Particle Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **State representation** | Single Gaussian | Single Gaussian (linearized) | Set of weighted samples |
| **Multi-modal support** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Computational cost** | Very low (O(n²)) | Low (O(n²)) | High (O(NM)) |
| **Handles non-linearity** | ❌ No | ⚠️ Approximately | ✅ Yes |
| **Global localization** | ❌ Requires good init | ❌ Requires good init | ✅ Yes |
| **Best use case** | Tracking / Local localization | GPS-INS fusion / SLAM | Kidnapped robot / global loc. |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Kalman Filter Recursive Loop — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial State x0 and Covariance P0] --> B[Receive Control Input ut]
    B --> C[Predict State: x_pred = A x_prev + B ut]
    C --> D[Predict Covariance: P_pred = A P_prev AT + Q]
    D --> E{Measurement zt Available?}
    E -->|No| J[Output Predicted Estimate]
    E -->|Yes| F[Compute Innovation: y_tilde = zt - H x_pred]
    F --> G[Compute Kalman Gain: K = P_pred HT S_inverse]
    G --> H[Update State: x_new = x_pred + K y_tilde]
    H --> I[Update Covariance: P_new = I - K H P_pred]
    I --> J[Output Corrected Estimate]
    J --> K[Increment Time t to t+1]
    K --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above shows the **closed-loop recursive structure** of the Kalman Filter. Note the two parallel paths: state estimate update (top branch) and covariance update (bottom branch). Both must be computed and propagated to the next time step.

## 4.2 Map-Based Localization System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Sensors] --> B[Sensor Pre-processing]
    C[Robot Actuators / Motors] --> D[Odometry Computation]
    B --> E[Feature Extraction]
    E --> F[Measurement Vector zt]
    D --> G[Control Vector ut]
    F --> H[Kalman Filter Core]
    G --> H
    I[Known Environment Map] --> J[Measurement Model H]
    J --> H
    H --> K[Robot Pose Estimate x hat t]
    H --> L[Uncertainty Covariance P t]
    K --> M[Navigation Planner]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Motor Commands]
    N --> C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **map** enters the filter indirectly through the observation matrix `H_t`, which depends on the relative geometry between the robot's predicted pose and the known landmark positions. Without the map, the measurement model cannot be defined.

## 4.3 Two-Stage Decomposition — Predict vs. Update

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Prediction_Stage [PREDICTION STAGE - Time Update]
        P1[Previous State x hat t-1] --> P2[Apply Motion Model A x + B u]
        P2 --> P3[A Posteriori State x hat t given t-1]
        P4[Previous Covariance P t-1] --> P5[Propagate Uncertainty P pred = A P AT + Q]
        P5 --> P6[A Posteriori Covariance P t given t-1]
    end
    subgraph Update_Stage [CORRECTION STAGE - Measurement Update]
        U1[Measurement zt] --> U2[Innovation nu = zt - H x hat pred]
        P3 --> U2
        U2 --> U3[Compute Kalman Gain K]
        P6 --> U3
        U3 --> U4[Update State: x hat t = x hat pred + K nu]
        U3 --> U5[Update Covariance: P t = I minus K H P pred]
        U4 --> U6[Optimal State Estimate]
        U5 --> U7[Posterior Covariance]
    end
    P3 --> U2
    P6 --> U3
```

## 4.4 Robot Pose and Landmark Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot at Pose x,y,theta] --> B[Sensor Reading to Landmark L]
    B --> C[Predicted Measurement from Map]
    C --> D[Compare with Actual Sensor Reading]
    D --> E{Innovation = z_actual minus z_predicted}
    E --> F[Innovation High - Filter Corrects Aggressively]
    E --> G[Innovation Low - Filter Trusts Prediction]
```

> [!TIP]
> The innovation $\tilde{y}_t = z_t - H_t \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}$ is the **bridge between predicted and observed worlds**. In a 2D map-based localization, this innovation has units of meters (range residual) and radians (bearing residual). A properly tuned Kalman Filter ensures that the Mahalanobis distance of the innovation $\tilde{y}_t^T S_t^{-1} \tilde{y}_t$ follows a chi-squared distribution, which is the basis for filter consistency checks (NEES / NIS tests).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the three fundamental assumptions required for the applicability of the linear Kalman Filter in robot localization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three fundamental assumptions of the linear Kalman Filter are:

1. **Linearity of system models** (1 mark): Both the motion model and the measurement model must be expressible as linear functions of the state vector. That is, $x_t = A_t x_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ and $z_t = H_t x_t$.

2. **Gaussian noise** (1 mark): All process noise and measurement noise must be additive, zero-mean, and Gaussian distributed, i.e., $\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ and $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$.

3. **Initial belief is Gaussian** (1 mark): The initial state estimate $\hat{x}_0$ must itself be a Gaussian random variable (i.e., the initial uncertainty $P_0$ is well-defined and finite).

Under these conditions, the Kalman Filter is the **optimal minimum-variance unbiased estimator** and the posterior belief remains Gaussian at every time step.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the Kalman Gain $K_t$ and explain its physical significance in the context of sensor fusion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Kalman Gain is given by:

$$K_t = P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T (H_t P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$$

It is a weighting matrix that determines the degree to which the new measurement $z_t$ influences the updated state estimate.

**Physical Significance (1.5 marks):**

- When the measurement noise covariance $R_t$ is **small** (highly accurate sensor), $K_t \to I$ and the filter trusts the sensor measurement completely.
- When the process noise covariance $Q_t$ has caused $P_{t\mid t-1}$ to be **large** (high prior uncertainty), $K_t$ is large and the filter relies heavily on the measurement to reduce uncertainty.
- Conversely, when $R_t$ is large or $P_{t\mid t-1}$ is small, $K_t \to 0$ and the filter essentially ignores the measurement, retaining its prediction.

Thus, $K_t$ acts as an **adaptive, optimal blending factor** that automatically balances trust between prediction and measurement based on their respective uncertainties.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the Kalman Filter measurement-update equations from the linear-Gaussian conditional probability framework. Clearly state the role of the innovation vector and the innovation covariance matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot moves along a 1D track. The system and measurement models are:
$x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + w_t$, where $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.2)$
$z_t = x_t + v_t$, where $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.4)$

Initial conditions: $\hat{x}_0 = 0$, $P_0 = 1.0$.
At $t=1$, $u_1 = 1.0$ and $z_1 = 1.3$.
At $t=2$, $u_2 = 1.0$ and $z_2 = 2.4$.

Compute $\hat{x}_{1\mid 1}$, $P_{1\mid 1}$, $\hat{x}_{2\mid 2}$, $P_{2\mid 2}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

We consider the prior estimate $\hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}$ and the measurement $z_t$ as jointly Gaussian random variables. Under the linear observation model $z_t = H_t x_t + \delta_t$, the joint distribution is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} \\ z_t \end{bmatrix} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left( \begin{bmatrix} \mu \\ H_t \mu \end{bmatrix},\, \begin{bmatrix} P_{t\mid t-1} & P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T \\ H_t P_{t\mid t-1} & H_t P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T + R_t \end{bmatrix} \right)$$

where $\mu = \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}$.

The optimal MMSE estimate of $x_t$ given $z_t$ is the conditional mean:

$$\hat{x}_{t\mid t} = \mathbb{E}[x_t \mid z_t] = \mu + \text{Cov}(x_t, z_t)\, \text{Var}(z_t)^{-1}\, (z_t - \mathbb{E}[z_t])$$

Identifying the cross-covariance as $P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T$ and the marginal variance of $z_t$ as $S_t = H_t P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T + R_t$:

$$\hat{x}_{t\mid t} = \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} + P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T S_t^{-1} (z_t - H_t \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1})$$

Defining the **innovation** $\tilde{y}_t = z_t - H_t \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}$ (which represents the discrepancy between what was observed and what was predicted) and the **Kalman gain** $K_t = P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T S_t^{-1}$:

$$\hat{x}_{t\mid t} = \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} + K_t \tilde{y}_t$$

The **innovation covariance** $S_t = H_t P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T + R_t$ represents the total uncertainty in the residual $\tilde{y}_t$, combining prediction uncertainty and sensor noise.

The posterior covariance, derived from the conditional Gaussian identity, is:

$$P_{t\mid t} = P_{t\mid t-1} - P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T S_t^{-1} H_t P_{t\mid t-1} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t\mid t-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Role of Innovation:** $\tilde{y}_t$ is the "new information" brought by the sensor — it is the only term that changes between time steps. If the filter is well-tuned, $\tilde{y}_t$ should be zero-mean Gaussian with covariance $S_t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Role of Innovation Covariance $S_t$:** It quantifies how "trustworthy" the innovation is. A large $S_t$ (high uncertainty) → small $K_t$ → the innovation contributes little to the update.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Time step t = 1:**

Prediction:
$$\hat{x}_{1\mid 0} = \hat{x}_0 + u_1 = 0 + 1.0 = 1.0$$
$$P_{1\mid 0} = P_0 + Q = 1.0 + 0.2 = 1.2$$

Innovation:
$$\tilde{y}_1 = z_1 - \hat{x}_{1\mid 0} = 1.3 - 1.0 = 0.3$$

Innovation covariance:
$$S_1 = P_{1\mid 0} + R = 1.2 + 0.4 = 1.6$$

Kalman gain:
$$K_1 = \frac{P_{1\mid 0}}{S_1} = \frac{1.2}{1.6} = 0.75$$

Updated state:
$$\hat{x}_{1\mid 1} = 1.0 + 0.75 \times 0.3 = 1.0 + 0.225 = 1.225$$

Updated covariance:
$$P_{1\mid 1} = (1 - 0.75) \times 1.2 = 0.3$$

**Time step t = 2:**

Prediction:
$$\hat{x}_{2\mid 1} = \hat{x}_{1\mid 1} + u_2 = 1.225 + 1.0 = 2.225$$
$$P_{2\mid 1} = P_{1\mid 1} + Q = 0.3 + 0.2 = 0.5$$

Innovation:
$$\tilde{y}_2 = z_2 - \hat{x}_{2\mid 1} = 2.4 - 2.225 = 0.175$$

Innovation covariance:
$$S_2 = P_{2\mid 1} + R = 0.5 + 0.4 = 0.9$$

Kalman gain:
$$K_2 = \frac{P_{2\mid 1}}{S_2} = \frac{0.5}{0.9} = 0.5556$$

Updated state:
$$\hat{x}_{2\mid 2} = 2.225 + 0.5556 \times 0.175 = 2.225 + 0.0972 = 2.3222$$

Updated covariance:
$$P_{2\mid 2} = (1 - 0.5556) \times 0.5 = 0.2222$$

**Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{\hat{x}_{1\mid 1} = 1.225, \quad P_{1\mid 1} = 0.3}$$
$$\boxed{\hat{x}_{2\mid 2} = 2.322, \quad P_{2\mid 2} = 0.222}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to **add $Q$ to $P$** during prediction (Step A). The covariance must be inflated by the process noise; otherwise, the filter becomes overconfident and diverges. Marks are deducted if $P_{t\mid t-1}$ is computed as equal to $P_{t-1\mid t-1}$ without the $Q$ term.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the two-stage recursive structure of the Kalman Filter for robot localization. Identify the inputs, outputs, and the key matrices used in each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot is to be localized in a 2D map using two range-only beacons at known map positions. Formulate the Kalman Filter measurement model $H_t$ for a single observation step and explain how the observation matrix depends on the robot's current pose estimate. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two-Stage Recursive Structure (7 marks):**

The Kalman Filter operates as a **two-stage recursive estimator** that runs continuously at the robot's control loop frequency.

**Stage 1 — Prediction (Time-Update) Stage:**

*Inputs:*
- Previous state estimate $\hat{x}_{t-1\mid t-1}$ (3×1 pose vector)
- Previous covariance $P_{t-1\mid t-1}$ (3×3 uncertainty matrix)
- Current control input $u_t$ (2×1: linear and angular velocity)
- Process noise covariance $Q_t$ (3×3)

*Operations:*
$$\hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} = A_t \hat{x}_{t-1\mid t-1} + B_t u_t$$
$$P_{t\mid t-1} = A_t P_{t-1\mid t-1} A_t^T + Q_t$$

*Outputs:* Predicted state $\hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}$ and predicted covariance $P_{t\mid t-1}$.

**Stage 2 — Correction (Measurement-Update) Stage:**

*Inputs:*
- Predicted state $\hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}$
- Predicted covariance $P_{t\mid t-1}$
- Current sensor measurement $z_t$ (e.g., range and bearing)
- Measurement noise covariance $R_t$
- Observation matrix $H_t$ (derived from map)

*Operations:*
$$\tilde{y}_t = z_t - H_t \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}$$
$$K_t = P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T (H_t P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$$
$$\hat{x}_{t\mid t} = \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} + K_t \tilde{y}_t$$
$$P_{t\mid t} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t\mid t-1}$$

*Outputs:* Updated state $\hat{x}_{t\mid t}$ and updated covariance $P_{t\mid t}$.

The corrected estimate is then fed back as the input to the next prediction step, completing the recursion.

**Functional Block Diagram (textual representation for answer script):**

```
  ┌──────────────── PREDICTION ────────────────┐
  │  x̂_{t-1|t-1}, P_{t-1|t-1}, u_t           │
  │          │                                  │
  │          ▼                                  │
  │   x̂_{t|t-1} = A x̂_{t-1|t-1} + B u_t      │
  │   P_{t|t-1} = A P_{t-1|t-1} A^T + Q       │
  └──────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                         │  x̂_{t|t-1}, P_{t|t-1}
                         ▼
  ┌──────────────── CORRECTION ────────────────┐
  │  Measurement z_t arrives                    │
  │          │                                  │
  │          ▼                                  │
  │   K_t = P_{t|t-1} H^T S^{-1}               │
  │   x̂_{t|t} = x̂_{t|t-1} + K_t (z_t - H x̂) │
  │   P_{t|t} = (I - K_t H) P_{t|t-1}          │
  └──────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
                  Output to Navigation
                  Loop back to t = t+1
```

**(b) Measurement Model Formulation (7 marks):**

Let the robot's state be $x_t = [x_r, y_r, \theta_r]^T$ and the beacon's known map position be $(x_b, y_b)$.

**Non-linear range measurement model:**

$$z_t = h(x_t) = \sqrt{(x_b - x_r)^2 + (y_b - y_r)^2} + v_t$$

This is non-linear. To use the linear Kalman Filter, we linearize via the **Jacobian** evaluated at the current predicted pose $\hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} = [\hat{x}_r, \hat{y}_r, \hat{\theta}_r]^T$:

$$H_t = \left.\frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right|_{x = \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}}$$

Computing the partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial h}{\partial x_r} = \frac{-(x_b - x_r)}{\sqrt{(x_b - x_r)^2 + (y_b - y_r)^2}} = -\frac{\Delta x}{\hat{r}}$$

$$\frac{\partial h}{\partial y_r} = \frac{-(y_b - y_r)}{\sqrt{(x_b - x_r)^2 + (y_b - y_r)^2}} = -\frac{\Delta y}{\hat{r}}$$

$$\frac{\partial h}{\partial \theta_r} = 0$$

where $\Delta x = x_b - \hat{x}_r$, $\Delta y = y_b - \hat{y}_r$, and $\hat{r} = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ is the predicted range.

Therefore, the observation matrix is the 1×3 row vector:

$$\boxed{H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{\Delta x}{\hat{r}} & -\dfrac{\Delta y}{\hat{r}} & 0 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Pose-dependence of $H_t$:**

The observation matrix $H_t$ is **not constant** — it is re-evaluated at every time step because it depends on the relative geometry $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \hat{r})$ between the robot's **current predicted pose** and the **known beacon position**. As the robot moves, $\hat{x}_r$ and $\hat{y}_r$ change, and therefore $H_t$ changes.

**If two beacons** $b_1$ and $b_2$ are observed simultaneously, $z_t$ becomes a 2×1 vector and $H_t$ becomes a 2×3 matrix:

$$H_t = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{\Delta x_1}{\hat{r}_1} & -\dfrac{\Delta y_1}{\hat{r}_1} & 0 \\ -\dfrac{\Delta x_2}{\hat{r}_2} & -\dfrac{\Delta y_2}{\hat{r}_2} & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

This increased measurement dimension **improves observability** and accelerates covariance convergence.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to write $H_t$ as a constant matrix independent of pose — this loses **3 marks** in part (b). Students must explicitly show the Jacobian computation and indicate that $H_t$ is **re-computed at every time step** using the current pose estimate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Probabilistic Map-Based Localization (Kalman Method)**

- **Core idea:** Maintain a Gaussian belief $(x_t, P_t)$ over robot pose; update recursively via prediction and correction.

- **Bayes Filter foundation:** $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) dx_{t-1}$.

- **Three KF assumptions:** Linear models, Gaussian noise, Gaussian initial belief.

- **Prediction stage (Time Update):** $\hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} = A \hat{x}_{t-1\mid t-1} + B u_t$ and $P_{t\mid t-1} = A P_{t-1\mid t-1} A^T + Q$.

- **Correction stage (Measurement Update):** Compute innovation $\tilde{y}_t$, Kalman gain $K_t$, update state and covariance.

- **Innovation:** $\tilde{y}_t = z_t - H_t \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1}$ — must be zero-mean Gaussian for a consistent filter.

- **Kalman Gain formula:** $K_t = P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T (H_t P_{t\mid t-1} H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$ — adaptive trust factor.

- **State update:** $\hat{x}_{t\mid t} = \hat{x}_{t\mid t-1} + K_t \tilde{y}_t$.

- **Covariance update (Joseph form preferred):** $P_{t\mid t} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t\mid t-1} (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t R_t K_t^T$.

- **$Q$** = process noise covariance (motion model uncertainty). Always **added** during prediction.

- **$R$** = measurement noise covariance (sensor noise). Always **added** during innovation covariance computation.

- **State vector for 2D localization:** $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — pose in 2D plane.

- **Non-linearity handling:** Use EKF (Extended Kalman Filter) by linearizing motion and measurement models around the current estimate via Jacobian computation.

- **Convergence:** $P_t$ decreases monotonically and reaches a steady state given by the Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE).

- **Limitation:** Single-Gaussian assumption → **cannot handle global localization or multi-modal beliefs** (use particle filters for those cases).

- **Practical applications:** GPS-INS fusion, autonomous vehicles, drone navigation, AGVs in warehouses, SLAM front-ends.

- **Examination tip:** Always specify whether the filter is 1D, 2D, or 3D; always carry units of $Q$ and $R$ in m² (or rad²) when writing matrices; always re-evaluate $H_t$ if the model is non-linear and linearized.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous Map Building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Autonomous Map Building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — AUTONOMOUS MAP BUILDING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Autonomous Map Building** is the process by which a mobile robot explores an *unknown* or *partially known* environment using its onboard sensors, and incrementally constructs a spatial representation (a map) of that environment **without human intervention**, while simultaneously estimating its own pose (position and orientation) within that map. This combined problem is formally known in robotics literature as **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**.

The process requires the robot to answer two interlocked questions at every time step:
1. **"Where am I?"** → Localization (pose estimation)
2. **"What does the world look like around me?"** → Mapping (map update)

Because neither the map nor the pose is known a priori, the two problems are **chicken-and-egg coupled**: an accurate map requires a known pose, and an accurate pose requires a known map.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — What KTU expects you to know:**
> - Types of maps (metric, topological, hybrid)
> - Map representation techniques
> - Sensor models used for measurement
> - Probabilistic update mechanisms
> - The role of uncertainty and data association
> - Challenges and the exploration vs. exploitation trade-off

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **waking up for the first time in a completely dark, unfamiliar house** with only a dim flashlight. You don't know the layout, your own location, or even where the walls are. To navigate:

- You sweep the flashlight around (this is your **sensor scan**).
- You see a wall — you sketch it roughly on paper (this is a **map update**).
- You take a few steps forward and turn — but now you're *uncertain* about where you are relative to that wall you saw earlier.
- You see the wall again from a new angle — this confirms your previous guess (a kind of **loop closure**).
- Slowly, your mental sketch of the house becomes more accurate, and you become more confident about your position.

That mental process — *perception → uncertain motion → re-perception → belief update* — is exactly what an autonomous map-building robot does, but in a mathematically rigorous, probabilistic manner.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Autonomous"?** The word stresses that no external infrastructure (no GPS, no pre-installed beacons, no teleoperation) is available. The robot is the sole agent responsible for both **perception**, **decision-making**, and **map construction**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Used in KTU Board Exams

| Term | Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Map** | A spatial data structure storing information about the environment (geometry, landmarks, topology). |
| **Pose** | The robot's state vector — usually $\mathbf{x}_t = (x, y, \theta)^\top$ in 2-D. |
| **Landmark** | A distinctive, re-observable feature in the environment (corner, pillar, edge). |
| **Occupancy Grid** | A discretized 2-D grid where each cell stores the probability of being occupied. |
| **Observation / Measurement** | A sensor reading $\mathbf{z}_t$ at time $t$ (range, bearing, image features). |
| **Odometry** | Dead-reckoning estimate of pose change from wheel encoders / IMU. |
| **Loop Closure** | Recognition of a previously visited place, used to correct accumulated drift. |
| **Data Association** | The process of matching current sensor observations to previously stored map features. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Boundary Values / Constants to Remember:**
> - Cell probability range: $P(m_i) \in [0, 1]$
> - Log-odds range: $l \in (-\infty, +\infty)$
> - Prior for an unknown cell: $P(m_i) = 0.5 \implies l = 0$
> - Standard sensor noise for sonar: $\sigma \approx 2\text{–}5\,\text{cm}$
> - Standard LiDAR range: up to $\mathbf{100\,m}$ with $\pm 2\,\text{cm}$ accuracy

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Autonomous Map Building

The entire process rests on three logical pillars, each handled probabilistically:

**(P1) Motion Model (Prediction Step)**
Given the previous pose estimate $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ and the control input $\mathbf{u}_t$ (odometry / wheel rotation), predict the new pose *before* sensing.

**(P2) Measurement Model (Update Step)**
Given the predicted pose, predict what the sensor *should* see. Compare this prediction with the actual measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$, and use the discrepancy to correct both pose and map.

**(P3) Map Representation**
Choose a data structure to store the environment — this dictates the entire algorithm.

### 2.2 Types of Map Representations

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU frequently tests the difference between metric, topological, and hybrid maps — memorize the table below.**

| Map Type | What is Stored | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metric — Occupancy Grid** | 2-D grid, each cell = occupied/free probability | Easy to build; intuitive | Memory grows as $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ with resolution |
| **Metric — Feature Map** | List of landmark coordinates $\{(x_i, y_i)\}$ | Compact; closed-form math | Needs detectable features |
| **Topological** | Graph of nodes (places) and edges (paths) | Very compact; great for large spaces | Hard to compute shortest geometric path |
| **Hybrid** | Topological graph + local metric grids at each node | Combines best of both | More complex implementation |

### 2.3 The Occupancy Grid Map (Most Commonly Asked in KTU)

The environment is discretized into a regular grid of cells $m_i$. Each cell holds a binary random variable:

$$m_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if cell } i \text{ is OCCUPIED} \\ 0 & \text{if cell } i \text{ is FREE} \end{cases}$$

The full map is the vector $\mathbf{m} = \{m_1, m_2, \dots, m_N\}$.

To avoid numerical underflow, the **log-odds representation** is used:

$$l_i = \log \frac{P(m_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t})}{P(m_i = 0 \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t})}$$

The update rule is the famous **additive form** (Moravec & Elfes, 1985):

$$l_i^{(t)} = l_i^{(t-1)} + \text{inv\_sensor\_model}(m_i, \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{z}_t) - l_0$$

where $l_0$ is the prior log-odds of the unknown cell ($l_0 = 0$ when prior is 0.5).

> [!TIP]
> **Why additive?** Because each measurement contributes *independent evidence*. The log-odds form converts Bayesian multiplication into a simple sum, making it ideal for real-time online map building.

### 2.4 Probabilistic SLAM — The Bayes Filter

The general SLAM posterior over robot trajectory $\mathbf{x}_{1:t}$ and map $\mathbf{m}$, given all measurements and controls, is:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_{1:t}, \mathbf{m} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$$

This is recursively updated by the **Bayes filter**:

$$p(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)\, p(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{m} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t-1})\, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

The term $\eta$ is a normalizer; the integral is the motion prediction; the leading term is the measurement update.

### 2.5 Sensor Models (Most Important in KTU)

**(A) Range Sensor Model** (Sonar / LiDAR)
For a single range reading $z_t$ from pose $(x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$, an obstacle is detected along the ray at distance $z_t$. The cell at the end of the ray is updated as **occupied**, and all cells along the ray (except the endpoint) are updated as **free** (ray-tracing).

**(B) Inverse Measurement Model**
The function used in the log-odds update:

$$\text{inv\_sensor\_model}(m_i, \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{z}_t) = \begin{cases} l_{occ} & \text{if } m_i \text{ is in the sensor field of view and } r \le z_t + \alpha/2 \\ l_{free} & \text{if } m_i \text{ is in the sensor field of view and } z_t - \alpha/2 < r < z_t \\ l_0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $r$ is the radial distance from robot to cell $m_i$, and $\alpha$ is the sensor beam-width (uncertainty in range).

### 2.6 The Kalman Filter Approach (EKF-SLAM)

When both motion and measurement are **linear-Gaussian**, the map is a list of landmarks, and the optimal estimator is the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**.

State vector:
$$\mathbf{y}_t = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{x}_t \\ \mathbf{m}_1 \\ \mathbf{m}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{m}_N \end{pmatrix}$$

Prediction:
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t \mid t-1} = A_t \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1 \mid t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t$$
$$P_{t \mid t-1} = A_t P_{t-1 \mid t-1} A_t^\top + R_t$$

Update:
$$K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^\top (H_t P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}$$
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t \mid t} = \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t \mid t-1} + K_t (\mathbf{z}_t - h(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t \mid t-1}))$$
$$P_{t \mid t} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t \mid t-1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question:**
> *"State the limitations of EKF-SLAM."*
> Answer: $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ computational cost; assumes Gaussian noise; brittle data association; quadratic memory.

### 2.7 Particle Filter Approach (FastSLAM)

FastSLAM decomposes SLAM into $N$ particle filters, each tracking $M$ landmark EKFs.

Particle weight update:
$$w_t^{[k]} = w_{t-1}^{[k]} \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t^{[k]}, \mathbf{m}_{t-1}^{[k]})$$

Per-particle landmark EKF:
$$p(\mathbf{m}_j \mid \mathbf{x}_{1:t}^{[k]}, \mathbf{z}_{1:t}) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_j^{[k]}, \Sigma_j^{[k]})$$

Complexity: $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$, much better than EKF-SLAM's $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the single most important thing to memorize before the exam.**

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pose state** | $\mathbf{x}_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)^\top$ | Position + heading | m, m, rad |
| **Control input** | $\mathbf{u}_t = (\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta \theta)$ | Odometry increment | m, m, rad |
| **Measurement** | $\mathbf{z}_t = (r, \phi)^\top$ | Range, bearing | m, rad |
| **Log-odds definition** | $l_i = \log \dfrac{P(m_i=1)}{P(m_i=0)}$ | Cell belief | unitless |
| **Probability recovery** | $P(m_i) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{l_i}}$ | Logistic inverse | unitless |
| **Log-odds update** | $l_i^{(t)} = l_i^{(t-1)} + \Delta l_i$ | Additive update | unitless |
| **Bayes filter** | $p(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}\vert\cdot) = \eta\, p(\mathbf{z}_t\vert\cdot)\int p(\mathbf{x}_t\vert\cdot)\,p(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}\vert\cdot)$ | Full posterior recursion | — |
| **EKF prediction** | $P_{t\vert t-1} = A_t P_{t-1} A_t^\top + R_t$ | Covariance propagation | — |
| **EKF gain** | $K_t = P H^\top (H P H^\top + Q)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | — |
| **Particle weight** | $w_t = w_{t-1}\, p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t)$ | Likelihood update | — |
| **EKF-SLAM cost** | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ | $N$ = landmarks | — |
| **FastSLAM cost** | $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$ | $M$ = particles | — |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Autonomous cars** (Waymo, Tesla): LiDAR + camera SLAM at 10 Hz.
- **Warehouse robots** (Amazon Kiva): Topological + metric SLAM on a 100,000-shelf grid.
- **Underwater AUVs**: Sonar-based feature SLAM in GPS-denied deep sea.
- **Mars rovers (NASA Perseverance)**: Visual-inertial SLAM on extraterrestrial terrain.
- **Domestic vacuum robots (Roomba)**: Low-cost occupancy grid using infrared + bumper sensors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes Rule to the Additive Log-Odds Update

**Goal:** Show that the recursive Bayesian update of an occupancy grid cell collapses into a simple *addition* of log-odds.

**Step 1 — Start with Bayes' rule for a single cell $m_i$:**

$$P(m_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid m_i = 1, \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1})\, P(m_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1})}{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1})}$$

*Textual explanation:* The probability that cell $i$ is occupied after seeing the latest measurement is proportional to the likelihood of that measurement given occupancy, times the prior occupancy probability.

**Step 2 — Form the odds ratio $O_i$:**

$$O_i^{(t)} = \frac{P(m_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t})}{P(m_i = 0 \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t})}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Bayes' rule into both numerator and denominator. The normalizing constant $P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1})$ cancels:**

$$O_i^{(t)} = \frac{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid m_i = 1)}{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid m_i = 0)} \cdot \frac{P(m_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1})}{P(m_i = 0 \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1})}$$

$$\implies O_i^{(t)} = \frac{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid m_i = 1)}{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid m_i = 0)} \cdot O_i^{(t-1)}$$

*Textual explanation:* The new odds are the previous odds multiplied by a *likelihood ratio* — a single number representing how strongly the new measurement favors occupancy.

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithm of both sides to obtain log-odds $l_i = \ln O_i$:**

$$l_i^{(t)} = l_i^{(t-1)} + \ln \frac{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid m_i = 1)}{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid m_i = 0)}$$

**Step 5 — Define the inverse sensor model contribution:**

$$\Delta l_i \triangleq \ln \frac{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid m_i = 1)}{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid m_i = 0)}$$

Substituting the discrete values $l_{occ}$ and $l_{free}$ from the inverse sensor model:

$$l_i^{(t)} = l_i^{(t-1)} + \begin{cases} l_{occ} - l_0 & \text{if cell along ray endpoint} \\ l_{free} - l_0 & \text{if cell along ray path} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\,l_i^{(t)} \;=\; l_i^{(t-1)} \;+\; \Delta l_i\,}$$

*Textual conclusion:* Each sensor measurement simply *adds* its evidence to the running log-odds total — the Bayesian product becomes an arithmetic sum. This is the **fundamental reason** occupancy grids are computationally cheap and used in real-time robotics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Probability Recovery from Log-Odds

**Step 1 — Start from definition:**

$$l_i = \ln \frac{P(m_i)}{1 - P(m_i)}$$

**Step 2 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$e^{l_i} = \frac{P(m_i)}{1 - P(m_i)}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply:**

$$e^{l_i}\,(1 - P(m_i)) = P(m_i)$$

**Step 4 — Expand and solve for $P(m_i)$:**

$$e^{l_i} - e^{l_i} P(m_i) = P(m_i)$$

$$e^{l_i} = P(m_i) + e^{l_i} P(m_i) = P(m_i)\,(1 + e^{l_i})$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\,P(m_i) \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + e^{-l_i}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l_i}}\,}$$

*Textual explanation:* This is the **logistic sigmoid function** applied to log-odds. It is used in the map-conversion step when the robot needs to display or threshold the grid.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Updating One Cell

> A sonar returns a range of $3.2\,\text{m}$. The cell at the ray endpoint receives a log-odds increment of $\Delta l_{occ} = +0.85$ (typical occupancy value). The cell previously had $l_i^{(t-1)} = -0.40$. Compute the new probability.

**Solution:**

$$l_i^{(t)} = l_i^{(t-1)} + \Delta l_{occ} = -0.40 + 0.85 = 0.45$$

$$P(m_i = 1) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.45}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.6376} = \frac{1}{1.6376} \approx 0.6107$$

**Conclusion:** The cell's occupancy probability rose from $\approx 40.2\%$ to $\approx 61.1\%$ after a single "occupied" sonar reading.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of SLAM Algorithms (High-Yield for KTU)

| Property | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM (RBPF) | Graph-SLAM | ORB-SLAM2 (Visual) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Map type** | Feature map | Feature map | Feature / pose graph | Sparse feature map |
| **Sensor** | Range / bearing | Range / bearing | Any | Monocular / stereo / RGB-D |
| **Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ optimize | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ per frame |
| **Assumption** | Gaussian noise, known correspondences | Particle-based, no linearity required | Sparse optimization | Feature-based, no metric depth needed |
| **Strength** | Mathematically optimal in linear-Gaussian case | Handles non-linearity, robust | Best for loop closure | Low-cost camera-only |
| **Weakness** | Quadratic cost, fragile data association | Particle depletion | Heavy offline optimization | Needs texture, scale ambiguity (mono) |
| **Typical use** | Theoretical baseline | Real-time robot demos | Post-processed mapping | Drones, AR, mobile phones |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Autonomous Map-Building System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Input z_t] --> B[Preprocessing and Noise Filtering]
    C[Odometry Input u_t] --> D[Motion Model Prediction x_t given x_t-1]
    D --> E[Predicted Pose and Predicted Map Belief]
    B --> E
    E --> F[Inverse Sensor Model Computation]
    F --> G{Map Representation Type}
    G --> H1[Occupancy Grid Update using Log Odds]
    G --> H2[Feature Map EKF Update]
    G --> H3[Topological Graph Node Add]
    H1 --> I[Updated Map Belief]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Loop Closure Detection Module]
    J --> K{Loop Closed}
    K -- Yes --> L[Global Pose Graph Optimization]
    K -- No --> M[Proceed to Next Time Step]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Exploration Planner]
    N --> O[Generate Next Control Command u_t+1]
    O --> P[Robot Actuator Output]
    P --> Q[Environment Interaction]
    Q --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam answer:**
> 1. Sensor and odometry enter in parallel.
> 2. Prediction and update merge in the "Predicted Pose + Map Belief" node.
> 3. The map representation choice branches into grid / feature / topological.
> 4. Loop closure feeds back as a global correction.
> 5. The planner closes the loop by sending the next control to the robot.

### 4.2 Occupancy Grid Update Flow (Cell-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: Cell m_i has prior l_i_t-1] --> B[Compute Distance r from robot to cell center]
    B --> C{Is cell within sensor beam footprint}
    C -- No --> Z[No update: skip cell]
    C -- Yes --> D{Is r less than measured range z_t}
    D -- Yes --> E[Mark cell FREE: add l_free - l_0 to l_i]
    D -- No --> F{Is r close to z_t within beam width alpha}
    F -- No --> Z
    F -- Yes --> G[Mark cell OCCUPIED: add l_occ - l_0 to l_i]
    E --> H[Store updated l_i_t for grid]
    G --> H
    Z --> I[Move to next cell]
    H --> I
```

### 4.3 EKF-SLAM Data Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pose x_t-1 and Map m_t-1] --> B[Predict step using motion model A_t and B_t]
    B --> C[Predicted state y_t given t-1 and predicted covariance P_t given t-1]
    C --> D[Predict expected measurement h_y_t given t-1]
    D --> E[Compare with actual measurement z_t to get innovation nu_t]
    E --> F[Compute Kalman gain K_t]
    F --> G[Update state y_t given t and covariance P_t given t]
    G --> H{Should we close a loop}
    H -- No --> I[Output SLAM estimate and proceed]
    H -- Yes --> J[Trigger global graph optimization and update all landmark covariances]
    J --> I
```

### 4.4 Map Representation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Map Type] --> B{Is environment small and structured}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Occupancy Grid Map]
    B -- No --> D{Can you extract distinct landmarks}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Feature Based Map with EKF or FastSLAM]
    D -- No --> F{Is geometric precision not required}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Topological Map]
    F -- No --> H[Use Hybrid Map: Topological graph with local grids]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define autonomous map building. List any two sensor types used in the process.*

**Model Answer:**

Autonomous map building is the capability of a mobile robot to construct a spatial representation of an unknown environment, while simultaneously estimating its own pose, using only its onboard sensors and without external aids. This is commonly formulated as the SLAM problem.

Two sensor types used:
1. **Range sensors** — Sonar, LiDAR, IR.
2. **Vision sensors** — Mono / stereo / RGB-D cameras.
*(Also acceptable: IMU, GPS denied wheel encoders, depth sensors.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *autonomous map building* = *just mapping*. Always mention **simultaneous localization**. KTU examiner deducts 1 mark if localization is missing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between metric and topological maps.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Metric Map | Topological Map |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | Continuous coordinates / grid cells | Graph of nodes and edges |
| Precision | High geometric accuracy | Approximate; path-based |
| Memory | Large | Compact |
| Example | Occupancy grid | Corridor-room graph |
| Best for | Small, structured spaces | Large, open spaces |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not write only a one-line "metric has coordinates, topological has nodes". KTU expects a **comparison table** with at least three differentiating points for full marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **MODULE 3 INTERNAL CHOICE — Choose ONE of the two questions below.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Equivalent]**

**(a)** Explain the **occupancy grid map** representation in detail. Derive the additive log-odds update rule from Bayes' theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an autonomous map-building system. Explain the role of the inverse sensor model. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**Step 1 — Definition of occupancy grid.**
The environment is discretized into $N$ cells $\{m_1, m_2, \dots, m_N\}$. Each cell stores a probability of occupancy $P(m_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t})$.

**Step 2 — Bayes' rule application.**

$$P(m_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid m_i = 1)\, P(m_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1})}{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 3 — Form odds ratio $O_i = P(m_i=1)/P(m_i=0)$.** Substitute and cancel normalizer.

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithm.**

$$l_i^{(t)} = l_i^{(t-1)} + \ln \frac{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid m_i = 1)}{P(\mathbf{z}_t \mid m_i = 0)}$$

**Step 5 — Define inverse sensor model.**

$$\text{inv\_sensor\_model}(m_i, \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{z}_t) = \begin{cases} l_{occ} & \text{ray endpoint in field of view} \\ l_{free} & \text{along ray before endpoint} \\ l_0 & \text{outside field of view} \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Final additive rule.**

$$\boxed{\,l_i^{(t)} = l_i^{(t-1)} + \text{inv\_sensor\_model}(m_i, \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{z}_t) - l_0\,}$$

**Conclusion:** Multiplicative Bayesian product becomes an additive log-odds update, enabling real-time implementation. *(7 marks: 1 definition, 2 derivation, 2 inverse model, 2 final rule and conclusion.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above):** Sensor input → Preprocessing → Motion model → Predicted state → Inverse sensor model → Map representation → Updated map → Loop closure detection → Planner → Actuator → Environment → (back to sensor).

**Role of inverse sensor model (write in 4 crisp points):**
1. It converts a raw sensor reading $\mathbf{z}_t$ into a belief update for every cell in the field of view.
2. It assigns $l_{occ}$ to the cell at the measured range (likely occupied).
3. It assigns $l_{free}$ to cells along the ray (likely free, since the beam traversed them).
4. It leaves all other cells untouched, preserving prior belief.

**Conclusion:** The inverse sensor model is the **bridge between physical measurements and probabilistic map beliefs**, and is the only place where sensor physics enters the map update equation. *(7 marks: 3 diagram, 4 explanation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Equivalent]**

**(a)** Explain the **EKF-SLAM algorithm** with state vector definition, prediction step, and update step equations. State its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **occupancy grid mapping, feature-based mapping, and topological mapping** in a table. State one application where each is best suited. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**Step 1 — State vector.**
The EKF-SLAM maintains a single combined state vector containing the robot pose and all landmark positions:

$$\mathbf{y}_t = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{x}_t \\ \mathbf{m}_1 \\ \mathbf{m}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{m}_N \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Prediction step.**
Given the previous state and control $\mathbf{u}_t$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t \mid t-1} = A_t \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t-1 \mid t-1} + B_t \mathbf{u}_t$$
$$P_{t \mid t-1} = A_t P_{t-1 \mid t-1} A_t^\top + R_t$$

**Step 3 — Update step.**
Compute innovation $\nu_t = \mathbf{z}_t - h(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t \mid t-1})$, Kalman gain, and update:

$$K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^\top (H_t P_{t \mid t-1} H_t^\top + Q_t)^{-1}$$
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t \mid t} = \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{t \mid t-1} + K_t \nu_t$$
$$P_{t \mid t} = (I - K_t H_t) P_{t \mid t-1}$$

**Step 4 — Limitations (any 3):**
- Computational cost is $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ in the number of landmarks.
- Assumes Gaussian noise and linearizable models.
- Brittle data association — false matches corrupt the entire map.
- Quadratic memory growth with map size.
- Cannot recover from incorrect loop closures.

*(7 marks: 1 state definition, 2 prediction, 2 update, 2 limitations.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Occupancy Grid | Feature Map | Topological Map |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data structure | 2-D array of cells | List of landmark vectors | Graph of nodes + edges |
| Memory cost | High ($\mathcal{O}(N^2)$) | Low ($\mathcal{O}(M)$) | Very low |
| Geometric accuracy | High | High | Low |
| Sensor suitability | Sonar, LiDAR | Cameras, laser landmarks | Any |
| Path planning ease | Easy (grid search) | Moderate (geometric) | Easy (graph search) |
| Loop closure | Difficult | Built-in via data association | Native (re-visit node) |
| Best application | Indoor room (Roomba) | Landmark-rich indoor (museum) | Large warehouse corridors |

**Best applications:**
- **Occupancy grid:** Domestic floor-cleaning robot.
- **Feature map:** Self-driving car in urban environment with curb and sign landmarks.
- **Topological map:** Multi-floor building navigation (lift between floors).

*(7 marks: 4 table, 3 applications.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master these before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **Autonomous map building = SLAM** (Simultaneous Localization **and** Mapping).
- [x] Three pillars: **Motion model, Measurement model, Map representation**.
- [x] Three map types: **Metric (grid / feature), Topological, Hybrid**.
- [x] **Occupancy grid**: cells store $P(m_i = 1)$; use **log-odds** for numerical stability.
- [x] **Log-odds update is ADDITIVE**: $l_i^{(t)} = l_i^{(t-1)} + \Delta l_i$.
- [x] **Probability recovery** uses the **logistic sigmoid**: $P(m_i) = 1/(1 + e^{-l_i})$.
- [x] **EKF-SLAM state vector** = pose + all landmarks; cost $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$.
- [x] **FastSLAM** uses particles + per-landmark EKF; cost $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$.
- [x] **Inverse sensor model** converts raw measurement to occupancy belief.
- [x] **Loop closure** detects re-visited places and corrects accumulated drift.
- [x] **Data association** = matching current observations to stored features.
- [x] **Log-odds of unknown cell** = 0; **occupied** → positive; **free** → negative.
- [x] **Exploration vs. exploitation** is the planning trade-off in autonomous mapping.
- [x] **Limitations of EKF-SLAM**: quadratic cost, Gaussian assumption, fragile association.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Final Warning:**
> In 14-mark questions, **always draw a labelled block diagram**. Examiners allocate **3 marks minimum** for a correct diagram. Text-only answers are penalised even if the derivation is perfect. Also, **always state units** in numerical problems — missing units cost 1 mark per occurrence.

---
