topics.title: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition — Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)**
> SLAM is the computational problem in robotics concerned with constructing or incrementally updating a **map of an unknown environment** while simultaneously **estimating the robot's pose (position and orientation)** within that very map. It is formally classified as an *online* state-estimation problem in which the robot has no prior knowledge of the world geometry and must rely entirely on its onboard sensors and control inputs.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, SLAM is treated as a **probabilistic inference** problem over the joint state:

$$P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

where the posterior probability of the robot trajectory $x_t$ and the map $m$ is computed given all sensor observations $z_{1:t}$ and control commands $u_{1:t}$ up to time $t$.

## 1.2 The "Chicken-and-Egg" Dilemma

SLAM is famously known as the **"chicken-and-egg problem"** of mobile robotics:

- To build an accurate map, the robot must know its exact pose at every instant.
- To estimate its pose, the robot must already have an accurate map.

> [!NOTE] **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Most 3-mark questions test whether the student can articulate this circular dependency. Examiners expect the answer: *"Localization requires a map, mapping requires localization — SLAM solves both simultaneously using probabilistic estimation."*

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **blindfolded and dropped inside a large, unfamiliar shopping mall** at midnight:

- You cannot see the full layout — you only feel walls, pillars, and doors with your hands as you walk.
- You have a **pedometer** (odometry) that tells you roughly how many steps you took, but it drifts over distance.
- As you explore, you must:
  1. **Remember** which walls you touched (build a map).
  2. **Track** which room you are currently in (localize yourself).
  3. **Recognize** when you return to a previously explored corridor (loop closure).

SLAM algorithms are the mathematical equivalent of this process — they use **Bayesian filtering, sensor fusion, and graph optimization** to perform all three tasks in real time, at **sensor frame-rates (typically 10 Hz – 100 Hz)**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Terminology

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Range |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Robot pose dimension | $n$ | **2 (2D)** or **3 (6-DOF: x, y, z, roll, pitch, yaw)** |
| Sensor scan rate | $f_s$ | **10 Hz – 100 Hz** (LiDAR), **30 Hz – 60 Hz** (Camera) |
| Map resolution | $\Delta$ | **0.05 m – 0.30 m** per cell (occupancy grid) |
| Process noise covariance | $Q_t$ | tuning parameter (diagonal matrix) |
| Measurement noise covariance | $R_t$ | tuning parameter (diagonal matrix) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Probabilistic Formulation of SLAM

The SLAM problem is mathematically decomposed into two **recursive Bayesian models**:

1. **Motion Model (Prediction Step):** Predicts the new robot state using the previous state and control input.

$$x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$$

2. **Observation Model (Measurement Step):** Predicts what the sensor should observe given the current pose and map.

$$z_{t,i} = h(x_t, m_i) + v_{t,i}, \quad v_{t,i} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$$

Where:
- $x_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — robot pose vector at time $t$
- $u_t \in \mathbb{R}^p$ — control input (velocity, steering angle, IMU bias, etc.)
- $z_{t,i} \in \mathbb{R}^k$ — observation of the $i^{th}$ landmark
- $m_i \in \mathbb{R}^q$ — coordinates of the $i^{th}$ landmark in the map
- $w_t, v_{t,i}$ — Gaussian white noise terms
- $Q_t, R_t$ — process and measurement noise covariance matrices

## 2.2 Two-Step Bayesian Recursion

> [!NOTE] **Core KTU Concept — Prediction then Update**

**Step 1 — Prediction (a-priori estimate):**

$$\hat{x}_t^- = f(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$$

$$P_t^- = A_t P_{t-1} A_t^T + Q_t$$

**Step 2 — Update (a-posteriori estimate using measurement $z_t$):**

$$K_t = P_t^- H_t^T \left( H_t P_t^- H_t^T + R_t \right)^{-1} \quad \text{(Kalman Gain)}$$

$$\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t \left( z_t - h(\hat{x}_t^-) \right)$$

$$P_t = (I - K_t H_t) P_t^-$$

Where $A_t = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\big\vert_{\hat{x}_{t-1}}$ and $H_t = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}\big\vert_{\hat{x}_t^-}$ are the **Jacobians** of the motion and observation functions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 — Must-Memorize Equations Table**

| # | Equation | Description | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | Full SLAM posterior | Foundation of all SLAM |
| 2 | $x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$ | Motion model | Odometry propagation |
| 3 | $z_{t,i} = h(x_t, m_i) + v_{t,i}$ | Observation model | Sensor measurement update |
| 4 | $\hat{x}_t^- = f(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$ | Predicted state | EKF-SLAM / PF-SLAM |
| 5 | $P_t^- = A_t P_{t-1} A_t^T + Q_t$ | Predicted covariance | Uncertainty propagation |
| 6 | $K_t = P_t^- H_t^T (H_t P_t^- H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | Sensor fusion weight |
| 7 | $\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t (z_t - h(\hat{x}_t^-))$ | State update | Measurement correction |
| 8 | $P_t = (I - K_t H_t) P_t^-$ | Updated covariance | Post-measurement uncertainty |
| 9 | $m(x, y) = \log \frac{P(\text{occupied} \mid z)}{P(\text{free} \mid z)}$ | Occupancy grid log-odds | Mapping front-end |
| 10 | $e_{ij} = z_{ij} - \hat{z}_{ij}$ | Loop closure error | Graph-SLAM back-end |

> **⚠️ Important Notation Note (KTU Board Standard):** In the equations above, the notation $A_t P_{t-1} A_t^T$ means matrix multiplication of $A_t$ with $P_{t-1}$ and the transpose of $A_t$ — NOT an absolute value. The vertical bar symbol must never be used inside the cells of a markdown table to avoid syntax breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

SLAM is the foundational algorithm behind:
- **Autonomous Vehicles** (e.g., Google's Waymo, Tesla's FSD Beta)
- **Domestic Vacuum Robots** (iRobot Roomba, Xiaomi Roborock)
- **Warehouse AGVs** (Amazon Kiva robots)
- **Aerial Drones** (DJI indoor navigation, DARPA SubT Challenge)
- **AR/VR Devices** (Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest 3 spatial mapping)
- **Planetary Rovers** (NASA Perseverance, ESA ExoMars)

The industry-standard implementations include **ORB-SLAM3** (visual), **Cartographer** (LiDAR, by Google), **Hector-SLAM**, **GMapping** (2D), and **LOAM** (3D LiDAR odometry).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the EKF-SLAM Update Step

The Extended Kalman Filter SLAM update step is the most heavily tested concept in KTU Board examinations. Below is the **complete, exhaustive derivation** showing every algebraic transition.

### Step 1 — Define the Innovation (Measurement Residual)

The innovation is the difference between the actual sensor measurement and the predicted measurement:

$$\nu_t = z_t - h(\hat{x}_t^-)$$

> *Textual logic:* The innovation measures "how wrong our prediction was." If $\nu_t \to 0$, the prediction matches the sensor; if $\nu_t$ is large, the sensor saw something unexpected.

### Step 2 — Innovation Covariance

The uncertainty associated with the innovation is:

$$S_t = H_t P_t^- H_t^T + R_t$$

> *Textual logic:* $H_t P_t^- H_t^T$ transforms the predicted pose uncertainty into the measurement space, and $R_t$ adds the inherent sensor noise. The sum is the **total uncertainty in the innovation**.

### Step 3 — Kalman Gain Computation

The optimal weighting factor that minimizes the a-posteriori error covariance is:

$$K_t = P_t^- H_t^T S_t^{-1} = P_t^- H_t^T (H_t P_t^- H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$$

> *Textual logic:* $K_t$ decides how much we "trust" the new measurement vs. our prediction. When $R_t$ is small (accurate sensor), $K_t$ is large and we trust the measurement more. When $P_t^-$ is small (we are confident about prediction), $K_t$ is small.

### Step 4 — State Update (Correction)

The a-posteriori state estimate is the prediction plus the Kalman-weighted innovation:

$$\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t \nu_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t (z_t - h(\hat{x}_t^-))$$

> *Textual logic:* We add a "correction term" to the prediction. The magnitude of the correction is proportional to the innovation and scaled by the Kalman gain.

### Step 5 — Covariance Update (Joseph form for numerical stability)

The standard form is:

$$P_t = (I - K_t H_t) P_t^-$$

> *Textual logic:* The a-posteriori uncertainty must be **smaller** than the a-priori uncertainty (otherwise the measurement gave us no information). This step formally shrinks the covariance ellipse.

For numerical robustness in real implementations, the **Joseph form** is preferred:

$$P_t = (I - K_t H_t) P_t^- (I - K_t H_t)^T + K_t R_t K_t^T$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 2D SLAM Landmark Observation

**Problem Setup:** A differential-drive robot at pose $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T = [2.0, 1.0, 30°]^T$ observes a landmark at known map position $m_i = [5.0, 4.0]^T$ via a range-bearing sensor.

**Observation Model (range $r$ and bearing $\phi$):**

$$h(x_t, m_i) = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ \phi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{i,x} - x)^2 + (m_{i,y} - y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{i,y} - y,\ m_{i,x} - x) - \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Compute Predicted Range:**

$$r = \sqrt{(5.0 - 2.0)^2 + (4.0 - 1.0)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.243 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Predicted Bearing:**

$$\phi = \text{atan2}(4.0 - 1.0,\ 5.0 - 2.0) - 30° = \text{atan2}(3, 3) - 30°$$

$$\phi = 45° - 30° = 15°$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Jacobian $H_t$ at the predicted state:**

For the range component: $\dfrac{\partial r}{\partial x} = \dfrac{x - m_{i,x}}{r} = \dfrac{2.0 - 5.0}{4.243} = \dfrac{-3.0}{4.243} \approx -0.707$

For the bearing component: $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \dfrac{m_{i,y} - y}{r^2} = \dfrac{4.0 - 1.0}{18} = \dfrac{3.0}{18} = 0.1667 \text{ rad/m}$

> *Textual logic:* The Jacobian $H_t$ linearizes the nonlinear observation function around the current estimate — this is what makes the filter "Extended."

**Step 4 — Apply the EKF Update:**

Given $P_t^- = \text{diag}(0.1, 0.1, 0.05)$ and $R_t = \text{diag}(0.2, 0.05)$:

$$H_t P_t^- H_t^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 + 0.05 & -0.035 \\ -0.035 & 0.028 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.10 & -0.035 \\ -0.035 & 0.028 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$S_t = H_t P_t^- H_t^T + R_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.30 & -0.035 \\ -0.035 & 0.078 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$K_t = P_t^- H_t^T S_t^{-1} \approx \text{(computed via matrix inversion)}$$

> The final pose correction $\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t (z_t - h(\hat{x}_t^-))$ is then applied iteratively as more landmark observations arrive.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Major SLAM Algorithms

> [!NOTE] **KTU Exam Tip:** This comparison table is a 14-mark question favorite. Memorize at least three rows.

| Algorithm | Sensor Type | Filter Type | Strength | Weakness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **EKF-SLAM** | LiDAR / Sonar | Extended Kalman Filter | Mathematically rigorous, optimal for small maps | $O(n^2)$ complexity, fragile data association |
| **FastSLAM** | LiDAR | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter | Handles non-Gaussian noise, scalable to ~50k landmarks | Particle depletion problem |
| **Graph-SLAM** | LiDAR / Camera | Pose-graph optimization | Globally consistent, handles loop closures well | Requires good initial estimate |
| **ORB-SLAM3** | Monocular / Stereo / RGB-D | Feature-based + BA | Excellent in textured environments, open-source | Fails in low-texture / low-light scenes |
| **LIO-SAM** | LiDAR + IMU | Factor graph + iSAM2 | High accuracy outdoors, tightly-coupled fusion | Computationally expensive |
| **Cartographer** | 2D/3D LiDAR | Scan-matching + optimization | Real-time, Google's battle-tested code | Parameter tuning is non-trivial |
| **Hector-SLAM** | 2D LiDAR | Scan-correlation | No odometry needed | Sensitive to fast motion |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a SLAM System

The diagram below illustrates the complete **sensor-to-map pipeline** of a typical LiDAR-based SLAM system. The architecture is divided into the **Front-End** (sensor processing and data association) and the **Back-End** (state estimation and optimization).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Input Layer] --> B[Sensor Pre-Processing Node]
    B --> C[Front-End Feature Extraction]
    C --> D[Data Association Module]
    D --> E[Back-End State Estimator EKF or Particle Filter]
    E --> F[Map Update Module]
    F --> G[Loop Closure Detection]
    G --> H[Pose Graph Optimization]
    H --> I[Global Consistent Map Output]
    E --> J[Robot Pose Output]
    F --> K[Occupancy Grid or Feature Map]

    A1[LiDAR Sensor] -.-> A
    A2[Camera Sensor] -.-> A
    A3[IMU Sensor] -.-> A
    A4[Wheel Odometry] -.-> A

    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style E fill:#98FB98
    style H fill:#87CEEB
    style I fill:#FFB6C1
    style K fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.2 Detailed Data-Flow and Control-Flow Diagram

The following diagram shows the **closed-loop interaction** between prediction, update, and map correction. It explicitly maps the equations from Section 3 into their corresponding functional blocks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRE[Prediction Step]
        P1[Previous Pose Xt minus 1] --> P2[Motion Model f Xt minus 1, Ut]
        P3[Process Noise Qt] --> P2
        P2 --> P4[Predicted Pose Xt bar]
        P5[Previous Covariance Pt minus 1] --> P6[At Pt minus 1 At transpose plus Qt]
        P6 --> P7[Predicted Covariance Pt bar]
    end

    subgraph UPD[Update Step]
        U1[Sensor Measurement Zt] --> U2[Innovation nu equals Zt minus h Xt bar]
        P4 --> U2
        U2 --> U3[Innovation Covariance St]
        P7 --> U3
        U4[Measurement Noise Rt] --> U3
        U3 --> U5[Kalman Gain Kt]
        P7 --> U5
        U5 --> U6[Corrected Pose Xt]
        U2 --> U6
        U5 --> U7[Updated Covariance Pt]
    end

    P4 --> OUT[Robot Pose and Map Output]
    U6 --> OUT
    U7 --> OUT

    style PRE fill:#FFF8DC
    style UPD fill:#E0FFFF
    style OUT fill:#FFA07A
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the SLAM Chicken-and-Egg Loop

The diagram below visually captures the **circular dependency** that makes SLAM a hard problem and shows how the probabilistic framework breaks the cycle using Bayes' theorem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Need Accurate Pose to Build Map] --> Q2[Need Accurate Map to Localize]
    Q2 --> Q1
    Q1 -.SLAM Solution.-> Q3[Joint Bayesian Estimation]
    Q2 -.SLAM Solution.-> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4[Simultaneous Pose and Map Estimate]
    Q4 --> R1[Robot Navigation]
    Q4 --> R2[Autonomous Path Planning]
    Q4 --> R3[Obstacle Avoidance]
    Q4 --> R4[Human Robot Interaction]

    style Q1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style Q2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style Q3 fill:#98FB98
    style Q4 fill:#87CEEB
    style R1 fill:#DDA0DD
    style R2 fill:#DDA0DD
    style R3 fill:#DDA0DD
    style R4 fill:#DDA0DD
```

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight:** The block diagrams above correspond to the software architecture used in production SLAM stacks like **ROS 2 Navigation2** and **Google Cartographer**. In a KTU viva, you may be asked to identify which block performs "data association" or "loop closure" — the answer maps directly to the labels in the diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define SLAM. Mention any two sensors commonly used in SLAM systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) is the computational technique that enables a mobile robot to **build a map of an unknown environment** while **simultaneously estimating its own position and orientation** within that map, using only onboard sensors and without prior knowledge of the environment.
>
> **Sensors (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. **2D/3D LiDAR** — provides accurate range measurements (accuracy ±2 cm, range up to 100 m).
> 2. **Stereo / Monocular / RGB-D Camera** — provides rich visual features for visual SLAM.
> 3. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** — provides high-rate acceleration and angular velocity data.
> 4. **Wheel Odometry Encoders** — provides incremental motion estimates.
> 5. **GPS** — provides global position (only outdoors).

---

**Q2. What is loop closure detection in SLAM? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Loop closure detection is the process of **recognizing that the robot has re-visited a previously mapped location** during its exploration. It is implemented by comparing the current sensor scan or visual feature set against the stored map using techniques like **Bag-of-Words (DBoW2)**, scan-to-map matching, or descriptor-based place recognition.
>
> **Importance (1 mark):** Loop closure is **critical for global map consistency**. Without it, accumulated odometry drift causes the map to become distorted, scaled, or duplicated. Detecting a loop closure triggers a **pose-graph optimization** step that re-aligns the entire trajectory and corrects accumulated errors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam — Module 3 Coverage]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the mathematical formulation of the SLAM problem, including the motion model and the observation model. Derive the recursive Bayesian estimation equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **EKF-SLAM** algorithm in detail, including the prediction and update steps with full mathematical equations. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Mathematical Formulation (3 marks):**

The SLAM problem is formulated as estimating the **joint posterior probability distribution**:

$$P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

This expresses the probability of the robot pose $x_t$ and the entire map $m$ given the history of measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$.

**Motion Model (2 marks):**

The robot's pose evolves according to:

$$x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$$

with $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ being zero-mean Gaussian process noise.

**Observation Model (1 mark):**

$$z_{t,i} = h(x_t, m_i) + v_{t,i}$$

with $v_{t,i} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ being zero-mean Gaussian measurement noise.

**Bayesian Recursion (1 mark — bonus):**

The posterior is updated recursively using:

$$P(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid x_t) \int P(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) P(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) dx_{t-1}$$

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**EKF-SLAM Prediction Step (3.5 marks):**

$$\hat{x}_t^- = f(\hat{x}_{t-1}, u_t)$$

$$P_t^- = A_t P_{t-1} A_t^T + Q_t$$

where $A_t = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\big\vert_{\hat{x}_{t-1}}$ is the Jacobian of the motion model.

**EKF-SLAM Update Step (3.5 marks):**

Innovation: $\nu_t = z_t - h(\hat{x}_t^-)$

Innovation covariance: $S_t = H_t P_t^- H_t^T + R_t$

Kalman gain: $K_t = P_t^- H_t^T S_t^{-1}$

State update: $\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t \nu_t$

Covariance update: $P_t = (I - K_t H_t) P_t^-$

The EKF-SLAM algorithm maintains a **single state vector** containing both robot pose and all landmark positions, with the covariance matrix growing as $O(n^2)$ where $n$ is the number of landmarks — this is its main scalability limitation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **front-end and back-end architecture** of a modern SLAM system. List the main responsibilities of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, and Graph-SLAM**. Mention one real-world implementation of each. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Front-End (3.5 marks):** The front-end is the **sensor-processing layer** that handles raw data and converts it into geometrically meaningful information. Its responsibilities are:
1. **Sensor pre-processing** — de-noising LiDAR scans, undistorting camera images, IMU bias correction.
2. **Feature extraction** — detecting SIFT, ORB, SURF, or LiDAR corner/keypoint features.
3. **Data association** — matching current observations to previously stored landmarks (using descriptor distance, Mahalanobis gating, or ICP).
4. **Local odometry estimation** — computing short-term relative motion via scan-matching or visual odometry.

**Back-End (3.5 marks):** The back-end performs **global state estimation and optimization**. Its responsibilities are:
1. **Filter-based estimation** — EKF, UKF, or Particle Filter for fusing front-end measurements over time.
2. **Pose-graph optimization** — building a graph where nodes are robot poses and edges are odometry/loop-closure constraints, then solving via **g2o, GTSAM, or Ceres Solver**.
3. **Loop closure integration** — using the front-end's loop detection signal to add new constraints and re-optimize the global map.
4. **Map management** — maintaining the final globally consistent occupancy grid, feature map, or point-cloud map.

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

| Criterion | EKF-SLAM | FastSLAM | Graph-SLAM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Filter Type** | Extended Kalman Filter | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter | Pose-graph optimization |
| **Complexity** | $O(n^2)$ — quadratic in landmarks | $O(M \log n)$ — $M$ particles | $O(n^3)$ per optimization iteration |
| **Strength** | Optimal for small linear-Gaussian maps | Handles non-linear / non-Gaussian data | Globally consistent, handles large loops |
| **Weakness** | Does not scale beyond ~1000 landmarks | Particle depletion | Requires good initial guess |
| **Real-world implementation** | **Matlab EKF-SLAM (Probabilistic Robotics by Thrun)** | **Montemerlo's FastSLAM 1.0/2.0** | **Google Cartographer, ORB-SLAM3** |

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing EKF-SLAM with Kalman Filter:** Many students write the basic Kalman Filter equations without the **Jacobian linearization** $A_t, H_t$. The "Extended" in EKF refers specifically to the Jacobian computation — forgetting it costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up prior and posterior notation:** Writing $\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t(z_t - h(\hat{x}_t^-))$ incorrectly with the *posteriori* on both sides. The minus sign in $\hat{x}_t^-$ denotes the **prediction (before measurement)** — examiners deduct marks for this confusion.
> 3. **Omitting noise distributions:** SLAM equations must always include the Gaussian noise assumption $w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ and $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$. A bare equation without noise loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the dimension of $P_t$:** In EKF-SLAM the covariance matrix is **augmented** to include both robot pose and landmark states — its dimension is $(3 + 2n) \times (3 + 2n)$ for 2D pose + 2D landmarks. Examiners test this in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Confusing "Full SLAM" vs. "Online SLAM":** Full SLAM estimates the *entire trajectory* $P(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$; Online SLAM estimates only the *current pose* $P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. This distinction is worth 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bullet-Point Summary**

- ✅ **SLAM = Simultaneous Localization and Mapping** — robot builds a map **and** estimates its pose at the same time, with **no prior map knowledge**.
- ✅ The problem is also called the **"chicken-and-egg problem"** of mobile robotics because localization needs a map and mapping needs localization.
- ✅ The mathematical goal is to estimate the **posterior** $P(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$.
- ✅ **Two core equations:** motion model $x_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t) + w_t$ and observation model $z_{t,i} = h(x_t, m_i) + v_{t,i}$.
- ✅ **EKF-SLAM** uses the Extended Kalman Filter with Jacobians $A_t$ and $H_t$ to linearize the non-linear models.
- ✅ The **Kalman Gain** $K_t = P_t^- H_t^T (H_t P_t^- H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$ balances prediction vs. measurement trust.
- ✅ **Loop closure** = recognizing a previously visited place; it triggers **pose-graph optimization** to fix accumulated drift.
- ✅ **Front-end** = sensor pre-processing, feature extraction, data association, local odometry.
- ✅ **Back-end** = global filter, pose-graph optimization, loop closure integration, map management.
- ✅ **Three major algorithm families:** EKF-SLAM (small maps), FastSLAM (medium, non-Gaussian), Graph-SLAM (large, globally consistent).
- ✅ **Sensors:** LiDAR (accurate, expensive), Camera (cheap, rich data, fails in low light), IMU (high-rate, drifts), Wheel Odometry (cheap, drifts fast), GPS (outdoor only).
- ✅ **Map representations:** Occupancy Grid (2D), Point Cloud (3D), Feature Map (sparse landmarks), Topological Map (graph of places).
- ✅ **Real-world stacks:** Google Cartographer (LiDAR), ORB-SLAM3 (Visual), LIO-SAM (LiDAR-IMU), ROS 2 Navigation2.
- ✅ **Industry applications:** Autonomous cars, domestic robots, warehouse AGVs, drones, AR/VR, planetary rovers, underwater AUVs.
- ✅ **Key constants to memorize:** Sensor scan rate 10–100 Hz, map resolution 0.05–0.30 m, LiDAR accuracy ±2 cm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graph Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Graph Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Graph Search** is a class of systematic exploration algorithms that traverse a graph (or tree) representation of a robot's discrete state space — either its **workspace** (geometric space) or **configuration space (C-space)** — to discover a feasible, and often optimal, sequence of transitions from an **initial node** to a **goal node**.

In robotics, a *node* represents a robot configuration (a pose, a grid cell, or a sampled state), and an *edge* represents a valid motion primitive or local move between two configurations. The search algorithm expands nodes selectively, guided by cost accumulators and (optionally) heuristic estimates, until a goal condition is met.

### 1.2 Key Vocabulary for KTU Exams

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **State Space Graph** | Directed/undirected graph G = (V, E) where V = states, E = feasible transitions |
| **Start Node (s)** | The robot's initial configuration from which search begins |
| **Goal Node (g)** | Target configuration the robot must reach |
| **Successor Function** | Generates neighbour nodes of the current node (via motion primitives) |
| **Open List (Frontier)** | Set of nodes discovered but not yet expanded |
| **Closed List (Explored Set)** | Set of nodes already expanded |
| **Path Cost g(n)** | Accumulated cost from start to node n |
| **Heuristic h(n)** | Estimated cost from n to the goal |
| **Evaluation f(n) = g(n) + h(n)** | Total estimated cost of a path through n |
| **Admissibility** | A heuristic that never overestimates true cost: h(n) ≤ h*(n) |
| **Consistency (Monotonicity)** | Triangle-inequality: h(n) ≤ c(n, n') + h(n') |
| **Branching Factor b** | Average number of successors per node |
| **Solution Depth d** | Number of edges in the shortest path found |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Blind vs. Informed Mice in a Maze"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine two mice placed in a maze.
> - **Mouse 1 (Uninformed Search — BFS, DFS, UCS):** Tries every direction systematically, with no sense of where the cheese lies. BFS explores layer-by-layer; DFS dives deep; UCS prefers the cheapest path-taken so far.
> - **Mouse 2 (Informed Search — Greedy, A\*):** Can *smell* the cheese and combines that estimate (heuristic) with the distance it has already travelled (path cost) to pick the most promising next step.

The heuristic is the "smell" — it makes the search *focused* and dramatically reduces the number of nodes expanded. In robotics, the *smell* is computed from a domain-aware estimate (e.g., straight-line distance in a warehouse, remaining path length on a road network).

### 1.4 Why Graph Search Matters in Robotics

Graph search underpins nearly every discrete planning layer in a robot:

1. **Mobile Robot Navigation** — grid-based floor plans for service robots in hospitals/warehouses.
2. **Manipulator Path Planning** — joint-space discretisation for pick-and-place arms.
3. **Autonomous Vehicles** — lane-graph search for route planning on road networks.
4. **Exploration & SLAM** — frontier-based exploration builds a search frontier over unknown maps.
5. **Multi-Robot Coordination** — joint-state graph search for collision-free scheduling.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Whenever a question mentions "shortest path," "minimum cost," or "optimal navigation in a discrete map," the examiner is almost always testing **Uninformed** *or* **Informed Graph Search** — your answer must classify the algorithm, justify its properties, and trace it on a small example.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-List Architecture of Graph Search

All classic graph search algorithms share the same backbone — a **frontier (open list)** and an **explored set (closed list)**. The *strategy* differs in *which* node is removed from the frontier for expansion.

**Generic Graph Search Procedure:**

1. Insert the start node s into the Open List with g(s) = 0, f(s) = h(s).
2. **Loop:** If Open List is empty → return FAILURE.
3. Remove node n with the *smallest* selection criterion (varies by algorithm).
4. If n satisfies the goal test → return reconstructed path (trace back via parent pointers).
5. For each successor n' of n:
   - Compute tentative g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n').
   - If n' is in Closed and tentative g ≥ stored g → **skip** (graph-search pruning).
   - If n' is in Open and tentative g ≥ stored g → **skip**.
   - Otherwise, set parent(n') = n, update g(n'), f(n'), and push n' into Open.
6. Insert n into Closed List.
7. Goto Step 2.

The **selection criterion** in Step 3 is the soul of the algorithm:

| Algorithm | Selection Criterion | Type |
|---|---|---|
| Breadth-First Search (BFS) | Shallowest depth (FIFO queue) | Uninformed |
| Depth-First Search (DFS) | Deepest node (LIFO stack) | Uninformed |
| Uniform-Cost Search (UCS) | Smallest g(n) | Uninformed |
| Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS) | DFS with bounded depth, increasing | Uninformed |
| Greedy Best-First | Smallest h(n) | Informed |
| A\* Search | Smallest f(n) = g(n) + h(n) | Informed |
| Bidirectional Search | From both s and g simultaneously | Hybrid |

### 2.2 The Cost Function Family

The cornerstone of informed search is the **evaluation function**:

$$
f(n) \;=\; g(n) \;+\; h(n)
$$

where:

- $g(n)$ = *actual* cost incurred from start to reach n (always known exactly).
- $h(n)$ = *estimated* cost from n to the nearest goal.
- $f(n)$ = *estimated* total cost of the cheapest solution passing through n.

**Heuristic Variants on a 2D Grid (common in KTU problems):**

| Heuristic | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Manhattan (L1) | $h(n) = \lvert x_n - x_g \rvert + \lvert y_n - y_g \rvert$ | Admissible for **4-connected** grids (no diagonals) |
| Euclidean (L2) | $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$ | Admissible for **8-connected** or continuous motion |
| Chebyshev (L∞) | $h(n) = \max\!\big(\lvert x_n - x_g \rvert,\, \lvert y_n - y_g \rvert\big)$ | Admissible when diagonal step cost = 1 |
| Octile | $h(n) = D \cdot \max(\Delta x, \Delta y) + (D_2 - D)\cdot \min(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ | Admissible for 8-connected with $D = 1$ and $D_2 = \sqrt{2}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** On an 8-connected grid where diagonal moves cost $\sqrt{2}$, the Manhattan distance *overestimates* and is therefore **inadmissible** — a favourite trap question in valuation. Always choose the heuristic that matches the motion model.

### 2.3 Admissibility vs. Consistency — When a Heuristic is "Good Enough"

> [!NOTE]
> **Admissibility:** A heuristic h is admissible iff for every node n,
>
> $$
> 0 \le h(n) \le h^{*}(n)
> $$
>
> where $h^{*}(n)$ is the true optimal cost from n to the goal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Consistency (Monotonicity):** A heuristic h is consistent iff for every node n and every successor n',
>
> $$
> h(n) \;\le\; c(n, n') \;+\; h(n')
> $$
>
> where $c(n, n')$ is the step cost.

**Implications for A\* performance:**

| Property | Guarantees |
|---|---|
| h is admissible | A\* finds an **optimal** path (graph search variant) |
| h is consistent | A\* never re-opens a closed node ⇒ **f-values are non-decreasing** along any path ⇒ efficient |
| h is **inadmissible** | A\* may return a *faster* but *sub-optimal* solution (sometimes desirable in time-critical robotics) |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Property | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Evaluation function | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Scalar cost |
| 2 | Manhattan heuristic | $h(n) = \lvert x_n - x_g \rvert + \lvert y_n - y_g \rvert$ | Grid cells |
| 3 | Euclidean heuristic | $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$ | Grid cells |
| 4 | Admissibility | $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ for all n | Inequality |
| 5 | Consistency | $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$ | Triangle inequality form |
| 6 | BFS time complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ for explicit graph; $O(b^{d})$ for unit-cost tree | branching factor b, depth d |
| 7 | BFS space complexity | $O(b^{d})$ | Stores frontier |
| 8 | DFS time complexity | $O(b^{m})$ where m = max depth | May explore entire tree |
| 9 | DFS space complexity | $O(b \cdot m)$ | Single path + siblings |
| 10 | UCS / Dijkstra time | $O\!\big((V + E)\log V\big)$ with a priority queue | Weighted graph |
| 11 | UCS space | $O(V)$ | All reached nodes |
| 12 | A\* time (worst) | $O(b^{d})$ | Exponential without good h |
| 13 | A\* space | $O(b^{d})$ | Same as BFS worst case |
| 14 | IDDFS time | $O(b^{d})$ | Like BFS completeness |
| 15 | IDDFS space | $O(b \cdot d)$ | Like DFS memory |
| 16 | Effective branching factor | $N + 1 = 1 + b^{*} + (b^{*})^{2} + \dots + (b^{*})^{d}$ | Used to benchmark heuristics |

> The pipe symbol `|` would have broken the markdown table; all absolute values above are written as `\lvert \cdot \rvert` for safe rendering.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AGVs (Amazon Kiva, GreyOrange):** A\* runs on a 2D grid discretisation of the warehouse floor, with Manhattan heuristics dominated by aisle layout.
- **Autonomous Cars (Apollo, Autoware):** A\* + Hybrid A\* operate on lanelet/road graphs for lane-level routing.
- **Game AI:** A\* powers NPC navigation in real-time strategy and RPG engines.
- **Drone Swarms:** Decentralised A\* variants coordinate collision-free flight in shared airspace.
- **Surgical Robots:** Sampling-based planning (RRT) generalises graph search to continuous C-spaces, with A\* on the resulting roadmap.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world intuition:** In every modern robotics stack you will find a graph search algorithm running somewhere — usually A\* at the *global planner* layer above a local controller.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Why the A\* Evaluation Function is f(n) = g(n) + h(n)

**Premise:** We want to find a path from s to g with minimum total cost. We expand nodes in some order from the frontier. Suppose at any step we have a candidate path through node n with cost $g(n)$ already incurred. The remaining cost to the goal is *unknown* but can be *estimated* as $h(n)$.

**Step 1 — Decompose the Total Cost.**

The total cost of a path s → n → g can be written as:

$$
C(\text{path through } n) \;=\; \underbrace{g(n)}_{\text{cost so far}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{unknown true remainder}}_{h^{*}(n)}
$$

**Step 2 — Replace the Unknown with an Estimate.**

We do not know $h^{*}(n)$, so we replace it with an admissible estimate $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$:

$$
\hat{C}(\text{path through } n) \;=\; g(n) + h(n) \;\equiv\; f(n)
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Selection Rule.**

Always expand the frontier node with the **smallest $f(n)$**. This greedy choice is justified as follows:

$$
f(n) \;\le\; f(n') \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad g(n) + h(n) \;\le\; g(n') + h(n')
$$

That is, n is at least as promising as n' according to the combined criterion.

**Step 4 — Optimality Consequence.**

If h is admissible, the node popped from the frontier is on an optimal path. (Standard proof uses contradiction: assume an optimal goal $g^{*}$ is in the frontier with $f(g^{*}) = g(g^{*}) = C^{*}$, while some non-optimal node n is popped with $f(n) \le f(g^{*}) \Rightarrow g(n) + h(n) \le C^{*}$. Since $h(n) \ge 0$, we get $g(n) \le C^{*}$, contradicting optimality of $g^{*}$.)

$$
\boxed{\,f(n) \;=\; g(n) \;+\; h(n)\,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — A\* on a 3 × 3 Grid

**Problem:** A mobile robot must travel from **S = (0, 0)** to **G = (2, 2)** on the following 3 × 3 grid. Cell (1, 1) is an obstacle. Moves are **4-connected** (up, down, left, right), each costing 1. Heuristic is **Manhattan distance** to the goal.

**Initial Grid (row, col indexing):**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
S & (0,1) & (0,2) \\
\hline
(1,0) & \times & (1,2) \\
\hline
(2,0) & (2,1) & G \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Step 1 — Initialise the Open and Closed lists.**

$$
\text{Open} = \{(0,0)\}, \quad \text{Closed} = \emptyset
$$

For the start node: $g(S) = 0$, $h(S) = \lvert 0 - 2 \rvert + \lvert 0 - 2 \rvert = 4$, so $f(S) = 0 + 4 = 4$.

**Step 2 — Expand (0, 0).** Successors: (0, 1) and (1, 0).

For (0, 1):
- $g = 0 + 1 = 1$
- $h = \lvert 0 - 2 \rvert + \lvert 1 - 2 \rvert = 2 + 1 = 3$
- $f = 1 + 3 = 4$

For (1, 0):
- $g = 0 + 1 = 1$
- $h = \lvert 1 - 2 \rvert + \lvert 0 - 2 \rvert = 1 + 2 = 3$
- $f = 1 + 3 = 4$

**Step 3 — Select next node.** Tie at $f = 4$. With tie-breaking by lower h, both remain tied; pick (0, 1). Closed = {(0, 0)}, Open = {(0, 1), (1, 0)}.

**Step 4 — Expand (0, 1).** Successors: (0, 2), (1, 1) [blocked], and parent (0, 0) [skip].

For (0, 2):
- $g = 1 + 1 = 2$
- $h = \lvert 0 - 2 \rvert + \lvert 2 - 2 \rvert = 2 + 0 = 2$
- $f = 2 + 2 = 4$

For (1, 0) — already in Open with $g = 1$. Tentative $g = 1 + 1 = 2$ which is **worse**, so skip.

**Step 5 — Next selection.** Open = {(0, 2) f=4, (1, 0) f=4}. Pick (0, 2).

For (1, 2):
- $g = 2 + 1 = 3$
- $h = \lvert 1 - 2 \rvert + \lvert 2 - 2 \rvert = 1 + 0 = 1$
- $f = 3 + 1 = 4$

**Step 6 — Continue.** The algorithm continues expanding tie-broken nodes until the goal (2, 2) is popped from the frontier.

**Step 7 — Final reconstructed path.**

$$
S \rightarrow (0,1) \rightarrow (0,2) \rightarrow (1,2) \rightarrow G
$$

**Total cost:** $g(G) = 4$, optimal since Manhattan lower bound = 4 and is achieved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Even with ties, A\* on this small grid expanded **all 7 reachable cells** because Manhattan gives equal $f$-values along the optimal frontier — illustrating why *informed* heuristics still benefit from a sharper estimate when the map is large.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Algorithm | Complete? | Optimal? | Time | Space | Information Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| BFS | ✅ (finite graph) | ✅ (uniform cost) | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(b^{d})$ | Depth only |
| DFS | ❌ (infinite trees) | ❌ | $O(b^{m})$ | $O(b \cdot m)$ | Depth only |
| IDDFS | ✅ | ✅ (uniform cost) | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(b \cdot d)$ | Depth only |
| UCS (Dijkstra) | ✅ | ✅ | $O((V + E)\log V)$ | $O(V)$ | $g(n)$ only |
| Greedy Best-First | ❌ (can get stuck) | ❌ | $O(b^{d})$ worst | $O(b^{d})$ worst | $h(n)$ only |
| **A\*** | ✅ (with admissible h) | ✅ (with admissible h) | $O(b^{d})$ worst, often much less | $O(b^{d})$ worst | $g(n)$ and $h(n)$ |
| Bidirectional | ✅ | ✅ (with right h) | $O(b^{d/2})$ | $O(b^{d/2})$ | Both ends |

**Proof sketch of A\* optimality (for the KTU 14-marker):**

Assume A\* returns a suboptimal goal node $g_{1}$ with $f(g_{1}) = g(g_{1}) = C_{1} > C^{*}$. Since an optimal goal $g^{*}$ is on the frontier (A\* checks all nodes), and at the moment $g_{1}$ was popped, the optimal node had $f(g^{*}) = g(g^{*}) = C^{*}$, by the selection rule $f(g_{1}) \le f(g^{*}) \Rightarrow C_{1} \le C^{*}$, contradiction. Hence A\* returns an optimal goal.

$$
\boxed{\text{A}^{*} \text{ is optimal whenever } h \text{ is admissible.}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Generic Graph Search Engine (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize: Insert Start s into Open List] --> B{Is Open List empty?}
    B -- Yes --> Z[Return FAILURE]
    B -- No --> C[Select node n via selection rule]
    C --> D{Is n a goal node?}
    D -- Yes --> Y[Reconstruct path via parent pointers and return SUCCESS]
    D -- No --> E[Insert n into Closed List]
    E --> F[Generate successors of n via motion primitives]
    F --> G{For each successor n prime}
    G --> H{n prime already in Closed with lower or equal g?}
    H -- Yes --> G
    H -- No --> I{n prime already in Open with lower or equal g?}
    I -- Yes --> G
    I -- No --> J[Set parent of n prime to n and update g n prime]
    J --> K[Compute heuristic h n prime and evaluation f n prime equals g n prime plus h n prime]
    K --> L[Insert n prime into Open List with priority f n prime]
    L --> G
    G -- All successors processed --> B
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Comparison Flow of Search Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root[Graph Search Algorithms] --> Uninformed[Uninformed]
    Root --> Informed[Informed]

    Uninformed --> BFS[BFS: FIFO by depth]
    Uninformed --> DFS[DFS: LIFO by depth]
    Uninformed --> UCS[UCS: priority by g n]
    Uninformed --> IDDFS[IDDFS: depth bounded DFS]

    Informed --> Greedy[Greedy: priority by h n]
    Informed --> AStar[A Star: priority by f n equals g n plus h n]
    Informed --> Bi[Bidirectional: from both ends]

    AStar --> Prop1[Admissible h ensures optimality]
    AStar --> Prop2[Consistent h ensures no re-expansion]
    AStar --> Prop3[Time space exponential in solution depth d]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — A\* Tree Expansion on the 3 × 3 Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[S at 0,0: g 0 h 4 f 4] --> A1[0,1: g 1 h 3 f 4]
    S --> A2[1,0: g 1 h 3 f 4]
    A1 --> B1[0,2: g 2 h 2 f 4]
    A1 --> BX[1,1 BLOCKED]
    A2 --> B3[2,0: g 2 h 2 f 4]
    B1 --> C1[1,2: g 3 h 1 f 4]
    C1 --> Goal[G at 2,2: g 4 h 0 f 4]
    B3 --> C3[2,1: g 3 h 1 f 4]
    C3 --> Goal
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each node label shows the cell coordinate and the values of g, h, and f. All eight reachable non-blocked cells share $f = 4$ because the Manhattan heuristic is *perfect* on this uniform-cost grid — A\* expands every cell yet still returns the optimal path.

### 4.4 Mermaid — Robot Path-Planning Stack (Where Graph Search Lives)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top[Mission Planner] --> Mid[Global Planner: A Star on road or grid graph]
    Mid --> Local[Local Planner: Dynamic Window Approach or MPC]
    Local --> Ctrl[Low Level Controller: PID or Torque]
    Ctrl --> Robot[Robot Actuators]
    Mid -. Feedback .-> Map[Map and Localization SLAM]
    Map -. Updated Graph .-> Mid
```

> **Why this matters:** Graph search rarely runs in isolation in modern robotics. The **A\*** block sits at the *global planning* layer, producing a coarse path on a discretised graph; the local planner refines it in real time using sensor feedback.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define graph search. Differentiate between informed and uninformed search with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Graph search is a systematic procedure that explores the state-space graph of a robot by iteratively expanding nodes from a frontier (open list) until a goal configuration is reached.

**Uninformed Search (1 mark):** Uses only the problem definition (no domain knowledge). Example: Breadth-First Search (BFS) — expands nodes in order of increasing depth; guaranteed to find the shallowest goal but is blind to where the goal actually is.

**Informed Search (1 mark):** Uses a problem-specific heuristic function $h(n)$ that estimates the cost from a node to the goal, dramatically reducing the number of nodes expanded. Example: A\* algorithm, which combines the path cost $g(n)$ with $h(n)$ via the evaluation function $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **What is an admissible heuristic? Why is the Manhattan distance admissible on a 4-connected grid but inadmissible on an 8-connected grid with diagonal cost 1?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Admissibility (1 mark):** A heuristic $h(n)$ is admissible if it never overestimates the true minimum cost $h^{*}(n)$ to the goal, i.e., $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ for all nodes n.

**4-connected grid (1 mark):** On a 4-connected grid each move changes exactly one coordinate by ±1, so the Manhattan distance $\lvert x_n - x_g \rvert + \lvert y_n - y_g \rvert$ is exactly the minimum number of steps required. Hence $h(n) = h^{*}(n)$ and admissibility is satisfied.

**8-connected grid (1 mark):** On an 8-connected grid a diagonal move reaches two coordinates in one step. A diagonal of $k$ cells requires only $k$ moves, but Manhattan counts $2k$ — it overestimates when a diagonal is shorter than the orthogonal detour, violating admissibility.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Choose **ONE** of the following two questions.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Theory + Worked Application** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the A\* search algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of the heuristic function, the conditions under which A\* is optimal, and how it differs from Dijkstra's algorithm.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider the 4 × 4 grid below. The robot starts at **S = (0, 0)** and must reach **G = (3, 3)**. The cell **(2, 1)** is blocked. The robot moves in **4 directions** with unit cost. Use **Manhattan distance** as the heuristic. **Run A\* and list every expanded node with its (g, h, f) values, the final path, and the optimal path cost.**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
S & (0,1) & (0,2) & (0,3) \\
\hline
(1,0) & (1,1) & (1,2) & (1,3) \\
\hline
(2,0) & \times & (2,2) & (2,3) \\
\hline
(3,0) & (3,1) & (3,2) & G \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

1. **A\* Algorithm (2 marks):** A\* is a best-first search that selects the node n from the open list minimising $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, where $g(n)$ is the cost from the start to n, and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate from n to the goal. A\* maintains an open list (priority queue ordered by f) and a closed list (expanded nodes). On expansion, each successor is relaxed; if a better g is found, parent pointers and g-values are updated, and the successor is re-pushed.

2. **Role of the Heuristic (2 marks):** The heuristic $h(n)$ directs the search toward the goal. A perfect heuristic ($h = h^{*}$) makes A\* expand only nodes on the optimal path. A*useless* heuristic ($h(n) = 0$ for all n) reduces A\* to Uniform-Cost Search. The quality of h is quantified by the **effective branching factor** $b^{*}$.

3. **Optimality Conditions (1.5 marks):** A\* returns an optimal solution iff $h$ is **admissible** ($h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$). If in addition $h$ is **consistent** ($h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$), A\* is also optimally *efficient* among all algorithms using the same heuristic — it expands the minimum possible number of nodes.

4. **A\* vs. Dijkstra (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Dijkstra (UCS) | A\* |
|---|---|---|
| Selection criterion | Smallest $g(n)$ | Smallest $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Heuristic use | None | Problem-specific $h(n)$ |
| Optimality | Always optimal | Optimal with admissible $h$ |
| Nodes expanded | All nodes within radius $C^{*}$ of start | Only nodes whose f is $\le C^{*}$ |
| Application | Generic shortest path | Goal-directed robotic search |

Dijkstra explores in concentric cost "rings" around the start; A\* biases the rings toward the goal.

**(b) Worked Example (7 marks):**

Compute $h(n) = \lvert x - 3 \rvert + \lvert y - 3 \rvert$ for every cell.

**Iteration 1 — Expand (0, 0).** $g = 0, h = 6, f = 6$.
Successors: (0, 1) and (1, 0).

| Cell | g | h | f |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0, 1) | 1 | 5 | 6 |
| (1, 0) | 1 | 5 | 6 |

Open = {(0, 1) f=6, (1, 0) f=6}. Closed = {(0, 0)}.

**Iteration 2 — Expand (0, 1)** (tie-break by lower h, both equal — pick lexicographically).
Successors: (0, 2), (1, 1), and skip parent.

| Cell | g | h | f |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0, 2) | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| (1, 1) | 2 | 4 | 6 |

Open = {(1, 0) f=6, (0, 2) f=6, (1, 1) f=6}. Closed = {(0, 0), (0, 1)}.

**Iteration 3 — Expand (0, 2).**
Successors: (0, 3), (1, 2) [skip (1, 1) already in Open with equal g].

| Cell | g | h | f |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0, 3) | 3 | 3 | 6 |
| (1, 2) | 3 | 3 | 6 |

**Iteration 4 — Expand (1, 0).**
Successors: (2, 0), (1, 1) [tentative $g = 2$, current $g(1,1) = 2$ — skip].

| Cell | g | h | f |
|---|---|---|---|
| (2, 0) | 2 | 4 | 6 |

**Iteration 5 — Expand (1, 1).** Successors: (1, 2) [tentative g=3 = current, skip], (2, 1) [BLOCKED, skip].

**Iteration 6 — Expand (0, 3).** Successors: (1, 3).

| Cell | g | h | f |
|---|---|---|---|
| (1, 3) | 4 | 2 | 6 |

**Iteration 7 — Expand (2, 0).** Successors: (3, 0), (2, 1) [BLOCKED].

| Cell | g | h | f |
|---|---|---|---|
| (3, 0) | 3 | 3 | 6 |

**Iteration 8 — Expand (1, 2).** Successors: (1, 3) [tentative g=4 = current, skip], (2, 2).

| Cell | g | h | f |
|---|---|---|---|
| (2, 2) | 4 | 2 | 6 |

**Iteration 9 — Expand (3, 0).** Successors: (3, 1).

| Cell | g | h | f |
|---|---|---|---|
| (3, 1) | 4 | 2 | 6 |

**Iteration 10 — Expand (1, 3).** Successors: (2, 3).

| Cell | g | h | f |
|---|---|---|---|
| (2, 3) | 5 | 1 | 6 |

**Iteration 11 — Expand (2, 2).** Successors: (2, 3) [tentative g=5 = current, skip], (3, 2).

| Cell | g | h | f |
|---|---|---|---|
| (3, 2) | 5 | 1 | 6 |

**Iteration 12 — Expand (3, 1).** Successors: (3, 2) [tentative g=5 = current, skip], (2, 1) [BLOCKED].

**Iteration 13 — Expand (2, 3).** Successors: (3, 3) = **G**.

| Cell | g | h | f |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(3, 3) G** | **6** | **0** | **6** |

**Goal reached! Optimal path cost = 6.**

**Reconstructed path (trace parents):**

$$
S \to (0,1) \to (0,2) \to (0,3) \to (1,3) \to (2,3) \to G
$$

**Verification:** 6 steps, no shorter path exists because Manhattan lower bound = $\lvert 0-3 \rvert + \lvert 0-3 \rvert = 6$ and is achieved. ✅

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the following graph search algorithms in terms of completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity: BFS, DFS, Uniform-Cost Search, and Greedy Best-First Search. Present your answer in a comparative table and explain *why* each algorithm has its specific limitations.

**(b) [7 marks]** For an 8-connected grid where straight moves cost 1 and diagonal moves cost $\sqrt{2}$, derive the **octile heuristic**. Prove that it is admissible. Compute the heuristic value from cell $(5, 2)$ to the goal $(1, 7)$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Table (4 marks) + Reasoning (3 marks):**

| Algorithm | Complete? | Optimal? | Time | Space | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| BFS | Yes (finite b) | Yes (uniform cost) | $O(b^{d})$ | $O(b^{d})$ | Memory explodes at large d |
| DFS | No (infinite trees) | No | $O(b^{m})$ | $O(b \cdot m)$ | Can get stuck in dead branches; not optimal |
| UCS | Yes | Yes | $O((V + E)\log V)$ | $O(V)$ | Explores all directions equally; no goal bias |
| Greedy Best-First | No (can get stuck) | No | $O(b^{m})$ worst | $O(b^{m})$ worst | Ignores $g(n)$; myopic; can be misled |

**Reasoning (3 marks):**

- BFS uses a FIFO queue; it expands all nodes at depth d before depth d+1, guaranteeing optimality only when step costs are uniform. It suffers from the **memory bottleneck** because every node in the frontier is retained.
- DFS uses a LIFO stack; it goes deep before going wide, giving $O(b \cdot m)$ memory but no optimality guarantee. DFS can wander forever in deep or cyclic branches unless cycle-detection (graph search) is added.
- UCS is Dijkstra's algorithm in a state space; it expands the node with the lowest $g(n)$ first. Optimal and complete, but it expands in expanding *cost rings*, not goal-directed, so on a large map it touches many irrelevant nodes.
- Greedy Best-First uses only $h(n)$ and rushes toward whichever node *looks* closest to the goal. Because $g(n)$ is ignored, the first solution found is often not the shortest. Greedy can also fail to reach a goal if the heuristic directs it into a dead-end.

**(b) Octile Heuristic Derivation (7 marks):**

**Setup (1 mark):** On an 8-connected grid, the robot can move in 8 directions: 4 straight (cost 1) and 4 diagonal (cost $\sqrt{2}$). Let $\Delta x = \lvert x_n - x_g \rvert$ and $\Delta y = \lvert y_n - y_g \rvert$, with $\Delta x \ge \Delta y$ (WLOG).

**Step 1 — Decompose the Path (2 marks):** An optimal 8-connected path takes exactly $\Delta y$ diagonal moves (one for each row or column difference in the smaller dimension) and $(\Delta x - \Delta y)$ straight moves (to cover the remaining gap). The total cost is:

$$
h_{\text{octile}}(n) = (\Delta x - \Delta y) \cdot 1 \;+\; \Delta y \cdot \sqrt{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the Expression (1 mark):**

$$
h_{\text{octile}}(n) = \Delta x \;+\; (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot \Delta y
$$

**Step 3 — Admissibility Proof (2 marks):**
- A diagonal move covers distance 1 in *both* coordinates and costs $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.
- Two straight moves cover the same coordinate change at cost $1 + 1 = 2 > \sqrt{2}$.
- Therefore, for any sequence of moves, replacing straight moves with diagonals (where possible) *cannot increase* the cost.
- The path counted by $h_{\text{octile}}$ is itself a feasible motion sequence in the 8-connected grid, so the true minimum cost $h^{*}(n) \le h_{\text{octile}}(n)$. Conversely, any path must cover at least $\Delta x$ total coordinate difference, and no path can do so for less than the cost computed. Hence $h_{\text{octile}}(n) = h^{*}(n)$ for *any* node (perfect heuristic on uniform-cost 8-connected grids).
- Therefore $h_{\text{octile}}(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ for all n → **admissible**. ✅

**Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation (1 mark):** For $n = (5, 2)$, $g = (1, 7)$:

$$
\Delta x = \lvert 5 - 1 \rvert = 4, \quad \Delta y = \lvert 2 - 7 \rvert = 5
$$

Take the *larger* as $\Delta x$: $\Delta x = 5, \Delta y = 4$.

$$
h_{\text{octile}} = 5 + (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot 4 = 5 + 4\sqrt{2} - 4 = 1 + 4\sqrt{2} \approx 1 + 5.657 \approx 6.657
$$

**Answer:** $h_{\text{octile}}\big((5,2), (1,7)\big) = 1 + 4\sqrt{2} \approx 6.66$.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Heuristic Admissibility on 8-connected grids (–2 marks typical):** Students often write "Manhattan distance is admissible" without specifying the motion model. On an 8-connected grid with diagonal cost 1, Manhattan *overestimates* and is **inadmissible** — examiners deduct 2 marks if the motion model is not stated.
>
> 2. **Confusing BFS optimality with weighted edges (–1 mark):** BFS is optimal *only* when all step costs are equal. If step costs differ (e.g., terrain cost), BFS gives the wrong answer — use UCS instead.
>
> 3. **Skipping the open/closed list in algorithm explanation (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students describe A\* as "use the heuristic" without mentioning the open list, closed list, or the relaxation step that prunes dominated nodes. KTU 2024 rubrics require both data structures to be named.
>
> 4. **A\* vs. Dijkstra confusion (–1 mark):** Stating that "A\* = Dijkstra with a heuristic" is partially correct, but students must clarify that Dijkstra corresponds to A\* with $h(n) = 0$. A generic statement without this equivalence loses a mark.
>
> 5. **Not showing g, h, f in tabular form for worked examples (–1 to –2 marks):** A worked A\* trace without a clean (g, h, f) table is hard to follow; the KTU model answer key always uses a table. Always present the per-node table.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Graph search** = systematic exploration of a state-space graph from s to g.
- [ ] **Generic skeleton** = Open list + Closed list + selection rule.
- [ ] **Uninformed algorithms:** BFS (FIFO), DFS (LIFO), UCS (priority by g), IDDFS (DFS with growing depth bound).
- [ ] **Informed algorithms:** Greedy (priority by h), A\* (priority by $f = g + h$).
- [ ] **Evaluation function:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — the soul of A\*.
- [ ] **Admissibility:** $h(n) \le h^{*}(n)$ → guarantees A\* optimality.
- [ ] **Consistency:** $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$ → guarantees A\* efficiency (no re-expansion).
- [ ] **Heuristic catalogue:** Manhattan (4-conn), Euclidean (continuous), Chebyshev, Octile (8-conn with $\sqrt{2}$).
- [ ] **Complexity rule of thumb:** BFS / A\* time & space are $O(b^{d})$; DFS / IDDFS space is $O(bd)$.
- [ ] **BFS optimality** requires uniform step cost; otherwise use UCS.
- [ ] **A\* is optimally efficient** — no other algorithm using the same admissible heuristic can expand fewer nodes.
- [ ] **Bidirectional A\*** roughly squares the speedup when both ends have good heuristics.
- [ ] **Real-world uses:** warehouse AGVs, autonomous cars (Apollo), robot manipulators, drone swarms.
- [ ] **Always present the (g, h, f) table** in worked examples; **always state the motion model** before picking a heuristic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deterministic Graph Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deterministic Graph Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of mobile robotics, manipulators, and autonomous decision-making systems, a **Deterministic Graph Search** refers to a class of state-space traversal algorithms in which, given an identical initial configuration, goal specification, and environment representation, the algorithm **always produces the exact same sequence of node expansions and the same final path**. There is no element of randomness, no probabilistic branching, and no stochastic tie-breaking.

A graph $G = (V, E)$ in robotics is typically a discretized representation of the **Configuration Space (C-space)** of the robot, where:

- $V$ represents valid robot configurations (nodes / states)
- $E$ represents feasible transitions between configurations (edges / actions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Deterministic Graph Search* is the systematic exploration of a finite or discretized state graph using well-defined transition rules, where successor ordering is fully pre-determined. The objective is to find a sequence of actions (a *plan* or *path*) that drives the robot from an initial state $s_0$ to a goal state $s_g$, while minimizing a cost function $c(s, a, s')$.

A search is **deterministic** if and only if:
$$
\text{SuccessorOrder}(n) = \mathcal{F}(n, G) \quad \text{(a pure function of node } n \text{ and graph } G\text{)}
$$

This contrasts sharply with **Randomized / Stochastic Search** (e.g., RRT, Monte Carlo Tree Search), which incorporates randomness into node selection.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are navigating through a **maze drawn on graph paper** (the grid is your graph). You have a paper and pencil, and you must follow a **strict, pre-defined rulebook**:

- **Rule 1:** "Always mark the cell you visit."
- **Rule 2:** "Always look at the lowest-numbered unvisited neighbor first."
- **Rule 3:** "Never revisit a marked cell."

If your friend, with the same maze and same rulebook, starts from the same entrance, they will **trace out the exact same path** as you. That is *deterministic* search.

**Real-world robotics analogy:** A warehouse AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) planning its route from the loading dock (node A) to a specific shelf (node G). The warehouse map is a graph, each aisle intersection is a node, and the deterministic planner (say, A*) will always choose the same optimal aisle sequence every time the request is made — assuming the map does not change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does determinism matter in robotics?**
> 1. **Repeatability & Certification** — Industrial robots (ISO 10218, ISO/TS 15066) must produce verifiable, reproducible motions.
> 2. **Safety Verification** — Determinism allows formal model checking of plans.
> 3. **Predictable Worst-Case Time** — Critical for real-time control loops in KUKA, FANUC, ABB controllers.
> 4. **Sim-to-Real Transfer** — Same plan works in simulation and on physical hardware.

## 1.3 The Search Problem — Formal Setup

A deterministic graph search problem is defined by the tuple:
$$
\langle S, s_0, s_g, A, c, \Gamma \rangle
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Robotics Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S$ | Finite set of states | Discretized grid cells in C-space |
| $s_0 \in S$ | Initial state | Robot's start pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$ |
| $s_g \in S$ | Goal state(s) | Target waypoint / end-effector pose |
| $A(s)$ | Action set available at state $s$ | $\{N, S, E, W\}$ in 4-connected grid |
| $c(s, a, s')$ | Step cost | Distance, time, or energy per move |
| $\Gamma(s, a)$ | Deterministic transition | $s' = \Gamma(s, a)$, always the same |

The objective is to compute an **action sequence (plan)**:
$$
\pi = \langle a_0, a_1, a_2, \dots, a_{n-1} \rangle
$$
such that applying $\pi$ starting from $s_0$ yields $s_g$, and the total cost
$$
J(\pi) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} c(s_i, a_i, s_{i+1})
$$
is minimized.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> *Graph Search* keeps track of all explored nodes (using a **Closed/Explored Set**), avoiding redundant work. *Tree Search* does not, and can re-explore the same state via different paths — a crucial exam point.

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Deterministic Graph Search

Deterministic search is broadly split into two families:

1. **Uninformed (Blind) Search** — Uses only the graph structure and step costs. Examples: BFS, Uniform-Cost Search (UCS), DFS, Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS), Bidirectional Search.
2. **Informed (Heuristic) Search** — Uses an estimate $h(n)$ of the cost-to-goal. Examples: Greedy Best-First Search, A* Search, Weighted A*, Iterative Deepening A* (IDA*).

Both families are deterministic; the distinction is the *amount of problem-specific knowledge* the algorithm is allowed to use.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Blueprint of a Deterministic Graph Search

Every deterministic graph search algorithm follows the same canonical loop. What distinguishes BFS from A* is **only the data structure used to order the frontier**.

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Create an empty *Open List* (Frontier / Fringe) and an empty *Closed List* (Explored Set). Insert the start node $s_0$ into Open with $g(s_0) = 0$ and $f(s_0)$ as defined by the algorithm.

- **Step 2 — Termination Check:** If Open is empty, return **FAILURE** (no path exists). If the selected node $n$ satisfies Goal-Test$(n)$, return the reconstructed path by back-pointers.

- **Step 3 — Node Selection:** Remove the *highest-priority* node $n$ from Open. Priority is defined by the algorithm:
  - BFS: smallest depth $d(n)$
  - UCS: smallest $g(n)$
  - DFS: largest depth (LIFO stack)
  - A*: smallest $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$

- **Step 4 — Expansion:** For every action $a \in A(n)$, compute $s' = \Gamma(n, a)$. If $s'$ is not in Closed, compute $g(s') = g(n) + c(n, a, s')$, set parent pointer $\text{parent}(s') = n$, and insert $s'$ into Open.

- **Step 5 — Loop:** Return to Step 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" Matters (KTU 2-Marker Insight):**
> The **Closed List** is what converts Tree Search into Graph Search. It is the single most important reason BFS on a graph is $O(V+E)$ rather than exponential. Examiners love asking this!

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 70% of KTU numerical questions on search complexity and optimality.**

| Algorithm | Evaluation Function $f(n)$ | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Complete? | Optimal? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| BFS | $f(n) = \text{depth}(n)$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | Yes (if $b$ finite) | Yes (uniform cost) |
| Uniform-Cost Search | $f(n) = g(n)$ | $O(b^{1 + \lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^{1 + \lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | Yes | Yes |
| DFS | $f(n) = -\text{depth}(n)$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ | No (infinite spaces) | No |
| Depth-Limited | $f(n) = -\text{depth}(n)$, cap $l$ | $O(b^l)$ | $O(bl)$ | No (if $l < d$) | No |
| IDDFS | $f(n) = -\text{depth}(n)$, iter. $l$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ | Yes | Yes (uniform cost) |
| Greedy Best-First | $f(n) = h(n)$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^m)$ | No | No |
| **A* Search** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $O(b^d)$ (worst case) | $O(b^d)$ | Yes (if finite branches) | Yes (if $h$ admissible) |
| IDA* | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, iter. threshold | $O(b^d)$ (typical) | $O(d)$ | Yes | Yes (if $h$ admissible) |
| Bidirectional | (uses BFS or A* both ways) | $O(b^{d/2})$ | $O(b^{d/2})$ | Yes | Yes |

**Notation used in the table:**
- $b$ = **branching factor** (max successors per node)
- $d$ = **depth of shallowest goal**
- $m$ = **maximum depth of search tree**
- $C^*$ = **cost of optimal solution**
- $\epsilon$ = **minimum positive step cost**
- $h(n)$ = **heuristic estimate** of cost from $n$ to goal

> [!TIP]
> **Critical for KTU:**
> When asked "Is A* optimal?" the answer is **"Yes, *iff* (if and only if) the heuristic $h(n)$ is admissible — i.e., $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$."** Memorize this conditional.

## 2.3 Heuristic Properties — Admissibility & Consistency

A heuristic $h$ is:

- **Admissible** if it never overestimates the true cost to the goal:
$$
0 \le h(n) \le h^*(n) \quad \forall \, n \in S
$$
where $h^*(n)$ is the true optimal cost from $n$ to $s_g$.

- **Consistent (Monotone)** if for every action $a$ leading from $n$ to $n'$:
$$
h(n) \le c(n, a, n') + h(n')
$$
A consistent heuristic is automatically admissible, but not vice versa.

- **Dominance:** $h_2$ dominates $h_1$ if $h_1(n) \le h_2(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$. A more dominant heuristic expands fewer nodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic robotics heuristic example — Euclidean distance:**
> For a point robot in a 2D plane with 4-connected grid movement, the heuristic
> $$h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$$
> is **admissible** (straight line is shortest) and **consistent**. This is the textbook heuristic for the 8-puzzle, grid-world, and mobile robot localization problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Search Algorithm Used | Why Deterministic? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Warehouse AGV Routing** (Amazon Kiva) | A* on a static grid | Predictable travel time for SLA compliance |
| **ADAS Path Planning** (lane change) | Hybrid A* (deterministic) | ISO 26262 functional safety requires reproducible plans |
| **Robot Manipulator IK** (UR5, ABB) | Deterministic Jacobian-based search | Repeatable trajectories for pick-and-place |
| **VLSI Routing** (chip design) | A* / Lee's Algorithm | CAD tools must be certifiable and bug-free |
| **Game AI** (chess engines) | Deterministic alpha-beta search | Tournament rules forbid randomness |
| **Drone Mission Planning** (PX4) | A* / Dijkstra over occupancy grid | CAA regulations require pre-flight plan approval |

## 2.5 Properties of the State-Space Graph for Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently tests the following distinctions:**

- **Static vs. Dynamic:** Static (offline A* in a known map) vs. Dynamic (D* Lite for unknown/moving obstacles).
- **Discrete vs. Continuous:** Discrete (grid graph) vs. Continuous (RRT, PRM). Deterministic graph search is fundamentally for *discretized* representations.
- **Deterministic vs. Stochastic Transitions:** We are studying the former. Stochastic transitions lead to MDPs and POMDPs (covered in later modules).
- **Fully vs. Partially Observable:** Graph search assumes full observability — the agent knows which node it is in.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why A* with an Admissible Heuristic is Optimal

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question**. The derivation is shown in full.

### Statement to Prove

> If $h(n)$ is admissible, then A* expanding nodes in order of $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ will expand the goal node $s_g$ via a path of optimal cost $g(s_g) = C^*$.

### Lemma 1 (Lower Bound on $f$ at any Unexpanded Node)

For any unexpanded node $n$, the optimal path that passes through $n$ has cost at least $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. This is because:
- $g(n)$ is the known cost to reach $n$.
- $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ (admissibility), so $h(n)$ is a lower bound on remaining cost.

Thus, for every unexpanded node $n$:
$$
f(n) \le C^* \quad \text{(if } h \text{ admissible, see proof below)}
$$

### Lemma 2 (Monotonicity of $f$ on Optimal Path)

Let $n$ lie on an optimal path to $s_g$. Define the path as $s_0 \to n_1 \to n_2 \to \dots \to s_g$. Then for each consecutive pair $(n_i, n_{i+1})$ on this optimal path:
$$
f(n_{i+1}) = g(n_{i+1}) + h(n_{i+1})
$$

Using the fact that $g(n_{i+1}) = g(n_i) + c(n_i, a_i, n_{i+1})$ and that on the optimal path $h^*(n_{i+1}) = h^*(n_i) - c(n_i, a_i, n_{i+1})$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
f(n_{i+1}) - f(n_i) &= g(n_{i+1}) + h(n_{i+1}) - g(n_i) - h(n_i) \\
&= c(n_i, a_i, n_{i+1}) + h(n_{i+1}) - h(n_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $h(n_{i+1}) \le h^*(n_{i+1}) = h^*(n_i) - c(n_i, a_i, n_{i+1})$ and $h(n_i) \le h^*(n_i)$:
$$
f(n_{i+1}) - f(n_i) \le 0 + 0 = 0
$$
So $f$ is **non-increasing** along the optimal path.

### Main Proof

Assume for contradiction that A* terminates by expanding a suboptimal goal $s_g'$ with $g(s_g') = C' > C^*$.

At the moment just before expansion of $s_g'$, there exists an unexpanded optimal goal $s_g$ on the frontier. By Lemma 1:
$$
f(s_g) = g(s_g) + h(s_g) = C^* + 0 = C^*
$$

By Lemma 2, all nodes on the optimal path have $f \le C^*$, so $s_g$ is in Open with $f \le C^*$.

Since A* expands the node with **minimum $f$**, and the algorithm expanded $s_g'$ with $f(s_g') = g(s_g') + h(s_g') = C' + 0 = C' > C^*$:
$$
\min_{n \in \text{Open}} f(n) \le f(s_g) = C^* < C' = f(s_g')
$$
This contradicts the choice of $s_g'$. ∎

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Trace: A* on a Robotics Graph

> **Problem (typical KTU 14-mark):** A mobile robot must navigate from $S$ to $G$ in the graph below. Edge costs are travel times in seconds. Use A* with the given heuristic $h$ to find the optimal path. Show the open list at every step.

| Node | S | A | B | C | D | E | G |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $h(n)$ (seconds) | **7** | **6** | **2** | **4** | **3** | **1** | **0** |

**Edges and costs:**
- $S \to A$: 1,  $S \to B$: 4
- $A \to B$: 2,  $A \to C$: 5,  $A \to D$: 12
- $B \to D$: 3,  $B \to E$: 7
- $C \to D$: 2,  $C \to G$: 7
- $D \to E$: 1,  $D \to G$: 3
- $E \to G$: 5

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Initialization:** Open = $\{(S, f=0+7=7)\}$. Closed = $\{\}$.

**Iteration 1:** Expand $S$ ($f=7$).
- Generate $A$: $g=1$, $f=1+6=7$
- Generate $B$: $g=4$, $f=4+2=6$

Open = $\{(B, 6), (A, 7)\}$. Closed = $\{S\}$.

**Iteration 2:** Expand $B$ ($f=6$).
- Generate $D$ via $B$: $g=4+3=7$, $f=7+3=10$
- Generate $E$ via $B$: $g=4+7=11$, $f=11+1=12$

Open = $\{(A, 7), (D, 10), (E, 12)\}$. Closed = $\{S, B\}$.

**Iteration 3:** Expand $A$ ($f=7$).
- $B$ already in Closed → skip.
- Generate $C$ via $A$: $g=1+5=6$, $f=6+4=10$
- $D$ already in Open with $g=7$ via B. Via A: $g=1+12=13$. Since $13 > 7$, do **not update**.

Open = $\{(C, 10), (D, 10), (E, 12)\}$. Closed = $\{S, B, A\}$.

**Iteration 4:** Expand $C$ ($f=10$, tie with D — deterministic tie-break: alphabetical, so C first).
- $D$ via $C$: $g=6+2=8$, $f=8+3=11$. Existing $D$ has $g=7 < 8$, no update.
- $G$ via $C$: $g=6+7=13$, $f=13+0=13$

Open = $\{(D, 10), (E, 12), (G, 13)\}$. Closed = $\{S, B, A, C\}$.

**Iteration 5:** Expand $D$ ($f=10$).
- $B$ in Closed → skip.
- $C$ in Closed → skip.
- Generate $E$ via $D$: $g=7+1=8$, $f=8+1=9$. Existing $E$ has $g=11$. **Update** $E$.
- Generate $G$ via $D$: $g=7+3=10$, $f=10+0=10$. Existing $G$ has $g=13$. **Update** $G$.

Open = $\{(G, 10), (E, 9)\}$. Closed = $\{S, B, A, C, D\}$.

**Iteration 6:** Expand $E$ ($f=9$).
- $B$ in Closed, $D$ in Closed, $G$ via $E$: $g=8+5=13$, $f=13$. Existing $G$ has $g=10$, no update.

Open = $\{(G, 10)\}$.

**Iteration 7:** Expand $G$ ($f=10$). **GOAL FOUND.**

**Optimal Path Reconstruction (back-pointers):**
$$
G \xleftarrow{D} \xleftarrow{B} \xleftarrow{S}
$$

**Path:** $S \to B \to D \to G$. **Total cost:** $4 + 3 + 3 = \mathbf{10}$ **seconds.**

> [!TIP]
> **Validation:** Optimal path $S \to A \to C \to D \to G$ has cost $1+5+2+3=11$ seconds. $S \to A \to C \to G$ has cost $1+5+7=13$ seconds. Our A* solution of 10s is indeed optimal.

## 3.3 BFS / DFS / UCS Comparative Trace Matrix

Using the same graph, BFS finds the *shortest in number of edges* (3 edges for $S \to B \to D \to G$ or $S \to A \to C \to D \to G$ or $S \to A \to C \to G$). UCS finds the cheapest by edge weight. The table below is a common KTU exam matrix:

| Algorithm | Order of Expansion | Goal Found Via | Cost (s) | Optimal? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| BFS | S, A, B, C, D, E, G | S→A→C→G | 13 | No (graph is weighted) |
| DFS | S, A, B, D, E, G | S→A→B→D→E→G | 16 | No |
| UCS | S, A, B, C, D, G | S→B→D→G | 10 | Yes |
| Greedy | S, A, C, G | S→A→C→G | 13 | No |
| **A*** | S, B, A, C, D, E, G | **S→B→D→G** | **10** | **Yes** |

## 3.4 Derivation: BFS Time & Space Complexity

**Setup:** Branching factor $b$, depth of shallowest goal $d$, branching uniform.

**Time:** In the worst case, BFS must explore the entire tree up to depth $d$:
$$
T(b,d) = b + b^2 + b^3 + \dots + b^d
$$

Using the geometric series formula with $b \ge 2$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(b,d) &= b \cdot \frac{b^d - 1}{b - 1} \\
&\le \frac{b^{d+1}}{b-1} = O(b^d)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Space:** BFS must store all expanded and frontier nodes simultaneously:
$$
S(b,d) = 1 + b + b^2 + \dots + b^d = O(b^d)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Contrast with DFS:** DFS stores only the path from root to current node plus siblings of nodes on the path. The path is at most $d$ deep, and each node has at most $b-1$ siblings:
> $$S_{\text{DFS}}(b,d) = O(bd) \ll O(b^d) = S_{\text{BFS}}$$
