# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Deterministic Search Algorithms (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This diagram shows how the KTU syllabus partitions deterministic search, what data structure drives each algorithm, and which theoretical guarantees they offer. This is the visual "cheat sheet" examiners expect you to reproduce from memory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Deterministic Graph Search"]
    rootA --> branchA["Uninformed Search"]
    rootA --> branchB["Informed Search"]

    branchA --> nodeBFS["BFS - Frontier is FIFO Queue - f is depth n"]
    branchA --> nodeUCS["Uniform Cost Search - Frontier is Priority Queue - f is g of n"]
    branchA --> nodeDFS["DFS - Frontier is LIFO Stack - f is negative depth"]
    branchA --> nodeIDDFS["IDDFS - Repeated DLS with increasing limit"]
    branchA --> nodeBIDI["Bidirectional Search - Two simultaneous BFS"]

    branchB --> nodeGBFS["Greedy Best First - Frontier is Priority Queue - f is h of n"]
    branchB --> nodeASTAR["A Star Search - Frontier is Priority Queue - f is g of n plus h of n"]
    branchB --> nodeWASTAR["Weighted A Star - f is g of n plus W times h of n where W is greater than 1"]
    branchB --> nodeIDASTAR["IDA Star - Iterative deepening using f threshold"]

    nodeBFS --> propBFS["Complete Yes - Optimal Only for Uniform Edge Costs"]
    nodeUCS --> propUCS["Complete Yes - Optimal for Any Positive Costs"]
    nodeDFS --> propDFS["Complete No for Infinite - Optimal No"]
    nodeIDDFS --> propIDDFS["Complete Yes - Optimal for Uniform Costs"]
    nodeBIDI --> propBIDI["Complete Yes - Optimal Yes - Time O of b to the d over 2"]

    nodeGBFS --> propGBFS["Complete No - Optimal No but Fast"]
    nodeASTAR --> propASTAR["Complete Yes - Optimal Yes if h is Admissible"]
    nodeWASTAR --> propWASTAR["Faster than A Star - Suboptimal by factor W"]
    nodeIDASTAR --> propIDASTAR["Complete Yes - Optimal Yes - Memory O of d"]
```

## 4.2 A* Search Tree Expansion (from the Worked Example)

This diagram visualizes the **order of node expansion** from the worked example in Section 3.2. Solid arrows are parent pointers; the boxed values are $f(n)$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepS["Node S  f 7"]
    stepS -- "1" --> stepA["Node A  f 7"]
    stepS -- "4" --> stepB["Node B  f 6"]
    stepA -- "2" --> stepB
    stepA -- "5" --> stepC["Node C  f 10"]
    stepA -- "12" --> stepD["Node D  f 10"]
    stepB -- "3" --> stepD
    stepB -- "7" --> stepE["Node E  f 9"]
    stepC -- "2" --> stepD
    stepC -- "7" --> stepG["Node G  f 10"]
    stepD -- "1" --> stepE
    stepD -- "3" --> stepG
    stepE -- "5" --> stepG

    stepS:::start
    stepG:::goal
    stepB:::expanded
    stepD:::expanded
    classDef start fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef goal fill:#99ff99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef expanded fill:#99ccff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px;
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Yellow node** = Start.
- **Green node** = Goal.
- **Blue nodes** = Nodes that were expanded before the goal was reached.
- Edge labels are step costs $c(n, a, n')$.
- The **first** time the algorithm reached $G$ was via $C$ (f = 13), but this is **not** the optimum. A* continued, and via $D$ (f = 10) found a cheaper path. The algorithm terminated when $G$ had the **lowest $f$** in the open list.

## 4.3 Generic Deterministic Search Loop (Block Diagram)

This is the canonical architecture for any deterministic search algorithm. The only block that changes between BFS, UCS, DFS, and A* is the **Ordering Rule** block.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["Input Graph G plus Start s0 plus Goal sg"]
    inp --> init["Initialize Open List with s0 - Closed List empty"]
    init --> check{"Open Empty or Node n is Goal"}
    check -- "Yes Empty" --> fail["Return FAILURE"]
    check -- "Yes Goal" --> success["Reconstruct Path via Parent Pointers - Return SUCCESS"]
    check -- "No Continue" --> pop["Pop node n from Open per Ordering Rule"]
    pop --> addClosed["Add n to Closed List"]
    addClosed --> expand["For each action a in A of n - Compute s prime equal to Gamma of n comma a"]
    expand --> valid{"s prime not in Closed"}
    valid -- "Yes" --> compute["Compute g of s prime equal to g of n plus c of n comma a comma s prime"]
    valid -- "No" --> skip["Skip s prime"]
    compute --> priority["Compute f of s prime per Algorithm - Insert into Open with Backpointer to n"]
    priority --> check
    skip --> check
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **graph search** and **tree search**. Why is the closed set essential for the optimality of Uniform-Cost Search? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
- In **tree search**, the algorithm does not remember which nodes it has already visited. The same state can be reached via multiple distinct paths, and each path is explored independently — leading to redundant work and possible infinite loops in cyclic graphs.
- In **graph search**, a **Closed List (Explored Set)** is maintained. Before a newly generated node $s'$ is inserted into the Open List, the algorithm checks if $s'$ is already in Closed; if so, $s'$ is discarded.
- The Closed Set is essential for UCS optimality because without it, a node might be re-expanded via a suboptimal path *after* it has already been popped with its optimal $g$ value. The Closed Set guarantees that the *first* time a node is expanded, it is via its minimum-cost path. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Define **admissible heuristic** and **consistent heuristic**. Is every consistent heuristic also admissible? Justify with one robotics example. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
- A heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** if $0 \le h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$, where $h^*(n)$ is the true optimal cost from $n$ to the goal. In other words, $h$ never overestimates.
- A heuristic is **consistent (monotone)** if for every action $a$ leading from $n$ to $n'$: $h(n) \le c(n, a, n') + h(n')$.
- **Yes, every consistent heuristic is admissible.** This follows by induction along any path from $n$ to the goal. The reverse is not always true.
- **Robotics Example:** For a point robot in a 4-connected grid, the Euclidean distance $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_g)^2 + (y_n - y_g)^2}$ is both admissible and consistent. Manhattan distance is consistent only on a 4-connected grid with uniform cost. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **A\* search algorithm** in detail. State and prove the condition under which A\* returns an optimal solution. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Description:** A* maintains an Open List (priority queue ordered by $f$) and a Closed List. For each node $n$, it computes:
$$
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
$$
where $g(n)$ is the cost from start to $n$, and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate of cost from $n$ to goal. At each step, the node with the smallest $f$ is expanded.

**Condition for Optimality:** A* is optimal **if and only if the heuristic $h$ is admissible**, i.e., $0 \le h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$.

**Proof (sketch):**
1. Let $C^*$ be the optimal cost. For any unexpanded node $n$, the best path *through* $n$ has cost at least $g(n) + h(n)$ because $h(n) \le h^*(n)$.
2. On the optimal path, $f$ is non-increasing (shown by substituting the consistency-like property on the optimal path).
3. Therefore, the optimal goal $s_g^*$ sits in Open with $f(s_g^*) = C^* + 0 = C^*$.
4. A* expands nodes in order of minimum $f$. So no node with $f > C^*$ can be expanded before $s_g^*$.
5. Hence the first goal expanded is via an optimal path. (7 marks)

---

**(b)** For the graph below, run **A\*** from $S$ to $G$ using the given heuristic $h$. Show the Open and Closed lists at every step. Determine the optimal path and its cost. (7 Marks)

| Node | S | A | B | C | D | G |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $h$ | 6 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |

Edges: $S \to A$ (2), $S \to B$ (5), $A \to B$ (2), $A \to C$ (3), $A \to D$ (8), $B \to D$ (4), $B \to G$ (10), $C \to D$ (1), $C \to G$ (5), $D \to G$ (2).

**Model Solution:**

| Step | Expand | Open (n, f) | Closed |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | — | $\{S(6)\}$ | $\{\}$ |
| 1 | S | $\{A(2+4=6),\ B(5+3=8)\}$ | $\{S\}$ |
| 2 | A | $\{B(2+2+3=7),\ D(2+8+1=11),\ C(2+3+2=7)\}$ | $\{S,A\}$ |
| 3 | B (tie w/ C; pick B alphabetically) | $\{C(7),\ D(11),\ G(5+4+10+0=19)\}$ | $\{S,A,B\}$ |
| 4 | C | $\{D(\min(11, 2+3+1+1=7)) \Rightarrow 7\},\ G(\min(19,2+3+5+0=10))\Rightarrow 10\}$ | $\{S,A,B,C\}$ |
| 5 | D (f=7) | $\{G(\min(10, 2+3+1+2+0=8) \Rightarrow 8)\}$ | $\{S,A,B,C,D\}$ |
| 6 | **G** | GOAL FOUND | — |

**Optimal Path:** $S \to A \to C \to D \to G$. **Total Cost:** $2+3+1+2 = \mathbf{8}$. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss the **limitations of Breadth-First Search (BFS)** in robot path planning. How does **Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)** overcome these limitations? Derive the time and space complexity of IDDFS. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of BFS:**
1. **Space Complexity is $O(b^d)$** — for a $100 \times 100$ grid ($b \approx 4$, $d \approx 200$), BFS would need to store billions of nodes. Infeasible for real robots with on-board memory.
2. **Ignores edge costs** — BFS finds the path with fewest edges, not the cheapest. In robotics, energy and time matter, not just "number of moves."
3. **No heuristic guidance** — BFS explores in *all directions* uniformly, including away from the goal. Highly inefficient in open spaces.

**How IDDFS Helps:**
IDDFS combines the *space efficiency of DFS* ($O(bd)$) with the *completeness of BFS*. It performs a series of depth-limited searches with increasing limits $l = 0, 1, 2, \dots$. It terminates as soon as a goal is found at depth $d$.

**Complexity Derivation:**
- Nodes at depth $k$ are generated $b^k$ times across all iterations (except the deepest).
$$
T_{\text{IDDFS}} = \sum_{k=0}^{d-1} b^k \cdot (d - k) + b^d \approx O(b^d)
$$
- Space is bounded by the deepest search: $O(bd)$.

(7 marks)

---

**(b)** Consider a **2-DOF planar robot arm** with link lengths $L_1 = L_2 = 1$ m. Its configuration space is discretized into a $20 \times 20$ grid. Using A* with the heuristic
$$
h(\theta_1, \theta_2) = \sqrt{\theta_1^2 + \theta_2^2}
$$
(in radians) and assuming a step cost of $0.1$ rad between adjacent configurations, calculate:
- (i) The maximum possible heuristic value $h_{\max}$.
- (ii) The maximum $f$-value that can be generated at a node with $g(n) = 0.5$ rad.
- (iii) The order of expansion for the first 5 nodes if the start is $(0, 0)$ and the goal is $(2, 2)$.

(7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum heuristic value:**
The maximum joint angle on the grid is $2$ rad (assuming $\theta_i \in [0, 2]$).
$$
h_{\max} = \sqrt{2^2 + 2^2} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828
$$

**(ii) Maximum $f$-value at $g(n) = 0.5$:**
$$
f_{\max} = g(n) + h_{\max} = 0.5 + 2\sqrt{2} \approx 3.328
$$

**(iii) Order of expansion:**
Step costs are $0.1$ per move. From $(0,0)$:
- $(0.1, 0)$: $f = 0.1 + \sqrt{0.1^2 + 2^2} = 0.1 + \sqrt{4.01} \approx 2.103$
- $(0, 0.1)$: $f = 0.1 + \sqrt{2^2 + 0.1^2} \approx 2.103$
- $(-0.1, 0)$: not in grid (assume angles $\ge 0$).
- $(0, -0.1)$: not in grid.

So A* expands $(0.1, 0)$ and $(0, 0.1)$ first (tie — deterministic alphabetical / lower-$x$-first rule picks $(0.1, 0)$). Next, it explores deeper toward the goal, eventually reaching $(2, 2)$ along the diagonal.

**Order:** $(0,0) \to (0.1, 0) \to (0, 0.1) \to (0.2, 0) \to (0.1, 0.1) \to (0, 0.2) \to \dots$

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing graph vs. tree search:** Many students forget to maintain the Closed List. This silently breaks UCS optimality. Always mention it explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to update existing nodes:** When A* finds a shorter path to a node already in Open, students often *append* the new entry instead of *updating* the old one. This causes A* to expand the same node twice. Examiners deduct heavily.
> 3. **Wrong tie-breaking:** A* must specify a *deterministic* tie-break (alphabetical, or insert order). Examiners dock 0.5–1 mark for vague statements like "we pick any node."
> 4. **Admissibility ≠ Optimality of A*:** Optimality requires $h$ to be admissible *and* the algorithm to expand the *first* goal reached via minimum $f$. Subtle but heavily tested.
> 5. **Misstating complexity:** Writing "BFS is $O(n)$" loses a mark. The correct answer is $O(b^d)$ or $O(V+E)$ with the *graph* version.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this for the night before the exam)**

- [ ] **Determinism** = same input → same node-expansion order → same path. No randomness.
- [ ] **Search Problem Tuple** $= \langle S, s_0, s_g, A, c, \Gamma \rangle$.
- [ ] **Graph Search** uses a *Closed List*; **Tree Search** does not.
- [ ] **BFS**: queue, $f = d$, complete & optimal for uniform cost, $O(b^d)$ space.
- [ ] **UCS**: priority queue, $f = g$, complete & optimal, expands in order of $g$.
- [ ] **DFS**: stack, $f = -d$, not complete in infinite spaces, $O(bd)$ space.
- [ ] **IDDFS**: repeated DLS, combines DFS space + BFS completeness.
- [ ] **Greedy Best-First**: $f = h$, fast but not optimal, can get stuck in local minima.
- [ ] **A\***: $f = g + h$, optimal iff $h$ is **admissible**.
- [ ] **Consistent heuristic** $\Rightarrow$ admissible (but not vice versa).
- [ ] **Dominance**: $h_2$ dominates $h_1 \Rightarrow h_2$ expands $\le$ nodes than $h_1$.
- [ ] **Classic robotics heuristic** = Euclidean / Manhattan distance on grid C-space.
- [ ] **A\* always expands nodes in non-decreasing $f$-order** — a key proof fact.
- [ ] **Heuristic must never overestimate**, else optimality is lost.
- [ ] **Bidirectional Search** halves the effective depth: $O(b^{d/2})$.
- [ ] **Industry use cases**: AGV routing (A*), ADAS lane change (Hybrid A*), VLSI (Lee's algo), manipulators (configuration-space search).
- [ ] **Real-time determinism** is required by ISO 10218 (industrial robots) and ISO 26262 (automotive) — non-negotiable for certification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Breadth First Search - Depth First Search - Dijkstra's Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Breadth First Search - Depth First Search - Dijkstra's Algorithm # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Graph Search Algorithms — Foundation of Robot Path Planning In the context of **Robotics and Automation**, a robot navigating a warehouse, an autonomous vehicle moving through a city, or a robotic arm sequencing tasks must constantly answer one fundamental question: *"Given a network of possible routes, what is the best way to reach my goal?"* The three algorithms covered in this module — **Breadth First Search (BFS)**, **Depth First Search (DFS)**, and **Dijkstra's Algorithm** — are the foundational graph traversal and shortest path techniques used in motion planning, task scheduling, and route optimization for mobile robots and automated systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > A **graph search algorithm** is a systematic procedure that examines the vertices and edges of a graph $G = (V, E)$ in a specific order to visit nodes, find paths, or determine minimum-cost routes between a source and destination. These algorithms are central to robot path planning in configuration spaces and roadmap-based navigation. --- ## 1.2 Breadth First Search (BFS) — The Ripple Explorer ### Formal Definition **Breadth First Search (BFS)** is a graph traversal algorithm that explores all vertices at the **present depth level** before moving on to the vertices at the next depth level. It uses a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) queue** to track the order of vertex exploration. ### Conceptual Analogy 🪨 > Imagine dropping a **pebble into a still pond**. The ripples spread outward in **concentric circles**. BFS behaves exactly like this — it explores all nodes that are **1 step away** before exploring nodes that are **2 steps away**, then **3 steps away**, and so on. > > In robotics, BFS is like a search-and-rescue robot sweeping a building **floor by floor, room by room**, before descending to the next floor. ### Key Properties - **Data Structure Used:** Queue (FIFO) - **Finds:** Shortest path in **unweighted** graphs - **Completeness:** Yes (will always find a solution if one exists) - **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ - **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** BFS is guaranteed to find the **minimum number of edges** (shortest path) in an **unweighted** graph. For weighted graphs, Dijkstra's algorithm must be used. --- ## 1.3 Depth First Search (DFS) — The Maze Explorer ### Formal Definition **Depth First Search (DFS)** is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as **far as possible** along each branch before backtracking. It uses a **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) stack** (or recursion) to maintain the traversal order. ### Conceptual Analogy 🕯️ > Picture yourself exploring a **dark cave** with a single rope tied at the entrance. You walk down one tunnel as far as it goes, and when it ends, you backtrack along the rope until you find another unexplored tunnel. That is DFS — it goes **deep** before going **wide**. > > In robotics, DFS is analogous to a **maze-solving robot** that follows one corridor to its dead end, then retreats and tries the next untried corridor. ### Key Properties - **Data Structure Used:** Stack (LIFO) or Recursion - **Finds:** Any path (not necessarily shortest) - **Completeness:** Yes (in finite graphs) - **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ - **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ > [!WARNING] > DFS does **NOT** guarantee the shortest path. It is best suited for problems like cycle detection, topological sorting, and solving puzzles (e.g., Rubik's cube, Sudoku) rather than route optimization. --- ## 1.4 Dijkstra's Algorithm — The Smart Navigator ### Formal Definition **Dijkstra's Algorithm** is a **greedy graph search algorithm** that finds the **shortest path** from a source vertex to all other vertices in a graph with **non-negative edge weights**. It maintains a set of visited vertices and continually selects the unvisited vertex with the **smallest known distance** from the source. ### Conceptual Analogy 🚗 > Imagine a **GPS navigation system** that knows the distance of every road segment. Dijkstra's algorithm works like a smart GPS that **always expands the closest known location first**, gradually building a shortest-path tree from the starting point. It is the conceptual ancestor of modern routing algorithms. > > In autonomous vehicles and mobile robots, Dijkstra's algorithm is used to compute the **minimum-cost trajectory** through a roadmap where edges represent traversable paths with associated costs (distance, time, or energy). ### Key Properties - **Data Structure Used:** Priority Queue (Min-Heap) or Distance Array - **Finds:** Shortest path in **weighted** graphs (non-negative weights) - **Completeness:** Yes - **Optimality:** Yes (for non-negative weights) - **Time Complexity:** $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ with a **binary heap**; $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ with an adjacency matrix - **Space Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** Dijkstra's algorithm **fails** for graphs with **negative edge weights**. For such cases, the **Bellman-Ford algorithm** is used. In robotics, edge weights typically represent traversable distances, energy consumption, or time — all of which are non-negative. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — BFS Algorithm **Step-by-step procedure:** 1. Create a `visited[]` array initialized to `false` for all vertices. 2. Create an empty **queue** `Q`. 3. Enqueue the **start vertex** $s$ and mark it as `visited`. 4. **While** $Q$ is **not empty**: - Dequeue vertex $u$ from $Q$. - **Process** $u$ (visit/output $u$). - **For each** neighbor $v$ of $u$: - If $v$ is **not visited**, mark it `visited` and enqueue $v$. 5. **End** when the queue becomes empty. ### The 'Why' Behind BFS - The **queue** ensures that vertices are processed in the order they are discovered. - Each vertex is enqueued **exactly once** and dequeued **exactly once** → $O(\vert V \vert)$ queue operations. - Each edge is examined **at most twice** (once from each endpoint) → $O(\vert E \vert)$ total work. - This yields a combined complexity of $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. --- ## 2.2 Operational Logic — DFS Algorithm **Step-by-step procedure (Iterative version using stack):** 1. Create a `visited[]` array initialized to `false` for all vertices. 2. Create an empty **stack** $S$. 3. Push the **start vertex** $s$ onto $S$. 4. **While** $S$ is **not empty**: - Pop vertex $u$ from $S$. - If $u$ is **not visited**: - Mark $u$ as `visited`. - **Process** $u$ (visit/output $u$). - Push all **unvisited** neighbors of $u$ onto $S$. 5. **End** when the stack becomes empty. ### The 'Why' Behind DFS - The **stack** enforces a LIFO discipline: the **most recently discovered** vertex is explored **first**. - DFS can be implemented **recursively**, where the system call stack replaces the explicit stack. - Backtracking is **implicit**: when a dead end is reached, recursion unwinds (or the stack pops), returning to the most recent vertex with unexplored neighbors. --- ## 2.3 Operational Logic — Dijkstra's Algorithm **Step-by-step procedure:** 1. Create a `dist[]` array. Set `dist[s] = 0` and `dist[v] = ∞` for all other vertices $v$. 2. Create a `visited[]` array initialized to `false`. 3. **While** there exist unvisited vertices: - Select the unvisited vertex $u$ with the **minimum** `dist[u]`. - Mark $u$ as `visited`. - **For each** neighbor $v$ of $u$: - If $v$ is unvisited, compute the **alternative distance**: $$\text{alt} = \text{dist}[u] + w(u, v)$$ - If $\text{alt} < \text{dist}[v]$: - Update `dist[v] = alt`. - Record the **predecessor** $\text{prev}[v] = u$. 4. **End** when all reachable vertices are visited. ### The 'Why' Behind Dijkstra - The **greedy choice** — always expand the unvisited vertex with the smallest tentative distance — is **provably optimal** when all edge weights are non-negative. - The **relaxation step** (updating `dist[v]`) is the core operation. It asks: *"Is going through $u$ a shorter route to $v$ than what I currently know?"* - Once a vertex is marked `visited`, its distance is **final** and will never be reduced (this is a direct consequence of the non-negative weight assumption). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Relaxation Formula — Heart of Dijkstra's Algorithm:** $$\text{dist}[v] = \min\Big(\text{dist}[v],\ \text{dist}[u] + w(u, v)\Big)$$ > This single equation encapsulates the entire algorithmic logic. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Concept** | **Formula / Property** | **Time Complexity** | **Space Complexity** | **Graph Type** | |:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | BFS — Traversal | Queue-based level-order visit | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Unweighted | | BFS — Shortest Path | Min edges from source | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Unweighted | | DFS — Traversal | Stack-based or recursive | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Unweighted/Weighted | | DFS — Path Found | Any path, not necessarily shortest | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Any | | Dijkstra — Relaxation | $\text{dist}[v] = \min(\text{dist}[v],\ \text{dist}[u] + w(u,v))$ | $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Weighted (non-negative) | | Dijkstra — Shortest Path Tree | $\sum w$ minimized from source | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ with array | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Weighted (non-negative) | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **BFS** → Social network friend suggestions, web crawlers (crawling pages level by level), broadcast networks, **robot wavefront planners** in grid-based maps. - **DFS** → Maze solving, topological sorting of task dependencies, cycle detection in robotic assembly sequences, solving puzzles like the **8-puzzle** or **Rubik's cube**. - **Dijkstra** → GPS navigation, network routing protocols (OSPF), **autonomous vehicle path planning**, robotic arm trajectory optimization with minimum energy, supply chain logistics. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Common Graph for All Three Algorithms Consider the following weighted undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = 6$ vertices and $\vert E \vert = 9$ edges. We will run BFS, DFS, and Dijkstra's algorithm starting from vertex **A**. ### Edge List (with weights) | Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | A–B | 4 | B–C | 1 | C–D | 8 | | A–C | 2 | B–D | 5 | D–E | 2 | | C–E | 10 | D–F | 6 | E–F | 3 | ### Graph Visualization (Conceptual) ``` B --- 1 --- C / \ / \ 4 5 2 10 / \ / \ A D --- 8 --- E \ / 6 3 \ / F --- ``` --- ## 3.2 BFS Execution — Step-by-Step Trace **Initialization:** - Queue: [ ] - Visited: { } - Start: Enqueue A → Queue: [A], Visited: {A} | **Step** | **Dequeue** | **Process** | **Enqueue Neighbors** | **Queue State** | **Visited Set** | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | A | Visit A | B (unvisited), C (unvisited) | [B, C] | {A, B, C} | | 2 | B | Visit B | D (unvisited); C already visited | [C, D] | {A, B, C, D} | | 3 | C | Visit C | E (unvisited); D already visited | [D, E] | {A, B, C, D, E} | | 4 | D | Visit D | F (unvisited); E already visited | [E, F] | {A, B, C, D, E, F} | | 5 | E | Visit E | F already visited | [F] | {A, B, C, D, E, F} | | 6 | F | Visit F | None | [ ] | {A, B, C, D, E, F} | **BFS Traversal Order:** A → B → C → D → E → F **BFS Shortest Path (in edges) from A:** - A → B : 1 edge - A → C : 1 edge - A → D : 2 edges (A→B→D) - A → E : 3 edges (A→B→D→E) - A → F : 3 edges (A→B→D→F) --- ## 3.3 DFS Execution — Step-by-Step Trace (Recursive) **Initialization:** - Stack: [A], Visited: { } - Push A, then mark and process A. | **Step** | **Current Vertex** | **Action** | **Recursion / Stack** | **Visited** | |:---:|:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | A | Visit A; go to B (first unvisited neighbor) | DFS(B) | {A} | | 2 | B | Visit B; go to A(visited) → C (unvisited) | DFS(C) | {A, B} | | 3 | C | Visit C; go to A(visited) → B(visited) → D (unvisited) | DFS(D) | {A, B, C} | | 4 | D | Visit D; go to B(visited) → C(visited) → E (unvisited) | DFS(E) | {A, B, C, D} | | 5 | E | Visit E; go to C(visited) → D(visited) → F (unvisited) | DFS(F) | {A, B, C, D, E} | | 6 | F | Visit F; go to D(visited) → E(visited). No unvisited. | Return | {A, B, C, D, E, F} | **DFS Traversal Order:** A → B → C → D → E → F > [!NOTE] > The traversal order is **identical** to BFS for this particular graph topology. In general, DFS and BFS produce **different** orders. DFS follows a deep path; BFS fans out level by level. --- ## 3.4 Dijkstra's Execution — Step-by-Step Trace (Detailed Derivation) **Initialization:** $$\text{dist}[A] = 0, \quad \text{dist}[B] = \text{dist}[C] = \text{dist}[D] = \text{dist}[E] = \text{dist}[F] = \infty$$ **Iteration 1: Select vertex with minimum dist (unvisited) = A (dist = 0)** - Mark A as visited. - Relax neighbors of A: $$\text{dist}[B] = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 4) = 4 \quad \text{prev}[B] = A$$ $$\text{dist}[C] = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 2) = 2 \quad \text{prev}[C] = A$$ **State after Iteration 1:** | Vertex | A | B | C | D | E | F | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | dist | **0** ✓ | 4 | 2 | ∞ | ∞ | ∞ | | prev | — | A | A | — | — | — | **Iteration 2: Minimum unvisited dist = C (dist = 2)** - Mark C as visited. - Relax neighbors of C (B, D, E): $$\text{dist}[B] = \min(4,\ 2 + 1) = 3 \quad \text{prev}[B] = C$$ $$\text{dist}[D] = \min(\infty,\ 2 + 8) = 10 \quad \text{prev}[D] = C$$ $$\text{dist}[E] = \min(\infty,\ 2 + 10) = 12 \quad \text{prev}[E] = C$$ **State after Iteration 2:** | Vertex | A | B | C | D | E | F | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | dist | 0 ✓ | **3** | **2** ✓ | 10 | 12 | ∞ | | prev | — | C | A | C | C | — | **Iteration 3: Minimum unvisited dist = B (dist = 3)** - Mark B as visited. - Relax neighbors of B (A, C, D): $$\text{dist}[A]:\ A\ \text{is visited, skip}$$ $$\text{dist}[C]:\ C\ \text{is visited, skip}$$ $$\text{dist}[D] = \min(10,\ 3 + 5) = 8 \quad \text{prev}[D] = B$$ **State after Iteration 3:** | Vertex | A | B | C | D | E | F | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | dist | 0 ✓ | 3 ✓ | 2 ✓ | **8** | 12 | ∞ | | prev | — | C | A | B | C | — | **Iteration 4: Minimum unvisited dist = D (dist = 8)** - Mark D as visited. - Relax neighbors of D (B, C, E, F): $$\text{dist}[E] = \min(12,\ 8 + 2) = 10 \quad \text{prev}[E] = D$$ $$\text{dist}[F] = \min(\infty,\ 8 + 6) = 14 \quad \text{prev}[F] = D$$ **State after Iteration 4:** | Vertex | A | B | C | D | E | F | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | dist | 0 ✓ | 3 ✓ | 2 ✓ | 8 ✓ | **10** | 14 | | prev | — | C | A | B | D | D | **Iteration 5: Minimum unvisited dist = E (dist = 10)** - Mark E as visited. - Relax neighbors of E (C, D, F): $$\text{dist}[F] = \min(14,\ 10 + 3) = 13 \quad \text{prev}[F] = E$$ **State after Iteration 5:** | Vertex | A | B | C | D | E | F | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | dist | 0 ✓ | 3 ✓ | 2 ✓ | 8 ✓ | 10 ✓ | **13** | | prev | — | C | A | B | D | E | **Iteration 6: Minimum unvisited dist = F (dist = 13)** - Mark F as visited. All vertices visited. **Algorithm terminates.** --- ## 3.5 Final Shortest Path Table & Tree Reconstruction | Vertex | Shortest Distance from A | Predecessor | Reconstructed Path | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | A | **0** | — | A | | B | **3** | C | A → C → B | | C | **2** | A | A → C | | D | **8** | B | A → C → B → D | | E | **10** | D | A → C → B → D → E | | F | **13** | E | A → C → B → D → E → F | **Shortest Path A → F:** A → C → B → D → E → F with total cost = 2 + 1 + 5 + 2 + 3 = **13** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Path Reconstruction Method:** Starting from the destination, follow the `prev[]` pointers backwards to the source, then reverse the sequence. This is a standard KTU-expected approach for Dijkstra trace questions. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Sample Weighted Graph (Algorithm Input) ```mermaid graph LR A((A)) ---|4| B((B)) A ---|2| C((C)) B ---|1| C B ---|5| D((D)) C ---|8| D C ---|10| E((E)) D ---|2| E D ---|6| F((F)) E ---|3| F ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — BFS Level-Order Traversal from A ```mermaid graph TB L0["LEVEL 0: A"] L1["LEVEL 1: B and C"] L2["LEVEL 2: D and E"] L3["LEVEL 3: F"] L0 --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 Q1["Queue: A"] Q2["Queue: B C"] Q3["Queue: C D E"] Q4["Queue: D E F"] Q5["Queue: EMPTY"] Q1 --> Q2 Q2 --> Q3 Q3 --> Q4 Q4 --> Q5 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — DFS Recursive Call Stack (from A) ```mermaid graph TD start["Call DFS A"] --> a1["Visit A"] a1 --> callB["Call DFS B"] callB --> b1["Visit B"] b1 --> callC["Call DFS C"] callC --> c1["Visit C"] c1 --> callD["Call DFS D"] callD --> d1["Visit D"] d1 --> callE["Call DFS E"] callE --> e1["Visit E"] e1 --> callF["Call DFS F"] callF --> f1["Visit F"] f1 --> ret1["Return"] ret1 --> ret2["Return to E"] ret2 --> ret3["Return to D"] ret3 --> ret4["Return to C"] ret4 --> ret5["Return to B"] ret5 --> ret6["Return to A"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Dijkstra's Shortest Path Tree (SPT) from A ```mermaid graph LR A((A
dist=0)) -->|2| C((C
dist=2)) C -->|1| B((B
dist=3)) B -->|5| D((D
dist=8)) D -->|2| E((E
dist=10)) E -->|3| F((F
dist=13)) style A fill:#90EE90 style C fill:#90EE90 style B fill:#90EE90 style D fill:#90EE90 style E fill:#90EE90 style F fill:#FFB6C1 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **green nodes** represent the relaxed/settled vertices at each stage. The **pink node F** is the final destination. The edges of the SPT (shown with solid arrows) may differ from the original graph edges — e.g., B is reached via C, not directly from A, because $w(A,C) + w(C,B) = 3 < w(A,B) = 4$. --- ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robot Path Planner Using Dijkstra ```mermaid graph TB MAP["Environment Map
Grid or Topological Graph"] --> GRAPH["Graph Constructor
G = V, E with weights"] GRAPH --> SRC["Source and Goal
Input Module"] SRC --> DIJK["Dijkstra Engine
Priority Queue"] DIJK --> DIST["Distance and Predecessor
Array Output"] DIST --> PATH["Path Reconstruction
Backward Trace"] PATH --> SMOOTH["Path Smoothing
and Validation"] SMOOTH --> CTRL["Motion Controller
Velocity and Steering"] CTRL --> ROBOT["Mobile Robot
or Manipulator"] ROBOT -->|"Sensor Feedback"| MAP style DIJK fill:#FFD700 style ROBOT fill:#87CEEB ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between Breadth First Search (BFS) and Depth First Search (DFS) traversal algorithms. Mention the data structure used by each.** #### Model Answer: | **Parameter** | **BFS** | **DFS** | |:---|:---|:---| | **Data Structure** | Queue (FIFO) | Stack (LIFO) or Recursion | | **Exploration Strategy** | Level-by-level (breadth-wise) | Branch-by-branch (depth-wise) | | **Backtracking** | No backtracking | Implicit backtracking on dead ends | | **Shortest Path (Unweighted)** | Guarantees shortest path | Does **not** guarantee shortest path | | **Memory Usage** | Higher (stores all nodes at current level) | Lower (stores path from root to current node) | | **Use Case** | Shortest path in unweighted graphs, level-order processing | Cycle detection, topological sort, maze solving | --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State Dijkstra's algorithm. Why does it fail for graphs with negative edge weights?** #### Model Answer: **Dijkstra's Algorithm** is a greedy single-source shortest path algorithm that finds the minimum cost path from a source vertex to all other vertices in a graph with **non-negative edge weights**. **Key Steps:** 1. Initialize `dist[source] = 0`, all others = $\infty$. 2. Repeatedly select the unvisited vertex $u$ with minimum `dist[u]`. 3. Relax all edges from $u$: `dist[v] = min(dist[v], dist[u] + w(u,v))`. 4. Mark $u$ as visited. Repeat until all vertices are visited. **Why it fails with negative weights:** Once Dijkstra marks a vertex as **visited**, it assumes the shortest distance to that vertex is **final** and will not be reconsidered. However, with negative edge weights, a longer path discovered **later** could potentially have a **lower total cost** (because traversing the negative edge reduces the cost). This violates the greedy assumption that the first time a vertex is selected, its distance is optimal. Thus, Dijkstra's algorithm produces **incorrect results** for graphs containing negative-weight edges. **Bellman-Ford algorithm** is used in such cases. --- ## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** Explain the Breadth First Search (BFS) algorithm with a suitable example. Show the step-by-step queue operations and mention its time complexity. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Apply Dijkstra's algorithm on the graph shown below to find the shortest path from vertex **S** to all other vertices. Show all intermediate steps in a table format and reconstruct the shortest path to vertex **T**. **(7 Marks)** **Graph for Part (b):** | Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | S–A | 7 | A–C | 3 | | S–B | 2 | B–A | 3 | | A–D | 5 | B–D | 1 | | C–D | 2 | D–T | 6 | | C–T | 9 | — | — | #### Model Solution: ### Part (a) — BFS Algorithm Explanation **Algorithm Steps:** 1. Start at the source vertex. Enqueue it and mark as visited. 2. While the queue is not empty: - Dequeue the front vertex $u$. - Visit $u$ (process/output $u$). - Enqueue all **unvisited** neighbors of $u$ and mark them as visited. **Example Graph:** ``` 1 A ----- B | | 2 3 | | C ----- D 4 ``` **BFS from A:** | **Step** | **Dequeue** | **Visit** | **Enqueue Neighbors** | **Queue** | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | A | A | B, C | [B, C] | | 2 | B | B | D | [C, D] | | 3 | C | C | D (already enqueued) | [D] | | 4 | D | D | None | [ ] | **BFS Order:** A → B → C → D **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ where $\vert V \vert$ is the number of vertices and $\vert E \vert$ is the number of edges. --- ### Part (b) — Dijkstra's Algorithm on Given Graph **Initialization:** $$\text{dist}[S] = 0,\quad \text{dist}[A] = \text{dist}[B] = \text{dist}[C] = \text{dist}[D] = \text{dist}[T] = \infty$$ **Iteration 1: Select S (dist = 0), mark visited. Relax S→A, S→B:** $$\text{dist}[A] = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 7) = 7,\quad \text{prev}[A] = S$$ $$\text{dist}[B] = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 2) = 2,\quad \text{prev}[B] = S$$ | Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | T | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | dist | 0 ✓ | 7 | **2** | ∞ | ∞ | ∞ | | prev | — | S | S | — | — | — | **Iteration 2: Select B (dist = 2), mark visited. Relax B→A, B→D:** $$\text{dist}[A] = \min(7,\ 2 + 3) = 5,\quad \text{prev}[A] = B$$ $$\text{dist}[D] = \min(\infty,\ 2 + 1) = 3,\quad \text{prev}[D] = B$$ | Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | T | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | dist | 0 ✓ | **5** | 2 ✓ | ∞ | **3** | ∞ | | prev | — | B | S | — | B | — | **Iteration 3: Select D (dist = 3), mark visited. Relax D→A, D→C, D→T:** $$\text{dist}[A] = \min(5,\ 3 + 5) = 5 \quad (\text{no change})$$ $$\text{dist}[C] = \min(\infty,\ 3 + 2) = 5,\quad \text{prev}[C] = D$$ $$\text{dist}[T] = \min(\infty,\ 3 + 6) = 9,\quad \text{prev}[T] = D$$ | Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | T | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | dist | 0 ✓ | 5 | 2 ✓ | **5** | 3 ✓ | **9** | | prev | — | B | S | D | B | D | **Iteration 4: Select A (dist = 5) and C (dist = 5) — tie; pick A first. Mark visited. Relax A→C:** $$\text{dist}[C] = \min(5,\ 5 + 3) = 5 \quad (\text{no change, since } 5 + 3 = 8 > 5)$$ | Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | T | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | dist | 0 ✓ | 5 ✓ | 2 ✓ | 5 | 3 ✓ | 9 | | prev | — | B | S | D | B | D | **Iteration 5: Select C (dist = 5), mark visited. Relax C→D, C→T:** $$\text{dist}[T] = \min(9,\ 5 + 9) = 9 \quad (\text{no change, since } 5 + 9 = 14 > 9)$$ **Iteration 6: Select T (dist = 9), mark visited. All vertices visited. Terminate.** **Final Shortest Distances and Paths from S:** | Vertex | Shortest Distance | Predecessor | Reconstructed Path | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | A | **5** | B | S → B → A | | B | **2** | S | S → B | | C | **5** | D | S → B → D → C | | D | **3** | B | S → B → D | | T | **9** | D | **S → B → D → T** | **Shortest Path S → T: S → B → D → T with total cost = 2 + 1 + 6 = 9** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Module Choice]` **(a)** Explain the Depth First Search (DFS) algorithm with a suitable example. How is it different from BFS in terms of the data structure used and path optimality? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For the following graph, perform BFS starting from vertex **1** and list the order in which vertices are visited. Also determine the shortest path (in terms of number of edges) from vertex **1** to vertex **6**. **(7 Marks)** **Adjacency List for Part (b):** | Vertex | Neighbors | |:---:|:---| | 1 | 2, 3 | | 2 | 1, 4, 5 | | 3 | 1, 6 | | 4 | 2 | | 5 | 2, 6 | | 6 | 3, 5 | #### Model Solution: ### Part (a) — DFS Algorithm Explanation **Algorithm Steps (Iterative):** 1. Push the source vertex onto a stack and mark as visited. 2. While the stack is not empty: - Peek/pop the top vertex $u$. - If $u$ is unvisited, visit $u$ and push all unvisited neighbors. **Algorithm Steps (Recursive):** 1. Mark the current vertex as visited. 2. Recursively visit each unvisited neighbor. **Example Graph:** ``` 1 / \ 2 3 / \ 4 5 ``` **DFS from 1 (Recursive, neighbors visited in ascending order):** 1 → 2 → 4 → (backtrack) → 5 → (backtrack to 1) → 3 **DFS Order:** 1 → 2 → 4 → 5 → 3 **Comparison with BFS:** | **Parameter** | **BFS** | **DFS** | |:---|:---|:---| | Data Structure | Queue | Stack / Recursion | | Path Optimality | Shortest path in unweighted graphs | Not guaranteed | | Memory Pattern | Stores all nodes at current level | Stores nodes along current path | --- ### Part (b) — BFS Execution on Given Graph **Initialization:** - Queue: [1], Visited: {1} **Step-by-step BFS Trace:** | **Step** | **Dequeue** | **Process** | **Enqueue Unvisited Neighbors** | **Queue** | **Visited** | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | 1 | Visit 1 | 2, 3 | [2, 3] | {1, 2, 3} | | 2 | 2 | Visit 2 | 4, 5 (1 already visited) | [3, 4, 5] | {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} | | 3 | 3 | Visit 3 | 6 (1 already visited) | [4, 5, 6] | {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} | | 4 | 4 | Visit 4 | None new (2 visited) | [5, 6] | {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} | | 5 | 5 | Visit 5 | None new (2, 6 visited) | [6] | {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} | | 6 | 6 | Visit 6 | None new | [ ] | {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} | **BFS Visitation Order:** 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 **Shortest Path from 1 to 6 (minimum edges):** The BFS tree levels from source vertex 1 are: - **Level 0:** {1} - **Level 1:** {2, 3} - **Level 2:** {4, 5, 6} Vertex 6 is at **Level 2**, so the shortest path has **2 edges**. **Shortest Path:** 1 → 3 → 6 (using the edge 1-3 and 3-6) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Queue vs. Stack Confusion:** Many students incorrectly use a stack for BFS or a queue for DFS. This is the **#1 most common error** and will result in **full marks deduction** for the algorithm section. > 2. **Dijkstra Relaxation Omission:** Students often update distances but forget to update the `prev[]` (predecessor) array. Without predecessor tracking, **path reconstruction is impossible**, and marks for "reconstruct the path" are forfeited. > 3. **Negative Weight Assumption:** Stating that Dijkstra's algorithm "always works" without mentioning the **non-negative weight** assumption is treated as an incomplete answer by KTU examiners. Always add the clause: *"for graphs with non-negative edge weights."* > 4. **Forgetting the `visited` flag:** In BFS/DFS, failing to mark a vertex as visited before enqueuing/pushing leads to **infinite loops** in cyclic graphs. This is a frequently tested conceptual pitfall. > 5. **Final Distance Confirmation:** KTU evaluators check the **last column** of the Dijkstra table carefully. If a distance is updated in an early iteration but the final value is **not boxed/highlighted** in the final table, partial marks may be withheld. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **BFS** uses a **queue (FIFO)** and explores vertices **level by level**; it guarantees the **shortest path in unweighted graphs**. - **DFS** uses a **stack (LIFO) or recursion** and explores vertices **branch by branch (deeply first)**; it does **NOT** guarantee the shortest path. - **Dijkstra's algorithm** uses a **greedy strategy** — always expand the unvisited vertex with the **minimum tentative distance** — and finds the **shortest path in weighted graphs with non-negative edge weights**. - The **relaxation formula** is the core of Dijkstra's algorithm: $\text{dist}[v] = \min(\text{dist}[v],\ \text{dist}[u] + w(u, v))$. - All three algorithms share the same fundamental time complexity of $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ for BFS/DFS, and $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert)$ for Dijkstra with a **binary heap**. - **Path reconstruction** is done by backtracking through the `prev[]` (predecessor) array from destination to source, then reversing the sequence. - Dijkstra's algorithm **fails for negative edge weights**; use **Bellman-Ford** in such cases. - In **robotics applications**, BFS is used in grid-based wavefront planners, DFS in configuration space exploration, and Dijkstra in roadmap-based shortest path planning. - **BFS** applications: shortest path (unweighted), connected components, bipartite checking, level-order tree traversal. - **DFS** applications: cycle detection, topological sort, strongly connected components, solving mazes and puzzles. - **Dijkstra** applications: GPS navigation, OSPF routing, robot trajectory optimization, supply chain logistics.

topics.title: D* Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** D* Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — D* Algorithms

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D\* (Dynamic A-Star) Algorithm** is an incremental heuristic search algorithm, formally known as *Stentz's D\* algorithm*, developed by Anthony Stentz in 1994. It is an extension of the classical A\* algorithm designed specifically to handle **dynamic environments** where the cost of traversable terrain or obstacle configuration changes during the robot's traversal.

In formal terms, D\* is an **anytime, replanning algorithm** that computes an optimal path from a start node $S$ to a goal node $G$ over a graph whose edge costs are subject to change between planning cycles. It reuses information from previous searches to efficiently repair the optimal path when changes in the environment are detected, rather than re-planning from scratch.

The algorithm is officially described as the **"Focused D\*"** family of algorithms, with subsequent extensions including **D\* Lite** (Koenig & Likhachev, 2002) and **Field D\*** (Ferguson & Stentz, 2006).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine you are driving from your home in Kerala (Kochi) to the KTU campus, navigating using Google Maps. The original route suggested goes through NH-66. Midway, you encounter a sudden roadblock due to a landslide at a hairpin bend. Instead of recomputing the entire route from your home (which would waste time), Google Maps *incrementally adjusts* only the affected portion of the path — from your current GPS position — to find a new optimal detour to the destination.

This is exactly what **D\* does**:
- The robot is the "car."
- The dynamic environment (moving obstacles, newly discovered obstructions) is the "landslide."
- The path is the "Google Maps route."
- D\* **incrementally re-plans** from the robot's current position toward the goal, rather than from the start.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary (KTU High-Frequency Terms)

| Term | Meaning | Unit/Type |
|------|---------|-----------|
| **Start Node (S)** | Robot's current/initial position | Grid cell / Configuration |
| **Goal Node (G)** | Target destination | Grid cell / Configuration |
| **State (X)** | A node/cell in the configuration space | Discrete state |
| **g(X)** | Cost from state X to goal G | Real number $\in [0, \infty)$ |
| **h(X)** | Heuristic estimate from X to G (typically Euclidean) | Real number $\geq 0$ |
| **rhs(X)** | "Right-hand side" one-step lookahead cost of X | Real number |
| **k(X)** | Key priority of X in the OPEN list (min of $k_1, k_2$) | Tuple $(k_1, k_2)$ |
| **Backpointer (bp(X))** | Successor of X in optimal path toward G | Node pointer |
| **OPEN list (U)** | Priority queue of inconsistent states | Data structure |
| **CLOSED list** | States already processed | Set |
| **RAISED state** | $g(X) > rhs(X)$ — cost has increased | Status flag |
| **LOWERED state** | $g(X) < rhs(X)$ — cost has decreased | Status flag |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 of PECDT633, D\* is studied as a **dynamic path planner** and contrasted with static planners like A\* and Dijkstra. Students are expected to know its state-space representation, priority-queue mechanics, and incremental replanning philosophy.

### 1.4 Why D\*? — Motivation Over A\*

> [!NOTE]
> - A\* assumes a **static** world map; if obstacles change, A\* must restart.
> - In real-world robotics (e.g., Mars rovers navigating unknown terrain), obstacle discovery is *continuous*.
> - D\* was designed precisely for the **CMU NavLab** autonomous vehicle project to address this.

| Property | A\* | D\* |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Direction of search | Start → Goal | Goal → Start (backward) |
| Replanning | Full re-search | **Incremental** |
| Dynamic obstacles | Cannot handle | **Native support** |
| Sensor feedback integration | Manual | Continuous |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

D\* operates on the principle of **backward search from goal to start**. The search direction is reversed compared to A\* because the **start state changes as the robot moves**, but the **goal state remains fixed**. Searching backward from the goal allows the algorithm to keep the search rooted at a stable point.

### Step 1: Initialization
- The goal node $G$ is placed in the OPEN list with $rhs(G) = 0$ and $g(G) = \infty$.
- All other nodes have $rhs(X) = g(X) = \infty$ initially.
- The robot's start state $S_{current}$ is set, and the algorithm computes an initial optimal path to $G$.

### Step 2: ComputeShortestPath()
- States are popped from the OPEN list in order of their key $k(X) = [k_1(X), k_2(X)]$.
- The algorithm expands nodes and propagates cost changes outward from the goal.

### Step 3: Environment Sensing — modify-cost(c, X, Y, cval)
- When the robot's sensors detect a change in the cost of edge $(X, Y)$ from $c(X, Y)$ to $cval$, this routine is called.
- It updates the local map and triggers **replanning only in the affected region**.

### Step 4: State Update & Insertion
- Affected nodes are re-inserted into the OPEN list with their updated keys.
- Only **inconsistent** nodes (where $g(X) \neq rhs(X)$) are processed.

### Step 5: Termination
- The algorithm terminates when the start state's key is greater than the minimum key in OPEN (no more replanning needed) or when the optimal path is found.

## 2.2 The Three Core Functions of D\*

### 2.2.1 CalculateKey(X)

$$k(X) = \begin{bmatrix} \min(g(X), rhs(X)) + h(X) \\ \min(g(X), rhs(X)) \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The key is a 2-tuple $(k_1, k_2)$ where:
> - $k_1(X) = \min(g(X), rhs(X)) + h(X)$ — the priority of expansion.
> - $k_2(X) = \min(g(X), rhs(X))$ — the secondary ordering (tie-breaker).

Lexicographic comparison: $k(A) < k(B)$ iff $k_1(A) < k_1(B)$ OR ($k_1(A) = k_1(B)$ AND $k_2(A) < k_2(B)$).

### 2.2.2 UpdateState(X)

$$rhs(X) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } X = G \\ \min_{Y \in Succ(X)} \left[ c(X, Y) + g(Y) \right] & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

If $X \neq G$, the **one-step lookahead** $rhs(X)$ is the minimum over all successors $Y$ of the cost of reaching $Y$ from $X$ plus the already-known cost from $Y$ to the goal.

### 2.2.3 Modify-Cost(c, X, Y, $\Delta c$)

When the cost of edge $(X, Y)$ changes by $\Delta c$, this routine:
1. Updates $c(X, Y) \leftarrow c(X, Y) + \Delta c$.
2. Calls UpdateState(X) to recompute $rhs(X)$.
3. If $X$ is inconsistent, re-inserts $X$ into the OPEN list.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown syntax.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Symbol Meaning |
|---|-------------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $g(X) = $ min cost from $X$ to $G$ | Actual path cost from $X$ to goal | $g(G) = 0$ |
| 2 | $rhs(X) = \min_{Y \in Succ(X)} [c(X, Y) + g(Y)]$ | One-step lookahead cost | $rhs(G) = 0$ |
| 3 | $k(X) = [\min(g(X), rhs(X)) + h(X),\ \min(g(X), rhs(X))]$ | Priority key | 2-tuple |
| 4 | $h(X) = \sqrt{(x_X - x_G)^2 + (y_X - y_G)^2}$ | Euclidean heuristic | Admissible |
| 5 | $h(X) = \vert x_X - x_G \vert + \vert y_X - y_G \vert$ | Manhattan heuristic | For 4-connected grids |
| 6 | $c(X, Y) \in [c_{min},\ c_{max}]$ | Edge traversal cost | $c_{min} = 1$, $c_{max} = \infty$ (obstacle) |
| 7 | $\Delta c = c_{new}(X, Y) - c_{old}(X, Y)$ | Cost change detected by sensors | Real number |
| 8 | RAISED: $g(X) > rhs(X)$ | Cost has increased since last expansion | Status |
| 9 | LOWERED: $g(X) < rhs(X)$ | A better path has been found | Status |
| 10 | Optimality: $k_{min}(OPEN) \geq k(S_{start})$ | Path is optimal when start key dominates | Termination cond. |
| 11 | $bp(X) = \arg\min_{Y \in Succ(X)} [c(X, Y) + g(Y)]$ | Backpointer — best successor | $\arg\min$ operator |
| 12 | Replanning complexity: $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ worst-case per replan | Same as A\* but only local | $\vert V \vert$ = affected nodes |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> D\* and its derivatives are deployed in:
> - **NASA Mars Exploration Rovers (MER Spirit & Opportunity):** Field D\* enabled real-time path planning on Martian terrain.
> - **DARPA Urban Challenge vehicles (2007):** Autonomous cars used D\* Lite for dynamic obstacle avoidance.
> - **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots):** Amazon Kiva robots use incremental planners inspired by D\*.
> - **Disaster response robots:** Operating in environments with constantly shifting debris.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Why D\* Searches Backward from Goal

### 3.1.1 Setup

In classical A\*, the search proceeds from the **start state $S$** toward the **goal $G$**, with the f-value defined as:

$$f_{A^*}(X) = g_{A^*}(X) + h_{A^*}(X)$$

where $g_{A^*}(X)$ is the cost from $S$ to $X$ and $h_{A^*}(X)$ is the heuristic estimate from $X$ to $G$.

### 3.1.2 The Problem with A\* in Dynamic Worlds

In a dynamic world, the robot's **start position $S$ changes** continuously (it moves), but the **goal $G$ is fixed**. In A\*, every search expansion depends on $S$ being the root. If the robot has moved to a new state $S'$, the previous search tree rooted at $S$ is **invalid**.

Therefore, the search must be **re-rooted** at every step, which means a fresh A\* search — computationally wasteful.

### 3.1.3 D\*'s Solution — Reverse the Search Direction

D\* reverses the search:

$$f_{D^*}(X) = g_{D^*}(X) + h_{D^*}(X)$$

where now $g_{D^*}(X)$ is the cost from $X$ to $G$ (backward), and $h_{D^*}(X)$ is the heuristic from $X$ to $S$ (estimated).

**Key insight:** Even though the robot moves (so $S$ changes), the search is rooted at $G$, which is fixed. As the robot progresses toward $G$, the heuristic $h(X)$ becomes more accurate and the search tree "naturally tracks" the new start position with minimal update.

### 3.1.4 Derivation of the $rhs(X)$ Equation

Starting from the optimality condition (Bellman equation) for path costs:

$$g^*(X) = \min_{Y \in Succ(X)} \left[ c(X, Y) + g^*(Y) \right]$$

In a planning problem where the goal $G$ satisfies $g^*(G) = 0$, we define the **one-step lookahead value** for any non-goal state as:

$$rhs(X) = \min_{Y \in Succ(X)} \left[ c(X, Y) + g(Y) \right]$$

with the boundary condition:

$$rhs(G) = 0$$

**Detailed algebraic expansion for a 4-connected grid cell $X = (i, j)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
rhs(i, j) &= \min \Big[ c((i,j),(i+1,j)) + g(i+1,j), \\
&\qquad c((i,j),(i-1,j)) + g(i-1,j), \\
&\qquad c((i,j),(i,j+1)) + g(i,j+1), \\
&\qquad c((i,j),(i,j-1)) + g(i,j-1) \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **dynamic programming update** at the heart of D\*.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — CalculateKey(X) Priority Function

### 3.2.1 Motivation

A priority queue must order states by some key. In A\*, the key is simply $f(X) = g(X) + h(X)$. In D\*, the key must also handle the case where $g(X) \neq rhs(X)$ (inconsistent states that need re-expansion).

### 3.2.2 Two-Case Key Derivation

**Case 1: Consistent state ($g(X) = rhs(X)$)**
The current cost estimate is valid, so the priority is the same as A\*:

$$k_1(X) = g(X) + h(X) = \min(g(X), rhs(X)) + h(X)$$

**Case 2: Inconsistent state ($g(X) \neq rhs(X)$)**
The state needs re-expansion. The new value (min of g, rhs) determines the priority:

$$k_1(X) = \min(g(X), rhs(X)) + h(X)$$

Combining both cases via the min operator:

$$k_1(X) = \min(g(X), rhs(X)) + h(X)$$

The second component of the key is just the path cost itself (used for tie-breaking):

$$k_2(X) = \min(g(X), rhs(X))$$

So the key is the tuple:

$$k(X) = \left[ \min(g(X), rhs(X)) + h(X),\ \min(g(X), rhs(X)) \right]$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — State Classification

The D\* algorithm classifies each state $X$ based on the relationship between $g(X)$ and $rhs(X)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Status}(X) &= \begin{cases}
\text{CONSISTENT} & \text{if } g(X) = rhs(X) \\
\text{RAISED} & \text{if } g(X) > rhs(X) \quad (\text{cost increased — re-expand needed}) \\
\text{LOWERED} & \text{if } g(X) < rhs(X) \quad (\text{better path found — propagate}) \\
\text{UNDISCOVERED} & \text{if } g(X) = rhs(X) = \infty
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analytical insight:**
- A **RAISED** state (Case 2) means a previously-cheap edge has become expensive. The robot's path may now be suboptimal through $X$. $X$ must be re-expanded with the new cost.
- A **LOWERED** state means a previously-expensive edge is now cheap. The optimal path through $X$ has changed and this information must propagate to predecessors.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Grid Setup:** Consider a 5×5 grid, with the robot at $S = (0,0)$ and goal at $G = (4,4)$. All cells have $c = 1$ except $(2,2)$ which is initially an obstacle ($c = \infty$).

**Step 1:** Set $rhs(G) = 0$, $g(G) = 0$, place $G$ in OPEN with key $[0+h(G), 0]$.

**Step 2:** Pop $G$, expand to neighbors: $(3,4), (4,3)$ get $rhs = 1$ and $g = \infty$ → LOWERED, inserted with key $[1+h, 1]$.

**Step 3:** Pop $(3,4)$ — its key is smallest. Update its predecessors, propagating the LOWERED state backward.

**Step 4:** After 25 expansions, the initial path is found. Robot moves along the path.

**Step 5:** At time $t_1$, sensor detects that cell $(2,2)$ is no longer an obstacle. Modify-Cost reduces $c$ from $\infty$ to $1$.

**Step 6:** Algorithm calls UpdateState on all affected predecessors of $(2,2)$. Several LOWERED states appear and are re-inserted. The path is repaired in $O(\text{local region})$ time, NOT a full re-search.

This demonstrates the **incremental replanning advantage** of D\*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 D\* Algorithm — Top-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Robot starts at S, Goal G fixed]) --> B[Initialize g X = infinity for all X]
    B --> C[Set rhs of G = 0]
    C --> D[Insert G into OPEN list with key kG]
    D --> E[Call ComputeShortestPath]
    E --> F{Path to G found?}
    F -- No --> G[Return failure]
    F -- Yes --> H[Robot follows path via backpointers]
    H --> I{Sensor detects new obstacle or cost change?}
    I -- No --> H
    I -- Yes --> J[Call ModifyCost edge X to Y with new cval]
    J --> K[Update rhs for affected predecessor X]
    K --> L{X inconsistent? gX not equal rhsX}
    L -- Yes --> M[Insert X into OPEN with new key]
    L -- No --> N[Skip — already optimal]
    M --> E
    N --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The loop on the right side (J → K → L → M → E) represents the **incremental replanning cycle** — the defining feature of D\*. Every time a sensor change is detected, only the *local region* is repaired and the search continues.

## 4.2 State Classification and Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Undiscovered
    Undiscovered --> Lowered : First rhs assignment
    Lowered --> Closed : Expanded by ComputeShortestPath
    Closed --> Raised : Sensor detects cost increase
    Raised --> Closed : Re-expanded with new rhs
    Closed --> Lowered : Sensor detects cost decrease
    Lowered --> Closed : Re-expanded with new rhs
    Closed --> [*] : Path fully repaired
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each transition in the diagram corresponds to a specific D\* routine:
> - *Undiscovered → Lowered*: First time a state's $rhs$ is computed.
> - *Lowered → Closed*: State is popped from OPEN and expanded.
> - *Closed → Raised*: `modify-cost` increases an incoming edge cost.
> - *Raised → Closed*: State is re-inserted with raised key and re-expanded.
> - *Closed → Lowered*: `modify-cost` decreases an incoming edge cost.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — D\* Replanning Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Perception[Perception Layer]
        P1[Laser Range Finder]
        P2[Stereo Camera]
        P3[Sonar Array]
    end
    subgraph Mapping[Local Mapping Layer]
        M1[Occupancy Grid Updater]
        M2[Cost Map Generator]
    end
    subgraph Planning[D Star Planning Layer]
        D1[ModifyCost Routine]
        D2[UpdateState Routine]
        D3[ComputeShortestPath]
        D4[OPEN List MinHeap]
        D5[Backpointer Table]
    end
    subgraph Control[Control Layer]
        C1[Motion Planner]
        C2[Wheel / Actuator Driver]
    end
    P1 --> M1
    P2 --> M1
    P3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D3 --> D5
    D5 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** The bottom feedback loop `C2 → P1` represents the **closed-loop perception–action cycle** that makes D\* truly dynamic. The robot's motion continuously feeds back to perception, which continuously updates the map, which continuously triggers replanning.

## 4.4 Comparison Schematic — A\* vs D\* Search Direction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start S] -- A* forward search --> G[Goal G]
    G -- D* backward search --> A
    A1[A* expands f X = gS to X + hX to G] -.-> A
    G1[D* expands k X = min g rhs + h back to S] -.-> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows visually emphasize the **reversal of search direction**: A\* pushes outward from $S$, while D\* pushes outward from $G$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define the D\* algorithm. Mention any two key differences between A\* and D\*.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **D\* (Dynamic A-Star)** is an incremental heuristic search algorithm developed by Anthony Stentz (1994) for path planning in **dynamic environments** where edge costs change during execution. It extends A\* by allowing the robot to **incrementally repair** its planned path when new obstacles are detected, rather than re-planning from scratch.

**Two key differences from A\*:**

| # | A\* | D\* |
|---|-----|-----|
| 1 | Searches **forward** from start to goal | Searches **backward** from goal to start |
| 2 | **Full re-planning** required when environment changes | **Incremental replanning** of only the affected region |
| 3 | Cannot handle dynamic obstacles natively | Specifically designed for dynamic environments |

*(Full marks: 1 for definition + 2 for differences.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Q2. What is meant by a "RAISED" and "LOWERED" state in D\*? State their conditions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In D\*, every state $X$ is classified by comparing its current cost estimate $g(X)$ with its one-step lookahead $rhs(X)$:

- **RAISED state:** A state where $g(X) > rhs(X)$. This means the cost of reaching the goal from $X$ has **increased** since the last expansion. The state must be **re-expanded** to recompute its optimal cost.
- **LOWERED state:** A state where $g(X) < rhs(X)$. This means a **better path** to the goal through $X$ has been discovered. The state must be **re-expanded** to propagate this improvement to its predecessors.

Mathematically:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RAISED:} \quad & g(X) > rhs(X) \\
\text{LOWERED:} \quad & g(X) < rhs(X) \\
\text{CONSISTENT:} \quad & g(X) = rhs(X)
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Full marks: 1 mark per state definition + 1 mark for the consistent case.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**
> *Note: For every Part B question, KTU provides an internal choice — students answer EITHER Option A OR Option B.*

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the working of the D\* algorithm with its key functions. Discuss how it handles dynamic obstacles. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Background and Motivation (1 mark):**
D\* algorithm, developed by Stentz (1994), is an incremental search algorithm designed for path planning in **partially known and dynamic environments**. Unlike A\*, which requires complete re-planning whenever the environment changes, D\* reuses previous search results and incrementally repairs the affected portion of the path.

**2. Direction of Search (1 mark):**
D\* searches **backward from the goal $G$** to the start $S$. This is because in mobile robotics, the **goal is fixed** while the **start position changes** as the robot moves. A backward search rooted at $G$ remains valid across robot motions.

**3. Key Functions (3 marks):**

- **CalculateKey(X):** Returns the priority key tuple:
  $$k(X) = [\min(g(X), rhs(X)) + h(X),\ \min(g(X), rhs(X))]$$
  This is used to order states in the OPEN list (a priority queue).

- **UpdateState(X):** Recomputes the one-step lookahead:
  $$rhs(X) = \min_{Y \in Succ(X)} [c(X, Y) + g(Y)]$$
  with $rhs(G) = 0$. If $X$ is inconsistent ($g(X) \neq rhs(X)$), it is re-inserted into OPEN.

- **ComputeShortestPath():** The main loop. Pops the minimum-key state from OPEN, classifies it as RAISED, LOWERED, or consistent, and propagates cost changes to neighbors until the path from $S$ to $G$ is optimal.

- **ModifyCost(c, X, Y, cval):** Called when the sensor detects a change in edge cost. Updates $c(X, Y)$ and triggers UpdateState on predecessors.

**4. Handling Dynamic Obstacles (2 marks):**
When the robot's sensor detects a new obstacle (e.g., $c$ changes from 1 to $\infty$) along its planned path:
1. `ModifyCost` is called on the affected edge.
2. The predecessors of the changed cell are marked RAISED.
3. The OPEN list propagates the cost increase backward.
4. Only the **local region** around the obstacle is replanned.
5. The robot follows the **new optimal backpointers** from its current position.

This local repair is what makes D\* suitable for **real-time navigation**.

---

#### (b) Consider a 4×4 grid with start at top-left (0,0) and goal at bottom-right (3,3). All cells have unit cost except (1,2) and (2,1) which are obstacles. Apply D\* to find the initial optimal path. Show at least 3 iterations of ComputeShortestPath and explain how the path would be repaired if (1,2) becomes traversable midway. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Grid Layout (○ = free, ✕ = obstacle):**
```
(0,0) ○ ─ ○ ─ ○ ─ ○ (0,3)
  │   ✕ │   ✕ │     │
(1,0) ○ ─ ✕ ─ ✕ ─ ○ (1,3)
  │   │   │   │     │
(2,0) ○ ─ ✕ ─ ○ ─ ○ (2,3)
  │   │   │   │     │
(3,0) ○ ─ ○ ─ ○ ─ ○ (3,3)  ← GOAL
```

**Iteration 1 (1 mark):**
- Pop goal $(3,3)$: $g = 0$, $rhs = 0$, key = $[0 + h(3,3), 0] = [6, 0]$ (using Manhattan heuristic: $\vert 3-0 \vert + \vert 3-0 \vert = 6$).
- Expand $(3,3)$'s neighbors: $(2,3)$ and $(3,2)$. Set $rhs(2,3) = 1$, $rhs(3,2) = 1$. Both LOWERED, inserted into OPEN.

**Iteration 2 (1 mark):**
- Pop $(2,3)$ (key $[1+5, 1] = [6, 1]$, smaller than $(3,2)$'s $[6, 1]$ — tied, broken arbitrarily).
- Neighbors: $(1,3), (3,3), (2,2)$. Set $rhs(1,3) = 2$, $rhs(2,2) = 2$. Both LOWERED, inserted.

**Iteration 3 (1 mark):**
- Pop $(3,2)$: neighbors $(2,2), (3,1), (3,3)$. Set $rhs(3,1) = 2$. $rhs(2,2)$ remains 2. Insert $(3,1)$ as LOWERED.

**Path Found (1 mark):**
After full expansion, backpointers trace: $(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (3,1) \to (3,2) \to (3,3)$, with total cost 6. This path **avoids** the obstacles $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$ by going around the bottom.

**Repair When $(1,2)$ Becomes Free (3 marks):**
1. **Detection:** Sensor at the robot's current position (say $(2,0)$) detects that $(1,2)$ is now free. Call `ModifyCost` to set $c((1,2), (1,3)) = 1$ (was $\infty$).
2. **Update predecessors:** `UpdateState(1,2)` recomputes $rhs(1,2) = \min[1 + g(1,3), 1 + g(2,2)] = \min[3, 3] = 3$. State $(1,2)$ becomes LOWERED.
3. **Re-insert into OPEN** with key $[\min(\infty, 3) + h, \min(\infty, 3)] = [3 + 4, 3] = [7, 3]$.
4. **Cascading update:** $(1,2)$'s predecessor $(0,2)$ recomputes $rhs$ and may now use the shortcut. New path: $(0,0) \to (0,1) \to (0,2) \to (1,2) \to (1,3) \to (2,3) \to (3,3)$, total cost 6 (same length, but different route).
5. **Key advantage:** Only the **local region** $\{(0,1), (0,2), (1,2), (1,3), (2,3)\}$ is re-expanded, NOT the entire grid.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) With a neat flowchart, explain the working of the D\* algorithm. Compare D\* with A\* in terms of search direction, replanning strategy, and time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. D\* Working — Flowchart Description (3 marks):**
Refer to Section 4.1 (the Mermaid flowchart in this document).

The flowchart shows:
- Initialization phase: $g(X) = \infty$, $rhs(G) = 0$, insert $G$ in OPEN.
- ComputeShortestPath loop: pop min-key state, expand neighbors, update $rhs$.
- Sensor feedback loop: detect cost change → `ModifyCost` → `UpdateState` → re-insert into OPEN.
- Termination: start's key dominates OPEN's minimum.

**2. Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | A\* | D\* |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Search direction | Forward (Start → Goal) | Backward (Goal → Start) |
| Cost function | $f(X) = g(X) + h(X)$ | $k(X) = [\min(g, rhs) + h, \min(g, rhs)]$ |
| Replanning | Full re-search from scratch | Incremental repair of affected region |
| Time complexity (initial) | $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ (same) |
| Time complexity (replan per change) | $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | $O(\vert E_{local} \vert \log \vert V_{local} \vert)$ — much faster |
| Heuristic | Required for optimality | Required; must remain admissible |
| Dynamic obstacle support | Not native | Native — designed for it |

**3. Engineering Trade-off (1 mark):**
D\* trades off **memory for time** — it stores the previous search tree (OPEN, CLOSED, backpointers) to enable incremental replanning. In highly dynamic environments with frequent cost changes, this trade-off is highly favorable.

---

#### (b) A mobile robot is navigating a 10×10 grid from start (0,0) to goal (9,9) using D\*. The heuristic used is Manhattan distance. The robot's current optimal path passes through cells (4,5) and (5,5). Suddenly, the sensor detects that cell (5,5) is now blocked. Describe step-by-step how D\* will repair the path. Compute the new path length using the Manhattan heuristic. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Problem Setup (1 mark):**
- Grid: 10×10, start $(0,0)$, goal $(9,9)$.
- Heuristic: $h(X) = \vert x_X - 9 \vert + \vert y_X - 9 \vert$.
- Original path length: $h(S) = 18$ (Manhattan distance).
- Path: $(0,0) \to (1,0) \to \ldots \to (4,5) \to (5,5) \to (6,5) \to \ldots \to (9,9)$.

**2. Detection Phase (1 mark):**
When the robot reaches $(4,5)$ and its onboard range sensor detects $(5,5)$ is now blocked, the routine `ModifyCost(c, (4,5), (5,5), \infty)` is invoked. The edge cost $c((4,5),(5,5))$ changes from 1 to $\infty$.

**3. UpdateState Phase (2 marks):**
- $rhs(5,5) = \min$ over its 4 neighbors:
  - $c((5,5),(4,5)) + g(4,5) = \infty + 14 = \infty$
  - $c((5,5),(6,5)) + g(6,5) = 1 + 12 = 13$
  - $c((5,5),(5,4)) + g(5,4) = 1 + 13 = 14$
  - $c((5,5),(5,6)) + g(5,6) = 1 + 13 = 14$
- So $rhs(5,5) = 13$. Since $g(5,5)$ was previously 15, we have $g(5,5) > rhs(5,5)$ → **RAISED state**.
- $(5,5)$ is re-inserted into OPEN with new key.

**4. Propagation Phase (2 marks):**
- $(4,5)$ recomputes $rhs$: now $c((4,5),(5,5)) = \infty$, so other neighbors are considered.
  - $c((4,5),(3,5)) + g(3,5) = 1 + 16 = 17$
  - $c((4,5),(4,4)) + g(4,4) = 1 + 16 = 17$
  - $c((4,5),(4,6)) + g(4,6) = 1 + 15 = 16$ ← minimum
  - $rhs(4,5) = 16$. Since $g(4,5) = 15$ previously, $g(4,5) < rhs(4,5)$ → **LOWERED**.
- $(4,5)$'s predecessor $(3,5)$ and $(4,4)$ cascade similarly.

**5. New Path Computation (1 mark):**
The repaired path detours via $(4,6)$:
$(0,0) \to \ldots \to (4,5) \to (4,6) \to (5,6) \to (6,6) \to \ldots \to (9,9)$.
- New path length: $h(S) + 2 = 18 + 2 = \mathbf{20}$ (since we deviate by 1 cell right, 1 cell down, then resume).

The D\* algorithm completes this repair in **O(local) time**, expanding only a small region around the obstacle, not the full grid.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $g$ direction:** Many students write $g(X)$ as "cost from start to X" in D\* — INCORRECT. In D\*, $g(X)$ is the cost from $X$ to the **goal**, because the search is backward. **Lose 1 mark** if mixed up.
> 2. **Forgetting $rhs(G) = 0$ boundary condition:** The goal node's $rhs$ is **always 0**, not recomputed from neighbors. Examiners deduct 1 mark if omitted.
> 3. **Mixing RAISED/LOWERED with A\*'s OPEN/CLOSED:** RAISED/LOWERED are **state classifications**; OPEN/CLOSED are **list memberships**. A state can be both RAISED and in OPEN simultaneously.
> 4. **Drawing incorrect flowcharts:** D\* has a **feedback loop** from the sensor to the planning phase. A linear A\*-like flowchart will lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Not explaining "incremental" advantage explicitly:** Examiners expect the word "incremental" and a contrast with A\*'s full re-search. Missing this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision bullets for KTU exam:**

- ⭐ D\* = **Dynamic A-Star**, developed by Stentz in 1994, for dynamic environments.
- ⭐ Search direction is **backward from goal to start** (key uniqueness).
- ⭐ Two key cost functions: $g(X)$ (cost from $X$ to goal) and $rhs(X)$ (one-step lookahead).
- ⭐ Priority key: $k(X) = [\min(g, rhs) + h(X), \min(g, rhs)]$.
- ⭐ State classifications: **RAISED** ($g > rhs$), **LOWERED** ($g < rhs$), **CONSISTENT** ($g = rhs$).
- ⭐ Boundary condition: $rhs(G) = 0$ and $g(G) = 0$.
- ⭐ `ModifyCost` routine is triggered by sensor updates and initiates local replanning.
- ⭐ `ComputeShortestPath` is the main expansion loop using a min-heap (OPEN list).
- ⭐ **D\* Lite** (2002) is a simplified, more efficient version of D\*.
- ⭐ **Field D\*** handles continuous (non-grid) configuration spaces — used in Mars rovers.
- ⭐ D\* does **NOT** guarantee optimality after every replan in highly dynamic worlds, but is **complete** (will find a path if one exists).
- ⭐ Time complexity: initial = same as A\*, but replanning cost grows only **locally**, not globally.
- ⭐ Admissible heuristic required (e.g., Manhattan for 4-connected grids, Euclidean for 8-connected).
- ⭐ Real-world deployments: NASA Mars rovers, DARPA autonomous vehicles, Amazon warehouse robots.
- ⭐ KTU frequently tests: definition (3 marks), differences from A\*, working mechanism with diagram, and small-grid numerical example.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potential Field Based Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Potential Field Based Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Potential Field Based Path Planning** is a classical reactive motion planning technique in robotics, originally proposed by **Oussama Khatib in 1986**, in which the robot is modeled as a charged particle moving through a scalar potential field artificially imposed over the robot's free workspace $C_{free}$. The goal configuration generates an **attractive potential** that pulls the robot toward it, while each obstacle generates a **repulsive potential** that pushes the robot away. The robot's instantaneous motion direction is obtained by descending the negative gradient of the composite potential field.

Mathematically, the total potential is expressed as:

$$U_{total}(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$$

where $q = (x, y)$ is the robot's configuration in 2-D workspace, $U_{att}$ is the attractive component, and $U_{rep}$ is the repulsive component. The virtual force is the negative gradient:

$$F(q) = -\nabla U_{total}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Potential Field Method is a **reactive / local path planning** approach. It is computationally light, real-time capable, but suffers from the **local minima problem**, which is a frequently asked KTU question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **steel marble rolling on a stretchable rubber sheet**:
- The **goal position** is a deep depression (valley) — the sheet is pulled downward, so any marble placed anywhere naturally rolls toward the goal.
- The **obstacles** are rigid pegs pushing the sheet upward, creating hills and bumps.
- The **robot** is the marble — it is always pushed downhill (toward goal) and away from the bumps (away from obstacles).

**Reading the picture:** The "height" of the sheet at any point $(x, y)$ is exactly the scalar potential $U(x, y)$. The robot's instantaneous direction of motion is always along the **steepest descent** of this imaginary landscape.

### 1.3 Engineering Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard attractive gain constant:** $\zeta \in [0.1, 5.0]$ (dimensionless, often set between **0.5 and 2.0** in practice)
- **Standard repulsive gain constant:** $\eta \in [0.1, 50.0]$ (often set between **1.0 and 20.0**)
- **Standard distance of influence (obstacle threshold):** $\rho_0 \in [0.5 \text{ m}, 3.0 \text{ m}]$ depending on robot footprint
- **Workspace dimensionality:** $n \in \{2, 3\}$ (2-D or 3-D configuration space $C$)
- **Velocity of approach (typical):** $v_{max} \in [0.1 \text{ m/s}, 1.0 \text{ m/s}]$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical units:** Potential is energy-like (Joules), but in KTU 2024 problems it is commonly treated as a **dimensionless scalar** when only the gradient (force/velocity) is of interest. Always verify units with the question paper.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Block: How the Algorithm Works

The Potential Field Method (PFM) executes in three logical stages at every control cycle of period $\Delta t$:

1. **Sensory Snapshot Stage:** The robot reads its current configuration $q_{current}$ from proprioceptive sensors (encoders, IMU) and the obstacle positions $q_{obs, i}$ from exteroceptive sensors (LiDAR, sonar, depth camera).
2. **Potential Computation Stage:** The onboard processor evaluates $U_{att}(q)$, $U_{rep}(q)$, and their gradients $\nabla U_{att}, \nabla U_{rep}$ using closed-form analytical expressions.
3. **Motion Execution Stage:** The robot computes the virtual force $F = -\nabla U_{total}$, normalizes it, multiplies by a step size $\Delta s$ or velocity $v_{max}$, and physically moves (or sends velocity command to wheel motors).
