**Why it works:** The negative gradient is the direction of **steepest descent** of the potential landscape — a fundamental property of scalar fields. As long as the field has no flat regions (zero gradient), the robot will be locally guided toward the goal while avoiding nearby obstacles.

### 2.2 The Attractive Potential

The attractive potential is typically defined as one of two canonical forms:

**Form 1 — Conical Potential (Far-field Quadratic):**

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}^{2}(q)$$

where $\rho_{goal}(q) = \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert = \sqrt{(x - x_{goal})^2 + (y - y_{goal})^2}$ is the Euclidean distance from the robot to the goal, and $\zeta > 0$ is the attractive gain.

**Form 2 — Paraboloid + Well (Khatib's Modified Form):**

$$U_{att}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}^{2}(q) & \text{if } \rho_{goal}(q) \leq d_{goal}^{*} \\[4pt] d_{goal}^{*} \cdot \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}(q) - \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot (d_{goal}^{*})^{2} & \text{if } \rho_{goal}(q) > d_{goal}^{*} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two forms?** The pure quadratic form (Form 1) creates forces that grow without bound as the robot gets farther from the goal, causing **overshoot and oscillations** near $q_{goal}$. Form 2 saturates the force beyond a threshold distance $d_{goal}^{*}$, giving smoother convergence. This is a **classic KTU 2-mark question**.

The corresponding attractive force is:

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -\zeta \cdot (q - q_{goal})$$

### 2.3 The Repulsive Potential

The repulsive potential is defined using the **distance of influence** $\rho_0$:

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} \cdot \eta \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2} & \text{if } \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\[6pt] 0 & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

where:
- $\eta > 0$ is the repulsive gain constant
- $\rho(q) = \lVert q - q_{obs} \rVert$ is the minimum distance from the robot to the nearest obstacle
- $\rho_0$ is the **distance of influence** — beyond this threshold the obstacle exerts zero force

The corresponding repulsive force magnitude is:

$$F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \eta \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)} \cdot \hat{n} & \text{if } \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\[6pt] 0 & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

where $\hat{n}$ is the unit vector pointing from the obstacle toward the robot.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Standard Value / Unit** |
|------------|-------------|---------------------------|
| $U_{att}(q)$ | Attractive potential (scalar) | Dimensionless or Joules |
| $U_{rep}(q)$ | Repulsive potential (scalar) | Dimensionless or Joules |
| $U_{total}(q)$ | Total potential $U_{att} + U_{rep}$ | Same as above |
| $\zeta$ | Attractive gain constant | $\zeta \in [0.5, 2.0]$ |
| $\eta$ | Repulsive gain constant | $\eta \in [1.0, 20.0]$ |
| $\rho_0$ | Distance of obstacle influence | $0.5 \text{ m} \leq \rho_0 \leq 3.0 \text{ m}$ |
| $\rho_{goal}$ | Distance to goal | Meters |
| $\rho$ | Distance to nearest obstacle | Meters |
| $q$ | Robot configuration $(x, y)$ | Meters |
| $q_{goal}$ | Goal configuration | Meters |
| $F(q)$ | Virtual force $= -\nabla U_{total}$ | Newtons or N·m |
| $d_{goal}^{*}$ | Threshold for switching attractive form | Meters |
| $C_{free}$ | Free configuration space | Continuous set |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The repulsive force formula in textbooks may appear in two equivalent forms (one inside-out, one outside-in). The direction $\hat{n}$ is **always from obstacle to robot**, never robot to obstacle. Direction sign errors cost full marks in 7-mark derivations.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

The Potential Field Method is used in:
- **Mobile robot navigation** (TurtleBot, Roomba variants)
- **Drone swarm collision avoidance** (geofenced UAVs)
- **Real-time industrial AGVs** (Automated Guided Vehicles in warehouses)
- **Robotic surgery** (steering flexible endoscopic tools around vital structures)
- **Autonomous vehicle local planners** as a sub-module within the behavioral layer

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering reality:** In modern autonomous vehicles, PFM is rarely used alone — it is fused with **A\***, **RRT\***, or **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** as a low-level reactive safety layer. The local minima problem makes it unsuitable as a global planner.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Attractive Force from Quadratic Potential

We start from the canonical attractive potential:

$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}^{2}(q)$$

**Step 1:** Expand the squared distance in 2-D:

$$\rho_{goal}^{2}(q) = (x - x_{goal})^{2} + (y - y_{goal})^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the expanded form:

$$U_{att}(x, y) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot \left[(x - x_{goal})^{2} + (y - y_{goal})^{2}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Compute the partial derivative with respect to $x$ (treating $y$ as constant):

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot 2(x - x_{goal}) = \zeta (x - x_{goal})$$

**Step 4:** Compute the partial derivative with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} = \zeta (y - y_{goal})$$

**Step 5:** Assemble the gradient vector:

$$\nabla U_{att}(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \zeta (x - x_{goal}) \\ \zeta (y - y_{goal}) \end{bmatrix} = \zeta \cdot (q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 6:** Apply the virtual force definition $F_{att} = -\nabla U_{att}$:

$$F_{att}(q) = -\zeta \cdot (q - q_{goal}) = \zeta \cdot (q_{goal} - q)$$

**Interpretation:** The attractive force is a vector of magnitude $\zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}$ directed from the robot $q$ toward the goal $q_{goal}$. The further the robot, the stronger the pull — which causes the overshoot problem near the goal.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Khatib's Modified Attractive Force (Saturated Form)

For the region $\rho_{goal} > d_{goal}^{*}$, the potential is linear:

$$U_{att}(q) = d_{goal}^{*} \cdot \zeta \cdot \rho_{goal}(q) - \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot (d_{goal}^{*})^{2}$$

**Step 1:** Compute the gradient of $\rho_{goal} = \sqrt{(x-x_{goal})^{2} + (y-y_{goal})^{2}}$:

$$\frac{\partial \rho_{goal}}{\partial x} = \frac{x - x_{goal}}{\rho_{goal}}, \quad \frac{\partial \rho_{goal}}{\partial y} = \frac{y - y_{goal}}{\rho_{goal}}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate $U_{att}$ with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial x} = d_{goal}^{*} \cdot \zeta \cdot \frac{x - x_{goal}}{\rho_{goal}}$$

**Step 3:** Similarly for $y$:

$$\frac{\partial U_{att}}{\partial y} = d_{goal}^{*} \cdot \zeta \cdot \frac{y - y_{goal}}{\rho_{goal}}$$

**Step 4:** Assemble and negate to get the force:

$$F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att} = -d_{goal}^{*} \cdot \zeta \cdot \frac{q - q_{goal}}{\rho_{goal}}$$

**Step 5:** Magnitude analysis:

$$\lVert F_{att} \rVert = d_{goal}^{*} \cdot \zeta = \text{constant (saturated)}$$

**Interpretation:** Beyond $d_{goal}^{*}$, the force magnitude is **constant** at $d_{goal}^{*} \cdot \zeta$ — no longer growing with distance. This eliminates overshoot. This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark derivation question**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Repulsive Force from FIRAS (Function of Inverse Relative Angle and Separation)

We start from the standard repulsive potential:

$$U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \eta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2} \quad \text{for } \rho(q) \leq \rho_0$$

**Step 1:** Let $u = \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}$, then $U_{rep} = \dfrac{\eta}{2} u^{2}$.

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = \eta \cdot u \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{1}{\rho}\right) = -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = -\frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot \frac{x - x_{obs}}{\rho} = -\frac{(x - x_{obs})}{\rho^{3}}$$

**Step 4:** Combine using $\rho = \sqrt{(x - x_{obs})^{2} + (y - y_{obs})^{2}}$:

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = -\eta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{(x - x_{obs})}{\rho^{3}}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the same for $y$:

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial y} = -\eta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{(y - y_{obs})}{\rho^{3}}$$

**Step 6:** Negate to get the repulsive force:

$$F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep} = \eta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\rho^{3}} \cdot (q - q_{obs})$$

**Step 7:** The unit vector $\hat{n} = \dfrac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho}$ is **from obstacle to robot**, confirming the push direction is away from the obstacle.

**Interpretation:** As $\rho \rightarrow 0$ (collision), $\frac{1}{\rho} \rightarrow \infty$, so $F_{rep} \rightarrow \infty$ — a hard collision-avoidance barrier. Beyond $\rho_0$, $F_{rep} = 0$ — the obstacle is "ignored".

### 3.4 Algorithm Step Matrix (Analytical Walkthrough)

| **Iteration $k$** | **Robot Pose $q_k = (x_k, y_k)$** | **$\rho_{goal}$** | **$\rho_{obs}$** | **$F_{att}$ vector** | **$F_{rep}$ vector** | **$F_{total}$** | **New Pose $q_{k+1}$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $(0, 0)$ | $\sqrt{20} \approx 4.47$ | $3.0$ | $(-1.79, -1.79)\zeta$ | $\approx 0$ (just outside $\rho_0$) | $(-1.79\zeta, -1.79\zeta)$ | $(-\epsilon, -\epsilon)$ |
| 1 | $(-\epsilon, -\epsilon)$ | $\approx 4.45$ | $\approx 3.0 - \epsilon$ | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $N$ | $(4, 4)$ | $0$ (reached) | varies | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | Goal reached |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** Always set $\Delta t \cdot v_{max}$ as the step size when asked to compute the new pose. The velocity command is $v = F_{total} / \lVert F_{total} \rVert \cdot v_{max}$ (normalized). Showing both **direction** and **magnitude** at each step earns full 7 marks.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example

**Given:** Goal $q_{goal} = (10, 0)$, obstacle at $q_{obs} = (5, 0)$, $\zeta = 1$, $\eta = 100$, $\rho_0 = 2$, robot at $q_0 = (2, 0)$, $v_{max} = 1$ m/s, $\Delta t = 0.1$ s.

**Step 1:** $\rho_{goal} = \lVert (2,0) - (10,0) \rVert = 8$, $\rho = \lVert (2,0) - (5,0) \rVert = 3$.

**Step 2:** Since $\rho = 3 > \rho_0 = 2$, repulsive force $= 0$. Only attractive force acts:

$$F_{att} = -\zeta (q - q_{goal}) = -1 \cdot ((2,0) - (10,0)) = (8, 0) \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Normalize and scale: $\hat{F}_{att} = (1, 0)$, $v = (1, 0)$ m/s.

**Step 4:** New pose: $q_1 = (2 + 1 \cdot 0.1, 0) = (2.1, 0)$.

**Step 5:** Repeat until $\rho_{goal} < 0.05$ m → goal reached. (Robot only enters obstacle influence zone when $\rho < 2$, i.e., when $x > 3$.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Algorithm Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Initialize q0, qgoal, qobs, zeta, eta, rho0]) --> B[Read Sensors: qcurrent, obstacle list]
    B --> C[Compute rho_goal = distance to goal]
    B --> D[Compute rho = min distance to obstacle]
    C --> E[Compute U_att and gradient]
    D --> F{rho less than rho0?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Compute U_rep and gradient]
    F -->|No| H[Set F_rep = 0]
    G --> I[Sum F_total = F_att + F_rep]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Normalize F_total to get velocity direction]
    J --> K[Apply v_max scaling]
    K --> L[Update qk+1 = qk + v times delta_t]
    L --> M{Goal reached? rho_goal less than eps}
    M -->|No| B
    M -->|Yes| N([Stop: Path Complete])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Force Decomposition Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Input Layer]
        QG[Goal Position qgoal]
        QO[Obstacle Position qobs]
        QR[Robot Pose q]
    end
    
    subgraph ATTRACT[Attractive Field Generator]
        AG[Compute rho_goal]
        AP[U_att Function]
        AF[Force F_att = negative gradient of U_att]
    end
    
    subgraph REPEL[Repulsive Field Generator]
        RO[Compute rho]
        CHK{Rho within rho0?}
        RP[U_rep Function]
        RF[Force F_rep = negative gradient of U_rep]
    end
    
    subgraph SUMMER[Force Summation]
        SUM[F_total = F_att plus F_rep]
        NORM[Normalize to unit vector]
        SCALE[Scale by v_max]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT[Motion Output]
        CMD[Velocity Command v]
        POSE[New Pose q next]
    end
    
    QR --> AG
    QG --> AG
    AG --> AP --> AF
    QR --> RO
    QO --> RO
    RO --> CHK
    CHK -->|Yes| RP --> RF
    CHK -->|No| RF
    AF --> SUM
    RF --> SUM
    SUM --> NORM --> SCALE --> CMD --> POSE
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Conceptual Map: Potential Field Types

```mermaid
mindmap
    root((Potential Field Path Planning))
        Attractive Potential
            Quadratic Form
            Conical Far Field
            Saturated Khatib Form
        Repulsive Potential
            FIRAS Function
            Distance Threshold rho0
            Singular Near Obstacle
        Composite Field
            Linear Superposition
            Gradient Descent
            Local Minima Risk
        Limitations
            Local Minima Trap
            Oscillation in Narrow Passages
            Goal Not Reachable with Obstacles Nearby
        Variants
            Virtual Force Field
            Harmonic Potential
            Navigation Function
            Random Walk Recovery
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Robot Obstacle Interaction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Robot
    participant Sensor as LiDAR Sensor
    participant Planner as PF Planner
    participant Motors as Wheel Motors
    
    loop Every delta_t seconds
        Robot->>Sensor: Trigger scan request
        Sensor-->>Robot: Return obstacle distances and angles
        Robot->>Planner: Send qcurrent and obstacle list
        Planner->>Planner: Compute F_att
        Planner->>Planner: Compute F_rep for obstacles within rho0
        Planner->>Planner: Sum F_total
        Planner->>Planner: Normalize and scale to v_max
        Planner-->>Robot: Return velocity command v
        Robot->>Motors: Send wheel velocities
        Motors-->>Robot: Acknowledge execution
        Robot->>Robot: Update odometry q next
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each block represents an **algorithmic stage** (not a physical component). The diamond-shaped nodes are decision branches where the planner evaluates conditional logic. This functional decomposition is what KTU examiners expect in 14-mark "Explain the algorithm with a block diagram" questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Potential Field Method of robot path planning. List any two advantages and one disadvantage.*

**Model Answer:**
The Potential Field Method, introduced by **Oussama Khatib (1986)**, treats the robot as a point mass in a scalar potential field $U_{total}(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$ defined over the configuration space. The goal exerts an attractive force pulling the robot, while each obstacle exerts a repulsive force pushing it away. The robot moves along the negative gradient $-\nabla U_{total}$.

- **Advantage 1:** Low computational cost — only $O(n)$ per cycle for $n$ obstacles.
- **Advantage 2:** Real-time reactive behavior with smooth trajectories.
- **Disadvantage:** Susceptible to the **local minima problem**, where the robot stalls at a non-goal point with zero net force.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the "local minima problem" in potential field path planning? How does it arise?*

**Model Answer:**
A **local minimum** is a configuration $q_{LM} \neq q_{goal}$ where the gradient vanishes, i.e., $\nabla U_{total}(q_{LM}) = 0$, trapping the robot because the net virtual force is zero.

**How it arises:** When the attractive force from the goal and the repulsive forces from obstacles **cancel exactly** at some point — e.g., a goal located behind a wide obstacle, or in a symmetric obstacle configuration. The robot's motion stops at $q_{LM}$ even though $q_{goal}$ is reachable by a different path.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the attractive force in Khatib's modified potential field method for a mobile robot. Explain why the saturated form is preferred over the pure quadratic form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A point mobile robot is at configuration $q = (2, 2)$ in a 2-D workspace. The goal is at $q_{goal} = (10, 8)$. A circular obstacle is centered at $q_{obs} = (5, 4)$ with a distance-of-influence $\rho_0 = 2$ m. Given $\zeta = 1.0$ and $\eta = 5.0$, compute the **total virtual force** on the robot. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to A(a):**

The modified attractive potential is piecewise:

$$U_{att}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} \zeta \rho_{goal}^{2} & \rho_{goal} \leq d_{goal}^{*} \\[3pt] d_{goal}^{*} \zeta \rho_{goal} - \frac{1}{2} \zeta (d_{goal}^{*})^{2} & \rho_{goal} > d_{goal}^{*} \end{cases}$$

**Case 1** ($\rho_{goal} \leq d_{goal}^{*}$): Taking the gradient,

$$F_{att} = -\nabla U_{att} = -\zeta (q - q_{goal})$$

**Case 2** ($\rho_{goal} > d_{goal}^{*}$): Differentiating the linear form gives

$$F_{att} = -d_{goal}^{*} \zeta \frac{(q - q_{goal})}{\rho_{goal}}$$

with constant magnitude $\lVert F_{att} \rVert = d_{goal}^{*} \zeta$.

**Why saturated form is preferred:** In the pure quadratic case, $F_{att} \to \infty$ as $\rho_{goal} \to \infty$, causing the robot to approach the goal at very high speed and **overshoot** when $\zeta$ is large. With the saturated form, the force magnitude is bounded by $d_{goal}^{*} \zeta$, ensuring **smooth convergence** without oscillations near $q_{goal}$. The threshold $d_{goal}^{*}$ is typically chosen as the robot's **braking distance** based on maximum deceleration.

---

**Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1:** Compute $\rho_{goal}$:

$$\rho_{goal} = \sqrt{(2-10)^{2} + (2-8)^{2}} = \sqrt{64 + 36} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\rho$ (distance to obstacle):

$$\rho = \sqrt{(2-5)^{2} + (2-4)^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Check obstacle influence. Since $\rho = 3.606 > \rho_0 = 2$, the obstacle does **not** influence the robot. So $F_{rep} = 0$.

**Step 4:** Compute attractive force (using Case 1, since we assume $d_{goal}^{*} \geq 10$):

$$F_{att} = -\zeta (q - q_{goal}) = -1.0 \cdot \left((2,2) - (10,8)\right) = (8, 6) \text{ N}$$

**Step 5:** Total force = $F_{att} + F_{rep} = (8, 6) + (0, 0) = (8, 6)$ N.

**Magnitude:** $\lVert F_{total} \rVert = \sqrt{64 + 36} = 10$ N.

**Unit direction:** $\hat{F} = (0.8, 0.6)$.

**Robot's next velocity (assuming $v_{max} = 1$ m/s):** $v = 0.1 \cdot (0.8, 0.6) = (0.08, 0.06)$ m/s.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat derivations the mathematical formulation of the repulsive potential in the Potential Field Method. How is the distance of influence $\rho_0$ chosen in practice? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the following robot path, justify why the Potential Field Method might fail. A robot must reach goal $G$ from start $S$ in a workspace where two obstacles $O_1$ and $O_2$ are arranged symmetrically such that $G$ lies exactly behind them. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B(a):**

The repulsive potential is defined as:

$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} \eta \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2} & \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\[6pt] 0 & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 1:** Let $\rho(q) = \lVert q - q_{obs} \rVert$. Taking the partial derivative of $U_{rep}$ with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial x} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Using $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{1}{\rho}\right) = -\frac{(x - x_{obs})}{\rho^{3}}$:

$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial x} = -\eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{(x - x_{obs})}{\rho^{3}}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore the repulsive force is:

$$F_{rep} = -\nabla U_{rep} = \eta \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{(q - q_{obs})}{\rho^{3}}$$

This force grows without bound as $\rho \to 0$, ensuring collision avoidance. Beyond $\rho_0$, it drops to zero.

**Choosing $\rho_0$:** In practice, $\rho_0$ is set to **$1.5$ to $3$ times the robot's diameter** for circular mobile robots, or **larger than the largest obstacle's bounding circle**. A larger $\rho_0$ makes the robot overly cautious (long detours), while a smaller $\rho_0$ risks near-collisions. Empirical tuning with simulation is common.

---

**Solution to B(b):**

In the symmetric obstacle configuration:

- The robot experiences a strong **attractive force** $F_{att}$ toward $G$ (located behind both obstacles).
- Each obstacle $O_1$ and $O_2$ exerts a **repulsive force** $F_{rep,1}$ and $F_{rep,2}$ symmetrically along the perpendicular bisector.
- At a point $P$ on the line of symmetry, midway between $O_1$ and $O_2$, the **horizontal components of the repulsive forces cancel** (by symmetry), but the **vertical components add** — pushing the robot directly away from the gap.
- The **attractive force** at $P$ has a component pointing toward $G$ (which lies beyond the obstacles).
- If these opposing forces **balance exactly**, the robot experiences a **net force of zero** at $P$: $\nabla U_{total}(P) = 0$.

This is precisely a **local minimum trap** — the robot stalls at $P$ indefinitely. This is a classic **GNRON** (Goal Not Reachable with Obstacles Nearby) condition, which violates the requirement that the goal be the unique global minimum of the navigation function. Remedies include:
- Adding a **random walk** when force magnitude is below a threshold.
- Using the **Navigation Function** of Koditschek-Rimon with a sphere-world assumption.
- Switching to a **global planner** (A\*, RRT\*) when local minima are detected.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Direction sign of $F_{rep}$:** Many students write the repulsive force as pointing **toward** the obstacle. The correct direction is **away from the obstacle** (from $q_{obs}$ to $q$). Direction sign errors lose **2 to 3 marks** out of 7.
> 2. **Confusion between $\rho$ and $\rho_0$:** $\rho$ is the *current* distance, $\rho_0$ is the *threshold* distance. The condition $\rho \leq \rho_0$ determines whether the obstacle is active.
> 3. **Forgetting the negative sign in $F = -\nabla U$:** The gradient points uphill; the force (motion direction) points **downhill** (negative gradient). Losing the negative sign is a **1-mark deduction** per occurrence.
> 4. **Unit mismatch:** Reporting force in Newtons without verifying that the potential was in Joules is acceptable in KTU dimensionless treatments, but explicitly stating "dimensionless" in step 1 avoids ambiguity.
> 5. **Local minima handling:** Students who only define the problem without proposing a **remedy** lose 2 marks in the application part of any 14-mark question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Potential Field Path Planning**

- [x] **Originator:** Oussama Khatib, 1986 — memorize for 2-mark questions.
- [x] **Core equation:** $U_{total} = U_{att} + U_{rep}$ and $F = -\nabla U_{total}$.
- [x] **Attractive potential:** Quadratic (Form 1) and Saturated (Form 2) — Form 2 is preferred to avoid overshoot.
- [x] **Repulsive potential:** Active only when $\rho \leq \rho_0$; grows to infinity as $\rho \to 0$.
- [x] **Repulsive force direction:** From obstacle → robot (push, not pull).
- [x] **Force computation:** Magnitude scales with $\eta$ (repulsive) and $\zeta$ (attractive).
- [x] **Local minima problem:** $\nabla U_{total} = 0$ at a non-goal point → robot stalls.
- [x] **GNRON condition:** Goal Not Reachable with Obstacles Nearby — special case of local minima.
- [x] **Remedies for local minima:** Random walk recovery, Navigation function, hybrid planners.
- [x] **Computational complexity:** $O(n)$ per cycle, $n$ = number of obstacles — real-time friendly.
- [x] **Limitation:** No global optimality guarantee; reactive only.
- [x] **2-D vs 3-D:** Math generalizes trivially; gradient operator $\nabla$ extends to $\mathbb{R}^{n}$.
- [x] **Application domains:** Mobile robots, AGVs, UAV swarms, surgical robots.
- [x] **Khatib's contribution:** Pioneered reactive force-based control in robotics literature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Formula Recall (Memory Aid):**
> - Attractive: $F_{att} = -\zeta (q - q_{goal})$ (near) or $-d_{goal}^{*}\zeta \frac{(q-q_{goal})}{\rho_{goal}}$ (far)
> - Repulsive: $F_{rep} = \eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \frac{(q-q_{obs})}{\rho^{3}}$ (when $\rho \leq \rho_0$)
> - Net motion: $q_{k+1} = q_k + v_{max} \cdot \frac{F_{total}}{\lVert F_{total} \rVert} \cdot \Delta t$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Obstacle Avoidance - Bug Algorithm - Vector Field Histogram - Dynamic Window Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Obstacle Avoidance in Mobile Robotics

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Obstacle avoidance is the strategic task of generating safe, collision-free motion commands for a mobile robot operating in an environment containing static or dynamic obstacles, with the objective of reaching a predefined target configuration while respecting the robot's kinematic and dynamic constraints.

In the **KTU 2024 syllabus framework**, obstacle avoidance is positioned as a *reactive layer* of the broader robot navigation stack (Sense → Plan → Act), distinct from global path planning (which operates on a known map) in that obstacle avoidance algorithms typically work in *real-time* with limited or no prior map information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus 2024:** The three algorithms asked in this module — **Bug Algorithm, VFH, and DWA** — are each considered mandatory topics. Examiners expect students to know (i) the algorithmic logic flow, (ii) the assumptions made about the robot and the environment, and (iii) the relative advantages and limitations of each.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Blindfolded Shopper"**
> Imagine a shopper in a hypermarket trying to reach the dairy section. The shopper **cannot see the full map** (no global planner). However, they have a **torchlight** (range sensor) and can **feel the wall** with their hand (contact/boundary sensor). Three strategies are possible:
> 1. **Walk straight toward the dairy fridge; if you hit a shelf, follow the shelf's edge until you can walk straight again.** → **Bug Algorithm**
> 2. **Build a mental "crowdedness map" of all the aisles you can see, and walk into the least-crowded direction that still points toward the goal.** → **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)**
> 3. **Think about how fast you are walking and how quickly you can turn; pick a speed and turn rate that lets you stop safely before hitting anyone.** → **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)**

---

## 1.3 The Three Algorithms at a Glance

| Algorithm | Sensing Assumption | Robot Model | Computational Load | Year Introduced |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Bug Algorithms** | Range sensor + contact / boundary following | Point robot | **Very Low** | 1986–1995 |
| **Vector Field Histogram (VFH)** | 2-D range scan (sonar/laser) | Circular robot | **Medium** | 1991 (Borenstein & Koren) |
| **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** | Range scan + odometry (velocity) | Non-holonomic / dynamic | **High** | 1997 (Fox, Burgard, Thrun) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics used in this Module:**
> - **C-space (Configuration Space):** Mathematical space in which the robot is reduced to a point and obstacles are dilated by the robot's radius.
> - **Translational velocity $v$:** measured in **m/s**.
> - **Rotational velocity $\omega$:** measured in **rad/s**.
> - **Translational acceleration $\dot{v}$:** measured in **m/s²**.
> - **Rotational acceleration $\dot{\omega}$:** measured in **rad/s²**.

---

## 1.4 Bug Algorithm — The Boundary Follower

**Formal Definition:**
A *Bug Algorithm* is a *reactive, local path planner* that assumes the robot is a **point mass** with perfect localization, perfect obstacle detection upon contact, and perfect range sensing in the direction of the goal. The robot executes two alternating behaviours:
1. **Motion-to-Goal** — move directly toward the target.
2. **Boundary-Following** — when blocked, circumnavigate the obstacle perimeter until a leave-condition is satisfied.

The algorithm terminates when the goal is reached or a *perimeter-following loop* is detected (failure).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Of the three Bug variants (Bug 0, Bug 1, Bug 2), the question bank emphasizes **Bug 1** and **Bug 2**, since Bug 0 is presented as the intuitive baseline.

### Variants
- **Bug 0:** Leaves the boundary at the *first* point where the line to goal is clear. Inefficient — may re-encounter the same obstacle.
- **Bug 1:** Performs a *complete* clockwise or anticlockwise traverse of the obstacle, memorises the closest approach to the goal, then leaves at that point. **Performance guarantee** = $d \le 1.5 \cdot p_{opt}$ where $d$ is path length and $p_{opt}$ is the optimal.
- **Bug 2:** Has a **m-line** (the straight line from start to goal). The robot follows the boundary only until it crosses the m-line at a point *closer to the goal* than the original hit point. **Performance guarantee** = $d \le \pi \cdot p_{opt} / 2 \approx 1.57 \cdot p_{opt}$ in the worst case, but in practice often better than Bug 1.

---

## 1.5 Vector Field Histogram (VFH) — The Polar Crowd Map

**Formal Definition:**
The VFH method (Borenstein & Koren, 1991) is a *real-time, grid-based obstacle avoidance* technique that converts a 2-D occupancy-grid map (or raw scan) into a **1-D polar histogram**, where each angular sector $\alpha_k$ stores an obstacle-density value. The robot then steers toward the angular valley (lowest density) in the direction closest to the goal.

> [!NOTE]
> VFH is essentially a **look-up and pick** algorithm. The robot does not think — it just asks, "In which angular direction is the obstacle density lowest and the goal lies nearby?"

### Three-Stage Pipeline
1. **Stage 1 — Cartesian Grid Map:** Maintain an occupancy grid (typically 25 cm × 25 cm cells) updated by range sensors.
2. **Stage 2 — Polar Histogram $H$:** Convert the grid into a polar histogram over $n$ sectors (typically $n = 72$ ⇒ each sector = $5°$).
3. **Stage 3 — Steering Decision:** Find a "valley" of sectors below a threshold $H_{th}$, and choose the sector closest to the goal direction.

### Improved Variants
- **VFH+ (1995)** — adds robot dimensions and turning radius explicitly.
- **VFH\* (1998)** — combines VFH with **A\*** look-ahead.

---

## 1.6 Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) — The Velocity Selector

**Formal Definition:**
The Dynamic Window Approach is a *velocity-space sampling* technique that searches the space of feasible translational and rotational velocities $(v, \omega)$ reachable within a short time interval, subject to the robot's acceleration limits, and selects the velocity pair that maximises an *objective function* balancing **goal heading, clearance, and progress speed**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Insight of DWA:** The robot's physical limits (motor torque, max acceleration, sampling period) define a small *dynamic window* in $(v, \omega)$ space. Searching this small window is computationally cheap, and every candidate in it is *guaranteed* to be dynamically reachable — no impossible trajectories are even considered.

### Three Spaces in DWA

$$
V_s = \{(v,\omega) \mid v \in [v_{min}, v_{max}] \ \text{and}\ \omega \in [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}]\}
$$
$$
V_d = \{(v,\omega) \mid v \in [v_c - \dot{v}\Delta t, v_c + \dot{v}\Delta t] \ \text{and}\ \omega \in [\omega_c - \dot{\omega}\Delta t, \omega_c + \dot{\omega}\Delta t]\}
$$
$$
V_a = \{(v,\omega) \mid v \le \sqrt{2 \cdot dist(v,\omega) \cdot \dot{v}_b} \ \text{and}\ \omega \le \sqrt{2 \cdot dist(v,\omega) \cdot \dot{\omega}_b}\}
$$

The final **search space** is $V_r = V_s \cap V_d \cap V_a$.

---

## 1.7 Comparative Conceptual Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Summary — "Three Levels of Robot Intelligence"**
> - **Bug** = a cockroach — touches the wall, follows it, no real brain.
> - **VFH** = a mouse — builds a partial mental map and picks the easiest corridor.
> - **DWA** = a driver — checks what is *physically possible to drive* in the next second, and chooses the safest fast option.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bug Algorithm — Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 Bug 0 — The Simplest Variant

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. From current position $q$, compute $\theta_{goal} = \text{atan2}(x_g - x, y_g - y)$.
2. Drive forward along $\theta_{goal}$ in small steps of length $\Delta s$.
3. If a sensor detects an obstacle within distance $d_{safe}$:
    - Switch to *boundary-following* mode (e.g., keep right side at distance $d_{boundary}$).
    - Continue boundary following.
4. At every step during boundary following, check if the *direct line* to the goal is free.
5. If free, leave the boundary and resume Motion-to-Goal.
6. If a closed loop is detected (robot returns to the original hit point), declare **failure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Bug 0 is **not** complete — it can fail in certain concave environments because the "first free line" rule can trap the robot in a local minimum. KTU 2024 valuation expects students to identify this as a *limitation*.

### 2.1.2 Bug 1 — Complete Boundary Traversal

**Operational flow:**
1. **Hit point $H$** — record the boundary point where Motion-to-Goal first failed.
2. **Boundary traverse** — walk the full perimeter of the obstacle, recording every position.
3. **Closest point $L$** — identify the perimeter point closest to the goal.
4. **Leave at $L$** — at the end of the traverse, switch back to Motion-to-Goal from $L$.

**Performance bound:**
$$
L_{Bug1} \le \frac{1}{2} (n + 1) \cdot L_{opt}
$$
where $n$ is the number of obstacles in the optimal path vicinity. Practically, for $n$ simple convex obstacles:
$$
L_{Bug1} \le 1.5 \cdot L_{opt}
$$

### 2.1.3 Bug 2 — m-line Triggered Leaving

**Operational flow:**
1. Define the **m-line** as the straight line segment connecting the start $S$ to the goal $G$.
2. From $S$, the robot moves along the m-line toward $G$.
3. At a **hit point $H$**, switch to boundary following.
4. While following the boundary, watch for a **leave point $L$** where:
    - The robot crosses the m-line, AND
    - The distance from $L$ to $G$ is **strictly less** than the distance from $H$ to $G$.
5. At $L$, leave the boundary and resume motion along the m-line.

**Why Bug 2 is often better than Bug 1:** Bug 2 leaves *as soon as* the heuristic is met, avoiding unnecessary traversal. In a typical uncluttered environment, $L_{Bug2} \approx L_{opt}$.

---

## 2.2 Vector Field Histogram (VFH) — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.2.1 Stage 1 — Occupancy Grid Update

For each cell $(i,j)$ in a 2-D grid covering a window of size $(2c+1) \times (2c+1)$ around the robot, a certainty value $C_{i,j}$ is updated based on sensor returns:
- Range reading at distance $r$ in direction $\phi$ → cell at $(x_c, y_c) = (r \cos\phi, r \sin\phi)$ increments $C$.
- Cells between the sensor and the obstacle (the "free cells") are decremented.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** A 25 cm × 25 cm grid with a $\pm 5$ m window around the robot gives a $41 \times 41$ grid, which is small enough for **real-time** updates at 20 Hz on embedded processors.

### 2.2.2 Stage 2 — Polar Histogram Construction

For each cell $(i,j)$ within the active window, the **obstacle density magnitude** is computed as:

$$
m_{i,j} = (C_{i,j}^*)^2 \cdot \left(a - b \cdot d_{i,j}\right)
$$

**Why the squared certainty and the linear distance factor?**
- $(C_{i,j}^*)^2$ — non-linearly amplifies confidence; a near-certain obstacle dominates.
- $(a - b \cdot d_{i,j})$ — gives a higher weight to **closer** obstacles (since $d_{i,j}$ is the distance from the robot to the cell).
- Constants $a$ and $b$ are tuned so that the expression remains positive across the active window.

Each cell contributes to a discrete set of angular sectors $k$. For a cell centred at angle $\beta_{i,j}$:

$$
h'_{i,j}(k) = 1 - \frac{\left(k \cdot \alpha - \beta_{i,j}\right)}{\alpha}, \quad \text{if}\ \beta_{i,j} - \alpha < k\alpha < \beta_{i,j} + \alpha
$$

The **polar histogram sector value** is then:

$$
H_k = \sum_{i,j} m_{i,j} \cdot h'_{i,j}(k)
$$

where the sum is over all cells whose direction falls in sector $k$, and $k \in \{1, 2, ..., n\}$ for $n$ sectors.

### 2.2.3 Stage 3 — Threshold and Valley Selection

1. A binary threshold map is built:
$$
\bar{H}_k = \begin{cases} 1, & H_k > H_{th} \ (\text{blocked}) \\ 0, & H_k \le H_{th} \ (\text{free}) \end{cases}
$$
2. **Candidate valleys** are maximal contiguous runs of free sectors.
3. The **steering direction** is chosen as the sector within the candidate valley that has minimum angular distance to the goal direction $\theta_{goal}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly assume the robot picks the *lowest* $H_k$ value. The correct behaviour is: pick the **candidate valley closest to the goal direction** (i.e., minimum $|\theta_{goal} - k\alpha|$). This is what makes VFH *goal-directed* rather than purely reactive.

### 2.2.4 VFH+ — Incorporating Robot Geometry

VFH+ adds:
- Robot **radius** $r_r$ and **safety margin** $r_s$ — so cells within $r_r + r_s$ are *always* blocked.
- A smoothed histogram that accounts for the **arc** swept by the robot at each sector as it moves (not just a point in space).

---

## 2.3 Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.3.1 The Three Velocity Spaces (Recap with Meaning)

| Space | Symbol | Constraint | Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Search space** | $V_s$ | Hardware limits $v \in [v_{min}, v_{max}], \omega \in [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}]$ | Defines all physically possible commands |
| **Dynamic window** | $V_d$ | Acceleration limits over $\Delta t$ | Defines commands reachable in one time step |
| **Admissible space** | $V_a$ | Must allow full stop before collision | Defines safe commands |
| **Resulting space** | $V_r = V_s \cap V_d \cap V_a$ | All constraints combined | Search over this set only |

### 2.3.2 The Objective Function

For each candidate $(v, \omega) \in V_r$, three components are evaluated:

$$
G(v, \omega) = \sigma \left( \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v, \omega) + \beta \cdot \text{dist}(v, \omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}(v, \omega) \right)
$$

**Component meanings:**
- $\text{heading}(v, \omega)$ = $1 - \frac{\theta_{goal} - \theta_{predicted}}{\pi}$ → **alignment with goal** (1 = perfectly aligned, 0 = $180°$ off).
- $\text{dist}(v, \omega)$ = distance to the closest obstacle along the **predicted trajectory arc** (0 if collision imminent).
- $\text{velocity}(v, \omega) = \vert v \vert$ → **reward for higher speed** (encourages forward progress).
- $\sigma(\cdot)$ = a smoothing function (often a simple normalisation over all candidates, e.g., $\sigma(x) = x / \sum x_i$).
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = tunable weights.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $\sigma$ (smoothing)?** Without smoothing, one component (e.g., distance, which can be 0) dominates and prevents fair comparison. Smoothing normalises each component across the candidate set so weights are meaningful.

### 2.3.3 Trajectory Prediction

A circular arc model is assumed:
$$
x(t) = x_0 + v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \cos(\theta_0 + \omega t)
$$
$$
y(t) = y_0 + v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sin(\theta_0 + \omega t)
$$
$$
\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \omega t
$$

For a short simulation horizon (typically 1–2 seconds discretised into 50–100 samples), the trajectory is checked against the occupancy grid for collisions.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Memorise Before Exam**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Bug 1 path bound | $L_{Bug1} \le 1.5 \cdot L_{opt}$ | Path-length ratio, dimensionless |
| 2 | Bug 2 path bound | $L_{Bug2} \le (\pi/2) \cdot L_{opt} \approx 1.57 \cdot L_{opt}$ | Worst case, often near 1 in practice |
| 3 | VFH cell weight | $m_{i,j} = (C_{i,j}^*)^2 (a - b \cdot d_{i,j})$ | $C_{i,j}^*$ dimensionless, $d_{i,j}$ in **m** |
| 4 | VFH sector contribution | $h'_{i,j}(k) = 1 - \dfrac{\vert k\alpha - \beta_{i,j} \vert}{\alpha}$ | Valid for $\vert k\alpha - \beta_{i,j} \vert < \alpha$ |
| 5 | VFH sector value | $H_k = \sum_{i,j} m_{i,j} \cdot h'_{i,j}(k)$ | Sectors indexed $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ |
| 6 | VFH angular resolution | $\alpha = 360° / n$ | Typically $n = 72 \Rightarrow \alpha = 5°$ |
| 7 | DWA search space | $V_s = [v_{min}, v_{max}] \times [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}]$ | Velocity-space box |
| 8 | DWA dynamic window | $V_d = [v_c - \dot{v}\Delta t, v_c + \dot{v}\Delta t] \times [\omega_c - \dot{\omega}\Delta t, \omega_c + \dot{\omega}\Delta t]$ | Current velocity $(v_c, \omega_c)$ |
| 9 | DWA admissible | $v \le \sqrt{2 \cdot d \cdot \dot{v}_b}, \quad \omega \le \sqrt{2 \cdot d \cdot \dot{\omega}_b}$ | $d$ = clearance, $\dot{v}_b, \dot{\omega}_b$ = braking limits |
| 10 | DWA objective | $G(v, \omega) = \sigma(\alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{dist} + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity})$ | $\alpha + \beta + \gamma$ weighted blend |
| 11 | DWA arc trajectory | $x(t) = x_0 + v \Delta t \cos(\theta_0 + \omega t)$ | Circular-arc model |
| 12 | Kinematic constraint | $\omega \cdot \Delta t = \Delta \theta$ | Heading change per cycle |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these algorithms live in industry:**
> - **Bug 1 / Bug 2** → teaching prototypes, simple floor-cleaning robots (early-generation Roomba), and any *complete* coverage planner in a structured indoor space.
> - **VFH / VFH+** → industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) in warehouses with **static** obstacles, e.g., Kiva/Amazon robots, where map is largely known.
> - **DWA** → autonomous cars (in early CMU NavLab work), differential-drive research platforms (TurtleBot, Pioneer), and any scenario where **kinematic and acceleration limits** must be respected.
> - **Modern hybrids** → ROS 2 navigation stack (`nav2`) uses **DWA** as the default local planner and **Teb Local Planner** as an alternative; VFH is offered as a plugin in older ROS 1 stacks.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bug 1 Path-Length Performance Bound

**Statement to prove:** For $n$ convex obstacles, $L_{Bug1} \le 1.5 \cdot L_{opt}$.

### Setup
- The optimal path $p_{opt}$ has length $L_{opt}$ and intersects the boundaries of $n$ obstacles at points $H_1, L_1, H_2, L_2, \ldots, H_n, L_n$ (hit-leave pairs in order).
- Each segment of the optimal path between obstacles is a straight line of length $\le L_{opt}$.

### Step 1 — Sum of straight-line segments

The straight-line motion portions of Bug 1 are exactly the **start-to-first-hit, leave-to-next-hit, …, last-leave-to-goal** segments. These are identical (geometrically) to the corresponding segments of the optimal path, so their total length is $\le L_{opt}$.

### Step 2 — Bound on each perimeter traversal

For each of the $n$ obstacles, Bug 1 walks **at most half the perimeter** (because it starts at $H_i$, walks the full perimeter once, and exits at $L_i$, the closest point to goal — geometrically this is at most half the perimeter).

The half-perimeter of a convex obstacle is **less than the straight-line distance** $H_i L_i$ plus the boundary length from $H_i$ to $L_i$ via the far side. The maximum ratio of half-perimeter to straight-line distance $H_i L_i$ is bounded by $\pi/2$ (a semicircle).

### Step 3 — Composition

$$
L_{Bug1} \le L_{opt} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot n \cdot \overline{HL}_{avg}
$$

For a single obstacle, a tighter bound uses the inequality: half-perimeter $\le \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{perimeter} \le \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot r$, and a careful case analysis (see Lumelsky's 1991 paper) yields:

$$
L_{Bug1} \le L_{opt} + \frac{n}{2} \cdot L_{opt} = \frac{(n+2)}{2} \cdot L_{opt}
$$

For a single convex obstacle ($n = 1$), this gives $L_{Bug1} \le 1.5 \cdot L_{opt}$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: VFH Sector Contribution Function

**Goal:** Show how a single cell's contribution is spread over the angular sectors it influences.

### Step 1 — Cell-to-Sector Geometry

A cell at polar angle $\beta_{i,j}$ affects sectors whose centre $k\alpha$ lies within $\pm \alpha$ of $\beta_{i,j}$. The influence is maximum at $k\alpha = \beta_{i,j}$ and decreases linearly to zero at the boundaries.

### Step 2 — Linear Decay Function

Define for sector $k$:

$$
h'_{i,j}(k) =
\begin{cases}
1 - \dfrac{\vert k\alpha - \beta_{i,j} \vert}{\alpha}, & \vert k\alpha - \beta_{i,j} \vert < \alpha \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Verification of Properties

- **Maximum value:** At $k\alpha = \beta_{i,j}$, $h' = 1$. ✓
- **Zero at boundary:** At $\vert k\alpha - \beta_{i,j} \vert = \alpha$, $h' = 0$. ✓
- **Continuity:** Linear, hence continuous. ✓
- **Width of influence:** A single cell influences **exactly 3 sectors** (centre + one on each side) when $\alpha$ is the sector width. For finer resolution (e.g., $\alpha = 5°$), this becomes the local smoothing kernel.

### Step 4 — Summing Across the Grid

$$
H_k = \sum_{i,j} m_{i,j} \cdot h'_{i,j}(k), \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $H_k$ is a *convolution* of the discrete cell-density map $m_{i,j}$ with the angular kernel $h'_{i,j}(k)$. It is a **discrete approximation of a 2-D polar density field** projected onto 1-D angular space.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: DWA Admissible Velocity Constraint

**Goal:** Show that the admissible-velocity constraint $v \le \sqrt{2 \cdot d \cdot \dot{v}_b}$ guarantees a collision-free stop.

### Step 1 — Braking Kinematics

The robot is at current translational velocity $v$ and must come to a stop before traveling distance $d$ (the distance to the closest obstacle along its trajectory). Assuming constant braking acceleration $-\dot{v}_b$:

$$
v_f^2 = v^2 - 2 \dot{v}_b \cdot s
$$

where $s$ is the stopping distance and $v_f = 0$ at the moment of stopping:

$$
0 = v^2 - 2 \dot{v}_b \cdot s \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \frac{v^2}{2 \dot{v}_b}
$$

### Step 2 — Safety Constraint

To stop *before* hitting the obstacle, we need $s \le d$:

$$
\frac{v^2}{2 \dot{v}_b} \le d
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $v$

$$
v^2 \le 2 \dot{v}_b \cdot d
$$

Taking the positive root (since $v \ge 0$ in the forward direction):

$$
\boxed{v \le \sqrt{2 \dot{v}_b \cdot d}}
$$

For rotational velocity, by an identical argument (with clearance $d$ and braking $\dot{\omega}_b$):

$$
\omega \le \sqrt{2 \dot{\omega}_b \cdot d}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the "Admissible Velocities" $V_a$ set in the original Fox et al. (1997) DWA paper.** The constraint couples the *velocity* command directly to the *measured clearance* — a clean and elegant safety guarantee.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Comparison

| Property | **Bug Algorithm** | **VFH** | **DWA** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Robot model** | Point | Circular (VFH+ adds radius) | Differential / car-like |
| **Map requirement** | None (reactive) | Local grid (or raw scan) | Local grid + current velocity |
| **Dynamics-aware** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Optimality** | Bounded by $1.5 \cdot p_{opt}$ (Bug 1) | Heuristic, no global bound | Local optimum in velocity space |
| **Local minima** | Can fail in concavities | Smooth, generally safe | Can get stuck in symmetric obstacles |
| **Computational cost** | $O(1)$ per step | $O(n_{cells})$ for grid | $O(N_{samples})$ for velocity space |
| **Output** | Direction of motion (left/right + forward) | Steering angle | Velocity $(v, \omega)$ |
| **Real-time capable** | ✅ Yes (any platform) | ✅ Yes (20 Hz typical) | ✅ Yes (10–50 Hz typical) |
| **Used in** | Research, teaching, Roomba-era | Warehouse AGVs, ROS 1 | Modern ROS 2 nav2 stack, TurtleBot |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** The progression Bug → VFH → DWA is also a progression in **cognitive sophistication** and **physical realism** of the robot model. The KTU syllabus uses this progression to introduce the student to the layered thinking in real navigation stacks.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: VFH on a Simple Scenario

**Problem:** A robot at origin $(0,0)$ with a $360°$ scan detects four cells of high certainty at the following polar coordinates (in metres and degrees):

| Cell | $d_{i,j}$ (m) | $\beta_{i,j}$ (°) | $C_{i,j}^*$ |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | 2.0 | 30 | 1.0 |
| 2 | 1.5 | 90 | 0.8 |
| 3 | 1.0 | 200 | 1.0 |
| 4 | 2.5 | 320 | 0.6 |

Goal direction: $\theta_{goal} = 0°$ (toward positive x-axis). Sector resolution $\alpha = 90°$, $n = 4$ sectors centred at $k\alpha = 45°, 135°, 225°, 315°$. Constants: $a = 30$, $b = 5$.

### Step 1 — Compute $m_{i,j}$

Using $m_{i,j} = (C_{i,j}^*)^2 \cdot (a - b \cdot d_{i,j})$:

- Cell 1: $m_{1,1} = 1.0^2 \cdot (30 - 5 \cdot 2.0) = 20$
- Cell 2: $m_{2,2} = 0.8^2 \cdot (30 - 5 \cdot 1.5) = 0.64 \cdot 22.5 = 14.4$
- Cell 3: $m_{3,3} = 1.0^2 \cdot (30 - 5 \cdot 1.0) = 25$
- Cell 4: $m_{4,4} = 0.6^2 \cdot (30 - 5 \cdot 2.5) = 0.36 \cdot 17.5 = 6.3$

### Step 2 — Compute Sector Contributions

Using $h'(k) = 1 - \frac{\vert k\alpha - \beta_{i,j} \vert}{\alpha}$ for the sector within $\pm 90°$:

**Sector $k=1$ (centre $45°$):**
- Cell 1 ($30°$): $h' = 1 - \frac{\vert 45 - 30 \vert}{90} = 1 - 0.167 = 0.833$
- Cell 4 ($320°$): $\vert 45 - 320 \vert = 275° > 90°$, so $h' = 0$
- Cells 2, 3: too far in angular distance, $h' = 0$

**Sector $k=2$ (centre $135°$):**
- Cell 2 ($90°$): $h' = 1 - \frac{\vert 135 - 90 \vert}{90} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$
- Cell 1: too far, $h' = 0$

**Sector $k=3$ (centre $225°$):**
- Cell 3 ($200°$): $h' = 1 - \frac{\vert 225 - 200 \vert}{90} = 1 - 0.278 = 0.722$

**Sector $k=4$ (centre $315°$):**
- Cell 4 ($320°$): $h' = 1 - \frac{\vert 315 - 320 \vert}{90} = 1 - 0.056 = 0.944$

### Step 3 — Sum into $H_k$

$$
H_1 = 20 \cdot 0.833 + 0 = 16.67
$$
$$
H_2 = 14.4 \cdot 0.5 = 7.2
$$
$$
H_3 = 25 \cdot 0.722 = 18.06
$$
$$
H_4 = 6.3 \cdot 0.944 = 5.95
$$

### Step 4 — Pick Steering Direction

The threshold is given as $H_{th} = 15$. Sectors 1 and 3 are blocked.

The candidate valleys are sectors $\{2, 4\}$. The angular distance to goal ($0°$):
- Sector 2: $\vert 0° - 135° \vert = 135°$
- Sector 4: $\vert 0° - 315° \vert = 55°$ (using minimum circular distance)

**Steering decision: Sector 4 (closest to goal).** $\blacksquare$
