# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bug Algorithm Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start at S] --> B[Move straight toward G along m-line]
    B --> C{Obstacle detected within d_safe?}
    C -- No --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Record Hit Point H]
    D --> E[Switch to boundary following]
    E --> F{Algorithm?}
    F -- Bug 0 --> G0[Leave at first point with clear line to G]
    F -- Bug 1 --> G1[Complete full perimeter traverse]
    F -- Bug 2 --> G2[Leave at first m-line crossing closer to G]
    G0 --> H{Goal reached?}
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    H -- Yes --> Z[STOP Goal Achieved]
    H -- No --> B
    E -.->|Loop detected| FAIL[FAILURE No Path]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read:** The square boxes are **states**; the diamonds are **decisions**; the labels on the arrows are the conditions. `FAIL` is a dead-end state — the algorithm has returned to $H$ without progress, signalling an unrecoverable situation.

---

## 4.2 VFH Three-Stage Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Range Sensor Scan] --> B[Stage 1: Cartesian Grid Map]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Polar Histogram H_k]
    C --> D[Threshold H_th applied]
    D --> E[Stage 3: Identify candidate valleys]
    E --> F{Valley closer to goal direction?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Select sector as steering direction]
    F -- No --> H[Pick next closest valley]
    H --> G
    G --> I[Output steering angle to motor controller]
    B -.->|Update certainty values| B
```

---

## 4.3 DWA Velocity-Space Search (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Current velocity v_c, w_c] --> B[Compute Search Space V_s]
    A --> C[Compute Dynamic Window V_d]
    A --> D[Read sensor scan]
    D --> E[Compute Admissible Space V_a from clearance]
    B --> F[V_r = V_s intersect V_d intersect V_a]
    C --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Sample N candidates in V_r]
    G --> H[Predict arc trajectory for each candidate]
    H --> I[Score by heading, dist, velocity]
    I --> J[Apply smoothing sigma]
    J --> K[Pick argmax G v w]
    K --> L[Command motors with v_star, w_star]
    L --> A
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mobile Robot Navigation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSING
        S1[Laser / Sonar Scanner]
        S2[Wheel Encoders / IMU]
    end
    subgraph PERCEPTION
        P1[Occupancy Grid Builder]
        P2[Robot Pose Estimator]
    end
    subgraph PLANNING
        G1[Global Planner: A star / Dijkstra]
        G2[Local Planner: DWA / VFH]
        G3[Reactive Layer: Bug Fallback]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL
        C1[Motor Command Generator]
        C2[Wheel Velocity Controller]
    end
    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P2
    P1 --> G2
    P1 --> G3
    P2 --> G1
    P2 --> G2
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> C1
    G3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight — The KTU 2024 framing:** The Bug algorithm is the *lowest* layer in the stack — it is invoked only if the local planner fails. This layered "graceful degradation" is the engineering pattern students must know.

---

## 4.5 Concept Map — How the Three Algorithms Relate

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Obstacle Avoidance))
    Bug Family
      Bug 0
      Bug 1
      Bug 2
      Boundary Following
      m-line Heuristic
    Vector Field Histogram
      Occupancy Grid
      Polar Histogram H_k
      Thresholding
      VFH Plus
      VFH Star
    Dynamic Window Approach
      Search Space V_s
      Dynamic Window V_d
      Admissible Space V_a
      Objective Function G
      Trajectory Rollout
    Common Assumptions
      Point or Circular Robot
      Known Goal Location
      Local Sensing
    Differences
      Dynamics Aware
      Map Aware
      Optimality Guarantees
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**"Differentiate between Bug 1 and Bug 2 algorithms in terms of leave-condition and performance guarantee."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bug 1 | Bug 2 |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Boundary traversal** | Complete traverse of full perimeter | Traverse only until leave condition is met |
| **Leave condition** | At the perimeter point closest to goal $G$ | At first m-line crossing that is closer to $G$ than the hit point |
| **Worst-case path bound** | $L \le 1.5 \cdot L_{opt}$ | $L \le (\pi/2) \cdot L_{opt} \approx 1.57 \cdot L_{opt}$ |
| **Practical performance** | Often over-walks (wastes time) | Often near-optimal in practice |
| **Memory requirement** | Must store all perimeter points | Stores only m-line and current position |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $L_{Bug1} = L_{Bug2}$. This is wrong — Bug 1 has the **tighter worst-case** bound of $1.5 L_{opt}$ in the standard formulation, but in practice Bug 2 is usually better. KTU 2024 valuations specifically mark the difference between *worst-case bound* and *practical performance*.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
**"What is the role of the dynamic window $V_d$ in the DWA algorithm? Why is it called 'dynamic'?"**

**Model Answer:**
- $V_d$ is the set of translational and rotational velocities $(v, \omega)$ that the robot can reach from its current velocity $(v_c, \omega_c)$ within one control cycle $\Delta t$, given its acceleration limits $\dot{v}$ and $\dot{\omega}$.
- Formally: $V_d = [v_c - \dot{v}\Delta t, v_c + \dot{v}\Delta t] \times [\omega_c - \dot{\omega}\Delta t, \omega_c + \dot{\omega}\Delta t]$.
- It is called *dynamic* because the window **shifts with the current velocity** — it is not a fixed region of velocity space. As the robot accelerates, the window moves to higher velocities; as it brakes, the window moves back to lower velocities.
- This guarantees that **every velocity candidate considered is dynamically reachable**, which is the key innovation of DWA over earlier "kinematic-only" planners.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Between TWO Sets)

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **Vector Field Histogram (VFH) algorithm** for obstacle avoidance. Clearly explain the construction of the **polar histogram $H_k$** from the occupancy grid, including the formula for the cell weight $m_{i,j}$ and the angular contribution function $h'_{i,j}(k)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot uses VFH. Given the following four detected cells with certainty values and positions, compute $H_k$ for sectors of width $\alpha = 90°$ centred at $45°, 135°, 225°, 315°$. Constants: $a = 30$, $b = 5$. The goal direction is $\theta_{goal} = 0°$. Threshold $H_{th} = 15$. Determine the steering sector. **(7 marks)**

**Given data:**

| Cell | $d_{i,j}$ (m) | $\beta_{i,j}$ (°) | $C_{i,j}^*$ |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | 2.0 | 30 | 1.0 |
| 2 | 1.5 | 90 | 0.8 |
| 3 | 1.0 | 200 | 1.0 |
| 4 | 2.5 | 320 | 0.6 |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — State the algorithm's purpose (1 mark):**
VFH is a real-time obstacle avoidance method that transforms a 2-D Cartesian occupancy grid into a 1-D polar histogram $H_k$, where each sector $k$ represents a discrete angular range of width $\alpha = 360°/n$. The robot then steers toward the angular valley closest to the goal.

**Step 2 — Three-stage pipeline (2 marks):**
1. **Cartesian grid map:** A local grid $(2c+1) \times (2c+1)$ around the robot stores certainty values $C_{i,j}$ updated from range sensors.
2. **Polar histogram:** Convert the grid into $n$ sectors. For each cell, compute $m_{i,j}$ and distribute its contribution to the three nearest sectors.
3. **Steering decision:** Threshold the histogram to find candidate valleys; pick the valley closest to $\theta_{goal}$.

**Step 3 — Cell weight formula (2 marks):**

$$
m_{i,j} = (C_{i,j}^*)^2 \cdot (a - b \cdot d_{i,j})
$$

where $C_{i,j}^*$ is the certainty value, $d_{i,j}$ is the distance from robot to cell, and $a, b$ are positive constants tuned so that $a - b \cdot d_{i,j} > 0$ in the active window.

**Step 4 — Angular contribution function (2 marks):**

$$
h'_{i,j}(k) =
\begin{cases}
1 - \dfrac{\vert k\alpha - \beta_{i,j} \vert}{\alpha}, & \vert k\alpha - \beta_{i,j} \vert < \alpha \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The sector value is then $H_k = \sum_{i,j} m_{i,j} \cdot h'_{i,j}(k)$.

**Sketch (free-hand, mention):** A polar plot with the robot at the centre, four sectors of $90°$ each, and bars showing $H_k$ magnitudes.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute $m_{i,j}$ for each cell (2 marks):**
- Cell 1: $m_1 = 1.0^2 \cdot (30 - 5 \cdot 2.0) = 20.0$
- Cell 2: $m_2 = 0.8^2 \cdot (30 - 5 \cdot 1.5) = 14.4$
- Cell 3: $m_3 = 1.0^2 \cdot (30 - 5 \cdot 1.0) = 25.0$
- Cell 4: $m_4 = 0.6^2 \cdot (30 - 5 \cdot 2.5) = 6.3$

**Step 2 — Compute $h'(k)$ for each cell-sector pair (2 marks):**
- Cell 1 → Sector 1: $h' = 1 - \frac{\vert 45 - 30 \vert}{90} = 0.833$
- Cell 2 → Sector 2: $h' = 1 - \frac{\vert 135 - 90 \vert}{90} = 0.500$
- Cell 3 → Sector 3: $h' = 1 - \frac{\vert 225 - 200 \vert}{90} = 0.722$
- Cell 4 → Sector 4: $h' = 1 - \frac{\vert 315 - 320 \vert}{90} = 0.944$
- (All other cell-sector pairs are outside the $\pm 90°$ influence window, so $h' = 0$.)

**Step 3 — Sum to obtain $H_k$ (1 mark):**
- $H_1 = 20.0 \times 0.833 = 16.67$
- $H_2 = 14.4 \times 0.500 = 7.20$
- $H_3 = 25.0 \times 0.722 = 18.06$
- $H_4 = 6.3 \times 0.944 = 5.95$

**Step 4 — Apply threshold and select valley (1 mark):**
Threshold $H_{th} = 15$. Sectors with $H_k > 15$ are blocked: Sectors 1 and 3.
- Free candidate valleys: Sectors $\{2, 4\}$.
- Angular distance to goal ($0°$):
  - Sector 2: $\vert 0 - 135 \vert = 135°$
  - Sector 4: $\min(\vert 0 - 315 \vert, 360 - 315) = 45°$
- **Closest free sector = Sector 4 (centred at $315°$).**

**Step 5 — Final steering direction (1 mark):**
The robot should steer to **$315°$ (i.e., slightly to the right of the negative y-axis, which is the upper-right region when facing $+x$ is the goal)**. Equivalently, in normalised angle it is $-45°$ or $315°$ measured from positive x-axis.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
**(a)** Explain the **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** for obstacle avoidance in mobile robots. Derive the **admissible velocity constraint** from kinematic considerations and show how it is incorporated into the search space. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot is at $v_c = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega_c = 0.2$ rad/s. Hardware limits: $v \in [0, 1.0]$ m/s, $\omega \in [-1.0, 1.0]$ rad/s. Acceleration limits: $\dot{v} = 0.5$ m/s², $\dot{\omega} = 1.0$ rad/s². Sampling period $\Delta t = 0.25$ s. The closest obstacle is at clearance $d = 1.2$ m, with braking limits $\dot{v}_b = 1.0$ m/s² and $\dot{\omega}_b = 1.5$ rad/s². Compute $V_s$, $V_d$, and $V_a$. Identify the final search rectangle $V_r$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Concept of DWA (2 marks):**
DWA (Fox, Burgard, Thrun, 1997) is a velocity-space local planner. Instead of searching the full $(v, \omega)$ plane, it restricts the search to velocities **dynamically reachable** in the next control cycle, then scores each candidate using a weighted sum of *heading, distance (clearance), and velocity*.

**Step 2 — The three sub-spaces (2 marks):**
- $V_s$ — bounded by hardware limits: $v \in [v_{min}, v_{max}]$, $\omega \in [\omega_{min}, \omega_{max}]$.
- $V_d$ — bounded by acceleration limits: $v \in [v_c - \dot{v}\Delta t, v_c + \dot{v}\Delta t]$ (and similarly for $\omega$).
- $V_a$ — bounded by safety (must be able to stop before collision).

**Step 3 — Derivation of admissible velocity (2 marks):**
From the kinematic relation $v_f^2 = v^2 - 2\dot{v}_b s$, with $v_f = 0$ at stop:

$$
s = \frac{v^2}{2 \dot{v}_b} \le d \ \Rightarrow\ v \le \sqrt{2 \dot{v}_b d}
$$

Similarly for $\omega$: $\omega \le \sqrt{2 \dot{\omega}_b d}$.

**Step 4 — Search space and objective (1 mark):**
$V_r = V_s \cap V_d \cap V_a$, scored by:

$$
G(v, \omega) = \sigma\left(\alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{dist} + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}\right)
$$

The pair $(v^*, \omega^*)$ that maximises $G$ is the commanded velocity.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute $V_s$ (1 mark):**
- $v \in [0, 1.0]$ m/s
- $\omega \in [-1.0, 1.0]$ rad/s
- $V_s = [0, 1.0] \times [-1.0, 1.0]$ m/s, rad/s.

**Step 2 — Compute $V_d$ (2 marks):**
- $v \in [v_c - \dot{v}\Delta t, v_c + \dot{v}\Delta t] = [0.5 - 0.5 \times 0.25, 0.5 + 0.5 \times 0.25] = [0.375, 0.625]$ m/s
- $\omega \in [\omega_c - \dot{\omega}\Delta t, \omega_c + \dot{\omega}\Delta t] = [0.2 - 1.0 \times 0.25, 0.2 + 1.0 \times 0.25] = [-0.05, 0.45]$ rad/s
- $V_d = [0.375, 0.625] \times [-0.05, 0.45]$.

**Step 3 — Compute $V_a$ (2 marks):**
- $v_{max,safe} = \sqrt{2 \cdot \dot{v}_b \cdot d} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.2} = \sqrt{2.4} \approx 1.549$ m/s
- $\omega_{max,safe} = \sqrt{2 \cdot \dot{\omega}_b \cdot d} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 1.2} = \sqrt{3.6} = 1.897$ rad/s
- $V_a = [0, 1.549] \times [-1.897, 1.897]$ m/s, rad/s.

**Step 4 — Compute $V_r = V_s \cap V_d \cap V_a$ (1 mark):**
- $v$: $\max(0, 0.375) \le v \le \min(1.0, 0.625, 1.549) = 0.625$ → $v \in [0.375, 0.625]$ m/s
- $\omega$: $\max(-1.0, -0.05, -1.897) = -0.05$ and $\min(1.0, 0.45, 1.897) = 0.45$
- $\omega \in [-0.05, 0.45]$ rad/s
- **Final $V_r = [0.375, 0.625] \times [-0.05, 0.45]$ m/s, rad/s.**

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
The dynamic window $V_d$ is the *tightest* bound here, so the search is actually limited by the robot's *acceleration* rather than by hardware limits or by safety. This makes sense: at 0.5 m/s, the robot cannot jump to 1.0 m/s in 0.25 s.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to confuse $\dot{v}$ (the **acceleration** limit used in $V_d$) with $\dot{v}_b$ (the **braking** limit used in $V_a$). These are typically **different values** in a real robot — the braking limit is usually higher than the acceleration limit. Mixing them up loses at least 2 marks in a 14-mark question.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorise the Night Before the Exam**

- [ ] **Bug Algorithm family** assumes a **point robot** with **perfect localisation** and **range sensing**.
- [ ] **Bug 0** = leave at first clear line; **Bug 1** = leave at closest-to-goal point after full perimeter; **Bug 2** = leave at m-line crossing closer to goal.
- [ ] **Bug 1 path bound:** $L \le 1.5 \cdot L_{opt}$ (single convex obstacle). **Bug 2 bound:** $L \le (\pi/2) \cdot L_{opt}$ (worst case).
- [ ] **VFH** transforms a 2-D **Cartesian grid** into a 1-D **polar histogram** $H_k$.
- [ ] **VFH cell weight:** $m_{i,j} = (C_{i,j}^*)^2 (a - b \cdot d_{i,j})$ — **squared** certainty and **linear** distance attenuation.
- [ ] **VFH angular spread:** A single cell influences **three** sectors with linear decay.
- [ ] **VFH decision:** Pick the **candidate valley closest to the goal direction**, NOT the lowest $H_k$ value.
- [ ] **DWA** searches only the **intersection** $V_s \cap V_d \cap V_a$.
- [ ] **DWA admissible velocity:** $v \le \sqrt{2 \dot{v}_b d}$ comes from $v_f^2 = v^2 - 2 \dot{v}_b s$ with $v_f = 0$.
- [ ] **DWA objective:** $G(v, \omega) = \sigma(\alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{dist} + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity})$.
- [ ] **Key distinction:** Bug is *reactive + boundary-following*; VFH is *grid-based + heuristic*; DWA is *dynamics-aware + velocity sampling*.
- [ ] **Engineering reality:** ROS 2 `nav2` default local planner is **DWA**; warehouse AGVs prefer **VFH/VFH+**; **Bug** variants are rare in industry but excellent teaching tools.
- [ ] **Assumption ladder:** Bug = no map, no dynamics; VFH = local map, no dynamics; DWA = local map + dynamics. Each step is more *physically faithful*.
- [ ] **Computational ladder:** Bug ($O(1)$) < VFH ($O(n_{cells})$) < DWA ($O(N_{samples})$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation Architectures - Modularity for Code Reuse and Sharing - Control Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Navigation Architectures - Modularity for Code Reuse and Sharing - Control Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Navigation Architecture — Formal Definition

A **Navigation Architecture** in mobile robotics is a structured, hierarchical framework of hardware and software components that collectively enable a robot to (a) perceive its environment, (b) determine its own pose (position and orientation), (c) plan a path toward a goal, and (d) execute motion commands while continuously updating its internal state. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, a navigation architecture specifies **how sensing, localization, planning, and motion control modules are interconnected** and how information flows between them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4:**
> The navigation architecture is the *backbone* of any autonomous mobile robot. Three pillars define it:
> 1. **Modularity** — independent, swappable software/hardware blocks.
> 2. **Code Reuse & Sharing** — common interfaces enabling cross-platform deployment.
> 3. **Control Localization** — closed-loop estimation and correction of pose.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Brain in a Mall"

Imagine you walk into a huge, unfamiliar shopping mall for the first time:

- **Sensing (Eyes, Ears):** You observe pillars, shops, and floor markings.
- **Localization (Mental Map):** You look at the "You Are Here" board and figure out exactly where you stand.
- **Planning (Thinking):** You mentally trace the shortest route to the food court.
- **Control (Legs):** You start walking, while continuously checking your position against the corridor signs.
- **Modularity:** Your "navigation skill" is a single mental block — you can use it tomorrow in a *different* mall because the *interface* (you → destination) hasn't changed, only the *map data* has.

That "mental block" you carry between malls is exactly what **modular navigation architecture** provides to a robot — a reusable framework, with only the *environment data* changing.

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary in KTU Module 4

| Term | Meaning in this Module |
|---|---|
| **Pose** | The $(x, y, \theta)$ triplet describing a robot's 2D position and heading |
| **Odometry** | Estimation of pose change from wheel encoders |
| **Middleware** | Software layer (e.g., ROS) enabling inter-module communication |
| **Behavior** | A closed-loop control law mapping sensors to actuators |
| **SLAM** | Simultaneous Localization **and** Mapping |
| **Coordinate Frame** | A reference origin + axis convention (e.g., world, robot, sensor) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** Pose in 2D is universally written as $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$, where $x, y$ are in **meters** and $\theta$ is in **radians**. All derivations below assume this convention unless stated otherwise.

---

## 1.4 Three Classical Navigation Architectures

| Architecture | Style | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deliberative (Horizon / Sense–Plan–Act)** | Sequential pipeline | Optimal paths | Slow, brittle to dynamic change |
| **Reactive (Behaviour-Based / Subsumption)** | Tight sensor-actuator loops | Fast, robust | No memory, myopic |
| **Hybrid (Three-Layer / AuRA)** | Combines both | Balanced | Complex software design |

The **modular, hybrid architecture** is the one universally taught in KTU Module 4 because it best demonstrates *code reuse* and *shared localization control*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Functional Pillars of a Modular Navigation System

A KTU-grade modular navigation architecture always contains these **five decoupled blocks**, communicating strictly through well-defined interfaces:

1. **Perception Module** — Raw sensor ingestion (LIDAR, camera, IMU, encoders).
2. **Localization Module** — Pose estimation $\hat{\xi}_t$ from sensor data.
3. **Mapping Module** — Maintains the world model $\mathcal{M}_t$.
4. **Planning Module** — Generates a global path $\mathcal{P}$ from start to goal.
5. **Control Module** — Translates the path into wheel velocities $(v, \omega)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Modularity Matters (KTU 2024 Outcome):** Because each module exposes a clean API, the same *Localization Module* can drive a vacuum robot, a warehouse AGV, or a self-driving car — only the *sensors* and *kinematics* differ.

## 2.2 The Three Laws of Code Reuse in Robotics Software

> [!NOTE]
> These are the *design heuristics* expected in 14-mark answers.

- **Encapsulation:** Each module hides its internal algorithm; e.g., a Kalman filter implementation can be swapped for a particle filter without touching the planner.
- **Abstraction via Interfaces:** Communication occurs over message types (e.g., `geometry_msgs/Pose`, `sensor_msgs/LaserScan`) — not over implementation details.
- **Composability:** Modules can be linked at run-time to form a *behavior* (e.g., "wall-follow" + "goal-seek" = "corridor navigation").

## 2.3 Control Localization — The Closed-Loop Idea

**Control Localization** is the act of using feedback control to *correct* the robot's estimated pose. It is the bridge between **estimation theory** (probabilistic) and **control theory** (deterministic).

The continuous-time feedback loop is:

$$\text{Sensors} \;\xrightarrow{z_t}\; \text{Localizer} \;\xrightarrow{\hat{\xi}_t}\; \text{Controller} \;\xrightarrow{u_t}\; \text{Actuators} \;\xrightarrow{\xi_t}\; \text{World} \;\xrightarrow{z_t}\; \text{Sensors}$$

The controller's job: drive the **innovation** (measurement residual) to zero:

$$\nu_t = z_t - h(\hat{\xi}_{t\mid t-1}) \;\longrightarrow\; 0$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\xi_t = [x_t,\; y_t,\; \theta_t]^T$ | Robot pose vector | m, m, rad |
| 2 | $\begin{bmatrix} x_{t+1} \\ y_{t+1} \\ \theta_{t+1} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} v_t \cos\theta_t \\ v_t \sin\theta_t \\ \omega_t \end{bmatrix} \Delta t$ | Unicycle kinematic update | m, m, rad |
| 3 | $u_t = [v_t,\; \omega_t]^T$ | Control input (linear & angular velocity) | m/s, rad/s |
| 4 | $\hat{\xi}_t = \hat{\xi}_{t\mid t-1} + K_t \,(z_t - h(\hat{\xi}_{t\mid t-1}))$ | Bayes/Kalman correction | m, m, rad |
| 5 | $K_t = P_{t\mid t-1} H^T (H P_{t\mid t-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | dimensionless |
| 6 | $v_c(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID control law (heading or distance) | m/s or rad/s |
| 7 | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \; p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\;\text{bel}(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$ | Bayes filter recursion (MCL foundation) | probability density |
| 8 | $T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | 2D homogeneous transform (world←robot) | homogeneous |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on absolute values in equations:** The KTU Formula Sheet replaces the vertical bar `|` with `\vert` to remain safe inside markdown tables. Example: $\lvert K_t \rvert < 1$ for stability.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Module-4 Concepts |
|---|---|
| **Warehouse Automation (Amazon Kiva)** | Modular AGVs share one ROS-based navigation stack across hundreds of robots |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Hybrid layered architecture with probabilistic localization |
| **Service Robotics** | Behavior-based code reuse across hospital, hotel, and home robots |
| **Agricultural Robotics** | Modular perception → planning → control enabling rapid deployment on different tractors |
| **Space Rovers (NASA)** | Tightly modular navigation to survive 20-min communication delay |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Odometric Pose Update (Unicycle Model)

> **Goal:** Show how wheel-encoder readings $(v, \omega)$ update the robot pose $\xi$.

A differential-drive (unicycle) robot moves along an arc of instantaneous radius:

$$r = \frac{v}{\omega}, \quad \omega \neq 0$$

**Step 1:** In time $\Delta t$, the arc length traversed is $s = v \Delta t$, and the heading change is $\Delta\theta = \omega \Delta t$.

**Step 2:** The center of rotation is offset from the robot by a perpendicular distance $r$ on the left (if $\omega > 0$). The new position is computed by rotating the displacement vector by the half-angle (exact arc geometry):

$$\Delta x = r \left[\sin(\theta + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta\right]$$

$$\Delta y = r \left[\cos\theta - \cos(\theta + \omega\Delta t)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $r = v/\omega$ and simplifying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x &= \frac{v}{\omega} \left[\sin(\theta + \omega\Delta t) - \sin\theta\right] \\
         &= \frac{v}{\omega} \cdot 2\cos\!\left(\theta + \tfrac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right) \\
         &= \frac{2v}{\omega}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\theta + \tfrac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta y &= \frac{v}{\omega}\left[\cos\theta - \cos(\theta + \omega\Delta t)\right] \\
         &= \frac{2v}{\omega}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\theta + \tfrac{\omega\Delta t}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** The exact discrete-time kinematic update becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \frac{2v_t}{\omega_t}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\omega_t \Delta t}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\omega_t \Delta t}{2}\right) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \frac{2v_t}{\omega_t}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\omega_t \Delta t}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\omega_t \Delta t}{2}\right) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \omega_t \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Linearized (small $\omega\Delta t$) Approximation (used in EKF):**
Apply $\sin(\alpha)\approx\alpha$ and $\cos(\alpha)\approx 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &\approx x_t + v_t\cos\theta_t \,\Delta t \\
y_{t+1} &\approx y_t + v_t\sin\theta_t \,\Delta t \\
\theta_{t+1} &\approx \theta_t + \omega_t \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the form listed in the KTU Formula Sheet (row 2).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both forms matter:** The exact form is used in simulators (high fidelity); the linearized form is used inside the Extended Kalman Filter (computational efficiency).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Bayes Filter Posterior for Localization

> **Goal:** Derive the recursive update that any probabilistic localizer (Kalman, EKF, Particle, MCL) implements.

Let $x_t$ denote the true pose, $z_t$ the measurement, and $u_t$ the control.

**Step 1 — Prior (Prediction):** Apply the Chapman–Kolmogorov equation over the Markovian state transition:

$$\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\;\text{bel}(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$$

*This integrates all possible previous poses, weighted by how the control $u_t$ could have moved us.*

**Step 2 — Correction (Measurement Update):** Multiply the prior by the measurement likelihood and normalize:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bel}(x_t) &= \eta \; p(z_t \mid x_t)\; \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) \\
\eta^{-1} &= \int p(z_t \mid x_t)\; \overline{\text{bel}}(x_t)\,dx_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine into a single recursive form (the KTU Bayes Localization Equation):**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{bel}(x_t) \;=\; \eta \; p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\;\text{bel}(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Specialization to Kalman Filter (linear-Gaussian case):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\text{bel}}(x_t) &\;\rightarrow\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_{t\mid t-1},\; P_{t\mid t-1}\right) \\
\mu_{t\mid t-1} &= A\mu_{t-1} + Bu_t \\
P_{t\mid t-1} &= A P_{t-1} A^T + Q \\
K_t &= P_{t\mid t-1} C^T (C P_{t\mid t-1} C^T + R)^{-1} \\
\mu_t &= \mu_{t\mid t-1} + K_t (z_t - C\mu_{t\mid t-1}) \\
P_t &= (I - K_t C) P_{t\mid t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Q$ is process noise covariance, $R$ is measurement noise covariance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability criterion:** The estimator is stable iff the spectral radius $\rho(A - K_t C) < 1$. This is what makes *Control* Localization *stable*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — PID Heading Controller (Feedback in Localization Control)

> **Goal:** Derive the closed-loop control law that drives heading error $e_\theta = \theta_{goal} - \theta_{current}$ to zero.

**Step 1 — Proportional term:** $u_P = K_p e_\theta(t)$

**Step 2 — Integral term (eliminates steady-state error):**
$$u_I = K_i \int_0^{t} e_\theta(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Derivative term (damps oscillations):**
$$u_D = K_d \frac{de_\theta(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Combine to obtain the angular velocity command:**

$$\omega(t) = K_p e_\theta(t) + K_i \int_0^t e_\theta(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de_\theta(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Linear distance controller (for $v$):**
With $e_d = \sqrt{(x_g - x)^2 + (y_g - y)^2} - d_{safe}$,

$$v(t) = K_p^d\, e_d(t) + K_i^d \int_0^t e_d(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d^d \frac{de_d(t)}{dt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ziegler–Nichols Tuning (KTU favourite):** Start with $K_i = K_d = 0$, raise $K_p$ until sustained oscillation, set $K_u$ and $T_u$, then $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $K_i = 2K_p / T_u$, $K_d = K_p T_u / 8$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Theory to Typical KTU 14-Mark Sub-Parts

| Sub-Question Pattern | Required Theory | Required Math | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Explain the modular navigation architecture with a neat block diagram." | 5-pillar model, interface contract | None | Forgetting to label *interfaces* between blocks |
| "Derive the unicycle kinematic update." | Section 3.1 | Exact + linearized forms | Skipping the exact form |
| "Explain Bayes filter-based localization." | Section 3.2 | Recursion + Kalman special case | Confusing *bel* and $\overline{\text{bel}}$ |
| "Design a PID controller for line-following robot." | Section 3.3 | Three-term control law | Forgetting anti-windup on $K_i$ |
| "How does ROS enable code reuse?" | Middleware pattern, pub/sub | None | Not naming a specific message type |
| "Compare reactive vs deliberative architecture." | Section 1.4 table | None | Mixing *control* and *localization* responsibilities |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — High-Level Modular Navigation Architecture (5-Pillar Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sensors: LIDAR, Camera, IMU, Encoders] -->|raw_msg z_t| B[Perception Module]
    B -->|filtered features| C[Localization Module]
    C -->|estimated pose hat_xi_t| E[Planning Module]
    C -->|estimated pose hat_xi_t| F[Control Module]
    D[World Map M_t] --> C
    D --> E
    G[Goal Pose xi_goal] --> E
    E -->|global path P| F
    F -->|velocity command u_t v_omega| H[Actuators: Motors and Wheels]
    H -->|physical motion| I[Physical World]
    I -->|sensor feedback| A
    C -.->|map update| D
    style A fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> **Reading guide:** The **green Localization Module** is the heart of *Control Localization*. The **pink Control Module** closes the loop with the **yellow Sensors** through the **world**.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Modularity & Code Reuse Pattern (Component Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COMMON[SHARED MIDDLEWARE: ROS or equivalent]
        MSG1[Pose Message]
        MSG2[LaserScan Message]
        MSG3[Twist Message]
    end
    subgraph ROBOT1[ROBOT A: Differential Drive]
        A1[Localization A]
        A2[Planner A]
        A3[Controller A]
    end
    subgraph ROBOT2[ROBOT B: Ackermann Steer]
        B1[Localization B]
        B2[Planner B]
        B3[Controller B]
    end
    A1 --- MSG1
    A2 --- MSG1
    A3 --- MSG3
    B1 --- MSG1
    B2 --- MSG1
    B3 --- MSG3
    MSG2 --- A1
    MSG2 --- B1
    style COMMON fill:#118ab2,color:#fff
    style ROBOT1 fill:#073b4c,color:#fff
    style ROBOT2 fill:#073b4c,color:#fff
```

> **Reading guide:** The **blue middleware** block is the *code-reuse contract*. Robots A and B inherit *Localization* and *Planner* logic untouched; only the *Controller* is rewritten because their kinematic models differ. This is the **core argument for modularity**.

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Control Localization Feedback Loop (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize --> Predict: set hat_xi_0, P_0
    Predict --> Update: motion model u_t
    Update --> Innovate: compute z_t minus predicted
    Innovate --> Correct: compute Kalman Gain K_t
    Correct --> Control: correct pose hat_xi_t
    Control --> Actuate: emit v_omega
    Actuate --> Predict: wait for next sensor tick
    Innovate --> Reject: innovation above threshold
    Reject --> Predict: skip update
    Correct --> [*]: goal reached
```

> **Reading guide:** The `Innovate` → `Reject` branch is the *outlier-rejection* step that makes the localizer **robust** to sensor glitches (e.g., a LIDAR reflection from glass). Examiners love this branch in 14-mark answers.

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Block-Level Functional Architecture (When Physical Drawings Are Impractical)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Layer 0: Hardware Abstraction HAL] --> L1[Layer 1: Drivers]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2: Sensor Fusion and Localization]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Path Planning]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Behavior Arbitration]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Motion Control]
    L5 --> L0
    L0 --> L6[Layer 6: Safety Monitor Watchdog]
    L6 --> L5
    style L0 fill:#f1c40f
    style L2 fill:#2ecc71
    style L4 fill:#e74c3c
    style L6 fill:#9b59b6,color:#fff
```

> **Reading guide:** This is the **AuRA (Autonomous Robot Architecture)** layered model. *Control Localization* sits at Layer 2. The **purple Watchdog (Layer 6)** is a non-functional cross-cutting concern — a hallmark of mature modular design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define **navigation architecture** and list its three classical paradigms. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> A *navigation architecture* is the structured software/hardware framework that integrates perception, localization, mapping, planning, and motion control of a mobile robot.
>
> The three classical paradigms are:
> 1. **Deliberative (Sense–Plan–Act)** — heavy planning, slow but globally optimal.
> 2. **Reactive (Behaviour-based / Subsumption)** — direct sensor-to-actuator loops, fast but memoryless.
> 3. **Hybrid (Three-Layer)** — combines deliberative global planning with reactive local control; the de-facto standard in modern robots.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
What is meant by **code reuse** in the context of a modular robotic system? Give one example. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> *Code reuse* means designing software components so that the same module can be deployed across multiple robots or applications without modification — only the configuration and message types differ.
>
> **Example:** A ROS-based Monte-Carlo Localization node can be used unchanged on a TurtleBot (differential drive), a Husky (4-wheel skid-steer), and a simulated Jackal, because the node subscribes to the abstract `sensor_msgs/LaserScan` topic and publishes the abstract `geometry_msgs/PoseWithCovariance` — both platform-agnostic.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - ✗ Drawing the *block diagram* without labelling **interfaces / message names** between modules ⇒ lose **2 marks**.
> - ✗ Writing the *Bayes filter* equation but failing to distinguish **bel** (posterior) from $\overline{\text{bel}}$ (prior) ⇒ lose **1 mark**.
> - ✗ In PID derivations, omitting the **anti-windup clause** for $K_i$ ⇒ lose **1 mark**.
> - ✗ Forgetting to mention **coordinate-frame transformation** when switching between robot and world frames ⇒ lose **1 mark**.
> - ✗ Stating the formula $\omega = v/r$ without defining **the sign convention** for left/right turns ⇒ lose **1 mark**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-pillar modular navigation architecture** used in modern mobile robots. Discuss how **modularity enables code reuse and sharing** with a suitable example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **discrete-time unicycle kinematic update** for a differential-drive robot, starting from the geometric arc model. Show both the *exact* and the *linearized* forms. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Diagram** (draw the same as Diagram A in Section 4.1). Label every interface.
2. **The Five Pillars** with one-line purpose each:
   - **Perception** — converts raw sensor data into features.
   - **Localization** — estimates pose $\hat{\xi}_t$ (the green block).
   - **Mapping** — maintains and updates the world model $\mathcal{M}_t$.
   - **Planning** — generates the global path $\mathcal{P}$ from $\xi_{start}$ to $\xi_{goal}$.
   - **Control** — converts path into $(v, \omega)$ commands.

3. **How Modularity enables Code Reuse:**
   - Each module exposes an **abstract interface** (e.g., `Pose`, `Twist`).
   - Implementations are swappable: a Kalman filter can be replaced by an MCL filter by editing only the *Localization Module* internals.
   - **Example (write this!):** A warehouse uses the *same* `move_base` ROS package on:
     - a TurtleBot (uses `dwa_local_planner`),
     - a Husky (uses `teb_local_planner`).
     Both share the global planner and the cost-map modules — only the local controller differs.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Exact form derivation** (follow Section 3.1, Steps 1–4):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \frac{2v_t}{\omega_t}\sin\!\left(\frac{\omega_t \Delta t}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\omega_t \Delta t}{2}\right) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \frac{2v_t}{\omega_t}\sin\!\left(\frac{\omega_t \Delta t}{2}\right)\sin\!\left(\theta_t + \frac{\omega_t \Delta t}{2}\right) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \omega_t \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Linearized form** (Step 5):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &\approx x_t + v_t \cos\theta_t \,\Delta t \\
y_{t+1} &\approx y_t + v_t \sin\theta_t \,\Delta t \\
\theta_{t+1} &\approx \theta_t + \omega_t \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion to write:** The exact form is preferred for *simulators*; the linearized form is mandatory inside the EKF Jacobian.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Bayes filter-based probabilistic localization** framework. Derive the recursive update equation and show how the **Kalman filter** is a special case under linear-Gaussian assumptions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a **PID controller** for a differential-drive robot to track a desired heading $\theta_{goal}$. Derive the control law and discuss tuning using the **Ziegler–Nichols** method. Add a note on **anti-windup**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Bayes filter derivation** (follow Section 3.2, Steps 1–3):

$$
\boxed{\;\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta\; p(z_t \mid x_t)\int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\,\text{bel}(x_{t-1})\,dx_{t-1}\;}
$$

2. **Two stages explained:**
   - **Prediction** (motion update) integrates over the previous belief.
   - **Correction** (measurement update) re-weights by the sensor likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t)$.

3. **Kalman specialization** (Section 3.2, Step 4) — write the five equations:
   - $\mu_{t\mid t-1} = A\mu_{t-1} + Bu_t$
   - $P_{t\mid t-1} = AP_{t-1}A^T + Q$
   - $K_t = P_{t\mid t-1}C^T(CP_{t\mid t-1}C^T + R)^{-1}$
   - $\mu_t = \mu_{t\mid t-1} + K_t(z_t - C\mu_{t\mid t-1})$
   - $P_t = (I - K_tC)P_{t\mid t-1}$

4. **Note to add:** For non-linear systems, replace $A$ and $C$ with their Jacobians $F_x$ and $H_x$ to obtain the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. **Error definition:** $e_\theta(t) = \theta_{goal} - \theta_{current}(t)$.

2. **Control law derivation** (Section 3.3, Steps 1–4):

$$\omega(t) = K_p e_\theta(t) + K_i \int_0^t e_\theta(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de_\theta(t)}{dt}$$

3. **Ziegler–Nichols tuning steps (write all 4):**
   - Set $K_i = K_d = 0$.
   - Increase $K_p$ until sustained oscillation; record the *ultimate gain* $K_u$ and *oscillation period* $T_u$.
   - Set $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $K_i = 2K_p/T_u$, $K_d = K_p T_u/8$.
   - Fine-tune for desired overshoot and settling time.

4. **Anti-windup note (MANDATORY for full marks):**
   When the actuator saturates, the integrator keeps accumulating error → *integrator wind-up*. Add a **back-calculation** term:

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = e_\theta(t) + \frac{1}{T_t}\bigl(\omega_{sat} - \omega\bigr)$$

where $T_t$ is the tracking time constant (typically $\sqrt{T_i T_d}$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 4):**

- [x] A **navigation architecture** integrates five decoupled modules: Perception, Localization, Mapping, Planning, Control.
- [x] The three classical paradigms are **Deliberative, Reactive, Hybrid**.
- [x] **Modularity** = encapsulation + interface abstraction + composability.
- [x] **Code reuse** in robotics is enabled by *middleware* (e.g., ROS topics, services, actions) that decouples modules.
- [x] The **robot pose** is $\xi = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — never forget the $\theta$.
- [x] **Unicycle exact update** uses the half-angle form; **linearized update** uses $v\cos\theta\,\Delta t$.
- [x] **Bayes filter** is the *universal* localization recursion; Kalman and Particle are special cases.
- [x] **Kalman Gain** $K_t$ trades process noise $Q$ against measurement noise $R$.
- [x] **PID control** needs $K_p, K_i, K_d$; always include **anti-windup**.
- [x] **Ziegler–Nichols** is the expected tuning method in KTU answers.
- [x] **Stability** of EKF requires $\rho(A - K_t C) < 1$.
- [x] Always draw **interfaces** between modules in block diagrams — examiners *will* deduct marks otherwise.
- [x] **2D homogeneous transform** $T \in SE(2)$ is the bridge between robot frame and world frame.
- [x] **Watchdog / Safety Monitor** is a non-functional cross-cutting layer in mature modular designs.
- [x] The same localization node can be reused across robots — that is the **single strongest argument** for modularity.

---

> **End of PECDT633 — Module 4 Notes: Navigation Architectures, Modularity for Code Reuse & Sharing, Control Localization.**

topics.title: Techniques for Decomposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Decomposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robotics and Automation**, *Techniques for Decomposition* refer to the systematic methodologies used to break down a high-level, complex robotic mission or application into a structured hierarchy of smaller, manageable, and executable sub-tasks, sub-functions, and primitive actions. The process is a foundational principle of **Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA)** and **Task-Level Programming (TLP)**, enabling controllers, AI planners, and human operators to translate abstract goals (e.g., "assemble a gearbox") into concrete sequences of actuator commands.

The fundamental objective is to convert a monolithic specification into a **Decomposition Tree** (or **AND/OR Task Graph**) where each node represents an operation with defined preconditions, postconditions, and resource requirements. This is a direct implementation of the **"Divide and Conquer"** paradigm applied to cyber-physical systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Decomposition techniques bridge the gap between *high-level AI planning* and *low-level servo control*. Mastery of this topic is mandatory for Part B (14-mark) questions in Module 4.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Cooking Recipe" Model)

Imagine you are teaching a robot to make a cup of tea. A naïve approach would be to issue a single mega-instruction: *"MakeTea()"* — which is impossible to execute because the robot does not know what tea *is* in operational terms.

**Decomposition solves this** by dissecting the goal exactly like writing a recipe:

1. **Mission (Top Level):** Prepare one cup of masala tea.
2. **Sub-Tasks (Mid Level):** Boil water, add tea leaves, add sugar, add milk, strain into cup, serve.
3. **Primitive Actions (Bottom Level):** `MoveArmTo(Kettle)`, `Gripper.Close()`, `MoveArmTo(Stove)`, `SwitchRelay(Stove, ON)`, `WaitForTemperature(100°C)`, `Pour(quantity=200ml)`.

Each "Primitive Action" is a single, atomic command that the robot's low-level controller can execute without ambiguity. This bottom-up compositional logic is the essence of all decomposition techniques.

### 1.3 Standardized Terminology & Metrics

- **Atomic Action (Primitive):** The smallest indivisible unit of robot behavior, typically requiring **≤ 1 second** of execution time and operating on a single degree of freedom or end-effector.
- **Task Frame:** A Cartesian coordinate system attached to a workpiece, used to localize decomposed sub-tasks.
- **Degree of Decomposition ($D_d$):** A conceptual metric indicating the depth of the task tree, calculated as:
  
  $$D_d = \text{Number of Levels in the Task Hierarchy} - 1$$

- **Reusability Index ($R_i$):** The fraction of sub-tasks that can be reused across different parent missions. High $R_i$ indicates a well-decomposed system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant Reminder:** Effective decomposition reduces computational complexity. A problem of complexity $O(n^2)$ decomposed into $k$ sub-problems of size $n/k$ drops to approximately $O(n^2/k)$ when solved in parallel.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Decomposition

The core philosophy of decomposition rests on three engineering pillars:

1. **Cognitive Simplification:** Humans cannot reliably program or verify monolithic code. Breaking a mission into 5–7 sub-tasks aligns with **Miller's Law (1956)** which states that working memory holds roughly $7 \pm 2$ chunks of information.
2. **Modularity & Reusability:** A well-decomposed sub-task (e.g., "PickObject") can be reused in hundreds of parent missions. This is the **DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)** principle applied to robotics.
3. **Fault Isolation & Debugging:** When a weld-mission fails, decomposition allows pinpointing the exact sub-task (e.g., "LowerElectrode" or "TriggerArc") that caused the failure, instead of debugging a 10,000-line monolithic program.

### 2.2 The Operational Logic Flow

Every decomposition, regardless of technique, follows a universal four-stage logical pipeline:

- **Stage 1 – Goal Formalization:** Convert the user mission into a formal objective $G$ with measurable success criteria (e.g., "Bolt Torqued to 25 Nm ± 0.5 Nm").
- **Stage 2 – Topological Splitting:** Apply one or more decomposition strategies to split $G$ into $\{g_1, g_2, \dots, g_n\}$.
- **Stage 3 – Primitive Mapping:** Map each $g_i$ to a library of robot primitives $\Pi = \{\pi_1, \pi_2, \dots, \pi_m\}$.
- **Stage 4 – Sequencing & Synchronization:** Define the partial order $\prec$ (precedence relations) among $\{g_i\}$ and synchronize via shared resources (common memory, interlocks, Petri nets).

### 2.3 The Seven Canonical Techniques for Decomposition

Below is the high-yield taxonomy that KTU examiners expect students to enumerate and differentiate:

| # | Technique | Splitting Criterion | Example (Welding Task) | KTU Examiner Cue |
|---|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Hierarchical (Top-Down)** | Levels of abstraction: Mission → Task → Action → Primitive | Mission→"WeldChassis" → Task→"WeldJoint1" → Action→"TraceSeam" → Primitive→"MoveL(p1,p2)" | Most common in 14-mark answers |
| 2 | **Functional Decomposition** | Robot subsystem function: Perception / Planning / Action | "Sense Seam" → "Plan Path" → "Execute Weld" | Pair with Sense-Plan-Act paradigm |
| 3 | **Temporal (Sequential/Parallel)** | Time-based ordering using AND/OR nodes | Parallel: SenseBothJoints; Sequential: Weld1 *then* Weld2 | Use Petri Nets or precedence graphs |
| 4 | **Spatial (Geometric)** | Subdivision of the Cartesian workspace into sub-regions (cells) | Workspace divided into 4 quadrants, each handled by a sub-routine | Common in mobile robots, AGVs |
| 5 | **Object-Oriented (Resource-Based)** | Decomposition based on the manipulated object | "GraspBracket" → "InsertBracket" → "ReleaseBracket" | Aligns with OOP in robot software |
| 6 | **Behavior-Based (Reactive)** | Decomposition into stimulus-response pairs (behaviors) | "ObstacleAvoidance" + "GoalSeeking" + "WeldBehavior" | Used in behavior-based robotics (Brooks) |
| 7 | **Primitive-Based (Skill-Based)** | Decomposition into reusable motion skills | Skills: "Move-To", "Grasp", "Insert", "Screw" | Foundation of ROS MoveIt, industrial offline programming |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Examiners *do not* accept "Task Decomposition" as a single answer. You must name a **specific technique**, justify *why* it is chosen for the given application, and provide a **worked example**. Memorizing all 7 names is non-negotiable for full marks.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table consolidates every quantitative expression you may be required to write in a KTU 2024 examination for this topic. Note the use of `\vert` in place of literal pipes to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Constraints | Engineering Utility |
|---------|-------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Degree of Decomposition | $D_d = L - 1$ where $L$ = total levels in task tree | Dimensionless integer $\geq 0$ | Quantifies how "deep" a hierarchy goes |
| Reusability Index | $R_i = \dfrac{N_{\text{reused sub-tasks}}}{N_{\text{total sub-tasks}}}$ | $0 \leq R_i \leq 1$ | Measures modularity of design |
| Sequential Execution Time | $T_{\text{seq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | Seconds | Time for purely serial sub-tasks |
| Parallel Execution Time | $T_{\text{par}} = \max_{i \in P} \left( t_i \right)$ | Seconds | Lower bound using critical path |
| Speedup Factor (Amdahl) | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $k$ = parallel processors | Justifies parallel decomposition |
| Task Complexity (Big-O after decomposition) | $T(n) = a T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$ | Master Theorem case | Validates decomposition efficiency |
| Primitive Action Count | $P_c = \vert \Pi \vert$ | Cardinality of primitive library | Sizing the skill library |
| Fault Localization Gain | $G_{\text{fault}} = \log_2(D_d + 1)$ | Bits of diagnostic information | How much decomposition helps debugging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** When a KTU Part B question asks *"Justify the choice of decomposition technique"*, citing the **Speedup Factor** or **Reusability Index** instantly elevates your answer from a 7-mark to a 14-mark response.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Spatial + Hierarchical decomposition enables 6-axis robots to share a workspace without collision (e.g., BMW, Tesla body shops).
- **Warehouse Robotics (Amazon Kiva):** Behavior-based decomposition of "Pod Retrieval" into "Navigate", "Lift", "Transport", "Deposit" primitives.
- **Surgical Robotics (Da Vinci):** Functional decomposition separates surgeon-intent perception from slave-arm actuation for safety.
- **Mars Rovers (NASA Perseverance):** Autonomous task decomposition via the **MEXEC** planner, which splits a sol's science goals into executable commands.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough: Building a Hierarchical Decomposition Tree

Consider a high-level mission: **"Palletize a Box on a Conveyor"**. We will derive the full hierarchical decomposition step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Mission Specification ($G$):**

$$G = \text{PlaceBoxAt}(x_b, y_b, z_b, \text{Conveyor}_A)$$

The goal $G$ has a measurable success condition: the box must be within a positional tolerance of $\pm 2 \text{ mm}$ and orientation tolerance of $\pm 1^\circ$.

**Step 2 — First-Level Decomposition (Task Level):**

The mission is split into three high-level sub-tasks based on the **Object-Oriented** criterion:

$$G \rightarrow \{G_1, G_2, G_3\}$$

where:

$$G_1 = \text{FetchBoxFrom}(\text{Pallet}_B)$$
$$G_2 = \text{TransportBox}(P_B \rightarrow \text{Conveyor}_A)$$
$$G_3 = \text{PlaceBoxOn}(\text{Conveyor}_A)$$

**Step 3 — Second-Level Decomposition (Action Level):**

Each task is further split using the **Functional Decomposition** criterion (Sense → Plan → Act):

$$\begin{aligned}
G_1 &\rightarrow \{S_1, P_1, A_1\} \\
    &= \{\text{LocateBox}, \text{PlanGrasp}, \text{ExecuteGrasp}\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Third-Level Decomposition (Primitive Level):**

Each action is finally mapped to atomic primitives from the library $\Pi$:

$$A_1 = \text{ExecuteGrasp} \rightarrow \{\pi_1, \pi_2, \pi_3, \pi_4\}$$

where:

$$\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &= \text{MoveJ}(q_{\text{home}}, q_{\text{approach}}) \\
\pi_2 &= \text{MoveL}(q_{\text{approach}}, q_{\text{pre-grasp}) \\
\pi_3 &= \text{Gripper.Close}(\text{Force} = 30 \text{ N}) \\
\pi_4 &= \text{MoveL}(q_{\text{grasp}}, q_{\text{retreat})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Partial Order Definition:**

The total precedence relation is:

$$\pi_1 \prec \pi_2 \prec \pi_3 \prec \pi_4 \prec S_2 \prec P_2 \prec A_2 \prec \dots$$

where $\prec$ denotes "must complete before."

**Step 6 — Quantitative Validation:**

The total sequential execution time is:

$$T_{\text{seq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{12} t_i = 2.5 + 1.2 + 0.8 + 1.0 + 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.8 + 1.5 + 1.0 + 0.8 + 1.2 + 0.5 = 12.1 \text{ s}$$

If three sub-tasks $(G_1, G_2, G_3)$ can be parallelized (e.g., perception running while robot moves), the critical path time is:

$$T_{\text{par}} = \max(t_{G_1}, t_{G_2}, t_{G_3}) = \max(3.2, 4.5, 4.4) = 4.5 \text{ s}$$

The achieved speedup is therefore:

$$S = \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{T_{\text{par}}} = \frac{12.1}{4.5} \approx 2.69$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For Exam Use)

Use this table in your KTU answer to compare two techniques side-by-side, which examiners love to see:

| Comparison Axis | Hierarchical Decomposition | Behavior-Based Decomposition |
|------------------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| Decomposition Driver | Levels of abstraction | Stimulus-response pairs |
| Representation | AND/OR Task Tree | Subsumption Architecture |
| Planning Required | Heavy (offline + online) | Minimal (reactive) |
| Optimal For | Structured industrial cells | Dynamic, unknown environments |
| Real-Time Re-planning | Slow, replanning full tree | Instant, layer arbitration |
| Cognitive Load on Programmer | High (full upfront modeling) | Low (add behaviors incrementally) |
| Predictability | Highly deterministic | Probabilistic, emergent |
| Failure Recovery | Backtrack to last successful node | Switch to higher-priority behavior |
| Example Domain | Automotive welding | Mars rover navigation |
| KTU Tag Frequency | 80% of past papers | 20% of past papers |

---

### 3.3 Master Theorem Application (For Speedup Justification)

When a robot's task planner recursively decomposes a workspace of size $n$ into $b$ sub-regions and performs local planning of size $f(n)$, the total planning time satisfies:

$$T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

Applying the **Master Theorem** to determine asymptotic complexity:

- **Case 1:** If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
- **Case 2:** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log n)$.
- **Case 3:** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$, then $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Let $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^2$. Then:

$$\log_b a = \log_2 4 = 2$$

Since $f(n) = n^2 = \Theta(n^2) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$, we are in **Case 2**, and:

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^2 \log n)$$

This derivation proves that **spatial decomposition** of a path-planning problem reduces complexity from a naive $O(n^3)$ to a far more efficient $O(n^2 \log n)$, which is a high-yield 14-mark answer ender.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Decomposition Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Level Mission G] --> B[Goal Formalization Module]
    B --> C[Decomposition Strategy Selector]
    C --> D1[Hierarchical]
    C --> D2[Functional]
    C --> D3[Temporal]
    C --> D4[Spatial]
    C --> D5[Object Oriented]
    C --> D6[Behavior Based]
    C --> D7[Primitive Based]
    D1 --> E[Task Tree Generator]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E
    E --> F[Primitive Mapping Library Pi]
    F --> G[Sequencer and Synchronizer]
    G --> H[Robot Controller Low Level]
    H --> I[Feedback and Fault Handler]
    I --> C
```

### 4.2 Canonical Hierarchical Decomposition Tree (Box Palletizing Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Mission Place Box on Conveyor] --> T1[Task 1 Fetch Box]
    M --> T2[Task 2 Transport Box]
    M --> T3[Task 3 Place Box]
    T1 --> A1[Action 1a Locate]
    T1 --> A2[Action 1b Plan Grasp]
    T1 --> A3[Action 1c Execute Grasp]
    A1 --> P1[Primitive MoveJ home to scan]
    A1 --> P2[Primitive SenseObject vision]
    A2 --> P3[Primitive ComputeGraspPose]
    A3 --> P4[Primitive MoveL to pre grasp]
    A3 --> P5[Primitive Gripper Close 30N]
    A2 --> P6[Primitive MoveL to grasp]
    A3 --> P7[Primitive MoveL to retreat]
    T2 --> A4[Action 2a Navigate Path]
    T2 --> A5[Action 2b Avoid Collisions]
    A4 --> P8[Primitive GlobalPlanner A star]
    A4 --> P9[Primitive LocalPlanner DWA]
    A5 --> P10[Primitive EmergencyStop]
    T3 --> A6[Action 3a Align Pose]
    T3 --> A7[Action 3b Release Object]
    A6 --> P11[Primitive IK Solve target pose]
    A7 --> P12[Primitive Gripper Open]
```

### 4.3 Behavior-Based Subsumption Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L3[Layer 3 Mission Planner Highest Priority] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2 Object Manipulation] --> L1
    L1[Layer 1 Obstacle Avoidance Lowest Priority]
    S1[Stimulus Goal Reached] --> L3
    S2[Stimulus Object Detected] --> L2
    S3[Stimulus Collision Imminent] --> L1
    L1 --> OUT[Motor Command Arbitration]
    L2 --> OUT
    L3 --> OUT
    OUT --> ACT[Robot Actuators]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart for Selecting a Decomposition Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the environment static and known?} -->|Yes| Q2{Is the task highly repetitive industrial?}
    Q1 -->|No dynamic| R1[Use Behavior Based Decomposition]
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Use Hierarchical plus Functional Decomposition]
    Q2 -->|No mixed| Q3{Are there multiple robots or parallel resources?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Use Temporal plus Spatial Decomposition]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is the focus on the manipulated object or skill reuse?}
    Q4 -->|Object| R4[Use Object Oriented Decomposition]
    Q4 -->|Skill| R5[Use Primitive Based Skill Decomposition]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and all labels are pure text without embedded formatting — fully compliant with Mermaid v10+ rendering rules.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define *Task Decomposition* in the context of robotics. Mention any two advantages of decomposing a complex robotic task.

**Model Answer:**
Task decomposition is the process of breaking a high-level robotic mission into a hierarchy of smaller, well-defined sub-tasks and atomic primitives that can be executed and verified independently.
*Advantages:* (i) Reduces programming complexity by limiting cognitive load to $7 \pm 2$ sub-tasks per level. (ii) Enables reuse of primitives across multiple missions, increasing modularity and reducing development time. *(Any two valid points, 1.5 marks each)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **Hierarchical Decomposition** and **Behavior-Based Decomposition** based on representation, planning requirement, and suitable application.

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Hierarchical | Behavior-Based |
|-------|--------------|----------------|
| Representation | AND/OR Task Tree | Subsumption layers |
| Planning | Heavy, offline + online | Minimal, reactive |
| Application | Static industrial cells | Dynamic unknown environments |

*(1 mark per correct row, 3 marks total)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Naming without justification** = maximum 7/14. Always state *why* a technique is chosen.
> 2. **Skipping the primitive level** = loss of 3 marks. Examiners explicitly check whether you map sub-tasks to atomic actions.
> 3. **No diagram = 2-mark penalty.** Always include an AND/OR task tree or Mermaid-style hierarchy.
> 4. **Confusing "Functional" with "Hierarchical"** is the most common blunder. Remember: Functional = Sense-Plan-Act axis; Hierarchical = Mission-Task-Action-Primitive axis.

---

#### **Part B – Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Hierarchical Decomposition** technique in robotics. Illustrate the decomposition of a mission *"Pick and Place a Component on a PCB"* using a clear task tree diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss any **four other techniques of decomposition** (Functional, Temporal, Spatial, Object-Oriented) with one industrial example each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Hierarchical decomposition structures a robotic mission into a strict top-down tree of four canonical levels:

1. **Mission Level** — the overall user objective.
2. **Task Level** — major logical sub-goals.
3. **Action Level** — concrete sub-functions.
4. **Primitive Level** — atomic, controller-executable commands.

For *"Pick and Place a Component on a PCB"*:

- **Mission:** MountResistor(R1) on PCB.
- **Tasks:** LocateFeeder → PickResistor → LocatePad → PlaceResistor.
- **Actions (per task):** Vision → Plan → Execute.
- **Primitives:** MoveJ, MoveL, GripperOpen, GripperClose, IKSolver, etc.

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**

```
Mission: MountResistor
 ├── Task 1: LocateFeeder
 │     ├── Action 1a: Vision
 │     │     └── Primitive: CaptureImage
 │     ├── Action 1b: Plan
 │     │     └── Primitive: ComputeFeederPose
 │     └── Action 1c: Move
 │           └── Primitive: MoveJ(q_home, q_feeder)
 ├── Task 2: PickResistor
 │     ├── Primitive: MoveL(approach)
 │     ├── Primitive: GripperClose(F=20N)
 │     └── Primitive: MoveL(retreat)
 ├── Task 3: LocatePad
 │     └── (similar Vision → Plan primitives)
 └── Task 4: PlaceResistor
       ├── Primitive: MoveL(pad_approach)
       ├── Primitive: PlaceCompliance(Z=0.1mm)
       └── Primitive: GripperOpen
```
*(4 marks for tree, 3 marks for explanation of levels)*

**(b)** Four additional techniques:

- **Functional Decomposition** — Splits the robot system by *function*: Perception (cameras, force sensors), Planning (IK solver, path planner), and Action (motor drivers). *Example:* In a CNC mill-tending cell, vision identifies raw stock, planner computes tool path, actuators execute the mill.
- **Temporal Decomposition** — Splits by *time* using AND/OR precedence. Sub-tasks may run sequentially (T1 ≺ T2) or in parallel (T1 ∧ T2). *Example:* Dual-arm assembly where one arm holds a bracket while the other inserts a screw.
- **Spatial Decomposition** — Divides the Cartesian workspace into geometric sub-regions (quadrants or voxels). *Example:* AGV fleet in a warehouse where the floor is divided into a grid; each cell hosts a sub-controller.
- **Object-Oriented Decomposition** — Centers decomposition on the *manipulated object*. Each object has associated methods (Grasp, Inspect, Place). *Example:* In a bin-picking cell, the "Bolt" object class encapsulates all bolt-specific primitives.

*(1.75 marks per technique: 1 for definition + 0.75 for example)*

---

#### **Part B – Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a task-level robot programming system**, clearly identifying where decomposition occurs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-DOF robot must perform an *arc-welding* task on a chassis. Apply **three suitable decomposition techniques** to break this mission into executable primitives, and justify your selection. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** A task-level programming system contains the following layered architecture (top to bottom):

1. **Mission Specification Layer** — accepts the human-readable goal.
2. **Task Planner / Decomposition Engine** — applies selected decomposition techniques to generate an AND/OR task tree.
3. **Primitive Library** — repository of atomic actions (MoveL, Grip, Sense).
4. **Sequencer** — defines the partial order among sub-tasks and handles resource locks.
5. **Controller / Servo Layer** — executes primitives in real time.
6. **Feedback / World Model** — updates the world state and triggers re-decomposition if faults occur.

*Decomposition specifically occurs at Layer 2.* *(4 marks for diagram, 3 marks for explanation)*

**(b)** Three suitable decomposition techniques for arc-welding:

1. **Hierarchical (Top-Down) Decomposition** — Splits the mission into "WeldSeam1, WeldSeam2, …" and further into "TraceStart → MaintainArc → TracePath → ExtinguishArc." *Justification:* Welding follows a strict sequential script; hierarchical trees provide predictability and ease of certification.
2. **Spatial Decomposition** — The chassis is divided into 4 quadrants; the robot welds one quadrant at a time to avoid cable entanglement and joint overheating. *Justification:* Spatial locality reduces joint thermal stress and simplifies collision checking.
3. **Functional Decomposition** — Separates *Seam Tracking* (vision sensor) from *Arc Control* (current regulator) from *Robot Motion* (path planner). *Justification:* Decoupling functions allows independent tuning of weld current versus tracking accuracy.

*(2 marks technique + 0.33 marks justification × 3, plus 1 mark for primitive list)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Decomposition = breaking a high-level mission into a structured hierarchy of sub-tasks and atomic primitives.
- **Four Canonical Levels:** Mission → Task → Action → Primitive.
- **Seven Techniques to Memorize:** Hierarchical, Functional, Temporal, Spatial, Object-Oriented, Behavior-Based, Primitive-Based.
- **Key Formulas:**
  - $D_d = L - 1$ (degree of decomposition)
  - $R_i = N_{\text{reused}} / N_{\text{total}}$ (reusability index)
  - $T_{\text{par}} = \max(t_i)$ (parallel execution time)
  - $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/k]$ (Amdahl's speedup)
- **Representation Tools:** AND/OR task trees, Petri nets, Gantt charts, subsumption layers.
- **Selection Rule of Thumb:**
  - Static, known, industrial → Hierarchical + Functional.
  - Dynamic, unknown → Behavior-Based.
  - Multi-robot, parallel → Temporal + Spatial.
  - Object-centric → Object-Oriented.
  - Skill-reuse focused → Primitive-Based.
- **Exam Day Checklist:**
  - Always name the technique.
  - Always justify the choice.
  - Always include a tree or block diagram.
  - Always map at least one sub-task down to the primitive level.
  - Always mention reusability, modularity, or fault-isolation as the benefit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alternatives for Navigation - Neural Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT633 - BASICS OF ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Alternatives for Navigation - Neural Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU Module 4 (Robot Navigation and Path Planning), **Neural Networks** represent a class of bio-inspired, connectionist computational models that learn complex, non-linear mappings directly from raw sensor data to control commands or navigational decisions, thereby serving as a powerful alternative to classical algorithmic approaches (such as A*, Dijkstra, potential field methods, and Bug algorithms) for autonomous robot navigation.

Mathematically, a neural network $\mathcal{N}$ is a parameterized function:

$$\mathcal{N}_{\theta} : \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{m}$$

where $\theta = \{W^{(l)}, b^{(l)}\}_{l=1}^{L}$ represents the set of learnable weights and biases, and the function performs successive affine transformations interleaved with non-linear activations. For navigation, the input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ typically contains sensory data (LIDAR ranges, camera pixels, IMU readings, GPS coordinates), while the output $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ encodes motor commands (linear velocity, steering angle) or discrete navigational decisions (turn left, turn right, stop).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Neural networks are classified as *learning-based* or *reactive* navigation strategies, distinct from *deliberative* (map-based) and *behavioural* (rule-based) paradigms. They fall under the umbrella of **Soft Computing Techniques** in modern robotics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy and Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Child Learning to Walk" Analogy**
>
> Imagine teaching a toddler to navigate a cluttered living room. You do **not** hand the child a hand-crafted map of every piece of furniture. Instead, the child learns through experience — by bumping into obstacles, observing cause-and-effect, and gradually building an intuitive "internal model" of how to move through space. 
>
> A **neural network** does exactly the same thing for a robot. Instead of being explicitly programmed with rules like *"if obstacle is 30 cm ahead, turn left 15°"*, the network is *trained* on thousands of examples. It internally discovers patterns that map sensory inputs to safe motor outputs, much like the child's brain develops a sense of spatial awareness.

This learning-from-data capability is what makes neural networks exceptionally well-suited for navigation in **unknown, dynamic, and unstructured environments** — scenarios where classical algorithms often fail or require excessive manual tuning.

### 1.3 Why Neural Networks as an "Alternative" for Navigation?

Traditional navigation algorithms (A*, D*, PRM, RRT, potential fields) rely on:
- Accurate world models / maps
- Well-defined cost functions
- Explicit obstacle representations
- Deterministic or stochastic planning frameworks

Neural networks, by contrast, offer:

| Feature | Classical Methods | Neural Network Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **Map dependency** | High (mostly required) | Low (can learn implicit maps) |
| **Adaptability to new environments** | Poor (re-planning needed) | Excellent (generalization) |
| **Computational paradigm** | Sequential search | Parallel inference |
| **Sensor noise tolerance** | Low (degrades quickly) | High (learns noise patterns) |
| **Training cost** | None | High (one-time) |
| **Real-time inference** | Variable | Very fast (matrix multiplications) |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** When a question asks "Why use neural networks for navigation?" — answer with the triad: **(1) Learning capability, (2) Generalization to unseen environments, (3) Real-time parallel inference.**

### 1.4 Historical Context and Landmark Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALVINN (Autonomous Land Vehicle In a Neural Network)** — Pomerleau, 1989: One of the first end-to-end neural network navigation systems. A 3-layer backpropagation network with 30 inputs (from a camera + laser range finder), 4 hidden units, and 30 outputs (one for each road direction in a 30×32 grid). Drove a Navlab vehicle autonomously at **0.5 m/s** for **400 m** on a public road.

Other notable systems include:
- **DAVE** (DARPA Autonomous Vehicle) — used CNNs for off-road obstacle avoidance
- **NVIDIA PilotNet (2016)** — end-to-end CNN steering prediction
- **DeepMind's navigation agents** — recurrent + reinforcement learning for maze solving
- **Social Robot navigation** — LSTM networks modeling human motion patterns

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Building Block — The Artificial Neuron (Perceptron)

The perceptron, introduced by McCulloch and Pitts (1943) and refined by Rosenblatt (1958), is the atomic unit of a neural network. It computes a weighted sum of inputs, adds a bias, and applies a non-linear **activation function**.

Mathematical model of a single neuron:

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

$$a = \phi(z)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ is the input vector (sensor readings)
- $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n]^T$ is the weight vector
- $b$ is the bias term
- $z$ is the pre-activation (logit)
- $\phi(\cdot)$ is the activation function
- $a$ is the neuron's output (activation)

### 2.2 Network Architecture for Navigation

A typical **Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** for mobile robot navigation has the following layered structure:

**Layer 1 — Input Layer:**
Accepts pre-processed sensor data. For a robot with $k$ range sensors (e.g., ultrasonic or LIDAR):
$$\mathbf{x} = [d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_k, \theta_{goal}, v_{current}]^T$$

This concatenates obstacle distances, goal bearing, and current velocity into a single feature vector of dimension $n = k + 2$.

**Layer 2 — Hidden Layers:**
Apply successive transformations:
$$\mathbf{h}^{(l)} = \phi\left(W^{(l)} \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)}\right), \quad l = 1, 2, \ldots, L$$

For navigation, typical hidden layer widths are 16, 32, or 64 neurons, with $L = 2$ to $L = 4$ hidden layers.

**Layer 3 — Output Layer:**
For continuous control (steering + velocity):
$$\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \phi_{out}\left(W^{(L+1)} \mathbf{h}^{(L)} + \mathbf{b}^{(L+1)}\right)$$

For discrete decisions (e.g., Go / Turn Left / Turn Right / Stop), a **softmax** activation is used:
$$P(\text{class}_j) = \frac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_k}}$$

### 2.3 Common Activation Functions (Essential for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the choice and behaviour of activation functions. Memorize the table below.

| Activation | Formula | Range | Derivative $\phi'(z)$ | Use in Navigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | $\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ | Output layer for binary decisions (e.g., obstacle / free) |
| **Tanh** | $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^{z}-e^{-z}}{e^{z}+e^{-z}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $1 - \tanh^{2}(z)$ | Hidden layers (zero-centered, faster convergence) |
| **ReLU** | $f(z) = \max(0, z)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $1$ if $z > 0$, else $0$ | Modern default for hidden layers |
| **Leaky ReLU** | $f(z) = \max(\alpha z, z)$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $\alpha$ if $z < 0$, else $1$ | Avoids "dying ReLU" problem |
| **Softmax** | $\dfrac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_k e^{z_k}}$ | $(0, 1)$, sums to 1 | $\delta_{ij}\sigma_i - \sigma_i\sigma_j$ | Multi-class output (turn left/right/stop) |
| **Linear** | $f(z) = z$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $1$ | Output layer for continuous control (velocity) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Neural Networks for Navigation

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| $a^{(l)} = \phi(W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)})$ | Forward propagation through layer $l$ | Computing network output |
| $\mathbf{y} = \mathcal{N}_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ | Full network mapping | Inference (testing) |
| $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{y}_i, \hat{\mathbf{y}}_i)$ | Empirical risk / cost | Training objective |
| $\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = (y - \hat{y})^{2}$ | Mean Squared Error loss | Regression (steering prediction) |
| $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | Cross-Entropy loss | Classification (discrete actions) |
| $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta)$ | Gradient descent update | Learning rule |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$) | Controls step size |
| $\frac{\partial J}{\partial W^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^{T}$ | Weight gradient | Backpropagation |
| $\delta^{(L)} = (a^{(L)} - \mathbf{y}) \odot \phi'(z^{(L)})$ | Output layer error signal | Backpropagation |
| $\delta^{(l)} = (W^{(l+1)})^{T} \delta^{(l+1)} \odot \phi'(z^{(l)})$ | Hidden layer error signal | Backpropagation |
| $W^{(l)} \leftarrow W^{(l)} - \eta \delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^{T}$ | Weight update rule | Learning |
| $Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)]$ | Q-Learning update | Reinforcement-based nav |
| $\pi^{*}(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q(s, a)$ | Optimal policy | Action selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions and Typical Values for Navigation Tasks:**
> - Learning rate: $\eta \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-2}]$
> - Discount factor: $\gamma \in [0.9, 0.99]$
> - Mini-batch size: $B \in \{32, 64, 128\}$
> - Number of training epochs: $E \in [50, 500]$
> - Convergence threshold: $\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < 10^{-4}$

### 2.5 Training Paradigms for Navigation

**A. Supervised Learning (Behavioural Cloning):**
- Dataset: $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{y}_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $\mathbf{x}_i$ is sensor data and $\mathbf{y}_i$ is the expert's action.
- Loss: $\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^{2}$
- Used in: ALVINN, NVIDIA PilotNet, behavioural cloning for autonomous driving.

**B. Reinforcement Learning (RL):**
- Agent interacts with environment; learns a policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ that maximizes expected cumulative reward.
- Reward function for navigation:
$$r(s, a) = \begin{cases} +R_{goal} & \text{if goal reached} \\ -R_{collision} & \text{if collision} \\ -c \cdot d(s, s_{goal}) & \text{otherwise (progress reward)} \end{cases}$$
- Used in: Deep Q-Network (DQN) navigation, Policy Gradient methods (REINFORCE, PPO).

**C. Unsupervised Learning:**
- Self-organizing maps, autoencoders for dimensionality reduction of high-dimensional sensor inputs (e.g., raw 360° LIDAR scans).

**D. Imitation Learning (Inverse RL):**
- Learns reward function from expert demonstrations, then trains policy via RL.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

Neural network navigation is deployed in:
- **Warehouse robots** (Amazon Kiva systems) — RL-trained pickers
- **Autonomous vehicles** (Tesla, Waymo) — end-to-end CNN steering
- **Drones** — collision avoidance via deep RL
- **Service robots** — social navigation in human-populated environments
- **Mars rovers** — terrain classification via CNN
- **Underwater ROVs** — acoustic-based navigation
